blocks:
Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَوْفُوا۟ بِٱلْعُقُودِ ۚ أُحِلَّتْ لَكُم بَهِيمَةُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ إِلَّا مَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ غَيْرَ مُحِلِّى ٱلصَّيْدِ وَأَنتُمْ حُرُمٌ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ مَا يُرِيدُ (١)
O you who have faith! Keep your agreements. You are permitted animals of grazing livestock, except what is [now] announced to you, disallowing game while you are in pilgrim sanctity. Indeed Allah decrees whatever He desires. (1)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُحِلُّوا۟ شَعَـٰٓئِرَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا ٱلشَّهْرَ ٱلْحَرَامَ وَلَا ٱلْهَدْىَ وَلَا ٱلْقَلَـٰٓئِدَ وَلَآ ءَآمِّينَ ٱلْبَيْتَ ٱلْحَرَامَ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرِضْوَٰنًا ۚ وَإِذَا حَلَلْتُمْ فَٱصْطَادُوا۟ ۚ وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَـَٔانُ قَوْمٍ أَن صَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ أَن تَعْتَدُوا۟ ۘ وَتَعَاوَنُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلْبِرِّ وَٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ۖ وَلَا تَعَاوَنُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٢)
O you who have faith! Do not violate Allah’s sacraments, neither the sacred month, nor the offering, nor the necklaces, nor those bound for the Sacred House, who seek their Lord’s grace and [His] pleasure. But when you emerge from pilgrim sanctity, you may hunt for game. Ill feeling for a people should not lead you, because they barred you from [access to] the Sacred Mosque, to transgress. Cooperate in piety and Godwariness, but do not cooperate in sin and aggression, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is severe in retribution. (2)
حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةُ وَٱلدَّمُ وَلَحْمُ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ وَمَآ أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ وَٱلْمُنْخَنِقَةُ وَٱلْمَوْقُوذَةُ وَٱلْمُتَرَدِّيَةُ وَٱلنَّطِيحَةُ وَمَآ أَكَلَ ٱلسَّبُعُ إِلَّا مَا ذَكَّيْتُمْ وَمَا ذُبِحَ عَلَى ٱلنُّصُبِ وَأَن تَسْتَقْسِمُوا۟ بِٱلْأَزْلَـٰمِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ فِسْقٌ ۗ ٱلْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن دِينِكُمْ فَلَا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَٱخْشَوْنِ ۚ ٱلْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِى وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمَ دِينًا ۚ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ فِى مَخْمَصَةٍ غَيْرَ مُتَجَانِفٍ لِّإِثْمٍ ۙ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣)
You are prohibited carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and what has been offered to other than Allah, and the animal strangled or beaten to death, and that which dies by falling or is gored to death, and that which is mangled by a beast of prey—barring that which you may purify —and what is sacrificed on stone altars [to idols], and that you should divide by raffling with arrows. All that is transgression. Today the faithless have despaired of your religion. So do not fear them, but fear Me. Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you, and I have approved Islam as your religion. But should anyone be compelled by hunger, without inclining to sin, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (3)

(Commentary)

The overarching purpose of the Surah, as indicated by reflection upon its opening and closing, the general nature of the verses contained within it, and the laws, exhortations, and stories it includes, is the call to fulfill covenants (الوفاء بالعهود) and preserve true pledges (المواثيق الحقة), whatever they may be. It serves as a profound warning against breaking them or treating them with indifference. It establishes that Allah’s custom (عادته تعالى) has consistently been to grant mercy, ease, and alleviation to those who are God-conscious (اتقى) and believe, then remain God-conscious and do good; while being severe toward those who act insolently, transgress, and tyrannize by breaking free from the bond of the covenant of obedience (ربقة العهد بالطاعة) and violating the limits of the pledges taken upon them in the religion (الدين).

For this reason, you find the Surah encompassing many laws regarding legal punishments (الحدود) and retribution (القصاص), as well as accounts such as the story of the Table (المائدة), the questioning of the Messiah, and the story of the two sons of Adam. It also refers to many of the injustices of the Children of Israel and their breaking of the pledges taken from them. Furthermore, it contains many verses in which Allah the Exalted bestows His favor upon people through matters such as the perfection of the religion (إكمال الدين), the completion of the blessing (إتمام النعمة), the lawfulness of good things (إحلال الطيبات), and the legislation of that which purifies people, without intending for them any hardship or difficulty.

This is appropriate for the time of the Surah’s revelation, as the transmitters of tradition do not differ that it was the last detailed (مفصلة) Surah revealed to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in the final days of his life. It has been reported in the narrations of both schools that it is abrogating (ناسخة) and not abrogated (غير منسوخة). Fittingly, this necessitates an emphasized exhortation to preserve the pledges taken for Allah the Exalted upon His servants and to remain steadfast in them.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَوْفُوا بِالْعُقُودِ — O you who have faith! Keep your agreements. (5:1). The term "agreements" (العقود) is the plural of "agreement" (عقد), which is the tying of one thing to another in a manner that makes their separation difficult, such as tying one rope or thread to another of its kind. Its necessary implication is the mutual commitment of one to the other and their lack of disjunction. This concept was initially considered by them in sensory matters, then it was borrowed and generalized for abstract matters, such as the contracts of transactions (عقود المعاملات) circulating among them—like selling, leasing, and the like—and all covenants and pledges. The word was applied to these because of the stability of the essential meaning, which, as you have learned, is necessity (اللزوم) and commitment (الالتزام).

Since the "agreement" (العقد)—which is the "covenant" (العهد)—applies to all religious pledges that Allah has taken from His servants regarding pillars and parts, such as monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the rest of the fundamental knowledge, acts of worship, and legislated laws, whether established or endorsed (including transaction contracts and others); and since the word "agreements" (العقود) is also a plural defined by the definite article (ال), the most appropriate view is to interpret "agreements" in the verse as encompassing everything that can truly be called an agreement.

By this, the weakness of what some exegetes have mentioned becomes apparent: that "agreements" refers only to the contracts people make among themselves, such as contracts of sale, marriage, and covenants, or what a person binds upon himself, such as an oath (اليمين).

Similarly, what others have mentioned—that it refers to the covenants the people of the pre-Islamic era (الجاهلية) made with one another for support and assistance against those who intended them harm or transgressed against them, which was the alliance (الحلف) circulating among them—is also weak.

Likewise, what others have stated—that it refers to the pledges taken from the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) to act according to what is in the Torah and the Gospel—is also weak. These are views for which there is no evidence from the perspective of the wording. Moreover, the apparent meaning of the plural defined by the definite article and the linguistic absolute application of "agreement" (العقد) to every contract and law does not harmonize with these restricted views. Thus, interpreting it as a general term (العموم) is the most appropriate.

(A Discourse on the Meaning of Agreement)

The Book, as you see from the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: أَوْفُوا بِالْعُقُودِ (Keep your agreements), indicates the command to fulfill agreements. In its apparent sense, it is general, encompassing everything that is conventionally termed an agreement (العقد) in a way that is compatible with fulfillment. An agreement is every act or word that represents the linguistic meaning of "tying" (العقد), which is a type of linking one thing to another such that it becomes binding and inseparable. Examples include the contract of sale (عقد البيع), which is the linking of the sold item to the buyer in terms of ownership (ملكاً) such that he may dispose of it as he wishes, while the seller no longer has ownership or right of disposal after the contract; and the marriage contract (عقد النكاح), which links the woman to the man such that he may enjoy the marital relationship with her, and the woman may not allow another to enjoy her; and the covenant (العهد) in which the one making the covenant empowers the one to whom it is made regarding what he has promised, and he has no right to break it.

The Quran has emphasized the fulfillment of agreements and covenants in all their meanings and instances with the utmost severity. It has condemned those who break pledges (الناقضين للمواثيق) with profound censure and threatened them with a harsh warning, while praising those who fulfill their covenant when they make one in many verses that need not be quoted here.

The verses have presented this matter in a manner that indicates it is something humans grasp through their primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and indeed it is so.

This is only because the covenant and its fulfillment are things that a human being can never dispense with in life; the individual and society are equal in this regard. If we were to reflect upon the social life of humans, we would find that all the advantages we benefit from and all the vital social rights we rely upon are built on the foundation of the general social contract (العقد الاجتماعي العام) and the secondary agreements and covenants that follow from it. We possess nothing of ourselves for the members of society, nor do they possess anything from us, except through a practical agreement, even if we do not express it in words—for words are only for the need of clarification. If it were permissible for a person to break what he has contracted or promised by choice—because of his ability to do so through power, authority, force, or some excuse he offers—the first thing to be destroyed by his breach would be social justice (العدل الاجتماعي), which is the pillar to which humans flee and take refuge from the bondage of exploitation and utilization.

For this reason, Allah, the Glorified, emphasized the preservation of the covenant and its fulfillment. He, the Exalted, said: وَأَوْفُوا بِالْعَهْدِ إِنَّ الْعَهْدَ كانَ مَسْؤُلاً — and fulfill the covenants; indeed all covenants are accountable. (17:34). The verse encompasses the individual covenant made between one person and another—as do most verses praising the fulfillment of covenants and condemning their breach—just as it encompasses the social covenant circulating between one people and another, or one nation and another. In fact, fulfillment of the latter is more important in the view of the religion than the individual covenant, because justice in it is more complete and the calamity in its breach is more widespread.

Therefore, the Mighty Book, even in the most delicate situations where breaking a covenant might seem easiest, has prohibited such breach with the most explicit speech and clearest explanation. He, the Exalted, said: بَراءَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ إِلَى الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ فَسِيحُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِي اللهِ وَأَنَّ اللهَ مُخْزِي الْكافِرِينَ وَأَذانٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ إِلَى النَّاسِ يَوْمَ الْحَجِّ الْأَكْبَرِ أَنَّ اللهَ بَرِيءٌ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ وَرَسُولُهُ فَإِنْ تُبْتُمْ فَهو خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ وَإِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِي اللهِ وَبَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَنْقُصُوكُمْ شَيْئاً وَلَمْ يُظاهِرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَحَداً فَأَتِمُّوا إِلَيْهِمْ عَهْدَهُمْ إِلى مُدَّتِهِمْ إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فَإِذَا انْسَلَخَ الْأَشْهُرُ الْحُرُمُ فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ وَخُذُوهُمْ وَاحْصُرُوهُمْ وَاقْعُدُوا لَهُمْ كُلَّ مَرْصَدٍ — [This is] a declaration of immunity from Allah and His Apostle to the polytheists with whom you had made a treaty. (1) Travel [freely] in the land for four months, and know that you cannot foil Allah, and that Allah shall disgrace the faithless. (2) [This is] an announcement from Allah and His Apostle to all the people on the day of the greater hajj: that Allah and His Apostle are absolved of the polytheists. So if you repent, that will be better for you, and if you turn away, then know that you cannot foil Allah. And announce a painful punishment to the faithless. (3) Excepting those of the polytheists with whom you have made a treaty, and who have not since then deficiently observed any of its terms with you, nor backed anyone against you. So fulfill the treaty with them until [the end of] its term. Indeed Allah loves the God-conscious. (4) Then, when the sacred months have passed, slay the polytheists wherever you may find them, and seize them and besiege them, and lie in wait for them at every ambush. (9:1-5).

These verses, as their context indicates, were revealed after the conquest of Mecca, when Allah had humbled the polytheists, annihilated their power, and removed their prestige. They command the Muslims to purify the land they had conquered and dominated from the filth of polytheism, and they declare the blood of the polytheists to be forfeit without any condition or restriction except that they believe. Despite this, the verses exclude a group of polytheists with whom the Muslims had a non-aggression treaty. They do not permit the Muslims to touch them with harm even when they were weakened and humbled, with no obstacle on their part to prevent it nor any defender to repel it—all of this out of respect for the covenant and regard for the side of godwariness (التقوى).

Yes, the one who breaks a covenant after its agreement must have the covenant he broke invalidated, and he should receive nothingness and falsehood as a consequence, as a transgression against him similar to the transgression he committed. He, the Exalted, said: كَيْفَ يَكُونُ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ عَهْدٌ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَعِنْدَ رَسُولِهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ عِنْدَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ فَمَا اسْتَقامُوا لَكُمْ فَاسْتَقِيمُوا لَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ — How can the polytheists have a treaty with Allah and His Apostle, except those with whom you made a treaty at the Holy Mosque? So long as they are steadfast with you, be steadfast with them. Indeed Allah loves the God-conscious. (9:7) — until He said — لا يَرْقُبُونَ فِي مُؤْمِنٍ إِلًّا وَلا ذِمَّةً وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُعْتَدُونَ فَإِنْ تابُوا وَأَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتَوُا الزَّكاةَ فَإِخْوانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَنُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ وَإِنْ نَكَثُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ عَهْدِهِمْ وَطَعَنُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ فَقاتِلُوا أَئِمَّةَ الْكُفْرِ إِنَّهُمْ لا أَيْمانَ لَهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَنْتَهُونَ — They observe no bond or treaty regarding a faithful [person], and it is they who are the transgressors. (10) Yet if they repent and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, then they are your brethren in faith. We elaborate the signs for a people who have knowledge. (11) But if they break their pledges after their treaty and revile your religion, then fight the leaders of unfaith—indeed they have no [binding] pledges—so that they may desist. (9:10-12). And He, the Exalted, said: فَمَنِ اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ فَاعْتَدُوا عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ — So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you, and be wary of Allah. (2:194). And He, the Exalted, said: وَلا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَآنُ قَوْمٍ أَنْ صَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ أَنْ تَعْتَدُوا وَتَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوى وَلا تَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوانِ وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ — and do not let ill will toward a people because they barred you from the Holy Mosque incite you to transgress. Help one another in piety and godwariness, and do not help one another in sin and aggression, and be wary of Allah. (5:2).

The sum of the matter is that Islam views the sanctity of the covenant and the obligation to fulfill it as absolute, whether the one making the covenant benefits from it or is harmed by it after the pledge has been secured. This is because upholding the side of social justice is more binding and obligatory than upholding any specific or personal benefit—unless one of the contracting parties breaks his covenant, in which case the other party may break it in a manner similar to how it was broken and transgress against him as he transgressed. For in that is an exit from the bond of exploitation and blameworthy superiority, which the champion of religion only rose to remove.

By my life, this is one of the high teachings brought by the religion of Islam to guide people toward observing the human primordial nature (الفطرة) in its judgment and preserving social justice, without which the thread of human society cannot be organized, and to remove the injustice of exploitation and utilization. The Mighty Book has explicitly stated this, and the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله followed it in his noble biography (سيرته). Were it not that this is a Quranic discussion, we would have mentioned to you some of his stories (peace be upon him) in this regard; you should refer to the books written on his biography and life history.

If you compare the custom (سنة) of Islam in respecting the covenant with the customs of civilized and uncivilized nations—especially what we hear and witness every day of the treatment of weak nations by strong ones in their treaties and agreements, preserving them as long as they yield profit or are required by the interests of their state, and breaking them with what is called an "excuse"—you will find the difference between the two customs in observing the right and serving the truth.

It is fitting for the religion to be thus, and for their customs to be so, for there are only two logics: a logic that says the truth must be observed regardless of the circumstances, and in its observation lie the benefits of society; and a logic that says the benefits of the nation must be observed by any means possible, even if it crushes the truth. The first logic is the logic of religion, and the second is the logic of all barbaric or civilized social systems—be they autocratic, democratic, communist, or otherwise.

You have also learned that Islam, in its determination in this matter, does not limit itself to the conventional covenant but generalizes its ruling to everything upon which a structure is built and recommends its observation. This discussion has further ramifications that you will encounter in future discourse, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: أُحِلَّتْ لَكُمْ بَهِيمَةُ الْأَنْعامِ إِلَّا ما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ (etc.) — Lawful for you is the animal of the cattle, except what will be recited to you. (5:1). "Lawfulness" (الإحلال) is permissibility (الإباحة). "Animal" (البهيمة) is a name for every four-legged creature of the land and sea, according to what is in al-Majma'. Based on this, the annexation of "animal" to "cattle" (الأنعام) is like the annexation of a species to its types, such as saying "the species of man" or "the genus of animal." It is also said that "animal" (البهيمة) refers to the fetus of the cattle, in which case the annexation is possessive. In any case, His saying: أُحِلَّتْ لَكُمْ بَهِيمَةُ الْأَنْعامِ means the eight pairs—that is, the eating of their meat. His saying: إِلَّا ما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ (except what will be recited to you) refers to what is coming in His saying: حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةُ وَالدَّمُ وَلَحْمُ الْخِنْزِيرِ وَما أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ اللهِ بِهِ — You are forbidden carrion and blood, the flesh of the swine, and what has been offered to other than Allah. (5:3).

His saying: غَيْرَ مُحِلِّي الصَّيْدِ وَأَنْتُمْ حُرُمٌ — game being unlawful when you are in the state of pilgrim sanctity. (5:1). This is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the pronoun of address in His saying "lawful for you" (أُحِلَّتْ لَكُمْ). Its meaning is the prohibition of that which was made lawful if its hunting occurs during the state of pilgrim sanctity (الإحرام), such as wild gazelles, cows, and donkeys if they are hunted. It is also said that it is a circumstantial clause for His saying "Keep" (أَوْفُوا) or for the pronoun of address in His saying "recited to you" (يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ). "Game" (الصيد) is a verbal noun used in the sense of the object (the hunted animal), just as "sanctity" (الحرم) is the plural of "sacred" (الحرام) in the sense of the one in the state of pilgrim sanctity (المحرم), which is an active participle.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحِلُّوا شَعائِرَ اللهِ وَلَا الشَّهْرَ الْحَرامَ وَلَا الْهَدْيَ وَلَا الْقَلائِدَ وَلَا آمِّينَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرامَ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلاً مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ وَرِضْواناً — O you who have faith! Do not violate the sacraments of Allah, nor the sacred month, nor the offering, nor the necklaces, nor those bound for the Sacred House, seeking the grace of their Lord and [His] pleasure. (5:2). This is a renewed address to the believers, indicating intense concern for the sanctities (حرمات) of Allah the Exalted.

"Violation" (الإحلال) is the permissibility associated with a lack of regard for sanctity and status. Its meaning is determined according to what it is annexed to: thus, violating the sacraments of Allah (شعائر الله) means not respecting them and abandoning them; violating the sacred month (الشهر الحرام) means not preserving its sanctity and fighting during it; and so on.

"Sacraments" (الشعائر) is the plural of "sacrament" (شعيرة), which is a sign (العلامة). What is meant by them are the signs of the Hajj and its rituals. The "sacred month" refers to the months of the lunar year that Allah has made sacred: al-Muharram, Rajab, Dhu al-Qa'dah, and Dhu al-Hijjah.

The "offering" (الهدي) is what is driven for the Hajj from sheep, cattle, and camels. "Necklaces" (القلائد) is the plural of "necklace" (قلادة), which is what is hung around the neck of the offering, such as a sandal or the like, so that it may be known that it is an offering for the Hajj and thus not be interfered with. "Those bound" (الآمين) is the plural of "one who intends" (آمّ), an active participle from "to intend" (أمّ), referring to those intending to visit the Sacred House. His saying: يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلاً (seeking grace) is a circumstantial clause for "those bound." "Grace" (الفضل) is wealth or financial profit, as it was applied in His saying: فَانْقَلَبُوا بِنِعْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَفَضْلٍ لَمْ يَمْسَسْهُمْ سُوءٌ — So they returned with Allah’s blessing and grace, and no evil touched them. (3:174). Or it refers to the reward of the Hereafter, or both wealth and reward.

Exegetes have differed in the interpretation of "sacraments," "necklaces," and other individual terms in the verse into various views. What we have chosen to mention is most appropriate to the context of the verse, and there is no benefit in addressing the details of the other views.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا حَلَلْتُمْ فَاصْطادُوا — But when you emerge from pilgrim sanctity, you may hunt. (5:2). This is a command occurring after a prohibition, which indicates nothing more than permissibility (الإباحة) in the sense of the absence of prevention. "Emerging" (الحل) and "releasing" (الإحلال)—both in the simple and augmented forms—carry the same meaning, which is exiting the state of pilgrim sanctity (الإحرام).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَآنُ قَوْمٍ أَنْ صَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ أَنْ تَعْتَدُوا — and do not let ill will toward a people because they barred you from the Holy Mosque incite you to transgress. (5:2). It is said: "he incited him" (جرمه يجرمه), meaning he carried him [to an act]. From this comes "crime" (الجريمة) for a sin, because it is "carried" in terms of its consequence, and for financial penalties and others because they are "carried" upon the criminal. Al-Raghib mentioned that the root of its meaning is "cutting." "Ill will" (الشنآن) is enmity and hatred. His saying: أَنْ صَدُّوكُمْ (because they barred you) is a substitute (بدل) or an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for "ill will." The sum of the verse's meaning is: Let not the enmity of a people—which is that they barred you from the Holy Mosque—incite you to transgress against them after Allah has given you victory over them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوى وَلا تَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوانِ — Help one another in piety and godwariness, and do not help one another in sin and aggression. (5:2). The meaning is clear, and this is the foundation of the Islamic custom (السنة الإسلامية). Allah, the Glorified, has interpreted "piety" (البر) in His speech as faith and excellence (الإحسان) in worship and transactions, as previously mentioned in His saying: وَلكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ — but piety is [that of] one who believes in Allah and the Last Day... (2:177), and the discussion on it has preceded. "Godwariness" (التقوى) is the monitoring of Allah's command and prohibition. Thus, the meaning of helping one another in piety and godwariness returns to gathering upon faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) on the basis of godwariness toward Allah, which is social righteousness and godwariness. Its opposite is helping one another in "sin" (الإثم), which is the evil deed that entails falling behind in the matters of a happy life, and in "aggression" (العدوان), which is transgressing against the true rights of people by stripping security from their souls, honor, or wealth. A portion of the discussion on this meaning has passed under His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اصْبِرُوا وَصابِرُوا وَرابِطُوا — O you who have faith! Be patient, and stand firm, and close [your] ranks... (3:200) in the fourth volume of this book.

Then He, the Glorified, emphasized His prohibition of gathering upon sin and aggression by saying: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ — and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is severe in retribution. (5:2). This is, in reality, an emphasis upon an emphasis.

His saying, the Exalted: حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةُ وَالدَّمُ وَلَحْمُ الْخِنْزِيرِ وَما أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ اللهِ بِهِ — You are forbidden carrion and blood, the flesh of the swine, and what has been offered to other than Allah. (5:3). These four are mentioned in what was revealed of the Quran before this Surah, such as Surah al-An'am and Surah al-Nahl, both of which are Meccan, and Surah al-Baqarah, which is the first detailed Surah revealed in Medina. He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ لا أَجِدُ فِي ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ مُحَرَّماً عَلى طاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُ إِلَّا أَنْ يَكُونَ مَيْتَةً أَوْ دَماً مَسْفُوحاً أَوْ لَحْمَ خِنزِيرٍ فَإِنَّهُ رِجْسٌ أَوْ فِسْقاً أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ اللهِ بِهِ فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ باغٍ وَلا عادٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — Say, ‘I do not find in what has been revealed to me anything forbidden to be eaten by one who wishes to eat it, unless it be carrion or spilt blood, or the flesh of swine—for that is indeed an impurity—or an impiety offered to other than Allah.’ But should someone be compelled, without being rebellious or aggressive, indeed your Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (6:145). And He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّما حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةَ وَالدَّمَ وَلَحْمُ الْخِنْزِيرِ وَما أُهِلَّ بِهِ لِغَيْرِ اللهِ فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ باغٍ وَلا عادٍ فَلا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — He has only forbidden you carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and what has been offered to other than Allah. But should someone be compelled, without being rebellious or aggressive, there shall be no sin upon him. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (2:173).

All these verses—as you see—prohibit these four mentioned at the beginning of this verse and resemble this verse also in the exception occurring at its end in His saying: فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ فِي مَخْمَصَةٍ غَيْرَ مُتَجانِفٍ لِإِثْمٍ فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — But should anyone be compelled by hunger, without inclining to sin, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5:3). Thus, the verse of al-Ma'idah, regarding these shared meanings between it and those verses, is an emphasis of those verses.

In fact, the prohibition of them—especially the first three, namely carrion, blood, and the flesh of swine—predates the revelation of the Meccan Surahs al-An'am and al-Nahl. For the verse of al-An'am justifies the prohibition of the three, or specifically the flesh of swine, by stating it is "impurity" (رجس), thus indicating the prohibition of eating impurity. He, the Exalted, said in Surah al-Muddaththir—which is among the Surahs revealed at the beginning of the Mission—: وَالرُّجْزَ فَاهْجُرْ — and keep away from all pollution. (74:5).

Similarly, what He enumerated in His saying: وَالْمُنْخَنِقَةُ وَالْمَوْقُوذَةُ وَالْمُتَرَدِّيَةُ وَالنَّطِيحَةُ وَما أَكَلَ السَّبُعُ — the strangled, the victim of a fatal blow, the victim of a fall, the victim of goring, and what the beast of prey has eaten from — are all instances of carrion (الميتة), as evidenced by His saying: إِلَّا ما ذَكَّيْتُمْ (except what you slaughter [lawfully]). They were only mentioned in the verse for a specific concern with clarifying the individuals of carrion and providing further explanation of the prohibited foods, without the verse containing a new legislation regarding them.

Likewise, what Allah the Exalted enumerated in His saying: وَما ذُبِحَ عَلَى النُّصُبِ وَأَنْ تَسْتَقْسِمُوا بِالْأَزْلامِ ذلِكُمْ فِسْقٌ — and what is sacrificed on nominal altars, and that you should divide by raffling with arrows: all that is transgression. (5:3). Although these two are mentioned for the first time in this Surah, He, the Exalted, justified their prohibition—or the prohibition of the second of them, according to a weak possibility—by "transgression" (الفسق), and transgression was already prohibited in the verse of al-An'am. Similarly, His saying: غَيْرَ مُتَجانِفٍ لِإِثْمٍ (without inclining to sin) indicates the prohibition of what was mentioned in the verse because it is "sin" (إثم), and the verse of al-Baqarah indicated the prohibition of sin. He, the Exalted, also said: وَذَرُوا ظاهِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَباطِنَهُ — Renounce outward sin and the inward [as well]. (6:120). And He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ — Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies, the outward among them and the inward, and sin...’ (7:33).

Thus, it has become clear and evident that the verse, in what it enumerates of prohibitions, does not include any new matter that was not preceded by a prohibition in the Meccan or Medinan verses that came before it, which encompassed the enumeration of prohibited foods such as meats and the like.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمُنْخَنِقَةُ وَالْمَوْقُوذَةُ وَالْمُتَرَدِّيَةُ وَالنَّطِيحَةُ وَما أَكَلَ السَّبُعُ إِلَّا ما ذَكَّيْتُمْ — the strangled, the victim of a fatal blow, the victim of a fall, the victim of goring, and what the beast of prey has eaten from, except what you slaughter [lawfully]. (5:3). The "strangled" (المنخنقة) is the animal that dies by strangulation, whether it occurs by chance or by the deliberate act of an agent, and by whatever tool or means, such as a rope tied around its neck that crushes its airway, or by inserting its head between two pieces of wood, as this method and its like were common among them in the pre-Islamic era.

The "victim of a fatal blow" (الموقوذة) is that which is struck until it dies. The "victim of a fall" (المتردية) is that which has fallen (تردت) from a high place, such as a mountain peak or a well and the like.

The "victim of goring" (النطيحة) is that which died from being gored by another. "What the beast of prey has eaten from" (ما أكل السبع) is that which the beast of prey has eaten—that is, eaten of its flesh—for "eating" relates to the thing eaten whether it consumes all or part of it. The "beast of prey" (السبع) is the predatory wild animal such as the lion, wolf, leopard, and the like.

His saying: إِلَّا ما ذَكَّيْتُمْ (except what you slaughter [lawfully]) is an exception for that which accepts lawful slaughter (التذكية) in the sense of cutting the four vessels (الأوداج الأربعة) while there is still a remnant of life in it, indicated by something like the movement of the tail or the trace of breathing and such. The exception, as we mentioned just now, relates to all the enumerated items that accept it, without being restricted to the last one without evidence.

These five matters—namely the strangled, the victim of a fatal blow, the victim of a fall, the victim of goring, and what the beast of prey has eaten from—are all individuals and instances of carrion (الميتة). This means that the victim of a fall or the victim of goring, for example, are only prohibited if they die from the fall or the goring. The evidence for this is His saying: إِلَّا ما ذَكَّيْتُمْ, for it is self-evident that they are not eaten as long as the soul is in their bodies; they are only eaten after its departure. At that point, they are either lawfully slaughtered or not. Allah, the Glorified, has excepted lawful slaughter, so nothing remains for the prohibition except if they die from a fall or goring without lawful slaughter. As for if a sheep—for example—falls into a well and is then brought out sound and in good condition, living for a short or long time, and then dies a natural death or is lawfully slaughtered by dhabh, it is not called a "victim of a fall." The context indicates this, as the items mentioned are those that have perished, and their perishing is attributed to the description mentioned for them, such as strangulation, being struck, falling, or goring.

The reason for specifying these instances of carrion by mention is to remove what might occur to the mind—that they are not carrion—based on the fact that they are rare individuals of it, and the mind usually jumps to the common individual, which is what dies of disease and the like without a sudden external cause. Thus, He, the Exalted, explicitly stated these rare individuals and instances by their names so that confusion is removed and the prohibition becomes clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما ذُبِحَ عَلَى النُّصُبِ — and what is sacrificed on nominal altars. (5:3). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "To set up" (نصب) something is to place it in a protruding position, like setting up a spear, a building, or a stone. "The altar" (النصيب) refers to stones set up over something; its plural is nasa'ib and nusub. The Arabs had stones they worshipped and sacrificed upon. He said: كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلى نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ — as if they were racing toward raised standards. (70:43). He said: وَما ذُبِحَ عَلَى النُّصُبِ. Its plural may also be said as ansab, as He said: وَالْأَنْصابُ وَالْأَزْلامُ — idols and raffling arrows. (5:90). Al-nasab and al-nusub also mean fatigue.

The intent of the prohibition of eating the meat of what is sacrificed on nominal altars is to prevent following the customs of the pre-Islamic era in that regard, for they had set up stones around the Kaaba which they sanctified and sacrificed upon, and it was among the customs of idolatry.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْ تَسْتَقْسِمُوا بِالْأَزْلامِ — and that you should divide by raffling with arrows. (5:3). "Arrows" (الأزلام) are the divining arrows. "Dividing by raffling" (الاستقسام) with arrows is to take a camel—or another animal—based on shares, then to cast the arrows to determine who has a share and who does not, and to determine the different shares themselves. This is gambling (الميسر), and its explanation has passed under His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ — They ask you concerning wine and gambling. (2:219) in the second volume of this book.

Al-Raghib said: "The share" (القسم) is the setting aside of a portion. It is said: "I divided such and such" (قسمت كذا) as a division (قسماً) and a portion (قسمة). The division of inheritance and the division of booty is their distribution among their owners. He said: لِكُلِّ بابٍ مِنْهُمْ جُزْءٌ مَقْسُومٌ — each of them having a designated share. (15:44). وَنَبِّئْهُمْ أَنَّ الْماءَ قِسْمَةٌ بَيْنَهُمْ — And inform them that the water is to be shared between them. (54:28). "I sought a division from him" (استقسمته) means I asked him to divide. Then it may be used in the sense of "divided" (قسم). He said: وَأَنْ تَسْتَقْسِمُوا بِالْأَزْلامِ. What he mentioned regarding "seeking a division" (استقسم) being in the sense of "divided" (قسم) is only in terms of its application to the instance. The true meaning is seeking the division by means of the arrows, which are the tools of this act; thus, using the tool is a request for the occurrence of the act resulting from it, so the form istif'al (seeking) is correct. The intent of dividing by arrows prohibited here, according to the apparent context, is the casting of arrows for the camel and the like to take what is in its meat of the share.

As for what some have mentioned—that the intent of dividing by arrows is the casting of arrows to seek knowledge of good and evil in actions, and to distinguish the beneficial from the harmful, such as one who intends to travel, marry, or begin a work, so he casts arrows to determine what has good in it from what does not—they said: this was common among the Arabs of the pre-Islamic era, and it is a type of omen-seeking (الطيرة). Further explanation of this will come in the following discussion of narrations. However, the context of the verse refuses to interpret the word as dividing in this sense. This is because the verse—which is in the position of enumerating prohibited foods, as indicated previously in His saying: إِلَّا ما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ—enumerates ten of its prohibitions: carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, what has been offered to other than Allah, the strangled, the victim of a fatal blow, the victim of a fall, the victim of goring, what the beast of prey has eaten from, and what is sacrificed on nominal altars. Then it mentions dividing by arrows, which carries the meaning of dividing meat by gambling, and the meaning of seeking knowledge of good and evil in matters. How can one doubt, after this clear context and successive clues, the necessity of interpreting the word as the division of meat through gambling? Would anyone acquainted with speech hesitate in this?

A similar example is that "visitation" (العمرة) is a verbal noun in the sense of "habitation" (العمارة), and it has another meaning, which is the visitation of the Sacred House. If it is annexed to the House, both meanings are possible, but it is not conceivable in His saying, the Exalted: وَأَتِمُّوا الْحَجَّ وَالْعُمْرَةَ لِلَّهِ — Complete the hajj and the ‘umrah for Allah. (2:196) except the first meaning [the pilgrimage]. Examples of this are many.

His saying: ذلِكُمْ فِسْقٌ (all that is transgression) possibly refers to all the mentioned items, or to the last two mentioned after His saying: إِلَّا ما ذَكَّيْتُمْ due to the intervention of the exception, or to the very last one. Perhaps the middle view is the best of the three.

His saying, the Exalted: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَاخْشَوْنِ — Today the faithless have despaired of your religion. So do not fear them, but fear Me. (5:3). The matter of this verse in its placement and then in its indication is remarkable. For if you reflect upon the beginning of the verse—namely His saying, the Exalted: حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةُ وَالدَّمُ until His saying ذلِكُمْ فِسْقٌ—and you add to it its conclusion—namely His saying: فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ فِي مَخْمَصَةٍ غَيْرَ مُتَجانِفٍ لِإِثْمٍ فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ—you will find it to be a complete speech, not dependent for the completion of its meaning or the communication of its intent upon anything from His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ (etc.) at all. You would find it a complete verse similar to the verses revealed before it in Surah al-An'am, al-Nahl, and al-Baqarah that clarify prohibited foods. In Surah al-Baqarah: إِنَّما حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةَ وَالدَّمَ وَلَحْمُ الْخِنْزِيرِ وَما أُهِلَّ بِهِ لِغَيْرِ اللهِ فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ باغٍ وَلا عادٍ فَلا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ (2:173), and what is in Surah al-An'am and al-Nahl is similar.

This results in the fact that His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (etc.) is a parenthetical speech (كلام معترض) placed in the middle of this verse, upon which the wording of the verse does not depend for its indication and clarification. This is true whether we say the verse was revealed in the middle of the [other] verse and thus was interspersed within it from the moment it was revealed, or we say that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is the one who commanded the scribes of the revelation to place the verse in this position despite the two verses being separate and differing in their revelation, or we say that it was placed in its current position during the compilation without its revelation being simultaneous. None of these possibilities affects what we have mentioned regarding this interspersed speech being parenthetical when measured against the beginning and end of the verse.

This is supported by the fact that the majority of narrations regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول)—if not all of them, and they are numerous reports—specifically mention His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (etc.) without addressing the original verse, namely His saying: حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةُ, at all. This also supports the independent revelation of His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ (etc.) separate from the beginning and the end, and that the occurrence of the verse in the middle of the [other] verse is based on the compilation of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله or the compilation of the compilers after him.

This is further supported by what was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from 'Abd ibn Humayd from al-Sha'bi, who said: "This verse was revealed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while he was at 'Arafah: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ (Today I have perfected for you your religion). Whenever verses pleased him, he would make them the beginning of the Surah." He said: "And Gabriel used to teach him how to perform the rituals."

Furthermore, these two sentences—namely His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ and His saying: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ—are close in content and linked in concept without doubt, due to the apparent close connection between the despair of the disbelievers regarding the religion of the Muslims and the perfection of the religion of the Muslims. The two contents are capable of being blended to form a single content with interconnected parts and joined edges, in addition to the unity of context between the two sentences.

This is supported by what we see: that the predecessors and successors among the exegetes of the Companions, the Followers, and the later scholars until our day have taken the two sentences as connected, one completing the other. This is only because they understood this from these two sentences and proceeded on the basis of their simultaneous revelation and their union in terms of indicating a single meaning.

This results in the fact that this parenthetical verse—namely His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ until His saying وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلامَ دِيناً—is a single, connected speech whose parts are joined, aimed at a single purpose established by the sum of the sentences without fragmentation. This is true whether we say it is linked to the verse surrounding it or not, for that does not affect at all the fact that this whole is a single parenthetical speech and not two speeches with two purposes. Furthermore, the "Today" (اليوم) repeated in His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا and in His saying: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ refers to a single day in which the disbelievers despaired and the religion was perfected.

Then, what is meant by this "Today" occurring in His saying, the Exalted: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ? Is what is meant by it the time of the appearance of Islam with the mission of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and his call, such that the meaning is that Allah sent down Islam to you, perfected the religion for you, completed the blessing upon you, and made the disbelievers despair of you?

There is no way to accept this, because the apparent context is that they had a religion which the disbelievers were greedy to invalidate or change, and the Muslims used to fear them regarding their religion; then Allah made the disbelievers despair of what they were greedy for and made the Muslims secure. It also implies that the religion was deficient and then Allah perfected it and completed His blessing upon them. They did not have a religion before Islam such that the disbelievers would be greedy for it or that Allah would perfect it and complete His blessing upon them.

Moreover, the necessary implication of the mentioned meaning is that His saying: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ would precede His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا, so that the speech would be sound in its order.

Or is what is meant by "Today" the period after the conquest of Mecca, wherein Allah invalidated the plot of the polytheists of Quraysh, removed their prestige, demolished the structure of their religion, and broke their idols, so their hope was cut off that they would stand on their feet, oppose Islam, and prevent the influence of its command and the spread of its fame?

There is no way to accept this either, for the verse indicates the perfection of the religion and the completion of the blessing, yet the religion was not perfected by the conquest of Mecca—which was in the eighth year of the Hijra—for many an obligation was revealed after that, and many a lawful or prohibited thing was legislated between then and the passing of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

Furthermore, His saying: الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (those who disbelieve) encompasses all the polytheists of the Arabs, and they were not all despairing of the religion of the Muslims then. Evidence for this is that many of the oppositions and treaties of non-interference were still considered valid and respected. They used to perform the Hajj of the pre-Islamic era according to the customs of the polytheists, and women used to perform Hajj naked, with their private parts exposed, until the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Ali (peace be upon him) with the verses of Immunity (البراءة), thereby invalidating the remnants of the pre-Islamic rituals.

Or is what is meant by "Today" the time after the revelation of Immunity (البراءة), when Islam had spread over almost the entire Arabian Peninsula, the traces of polytheism had vanished, and the customs of the pre-Islamic era had died out, such that the Muslims did not see any of the polytheists in the religious sites and the rituals of Hajj? The matter became pure for them, and Allah exchanged their fear for security, such that they worshipped Him and did not associate anything with Him.

There is no way to accept this either. Although the polytheists of the Arabs despaired of the religion of the Muslims after the revelation of the verses of Immunity and the folding of the carpet of polytheism from the Peninsula and the erasure of pre-Islamic rituals, the religion was not yet perfected. Obligations and laws were revealed after that, including what is in this Surah (Surah al-Ma'idah), and they have agreed upon its revelation at the end of the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله era, and it contains much regarding the laws of the lawful and prohibited, and legal punishments and retribution.

Thus, it is established that there is no way to entertain the possibility that "Today" in the verse carries its broad meaning that fits the content of the verse according to initial consideration, such as the time of the appearance of the Islamic call, or the time after the conquest of Mecca, or after the revelation of the verses of Immunity. There is no way but to say that what is meant by "Today" is the day of the revelation of the verse itself, which is the day of the revelation of the Surah—if His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا is parenthetical and linked in meaning to the verse surrounding it—or after the revelation of Surah al-Ma'idah at the end of the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله era, due to His saying, the Exalted: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ.

So, is what is meant by "Today" the day of the conquest of Mecca specifically? Or the day of the revelation of Immunity specifically? What has preceded of the objections to the second and third possibilities is sufficient to invalidate this.

Or is what is meant by "Today" the Day of 'Arafah during the Farewell Pilgrimage, as mentioned by many exegetes and reported in some narrations? If so, what is meant by the despair of the disbelievers regarding the religion of the Muslims on that day? If what is meant by despair regarding the religion is the despair of the polytheists of Quraysh from prevailing over the religion of the Muslims, that occurred on the day of the Conquest in the year 8, not the Day of 'Arafah in the year 10. If what is meant is the despair of the polytheists of the Arabs from that, it occurred at the revelation of Immunity, which was in the ninth year of the Hijra. If what is meant is the despair of all the disbelievers, encompassing the Jews, Christians, Magians, and others—which is what the absolute application of His saying: الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا necessitates—then these were not yet despairing of prevailing over the Muslims, for Islam had not yet manifested power, prestige, and dominance outside the Arabian Peninsula on that day.

From another perspective, we must reflect upon what this day—which is the Day of 'Arafah, the ninth of Dhu al-Hijjah in the tenth year of the Hijra—possesses of significance that fits His saying: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي in the verse.

It might be possible to say that what is meant by it is the perfection of the matter of Hajj by the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله presence in it himself, and his teaching the people a practical teaching accompanied by speech.

However, the objection to this is that his merely teaching the people their Hajj rituals—when he had commanded them to perform the Hajj of enjoyment (حج التمتع) and it soon became abandoned, and the legislation of the pillars of religion such as prayer, fasting, Hajj, zakat, jihad, and others had preceded it—cannot properly be called a perfection of the religion. How can teaching one of the obligations of the religion be called a perfection of that obligation, let alone be called a perfection of the entire religion?

Furthermore, this possibility necessitates the severance of the link between the first sentence—namely His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ—and this sentence—namely His saying: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ. What link is there between the despair of the disbelievers regarding the religion and the Messenger of Allah's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله teaching the Hajj of enjoyment to the people?

It might also be possible to say that what is meant by it is the perfection of the religion by the revelation of the remaining lawful and prohibited matters on this day in Surah al-Ma'idah, such that there is no lawful or prohibited thing after it; and by the perfection of the religion, despair took hold of the hearts of the disbelievers, and its traces appeared on their faces.

However, we must be discerning in identifying these disbelievers who were described in the verse by His saying: الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا according to this estimation—who are they? If the disbelievers of the Arabs are meant, Islam had encompassed them by then and there was none among them who manifested anything other than Islam, which was Islam in reality; so who are the despairing disbelievers?

If the disbelievers from other than them are meant, such as the rest of the nations and generations, you have already learned that they were not despairing on that day of prevailing over the Muslims.

Then we reflect upon the matter of the closure of the door of legislation with the revelation of Surah al-Ma'idah and the passing of the Day of 'Arafah. Many narrations have been reported—which cannot be ignored in number—regarding the revelation of laws and obligations after that day, as in the "Verse of Summer" (1) and the verses of usury (الربا), such that it was narrated from 'Umar that he said in a sermon he delivered: "Among the last of the Quran to be revealed is the verse of usury, and the Messenger of Allah died before he explained it to us, so leave what makes you doubt for what does not make you doubt," the hadith. Al-Bukhari narrated in the Sahih from Ibn 'Abbas, who said: "The last verse revealed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was the verse of usury," among other narrations.

The researcher does not have the right to weaken the narrations and prioritize the verse over them, because the verse is neither explicit nor apparent in that the "Today" meant in it is this specific day; rather, it is a possible view that depends for its determination upon the negation of every possibility that contradicts it, and these reports are no less than a possibility devoid of support.

Or it is said: What is meant by the perfection of the religion is the clearing of the Sacred House for them and the expulsion of the polytheists from it, such that the Muslims performed Hajj while the polytheists did not mix with them.

The objection to this is: The matter had already become pure for the Muslims in what was mentioned a year before that, so what is the meaning of restricting it to "Today" in His saying: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ? Furthermore, even if we grant that this clearing is a completion of the blessing, we do not grant that it is a perfection of the religion. What meaning is there in calling the clearing of the House a perfection of the religion? The religion is nothing but a collection of beliefs and laws, and its perfection is only that a number be added to the number of its parts and portions. As for the atmosphere being clear for their implementation and the removal of obstacles and interferences from acting upon them, that is not called a perfection of the religion at all. Moreover, the problem of the disbelievers' despair regarding the religion remains as it was.

It can be said: What is meant by the perfection of the religion is the detailed explanation of these prohibitions so that the Muslims may adopt them and avoid them, and not fear the disbelievers in that, because they have already despaired of their religion through Allah's strengthening of the Muslims, the manifestation of their religion, and their triumph over the disbelievers.

To clarify: the wisdom of being content in the early period of Islam with mentioning the four prohibitions—namely carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and what was offered to other than Allah—occurring in some Meccan Surahs, and leaving the detailed explanation of what is included in them of other things that Islam disliked for the Muslims among what is mentioned in this verse until after the conquest of Mecca, was only gradualness (التدرج) in prohibiting these impurities and being severe in them, just as there was gradualness in the prohibition of wine, so that the Arabs would not be repelled by Islam and would not see in it a hardship by which they might hope for the return of those who believed from among their poor—who were the majority of the foremost early believers.

This detailed explanation of the prohibitions came after the strength of Islam, Allah's expansion for its people, and their empowerment, and after the polytheists had despaired thereby of the people's repulsion from it, and their greed for prevailing over them and removing their religion by overwhelming force had vanished. Thus, the believers were more worthy of not caring for them through compromise, nor fearing them for their religion or themselves.

So, what is meant by "Today" is the Day of 'Arafah from the year of the Farewell Pilgrimage. It is the day in which this verse was revealed, clarifying what remained of the laws by which Islam invalidated the remnants of pre-Islamic humiliation, impurities, and illusions, and bringing glad tidings of the Muslims' complete triumph over the polytheists—a triumph in which they have no greed for its disappearance, and with which there is no need for any compromise with them or fear of the consequence of their matter.

Thus, Allah, the Glorified, informs them in the verse that the disbelievers themselves have despaired of the disappearance of their religion, and that it is appropriate for them—since He has exchanged their weakness for strength, their fear for security, and their poverty for wealth—not to fear other than Him, the Exalted, and to desist from the details of what Allah has forbidden in the verse, for in it is the perfection of their religion. This is what some have mentioned by summarizing what is in the transmission.

The objection to this is: This speaker intended to combine several of the mentioned possibilities to repel with each possibility the objection directed at the other possibility, but he became entangled in all the pitfalls and corrupted both the wording and the meaning of the verse.

He overlooked that the intent of "despair" (اليأس), if it is the despair based on the appearance and strength of Islam—which occurred with the conquest of Mecca or the revelation of the verses of Immunity—it would not be correct to say on the Day of 'Arafah in the tenth year: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ, for they had despaired a year or two before that. Rather, the wording sufficient for it would be to say: "they have already despaired," as the speaker himself expressed in his speech clarifying the meaning, or to say: "they are despairing."

He also overlooked that this gradualness he mentioned in the prohibitions of food, and his comparing their prohibition to the prohibition of wine—if what is meant by it is gradualness in terms of prohibiting some individuals after others—you have already learned that the verse does not include more than what the verses of prohibition revealed before this verse include, namely the verses of al-Baqarah, al-An'am, and al-Nahl, and that the strangled and the victim of a fatal blow (etc.) are among the individuals of what was mentioned therein.

(1) It is the verse of kalalah mentioned at the end of Surah al-Nisa'.

If the intention behind it was a gradual progression in terms of general and detailed clarification—out of fear that people might refuse to accept it—then it is misplaced. For what was mentioned explicitly in the surahs prior to al-Ma’idah—namely, carrion (الميتة), blood (الدم), the flesh of swine (لحم الخنزير), and that which has been dedicated to other than Allah (ما أهل لغير الله به)—is more prevalent in its instances, more frequent in its occurrence, and more impactful in the hearts of people than things like the strangled (المنخنقة), the beaten to death (الموقوذة), and others. These latter are rare occurrences and anomalous in existence. Why, then, would those four—which are more important, more impactful, and more frequent—be explicitly declared prohibited without any such fear, while caution is exercised regarding the mention of things that are insignificant in comparison, leading to a gradual progression in clarifying their prohibition and a fear of explicit declaration?

Furthermore, even if this were granted, it would not constitute the perfection of the religion (إكمال الدين). Is it correct to call the legislation of rulings "religion" (ديناً), and their delivery and clarification "the perfection of the religion"? Even if granted, it would only be the perfection of part of the religion and the completion of part of the blessing (النعمة), not the whole. Yet Allah, the Exalted, said: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي — "Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you" (5:3), expressing it absolutely without any restriction.

Moreover, He, the Exalted, has clarified many rulings on many days; why was this ruling on this specific day singled out for such distinction that Allah named it—or named its detailed clarification—the perfection of the religion and the completion of the blessing?

Or is the meaning of "perfecting the religion" its completion by closing the door of legislation after this verse which clarifies the details of prohibited foods? If so, what of the rulings that descended between the revelation of al-Ma’idah and the passing of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله? Indeed, what of the other rulings revealed after this verse within Surah al-Ma’idah itself? Reflect upon this.

After all this, what is the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلامَ دِيناً — "and I have approved Islam as your religion" (5:3)—the implied meaning being: "Today I have approved..."—if the intent of the speech was to show favor for what was mentioned in the verse regarding prohibitions on the Day of Arafah in the tenth year? What is the reason for singling out this day as the one in which Allah, the Glorified, approved Islam as the religion, when there is no matter specific to this day that suits such approval?

Beyond all this, most of the objections raised against the previous views, or similar ones already clarified, apply to this view as well; we shall not prolong the discussion by repeating them.

Or perhaps the "Day" refers to one of the days between Arafah and the Prophet’s صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله arrival in Medina, according to some of the views mentioned regarding the meaning of the disbelievers' despair and the meaning of perfecting the religion.

This view is subject to the same objections as the others, according to the detailed explanation provided earlier.

This concludes one part of the inquiry into the verse based on a survey of what has been said or could be said to justify its meaning. Let us now investigate it from another path that suits the specific method of inquiry of this book.

His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ — "Today the faithless have despaired of your religion. So do not fear them" (5:3). Despair (اليأس) is the opposite of hope (الرجاء), and the religion (الدين) was revealed from Allah gradually. This indicates that the disbelievers had a desire regarding the religion of the Muslims—which is Islam—and they had been hoping for its disappearance in some manner for a long time. Their agenda threatened Islam time and again, and the religion was in danger from them day after day, such that it was only right for the believers to be wary and fearful of it.

Thus, His saying: فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ — "So do not fear them" (5:3) is an assurance from Him, the Glorified, to the believers regarding that which they were in danger of, and a secret regarding their fear. Allah, the Exalted, said: وَدَّتْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَوْ يُضِلُّونَكُمْ — "A group of the People of the Book would love to lead you astray" (3:69). And He, the Exalted, said: وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِكُمْ كُفَّاراً حَسَداً مِنْ عِنْدِ أَنْفُسِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْحَقُّ فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللهُ بِأَمْرِهِ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — "Many of the People of the Book would love to turn you back into faithless ones after your having believed, out of an envy from their own souls, after the truth has become manifest to them. So excuse and forbear until Allah brings about His command. Indeed Allah has power over all things" (2:109).

The disbelievers were only lying in wait for the Muslims because of their religion. Their chests only tightened and their hearts only broke because the religion was stripping away their sovereignty, their honor, and their unrestrained indulgence in everything their natures desired, their souls were familiar with, and their habits were accustomed to—putting an end to their enjoyment of whatever they craved without restriction or condition.

Thus, the religion itself was the object of their hatred, not the people of the religion except by virtue of their true religion. Their intention was not the annihilation of the Muslims or the destruction of their collective, but rather to extinguish the light of Allah and to solidify the pillars of polytheism (الشرك) that were shaken and disturbed by it, and to return the believers to disbelief, as mentioned in His saying: لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِكُمْ كُفَّاراً — "to turn you back into faithless ones after your having believed" (2:109). Allah, the Exalted, said: يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِؤُا نُورَ اللهِ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ وَاللهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْكافِرُونَ هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى وَدِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَلَوْ كره المشركون — "They desire to extinguish the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah shall perfect His light though the faithless should be averse. It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse" (61:8-9).

And He, the Exalted, said: فَادْعُوا اللهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْكافِرُونَ — "So supplicate Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him, though the faithless should be averse" (40:14).

Therefore, they had no concern except to uproot this "goodly tree" from its source and to demolish this "lofty edifice" from its foundation by tempting the believers, spreading hypocrisy (النفاق) within their community, and sowing doubts and superstitions among them to corrupt their religion.

In the beginning, they attempted to weaken the resolve of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and diminish his determination in the religious call (الدعوة) through wealth and status, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَانْطَلَقَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْهُمْ أَنِ امْشُوا وَاصْبِرُوا عَلى آلِهَتِكُمْ إِنَّ هذا لَشَيْءٌ يُرادُ — "Their elite go about [saying], ‘Go and stand by your gods! This is indeed the preferred thing’" (38:6), or through compromise and flattery, as indicated by His saying: وَدُّوا لَوْ تُدْهِنُ فَيُدْهِنُونَ — "They wish that you should be soft [with them], so that they [too] would be soft [with you]" (68:9), and His saying: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً — "Had We not fortified you, you would have certainly leaned towards them a little" (17:74), and His saying: قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ لا أَعْبُدُ ما تَعْبُدُونَ وَلا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ — "Say, ‘O faithless ones! I do not worship what you worship, nor do you worship what I worship’" (109:1-3), according to what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول).

Their final hope for the disappearance of the religion and the death of the true call was that it would die with the death of the one established with its command, as he had no offspring. They viewed it as a kingdom in the form of prophecy, and a sovereignty in the garb of a call and messengership. If he were to die or be killed, his trace would be cut off, and the memory of him and his religion would die, as is usually witnessed in the state of kings and tyrants: however much they reach in terms of loftiness, tyranny, and subjugation of people, their memory dies with their death, and their traditions and laws that governed the people are buried with them in their graves. This hope of theirs is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ شانِئَكَ هُوَ الْأَبْتَرُ — "Indeed it is your enemy who is without posterity" (108:3), according to what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation.

These and similar things were wishes that allowed hope to take root in their souls, making them covet the extinguishing of the light of religion, and decorating their imaginations with the idea that this pure call was nothing but a passing tale that the fates would soon prove false, and that the passage of days and nights would bring to an end and erase its trace. However, the gradual emergence of Islam over every religion and people that opposed it, the spread of its fame, and the elevation of its word through power and strength, put an end to these wishes. Thus, they despaired of corrupting the resolve of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, halting his determination regarding what he intended, or tempting him with wealth or status.

The strength and power of Islam made them despair of all those means—the means of hope—except for one: that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was without posterity, having no son to succeed him in his affair and to stand for the religious call as he had. Thus, his religion would die with his death. For it is self-evident that the perfection of the religion (كمال الدين) in terms of its rulings and knowledge—no matter how far it reaches—cannot by itself preserve itself. A tradition among the newly established traditions or followed religions does not remain in its freshness and purity by itself, nor by the spread of its fame, nor by the multitude of its adherents. Similarly, it is not erased or obliterated by force, compulsion, threat, temptation, torment, or otherwise, except by the death of its carriers, its preservers, and those established with the management of its affairs.

From all that has preceded, it becomes clear that the complete despair of the disbelievers could only be realized, according to sound reasoning, when Allah appoints for this religion one who stands in the place of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in its preservation and the management of its affairs, and in guiding the nation established by it. Following that would be the despair of those who disbelieve in the religion of the Muslims, as they witness the religion exiting the stage of being established by a personal carrier to the stage of being established by a species-based carrier (الحامل النوعي). This would be the perfection of the religion (إكمال الدين) by transforming it from the quality of origination (الحدوث) to the quality of survival (البقاء), and the completion of this blessing (إتمام النعمة). It is not far-fetched that His saying, the Exalted: وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِكُمْ كُفَّاراً حَسَداً مِنْ عِنْدِ أَنْفُسِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْحَقُّ فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللهُ بِأَمْرِهِ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — "Many of the People of the Book would love to turn you back into faithless ones after your having believed... So excuse and forbear until Allah brings about His command" (2:109), by including the phrase "until He brings about" (حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ), is an allusion to this meaning.

This supports the narrations stating that the verse was revealed on the Day of Ghadir Khumm, which is the eighteenth of Dhu al-Hijjah in the tenth year of the Hijrah, regarding the matter of the mastership (ولاية) of Ali (peace be upon him). Based on this, the two segments are linked in the clearest of connections, and none of the aforementioned objections apply to it.

Furthermore, once you have understood the meaning of despair (اليأس) in the verse, you will know that "the Day" (اليوم) in His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ — "Today the faithless have despaired of your religion" (5:3) is an adverbial qualifier (ظرف) related to His saying "have despaired" (يَئِسَ). Its advancement (التقديم) is to indicate the magnitude of the day's affair and the greatness of its status, because of the religion's transition from the stage of being established by a personal sustainer to the stage of being established by a species-based sustainer (القيم النوعي), and from the quality of manifestation and origination to the quality of survival and permanence.

The verse should not be compared to His upcoming saying: الْيَوْمَ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّباتُ — "Today all the good things are made lawful for you" (5:5), for the context of the two verses is different. His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ — "Today [they] have despaired" (5:3) is in the context of a parenthetical statement (الاعتراض), while His saying: الْيَوْمَ أُحِلَّ — "Today [they] are made lawful" (5:5) is in the context of a new beginning (الاستيناف). The two rulings are also different: the ruling of the first verse is ontological (تكويني), containing glad tidings on one hand and a warning on the other, while the ruling of the second is legislative (تشريعي), informing of a favor. Thus, His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ indicates the greatness of the day's affair because it contains a benefit of immense utility, which is the despair of the disbelievers regarding the religion of the believers. The meaning of "those who disbelieve" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا)—as previously alluded to—is disbelievers in general, including idolaters, Jews, Christians, and others, due to the absolute nature of the expression.

As for His saying: فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَاخْشَوْنِ — "So do not fear them, but fear Me" (5:3), the prohibition is advisory (إرشادي), not mandatory (مولوي). Its meaning is that there is no cause for fear after the despair of those from whom you were in danger—and it is well known that a person does not pursue a matter after complete despair of attaining it, nor do they strive for what they know their striving in will be in vain. Thus, you are in safety from the side of the disbelievers, and it is not appropriate for you, despite that, to fear them for your religion; so do not fear them, but fear Me.

From here it becomes clear that the meaning of His saying: وَاخْشَوْنِ — "but fear Me" (5:3), according to the context, is: "Fear Me in that which you were supposed to fear them for, were it not for their despair, which is the religion and its being snatched from your hands." This is a type of threat to the Muslims, as is apparent, and for this reason, we did not interpret the verse as a mere expression of favor.

What we have mentioned is supported by the fact that fearing Allah, the Glorified, is obligatory in any case, without being tied to one situation over another or one condition over another. There would be no reason for the transition (الإضراب) from His saying: فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ — "So do not fear them" (5:3) to His saying: وَاخْشَوْنِ — "but fear Me" (5:3) were it not a specific fear in a specific case.

The verse should not be compared to His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تَخافُوهُمْ وَخافُونِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "So do not fear them, but fear Me, should you be faithful" (3:175), because the command to fear Allah in that verse is conditioned upon faith, and the address is mandatory (مولوي). Its import is that it is not permissible for the believers to fear the disbelievers for their own lives; rather, they must fear Allah, the Glorified, alone.

Thus, that verse forbids them from what they have no right to do, which is fearing them for their own lives, whether they were commanded to fear Allah or not. For this reason, the command to fear Allah is subsequently justified by a clause suggesting causality, which is His saying: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "should you be faithful" (3:175). This is in contrast to His saying: فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَاخْشَوْنِ — "So do not fear them, but fear Me" (5:3), for this fear of theirs was a fear of them for their religion, and it is not hated by Allah, the Glorified, because it actually returns to seeking His pleasure. Rather, the prohibition of it is only because the cause inviting it—which was the disbelievers' lack of despair regarding it—has been removed and its effect has fallen. Thus, the prohibition of it is advisory (إرشادي), and likewise the command to fear Allah Himself. The import of the speech is that it is obligatory to fear for the matter of religion, but the cause of fear until today was with the disbelievers, so you used to fear them because of their hope in your religion; today they have despaired, and the cause has transferred to what is with Allah, so fear Him alone. Understand that.

The verse, due to His saying: فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَاخْشَوْنِ, is not devoid of threat and warning, because it contains a command for a specific fear rather than the general fear that is obligatory for the believer in every case and in all circumstances. Let us then look into the specificity of this fear, and what is the cause necessitating its obligation and the command for it?

There is no doubt that the two segments—namely His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ — "Today [they] have despaired" (5:3) and His saying: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي — "Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you" (5:3)—are linked and directed toward a single purpose, as previously explained. The religion that Allah perfected today, and the blessing He completed today—which are one thing in reality—is that which the disbelievers coveted and the believers feared them for. So Allah made them despair of it, perfected it, completed it, and forbade them from fearing them for it. Thus, that which He commanded them to fear Him for is that very thing: that Allah might snatch the religion from their hands and strip them of this gifted blessing.

Allah, the Glorified, has clarified that there is no cause for the stripping of a blessing except disbelief (الكفر) in it, and He threatened the ungrateful with the severest threat. Allah, the Exalted, said: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً نِعْمَةً أَنْعَمَها عَلى قَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ وَأَنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ — "That is because Allah would never change a blessing He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls, and Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing" (8:53). And He, the Exalted, said: وَمَنْ يُبَدِّلْ نِعْمَةَ اللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُ فَإِنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ — "And whoever changes Allah’s blessing after it has come to him, then Allah is indeed severe in retribution" (2:211). And He struck a universal parable for His blessings and what the matter of disbelief in them leads to, saying: وَضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً قَرْيَةً كانَتْ آمِنَةً مُطْمَئِنَّةً يَأْتِيها رِزْقُها رَغَداً مِنْ كُلِّ مَكانٍ فَكَفَرَتْ بِأَنْعُمِ اللهِ فَأَذاقَهَا اللهُ لِباسَ الْجُوعِ وَالْخَوْفِ بِما كانُوا يَصْنَعُونَ — "Allah draws a parable: A town that was secure and at rest, its provision coming to it abundantly from every quarter, but it was ungrateful for Allah’s blessings. So Allah made it taste the garment of hunger and fear because of what they used to do" (16:112).

Thus, the verse—namely His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ until His saying: دِيناً—announces that the religion of the Muslims is in safety from the side of the disbelievers, protected from the danger directed from them, and that no corruption or destruction can seep into it except from the Muslims themselves. This would only be through their disbelief in this complete blessing and their rejection of this perfect, approved religion.
