It is a metonym for "sexual intercourse" (الجماع) out of etiquette, to protect the tongue from explicit mention of what natures refuse to mention explicitly.

If you say: "If it were so, then expressing it as He expressed it previously with His saying: وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ جُنُبًا would have been more appropriate, as it is more eloquent in observing etiquette."

I say: Yes, but a point observed in the speech would have been lost, which is the indication that the matter is something necessitated by nature, as previously explained. The expression "ritual impurity" (الجنابة) lacks the intimation of this point.

The corruption of what was attributed to some—that the intent of "touching women" is literal touching in an explicit sense without it being a metonym for intercourse—has also appeared. The reason for its corruption is that the context of the verse does not suit it; it only suits the metonym. For Allah, the Glorified, began His speech by explaining the ruling of the minor ritual state through ablution and the ruling of ritual impurity through the major bath in the normal state—which is the state of finding water. Then the speech moved to explaining the ruling in the abnormal state—which is the state of lacking water—so He explained in it the state of the "substitute for ablution" (بدل الوضوء), which is dry ablution. It was more appropriate and natural to mention the state of the "substitute for the major bath" (بدل الغسل) as well, as it is the counterpart of ablution. He mentioned what can be applied to it, which is His saying: أَوْ لَامَسْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ by way of metonymy. Thus, the intent is that, undoubtedly. There is no reason to restrict the speech to explaining the ruling of the substitute for ablution—one of the two counterparts—and completely neglecting the ruling of the substitute for the other counterpart, which is the major bath.

Fifth: From what preceded, the corruption of the objections raised against the verse appears. Among them is that the mention of sickness and travel is "redundant" (مستدرك), for they only necessitate dry ablution by the joining of one of the last two divisions—the ritual state and touching—while those two necessitate it even if there were no sickness or travel. Thus, mentioning the latter two suffices for mentioning the first two. The answer is that the mention of the last two divisions is not for the purpose of their joining one of the first two; rather, each of the four is an independent division mentioned for a purpose specific to it that would be lost by its removal from the speech, as previously explained.

Among them: that the second division, which is His saying: أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ, is redundant. This is by the same reasoning as the previous objection, except that since the excuse of sickness that necessitates the transition to the substitute is the inability to use existing water, not the lack of finding water, it was necessary to assume that in the speech; mentioning the last two divisions with the lack of finding water does not suffice for it. The result of this view is that travel alone is redundant. The answer is that "not finding" in the verse is a metonym for the "inability to use water," encompassing the state of its presence or absence, as previously mentioned.

Among them: that His saying: فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا مَاءً suffices for mentioning all the divisions. If "and if you do not find water" were said in place of His saying: وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ مَرْضَىٰ (etc.), it would be more concise and clearer. The answer: that in it is the loss of the points previously mentioned.

Among them: that if "and if you are unable to [use] water" or what gives its meaning were said, it would have been better, for its inclusion of the excuse of sickness in addition to the excuse of others. The answer: that it was conveyed through metonymy, which is more eloquent.

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَيَمَّمُوا صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا فَامْسَحُوا بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ مِنْهُ — "...then make dry ablution (التيمم) with clean ground and wipe your faces and your hands with it." Dry ablution (التيمم) is "intending" (القصد). Ground (الصعيد) is the surface of the earth. Its description as clean (طيب) — and cleanness (الطيب) in a thing is its being in a state necessitated by its nature — is to intimate the condition of its being in its original state, like ordinary soil and stones, excluding what has left the state of "earthiness" through cooking, ripening, or other factors of change, such as gypsum, lime, porcelain, and mineral materials. The Exalted said: وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَبَاتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ ۖ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لَا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِدًا — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the permission of its Lord, and as for that which is bad, it does not yield except scantily" (7:58). From this are derived the conditions taken by the Sunnah regarding the ground with which dry ablution is performed.

It is sometimes said: the intent of "clean" (الطيب) is "purity" (الطهارة), thus indicating the requirement of purity in the ground.

His saying: فَامْسَحُوا بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ مِنْهُ — the application of what He mentioned in dry ablution for wiping matches what He mentioned in ablution for washing. Thus, dry ablution is in reality an ablution in which the two wipings—the wiping of the head and the wiping of the feet—have been dropped, and the two washings—the washing of the face and the hands to the elbows—have been replaced by two wipings, and water has been replaced by dust as a "lightening" (تخفيف).

This intimates that the two limbs in dry ablution are the same two limbs in ablution. Since the Exalted expressed it with "wiping" made transitive by the preposition "with" (بـ), it indicated that what is considered in dry ablution is the wiping of part of the two limbs of washing in ablution—meaning part of the face, and part of the hand to the elbow. This matches what has been reported from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) regarding the delimitation of the wiped part of the face as being between the two sides of the forehead, and the wiped part of the hand as being from the wrist down.

By this, the corruption of what some mentioned regarding the delimitation of the hand as being from the armpits down appears. And what others mentioned—that what is considered of the hand in dry ablution is the exact same as what was considered in ablution, which is from the elbow down—is also corrupt, for it does not suit the wiping made transitive by "with" (بـ) which indicates the passing of the wiper over part of the wiped object.

The "from" (من) in His saying: مِنْهُ is as if it were "initial" (ابتدائية), meaning that the wiping of the face and hands should be "started from" the ground. The Sunnah has explained it as being by striking the hands on the ground and then wiping the face and hands with them.

It appears from some that "from" (من) here is "partitive" (تبعيضية), thus implying that there should be a remnant of the ground, such as dust and the like, on the hands after striking, for wiping the face and hands. He concluded from this the obligation of the ground struck upon containing something of dust to be wiped from onto the face and hands; thus, dry ablution on a smooth stone to which no dust has attached would not be valid. The apparent meaning is what we presented first—and Allah knows best—and the ruling he concluded is not exclusive to what he assumed.

His saying, the Exalted: مَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ حَرَجٍ وَلَٰكِنْ يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ — "Allah does not desire to put you to any hardship (حرج), but He desires to purify you..." The entry of "any" (مِنْ) upon the object of "He desires" is to emphasize the negation. Thus, there is no ruling intended for hardship among the religious rulings at all. For this reason, the negation was attached to the "intention of making" rather than to the hardship itself.

Hardship (الحرج) is of two types: a hardship that affects the "basis of the ruling" (ملاك الحكم) and its sought benefit, in which case the ruling is issued as "hardship-inducing in its essence" due to following its basis—as if the enjoyment of food were prohibited for the purpose of attaining the trait of asceticism; the ruling is then hardship-inducing from the start. And a hardship that affects the ruling "incidentally" (بعرض) from outside due to accidental causes; some of its instances then become hardship-inducing, and the ruling falls away in those hardship-inducing instances, but not in others where there is no hardship—like one who finds hardship in standing during prayer due to a sickness that harms him; the obligation of standing then falls away from him, but not from others who are capable of it.

His "turning away" (إضراب) with His saying: وَلَٰكِنْ يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ from His saying: مَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ حَرَجٍ indicates that the intent of the verse is the negation of the hardship that is in the basis—meaning that the rulings He makes for you are not hardship-inducing ones legislated for the purpose of hardship. This is because the meaning of the speech is: "Our intention by these made rulings is your purification and the completion of the blessing—which is the basis—not to make things difficult for you or put you to hardship." For this reason, when We found ablution and the major bath to be hardship-inducing for you upon the lack of water, We transitioned from the obligation of ablution and the major bath to the obligation of dry ablution, which is within your capacity. The ruling of purification did not being nullified from the start, due to the desire to purify you and complete the blessing upon you, so that you may be grateful.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَٰكِنْ يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ — "...but He desires to purify you and to complete His blessing upon you so that you may be grateful." The necessary consequence of the preceding meaning of the negation of the intention of hardship is that the intent of His saying: يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ is that the legislation of ablution, the major bath, and dry ablution is only for the attainment of "purity" (الطهارة) in you, as they are the causes for that. This purity, whatever it may be, is not a purity from "physical filth" (الخبث); rather, it is a "spiritual purity" (طهارة معنوية) attained by one of these three acts, and it is that which prayer is conditioned upon in reality.

It is possible to derive from this the lack of obligation to perform the act of purification when standing for every prayer if the prayer-performer is in a state of purity that has not been invalidated. This does not contradict the apparent "absolute application" (الإطلاق) of the beginning of the verse, because legislation is broader than what is by way of obligation.

As for His saying: وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ — "and to complete His blessing upon you," the meaning of "blessing" (النعمة) and its "completion" (إتمامها) has already passed in the discussion on His saying, the Exalted: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي — "Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you" (5:3). And the meaning of "gratitude" (الشكر) was discussed under His saying, the Exalted: وَسَيَجْزِي اللَّهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ — "and Allah will soon reward the grateful" (3:144) in the fourth volume of the book.

The intent of "blessing" in the verse is the "religion" (الدين), not from the perspective of its parts of knowledge and rulings, but from the perspective of its being the "submission of the face to Allah" (إسلام الوجه لله) in all affairs. This is the "guardianship of Allah" (ولاية الله) over the servants through what He judges among them. This is only completed by the legislation encompassing all religious rulings, among which is the ruling of the three purifications.

From here it appears that between the two goals—meaning His saying: لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ and His saying: لِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ—there is a difference. It is that "purity" is a goal for the legislation of the three purifications, unlike the "completion of the blessing," for it is a goal for the legislation of all rulings, and the three purifications have only their share of it. Thus, the two goals are "specific" (خاصة) and "general" (عامة).

Accordingly, the meaning is: "But We desire by making the three purifications the attainment of purity by them specifically for you, and because they are part of the religion which is completed by the legislation of all of them as a blessing from Allah upon you, so that you may thank Allah for His blessing and He may then devote you to Himself." Understand that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَمِيثَاقَهُ الَّذِي وَاثَقَكُمْ بِهِ إِذْ قُلْتُمْ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا — "Remember Allah’s blessing upon you and His covenant with which He bound you when you said, ‘We hear and we obey.’" This is the covenant that was taken from them upon Islam, as evidenced by His reminding them of it with His saying: إِذْ قُلْتُمْ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا. For it is "absolute hearing" and "absolute obedience," which is submission (الإسلام) to Allah. What is meant by "blessing" in His saying: وَاذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ are the beautiful gifts that Allah, the Glorified, bestowed upon them in the radiance of Islam. This is the "disparity" (التفاضل) between their state in their "ignorance" (جاهليتهم) and their state in their Islam—of security, well-being, wealth, clarity of hearts, and purity of deeds, as He, the Exalted, said: وَاذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ كُنْتُمْ أَعْدَاءً فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُمْ بِنِعْمَتِهِ إِخْوَانًا وَكُنْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَفَا حُفْرَةٍ مِنَ النَّارِ فَأَنْقَذَكُمْ مِنْهَا — "And remember Allah’s blessing upon you when you were enemies, then He brought your hearts together, so you became brothers by His blessing. And you were on the brink of a pit of Fire, whereat He saved you from it" (3:103).

Or, Islam in its "reality" (حقيقته) is what is meant by "blessing," for it is the "mother of blessings" from which every blessing is nursed, as previously explained. It is not hidden from you that the intent of the blessing being Islam in its reality or "guardianship" (الولاية) is only the determination of the "referent" (المصداق) rather than the identification of the "concept of the word" (مفهوم اللفظ). For the concept is what the language identifies, and we have no argument regarding it.

Then He reminded them of Himself and that He is Knower of the secrets of the corners of the hearts, so He commanded them to "godwariness" (التقوى) with His saying: وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِذَاتِ الصُّدُورِ — "and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah knows best what is in the breasts."

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Tahdhib, narrated with a chain to Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِذَا قُمْتُمْ إِلَى الصَّلَاةِ — He said: "When you rise from sleep." The narrator—who is Ibn Bukayr—said: I asked, "Does sleep invalidate ablution?" He replied: "Yes, when it overcomes the hearing such that one does not hear a sound."

I say: This meaning is reported in other narrations. Al-Suyuti reported it in al-Durr al-Manthur from Zayd ibn Aslam and al-Nahhas. This does not contradict what we presented—that the intent of "standing for prayer" is the intention for it—because what we mentioned is the meaning of "standing" (القيام) from the perspective of its being transitive by "to" (إلى), while what is in the narration is its meaning from the perspective of its being transitive by "from" (من) [implied].

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Zurarah, who said: I said to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), "From where did you know and say that wiping is of part of the head and part of the feet?" He laughed and then said: "O Zurarah, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said it, and the Book from Allah descended with it. For Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: فَاغْسِلُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ (then wash your faces); so we knew that the face in its entirety should be washed. Then He said: وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ إِلَى الْمَرَافِقِ (and your hands up to the elbows); so He connected the hands to the elbows with the face, and we knew that they should be washed up to the elbows. Then He separated the speech and said: وَامْسَحُوا بِرُؤُوسِكُمْ (and wipe your heads); so we knew when He said بِرُؤُوسِكُمْ that the wiping is of part of the head because of the position of the [preposition] ba. Then He connected the feet with the head just as He connected the hands with the face, saying: وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ إِلَى الْكَعْبَيْنِ (and your feet up to the ankles); so we knew when He connected them with the head that the wiping is upon part of them. Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) explained that to the people, but they wasted it. Then He said: فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا مَاءً فَتَيَمَّمُوا صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا فَامْسَحُوا بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ مِنْهُ (and you cannot find water, then make dry ablution with clean ground and wipe your faces and your hands with it). So when He set aside ablution if they do not find water, He established part of the washing as a wiping—because He said: بِوُجُوهِكُمْ (your faces [with the ba]) and then connected to it وَأَيْدِيكُمْ (and your hands). Then He said: مِنْهُ (with it), meaning from that dry ablution, because He knew that all of that [the dust] would not run over the face—for some of that ground attaches to part of the palm and does not attach to another part. Then Allah said: مَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ حَرَجٍ (Allah does not desire to put you to any hardship); and hardship (الحرج) is constriction (الضيق)."

I say: His saying: "Then He said: if you do not find water," is a transmission of the verse by meaning.

In it [al-Kafi], with its chain to Zurarah and Bukayr: that they asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the ablution of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). He called for a basin—or a vessel—containing water. He dipped his right hand, scooped a handful, poured it over his face, and washed his face with it. Then he dipped his left hand, scooped a handful, poured it over his right arm, and washed his arm with it from the elbows to the palm—he did not return it to the elbows. Then he dipped his right palm, poured it over his left arm from the elbow, and did with it what he had done with the right. Then he wiped his head and his feet with the moisture of his palm, not creating new water for them. Then he said: "And he does not enter his fingers under the [sandal] strap." Then he said: "Indeed Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: إِذَا قُمْتُمْ إِلَى الصَّلَاةِ فَاغْسِلُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ; so he has no right to leave anything of his face without washing it, and He commanded that the hands be washed to the elbows, so he has no right to leave anything of his hands to the elbows without washing it—because Allah says: فَاغْسِلُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ إِلَى الْمَرَافِقِ. Then He said: وَامْسَحُوا بِرُؤُوسِكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ إِلَى الْكَعْبَيْنِ; so if he wipes with something of his head—or with something of his feet between the ankles to the fingertips—it has sufficed him." He [the narrator] said: We asked, "Where are the ankles (الكعبان)?" He said: "Here," meaning the joint below the leg bone. We asked, "What is this?" He said: "This is from the leg bone, and the ankle is lower than that." We said, "May Allah set you right, does one handful suffice for the face and a handful for the arm?" He said: "Yes, if you are thorough with it, and two will cover all of that."

I say: The narration is among the famous ones. Al-Ayyashi reported it from Bukayr and Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), and from Abdullah ibn Sulayman from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) similarly. Regarding its meaning and the meaning of the previous narration, there are other narrations.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, al-Ayyashi from Zurarah ibn A'yan, and Abu Hanifah from Abu Bakr ibn Hazm, who said: A man performed ablution and wiped over his leather socks (الخفين), then entered the mosque and prayed. Ali (peace be upon him) came, stepped on his neck, and said: "Woe to you! Do you pray without ablution?" He replied: "Umar ibn al-Khattab commanded me." He [the narrator] said: So he [Ali] took him by the hand and ended up with him at his [Umar's] place, and said: "Look at what this one narrates about you," and he raised his voice. He [Umar] said: "Yes, I commanded him, for the Messenger of Allah wiped." He [Ali] said: "Before [Surah] al-Ma'idah or after it?" He said: "I do not know." He [Ali] said: "Then why do you issue a legal verdict when you do not know? The Book has preceded the leather socks."

I say: During the time of Umar, the disagreement regarding wiping over leather socks became widespread, as did the statement of Ali (peace be upon him) that it was abrogated by the verse of al-Ma'idah, as appears from the narrations. For this reason, it was reported from some of them like al-Bara, Bilal, and Jarir ibn Abdullah that they narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wiping over leather socks after the revelation of al-Ma'idah. This is not devoid of [suspicion]; it is as if he thought that the abrogation was only claimed by a matter not based on the verse, but it is not so. For the verse only establishes wiping over the feet to the ankles, and the leather sock is not a foot at all. This is the meaning of the following narration.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Khorasani—raising the tradition—who said: A man came to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) and asked him about wiping over leather socks. He looked down at the ground for a long while, then raised his head and said: "Indeed Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, commanded His servants with purification and divided it among the limbs. He made for the face a portion of it, for the head a portion of it, for the feet a portion of it, and for the hands a portion of it. So if your leather socks are among these parts, then wipe over them."

In it also, from al-Hasan ibn Zayd from Ja'far ibn Muhammad: that Ali opposed the people regarding wiping over leather socks during the time of Umar ibn al-Khattab. They said: "We saw the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wiping over leather socks." He [the narrator] said: Ali (peace be upon him) said: "Before the revelation of al-Ma'idah or after it?" They said: "We do not know." He said: "But I know that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) left wiping over leather socks when al-Ma'idah was revealed. And for me to wipe over the back of a donkey is more beloved to me than to wipe over leather socks," and he recited this verse: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — to His saying — الْمَرَافِقِ وَامْسَحُوا بِرُؤُوسِكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ إِلَى الْكَعْبَيْنِ.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and al-Nahhas in his Nasikh reported from Ali: that he used to perform ablution for every prayer and recite: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا قُمْتُمْ إِلَى الصَّلَاةِ (the verse).

I say: Its clarification has already passed.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to al-Halabi from Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: أَوْ لَامَسْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ. He said: "It is sexual intercourse (الجماع), but Allah is Screen-covering (ستير) and loves screening, so He did not name it as you name it."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah, who said: I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about dry ablution. He said: "Ammar ibn Yasir came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'I became ritually impure and I have no water.' He said: 'How did you do, O Ammar?' He said: 'I took off my clothes and then rolled in the ground.' He said: 'Thus does the donkey do! Allah only said: فَامْسَحُوا بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ مِنْهُ.' Then he [the Prophet] placed both his hands on the ground, then wiped them, then wiped from between his eyes to below his eyebrows, then rubbed one of his hands with the other on the back of the palm, starting with the right."

In it, from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah obligated washing on the face and the arms, and wiping on the head and the feet. Then when the state of travel, sickness, and necessity came, Allah set aside the washing and established the washing as a wiping, saying: وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ مَرْضَىٰ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ أَوْ جَاءَ أَحَدٌ مِنْكُمْ مِنَ الْغَائِطِ أَوْ لَامَسْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ — to His saying — وَأَيْدِيكُمْ مِنْهُ."

In it, from Abd al-A'la, the client of the family of Sam, who said: I said to Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him), "I stumbled and my nail was torn off, so I placed a bandage (مرارة) on my finger; how should I do with ablution?" He [the narrator] said: He (peace be upon him) said: "This and its like are known from the Book of Allah, the Blessed and Exalted: مَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ — 'He has not placed upon you any hardship in religion.'"

I say: An intimation toward the verse of Surah al-Hajj negating hardship. In his turning away from the end of the verse of ablution to what is at the end of Surah al-Hajj is an indication of what we presented regarding the meaning of the negation of hardship. In the reports we have transmitted are many points that become clear through what we presented in the explanation of the verses; so let them be received as an explanation for the narrations.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُونُوا۟ قَوَّٰمِينَ لِلَّهِ شُهَدَآءَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَـَٔانُ قَوْمٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا۟ ۚ ٱعْدِلُوا۟ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٨)
O you who have faith! Be maintainers, as witnesses for the sake of Allah, of justice, and ill feeling for a people should never lead you to be unfair. Be fair; that is nearer to Godwariness, and be wary of Allah. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (8)
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ۙ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (٩)
Allah has promised those who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward. (9)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٠)
As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell. (10)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ هَمَّ قَوْمٌ أَن يَبْسُطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فَكَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنكُمْ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١١)
O you who have faith! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when a people set out to extend their hands against you, but He withheld their hands from you, and be wary of Allah, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (11)
۞ وَلَقَدْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَبَعَثْنَا مِنْهُمُ ٱثْنَىْ عَشَرَ نَقِيبًا ۖ وَقَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مَعَكُمْ ۖ لَئِنْ أَقَمْتُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَيْتُمُ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَءَامَنتُم بِرُسُلِى وَعَزَّرْتُمُوهُمْ وَأَقْرَضْتُمُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا لَّأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَلَأُدْخِلَنَّكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ فَمَن كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (١٢)
Certainly Allah took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We raised among them twelve chiefs. And Allah said, ‘I am with you! Surely, if you maintain the prayer and give the zakat and have faith in My apostles and support them and lend Allah a good loan, I will surely absolve you of your misdeeds, and I will surely admit you into gardens with streams running in them. But whoever of you disbelieves after that has certainly strayed from the right way.’ (12)
فَبِمَا نَقْضِهِم مِّيثَـٰقَهُمْ لَعَنَّـٰهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَا قُلُوبَهُمْ قَـٰسِيَةً ۖ يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِۦ ۙ وَنَسُوا۟ حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ وَلَا تَزَالُ تَطَّلِعُ عَلَىٰ خَآئِنَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنْهُمْ ۖ فَٱعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَٱصْفَحْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٣)
Then, because of their breaking their covenant We cursed them and made their hearts hard: they pervert words from their meanings, and have forgotten a part of what they were reminded. You will not cease to learn of some of their treachery, excepting a few of them. Yet excuse them and forbear. Indeed Allah loves the virtuous. (13)
وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا نَصَـٰرَىٰٓ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَهُمْ فَنَسُوا۟ حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَأَغْرَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ ٱلْعَدَاوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ وَسَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ (١٤)
Also from those who say, ‘We are Christians,’ We took their pledge; but they forgot a part of what they were reminded. So We stirred up enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection, and soon Allah will inform them concerning what they had been doing. (14)

(Commentary)

The connection between these verses is clear and beyond doubt; they constitute a series of addresses to the believers regarding the universal matters of their concern in both the Hereafter and this world, whether as individuals or as a community.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا قَوَّامِينَ لِلَّهِ شُهَداءَ بِالْقِسْطِ وَلا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَآنُ قَوْمٍ عَلى أَلَّا تعدلوا "O you who have faith! Be maintainers, as witnesses for the sake of Allah, with justice, and ill feeling for a people should never lead you to be unfair" (5:8). This verse is similar to the verse in Surah al-Nisa’: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا قَوَّامِينَ بِالْقِسْطِ شُهَداءَ لِلَّهِ وَلَوْ عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوِ الْوالِدَيْنِ وَالْأَقْرَبِينَ إِنْ يَكُنْ غَنِيًّا أَوْ فَقِيراً فَاللهُ أَوْلى بِهِما فَلا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوى أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا وَإِنْ تَلْوُوا أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً "O you who have faith! Be maintainers of justice and witnesses for the sake of Allah, even if it should be against yourselves or [your] parents and near relatives, and whether it be [someone] rich or poor, for Allah has a greater right over them both. So do not follow [your] desires, lest you should be unfair, and if you distort [your testimony] or disregard [it], Allah is indeed well aware of what you do" (4:135).

The difference between the two verses is that the verse in al-Nisa’ is in the context of prohibiting deviation from justice in testimony due to following personal desire (اتباع الهوى)—where the witness favors the one for whom he testifies due to kinship or the like, thus testifying in a way that benefits them contrary to the truth. This verse—meaning the verse in al-Ma’idah—is in the context of deterring deviation from justice in testimony due to hatred (شنآن) and animosity from the witness toward the one against whom he testifies; thus, he gives testimony against him seeking a kind of revenge and the nullification of his right.

This difference in the objective of the discourse is what necessitated the variation of the qualifiers in the two verses. In the verse of al-Nisa’, He said: "Be maintainers of justice and witnesses for the sake of Allah," while in the verse of al-Ma’idah, He said: "Be maintainers, as witnesses for the sake of Allah, with justice."

This is because the objective in the verse of al-Ma’idah, being the deterrence from injustice in testimony due to prior enmity from the witness toward the one testified against, necessitated qualifying the testimony with justice (القسط). Thus, He commanded justice in testimony and that it should not involve injustice even against an enemy. This is in contrast to testifying for someone unjustly due to prior love and desire; such an act is not typically termed "injustice in testimony" or "deviation from justice" in common parlance, even if in reality it is not free from wrongdoing and bias. Therefore, in the verse of al-Ma’idah, He commanded testimony with justice (بالقسط) and made it a branch of the command to stand for Allah (القيام لله). In the verse of al-Nisa’, He commanded testimony for Allah (لله)—meaning that desire should not be followed therein—and made it a branch of the command to stand for justice (القيام بالقسط).

For this reason also, in the verse of al-Ma’idah, He branched from the command of testimony with justice His saying: اعْدِلُوا هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوى وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ "Be just; that is nearer to God-consciousness. And be wary of Allah" (5:8). Thus, He called to justice and considered it a means to attaining God-consciousness (التقوى) — the inner state of godwariness that protects one from His displeasure. He reversed the order in the verse of al-Nisa’, branching from the command of testimony for Allah His saying: فَلا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوى أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا "So do not follow [your] desires, lest you should be unfair" (4:135). Thus, He prohibited following desire and abandoning God-consciousness, considering it an evil means leading to the abandonment of justice.

Then, in both verses, He warned against the abandonment of God-consciousness with a single warning. In the verse of al-Nisa’, He said: "and if you distort [your testimony] or disregard [it], Allah is indeed well aware of what you do"—meaning if you are not God-conscious. In the verse of al-Ma’idah, He said: "And be wary of Allah; indeed Allah is well aware of what you do." As for the meaning of "maintainers for Allah, witnesses with justice," it has already been clarified in the discussion of the preceding verses.

His saying, the Exalted: اعْدِلُوا هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوى "Be just; that is nearer to God-consciousness" (5:8). The pronoun refers back to justice (العدل) as indicated by His saying: "Be just." The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ "Allah has promised those who have faith and do righteous deeds that for them there will be forgiveness and a great reward" (5:9). The second sentence—meaning His saying: "for them there will be forgiveness"—is the performative instantiation (إنشاء) of the promise that He reported with His saying: "Allah has promised."

This is, as has been said, more emphatic in expression than His saying: وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً "Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward" (48:29). This is not, as some have claimed, because it is a report after a report—for that is an error—but rather because it is an explicit performative instantiation of the promise without it being merely implied, as is the case in the verse of Surah al-Fath.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ "As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell" (5:10). Al-Raghib said: "The term al-jahmah (الجحمة) refers to the intensity of the fire's blazing, and from it comes al-jahim (الجحيم)." The verse contains the threat itself and stands in contrast to His saying in the previous verse: "for them there will be forgiveness and a great reward."

The qualification of disbelief (الكفر) with the denial of the signs (تكذيب الآيات) is to exclude that disbelief which is not accompanied by the denial of the indicative signs, and which does not reach the level of rejecting the truth while knowing it to be the truth—as in the case of the oppressed (الاستضعاف). In such a case, their matter is with Allah; if He wills, He forgives them, and if He wills, He punishes them. Thus, these two verses are a beautiful promise for those who believe and perform righteous deeds, and a severe threat for those who disbelieve and deny the signs of Allah. Between these two stages are intermediate stages and intervening stations whose matter and ultimate end Allah, the Glorified, has left ambiguous.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ هَمَّ قَوْمٌ أَنْ يَبْسُطُوا "O you who have faith! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when a people intended to stretch out..." (5:11). This content is applicable to multiple and diverse events that occurred between the disbelievers and the Muslims, such as the battles of Badr, Uhud, al-Ahzab, and others. Thus, it appears that what is intended is the general intent of the polytheists to kill the believers and erase the traces of Islam and the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts.

What some commentators have mentioned—that it refers to the intent of some polytheists to kill the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or the intent of some Jews to assassinate him (the stories of which will follow)—is far from the apparent meaning of the wording, as is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ "And be wary of Allah, and in Allah let the faithful put their trust" (5:11). This is a command for God-consciousness and trust (التوكل) in Allah. In reality, it is a prohibition and a severe warning against abandoning God-consciousness and abandoning trust in Allah, the Glorified. The evidence for this is the narrative He recounted regarding the taking of the covenant (الميثاق) from the Children of Israel and from those who said, "We are Christians," followed by both groups breaking the divine covenant and Allah afflicting them with a curse, the hardening of hearts, and the forgetting of a portion of their religion, and the instigation of enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection.

He only mentioned the story to use it as a witness against the believers and to place it before their eyes so they might take heed and realize that the Jews and Christians were only afflicted with what they were afflicted with because of their forgetting the covenant of Allah, the Glorified. It was nothing other than a covenant of submission (الإسلام) to Allah, which they ratified with hearing and obedience. The necessary consequence of that was that they should be wary of opposing their Lord and that they should trust in Him in the matters of their religion—meaning they should take Him as a guardian (وكيل) therein, choosing what He chooses for them and abandoning what He dislikes for them. The path to this is the obedience of His messengers by believing in them and abandoning the following of anyone other than Allah and His messengers, such as those who call to themselves and demand submission to their command, like tyrants, transgressors, and others, even the rabbis and monks. For there is no obedience except to Allah or to those whose obedience He has commanded.

However, they cast it behind their backs, so they were distanced from the mercy of Allah and they distorted the words from their contexts, interpreting them in ways other than what was intended. This necessitated that they forgot a portion of the religion, which was a share and a portion whose departure took with it all goodness and felicity. This corrupted what remained in their hands of the religion, for religion is a composite of knowledge and laws, some of which are connected to others; the corruption of some leads to the corruption of the rest, especially the pillars and foundations (الأصول). This is like one who prays but not for the sake of Allah, or spends but not for the pleasure of Allah, or fights but not to exalt the word of truth.

Thus, what remained in their hands did not benefit them, as it was distorted and corrupt, nor were they able to do without what they had forgotten of the religion; for there is no self-sufficiency from religion, especially its foundations and pillars.

From this, it is known that the context requires warning the believers against opposing God-consciousness and abandoning trust in Allah by mentioning this story and calling them to take heed from it.

From this, it also becomes clear that what is intended by trust (التوكل) is that which encompasses both legislative (تشريعية) and ontological (تكوينية) matters, or what is specific to the legislative—in the sense that Allah, the Glorified, commands the believers to obey Allah and His Messenger in His religious laws and what His Messenger has brought and clarified for them, and to entrust the matter of religion and divine laws to their Lord. They must refrain from acting independently and from manipulating what He has entrusted to them of His laws, just as He commands them to obey Him in the system of causes and effects (الأسباب والمسببات) that He has established for them. They should proceed according to this system without relying upon it or granting it independence and lordship (ربوبية), while waiting for what Allah intends and chooses for them of results through His governance and will.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَخَذَ اللهُ مِيثاقَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ وَبَعَثْنا مِنْهُمُ اثْنَيْ عَشَرَ نَقِيباً "Allah certainly took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We raised among them twelve chiefs..." (5:12). Al-Raghib said: "Al-naqb (النقب) in a wall or skin is like a hole in wood." He said: "And the naqib (النقيب) is the one who searches out a people and their conditions; its plural is nuqaba’ (نقباء)."

Allah, the Glorified, recounts to the believers of this community what occurred with the Children of Israel regarding the strengthening of their religion and the stabilization of their affair through the taking of the covenant, the raising of the chiefs, the delivery of the clarification, and the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة), followed by how they met this with the breaking of the covenant, and how Allah, the Glorified, met them with the curse and the hardening of hearts, and so on.

So He said: "Allah certainly took a pledge from the Children of Israel"—which is what He mentions frequently in Surah al-Baqarah and elsewhere—"and We raised among them twelve chiefs." It appears that they were the heads of the twelve tribes, acting as governors over them, managing their affairs. Their relationship to their tribes is, in a sense, like the relationship of the "holders of authority" (أولي الأمر) to the individuals in this community; they are the point of reference in matters of religion and the world, except that they do not receive revelation nor do they legislate a law, for that belongs only to Allah and His Messenger. "And Allah said, 'I am with you'"—an announcement of protection and observation. From this, it follows that He will help them if they obey Him and forsake them if they disobey Him. Therefore, He mentioned both matters together, saying: لَئِنْ أَقَمْتُمُ الصَّلاةَ وَآتَيْتُمُ الزَّكاةَ وَآمَنْتُمْ بِرُسُلِي وَعَزَّرْتُمُوهُمْ "If you maintain the prayer and give the zakat and believe in My apostles and support them..." (5:12). Support (التعزير) is assistance accompanied by veneration. What is intended by the "apostles" are those who would face them with their mission and call in the future, such as Jesus and Muhammad (عليه السلام) and others whom Allah sent between Moses and Muhammad (عليه السلام). وَأَقْرَضْتُمُ اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً "and lend Allah a good loan"—which refers to recommended spending (الإنفاق المندوب) other than the obligatory zakat. لَأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ وَلَأُدْخِلَنَّكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ "I will surely absolve you of your misdeeds and I will surely admit you into gardens with conduits running amidst them" (5:12). This is what pertains to the beautiful promise. Then He said: فَمَنْ كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ مِنْكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ "But whoever of you disbelieves after that has certainly strayed from the right way" (5:12).

His saying, the Exalted: فَبِما نَقْضِهِمْ مِيثاقَهُمْ لَعَنَّاهُمْ وَجَعَلْنا قُلُوبَهُمْ قاسِيَةً "So because of their breaking their pledge We cursed them and made their hearts hard" (5:13). He mentioned the recompense for disbelieving in the aforementioned covenant as straying from the right way (سواء السبيل). This is a summary mention which is detailed by the types of afflictions in this verse, some of which Allah, the Glorified, attributed to Himself—such as the curse and the hardening of hearts, where such attribution is appropriate—and some of which He attributed to themselves as occurring by their own choice, as He intends by His saying: وَلا تَزالُ تَطَّلِعُ عَلى خائِنَةٍ مِنْهُمْ "and you will not cease to learn of some treachery on their part" (5:13). All of this is their recompense for disbelieving in the signs of Allah, at the head of which was the covenant taken from them, or the recompense for their disbelief in the covenant specifically; for the "right way" from which they strayed is the path of felicity by which their world and their Hereafter are built.

So His saying: "So because of their breaking their pledge"—it appears that this is the disbelief for which Allah threatened them in the previous verse. The particle ma (ما) in His saying "So because of" (فبما) is for emphasis and provides a sense of ambiguity for the purpose of magnification, or belittlement, or otherwise. The meaning is: "So because of a certain breaking of their pledge by them, We cursed them." The curse (اللعن) is the distancing from mercy. "And We made their hearts hard (قاسية)." Hardness (قسوة) of the heart is derived from the hardness of stones, which is their solidity. A "hard" heart is one that does not humble itself before the truth nor is it affected by mercy. He, the Exalted, said: أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ اللهِ وَما نَزَلَ مِنَ الْحَقِّ وَلا يَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَطالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَمَدُ فَقَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَكَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ فاسِقُونَ "Is it not time yet for those who have faith that their hearts should be humbled for Allah’s remembrance and to the truth which has come down [to them], and that they should not be like those who were given the Book before? Time passed them by and so their hearts were hardened, and many of them are transgressors" (57:16).

In short, the consequence of their hearts' hardness was that they returned to يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَنْ مَواضِعِهِ "perverting the words from their contexts" (5:13) by interpreting them in ways that do not please Allah, the Glorified, and by omission, addition, or alteration; all of this is part of distortion (التحريف). This led them to miss clear realities of the religion: وَنَسُوا حَظًّا مِمَّا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ "and they have forgotten a part of what they were reminded of" (5:13). This was nothing other than a portion of the foundations (الأصول) upon which felicity revolves, and nothing can take its place except that which seals upon them the necessary wretchedness—such as their belief in anthropomorphism (التشبيه), the finality of Moses' prophethood, the permanence of the Torah's law, and the invalidity of abrogation (النسخ) and divine revision (البداء), among other things.

"and you will not cease to learn of some treachery (خائنة) on their part"—meaning a treacherous group among them, or treachery from them—إِلَّا قَلِيلاً مِنْهُمْ فَاعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَاصْفَحْ إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ "except a few of them. Yet excuse them and forbear. Indeed Allah loves the virtuous" (5:13). It has been mentioned repeatedly that the exception of a few of them does not contradict the establishment of the curse and punishment for the collective, which is the people and the nation. (1)

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّا نَصارى أَخَذْنا مِيثاقَهُمْ فَنَسُوا حَظًّا مِمَّا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ فَأَغْرَيْنا "Also from those who say, ‘We are Christians,’ We took a pledge; but they forgot a part of what they were reminded of. So We stirred up..." (5:14). Al-Raghib said: "Ghariya (غري) with such-and-such" means he became attached to it and stuck to it. The origin of this is from al-ghira’ (الغراء), which is glue. "I stirred up (أغريت) so-and-so with such-and-such" is like making him attached to it.

The Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, was a prophet of mercy who called people to reconciliation and peace, and urged them to look toward the Hereafter and turn away from the pleasures and ornaments of the world, and forbade them from competing for this lowly gain. (2) When they forgot a portion of what they were reminded of, Allah, the Glorified, established in their hearts war in place of peace and reconciliation, and replaced the brotherhood and affection to which they were invited with enmity and hatred, as He says: "but they forgot a part of what they were reminded of. So We stirred up enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection." This enmity and hatred which Allah, the Exalted, mentioned have become deeply rooted dispositions (ملكات) fixed among these Christian nations, like the fire of the Hereafter from which they have no escape; whenever they desire to emerge from its anguish, they are returned into it and told, "Taste the punishment of the burning."

Since Jesus son of Mary (عليه السلام) was raised, and his disciples and the traveling preachers among their students differed among themselves, discord broke out between them. It did not cease to grow and increase until it turned into wars, killings, raids, and various forms of displacement and expulsion, eventually leading to great world wars that threaten the earth with ruin and humanity with annihilation and extinction.

All of this is from the transformation of blessing into affliction and the production of straying from effort: وَسَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ اللهُ بِما كانُوا يَصْنَعُونَ "and soon Allah will inform them of what they had been doing" (5:14).

(1) A strange opinion found in some commentaries is that the "few" refers to Abdullah ibn Salam and his companions, despite the fact that Abdullah ibn Salam had embraced Islam a long time before the revelation of this Surah. The apparent meaning of the verse is the exception of some Jews who had not yet embraced Islam at the time of the verse's revelation. (2) Refer in this regard to the statements of the Messiah (عليه السلام) in his various positions narrated from him in the four Gospels.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا كُنتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَن كَثِيرٍ ۚ قَدْ جَآءَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ نُورٌ وَكِتَـٰبٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٥)
O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book, and excusing many [an offense of yours]. Certainly, there has come to you a light from Allah, and a manifest Book. (15)
يَهْدِى بِهِ ٱللَّهُ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ رِضْوَٰنَهُۥ سُبُلَ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ وَيُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِۦ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (١٦)
With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace, and brings them out from darkness into light by His will, and guides them to a straight path. (16)
لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۚ قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا إِنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُهْلِكَ ٱلْمَسِيحَ ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُۥ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ۗ وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۚ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٧)
They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah should He wish to destroy the Messiah, son of Mary, and his mother, and everyone upon the earth?’ To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them. He creates whatever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. (17)
وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ نَحْنُ أَبْنَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱللَّهِ وَأَحِبَّـٰٓؤُهُۥ ۚ قُلْ فَلِمَ يُعَذِّبُكُم بِذُنُوبِكُم ۖ بَلْ أَنتُم بَشَرٌ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقَ ۚ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٨)
The Jews and the Christians say, ‘We are Allah’s children and His beloved ones.’ Say, ‘Then why does He punish you for your sins?’ No, you are humans from among His creatures. He forgives whomever He wishes, and punishes whomever He wishes, and to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them, and toward Him is the return. (18)
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلَىٰ فَتْرَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ مَا جَآءَنَا مِنۢ بَشِيرٍ وَلَا نَذِيرٍ ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بَشِيرٌ وَنَذِيرٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٩)
O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying [the Divine teachings] for you after a gap in [the appearance of] the apostles, lest you should say, ‘There did not come to us any bearer of good news nor any warner.’ Certainly, there has come to you a bearer of good news and a warner. And Allah has power over all things. (19)

(Commentary)

When the Exalted mentioned His taking of the covenant (الميثاق) from the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) to support His messengers, to honor them, and to preserve what He gave them of the Book, and then mentioned their breaking of His covenant which He had bound them with, He called them to believe in His Messenger whom He sent and His Book which He revealed. He did this by way of introducing both to them, establishing clear proof (البينة) for the truthfulness of the message and the veracity of the Book, and completing the proof (إتمام الحجة) against them in that regard. As for the introduction, it is contained in His saying: "O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much..." (5:15), and His saying: "O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you after a gap..." (5:19).

As for the establishment of clear proof (البينة), it is found in His saying: "clarifying for you much of what you used to hide..." (5:15). Indeed, that is an excellent witness to the truthfulness of the message coming from an unlettered man (أمي) who informs of that which there is no way to know except for the elite among their scholars. Likewise is His saying: "With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure..." (5:16), for the true objectives whose veracity is beyond doubt are an excellent witness to the truthfulness of the message and the veracity of the Book.

As for the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة), it is what is contained in His saying: "lest you should say, ‘There did not come to us any bearer of good news nor any warner.’ So there has certainly come to you a bearer of good news and a warner. And Allah has power over all things" (5:19). Within these verses, Allah the Exalted has also refuted the claim of some: "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary’" (5:17), and the claim of the Jews and Christians: "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones" (5:18).

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيراً مِمَّا كُنْتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ — "O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book, and bypassing many [others]." As for his clarification of much that they used to hide of the Book, it is like his clarification of the verses of prophecy and its glad tidings, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "Those who follow the Apostle, the unlettered prophet, whom they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel..." (7:157), and His saying, the Exalted: "they recognize him just as they recognize their sons" (2:146), and His saying: "Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves... That is their description in the Torah and their description in the Evangel" (48:29). It is also like the clarification by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of the ruling regarding the man [the adulterer] which they had concealed and over which they had stubbornly resisted the truth, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, which will follow: "O Apostle! Do not let those who outpace each other in faithlessness grieve you..." (5:41). This ruling—meaning the ruling of stoning (الرجم)—is present even now in the twenty-second chapter of the Book of Deuteronomy in the Torah currently circulating among them.

As for his bypassing (عفوه) many [others], it refers to his leaving aside much of what they used to hide of the Book. This is evidenced by the discrepancies found in the two Books [the Torah and the Gospel], such as the Torah containing matters regarding monotheism (التوحيد) and prophecy (النبوة) that cannot correctly be attributed to Him, the Exalted, such as anthropomorphism (التجسم) and incarnation in a place (الحلول في المكان) and the like; and what the intellect (العقل) deems impossible to attribute to the noble prophets, such as various types of disbelief, immorality, and lapses; and the Torah’s total lack of mention of the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and the life hereafter — whereas no religion can stand except upon the foundation of the Return; and the inclusion in the Gospels they possess, especially the Gospel of John, of paganistic beliefs.

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ جاءَكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ نُورٌ وَكِتابٌ مُبِينٌ — "Certainly there has come to you from Allah a light and a manifest Book." The apparent meaning of His saying: "there has come to you from Allah" is that this "comer" subsists through Him, the Exalted, in a manner of subsistence like that of a clarification (البيان) or speech (الكلام) subsisting in the clarifier or the speaker. This supports the view that what is meant by the "light" (النور) is the Quran. Based on this, His saying: "and a manifest Book" (وَكِتابٌ مُبِينٌ) would be coordinated to it as an explanatory coordination (عطف تفسير), meaning both "the light" and "the manifest Book" refer to the Quran. Allah the Exalted has named the Quran "light" in several places in His speech, such as His saying, the Exalted: "and follow the light that has been sent down with him" (7:157), and His saying: "So believe in Allah and His Apostle and the light We have sent down" (64:8), and His saying: "and We have sent down to you a manifest light" (4:174).

It is also possible that what is meant by "the light" is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as might be suggested by the opening of the verse. Allah the Exalted has counted him as a light in His saying: "and as a radiant lamp" (33:46).

His saying, the Exalted: يَهْدِي بِهِ اللهُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوانَهُ سُبُلَ السَّلامِ — "With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace." The preposition "with" (بـ) in His saying: "With it" (بِهِ) denotes the instrument (الآلة), and the pronoun refers back to "the Book" or to "the light," whether the latter refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or the Quran. The ultimate result is the same, for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is one of the apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) in the stage of guidance (الهداية), as is the Quran, while the reality of guidance (حقيقة الهداية) subsists through Him [Allah]. He, the Exalted, said: "You cannot guide whomever you wish, but Allah guides whomever He wishes" (28:56), and He said: "Accordingly We have enlightened you with a spirit from Our command. You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith; but We made it a light by which We guide whomever We wish of Our servants, and indeed you guide to a straight path, the path of Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. Behold, all matters return to Allah" (42:52-53). These verses, as you see, attribute guidance to the Quran and to the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while simultaneously returning it to Allah, the Glorified. Thus, He is the Guide in reality (حقيقة), and others are apparent causes (سبب ظاهري) subjugated to bring the matter of guidance to life.

The Exalted qualified His saying: "With it Allah guides" with His saying: "those who follow [the course of] His pleasure." This amounts to conditioning the actualization of divine guidance upon the following of His pleasure. Thus, what is meant by guidance (الهداية) here is the "conveyance to the objective" (الإيصال إلى المطلوب), which is that Allah leads him into one of the ways of peace (سبل السلام), or all of the ways, or most of them, one after another.

The Exalted left "peace" (السلام) absolute; thus, it is safety and deliverance from every wretchedness that disrupts the happiness of life in this world or the hereafter. This accords with how the Quran describes submission (الإسلام) to Allah, faith (الإيمان), and godwariness (التقوى) as prosperity (الفلاح), triumph (الفوز), and security (الأمن), and the like. It has already been mentioned in the discussion on His saying, the Exalted: "Guide us on the straight path" (1:6) in the first volume of this book, that Allah, the Glorified, possesses many ways (سبل) according to the differing states of His traveling servants, all of which unite in a single path attributed to Him, the Exalted, which He calls in His speech "the straight path" (الصراط المستقيم). He, the Exalted, said: "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous" (29:69), and He, the Exalted, said: "This is indeed My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way" (6:153). This indicates that He has many ways, but they all unite in leading to His dignity, without separating their travelers or distinguishing the travelers of one way from the travelers of another, as is the case with ways other than His path.

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: Allah, the Glorified, guides and leads, by means of His Book or by means of His Prophet, whoever follows His pleasure into ways whose nature is such that whoever travels them is safe from the wretchedness of the life of this world and the hereafter, and from everything that embitters a happy life.

Thus, the matter of guidance to peace and happiness revolves around following the pleasure of Allah. He, the Exalted, has said: "He does not approve of faithlessness for His servants" (39:7), and He said: "Allah is not pleased with the transgressing lot" (9:96). Ultimately, this depends on avoiding the path of wrongdoing (الظلم) and refraining from joining the ranks of the wrongdoers (الظالمين), for Allah, the Glorified, has denied them His guidance and made them despair of attaining this divine dignity by His saying: "and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot" (62:5). Thus, this verse—meaning His saying: "With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace"—runs in a sense like His saying: "Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be security, and they are the guided ones" (6:82).

His saying, the Exalted: وَيُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِ — "and He brings them out from darkness into light by His will." The pluralization of "darkness" (الظلمات) and the singularization of "light" (النور) is an indication that the path of truth (الحق) has no discrepancy or division within it, even if it multiplies according to stations and positions, unlike the path of falsehood (الباطل).

When the "bringing out from darkness into light" is attributed to someone other than Him, the Exalted, such as a prophet or a book, the meaning of His "permission" (إذنه) therein is His authorization and pleasure, as He, the Exalted, said: "A Book We have sent down to you that you may bring mankind out from darkness into light by the command of their Lord" (14:1). He qualified his bringing them out from darkness into light with the permission of their Lord to exclude independence in causality (الاستقلال في السببية), for the true cause (السبب الحقيقي) of that is Allah, the Glorified. He also said: "Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs: ‘Bring your people out from darkness into light’" (14:5). He did not qualify it with "permission" here because the command itself contains that meaning.

When this is attributed to Allah, the Exalted, the meaning of bringing them out "by His permission" is bringing them out "by His knowledge" (بعلمه). The word "permission" (الإذن) has come with the meaning of knowledge; it is said: أذن به meaning "he knew of it." From this category is His saying, the Exalted: "An announcement from Allah and His Apostle" (9:3), and "Say, ‘I have proclaimed [it] to you all alike’" (21:109), and His saying: "And proclaim the Hajj to the people" (22:27), among other verses.

As for His saying, the Exalted: وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — "and guides them to a straight path," the word "guidance" (الهداية) has been repeated because His saying: "and He brings them out" intervened between His saying: "With it Allah guides" and this sentence. Furthermore, because the straight path (الصراط المستقيم)—as explained previously in Surah al-Fatihah—is a path that oversees (مهيمن) all other paths, the guidance to it is also a guidance that oversees all other types of guidance related to particular ways (السبل الجزئية).

The indefiniteness (التنكير) of His saying: "a straight path" (صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ) does not contradict the fact that what is meant is the unique straight path which Allah, the Exalted, attributes in His speech to Himself—except in Surah al-Fatihah—because the context indicates that. Rather, the indefiniteness is to magnify its status and emphasize its importance.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ — "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’" These are one of the three groups whose views were previously cited in Surah Al 'Imran, namely those who believe in the union (اتحاد) of Allah, the Glorified, with the Messiah, such that he is God and man simultaneously. It is possible to apply this sentence—meaning their saying: "Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary"—to the belief in sonship and to the belief in "the third of three" as well, although the apparent meaning of the sentence is the attainment of identity (العينية) through union (الاتحاد).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ فَمَنْ يَمْلِكُ مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً إِنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يُهْلِكَ الْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً — "Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah should He wish to destroy the Messiah, son of Mary, and his mother, and everyone upon the earth?’" This is a proof (برهان) to invalidate their claim by way of its internal contradiction. For when they posited that the Messiah, despite being God, was a human being as they described him as "the son of Mary," they allowed for him what is allowed for any human being assumed to be among the inhabitants of this earth. All of them, like all other parts of the heavens and the earth and what is between them, are owned (مملوكون) by Allah, the Exalted, and are subjugated under His ownership and authority. Thus, He, the Exalted, has the right to dispose of them as He wishes, and to judge for them or against them however He wills. He has the power to destroy the Messiah just as He has the power to destroy his mother and everyone on earth equally, without any distinction for the Messiah over others. And how can destruction be possible for Allah, the Glorified?! Thus, their positing that the Messiah is a human being invalidates their positing that he is Allah, the Glorified, due to the contradiction.

So His saying: "Who can avail anything against Allah" is an allusion (كناية) to the absolute negation of any preventer. To "own" (يملك) something against Allah means to have authority (السلطنة) over Him, the Exalted, in some matter pertaining to Him, the consequence of which would be the cessation of His authority over that thing. This would mean that some cause among causes acts independently in its effect on something such that it prevents His effect or overcomes Him in it. But there is no ownership (ملك) except for Allah alone, who has no partner, except for what He has granted others to own in a way that does not invalidate His own ownership and authority.

In His saying: "should He wish to destroy the Messiah, son of Mary, and his mother, and everyone upon the earth," He only qualified the Messiah with the phrase "son of Mary" to demonstrate that he is a complete human being subject to Lordly influence like all other humans. For that same reason, He coordinated "his mother" to him, as she is undoubtedly of the same genus (مسانخة) as him, and coordinated "everyone upon the earth" to him, because the ruling applies to all equally.

From here it becomes clear that in this qualification and coordination there is a hint toward the "proof of contingency" (برهان الإمكان). Its summary is that the Messiah is similar to other human individuals, such as his mother and everyone else on earth; thus, what is possible for them is possible for him, because the ruling for similar things (الأمثال) regarding what is possible and what is impossible is one. Since it is possible for others to fall under the ruling of destruction, it is possible for him as well, and there is no preventer to prevent it. If he were Allah, the Glorified, this would not be possible for him.

His saying: "To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them" serves as the rationale (تعليل) for the preceding sentence. The explicit mention of "whatever is between them," despite the Quran often expressing the world of creation as "the heavens and the earth" only, is to make the speech closer to being explicit and safer from the entry of delusions and doubts. Thus, no deluded person can imagine that He mentioned the heavens and the earth but did not mention what is between them, while the subject of the discussion is among what is between them.

The advancement of the predicate (تقديم الخبر)—meaning His saying: "To Allah" (وَلِلَّهِ)—is to denote exclusivity (الحصر), and with that the explanation is completed. The meaning is: How can any preventer prevent His will, the Exalted, to destroy the Messiah and others, and the occurrence of what He willed of that, when absolute ownership (الملك) and authority in the heavens and the earth and what is between them belong to Allah the Exalted, and no one else has any ownership? Thus, there is no preventer to stop the execution of His judgment and the passage of His command.

His saying: "He creates whatever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things" serves as the rationale for the sentence preceding it, namely His saying: "To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them." For "sovereignty" (المُلك)—with a damma on the mim—which is a type of authority and ownership over the authority of people and what they own, is only constituted by the encompassment of power (القدرة) and the efficacy of will (المشيئة). Allah, the Glorified, possesses that in all the heavens and the earth and what is between them. He has power over all things and He creates whatever things He wishes. Thus, He has absolute sovereignty in the heavens and the earth and what is between them. His creating whatever He wishes and His power over all things is the proof for His sovereignty, just as His sovereignty is the proof that He has the right to wish the destruction of all and then execute His will if He so wished. It is also the proof that no one among them shares in His divinity (ألوهيته).

As for the proof for the efficacy of His will and the encompassment of His power, it is that He is Allah, exalted is His Name. Perhaps for this reason, the majestic name "Allah" was repeated several times in the verse. Thus, the assumption of divinity in anything leads to the conclusion that He has no partner in His divinity.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَتِ الْيَهُودُ وَالنَّصارى نَحْنُ أَبْناءُ اللهِ وَأَحِبَّاؤُهُ — "The Jews and the Christians say, ‘We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones.’" There is no doubt that they were not claiming actual sonship (النبوة الحقيقية) as most Christians claim for the Messiah (peace be upon him); neither the Jews nor the Christians were claiming that in reality. Rather, they were applying it to themselves as an honorific title (إطلاقا تشريفيا) by way of a type of metaphor (تجوز). This usage has appeared frequently in their sacred books, as in the case of Adam, Jacob, David, Ephraim, and Jesus, and it was also applied to the righteous among the believers.

In any case, what was meant by "sons" is that they are to Allah, the Glorified, in the position of sons to a father. They are like the sons of a king in relation to him, set apart from the subjects and distinguished by the characteristic of proximity (القرب), which necessitates that they not be treated like the common subjects. It is as if they are exempted from the execution of laws and rulings applied among the people, because their connection to the throne of the king is not compatible with their being punished as others are punished, nor with their being made to stand where the rest of the subjects stand. They are not to be held in contempt as others are. All of this is because of the bond of love and honor that follows the bond of lineage.

Thus, what is meant by this "sonship" is special distinction and proximity. The coordination of His saying: "and His beloved ones" to His saying: "the sons of Allah" is like an explanatory coordination, though not strictly so. Their purpose in claiming this distinction and belovedness was to establish its necessary consequence (لازمه), which is that there is no way to punish or penalize them; they will only ever attain grace and honor, because His punishing them would contradict the special merit He granted them and the honor He bestowed upon them.

The evidence for this is what appeared in the refutation against them in His saying, the Exalted: "He forgives whomever He wishes and punishes whomever He wishes." For if they did not mean by their saying: "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones" that there is no way to punish them even if they did not respond to the true call, there would be no reason to mention this sentence: "He forgives..." as a refutation against them, nor would His saying: "Rather you are humans from among His creatures" have a fitting and appropriate place. So the meaning of their saying: "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones" is: We are the elite of Allah and His beloveds; He has no way to punish us regardless of what we do or what we leave, because the negation of the way [to punishment] and the occurrence of total security from every disliked and feared thing is the necessary consequence of the meaning of distinction and love.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ فَلِمَ يُعَذِّبُكُمْ بِذُنُوبِكُمْ — "Say, ‘Why then does He punish you for your sins?’" He commanded His Prophet to argue against them and refute their claim with proof. These are two proofs: the first is a refutation (النقض) based on the punishment actually occurring to them, and the second is a counter-argument (المعارضة) with a proof that yields the opposite of their claim.

The summary of the first proof, contained in His saying: "Why then does He punish you for your sins?" is that if your claim were true—that you are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones, secure from divine punishment and that there is no way for it to reach you—then you would be secure from every punishment, whether in the hereafter or in this world. So what is this punishment occurring to you and continuing among you because of your sins? As for the Jews, they have never ceased committing sins, such as their killing of their prophets and the righteous among their people, their transgression in breaking the divine covenants taken from them, their distorting of words from their places, their concealing of the signs of Allah and disbelieving in them, and every form of tyranny and aggression. They have tasted the evil consequence of their affair as an exemplary punishment (نكالا) upon them, from the transformation (مسخ) of some of them to the striking of abasement and poverty upon others, and the domination of wrongdoers over them who kill them, violate their honor, and destroy their lands. They have no life except the life of one who is wasting away (الحرض)—neither alive such that he is hoped for, nor dead such that he is forgotten.

As for the Christians, the corruption of the acts of disobedience and sins occurring among their nations is no less than what was among the Jews, nor are the types of punishment that descended upon them before the mission [of the Prophet], during its time, and after it until today. History preserves all of that or most of it for them, and the Quran recounts much of it, as in Surah al-Baqarah, Al 'Imran, al-Nisa, al-Ma'idah, al-A'raf, and others.

It is not for these people to say: "These calamities, afflictions, and trials descending upon us are merely of the category of 'affliction for the sake of loyalty' (البلاء للولاء), and there is no evidence that they stem from divine wrath that drags along exemplary punishment and evil consequences. Similar things descended upon the righteous servants of Allah among the prophets and messengers, such as Abraham, Ishmael, Jacob, Joseph, Zechariah, John, and others, and similar things descended upon you, O community of Muslims, as in the battles of Uhud, Mu'tah, and others. So why is it that when these misfortunes befall us, they are counted as divine punishments, but when they befall you, they become blessings and honors?"

This is because there is no doubt for anyone that these physical misfortunes and worldly calamities and afflictions occur to the believers just as they occur to the disbelievers, and they take the righteous and the wicked together—the way of Allah (سنة الله) which has passed among His servants. However, they differ in title (عنوانا) and effect (أثرا) according to the person's position regarding righteousness or wickedness, and the servant's station before his Lord.

There is no doubt that for one in whose soul righteousness has become established and in whose essence human virtue has taken root—like the noble prophets and those who follow them—the worldly calamities and trials descending upon him only result in the actualization of the latent virtues in his soul, from which he and others benefit through their good effects. This type of trial, which contains what the natural disposition (الطبع) dislikes, is nothing but divine training (تربية إلهية), or if you wish, an elevation of rank (ترفيعا للدرجة).

As for one who has not yet settled upon happiness or wretchedness and has not yet taken the necessary path of happiness, when calamities descend upon him and the turns of fortune revolve around him, they result in the determination of his path and the distinction of his position, whether toward disbelief or faith, righteousness or wickedness. This type of affliction and trial should only be called divine examinations and tests (امتحانات وابتلاءات إلهية) that carve a path for the person toward Paradise or toward the Fire.
