This matter was taken to such an extreme that a group went so far as to deny the authoritative status (حجية) of the apparent meanings (ظواهر) of the Book, while upholding the authority of works like Misbah al-Shari'ah, Fiqh al-Rida, and Jami' al-Akhbar! The excess reached a point where some stated that a narration (الحديث) can interpret the Quran even if it contradicts its explicit indication (صريح دلالته). This parallels what some of the majority (الجمهور) mentioned: that a report (الخبر) can abrogate (ينسخ) the Book. Perhaps the situation of the Ummah as it appears to other researchers—as some have noted—is that: "The Sunnis took the Book and left the Progeny (العترة), which led to them leaving the Book because of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'Indeed, the two shall never separate.' And the Shi'a took the Progeny and left the Book, which led to them leaving the Progeny because of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'Indeed, the two shall never separate.' Thus, the Ummah has left both the Quran and the Progeny—the Book and the Sunnah—together."

This methodology followed regarding narrations is one of the factors that contributed to the severing of the link between the Islamic sciences—both the religious and the literary sciences—and the Quran. This occurred despite the fact that all of them are like branches and fruits of this "goodly tree whose root is firmly fixed and whose branch is in the sky, yielding its fruit at all times by the leave of its Lord." Indeed, if you reflect upon the state of these sciences, you will find they have been organized in a manner that they have no need for the Quran at all. It is possible for a student to learn them all—morphology (الصرف), grammar (النحو), rhetoric (البيان), linguistics (اللغة), the science of narrations (الحديث), the study of narrators (الرجال), the principles of narration (الدراية), jurisprudence (الفقه), and legal theory (الأصول)—reaching the end of these studies, becoming proficient, and then attaining the rank of ijtihad (الاجتهاد) — the process of deriving legal rulings from original sources — and mastery therein, without ever having read the Quran or even touching a copy of the Scripture (المصحف). Thus, in reality, nothing remains for the Quran except its recitation to earn spiritual reward, or its use as an amulet (تميمة) for children to protect them from the calamities of time! Take heed, then, if you are among those capable of reflection.

Let us return to our subject: the state of inquiry regarding the Quran and narrations during the era of Umar was as you have heard. The scope of theological (كلامية) inquiries expanded during this era because the vast conquests naturally led to the intermingling of Muslims with other nations and followers of various religions and sects (الملل والنحل), among whom were scholars, rabbis (الأحبار), bishops (الأساقفة), and patriarchs (البطارقة) who researched religions and schools of thought. Thus, the beacon of theology (الكلام) was raised, though it had not yet been formally compiled, as the works attributed to it are only mentioned in the biographies of those who came after this era.

The situation remained thus during the era of Uthman, despite the people's uprising against the Caliphate; he was only successful in gathering the codices (المصاحف) and agreeing upon a single codex.

Then the matter continued as such during the caliphate of Ali (peace be upon him) and his preoccupation with rectifying the corruption that had afflicted the Muslim society due to internal disputes and the successive wars that followed.

However, he (peace be upon him) established the science of grammar (علم النحو) and dictated its universal principles (كلياته) to Abu al-Aswad al-Du'ali, one of his companions, commanding him to collect the particular (جزئيات) rules. Beyond that, he was only able to deliver expositions through sermons and narrations containing the foundational elements of religious knowledge (المعارف الدينية) and the most precious Quranic secrets. Furthermore, he engaged in theological disputations (احتجاجات كلامية) that are preserved in the compendiums of narrations.

The situation regarding the Quran and narrations remained thus during the era of Mu'awiyah and the Umayyads and Abbasids after him, until approximately the beginning of the fourth century of the Hijrah, which marks the end of the era of the Twelve Imams according to the Shi'a. No significant development occurred in the method of researching the Quran and narrations, except for what happened during Mu'awiyah's era regarding the exertion of effort to suppress the mention of the People of the House (أهل البيت) (peace be upon them), efface their legacy, and fabricate narrations (وضع الأحاديث). The religious government had transformed into an absolute monarchy (سلطنة استبدادية), and the Islamic Sunnah was changed into imperial rule. There was also the command of Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz to record the narrations, whereas the traditionists (المحدثون) had previously dealt with narrations through oral transmission and memorization without being bound by writing.

During this period, Arabic literature (الأدب العربي) flourished to its utmost extent. This began from the time of Mu'awiyah, who went to great lengths to promote poetry, followed by the Umayyads and then the Abbasids. Hundreds or thousands of dinars would sometimes be spent for a single verse of poetry or a literary nuance. People devoted themselves to poetry and its transmission, as well as the history and battles of the Arabs (أيام العرب), earning significant wealth through this. The Umayyads benefited from its promotion and the expenditure of wealth on it to strengthen their position against the Banu Hashim, as did the Abbasids later against the Banu Fatima. Similarly, they went to great lengths in honoring scholars to use them to influence the people and make them carry out whatever they desired and decreed.

The influence of poetry and literature in the scholarly community reached such a point that you see many scholars citing a poet's verse or a common proverb as evidence in intellectual issues or scientific inquiries, making it the final judgment. Often, they would build theoretical objectives upon linguistic issues, or at the very least, engage in a linguistic investigation of the subject's name before entering the actual inquiry. All of these matters had deep effects on the logic of researchers and their scholarly trajectory.

In those days, theological inquiry (البحث الكلامي) flourished, and books and treatises were written on it. It was not long before they split into two great factions: the Ash'arites (الأشاعرة) and the Mu'tazilites (المعتزلة). The foundations of their views existed during the time of the Caliphs, and even during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as evidenced by the reported disputations of Ali (peace be upon him) regarding compulsion (الجبر), delegation (التفويض), predestination (القدر), capacity (الاستطاعة), and other issues, as well as what has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding that.(1) The two groups became distinct during this time by the differentiation of their methodologies: the Mu'tazilites prioritized what the independent intellect (العقل) determines over the religious apparent meanings (الظواهر الدينية), such as the doctrine of rational beauty and ugliness (الحسن والقبح العقليين), the impossibility of choosing one of two equal options without a preponderant factor (الترجيح من غير مرجح), the impossibility of imposing a duty beyond one's capacity (التكليف بما لا يطاق), capacity, delegation, and so on. The Ash'arites, on the other hand, prioritized the apparent meanings over the judgment of the intellect, asserting the negation of rational beauty and ugliness, the permissibility of choosing without a preponderant factor, the negation of capacity, the doctrine of compulsion, the pre-eternity of the Word of Allah (قدم كلام الله), and other matters mentioned in their books.

Then they organized the discipline, established technical terminologies (الاصطلاحات), and added issues to counter the philosophers in metaphysical inquiries (المباحث المعنوية) regarding general matters (الأمور العامة). This occurred after the translation of philosophical books into Arabic and the spread of their study among Muslims. The matter is not as some have mentioned—that theology (التكلم) appeared or branched out in Islam into Mu'tazilism and Ash'arism only after the transfer of philosophy to the Arabs; the existence of most of their issues and views in the narrations prior to that proves otherwise.

The Mu'tazilites continued to increase in number and grow in power and prestige from their first appearance until the early Abbasid era (the beginning of the third century AH). Then they began to follow the path of decline and fall until the Ayyubid kings eradicated them, and they became extinct. During their time and after them, countless people were killed for the crime of Mu'tazilism, known only to Allah, the Glorified. At that point, the atmosphere of theological inquiry became clear for the Ash'arites without opposition, and they delved deeply into it after their jurists (فقهاؤهم) had initially considered it a sin. Ash'arism has remained prevalent among them to this day.

The Shi'a also had a long-standing involvement in theology (التكلم). Their first emergence in theology was after the passing of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Most of them were from the Companions (الصحابة), such as Salman, Abu Dharr, al-Miqdad, Ammar, Amr ibn al-Hamiq, and others, and from the Followers (التابعين), such as Rashid, Kumayl, Maytham, and other Alids whom the Umayyads eradicated. Then they became firmly rooted and their cause grew strong again

(1) Such as the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in what is narrated from him: لا جبر ولا تفويض بل أمر بين أمرين — "There is neither compulsion nor delegation, but a matter between the two matters," and his saying: القدرية مجوس هذه الأمة — "The Qadariyyah are the Magians of this Ummah."

during the time of the two Imams: al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them). They took up research and the authorship of books and treatises, and they continued to strive under the oppression and persecution of governments until they were granted some security during the Buyid state (1). Then they were suppressed again until the situation cleared for them with the emergence of the Safavid state in Iran (2), and they have continued as such until today.

The character of their theological inquiry was more similar to the Mu'tazilites than the Ash'arites. For this reason, some views sometimes became conflated, such as the doctrine of beauty and ugliness, the issue of choosing without a preponderant factor, the issue of predestination, and the issue of delegation. Also for this reason, the matter became confused for some people, who considered the two groups—the Shi'a and the Mu'tazilites—as having a single methodology in theological inquiry, like two racing horses. This is an error, for the principles narrated from the Imams of the People of the House (أهل البيت) (peace be upon them)—which are authoritative for the group—do not align with the Mu'tazilite taste in any way.

In short, the art of theology (فن الكلام) is a noble discipline that defends the true religious knowledge (المعارف الحقة الدينية). However, the Muslim theologians erred in their method of inquiry; they did not distinguish between intellectual judgments (الأحكام العقلية) and they conflated the truth (الحق) with the accepted (المقبول), as will be clarified to some extent later.

During this period of time, the sciences of the ancients—logic (المنطق), mathematics (الرياضيات), natural sciences (الطبيعيات), metaphysics (الإلهيات), medicine (الطب), and practical wisdom (الحكمة العملية)—were translated into Arabic. A portion was translated during the Umayyad era and then completed in the early Abbasid era. They translated hundreds of books from Greek, Roman, Indian, Persian, and Syriac into Arabic. People turned to studying various sciences and soon became independent in their thought, composing books and treatises on them. This used to enrage the scholars of the time, especially what they witnessed of the open display of atheists (الملاحدة) from the materialists (الدهرية), naturalists (الطبيعية), Manichaeans (المانوية), and others against the established matters of religion, and the attacks of the Muslim philosophizers (المتفلسفون) against religion and its people, treating the foundations of Islam and the pure landmarks of the Shari'ah with contempt and derision (and there is no disease like ignorance).

Among the things that enraged them most was what they heard from them regarding issues built upon premises (أصول موضوعة) taken from astronomy (الهيئة) and natural sciences, such as the Ptolemaic celestial spheres, their being a fifth nature, the impossibility of their tearing or mending (الخرق والالتيام), the pre-eternity of the spheres and celestial bodies in their individual identities (بالشخص), the pre-eternity of the elements in their species (بالنوع), the pre-eternity of species, and the like. These are issues built upon premises that were not proven in philosophy, yet the ignorant among the philosophizers presented them in the guise of proven issues. The materialists and their like, who at that time identified with philosophy, added other matters from their falsehoods, such as the doctrine of reincarnation (التناسخ) and the denial of the Resurrection (المعاد), especially the physical Resurrection (المعاد الجسماني). Through all of this, they attacked the apparent meanings of religion. Sometimes one of them would say: "Religion is a collection of traditional duties brought by the prophets to cultivate and perfect simple, naive minds; as for the philosopher who deals with the real sciences (العلوم الحقيقية), he has no need for them or what they brought." They were proficient in the methods of demonstration.

This prompted the jurists and theologians and drove them to confront them with rejection and to destroy them by any means available—through disputation, praying against them, disassociating from them, and declaring them disbelievers (تكفير)—until they broke their momentum, dispersed their gathering, and destroyed their books during the time of al-Mutawakkil. Philosophy almost became extinct after him until it was revived again by the Second Teacher, Abu Nasr al-Farabi (d. 339 AH), then after him by the Chief Master (الشيخ الرئيس), Abu Ali al-Husayn ibn Abd Allah ibn Sina (d. 428 AH), and then others among the luminaries of philosophy such as Abu Ali ibn Miskawayh, Ibn Rushd of Andalusia, and others. Since then, philosophy has continued to survive among a few practitioners, fluctuating between weakness and strength.

Although it was initially transferred to the Arabs, only a rare few among them became famous for it, such as al-Kindi and Ibn Rushd; it finally settled in Iran. Although the Muslim theologians opposed philosophy and rejected its people most vehemently, the majority of them accepted logic (المنطق), composing treatises and books on it because they found it consistent with the natural way of demonstration (الاستدلال الفطري).

However—as you have heard—they erred in its application. They applied the judgment of real definitions (الحدود الحقيقية) and their parts to conventional concepts (المفاهيم الاعتبارية), and they used demonstration (البرهان) in conventional propositions (القضايا الاعتبارية) where only dialectical syllogism (القياس الجدلي) is applicable. Thus, you see them discussing theological subjects such as beauty and ugliness, reward and punishment, the nullification of deeds (الحبط), and grace (الفضل) in terms of their genera (أجناسها), differentia (فصولها), and definitions (حدودها)—and where are these from a real definition? They use necessity (الضرورة) and impossibility (الامتناع) as evidence in issues of legal theory (الأصول) and theological issues from the branches of religion. This is an instance of employing realities (الحقائق) in conventional matters (الأمور الاعتبارية). They demonstrate regarding matters pertaining to the Necessary Being (الواجب تعالى) by saying: "It is necessary for Him to do such-and-such" and "It is ugly for Him to do such-and-such," thus judging realities by conventions and considering it a demonstration, while in reality, it is nothing but a poetic syllogism (القياس الشعري).

The excess in this chapter reached such a point that one of them said: "Allah, the Glorified, is too transcendent for convention (الاعتبار)—the reality of which is illusion (الوهم)—to creep into His judgment and action; therefore, everything He has brought into existence (تكوينًا) or legislated (تشريعًا) are real, factual matters." Another said: "Allah, the Glorified, is too powerful to decree a judgment and then be unable to establish a demonstration for it; thus, demonstration encompasses both the existential (التكوينيات) and the legislative (التشريعيات) realms together." These are among the sayings that are, by my life, among the tragedies of science and its people, and the necessity of recording and discussing them in scholarly writings is an even greater tragedy.

During this period, Sufism (التصوف) appeared among the Muslims. It had a root during the era of the Caliphs appearing in the garb of asceticism (الزهد). Then the matter became clear with the open display of the Sufis in the early Abbasid era with the appearance of men among them such as Abu Yazid, al-Junayd, al-Shibli, Ma'ruf, and others.

These people believe that the path to the reality of human perfection and the attainment of the realities of knowledge is to enter the Path (الطريقة) — a method of spiritual discipline within the Shari'ah to attain the Truth — which is a form of spiritual exercise (ارتياض) through the Shari'ah to reach the Truth (الحقيقة). Most of them, from both the elite and the commoners, attribute themselves to Ali (peace be upon him).

Since these people claimed matters of miracles (الكرامات) and spoke of things that contradict the apparent meanings of religion and the judgment of the intellect—claiming they have correct meanings that the understanding of the people of the apparent (أهل الظاهر) cannot reach—it was heavy upon the jurists and the general Muslims to hear them. They rejected them and met them with disassociation and charges of disbelief. They were sometimes imprisoned, lashed, killed, crucified, expelled, or exiled—all because of their lack of restraint and their indulgence in sayings they called "the secrets of the Shari'ah." If the matter were as they claimed, and these were the core of the Truth while the religious apparent meanings were like a shell over them, and it was necessary to manifest and proclaim them as they do, then the Legislator of the Shari'ah would have been more worthy of observing their state and announcing their matter as they announce it. And if they are not the truth, then what is after the truth except falsehood?

In the beginning, these people did not indicate their views on the Path except through speech. Then they added to that—after they had gained some place in the hearts—by composing books and treatises after the third century AH. Then they went further by explicitly stating their views on both the Truth and the Path after that, and what they composed in poetry and prose spread throughout the earth.

They continued to increase in number, preparation, influence in the hearts of the commoners, and prestige until they reached their absolute peak in the sixth and seventh centuries. Then they regressed in their course, their cause weakened, and the general public turned away from them.

The reason for their decline was, firstly, that when any vital matter that concerns the general public becomes the object of intense devotion and the hearts become infatuated with it, the souls of a group of ambitious people long to profit through its path. They then donned its garb and appeared in the form of its people and its elite, corrupting it, which was followed by the people's aversion to it.

Secondly, a group of their masters mentioned that the method of self-knowledge (معرفة النفس) is an innovated method (طريقة مبتدعة) that the Legislator of the Shari'ah did not mention in what He legislated, except that it is a pleasing path that Allah, the Glorified, accepted, just as He accepted the innovated monasticism among the Christians. The Exalted said: وَرَهْبانِيَّةً ابْتَدَعُوها ما كَتَبْناها عَلَيْهِمْ إِلَّا ابْتِغاءَ رِضْوانِ اللهِ فَما رَعَوْها حَقَّ رِعايَتِها — "And as for monasticism, they innovated it—We did not prescribe it for them—only seeking Allah’s pleasure; but they did not observe it with due observance." (57:27).

The majority of them accepted this, which allowed them to innovate rituals and etiquettes for the spiritual journey (السلوك) that were not known in the Shari'ah. Thus, they continued to innovate new traditions and abandon Shari'ah ones, until it led to the Shari'ah being on one side and the Path on the other. This naturally led to indulgence in prohibitions, the abandonment of obligatory duties from the religious rites, the lifting of religious obligations (رفع التكاليف), and the appearance of groups like the Qalandariyyah. Nothing remained of Sufism except begging and the use of opium and bhang, which they called "annihilation" (الفناء).

That which the Book and the Sunnah judge—and they both lead to the judgment of the intellect—is that the statement that beneath the apparent meanings of the Shari'ah are realities that constitute its inner depth (باطنها) is true. The statement that man has a path to attaining them is true. However, the path is only the proper use of the religious apparent meanings as they ought to be used, and nothing else. Far be it that there should be an inner reality to which the outer does not lead; the outer (الظاهر) is the title of the inner and its path. Far be it that there should be something else closer than what the Legislator of the religion indicated, which He overlooked, or was lenient about, or turned away from entirely for some reason, while He, the Mightiest of speakers, says: وَنَزَّلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ تِبْياناً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "We have sent down the Book to you as an exposition of all things" (16:89). In short, these are three paths in the search for realities and their discovery: the religious apparent meanings, the path of intellectual inquiry, and the path of self-purification. Each was taken by a group of Muslims, despite the conflict and mutual rejection between the three groups. Their gathering in this is like the angles of a triangle: whenever you increase the magnitude of one, you decrease the other two, and vice versa.

Discourse on exegesis (التفسير) varied drastically according to the different orientations of the exegetes, meaning that scholarly inquiry was mostly imposed upon the Quran rather than the reverse, except in rare cases.

You have already learned that the Book confirms whatever is true from each of these paths. Far be it that there should be a true inner reality that its outer does not agree with, and far be it that there should be a truth, whether outer or inner, that true demonstration (البرهان الحق) rejects or contradicts.

Therefore, a group of scholars, with the scholarly capital they possessed and despite their different orientations, sought to reconcile the religious apparent meanings and gnosis (العرفان), such as Ibn al-Arabi, Abd al-Razzaq al-Kashani, Ibn Fahd, the Second Martyr (al-Shahid al-Thani), and al-Fayd al-Kashani.

Others sought to reconcile philosophy and gnosis, such as Abu Nasr al-Farabi, Shaykh al-Suhrawardi—the author of al-Ishraq—and Shaykh Sa'in al-Din Muhammad Turkah.

Others sought to reconcile the religious apparent meanings and philosophy, such as al-Qadi Sa'id and others.

Others sought to reconcile all of them, such as Ibn Sina in his exegeses and books, Sadr al-Muta'allihin al-Shirazi in his books and treatises, and several who came after him.

Despite all this, the deep-rooted disagreement remains as it was; the abundance of efforts to cut its root only increases its entrenchment, and the efforts to extinguish its fire only increase its blaze: "I found every amulet to be of no use." You do not see the people of any of these arts except that they accuse the others of ignorance, heresy (زندقة), or foolishness of opinion, while the commoners disassociate from them all.

All of this is because the Ummah failed on the first day to respond to the Book's call for social reflection: وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَلَا تَفَرَّقُوا — "Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided." And the discourse has many branches.

O Allah, guide us to what pleases You regarding us, unite our word upon the truth, and grant us from Yourself a guardian, and grant us from Yourself a helper.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيراً — "O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much..." (the verse): Ibn al-Daris, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Whoever disbelieves in stoning (الرجم) has disbelieved in the Quran from where he does not realize." The Exalted said: يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيراً مِمَّا كُنْتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ الْكِتابِ — "O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book." He said: "Stoning was among what they hid."

I say: This is an allusion to what will come in the exegesis of His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ لا يَحْزُنْكَ — "O Apostle! Do not let those who outpace each other in unfaith grieve you..." until the end of the verses (5:41), regarding the narration of the Jews concealing the ruling of stoning during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his uncovering of that.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلى فَتْرَةٍ مِنَ الرُّسُلِ — "...clarifying for you after a gap between the apostles..." (the verse), he said: "He said: 'During an interruption of the apostles.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Abu Hamzah Thabit ibn Dinar al-Thumali and Abu al-Rabi', who said: "We performed the pilgrimage with Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in the year in which Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik performed the pilgrimage. With him was Nafi', the client of Umar ibn al-Khattab. He looked at Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) at the corner of the House (the Kaaba) while the people had gathered around him. Nafi' said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, who is this around whom the people are crowding?' He replied: 'This is the prophet of the people of Kufa; this is Muhammad ibn Ali.' He said: 'I bear witness that I shall go to him and ask him about matters that none can answer me except a prophet or the successor (وصي) of a prophet.' He said: 'Then go and ask him; perhaps you will embarrass him.'"

"So Nafi' came until he leaned against the people, then he looked over at Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and said: 'O Muhammad ibn Ali, I have read the Torah, the Gospel, the Psalms, and the Criterion (الفرقان), and I have known their lawful and their prohibited. I have come to ask you about matters that none can answer except a prophet or the successor of a prophet.' Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) raised his head and said: 'Ask whatever occurs to you.' He said: 'Tell me, how many years were there between Jesus and Muhammad?' He said: 'Shall I tell you according to my word or according to your word?' He said: 'Tell me according to both words.' He said: 'As for my word, it is five hundred years; as for your word, it is six hundred years.'"

I say: Various reports have been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) for the verses, such as what al-Tabari narrated from Ikrimah: "The Jews asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about the ruling of stoning. He asked about the most learned among them, and they pointed to Ibn Suriya. He adjured him by Allah: 'Do they find the ruling of stoning in their book?' He replied: 'When it became frequent among us, we would lash a hundred and shave the heads.' So he judged them with stoning, and Allah revealed: يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ — 'O People of the Book...' until His saying: صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — '...a straight path.'" And what he also narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Ibn Abi, Bahri ibn Amr, and Shas ibn Adi came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He spoke to them and they spoke to him. He invited them to Allah and warned them of His vengeance. They said: 'What are you trying to frighten us with, O Muhammad? By Allah, we are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones'—like the saying of the Christians. So Allah revealed concerning them: وَقالَتِ الْيَهُودُ وَالنَّصارى — 'The Jews and the Christians say...'" (until the end of the verse).

And what he also narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah invited the Jews to Islam, encouraging them in it and warning them, but they refused him. Mu'adh ibn Jabal, Sa'd ibn Ubadah, and Uqbah ibn Wahb said to them: 'O assembly of Jews, be conscious of Allah! By Allah, you certainly know that he is the Messenger of Allah. You used to mention him to us before his mission and describe him to us by his attributes.' Rafi' ibn Huraymah and Wahb ibn Yahuda said: 'We never said this to you, and Allah has not sent down any book after Moses, nor has He sent any bearer of good news or warner after him.' So Allah revealed: يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلى فَتْرَةٍ — 'O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you after a gap...'" (the verse). This was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from him and others, and other things were narrated as well.

The contents of these narrations, like most of what has been reported regarding theoretical occasions (أسباب نظرية), are merely applications of the cases to the contents of the verses and then judging them to be the occasions of revelation. Thus, they are theoretical occasions, while the verses are as if they were absolute in their revelation.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ جَعَلَ فِيكُمْ أَنۢبِيَآءَ وَجَعَلَكُم مُّلُوكًا وَءَاتَىٰكُم مَّا لَمْ يُؤْتِ أَحَدًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٠)
When Moses said to his people, ‘O my people, remember Allah’s blessing upon you when He appointed prophets among you, and made you kings, and gave you what none of the nations were given. (20)
يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ ٱلْمُقَدَّسَةَ ٱلَّتِى كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَرْتَدُّوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَارِكُمْ فَتَنقَلِبُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (٢١)
O my people, enter the Holy Land which Allah has ordained for you, and do not turn your backs, or you will become losers.’ (21)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّ فِيهَا قَوْمًا جَبَّارِينَ وَإِنَّا لَن نَّدْخُلَهَا حَتَّىٰ يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا فَإِن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّا دَٰخِلُونَ (٢٢)
They said, ‘O Moses, there are a tyrannical people in it. We will not enter it until they leave it. But once they leave it, we will go in.’ (22)
قَالَ رَجُلَانِ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمَا ٱدْخُلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْبَابَ فَإِذَا دَخَلْتُمُوهُ فَإِنَّكُمْ غَـٰلِبُونَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَتَوَكَّلُوٓا۟ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٢٣)
Said two men from among those who were Godfearing and whom Allah had blessed: ‘Go at them by the gate! For once, you have entered it, you will be victors. Put your trust in Allah, should you be faithful.’ (23)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّا لَن نَّدْخُلَهَآ أَبَدًا مَّا دَامُوا۟ فِيهَا ۖ فَٱذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَرَبُّكَ فَقَـٰتِلَآ إِنَّا هَـٰهُنَا قَـٰعِدُونَ (٢٤)
They said, ‘O Moses, we will never enter it so long as they remain in it. Go ahead, you and your Lord, and fight! We will be sitting right here.’ (24)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى لَآ أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِى وَأَخِى ۖ فَٱفْرُقْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٢٥)
He said, ‘My Lord! I have no power over [anyone] except myself and my brother, so part us from the transgressing lot.’ (25)
قَالَ فَإِنَّهَا مُحَرَّمَةٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ۛ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً ۛ يَتِيهُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ فَلَا تَأْسَ عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٢٦)
He said, ‘It shall be forbidden them for forty years: they shall wander about in the earth. So do not grieve for the transgressing lot.’ (26)

(Commentary)

The verses are not devoid of connection to what preceded them, as they encompass their breaking of some of the covenants taken from them—specifically the covenant of hearing and obeying Moses—and their confronting Moses (peace be upon him) with an explicit rejection of what he called them to, and their being afflicted, as a punishment for this sin of theirs, with the wandering (التيه), which is a divine punishment.

In some reports (الأخبار), there is an indication that these verses were revealed before the Battle of Badr in the early period of the Migration (الهجرة), as will be pointed out in the following traditional discussion (البحث الروائي), God willing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ يا قَوْمِ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ — When Moses said to his people, ‘O my people, remember Allah’s blessing upon you (until the end of the verse). The verses revealed concerning the stories of Moses indicate that this story—Moses calling them to enter the Holy Land—only occurred after their departure from Egypt, just as His saying in this verse: وَجَعَلَكُمْ مُلُوكاً (and made you kings) also indicates this.

His saying: وَآتاكُمْ ما لَمْ يُؤْتِ أَحَداً مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ (and gave you what He had not given to any other among the nations) indicates the precedence of several signs (الآيات) revealed to them, such as the manna (المن) and the quails (السلوى), the gushing of springs from the stone, and the shading of the clouds.

His saying: الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ (the transgressing people), repeated twice, indicates the realization of opposition and disobedience to the Messenger from them time after time before this story, until they became characterized by the quality of transgression (الفسق).

These are contextual clues (قرائن) indicating that the story—meaning the story of the wandering (التيه)—occurred in the latter part of the period of Moses’ (peace be upon him) stay among them after Allah the Exalted sent him to them, and that most of the stories narrated in the Quran about them occurred before that.

So, Moses’ saying to them: اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ (Remember Allah’s blessing upon you) is intended to mean the sum total of the blessings with which Allah favored them and bestowed upon them. He only began with this as a preamble to what he was about to summon them to—entering the Holy Land. He reminded them of their Lord’s blessings so that they might be energized thereby to seek the increase and completion of the blessing; for Allah had blessed them with the mission of Moses, which guided them to His religion, their salvation from the House of Pharaoh, the revelation of the Torah, and the legislation of the Law (الشريعة). Nothing remained for the completion of the blessing except that they possess a holy land in which they could independently settle and attain sovereignty.

He divided the blessing of which he reminded them into three categories during the detailed explanation, saying: إِذْ جَعَلَ فِيكُمْ أَنْبِياءَ (when He appointed prophets among you). These are the prophets within their ancestral line, such as Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and the prophets after them, or specifically the prophets from the Children of Israel like Joseph, or the Tribes (الأسباط), Moses, and Aaron. Prophethood (النبوة) is another blessing.

Then He said: وَجَعَلَكُمْ مُلُوكاً (and made you kings), meaning independent in yourselves, having emerged from the humiliation of the Pharaohs' enslavement and the domination of tyrants. A king (الملك) is none other than one who is independent in the affairs of himself, his family, and his wealth. The Children of Israel in the time of Moses were following a social sunnah (سنة اجتماعية) — a customary social practice — which is the best of practices: the sunnah of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — which commanded them to obey Allah and His Messenger, to maintain complete justice in their society, and to refrain from transgressing against other nations, without some of them lording over others or their classes differing in a way that would disrupt the social order. They had none over them except Moses, and he was a prophet who did not follow the path of a king or a tribal chief who exalts himself over them unjustly.

It has been said: The meaning of making them kings is what Allah decreed for them of the kingship that began from Saul (طالوت) to David, until the last of their kings. According to this, the speech is a promise of kingship and an announcement of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — for kingship did not become established among them until a time after Moses. This view is not bad, but it does not align well with His saying: وَجَعَلَكُمْ مُلُوكاً (and made you kings), as He did not say: "and made from among you kings," as He said: جَعَلَ فِيكُمْ أَنْبِياءَ (appointed prophets among you).

It is possible that the meaning of kingship (الملك) is simply the centering of authority within a group, thus including the system of eldership (الشيخوخة). According to this, Moses (peace be upon him) would be a king, and after him Joshua the Prophet, and Joseph had been a king before, ending with the well-known kings: Saul, David, Solomon, and others. However, this view is also subject to the same objection as the previous one.

Then He said: وَآتاكُمْ ما لَمْ يُؤْتِ أَحَداً مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ (and gave you what He had not given to any other among the nations). These are the divine cares and graces that were coupled with brilliant signs (آيات) capable of rectifying their lives had they remained upright upon what they said and persisted in what they covenanted. These are the clear signs (الآيات البينات) that surrounded them from every side during the days they were in Egypt, and after Allah saved them from Pharaoh and his people. For there did not abound or occur in succession such miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزات), radiant proofs (البراهين الساطعات), and blessings enjoyed in life upon any of the past nations prior to the era of Moses as those that abounded and occurred in succession for the Children of Israel.

Based on this, there is no basis for the claim of some that "the nations" (العالمين) refers to the people of their own time. This is because the verse denies that any nation up to that time had been given the blessings that the Children of Israel were given, and that is indeed the case.

His saying, the Exalted: يا قَوْمِ ادْخُلُوا الْأَرْضَ الْمُقَدَّسَةَ الَّتِي كَتَبَ اللهُ لَكُمْ وَلا تَرْتَدُّوا عَلى أَدْبارِكُمْ فَتَنْقَلِبُوا خاسِرِينَ — O my people, enter the Holy Land which Allah has ordained for you, and do not turn your backs, or you will become losers. He commanded them to enter the Holy Land, and he could infer from their condition a tendency toward rebellion and refusal to accept. Therefore, he emphasized his command with the prohibition against turning back and mentioned that it would result in loss (الخسران). The evidence that he inferred rejection from them is his describing them as "transgressors" (الفاسقين) after their rejection; for a single rejection, which is one act of transgression (فسق), does not justify applying the term "the transgressors" (الفاسقين) to them, which indicates a type of persistence and repetition.

He described the land as "holy" (المقدسة), and they have interpreted it as "purified from polytheism" due to the dwelling of prophets and believers therein. Nothing has appeared in the Noble Quran that explicitly explains this word. What can be derived from it, approaching this meaning, is His saying, the Exalted: إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَرْضَى الَّذِي بارَكْنا حَوْلَهُ (to the Remote Mosque whose environs We have blessed) (17:1), and His saying: وَأَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَمَغارِبَهَا الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها (And We made the people who were abased the heirs to the east and west of the land which We had blessed) (7:137). Blessing (المباركة) in the land is nothing other than placing much good (الخير الكثير) in it, and among the "much good" is the establishment of religion and the removal of the filth of polytheism (الشرك).

His saying: كَتَبَ اللهُ لَكُمْ (Allah has ordained for you). The apparent meaning of the verses is that what is intended is the decree (قضاء) of their settling therein. This is not contradicted by His saying at the end of them: فَإِنَّها مُحَرَّمَةٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً (It shall be forbidden them for forty years); rather, it confirms it. For His saying: كَتَبَ اللهُ لَكُمْ is a general statement in which the mention of the time and even the specific individuals is left ambiguous (أبهم), as the address is to the nation without addressing the state of individuals and persons. As it has been said: those who heard this address, who were present and obligated by it, died and perished to the last man in the wandering (التيه), and none entered the Holy Land except their children and grandchildren with Joshua son of Nun. In short, His saying: فَإِنَّها مُحَرَّمَةٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً is not devoid of an indication that it was ordained (مكتوبة) for them after that.

This "ordaining" (الكتابة) is what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَنُرِيدُ أَنْ نَمُنَّ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَنَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً وَنَجْعَلَهُمُ الْوارِثِينَ وَنُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ (And We desired to show favour to those who were abased in the land, and to make them imams, and to make them the heirs, and to establish them in the land) (28:5-6). Moses (peace be upon him) used to hope for that for them on the condition of seeking help from Allah and being patient, where he says: قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ اسْتَعِينُوا بِاللهِ وَاصْبِرُوا إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُها مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ قالُوا أُوذِينا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَأْتِيَنا وَمِنْ بَعْدِ ما جِئْتَنا قالَ عَسى رَبُّكُمْ أَنْ يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ (Moses said to his people, ‘Plead with Allah for help and be patient. Indeed the earth belongs to Allah: He gives its inheritance to whomever He wishes of His servants, and the outcome will be in favour of the God-conscious.’ They said, ‘We were tortured before you came to us and after you came to us.’ He said, ‘Maybe your Lord will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land, and then He will see how you act.’) (7:129).

This is what the Exalted informs of its fulfillment by His saying: وَأَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَمَغارِبَهَا الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ الْحُسْنى عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ بِما صَبَرُوا (And We made the people who were abased the heirs to the east and west of the land which We had blessed, and the best word of your Lord was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because of their patience) (7:137). Thus, the verse indicates that their seizing of the Holy Land and their settling therein was a divine word (كلمة إلهية), an ordainment (كتاب), and a settled decree (قضاء مقضيا) conditioned upon patience (الصبر) in obedience, refraining from disobedience, and through the course of events.

We have generalized "patience" (الصبر) because of the absolute nature of the verse, and because difficult events were accumulating upon them during the days of Moses, accompanied by divine commands and prohibitions. Whenever they persisted in disobedience, the difficult obligations intensified upon them, as indicated by their reports mentioned in the Noble Quran.

This is what is apparent from the Quran regarding the meaning of the Holy Land being ordained (كتابة) for them. The verses, nonetheless, are ambiguous regarding the time of the ordainment and its duration, except that His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verses of Surah al-Isra: وَإِنْ عُدْتُمْ عُدْنا وَجَعَلْنا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكافِرِينَ حَصِيراً (but if you revert, We [too] shall revert, and We have made hell a prison for the faithless) (17:8), and likewise the saying of Moses to them at the end of the previous verse: عَسى رَبُّكُمْ أَنْ يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ (Maybe your Lord will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land, and then He will see how you act) (7:129), and also his saying: وَإِذْ قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ — until he said — وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكُمْ لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَلَئِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ (And when your Lord proclaimed, ‘If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe.’) (14:7), and similar verses, indicate that this ordainment is a conditional ordainment (كتابة مشترطة), not an absolute one that is incapable of change or alteration.

Some commentators have mentioned that Moses' intent in what is narrated of his saying in the verse: كَتَبَ اللهُ لَكُمْ (Allah has ordained for you) refers to what Allah promised Abraham (peace be upon him). Then he mentioned what is in the Torah regarding Allah's promise to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob that He would give the land to their descendants, and he prolonged the discussion on that.

It does not concern us to investigate that based on the condition of the "Book," whether these promises are from the original Torah or from what the hand of corruption (التحريف) has toyed with, for the Quran is not interpreted by the Torah.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا يا مُوسى إِنَّ فِيها قَوْماً جَبَّارِينَ وَإِنَّا لَنْ نَدْخُلَها حَتَّى يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها فَإِنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها فَإِنَّا داخِلُونَ — They said, ‘O Moses, indeed there is a huge people in it, and we will not enter it until they leave it. But if they leave it, we will enter.’ Al-Raghib said: The root of al-jabr (الجبر) is the rectification of a thing by a kind of force (قهر). It is said: jabartuhu fa-injabara (I set it and it was set). He said: al-jabr is sometimes used for pure rectification, such as the saying of Ali (may Allah be pleased with him): "O Mender (جابر) of every broken thing and O Easer of every difficulty," and from this is their calling bread Jabir ibn Habbah. Sometimes it is used for pure force (القهر), such as his (peace be upon him) saying: "There is no compulsion (جبر) and no total delegation (تفويض)." He said: al-ijbar (الإجبار) is originally compelling another to mend another, but it has become conventionally used for pure coercion (الإكراه), so it is said: "I compelled him (أجبرته) to such-and-half," just as you say: "I coerced him." He said: al-jabbar (الجبار) as an attribute of a human is said of one who mends a deficiency by claiming a status of loftiness that he does not deserve, and this is only said by way of censure, such as His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَخابَ كُلُّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ (and every obstinate tyrant was disappointed) and His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّاراً شَقِيًّا (and He has not made me a tyrant, wretched) and His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: إِنَّ فِيها قَوْماً جَبَّارِينَ (indeed there is a huge people in it). He said: Because of the conceptualization of force through superiority over peers, it is said: "a huge palm tree" (nakhlatun jabbarah) and "a huge she-camel" (naqatun jabbarah). End of the relevant portion.

Thus, it becomes clear that what is meant by "the huge people" (الجبارين) are those of power and strength who compel people to what they desire.

Their saying: وَإِنَّا لَنْ نَدْخُلَها حَتَّى يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها (and we will not enter it until they leave it) is a condition they set—the departure of the huge people—for entering the land. Its reality is a rejection of Moses' command, even if they promised a second time to enter upon the condition by their saying: فَإِنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها فَإِنَّا داخِلُونَ (But if they leave it, we will enter).

There have appeared in several reports (الأخبار) regarding the description of these "huge people" from the Amalekites (العمالقة), the greatness of their bodies, and the height of their stature, strange things that no sound intellect can believe, and there is nothing in terrestrial remains or natural research to support them; they are nothing but fabrications and interpolations.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَجُلانِ مِنَ الَّذِينَ يَخافُونَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمَا — Two men from among those who were God-conscious and whom Allah had blessed said... (until the end of the verse). The apparent context is that what is meant by "fear" (المخافة) is the fear of Allah, the Glorified, and that there were men who feared Allah lest they disobey His command and the command of His Prophet. Among them were these two men who said what they said, and they were distinguished from among those who fear by the fact that Allah had blessed them. It has already been mentioned in previous places of the Book that when "blessing" (النعمة) is used absolutely in the convention of the Quran, it refers to divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية). Thus, they were among the friends (أولياء) of Allah the Exalted, and this in itself is a contextual clue that what is meant by "fear" is the fear of Allah, the Glorified, for the friends of Allah do not fear other than Him. He, the Exalted, said: أَلا إِنَّ أَوْلِياءَ اللهِ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ولا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve) (10:62).

It is possible that the omitted object of "blessed" (أَنْعَمَ)—meaning the thing bestowed—is fear itself, so the meaning would be that Allah blessed them with the fear of Him. The object of "they fear" (يَخافُونَ) would be omitted because it is sufficient to mention it in His saying: أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمَا (whom Allah had blessed), since it is known that their fear was not of those huge people; otherwise, they would not have called the Children of Israel to enter by saying: ادْخُلُوا عَلَيْهِمُ الْبابَ (Enter the gate against them).

Some commentators mentioned: The plural pronoun in "they fear" (يَخافُونَ) refers to the Children of Israel, and the pronoun referring back to the relative pronoun is omitted. The meaning would be: "And two men from those whom the Children of Israel feared, Allah had blessed the two men with Islam." They supported this with what is attributed to Ibn Jubayr of the reading yukhafuna (يُخافُونَ) with a damma on the ya. They said: This is because two men from the Amalekites had believed in Moses and joined the Children of Israel, then they said to the Children of Israel what they said to show them the way to victory over the Amalekites and the seizure of their cities and land. This interpretation was based on some reports narrated in the commentary of the verses, but it is from the solitary reports (الآحاد) that contain what has no witness from the Book or elsewhere.

His saying: ادْخُلُوا عَلَيْهِمُ الْبابَ (Enter the gate against them). Perhaps what is meant by it is the first city of the cities of those tyrants adjacent to the Children of Israel, which was, as it is said, Jericho; this is a common usage. Or what is meant is the gate of the city.

His saying: فَإِذا دَخَلْتُمُوهُ فَإِنَّكُمْ غالِبُونَ (for once you have entered it, you will be victors). This is a promise from the two of them to the people of victory and triumph over the enemy. They only spoke with such certainty by relying on what Moses (peace be upon him) had mentioned—that Allah had ordained that land for them—due to their faith in the truth of his report, or because they knew that through the light of divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية). The majority of the commentators of both sects have mentioned that the two men were Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh, and they were among the twelve chieftains (نقباء) of the Children of Israel.

Then they called them to trust in their Lord by saying: وَعَلَى اللهِ فَتَوَكَّلُوا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ (and put your trust in Allah, should you be faithful), because Allah, the Glorified, is sufficient for whoever trusts in Him, and in this is a heartening and encouragement for them.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا يا مُوسى إِنَّا لَنْ نَدْخُلَها أَبَداً ما دامُوا فِيها — They said, ‘O Moses, we will never enter it so long as they are in it.’ (The verse). Their repeating their saying: إِنَّا لَنْ نَدْخُلَها (we will never enter it) a second time was to make Moses (peace be upon him) despair of persisting in his call, so that he would not return to calling them time after time.

In the speech are aspects of insult, disparagement, and mockery of the station of Moses and what he reminded them of regarding the command of their Lord and His promise. They presented the speech in a strange sequence: they first turned away from addressing the two men who were calling to the summons of Moses (peace be upon him), then they were brief with Moses after they had been lengthy by mentioning the reason and specifics at the start of their speech. Brevity after lengthiness in a context of dispute and response indicates boredom with the talk and a dislike of hearing the discourse continue from the other disputant. Then they emphasized their saying: لَنْ نَدْخُلَها (we will never enter it) a second time with their saying: أَبَداً (never). Then ignorance emboldened them to what is greater than all of that, which is their saying, branching off from their rejection of the call: فَاذْهَبْ أَنْتَ وَرَبُّكَ فَقاتِلا إِنَّا هاهُنا قاعِدُونَ (So go you and your Lord and fight! We will stay here sitting.)

In the speech is the clearest indication of their being anthropomorphists (مشبهين) like the idolaters, and so they were; for they are the ones who said, as Allah the Glorified narrates of them in His saying: وَجاوَزْنا بِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْبَحْرَ فَأَتَوْا عَلى قَوْمٍ يَعْكُفُونَ عَلى أَصْنامٍ لَهُمْ قالُوا يا مُوسَى اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً كَما لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ قالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ (We carried the Children of Israel across the sea, whereat they came upon a people who were devoted to some idols of theirs. They said, ‘O Moses, make for us a god like the gods they have.’ He said, ‘You are indeed an ignorant people.’) (7:138). They have not ceased to be upon corporealism (التجسيم) and anthropomorphism (التشبيه) until today, as indicated by their books circulating among them.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي لا أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِي وَأَخِي فَافْرُقْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ — He said, ‘My Lord! I have no control over [anyone] except myself and my brother, so make a separation between us and the transgressing people.’ The context indicates that his saying: إِنِّي لا أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِي وَأَخِي (I have no control over [anyone] except myself and my brother) is an allusion (كناية) to the denial of the power to compel anyone other than himself and his brother to what he brought them of the call. For it was only within his power to compel himself to carry out what he called to, and to compel his brother Aaron—who was a sent prophet and his successor during his life—not to rebel against the command of Allah, the Glorified. Or the meaning is that he has no power except over himself, and his brother has no power except likewise.

His intent was not to deny absolute power, even from the perspective of the response of the respondent due to faith and the like, such that it would contradict the apparent context of the verse that the two men were from those who fear, and others besides them were believers in him responding to his call. For he did not mention among those he "controls" even his family and his brother's family, despite the fact that it is apparent they would not have disobeyed his commands.

This is because the situation only necessitates that. He called them to a divinely ordained matter, conveyed it, and left them with no excuse, but the Israelite community rejected his call in the most hideous and ugliest manner. The requirement of this state was for him to say: "My Lord, I have conveyed and left no excuse, and I have no power in establishing Your command except over myself, and likewise my brother. We have performed what was incumbent upon us of the obligatory duty, but the people have faced us with the strongest refusal, and we are now in despair of them, and the path is cut off. So, You untie this knot and prepare, by Your Lordship, the way to attaining what You promised them of the completion of the blessing, making them heirs to the land, and making them successors therein; and judge and separate between us and these transgressors."

This instance—unlike all the instances in which they disobeyed Moses' command, such as the matter of the vision, the worship of the calf, entering the gate, the saying of hittah, and others—is characterized by the explicit rejection by the Israelite community of his command without any gentleness or compliance. Had Moses left them to their state and overlooked his command, the call would have been nullified from its root, no command or prohibition would have been effective for him after that, and the pillars of the unity he had created among them would have crumbled.

By this explanation, it becomes clear, firstly: that the requirement of this state was for Moses (peace be upon him) to address in his complaint to his Lord the state of himself and his brother, as they were the conveyers from Allah the Exalted, and not to address the state of other believers, even if they were not rebellious. For they have no role in the conveyance (التبليغ) and the call (الدعوة), and the situation only necessitates addressing the state of the conveyer of the ruling, not the one who acts upon it, takes it, and responds to it.

Secondly: the situation necessitated Moses' (peace be upon him) return to his Lord with a complaint, which is in reality a seeking of aid in carrying out the divine command.

Thirdly: his saying: وَأَخِي (and my brother) is conjoined to the ya in his saying: إِنِّي (I), and the meaning is: "and my brother is like me, he controls only himself," not conjoined to his saying: نَفْسِي (myself). For that is contrary to what the context necessitates, even if the meaning is correct under all suppositions; for just as Moses and Aaron each controlled from himself obedience and compliance, Moses controlled from Aaron's self obedience due to his being his successor during his life, and likewise they both controlled from those who were sincere to Allah among the believers hearing and obedience.

Fourthly: his saying: فَافْرُقْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ (so make a separation between us and the transgressing people) is not a prayer from him against the Children of Israel for a decisive judgment followed by the descent of punishment upon them, or for a separation between the two of them and the people by removing the two of them from among them or by taking their souls. For he (peace be upon him) was calling them to what Allah had ordained for them of the completion of the blessing, and he was the one through whose hand Allah had ordained the favor upon the Children of Israel by saving them and making them successors in the land, as He, the Exalted, said: وَنُرِيدُ أَنْ نَمُنَّ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَنَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً وَنَجْعَلَهُمُ الْوارِثِينَ (And We desired to show favour to those who were abased in the land, and to make them imams, and to make them the heirs) (28:5).

The Children of Israel knew that from him, as is derived from their saying as Allah narrated: قالُوا أُوذِينا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَأْتِيَنا وَمِنْ بَعْدِ ما جِئْتَنا (They said, ‘We were tortured before you came to us and after you came to us.’) (7:129).

This is also evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تَأْسَ عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ (So do not grieve for the transgressing people), for it reveals that Moses (peace be upon him) was compassionate toward them regarding the descent of divine wrath, and it was expected that he would grieve because of the descent of the affliction of the wandering (التيه) upon them.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ فَإِنَّها مُحَرَّمَةٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً يَتِيهُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَلا تَأْسَ عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ — He said, ‘It shall be forbidden them for forty years: they shall wander about in the earth. So do not grieve for the transgressing people.’ The pronoun in His saying: فَإِنَّها (it) refers to the Holy Land. What is meant by "forbidden" (التحريم) is the ontological prohibition (التحريم التكويني), which is the decree (القضاء). The wandering (التيه) is bewilderment. The lam in "the earth" (الأرض) is for specific reference (للعهد). His saying: فَلا تَأْسَ (So do not grieve) is a prohibition against al-asa (الأسى), which is grief. Allah the Exalted has confirmed the saying of Moses (peace be upon him) where he described them in his prayer as "transgressors" (الفاسقين).

The meaning is: that the Holy Land—meaning its entry and possession—is forbidden to them, meaning We have decreed that they shall not be granted the success to enter it for forty years, during which they shall travel in the earth bewildered; they are neither city-dwellers who find rest in one of the cities, nor are they desert-dwellers who live the life of tribes and nomads. So do not grieve for the transgressing people because of the descent of this affliction upon them, for they are transgressors who do not deserve to be grieved for when they are made to taste the consequence of their affair.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: "Among the Children of Israel, if one of them had a servant, a beast, and a wife, he was recorded as a king."

In the same source: Abu Dawud narrated in his Marasil from Zayd ibn Aslam: regarding His saying: وَجَعَلَكُمْ مُلُوكاً (and made you kings), he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "A wife, a dwelling, and a servant."

I say: Other narrations besides these two have been narrated with this meaning, except that the verse in its context does not suit this interpretation. For even if it were possible that it was the custom of the Children of Israel to call everyone who had a house, a wife, and a servant a "king" or to record him as a "king," it is self-evident that they were not all—even the servants—of this description, possessing houses, wives, and servants. Those among them who were of this description were only some of them, and other nations and generations are similar to them in that. Taking houses, wives, and servants is a current custom in all nations; no nation is devoid of that. If that were the case, it would not be a matter specific to the Children of Israel such that Allah would favor them in His speech by saying that He made them kings, while the verse is in the station of bestowing favor.

Perhaps the realization of this necessitated what occurred in some narrations, such as from Qatadah: that they were the first to possess servants, but history does not confirm this.

In Amali al-Mufid, with its chain of narration from Abu Hamzah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) who said: "When Moses reached the Holy Land with them, he said to them: ادْخُلُوا الْأَرْضَ الْمُقَدَّسَةَ الَّتِي كَتَبَ اللهُ لَكُمْ وَلا تَرْتَدُّوا عَلى أَدْبارِكُمْ فَتَنْقَلِبُوا خاسِرِينَ — and Allah had ordained it for them — قالُوا يا مُوسى إِنَّ فِيها قَوْماً جَبَّارِينَ وَإِنَّا لَنْ نَدْخُلَها حَتَّى يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها فَإِنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها فَإِنَّا داخِلُونَ قالَ رَجُلانِ مِنَ الَّذِينَ يَخافُونَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمَا ادْخُلُوا عَلَيْهِمُ الْبابَ فَإِذا دَخَلْتُمُوهُ فَإِنَّكُمْ غالِبُونَ وَعَلَى اللهِ فَتَوَكَّلُوا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ قالُوا يا مُوسى إِنَّا لَنْ نَدْخُلَها أَبَداً ما دامُوا فِيها فَاذْهَبْ أَنْتَ وَرَبُّكَ فَقاتِلا إِنَّا هاهُنا قاعِدُونَ قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي لا أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِي وَأَخِي فَافْرُقْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ — so when they refused to enter it, Allah forbade it to them — so they wandered in four leagues (فراسخ) for forty years, wandering in the earth — فَلا تَأْسَ عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ."

Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "When evening came, their caller would cry out: 'Departure!' So they would depart with chanting and urging until when they reached the time before dawn — Allah commanded the earth and it turned with them, so they would find themselves in their dwelling — from which they had departed. They would say: 'You have missed the way!' So they remained in this for forty years, and the manna and quails descended upon them until they all perished except two men: Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh, and their children — and they were wandering in about four leagues — and when they wanted to depart — their clothes and their sandals would harden upon them."

He said: "And there was a stone with them — when they alighted, Moses would strike it with his staff — and twelve springs would gush from it — a spring for each tribe. When they departed, the water would return to the stone — and the stone would be placed upon the beast." (The narration).

I say: The narrations regarding meanings close to these are many from the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis. His saying in the narration: "And Abu Abdillah said (etc.)" is another narration. These narrations, although they contain matters regarding the meaning of the wandering (التيه) and other things for which no support is found in His speech, the Exalted, nevertheless do not contain anything that contradicts the Book. The affair of the Children of Israel in the time of Moses (peace be upon him) was strange, with supernatural events (خوارق العادة) surrounding their lives from every side, so there is no harm in their wandering being in this manner mentioned in the narrations.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Mus'adah ibn Sadaqah from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): that he was asked about the saying: ادْخُلُوا الْأَرْضَ الْمُقَدَّسَةَ الَّتِي كَتَبَ اللهُ لَكُمْ (Enter the Holy Land which Allah has ordained for you). He said: "He ordained it for them, then He erased it, then He ordained it for their children and they entered it — and Allah erases what He wills and establishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book."

I say: This meaning has also been narrated from Isma'il al-Ju'fi from him (peace be upon him), and from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him). He (peace be upon him) compared the "ordaining" (الكتابة) with respect to those who heard Moses' address to enter the land, and with respect to those who actually entered it, thus resulting in al-bada' (البداء) — the manifestation of a new divine decree after a previous one — specifically regarding those for whom it was ordained. This does not contradict the apparent context of the verse that those for whom it was ordained are the ones who entered, and they were only forbidden entry for forty years and were granted it after that. For the address in the verse is directed, according to the meaning, to the Israelite community, so those for whom entry was ordained are unified with those who entered by virtue of them all being one nation for whom entry was ordained in general, then they were forbidden entry for a period and granted it after that. There is no al-bada' according to this, even if with respect to specific individuals there is al-bada'.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abd al-Rahman ibn Yazid from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "David the Prophet died on a Saturday suddenly — so the birds shaded him with their wings. And Moses, the one spoken to by Allah (كليم الله), died in the wandering (التيه) — so a caller cried out from the heaven: 'Moses has died — and what soul does not die?'"

Ayahs

۞ وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ٱبْنَىْ ءَادَمَ بِٱلْحَقِّ إِذْ قَرَّبَا قُرْبَانًا فَتُقُبِّلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِمَا وَلَمْ يُتَقَبَّلْ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخَرِ قَالَ لَأَقْتُلَنَّكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنَّمَا يَتَقَبَّلُ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٢٧)
Relate to them truly the account of Adam’s two sons. When the two of them offered an offering, it was accepted from one of them and not accepted from the other. [One of them] said, ‘Surely I will kill you.’ [The other one] said, ‘Allah accepts only from the Godwary. (27)
لَئِنۢ بَسَطتَ إِلَىَّ يَدَكَ لِتَقْتُلَنِى مَآ أَنَا۠ بِبَاسِطٍ يَدِىَ إِلَيْكَ لِأَقْتُلَكَ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٨)
Even if you extend your hand toward me to kill me, I will not extend my hand toward you to kill you. Indeed, I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (28)
إِنِّىٓ أُرِيدُ أَن تَبُوٓأَ بِإِثْمِى وَإِثْمِكَ فَتَكُونَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٩)
I desire that you earn [the burden of] my sin and your sin, to become one of the inmates of the Fire, and such is the requital of the wrongdoers.’ (29)
فَطَوَّعَتْ لَهُۥ نَفْسُهُۥ قَتْلَ أَخِيهِ فَقَتَلَهُۥ فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٣٠)
So his soul prompted him to kill his brother, and he killed him, and thus became one of the losers. (30)
فَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ غُرَابًا يَبْحَثُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُرِيَهُۥ كَيْفَ يُوَٰرِى سَوْءَةَ أَخِيهِ ۚ قَالَ يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰٓ أَعَجَزْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِثْلَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْغُرَابِ فَأُوَٰرِىَ سَوْءَةَ أَخِى ۖ فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ ٱلنَّـٰدِمِينَ (٣١)
Then Allah sent a crow, exploring in the ground, to show him how to bury the corpse of his brother. He said, ‘Woe to me! Am I unable to be [even] like this crow and bury my brother’s corpse?’ Thus he became regretful. (31)
مِنْ أَجْلِ ذَٰلِكَ كَتَبْنَا عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنَّهُۥ مَن قَتَلَ نَفْسًۢا بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسَادٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّمَا قَتَلَ ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا وَمَنْ أَحْيَاهَا فَكَأَنَّمَآ أَحْيَا ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ إِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لَمُسْرِفُونَ (٣٢)
That is why We decreed for the Children of Israel that whoever kills a soul, without [its being guilty of] manslaughter or corruption on the earth, is as though he had killed all mankind, and whoever saves a life is as though he had saved all mankind. Our apostles certainly brought them manifest signs, yet even after that, many of them commit excesses on the earth. (32)

(Commentary)

The verses recount the story of the two sons of Adam, demonstrating how envy (الحسد) can drive a human being to the point of unjustly murdering his brother, thereby becoming one of the losers (الخاسرين) and feeling a regret (ندامة) that yields no benefit. In this sense, the passage is connected to the preceding discourse regarding the Children of Israel and their disdain for believing in the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); for indeed, their refusal to accept the true call was born of nothing but envy and transgression (بغيا). Such is the nature of envy: it incites a person to kill his brother and then plunges him into a regret and sorrow from which there is no escape. Thus, they should take heed from this story and not persist so stubbornly in their envy and subsequent disbelief.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ابْنَيْ آدَمَ بِالْحَقِّ — "Relate to them truly the account of Adam’s two sons" (5:27). Recitation (التلاوة) is derived from "following" (التلو), and it refers to reading; it is so named because the one reciting an account (النبأ) brings forth its parts one following the other. An account (النبأ) is news that possesses utility and benefit. An offering (القربان) is that by which one seeks nearness (يتقرب به) to Allah, the Glorified, or to another; it is originally a verbal noun (مصدر) that is neither dualized nor pluralized. Acceptance (التقبل) is receiving something with increased care and attention toward the accepted object. The pronoun in His saying عَلَيْهِمْ ("to them") refers to the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), as they are the intended audience in the flow of the discourse.

The one named "Adam" here is the same Adam whom the Quran mentions as the father of humanity. Some exegetes have claimed he was a man from the Children of Israel whose two sons disputed over an offering they had presented, leading one to kill the other—Cain (قابيل) killing Abel (هابيل). They argue this is why Allah said after recounting the story: مِنْ أَجْلِ ذلِكَ كَتَبْنا عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ — "That is why We decreed for the Children of Israel..." (5:32).

This view is invalid for several reasons. First, the Quran mentions no one by the name of Adam except the one it identifies as the father of humanity. Had the verse intended someone else, it would have been necessary to provide a contextual clue (قرينة) to prevent the story from becoming ambiguous.

Second, some specific details of the story, such as His saying: فَبَعَثَ اللهُ غُراباً — "Then Allah sent a crow" (5:31), only suit the initial state of humanity, when man lived with a primitive intellect (سذاجة من الفكر) and simple perception, acquiring information through his innate capacity (استعداده الجبلي) by accumulating experiences from particular events (الحوادث الجزئية) occurring one after another. The verse clearly indicates that the killer did not know that a dead body could be hidden by burying it in the earth. This characteristic fits the state of the son of Adam, the father of humanity, not a man from the Children of Israel, who were a people of civilization and urbanity (أهل حضارة ومدنية) according to their national history; such matters would certainly not have been hidden from any of them.

Third, regarding their argument that His saying after the completion of the story—مِنْ أَجْلِ ذلِكَ كَتَبْنا عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ ("That is why We decreed for the Children of Israel")—serves as a response to an objection raised against the verse. The objection is: why was this decree specific to the Children of Israel when the logic of the story—which is what Allah decreed—applies to all humanity: that whoever kills a soul, it is as if he has killed all of mankind, and whoever saves a soul, it is as if he has saved all of mankind?

The proponent of that view answered by saying that the killer and the killed were not the sons of Adam, the father of humanity, such that their story would represent one of the primary events (الحوادث الأولية) of the human species and serve as a lesson for all who came after them. Rather, they were the sons of a man from the Children of Israel, and their account was part of their specific national history; thus, it was taken as a written lesson specifically for the Children of Israel.

However, this does not resolve the core of the problem. The question remains: the fact that killing one is like killing all, and saving one is like saving all, is a meaning linked to every murder occurring within this species, without being specific to one group over another. Countless murders occurred before the Children of Israel and before this specific murder referred to; why then was it predicated on a specific murder and decreed for a specific people?

Furthermore, had the matter been as they claim, it would have been more appropriate to say: "Whoever among you kills a soul..." so as to be specific to them. Then the question would return regarding this specification, despite its inherent lack of soundness.

The answer to the original problem is that what is contained in His saying: أَنَّهُ مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْساً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ — "that whoever kills a soul, without [its being guilty of] manslaughter..." (5:32) is a profound wisdom (حكمة بالغة) and not merely a legislated ruling (حكم مشرع). The meaning of "decreeing it upon them" is the exposition of this wisdom to them, despite its benefit being general to them and others—much like the wisdoms and exhortations (المواعظ) explained in the Quran for the community of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while their benefit is not restricted to them. It was mentioned in the verse that He explained it to them because the verses are directed toward their admonition, awakening, and rebuke for their envy of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and their persistence in enmity, inciting sedition, and initiating wars against the Muslims. For this reason, He followed His saying مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْساً ("whoever kills a soul") with His saying: وَلَقَدْ جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنا بِالْبَيِّناتِ ثُمَّ إِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنْهُمْ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ فِي الْأَرْضِ لَمُسْرِفُونَ — "Certainly Our apostles came to them with clear proofs, then indeed many of them, after that, are transgressors on the earth" (5:32).

Moreover, the original story as they recounted it has no basis in narration (رواية) or history.

Thus, it becomes clear that His saying: نَبَأَ ابْنَيْ آدَمَ بِالْحَقِّ ("the account of Adam’s two sons truly") refers to the story of the two sons of Adam, the father of humanity. Restricting the speech with the phrase بِالْحَقِّ ("truly" or "with the truth")—which relates either to the "account" or to the command "relate"—is not devoid of an indication that the common version of the account circulating among them was not free from distortion (تحريف) or omission. This is indeed the case, as the story exists in the fourth chapter of Genesis in the Torah, yet it contains no mention of the sending of the crow and its scratching in the earth. Furthermore, that version of the story explicitly anthropomorphizes the Lord—exalted is He far above such things.

His saying: إِذْ قَرَّبا قُرْباناً فَتُقُبِّلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِما وَلَمْ يُتَقَبَّلْ مِنَ الْآخَرِ — "when they both offered an offering, and it was accepted from one of them, and not accepted from the other" (5:27). The context suggests that each of them presented something to the Lord to seek nearness. The word "offering" (قربان) was not dualized because it is originally a verbal noun that does not take dual or plural forms.

His saying: قالَ لَأَقْتُلَنَّكَ قالَ إِنَّما يَتَقَبَّلُ اللهُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ — "One of them said, ‘I will surely kill you.’ The other said, ‘Allah accepts only from the God-conscious’" (5:27). The first speaker is the killer and the second is the one killed. The context indicates they both knew that the offering of one had been accepted and the other's had not. As for how they knew this or by what means they inferred it, the verse is silent.

However, it is mentioned elsewhere in the speech of the Exalted that it was customary among previous nations, or specifically among the Children of Israel, for the acceptance of an offering to be manifested by fire consuming it. The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ عَهِدَ إِلَيْنا أَلَّا نُؤْمِنَ لِرَسُولٍ حَتَّى يَأْتِيَنا بِقُرْبانٍ تَأْكُلُهُ النَّارُ قُلْ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِي بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَبِالَّذِي قُلْتُمْ فَلِمَ قَتَلْتُمُوهُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "Those who say, ‘Allah has pledged us not to believe in any apostle unless he brings us an offering consumed by fire.’ Say, ‘Apostles before me certainly brought you manifest signs and what you speak of. Then why did you kill them, if you are truthful?’" (3:183). The concept of an offering (القربان) is well-known among the People of the Book to this day.
