Although the Quran does not fully confirm the Torah circulating among them today, nor the four Gospels attributed to Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John—which are other than the Gospel the Quran mentions as being revealed to the Messiah himself—they nevertheless all confirm this meaning, as will be pointed out, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلْيَحْكُمْ أَهْلُ الْإِنجِيلِ بِمَا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ فِيهِ "Let the people of the Gospel judge by what Allah has sent down in it" (to the end of the verse). In it was revealed the confirmation of the Torah in its laws, except for the excepted abrogated rulings mentioned in the Gospel revealed to Jesus (عليه‌السلام). Since the Gospel confirmed the Torah in what it legislated and made lawful some of what was forbidden in it, acting upon what is in the Torah—regarding the prohibitions not made lawful by the Gospel—is acting upon what Allah revealed in the Gospel. This is evident.

From here, the weakness of the argument used by some commentators becomes clear—that the Gospel contains detailed laws just as the Torah did. The reason for the weakness is apparent.

As for His saying: وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْفَاسِقُونَ "Those who do not judge by what Allah has sent down—it is they who are the transgressors (الفاسقون)." This is an intensification of the command indicated by His saying: "Let... judge." Allah, the Glorified, repeated this statement for emphasis three times: twice regarding the Jews and once regarding the Christians, with slight variation. He said: "Those who do not judge by what Allah has sent down—it is they who are the faithless... it is they who are the wrongdoers... it is they who are the transgressors." Thus, He recorded against them unfaith, wrongdoing, and transgression.

Perhaps the reason for mentioning transgression (الفسق) when addressing what pertains to the Christians, and unfaith (الكفر) and wrongdoing (الظلم) in what pertains to the Jews, is that the Christians replaced monotheism with the Trinity and rejected the rulings of the Torah by Paul taking the religion of the Messiah as an independent religion, separate from the religion of Moses, in which rulings were lifted through atonement (التفدية). Thus, the Christians exited from monotheism and its law through interpretation (تأول), so they "transgressed" from the true religion of Allah. الفسق (transgression) is the exiting of a thing from its proper place, like the exiting of the date's core from its skin.

As for the Jews, the matter was not ambiguous to them regarding what they had of the religion of Moses (عليه‌السلام); rather, they rejected the rulings and knowledge of which they had certain knowledge, which is unfaith in Allah's signs and wrongdoing against them.

The three verses—namely His saying: "Those who do not judge by what Allah has sent down—it is they who are the faithless... it is they who are the wrongdoers... it is they who are the transgressors"—are absolute verses not exclusive to one people over another, even if they apply to the People of the Book in this context.

Commentators have differed regarding the meaning of the "unfaith" of one who does not judge by what Allah has revealed—such as a judge who judges by other than what Allah has revealed, a ruler who rules contrary to what Allah has revealed, or an innovator (المبتدع) who establishes a practice other than the Sunnah. This is a jurisprudential (فقهية) issue in which the truth is that opposing a legal ruling or any established matter in religion—while knowing its establishment and rejecting it—necessitates unfaith (الكفر). In the case of knowing its establishment but not rejecting it, it necessitates transgression (الفسق). In the case of not knowing its establishment while rejecting it, it necessitates neither unfaith nor transgression, as it is a deficiency for which one is excused, unless one was negligent in any of its preliminaries. One should refer to the books of jurisprudence regarding this.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنزَلْنَا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ وَمُهَيْمِنًا عَلَيْهِ "We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book and as a guardian (مهيمنا) over it." The هيمنة (guardianship/authority) of one thing over another—as concluded from its meaning—is for a thing to have authority over another in its preservation, monitoring, and various types of disposal (التصرف). This is the state of the Quran, which Allah described as an "explanation of all things" in relation to the heavenly Books before it: it preserves from them the established, unchanging principles and abrogates from them the branches (الفروع) that ought to be abrogated—those subject to change and substitution—so that it suits the state of man according to his traversal of the path of progress and perfection over time. The Exalted said: إِنَّ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَهْدِي لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ "Indeed this Quran guides to that which is most upright" (17:9). And He said: مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِّنْهَا أَوْ مِثْلِهَا "For any verse We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or its like" (2:106). And He said: الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوبًا عِندَهُمْ فِي التَّوْرَاةِ وَالْإِنجِيلِ يَأْمُرُهُم بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَاهُمْ عَنِ الْمُنكَرِ وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ الطَّيِّبَاتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْخَبَائِثَ وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَالْأَغْلَالَ الَّتِي كَانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِهِ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنزِلَ مَعَهُ ۙ أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ "—those who follow the Apostle, the untaught prophet, whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel, who bids them to do what is right and forbids them from what is wrong, makes lawful to them all the good things and forbids them from all vicious things, and relieves them of their burdens and the shackles that were upon them—those who believe in him, honour him, and help him and follow the light that has been sent down with him, they are the felicitous" (7:157).

Thus, this phrase—namely His saying: "and as a guardian over it"—completes His saying: "confirming what was before it of the Book" as an explanatory completion. For without it, one might imagine from the Quran's confirmation of the Torah and the Gospel that it confirms the laws and rulings within them as a confirmation of retention without change or substitution. However, describing it as a "guardian" (مهيمنا) explains that its confirmation of them is a confirmation that they are true knowledge and laws from Allah, and it is for Allah to dispose of them as He wills through abrogation and completion, as indicated by His saying at the end: وَلَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَلَٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِي مَا آتَاكُمْ "Had Allah wished, He would have made you a single community, but [He didn’t] that He might test you in what He has given you."

So the meaning of "confirming what was before it" is the affirmation of the knowledge and rulings within them in a manner that suits the state of this nation. This is not contradicted by the abrogation, completion, and addition that have occurred, just as the Messiah (عليه‌السلام) or his Gospel was a confirmer of the Torah while making lawful some of the prohibitions within it, as Allah narrated from him in His saying: وَمُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ مِنَ التَّوْرَاةِ وَلِأُحِلَّ لَكُم بَعْضَ الَّذِي حُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ "and to confirm what was before me of the Torah, and to make lawful to you some of what had been forbidden to you" (3:50).

His saying, the Exalted: فَاحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِمَا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَاءَهُمْ عَمَّا جَاءَكَ مِنَ الْحَقِّ "So judge between them by what Allah has sent down, and do not follow their desires against the truth that has come to you." That is, since the law revealed to you and deposited in the Book is the truth—and it is the truth in what it agrees with of the Books before it and the truth in what it differs from them by virtue of its guardianship over them—you have no choice but to judge between the People of the Book—as the apparent meaning of the previous verses supports—or between people—as the subsequent verses support—by what Allah has revealed to you. Do not follow their desires by turning away and deviating from the truth that has come to you.

From here, it appears permissible that the intended meaning of "judge between them" is judging between the People of the Book or judging between people. However, the first meaning is weakened by its need for an estimation, such as our saying: "judge between them if you judge," for Allah, the Glorified, did not make judging between them obligatory upon him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), but rather gave him the choice between judging and disregarding by His saying: "So if they come to you, judge between them, or disregard them" (the verse). Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, mentioned the hypocrites along with the Jews at the beginning of the verses, so there is no reason to restrict the return of the pronoun to the Jews alone since they were mentioned first, and others were mentioned with them. Thus, it is more appropriate for the pronoun to refer to "people" due to the indication of the context.

It also appears that His saying: "against (عن) what has come to you" is connected to His saying: "and do not follow," by imbuing it with the meaning of deviation or turning away.

His saying, the Exalted: لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنَا مِنكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَمِنْهَاجًا "For each [community] among you We had appointed a code [of law] (شرعة) and a path (منهاجا)." Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: الشرع (the law) is the laying out of a clear path. It is said: "I laid out (shara'tu) a path for him." Al-shar' is a verbal noun, then it was made a name for the laid-out path, so it was called shar', shara', and shari'ah. This was used metaphorically for the divine way. He said: "a code (shir'ah) and a path (minhaj)." (Until he said): Some said: the shari'ah was called shari'ah by way of comparison to the shari'ah (watering place) of water. (End of quote).

Perhaps shari'ah in the second sense is taken from the first sense due to the clarity of the path to water for them because of frequent coming and going. He said: النهج (the path) is the clear way. Nahaja al-amr and anhaja mean it became clear. From it come manhaj al-tariq and minhajuhu.

(A Discourse on the Meaning of Shari'ah)

(And the Difference Between it, Din, and Millah in the Quranic Usage)

The meaning of shari'ah (law), as you have learned, is the way (الطريقة). Din (religion) and likewise millah (creed/community) are also an adopted way. However, it is apparent from the Quran that it uses shari'ah in a more specific sense than din, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِندَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلَامُ "Indeed, with Allah religion (الدين) is submission (الإسلام)" (3:19), and His saying: وَمَن يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ الْإِسْلَامِ دِينًا فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ وَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ "Should anyone follow a religion other than submission, it shall never be accepted from him, and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter" (3:85), when these are joined with His saying: "For each [community] among you We had appointed a code and a path" (the verse), and His saying: ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاكَ عَلَىٰ شَرِيعَةٍ مِّنَ الْأَمْرِ فَاتَّبِعْهَا "Then We set you on a code (شريعة) of the [divine] command; so follow it" (45:18).

Thus, it is as if shari'ah is the way paved for one of the nations or for one of the prophets who were sent with it—such as the law of Noah, the law of Abraham, the law of Moses, the law of Jesus, and the law of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Din is the Sunnah and the divine way universal to all nations. Thus, shari'ah accepts abrogation, whereas din in its broad sense does not.

There is another difference: din is attributed to an individual and a group regardless of who they are, but shari'ah is not attributed to an individual unless he is its founder or the one in charge of its affair. One says: "the religion of the Muslims" and "the religion of the Jews and their law (shari'atuhum)." One says: "the religion of Allah and His law," and "the religion of Muhammad and his law." One says: "the religion of Zayd and 'Amr," but one does not say: "the law (shari'ah) of Zayd and 'Amr." Perhaps this is because the word shari'ah alludes to the active meaning, which is the paving and establishing of the path. It is permissible to say: "the way paved by Allah" or "the way paved for the Prophet or for such-and-such a nation," but not: "the way paved for Zayd," as he has no exclusivity in it.

In any case, what is gathered from this is that shari'ah is more specific in meaning than din. As for His saying, the Exalted: شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus" (42:13), it does not contradict this. The verse only indicates that the law of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prescribed for his nation is the sum of Allah's (the Glorified) testaments to Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus, in addition to what He revealed to Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وعليهم). This is a metaphor either for Islam being a compendium of the merits of all previous laws and more, or for all laws having a single reality in their essence (اللب), even if they differ according to the differences in the nations' readiness, as suggested or indicated by His saying after it: أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ "‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided in it’" (42:13).

Thus, the relation of specific laws to the religion—which is one, while laws abrogate one another—is like the relation of particular rulings in Islam, which contain the abrogating (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ), to the origin of the religion. Allah, the Glorified, has not made His servants worship except through one religion, which is submission (الإسلام) to Him. However, He led them to attain that through different paths and established for them various traditions according to the differences and varieties of their readiness. These are the laws of Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وعليهم). Similarly, He may abrogate some rulings with others within a single law due to the expiration of the interest (مصلحة) of the abrogated ruling and the appearance of the interest of the abrogating ruling—such as the abrogation of eternal imprisonment for the adultery of women with flogging and stoning, and so on. This is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "Had Allah wished, He would have made you a single community, but [He didn’t] that He might test you in what He has given you" (the verse).

As for al-millah (the creed/community), it is as if it refers to the vital tradition (السنة الحيوية) followed among people. It carried the meaning of dictation (الإملال) and transcription (الإملاء), thus being the way taken from another. Its original meaning is not perfectly clear, but it most closely resembles being synonymous with shari'ah, in the sense that millah, like shari'ah, is a specific way, unlike din. However, there is a difference between them in that shari'ah is used with the consideration that it is a path paved by Allah the Exalted for people to walk toward Him, while millah is only applied to it because it is taken from another through practical following (الاتباع العملي). Perhaps for this reason, it is not attributed to Allah, the Glorified, as din and shari'ah are; one says: "the religion of Allah" and "the law of Allah," but one does not say: "the millah of Allah."

Rather, it is attributed to the Prophet, for example, insofar as it is his biography and tradition, or to the nation insofar as they are followers and practitioners of it. The Exalted said: مِلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ "the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists" (2:135). And the Exalted said, narrating from Joseph (عليه‌السلام): إِنِّي تَرَكْتُ مِلَّةَ قَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَهُم بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ كَافِرُونَ * وَاتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ آبَائِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ "‘Indeed I have left the creed of a people who do not believe in Allah and who are faithless with respect to the Hereafter. I follow the creed of my fathers, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob’" (12:37-38). And the Exalted said, narrating from the disbelievers in their words to their prophets: لَنُخْرِجَنَّكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِنَا أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنَا "‘We will surely expel you from our land, or you shall return to our creed’" (14:13).

In summary, din in the Quranic usage is more general than shari'ah and millah, and the latter two are like synonyms with some difference in terms of linguistic nuance.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَلَٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِي مَا آتَاكُمْ "Had Allah wished, He would have made you a single community, but [He didn’t] that He might test you in what He has given you." This is an explanation of the reason for the difference in laws. The intent of making them a single community is not the ontological (تكويني) making in the sense of a single species, for people are individuals of a single species living on a single pattern, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْلَا أَن يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً لَّجَعَلْنَا لِمَن يَكْفُرُ بِالرَّحْمَنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفًا مِّن فِضَّةٍ وَمَعَارِجَ عَلَيْهَا يَظْهَرُونَ "Were it not that mankind would have become a single community [of faithless people], We would have surely provided for those who are faithless to the All-beneficent, silver roofs for their houses and [silver] stairs by which they ascend" (43:33).

Rather, the intent is taking them, by way of convention (الاعتبار), as a single community on a single level of readiness and preparation, such that a single law would be legislated for them due to the proximity of their observed degrees. So His saying: "Had Allah wished, He would have made you a single community" is like placing the cause of the condition in the position of the condition itself, so that by its presence, the meaning of the recompense (الجزاء)—namely His saying: "but [He didn’t] that He might test you in what He has given you"—becomes clear. That is, to test you in what He has granted you and bestowed upon you. Inevitably, these gifts referred to in the verse differ among nations. These are not differences according to dwellings, languages, and colors, for Allah never legislated two or more laws in a single time. Rather, they are differences according to the passage of time and the elevation of man in the stages of readiness and preparation. Divine obligations and legislated rulings are nothing but a divine trial for man in various positions of life. If you wish, say: a bringing of him from potentiality (القوة) to actuality (الفعل) in the two aspects of felicity and wretchedness. If you wish, say: a distinction of the party of the All-Beneficent and His servants from the party of Satan. The expression for this has varied in the Mighty Book, but the outcome of all is a single meaning. The Exalted said, following the path of trial: وَتِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُدَاوِلُهَا بَيْنَ النَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنكُمْ شُهَدَاءَ ۗ وَاللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ الظَّالِمِينَ * وَلِيُمَحِّصَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَيَمْحَقَ الْكَافِرِينَ * أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ جَاهَدُوا مِنكُمْ وَيَعْلَمَ الصَّابِرِينَ "We make such vicissitudes rotate among mankind, so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take witnesses from among you, and Allah does not like the wrongdoers. And so that Allah may purge those who have faith and wipe out the faithless. Do you suppose that you will enter paradise while Allah has not yet ascertained those of you who have waged jihad and ascertained the steadfast?" (3:140-142), among other verses.

He said, following the second path: فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ * وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ "Then if there comes to you any guidance from Me, whoever follows My guidance shall not go astray, nor be wretched. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection" (20:123-124).

He said, following the third path: وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي خَالِقٌ بَشَرًا (until He said) قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَا أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ * إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ * قَالَ هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ * إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ * وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ "When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to create a human...’ He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them all, except Your exclusive servants among them.’ He said, ‘This is a straight path [leading] to Me. Indeed as for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you. Indeed hell is the tryst of them all’" (15:28-43), among other verses.

In short, since the divine gifts to the human species in terms of readiness and preparation were different according to the differences of times, and the law and divine tradition required to be implemented among them to complete the felicity of their lives—which are the divine trials—inevitably differed according to the differences in the levels and varieties of readiness, this resulted in the necessity of the difference in laws. Therefore, the Exalted justified what He mentioned regarding the difference in code and path by stating that His will was attached to your trial and testing in what He bestowed upon you, saying: "For each [community] among you We had appointed a code and a path. Had Allah wished, He would have made you a single community, but [He didn’t] that He might test you in what He has given you."

So the meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: For each nation among you, We have appointed (a legislative appointment) a code and a path. Had Allah wished, He would have taken you as a single community and legislated a single law for you. However, He appointed different laws for you to test you in the various blessings He gave you. The difference in blessings necessitated a difference in the trial, which is the title of the appointed obligations and rulings; thus, difference was inevitably cast between the laws.

These different nations are the nations of Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وعليهم), as indicated by what Allah bestows as a favor upon this nation by His saying: شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus" (42:13).

His saying, the Exalted: فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ إِلَى اللهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً — "So vie with one another in good deeds. To Allah shall be the return of you all" (5:48). Vying (الاستباق) means to take the lead, and the return (المرجع) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) meaning "returning." This speech branches out from His saying: لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنا مِنْكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَمِنْهاجاً — "For each [community] among you We had appointed a code [of law] and a path" (5:48), by way of its necessary implication. That is: We have made this true law, which is the guardian (المهيمنة) over all other laws, a law for you. In it lies your good and your welfare inevitably; therefore, vie with one another in good deeds (الخيرات), which are the rulings and the religious obligations (التكاليف). Do not occupy yourselves with the matter of these differences that exist between you and others, for your return altogether is to your Lord, the Exalted; He will then inform you of that in which you used to differ, and He will judge between you with a decisive judgment and decree with a just decree.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنِ احْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ — "And judge between them by what Allah has sent down, and do not follow [their desires]" (5:49). This opening is identical to what appeared in the previous verse: أَنِ احْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ — "judge between them by what Allah has sent down, and do not follow their desires" (5:48). However, they differ in what branches out from each of them. From this, it is understood that the repetition is for the sake of securing this specific benefit. The first verse commands judging by what Allah has sent down and warns against following the desires of the people because that which Allah sent down is the law (الشريعة) ordained for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and for his community; thus, it is obligatory upon them to vie with one another in these good deeds. The second verse commands judging by what Allah has sent down and warns against following the desires of the people, explaining that their turning away—if they turn away from what Allah has sent down—is indicative of a divine misguidance (إضلال إلهي) toward them due to their defiance (الفسق). Indeed, Allah the Exalted has said: يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ — "He misleads many thereby, and guides many thereby; and He misleads no one thereby except the transgressors" (2:26).

Thus, it is concluded from what has preceded that this verse serves as an exposition (البيان) for some of the meanings contained in the previous verse that required clarification. This is that the turning away of the followers of desires from following what Allah has sent down with the truth is only because they are defiant (فاسقين). Allah has willed to afflict them for some of their sins that necessitated their defiance. This "afflicting" (الإصابة) is, outwardly, the misguidance (الإضلال). Thus, His saying: وَأَنِ احْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ — "And judge between them by what Allah has sent down" is a conjunction (عطف) joined to "the Book" (الكتاب) in His saying: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ — "We have sent down to you the Book" (5:48), as has been suggested. In that case, it is more appropriate that the particle an (أن) carries an allusion to the verbal-noun meaning (المعنى الحدثي). The meaning then becomes: "We have sent down to you what has been prescribed (كتب) for them of rulings, and [We have sent down] that you should judge between them by what Allah has sent down..." (etc.).

His saying: وَاحْذَرْهُمْ أَنْ يَفْتِنُوكَ عَنْ بَعْضِ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ إِلَيْكَ — "and beware of them lest they should tempt you away from some of what Allah has sent down to you" (5:49). His commanding the Prophet to be wary of their temptation, despite his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله being protected (معصوماً) by the protection (عصمة) of Allah, is only because the power of protection (قوة العصمة) does not necessitate the nullification of free will (الاختيار) or the falling away of the religious obligations (التكاليف) based upon it. For protection is of the nature of intellectual dispositions (الملكات العلمية); and knowledge and perceptions do not remove the active and motive powers in the limbs—nor the limbs that carry them—from the state of equal possibility (استواء نسبة) between performing an act or refraining from it.

Just as the certain knowledge (العلم الجازم) that a food is poisoned protects a person from consuming and eating it, yet the limbs used for nourishment—such as the hand, the mouth, the tongue, and the teeth—are inherently capable of performing their function in this eating and being nourished by it, and are inherently capable of remaining still and doing nothing despite the possibility of action. Thus, the act remains volitional (اختياري), even if its occurrence is as if impossible as long as this knowledge persists.

A portion of the discussion on this has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ وَكانَ فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيماً — "they cannot harm you in any way. Allah has sent down to you the Book and wisdom, and He has taught you what you did not know, and Allah’s grace upon you has been great" (4:113).

His saying: فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّما يُرِيدُ اللهُ أَنْ يُصِيبَهُمْ بِبَعْضِ ذُنُوبِهِمْ — "But if they turn away, then know that Allah desires to strike them for some of their sins" (5:49) is an exposition of the matter of their misguidance following their defiance, as previously mentioned. In this, there is a return to the beginning of the discourse in these verses: يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ لا يَحْزُنْكَ الَّذِينَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْكُفْرِ — "O Apostle! Do not let those who vie with one another in [their] unfaith grieve you" (5:41). Thus, it contains a consolation for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, a soothing of his soul, and a teaching for him of that which prevents grief from creeping into his heart. This is how Allah, the Glorified, has acted in most instances where He forbade the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from grieving over their turning away from the true call and their disdain for accepting what guides them to the path of rectitude and success. He explained to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that they cannot frustrate Allah in His kingdom, nor can they overcome Him; rather, Allah is dominant over His affair. He is the One who misleads them because of their defiance, causes their hearts to swerve because of their own swerving, and places abomination (الرجس) upon them by withdrawing His success (توفيق) from them and leading them on gradually (استدراج). The Exalted said: وَلا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَبَقُوا إِنَّهُمْ لا يُعْجِزُونَ — "Let the faithless not suppose that they have outmaneuvered [Us]. Indeed they cannot frustrate [Allah]" (8:59). Since the matter belongs to Allah, the Glorified, and He is the One who repels every filthy abomination from the pure sanctuary of His religion, then nothing of what He willed has escaped Him, and there is no reason for grief when nothing has been lost.

Perhaps this is what is alluded to in His saying: فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّما يُرِيدُ اللهُ — "But if they turn away, then know that Allah [desires]..." rather than saying: "But if they turn away, then Allah only desires..." or what would convey that meaning. The meaning thus resolves into teaching that their turning away is only by divine subjugation (تسخير إلهي); therefore, it should not grieve the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. For he is a messenger calling to the way of his Lord; if anything were to grieve him, it should only be if he were to overcome the will of Allah in the matter of the religious call. Since Allah, the Glorified, is not frustrated by anything, but rather He is the One who drives them here and there by divine subjugation, withdrawal of success, and planning (مكر), then there is no cause for grief.

The Exalted has explained this reality in another way in His saying: فَلَعَلَّكَ باخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ عَلى آثارِهِمْ إِنْ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهذَا الْحَدِيثِ أَسَفاً إِنَّا جَعَلْنا ما عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَها لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً وَإِنَّا لَجاعِلُونَ ما عَلَيْها صَعِيداً جُرُزاً — "You might perhaps kill yourself with grief over their footsteps if they do not believe in this discourse. Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct. And indeed We will turn whatever is on it into a barren plain" (18:6-8). He explained that Allah the Exalted did not intend by sending the messengers and the religious warning and glad tidings that all people should believe in the same way a human intends his own needs and goals. Rather, all of that is a trial and a test (امتحان وابتلاء) by which people are tested so that he who is best in conduct may be distinguished. Otherwise, the world and all that is in it will be nullified and perish, and nothing will remain but the bare ground, devoid of these disbelievers who turn away from the true discourse and from everything their hearts are attached to. Thus, there is no cause for regret (الأسف), as this does not bring failure to Our effort, nor nullification of Our power, nor exhaustion of Our will.

His saying: وَإِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ لَفاسِقُونَ — "and indeed many of the people are transgressors" (5:49) is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for His saying: أَنَّما يُرِيدُ اللهُ أَنْ يُصِيبَهُمْ — "that Allah desires to strike them" (etc.), according to what has been previously explained.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَحُكْمَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ يَبْغُونَ وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ اللهِ حُكْماً لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ — "Do they seek the judgement of [the days of] pagan ignorance? But who is better than Allah in judgement for a people who have certainty?" (5:50). This is a branching out, in the form of an interrogation, from what was explained in the previous verse regarding their turning away, despite the fact that what they turn away from is the judgment of Allah sent down to them and the truth which they knew to be the truth. It is also possible that it stands as the necessary conclusion of what was explained in all the preceding verses.

The meaning is: If these rulings and laws are true, sent down from Allah, and there is no true judgment beyond them, then there can be nothing else but the judgment of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) — the period of pre-Islamic paganism characterized by the absence of divine guidance — which arises from following desires. So these people who turn away from the true judgment, what do they desire by their turning away when there is nothing there but the judgment of the Age of Ignorance? Do they seek the judgment of the Age of Ignorance while the situation is that no one is better in judgment than Allah for those who claim to have faith?

Thus, His saying: أَفَحُكْمَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ يَبْغُونَ — "Do they seek the judgement of [the days of] pagan ignorance?" is a reproachful interrogation (استفهام توبيخي), and His saying: وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ اللهِ حُكْماً — "But who is better than Allah in judgement" is a rhetorical denial (استفهام إنكاري), meaning: no one is better in judgment than Allah, and judgment is only followed for its goodness. His saying: لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ — "for a people who have certainty" (5:50): the use of the attribute of certainty (اليقين) is an insinuation (تعريض) against them that if they were truthful in their claim of faith in Allah, they would have certainty in His signs; and those who have certainty in the signs of Allah deny that anyone could be better in judgment than Allah, the Glorified.

Know that in these verses there are instances of transition (الالتفات) from the first person singular or plural to the third person and vice versa, such as His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ — "Indeed Allah loves the just" (5:42), then His saying: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ — "We sent down the Torah" (5:44), then His saying: بِمَا اسْتُحْفِظُوا مِنْ كِتابِ اللهِ — "by what they were entrusted of Allah’s Book" (5:44), then His saying: وَاخْشَوْنِ — "and fear Me" (5:44), and so on. Whichever of these chooses the third person with the Majestic Name (Allah) is intended to magnify the matter by magnifying its Author.

Whichever of them is in the first person singular is intended to show that the matter belongs to Allah alone; no guardian (ولي) intervenes in it, nor does any intercessor (شفيع) mediate. If it is an encouragement or a promise, then the one fulfilling it is Allah, the Glorified, and He is the most generous in fulfilling His promise. If it is a warning or a threat, then it is most severe and difficult, and it cannot be diverted from a person by an intercessor or a guardian, since the matter belongs to Allah Himself, and every intermediary has been negated and every intervening cause removed. Understand this well. Some discussion on this has already passed in previous sections.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ لا يَحْزُنْكَ الَّذِينَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْكُفْرِ — "O Apostle! Do not let those who vie with one another in [their] unfaith grieve you" (5:41), it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "A woman from Khaybar, of noble status among them, committed adultery with a man from their nobility, and both were married (محصنان). They disliked stoning them, so they sent word to the Jews of Medina and wrote to them to ask the Prophet about this, hoping that he might provide a concession (رخصة) for them. A group of them set out, including Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, Ka'b ibn Asid, Shu'bah ibn 'Amr, Malik ibn al-Sayf, Kinanah ibn Abi al-Huqayq, and others. They said: 'O Muhammad, inform us about the male and female adulterer when they are married; what is their legal punishment (حدهما)?' He said: 'Will you be satisfied with my judgment in that?' They said: 'Yes.' Then Gabriel descended with the [ruling of] stoning and informed him of that. They refused to accept it. Gabriel then said: 'Place between yourself and them Ibn Suriya,' and he described him to him."

"The Prophet said: 'Do you know a young man, beardless, fair-skinned, one-eyed, who lives in Fadak, called Ibn Suriya?' They said: 'Yes.' He said: 'What kind of man is he among you?' They said: 'The most knowledgeable Jew remaining on the face of the earth regarding what Allah sent down to Moses.' He said: 'Send for him.' They did so, and Abdullah ibn Suriya came to them."

"The Prophet said to him: 'I adjure you by Allah, besides whom there is no god—who sent down the Torah to Moses, split the sea for you, saved you and drowned the house of Pharaoh, shaded you with clouds, and sent down to you manna and quails—do you find in your book stoning for one who is married?' Ibn Suriya said: 'Yes, and by Him whom you have mentioned to me—were it not for the fear that the Lord of the Torah would burn me if I lied or altered [the truth], I would not have confessed to you. But tell me, how is it in your book, O Muhammad?' He said: 'When four reliable witnesses testify that he has entered it into her as the applicator enters the kohl-pot, stoning becomes obligatory upon him.' Ibn Suriya said: 'Thus did Allah send it down in the Torah to Moses.'"

"The Prophet said to him: 'So what was the first thing by which you took a concession regarding the command of Allah?' He said: 'Whenever a nobleman committed adultery, we would leave him; but when a weak person committed adultery, we would carry out the legal punishment (الحد) upon him. Then adultery increased among our nobility until the cousin of a king of ours committed adultery, and we did not stone him. Then another man committed adultery and the king wanted to stone him, but his people said: "No, not until you stone so-and-so"—meaning his cousin. So we said: "Come, let us gather and establish something less than stoning that applies to both the noble and the lowly." So we established flogging and blackening the face (التحميم), which is that they are flogged forty lashes, then their faces are blackened, then they are carried on two donkeys with their faces toward the donkey's rear, and they are paraded around. They made this the substitute for stoning.'"

"The Jews said to Ibn Suriya: 'How quickly you informed him! You were not worthy of our trust when we brought you, but you were absent and we disliked to backbite you.' He said: 'He adjured me by the Torah, and were it not for that, I would not have informed him.' So the Prophet ordered them both to be stoned at the door of his mosque, and he said: 'I am the first to revive Your command when they have put it to death.' Then Allah sent down regarding this: يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيراً مِمَّا كُنْتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ — "O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book, and excusing many [an omission]" (5:15). Ibn Suriya stood up, placed his hands on the knees of the Messenger of Allah, and said: 'This is the station of one seeking refuge with Allah and with you from mentioning to us the "much" that you were commanded to excuse.' So the Prophet turned away from that."

"Then Ibn Suriya asked him about his sleep, and he said: 'My eyes sleep but my heart does not sleep.' He said: 'You have spoken the truth. Now inform me about the resemblance of a child to his father—having nothing of his mother's resemblance—or to his mother—having nothing of his father's resemblance.' He said: 'Whichever of the two prevails and precedes the fluid of the other, the resemblance belongs to it.' He said: 'You have spoken the truth. Now inform me what the man contributes to the child and what the woman contributes?' The Messenger of Allah was overcome by a state of trance for a long time, then he recovered with his face reddened and dripping with sweat. He said: 'The flesh, the blood, the nail, and the fat are from the woman; and the bone, the nerve, and the veins are from the man.' He said to him: 'You have spoken the truth; your affair is the affair of a prophet.'"

"Ibn Suriya embraced Islam at that point and said: 'O Muhammad, which of the angels comes to you?' He said: 'Gabriel.' He said: 'Describe him to me.' The Prophet described him, and he said: 'I bear witness that he is in the Torah as you have said, and that you are truly the Messenger of Allah.'"

"When Ibn Suriya embraced Islam, the Jews fell upon him and insulted him. When they intended to depart, the Banu Qurayzah clung to the Banu al-Nadir and said: 'O Muhammad, our brothers the Banu al-Nadir—our father is one, our religion is one, and our prophet is one—yet when they kill one of us, retaliation (يقاد) is not taken, and they give us his blood-money (ديته) as seventy wasqs of dates. But when we kill one of them, they kill the killer and take from us double: one hundred and forty wasqs of dates. If the slain is a woman, they kill a man from us for her; and for a man of theirs, two men from us; and for a slave, a free man from us. Our wounds are valued at half of their wounds. So judge between us and them.' Then Allah sent down the verses regarding stoning and retaliation (القصاص)."

I say: Al-Tabarsi in al-Majma' has attributed this to the narration of a group of exegetes in addition to his narration from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him). Something close to the beginning of the story is narrated in the Sunni collections and their commentaries through several chains from Abu Hurayrah, Bara' ibn 'Azib, Abdullah ibn 'Umar, Ibn Abbas, and others; the narrations are similar. The end of the story is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from 'Abd ibn Humayd and Abu al-Shaykh from Qatadah, and from Ibn Jarir, Ibn Ishaq, al-Tabarani, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn al-Mundhir, and others from Ibn Abbas.

As for what occurred in the narration regarding Ibn Suriya's confirmation of the existence of the ruling of stoning in the Torah, and that it is what is meant by His saying: وَكَيْفَ يُحَكِّمُونَكَ وَعِنْدَهُمُ التَّوْراةُ فِيها حُكْمُ اللهِ — "But how is it that they make you a judge, while they have the Torah, in which is Allah’s judgement?" (5:43), it is also supported by the existence of the ruling in the Torah circulating today in a manner close to what is in the hadith.

In the twenty-second chapter of the Book of Deuteronomy in the Torah, it states: "[(22) If a man is found lying with a woman married to a husband, both of them shall die: the man who lay with the woman, and the woman; so you shall purge the evil from Israel. (23) If there is a young woman who is a virgin betrothed to a husband, and a man finds her in the city and lies with her, (24) then you shall bring them both out to the gate of that city, and you shall stone them with stones until they die: the young woman because she did not cry out in the city, and the man because he humbled his neighbor's wife; so you shall purge the evil from among you.]"

This, as you see, restricts stoning to certain cases.

As for what occurred in the narration regarding their questioning the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about the ruling of blood-money (الدية) in addition to their questioning about the ruling of adultery by a married person, it has already been mentioned that the verses are not devoid of support for that. What the verse mentioned regarding the ruling of retaliation (القصاص) in killing and wounding—that it is written in the Torah—is indeed present in the Torah circulating today. In the twenty-first chapter of the Book of Exodus in the Torah, it states: "[(12) He who strikes a man so that he dies shall surely be put to death. (13) However, if he did not lie in wait, but God delivered him into his hand, then I will appoint for you a place where he may flee... (23) But if any harm follows, then you shall give life for life, (24) eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot, (25) burn for burn, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.]"

And in the twenty-fourth chapter of the Book of Leviticus, it states: "[(17) Whoever kills any man shall surely be put to death. (18) Whoever kills an animal shall make it good, animal for animal. (19) If a man causes a disfigurement in his neighbor, as he has done, so shall it be done to him— (20) fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth; as he has caused a disfigurement in a man, so shall it be done to him.]"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, Abu Dawud, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "Allah sent down: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَحْكُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ، الظَّالِمُونَ، الْفاسِقُونَ — 'Those who do not judge by what Allah has sent down—it is they who are the faithless... the wrongdoers... the transgressors' (5:44, 45, 47). Allah sent it down regarding two groups of Jews. One had overpowered the other in the Age of Ignorance until they agreed and made peace on the condition that every person killed by the 'mighty' group from the 'lowly' group, his blood-money would be fifty wasqs; and every person killed by the 'lowly' group from the 'mighty' group, his blood-money would be one hundred wasqs. They remained upon this until the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله arrived in Medina. Both groups submitted to the arrival of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—at that time he had not yet gained dominance over them. The 'lowly' group stood up and said: 'Has this ever happened in any two tribes whose religion is one, whose lineage is one, and whose city is one—that the blood-money of some of them is half the blood-money of others? We only gave this to you out of your oppression of us and our fear of you. But now that Muhammad has arrived, we will not give you that.' War almost broke out between them, then they agreed to make the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله the judge between them. The 'mighty' group thought and said: 'By Allah, Muhammad will not give you from them double what he gives them from you. They have spoken the truth; they only gave us this out of oppression and coercion.' So they sent word secretly to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Allah informed His Messenger of their entire affair and what they intended. So Allah sent down: يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ لا يَحْزُنْكَ الَّذِينَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْكُفْرِ — up to His saying — وَمَنْ لَمْ يَحْكُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ. Then he said: 'By Allah, it was sent down regarding them.'"

I say: Al-Qummi narrated the story in his commentary in a long hadith, in which it is mentioned that Abdullah ibn Ubayy was the one speaking on behalf of the Banu al-Nadir—the "mighty" group—and frightening the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding their power, and that he was the one who said: إِنْ أُوتِيتُمْ هذا فَخُذُوهُ وَإِنْ لَمْ تُؤْتَوْهُ فَاحْذَرُوا — "If you are given this, take it; but if you are not given it, then beware" (5:41).

The first narration is more sound in its text than this one, because its content is more consistent and more applicable to the context of the verses. For the beginnings of the verses, especially the first two, do not contextually align with what was mentioned of the story of blood-money between the Banu al-Nadir and Banu al-Qurayzah, as is not hidden from one acquainted with the styles of speech. It is not unlikely that the narration is of the type of applying a story to the Quran, similar to many narrations of the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول). It is as if the narrator found the story applicable to such as His saying: وَكتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ فِيها أَنَّ النَّفْسَ بِالنَّفْسِ — "We prescribed for them in it: a life for a life" (5:45) and what precedes it, then saw the connection of the verses starting from His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ لا يَحْزُنْكَ الَّذِينَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْكُفْرِ, so he took all the verses as having been sent down regarding this story, having overlooked the story of stoning. And Allah knows best.

In the commentary of al-'Ayyashi, from Sulayman ibn Khalid who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: 'When Allah intends good for a servant, He marks his heart with a white spot, opens the ears of his heart, and appoints an angel over him to guide him. And when Allah intends evil for a servant, He marks his heart with a black spot, closes the ears of his heart, and appoints a devil over him to mislead him.'"

"Then he recited this verse: فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ وَمَنْ يُرِدْ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً — "Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam, and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow and straitened" (6:125). And he said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِمْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Indeed those against whom your Lord’s word has become due will not believe" (10:96). And he said: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يُطَهِّرَ قُلُوبَهُمْ — "They are the ones whose hearts Allah did not desire to purify" (5:41)."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to al-Sakuni from Abu Abdillah who said: "Illicit gain (السحت) is the price of carrion, the price of a dog, the price of wine, the dowry of a prostitute, bribery in judgment (الرشوة في الحكم), and the fee of a soothsayer."

I say: What he mentioned in the narration is an enumeration without restriction; the types of illicit gain are many, as found in the narrations. Regarding this meaning and what is close to it, there are many narrations through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, 'Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib: that he was asked about illicit gain (السحت), and he said: "Bribes." It was said to him: "In judgment?" He said: "That is disbelief (الكفر)."

I say: His saying "That is disbelief" is as if an allusion to what occurred among the verses under discussion, in His saying, the Exalted, in the context of censuring illicit gain and bribery in judgment: وَلا تَشْتَرُوا بِآياتِي ثَمَناً قَلِيلاً وَمَنْ لَمْ يَحْكُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ — "and do not sell My signs for a paltry price. Those who do not judge by what Allah has sent down—it is they who are the faithless" (5:44). It has been repeated in narrations from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon them) that they both said: "As for bribery in judgment, that is disbelief in Allah and His Messenger." The narrations regarding the interpretation of illicit gain and its prohibition are many, narrated through both Shi'i and Sunni paths and preserved in their collections.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فإِنْ جاؤُكَ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ — "So if they come to you, judge between them" (5:42): Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Nahhas in his Nasikh, al-Tabarani, al-Hakim—who authenticated it—Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "Two verses were abrogated from this Surah—meaning al-Ma'idah: the verse of the garlands (القلائد) and His saying: فَإِنْ جاؤُكَ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ أَوْ أَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ — 'So if they come to you, judge between them, or disregard them' (5:42). The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to have a choice: if he wished, he judged between them, and if he wished, he disregarded them and returned them to their own laws. Then there was sent down: فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ — 'so judge between them by what Allah has sent down, and do not follow their desires' (5:48). He said: 'So the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded to judge between them by what is in our Book.'"

In the same source, Abu 'Ubayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas: regarding His saying: فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ أَوْ أَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ, he said: "This verse abrogated it: وَأَنِ احْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ — 'And judge between them by what Allah has sent down' (5:49)." I say: Something similar was also narrated from Abd al-Razzaq from 'Ikrimah. However, what is concluded from the content of the verses does not agree with this abrogation (النسخ). The apparent connection from the context of the verses necessitates that they were sent down all at once; in that case, there is no meaning for some of them abrogating others. Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنِ احْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ is not an independent verse in its meaning but is linked to what preceded it; thus, there is no basis for it being an abrogating verse (ناسخة). Even if abrogation were correct despite this, then what preceded it—namely His saying: فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ in the previous verse—would be more worthy of being the abrogator. Moreover, you have already learned that the most apparent view is that the pronoun in His saying, the Exalted: بَيْنَهُمْ — "between them" refers to people in general, not specifically to the People of the Book or the Jews. Furthermore, it has already been mentioned at the beginning of the discussion on this Surah that Surah al-Ma'idah is abrogating (ناسخة) and not abrogated (منسوخة).

In the commentary of al-'Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ فِيها هُدىً وَنُورٌ — "We sent down the Torah, wherein is guidance and light" (5:44): from Abu 'Amr al-Zubayri from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Among the things by which the Imamate (الإمامة) is deserved are: purification and purity from sins and destructive acts of disobedience that necessitate the Fire; then the illuminating knowledge (العلم المنور)—and in another version: the hidden knowledge (المكنون)—of everything the community needs of its lawful and prohibited, and knowledge of its Book: its specific and general, its decisive (المحكم) and metaphorical (المتشابه), the subtleties of its knowledge, the wonders of its interpretation (تأويله), and its abrogating (الناسخ) and abrogated (المنسوخ)." I said: "What is the proof that the Imam can only be one who is knowledgeable of these things you have mentioned?" He said: "The saying of Allah regarding those whom Allah permitted to govern and made them its people: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ فِيها هُدىً وَنُورٌ يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ الَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا لِلَّذِينَ هادُوا وَالرَّبَّانِيُّونَ وَالْأَحْبارُ — 'We sent down the Torah, wherein is guidance and light, by which the prophets who had submitted [to Allah] would judge the Jews, and [so would] the godly (الربانيون) and the scribes (الأحبار)' (5:44). These are the Imams, other than the prophets, who nurture (يربون) the people with their knowledge. As for the scribes (الأحبار), they are the scholars (العلماء) other than the godly (الربانيين). Then He informed [us], saying: بِمَا اسْتُحْفِظُوا مِنْ كِتابِ اللهِ وَكانُوا عَلَيْهِ شُهَداءَ — 'by what they were entrusted of Allah’s Book and were witnesses to it' (5:44); and He did not say 'by what they were made to carry' (بما حملوا) of it."

I say: This is a subtle deduction from him (peace be upon him) by which a wondrous meaning of the verse becomes apparent—a meaning more precise than what was previously explained. Its summary is: the order adopted by the verse in its enumeration—mentioning the prophets, then the godly (الربانيين), then the scribes (الأحبار)—indicates their ranking according to merit and perfection. Thus, the godly are below the prophets and above the scribes; and the scribes are the religious scholars who carried its knowledge through teaching and learning.

Allah, the Glorified, has informed us of the nature of the knowledge of the godly by His saying: بِمَا اسْتُحْفِظُوا مِنْ كِتابِ اللهِ وَكانُوا عَلَيْهِ شُهَداءَ. If what was meant by that was the nature of the knowledge of the scholars, it would have been said: "by what they were made to carry," just as He said: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا التَّوْراةَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوها — "The example of those who were made to bear the Torah then did not bear it" (62:5). For being entrusted (الاستحفاظ) is the seeking of preservation (الحفظ), and its meaning is the obligation of preservation, similar to His saying: لِيَسْئَلَ الصَّادِقِينَ عَنْ صِدْقِهِمْ — "that He may question the truthful concerning their truthfulness" (33:8), meaning: that He may obligate them to manifest what was latent in their souls of the quality of truthfulness. This preservation (الحفظ) and then the witnessing (الشهادة) over the Book cannot be completed except with a protection (عصمة) that is not the characteristic of anyone other than the Imam protected by Allah, the Glorified. For Allah, the Glorified, based His permission for them to judge upon their preservation of the Book, and He considered their witnessing based upon that. It is impossible that He would consider their witnessing over the Book—which is that by which the Book is established—while allowing for error and mistake on their part.

Therefore, this preservation and witnessing are different from the preservation and witnessing that exist among us, the masses of people. Rather, they are of the nature of the preservation of deeds and the witnessing that preceded in His saying, the Exalted: لِتَكُونُوا شُهَداءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيداً — "that you may be witnesses to mankind, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you" (2:143), as passed in the first volume of this book.

The attribution of this preservation and witnessing to all of them, while the ones performing them are some of them, is like the attribution of witnessing over deeds to the entire community, while the ones performing it are some of them. This is a common usage in the Quran, similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ — "Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood" (45:16).

This does not contradict the obligation of the scribes (الأحبار) to preserve and witness, and the taking of the covenant from them for that, because that is a legal, conventional establishment (ثبوت شرعي اعتباري), distinct from the real establishment (الثبوت الحقيقي) which depends on a real preservation free from mistake and error. The divine religion, just as it is not complete without the latter, is not complete without the former.

Thus, it is established that there is a station between the stations of the prophets and the scribes, and that is the station of the Imams. Allah, the Glorified, has informed of this in His saying: وَجَعَلْنا مِنْهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا لَمَّا صَبَرُوا وَكانُوا بِآياتِنا يُوقِنُونَ — "And We appointed among them imams to guide [the people] by Our command, when they had been patient and had conviction in Our signs" (32:24). This is not contradicted by His saying: وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نافِلَةً وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنا صالِحِينَ وَجَعلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا — "And We gave him Isaac, and Jacob as well, as a bonus, and each of them We made righteous. We made them imams, guiding by Our command" (21:72-73). For the gathering of prophethood and Imamate in one group does not contradict their separation in others. A portion of the discussion on Imamate has already passed under His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذِ ابْتَلى إِبْراهِيمَ رَبُّهُ بِكَلِماتٍ — "And when his Lord tested Abraham with certain words" (2:124) in the first volume of this book.

In summary, the godly (الربانيون) and the Imams—who are the intermediate realms (البرازخ) between the prophets and the scribes—possess the knowledge of the truth of the Book and the witnessing over it with the true witnessing.

This applies to the godly and the Imams from the Children of Israel, but the verse indicates that this is because the Torah is a book sent down from Allah, the Glorified, containing guidance and light—that is, the doctrinal and practical knowledge needed by the community. Since that is what necessitates this entrustment (استحفاظ) and witnessing for those who cannot perform them except the godly and the Imams, then this is the state of every book sent down from Allah containing divine knowledge and practical rulings. By this, the objective is established.

Thus, his saying (peace be upon him): "These are the Imams, other than the prophets" means they are lower in station than the prophets according to the order taken in the verse, just as the scribes—who are the scholars—are below the godly. His saying: "who nurture (يربون) the people with their knowledge" is apparent in that he (peace be upon him) took the word rabbani (الرباني) from the root of "nurturing" (التربية) rather than "lordship" (الربوبية). The meanings of the remaining phrases of the narration have become clear through the summary of the meaning we have provided.

Perhaps this meaning is what he (peace be upon him) intended in what al-'Ayyashi also narrated from Malik al-Juhani who said: "Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ فِيها هُدىً وَنُورٌ — up to His saying — بِمَا اسْتُحْفِظُوا مِنْ كِتابِ اللهِ; he said: 'It was sent down regarding us.'"

In Tafsir al-Burhan, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَحْكُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ — "Those who do not judge by what Allah has sent down—it is they who are the faithless" (5:44): from al-Kafi, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Muskan in a marfu' narration, he said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Whoever judges in [a matter of] two dirhams with an unjust judgment, then compels (جبر) [the parties] to it, he is among the people of this verse: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَحْكُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ.' I said: 'And how does he compel them to it?' He said: 'He has a whip and a prison; he judges against him, and if he is satisfied with his judgment, [well and good], otherwise he strikes him with his whip and detains him in his prison.'"

I say: Al-Shaykh narrated it in al-Tahdhib with his chain to Ibn Muskan in a marfu' narration from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and al-'Ayyashi narrated it in his commentary as a mursal narration from him. The meaning of the beginning of the hadith is also narrated through other paths from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

The purpose of restricting the judgment to "compulsion" (الجبر) is to convey that the judgment must be such that an effect follows from it, being a decisive judgment (حكماً فصلاً) by its very nature; otherwise, a mere verbal pronouncement is not called a judgment (حكماً).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Sa'id ibn Mansur, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "Allah only sent down وَمَنْ لَمْ يَحْكُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ and الظَّالِمُونَ and الْفاسِقُونَ regarding the Jews specifically."

I say: Regarding this, the three verses are absolute (مطلقة); there is no evidence to restrict them, and the specific occasion does not necessitate altering the absolute nature of the wording. Furthermore, the occasion of the third verse was the Christians, not the Jews. Moreover, Ibn Abbas has been narrated to have said what contradicts this.

In the same source, 'Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Hakim ibn Jubayr who said: "I asked Sa'id ibn Jubayr about these verses in al-Ma'idah. I said: 'A group claims that they were sent down regarding the Children of Israel and were not sent down regarding us.' He said: 'Read what is before them and what is after them.' So I read to him. He said: 'No, rather they were sent down regarding us.' Then I met Miqsam—the client of Ibn Abbas—and I asked him about these verses in al-Ma'idah. I said: 'A group claims that they were sent down regarding the Children of Israel and were not sent down regarding us.' He said: 'They were sent down regarding the Children of Israel and were sent down regarding us; and whatever was sent down regarding us and them belongs to us and them.'"

"Then I entered upon Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) and asked him about these verses in al-Ma'idah, and I told him that I had asked Sa'id ibn Jubayr and Miqsam about them. He said: 'What did Miqsam say?' I informed him. He said: 'He spoke the truth—but it is a disbelief (كفر) not like the disbelief of polytheism, a defiance (فسق) not like the defiance of polytheism, and a wrongdoing (ظلم) not like the wrongdoing of polytheism.' Then I met Sa'id ibn Jubayr and informed him of what he had said. Sa'id ibn Jubayr said to his son: 'How did you see him? (meaning the Imam). I have found him to have a merit over you and over Miqsam.'"

I say: The applicability of the narration to what appears from the verse has become clear in the preceding exposition.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to al-Halabi from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), and in the commentary of al-'Ayyashi from Abu Basir from him (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ تَصَدَّقَ بِهِ فَهُوَ كَفَّارَةٌ لَهُ — "and if anyone waives it out of charity, that shall be an atonement for him" (5:45). He said: "It atones for his sins in proportion to what he pardoned of a wound or otherwise."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from a man of the Ansar from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: regarding His saying: فَمَنْ تَصَدَّقَ بِهِ فَهو كَفَّارَةٌ لَهُ, he said: "A man whose tooth is broken, or his hand is cut, or something is cut, or he is wounded in his body, and he pardons that—then an amount of his sins is removed from him. If it was a quarter of the blood-money, then a quarter of his sins; if it was a third, then a third of his sins; and if it was the full blood-money, his sins are removed accordingly." I say: Something similar was also narrated from al-Daylami from Ibn 'Umar. Perhaps what occurred in this narration and the one before it regarding the division of atonement (التكفير) according to the division of pardon is derived from treating the blood-money legally—which is divisible—as being in the place of retaliation (القصاص), then weighing both retaliation and blood-money against the forgiveness of sins, which is also divisible; thus, the part corresponds to the part just as the whole corresponds to the whole.

In the commentary of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنا مِنْكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَمِنْهاجاً — "For each [community] among you We had appointed a code [of law] and a path" (5:48), he said: "For every prophet, a law and a way."

In Tafsir al-Burhan, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَفَحُكْمَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ يَبْغُونَ — "Do they seek the judgement of [the days of] pagan ignorance?" (5:50): from al-Kafi, with its chain to Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Khalid from his father in a marfu' narration from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Judges are four: three are in the Fire and one is in Paradise. A man who judges with injustice (بجور) while he knows—he is in the Fire. A man who judged with injustice while he did not know—he is in the Fire. A man who judged with the truth while he did not know—he is in the Fire. And a man who judged with the truth while he knows—he is in Paradise."

And he (peace be upon him) said: "Judgment is of two kinds: the judgment of Allah and the judgment of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). Whoever misses the judgment of Allah has judged by the judgment of the Age of Ignorance."

I say: Regarding both meanings, there are many reports through the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis preserved in the reports on judgment and testimonies. The verse alludes to, and indeed indicates, both meanings. As for the first meaning, it is because judging with injustice—whether one knows it or judges without knowledge and it happens to be unjust—as well as judging with the truth without knowledge, all of that is from following desires (اتباع الهوى). Allah has forbidden this by His saying: فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ عَمَّا جاءَكَ مِنَ الْحَقِ — "so judge between them by what Allah has sent down, and do not follow their desires against the truth that has come to you" (5:48). He warned against following desires in judgment and contrasted it with judging by the truth; thus, it is known from this that knowledge of the truth is a condition for the permissibility of judgment, otherwise it is not permissible because it involves following desires.

Furthermore, the term "judgment of the Age of Ignorance," which is the opposite of the judgment of Allah the Exalted, truly applies to it.

As for the second meaning—which is the division of judgment into the judgment of the Age of Ignorance and the judgment of Allah—it is derived from the outward meaning of His saying, the Exalted: أَفَحُكْمَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ يَبْغُونَ وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ اللهِ حُكْماً by virtue of the contrast established between the two judgments. And Allah knows best.

In the commentary of al-Tabari, from Qatadah: regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ فِيها هُدىً وَنُورٌ يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ الَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا لِلَّذِينَ هادُوا وَالرَّبَّانِيُّونَ وَالْأَحْبارُ (5:44), he said: "As for the godly (الربانيون), they are the jurists (فقهاء) of the Jews; and as for the scribes (الأحبار), they are their scholars (علماؤهم)." He said: "It was mentioned to us that the Prophet of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said when this verse was sent down: 'We judge over the Jews and over those besides them of the followers of [other] religions.'"

I say: Al-Suyuti also narrated it regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ (the verse) from 'Abd ibn Humayd and from Ibn Jarir from Qatadah.

The outward sense of the narration is that what is quoted of the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying is connected to the verse—meaning that the verse is the proof for that. This is problematic because the verse only indicates judging by the Torah over the Jews, due to His saying, the Exalted: لِلَّذِينَ هادُوا — "the Jews", not over those other than the Jews, nor judging by other than the Torah, as is the outward sense of the narration—unless what is meant by his saying "We judge" is that "the prophets judge such and such," which, besides being a weak meaning, has no connection to the verse.

It appears that some of the narrators made a mistake in quoting the verse, and that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله only said it after the descent of His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ (the verses). This aligns with what was previously mentioned: that the outward sense of the verse is that the pronoun in His saying بَيْنَهُمْ — "between them" refers to people in general, not specifically to the Jews. Thus, the narrator took one verse in place of the other.

Ayahs

۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْيَهُودَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۘ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُم مِّنكُمْ فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنْهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥١)
O you who have faith! Do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies: they are allies of each other. Any of you who allies with them is indeed one of them. Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (51)
فَتَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِيهِمْ يَقُولُونَ نَخْشَىٰٓ أَن تُصِيبَنَا دَآئِرَةٌ ۚ فَعَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِٱلْفَتْحِ أَوْ أَمْرٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِۦ فَيُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَسَرُّوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ (٥٢)
Yet you see those in whose hearts is a sickness rushing to them, saying, ‘We fear lest a turn of fortune should visit us.’ Maybe Allah will bring about a victory, or a command from Him, and then they will be regretful for what they kept secret in their hearts, (52)
وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ۙ إِنَّهُمْ لَمَعَكُمْ ۚ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (٥٣)
and the faithful will say, ‘Are these the ones who swore by Allah with solemn oaths that they were with you?!’ Their works have failed, and they have become losers. (53)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَن يَرْتَدَّ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِۦ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِى ٱللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُۥٓ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ يُجَـٰهِدُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَخَافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لَآئِمٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٥٤)
O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion, Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the faithful, stern towards the faithless, waging jihad in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer. That is Allah’s grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (54)

(Commentary)

A general survey of these verses necessitates a pause regarding the connection of these verses to what precedes them, as well as the connection of what follows them—such as His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ (Only Allah is your guardian and His Apostle...) [Qarai 5:55] until the end of the two verses—and then the connection of His saying after them: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا دِينَكُمْ هُزُواً (O you who have faith! Do not take those who take your religion in mockery...) [Qarai 5:57] through the completion of several verses, and finally the connection of His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ (O Apostle! Communicate...) [Qarai 5:67].

As for these four verses, they mention the Jews and the Christians. The Quran would not mention their affairs in its Meccan verses due to the lack of pressing need for it at that time; rather, it addresses their condition in the verses revealed in Medina. Nor does it address them in the verses revealed in the early period of the Migration (الهجرة), for the Muslims at that time were only afflicted by mixing and social interaction with the Jews, or making peace treaties with them, or specifically repelling their plots and deception, to the exclusion of the Christians—except in the latter half of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) stay in Medina. Perhaps these four verses were revealed during that period, and perhaps the "victory" (الفتح) mentioned therein refers to the Conquest of Mecca.

However, it has been previously established that the reliance is upon the Surah al-Ma'idah being revealed in the year of the Farewell Pilgrimage (حجة الوداع), by which time Mecca had already been conquered. Is the intended "victory" then a victory other than the Conquest of Mecca? Or were these verses revealed before the Conquest of Mecca and before the revelation of the entire Surah?

Furthermore, regarding the last verse—namely His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَنْ يَرْتَدَّ مِنْكُمْ (O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion...) [Qarai 5:54]—is it connected to the three verses preceding it? And who is intended by "these people" whose apostasy is anticipated?

And who are "these others" whom Allah promised He would bring? Each of these matters adds ambiguity upon ambiguity. The reports concerning the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) are scattered and represent nothing more than the views of the early exegetes, as is the case with most transmitted reports of revelation regarding the verses. This gross discrepancy also confuses the mind in understanding the meaning. Add to all this the interference of sectarian biases in the judgments passed on them, as you will encounter evidence of this in the narrations and the statements of exegetes from both the early and later generations.

What contemplation (التدبر) upon the verses yields is that these four verses, as we have cited them, are connected in their parts but detached from what precedes and follows them. The fourth verse is among the completions of the purpose intended for clarification within them. However, one must exercise caution in understanding their meaning, avoiding the laxity and oversimplifications permitted by the views of researching exegetes regarding the verses, especially concerning the attributes and descriptions mentioned therein, as will follow.

The summary of what is obtained from the verses is that Allah, the Glorified, warns the believers therein against taking the Jews and Christians as guardians (أولياء), threatening them most severely in that regard. He points out, in a Quranic prophecy (ملحمة قرآنية), what this alliance will lead to in terms of the collapse of the structure of the religious way of life, and that Allah will raise a people who will take charge of the matter and restore the structure of the religion to its original flourishing state.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ (O you who have faith! Do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies: they are allies of each other...) [Qarai 5:51]. It is stated in al-Majma': "Taking (الاتخاذ) is the reliance upon a thing to prepare it for a matter. It is the ifti'al form from 'taking' (الأخذ). Its origin is al-i'tikhadh, where the hamzah was changed to a ta' and elided into the following ta'. Similar to it is al-itti'ad from 'promise' (الوعد). 'Taking' (الأخذ) occurs in several ways: you say 'he took the book' when he received it, 'he took the offering' when he accepted it, and 'Allah took him from his place of safety' when He destroyed him. Its root is the passage of a thing from one direction to another." (End of quote).

Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Proximity (الولاء) and succession (التوالي) occur when two or more things happen such that there is nothing between them that does not belong to them. This is used metaphorically for closeness in terms of place, lineage, religion, friendship, support, and belief." (End of the relevant portion). The full investigation into the meaning of guardianship (الولاية) will follow.
