Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned His party in another place in His speech with a content close to this place, and He marked them with success (الفلاح), saying: لا تَجِدُ قَوْماً يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ يُوادُّونَ مَنْ حَادَّ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَلَوْ كانُوا آباءَهُمْ أَوْ أَبْناءَهُمْ أَوْ إِخْوانَهُمْ أَوْ عَشِيرَتَهُمْ أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ — until He said: — أُولئِكَ حِزْبُ اللهِ أَلا إِنَّ حِزْبَ اللهِ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ — "You will not find a people believing in Allah and the Last Day love those who oppose Allah and His Apostle, even though they be their fathers, or their sons, or their brothers, or their clansmen. They are the ones in whose hearts He has inscribed faith, and whom He has helped with a spirit from Him... They are Allah’s confederates. Look! The confederates of Allah are indeed the felicitous" (58:22).

Success (الفلاح) is triumph and the attainment of the goal, which is victory (الغلبة) and mastery over the desired object. This victory and success are what Allah promised the believers in the best of what He promised them and gave them glad tidings of attaining. He, the Exalted, said: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ — "Certainly the faithful have attained salvation" (23:1). The verses regarding this are many, and the term is used absolutely in all of them. Thus, the intended meaning is absolute victory and absolute success, i.e., triumphing with happiness, winning the truth, prevailing over wretchedness, and nullifying falsehood in this world and the Hereafter. As for this world, it is through the "good life" (الحياة الطيبة) that exists in a righteous society of the guardians of Allah in a land purified from the guardians of Satan, based on God-consciousness (تقوى) and wariness (ورع). As for the Hereafter, it is in the proximity of the Lord of all the worlds.

(Discussion of Narrations)

In al-Kafi, narrated from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, from 'Umar ibn Udhaynah, from Zurarah, al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, Bukayr ibn A'yan, Muhammad ibn Muslim, Burayd ibn Mu'awiyah, and Abu al-Jarud, all from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, commanded His Messenger regarding the guardianship (ولاية) of Ali and sent down to him: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ـ الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ (Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while they bow). He made the guardianship of the possessors of authority (أولي الأمر) obligatory, but they did not know what it was. So Allah commanded Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to explain the guardianship to them—just as he explained the prayer, the zakat, the fasting, and the pilgrimage.

When that came to him from Allah, the breast of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) became straitened, and he feared that they would apostatize from their religion and that they would call him a liar. So his breast became straitened and he turned back to his Lord, the Mighty and Majestic—so Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed to him: يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ ـ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَما بَلَّغْتَ رِسالَتَهُ وَاللهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ (O Apostle! Communicate that which has been sent down to you from your Lord, and if you do not, you will not have communicated His message, and Allah shall protect you from the people). So he carried out the command of Allah, exalted is His mention, and established the guardianship of Ali (peace be upon him) on the day of Ghadir Khumm—and he called out: 'The prayer is congregational,' and commanded the people that the witness should inform the absent."

'Umar ibn Udhaynah said: "They all said, except Abu al-Jarud: Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: 'And it was the last obligation, and the guardianship (الولاية) was the last of the obligations—so Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent down: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي (Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you).' Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: 'Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: I shall not send down any obligation upon you after this—I have perfected the obligations for you.'"

In al-Burhan and Ghayat al-Maram, narrated from al-Saduq with his chain of transmission from Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا, he said: "A group of Jews embraced Islam—among them Abdullah ibn Salam, Asad, Tha'labah, Ibn Yamin, and Ibn Suriya—so they came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Prophet of Allah, indeed Moses bequeathed to Joshua ibn Nun, so who is your legatee (وصيك), O Messenger of Allah? And who is our guardian (ولينا) after you?' So this verse was revealed: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ـ الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Stand up,' so they stood up and came to the mosque—and there was a beggar coming out. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'O beggar, did anyone give you anything?' He said: 'Yes, this ring.' He said: 'Who gave it to you?' He said: 'That man who is praying gave it to me.' He said: 'In what state did he give it to you?' He said: 'He was bowing.' So the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said 'Allahu Akbar' and the people of the mosque said 'Allahu Akbar.'

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Ali is your guardian after me.' They said: 'We are pleased with Allah as Lord, with Muhammad as Prophet, and with Ali ibn Abi Talib as guardian.' So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent down: وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ـ فَإِنَّ حِزْبَ اللهِ هُمُ الْغالِبُونَ (And whoever takes for his guardians Allah, His Apostle and the faithful [should know that] Allah’s confederates are indeed the victorious)." The narration continues.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, he said: "My father narrated to me, from Safwan, from Aban ibn 'Uthman, from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): 'While the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was sitting and with him was a group of Jews—among them Abdullah ibn Salam—this verse was revealed to him. So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out to the mosque and a beggar met him. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Did anyone give you anything?" He said: "Yes, that person praying." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) came and it was Ali (peace be upon him).'"

I say: And al-'Ayyashi narrated it in his Tafsir from him (peace be upon him).

In Amali al-Shaykh, he said: "Muhammad ibn Muhammad—meaning al-Mufid—narrated to us, he said: Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Muhammad al-Katib narrated to me, he said: al-Hasan ibn Ali al-Za'farani narrated to us, he said: Abu Ishaq Ibrahim ibn Muhammad al-Thaqafi narrated to us, he said: Muhammad ibn Ali narrated to us, he said: al-'Abbas ibn Abdullah al-'Anbari narrated to us, from Abd al-Rahman ibn al-Aswad al-Kindi al-Yashkuri, from 'Awn ibn 'Ubaydullah, from his father, from his grandfather Abu Rafi', who said: 'I entered upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) one day while he was sleeping—and there was a snake in the corner of the house. I disliked to kill it and wake the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—and I thought that he was being inspired (يوحى إليه), so I lay down between him and the snake. I said: "If there is any harm from it, let it be to me instead of him."

I stayed for a short while, then the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) woke up while he was reciting: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا—until he reached the end of the verse. Then he said: "Praise be to Allah who completed His blessing upon Ali, and congratulations to him for the grace of Allah which He gave him." Then he said to me: "What is the matter with you here?" So I informed him of the news of the snake. He said to me: "Kill it," so I did. Then he said to me: "O Abu Rafi', how will you be when a people fight Ali while he is on the truth and they are on falsehood? Jihad against them is a right for Allah, exalted is His Name—so whoever is not able with his heart, there is nothing beyond that." I said: "O Messenger of Allah, pray to Allah for me—if I reach them, that He strengthens me to fight them." He said: So the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prayed and said: "Indeed, for every prophet there is a trustworthy one (أمين), and indeed my trustworthy one is Abu Rafi'."'"

He said: "When the people pledged allegiance to Ali after 'Uthman, and Talhah and al-Zubayr set out, I remembered the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—so I sold my house in Medina and a land of mine in Khaybar—and I went out with myself and my children with the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) to be martyred before him. I did not join him until he returned from Basra, and I went out with him to Siffin—and I fought before him there and at al-Nahrawan as well. I remained with him until Ali (peace be upon him) was martyred. So I returned to Medina and I had no house or land there—so al-Hasan ibn Ali (peace be upon him) gave me a land in Yanbu', and he allotted for me half of the house of the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), so I settled there with my family."

In Tafsir al-'Ayyashi, with his chain of transmission from al-Hasan ibn Zayd, from his father Zayd ibn al-Hasan, from his grandfather, who said: "I heard 'Ammar ibn Yasir saying: 'A beggar stood before Ali ibn Abi Talib while he was bowing in a voluntary prayer—so he pulled off his ring and gave it to the beggar. He came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and informed him of that—so this verse was revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ـ الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ—(to the end of the verse). The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited it to us—then he said: "Whomever I am his master (مولاه), Ali is his master. O Allah, befriend the one who befriends him, and be an enemy to the one who is his enemy."'"

In Tafsir al-'Ayyashi, from al-Mufaddal ibn Salih, from some of his companions, from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them), who said: "When this verse was revealed: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا—that was difficult for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—and he feared that Quraysh would call him a liar—so Allah sent down: يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ (the verse). So he established that on the day of Ghadir Khumm."

In it, from Abu Jamilah, from some of his companions, from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Indeed Allah inspired me to love four: Ali, Abu Dharr, Salman, and al-Miqdad.' I said: 'What about the large number of people—did no one know this matter?' He said: 'Yes, three.' I said: 'These verses that were sent down: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا—and His saying: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ—did no one ask concerning whom they were revealed?' He said: 'From there it came to them; they were not asking.'"

In Ghayat al-Maram, from al-Saduq with his chain of transmission from Abu Sa'id al-Warraq, from his father, from Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his father, from his grandfather: in the narration of Ali’s (peace be upon him) appeal to Abu Bakr—when Abu Bakr took the caliphate, and he (peace be upon him) mentioned his virtues to Abu Bakr—and the texts concerning him from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Among what he said to him was: "I adjure you by Allah, does the guardianship (الولاية) from Allah—along with the guardianship of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the verse of the zakat of the ring—belong to me or to you?" He said: "Rather, to you."

In Majalis al-Shaykh, with his chain of transmission to Abu Dharr: in the narration of the Commander of the Faithful’s (peace be upon him) appeal to 'Uthman, al-Zubayr, Abd al-Rahman ibn 'Awf, and Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas—on the day of the Shura and his argumentation against them—with the texts from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and all of them were confirming him (peace be upon him) in what he said. Among what he (peace be upon him) mentioned was: "Is there anyone among you who gave zakat while he was bowing, so that there was revealed concerning him: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ـ الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ—other than me?" They said: "No."

In al-Ihtijaj, in the letter of Abu al-Hasan the Third, Ali ibn Muhammad al-Hadi (peace be upon him), to the people of Ahvaz when they asked him about compulsion (الجبر) and delegation (التفويض):

He (peace be upon him) said: "The entire nation has agreed, with no difference among them in that: that the Quran is truth, there is no doubt in it according to all its sects—so they are correct in their state of agreement upon it, and guided in confirming what Allah sent down, because of the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): 'My nation shall not agree upon an error.' So he (peace be upon him) informed that what the nation has agreed upon—and some of them did not differ with others—is the truth. This is the meaning of the hadith, not what the ignorant have interpreted, nor what the antagonists have said of nullifying the judgment of the Book and following the judgments of forged hadiths and embellished narrations, and following the ruinous and destructive desires that contradict the text of the Book and the verification of the clear and luminous verses. We ask Allah to grant us success for prayer and to guide us to righteousness."

Then he (peace be upon him) said: "So if the Book testifies to the truth of a report—and its verification—and a group of the nation denies it—opposing it with one of these forged hadiths, they become, by their denial and rejection of the Book, in error. The most correct report is that whose verification is known from the Book—like the report agreed upon from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who said: 'I am leaving behind among you two successors: the Book of Allah and my progeny. As long as you hold fast to them, you will never go astray after me—and they shall never separate until they return to me at the Pond.' And the other wording from him in this very sense is his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): 'I am leaving among you the two weighty things (الثقلين): the Book of Allah and my progeny, the people of my house, and they shall never separate until they return to me at the Pond; as long as you hold fast to them, you will never go astray.'"

"We found the witnesses of this hadith explicitly in the Book of Allah—like His saying: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ـ الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ. Then the narrations of the scholars agreed—in that for the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): that he gave his ring in charity while he was bowing, so Allah thanked that for him and sent down the verse concerning him. Then we found the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—having distinguished him from his companions with this wording: 'Whomever I am his master, Ali is his master—O Allah, befriend the one who befriends him and be an enemy to the one who is his enemy.' And his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): 'Ali shall pay my debt, and fulfill my promise, and he is my successor over you after me.' And his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when he appointed him over Medina—so he said: 'O Messenger of Allah, do you leave me behind over the women and children?' So he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Are you not pleased to be to me in the position of Aaron to Moses—except that there is no prophet after me?'"

"So we knew that the Book testified to the confirmation of these reports and the verification of these witnesses. Thus, it is necessary for the nation to acknowledge them—since these reports agreed with the Quran. When we found that agreeing with the Book of Allah, and we found the Book of Allah agreeing with these reports and being a proof for them, following [them] became an obligation—none oversteps it except the people of antagonism and corruption."

In al-Ihtijaj, in a narration from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "The hypocrites said to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): 'Does there remain for your Lord over us, after what He has made obligatory upon us, anything else—that He makes obligatory, so you mention it and our souls may be at rest that nothing else remains?' So Allah sent down regarding that: قُلْ إِنَّما أَعِظُكُمْ بِواحِدَةٍ — 'Say, "I give you just one admonition"' (34:46)—meaning the guardianship (الولاية). So Allah sent down: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ـ الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ. And there is no difference among the nation that no one gave zakat—on that day while he was bowing—except one man." The narration continues.

In al-Ikhtisas by al-Mufid, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn 'Isa, from al-Qasim ibn Muhammad al-Jawhari, from al-Hasan ibn Abi al-'Ala', who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'The legatees (الأوصياء)—is their obedience obligatory?' He said: 'Yes, they are the ones of whom Allah said: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ (Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you)—and they are the ones of whom Allah said: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ـ الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ.'"

I say: And it was narrated in al-Kafi from al-Husayn ibn Abi al-'Ala' from him (peace be upon him), and what is in its sense was narrated from Ahmad ibn 'Isa from him (peace be upon him).

The attribution of the revelation of what was sent down concerning Ali (peace be upon him) to all the Imams (peace be upon them) is because they are one Household (أهل بيت واحد) and their affair is one.

It is narrated in the Tafsir of al-Tha‘labi: Abu al-Hasan Muhammad ibn al-Qasim al-Faqih informed us, saying: ‘Abdullah ibn Ahmad al-Sha‘rani narrated to us, saying: Abu ‘Ali Ahmad ibn ‘Ali ibn Razin informed us, saying: al-Muzaffar ibn al-Hasan al-Ansari narrated to us, saying: al-Sarri ibn ‘Ali al-Warraq narrated to us, saying: Yahya ibn ‘Abd al-Hamid al-Jumani narrated to us, from Qays ibn al-Rabi‘, from al-A‘mash, from ‘Abayah ibn al-Rib‘i, who said: ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abbas (رضي الله عنه) narrated to us—while he was sitting by the edge of Zamzam saying, "The Messenger of Allah said"—when a man wearing a turban approached. Every time Ibn ‘Abbas said, "The Messenger of Allah said," the man also said, "The Messenger of Allah said."

Ibn ‘Abbas said to him: "I adjure you by Allah, who are you?" The man uncovered the turban from his face and said: "O people! Whoever knows me, knows me; and whoever does not know me, I am Jundub ibn Junadah al-Badri, Abu Dharr al-Ghifari. I heard the Messenger of Allah with these two [ears]—otherwise, may they be struck deaf—and I saw him with these two [eyes]—otherwise, may they be struck blind—saying: 'Ali is the leader of the righteous (البررة) and the slayer of the faithless (الكفرة); victorious is he who supports him, and forsaken is he who abandons him.'"

"Behold, one day I offered the noon prayer (صلاة الظهر) with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). A beggar asked for alms in the mosque, but no one gave him anything. The beggar raised his hands toward the heaven and said: 'O Allah! Bear witness that I asked in the mosque of the Messenger of Allah, and no one gave me anything.' 'Ali was in the state of bowing (راكعاً), so he gestured to him with his right pinky finger, on which he wore a ring. The beggar approached and took the ring from his pinky, all while the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was watching. When the Prophet finished his prayer, he raised his head toward the heaven and said: 'O Allah! Moses asked You, saying: "My Lord! Open my breast for me. Make my affair easy for me. Release the knot from my tongue, that they may understand my speech. Appoint for me a minister from my family, Aaron, my brother. Strengthen my back by him, and make him a partner in my affair." So You sent down to him a speaking Quran: "We will strengthen your arm by your brother, and We will give you both a certain authority, so they will not reach you [to do any harm] because of Our signs."'"

"'O Allah! I am Muhammad, Your Prophet and Your chosen one (صفيك). O Allah! Open my breast for me, make my affair easy for me, and appoint for me a minister (وزيراً) from my family, 'Ali; strengthen my back by him.'"

Abu Dharr said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had not even finished his words when Gabriel descended to him from Allah, the Exalted, and said: 'O Muhammad, read!' He asked: 'What shall I read?' Gabriel said: 'Read: "Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while they are bowing."'"

And from al-Jam‘ bayna al-Sihah al-Sittah by Razin, from the third volume regarding the commentary (تفسير) of Surah al-Ma’idah:

Regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ (Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle...): [The verse] is from the Sahih of al-Nasa’i, narrated from Ibn Salam, who said: "I came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'Our people have shown us hostility since we believed Allah and His Messenger, and they have sworn not to speak to us.' Then Allah, the Exalted, sent down: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ (Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while they are bowing.)"

"Then Bilal gave the call for the noon prayer (صلاة الظهر), and the people stood to pray. Among those prostrating, bowing, and begging, a beggar was asking for alms. 'Ali gave him his ring while he was bowing. The beggar informed the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), so the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited to us: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فَإِنَّ حِزْبَ اللهِ هُمُ الْغالِبُونَ (Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while they are bowing. Whoever takes for his guardians Allah, His Apostle and the faithful [should know that] the confederates of Allah are indeed the victorious.)"

And from the Manaqib of Ibn al-Maghazili al-Shafi‘i, regarding the commentary (تفسير) of His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ (Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle...): He said: Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn ‘Uthman informed us, saying: Abu Bakr Ahmad ibn Ibrahim ibn Shadhan al-Bazzaz informed us by permission, saying: al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali al-‘Adawi narrated to us, saying: Salamah ibn Shabib narrated to us, saying: ‘Abd al-Razzaq narrated to us, saying: Mujahid informed us from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ (Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while they are bowing.)—he said: "It was revealed concerning 'Ali."

And from him, he said: Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Tawan informed us, saying: Abu Ahmad ‘Umar ibn ‘Abdullah ibn Shawdhab informed us, saying: Muhammad ibn al-‘Askari al-Daqqaq narrated to us, saying: Muhammad ibn ‘Uthman ibn Abi Shaybah narrated to us, saying: ‘Abadah narrated to us, saying: ‘Umar ibn Thabit narrated to us, from Muhammad ibn al-Sa’ib, from Abu Salih, from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "'Ali was bowing (راكعاً) when a poor man (مسكين) came to him, and he gave him his ring. The Messenger of Allah asked: 'Who gave you this?' He replied: 'This bowing man gave it to me.' Then Allah revealed this verse: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful...) to the end of the verse."

And from him, he said: Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Tawan narrated to us by permission: that Abu Ahmad ‘Umar ibn ‘Abdullah ibn Shawdhab informed them, saying: Muhammad ibn Ja‘far ibn Muhammad al-‘Askari narrated to us, saying: Muhammad ibn ‘Uthman narrated to us, saying: Ibrahim ibn Muhammad ibn Maymun narrated to us, saying: ‘Ali ibn ‘Abis narrated to us, saying: "Abu Maryam and I visited ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Ata’. Abu Maryam said: 'Relate to 'Ali the narration (الحديث) you told me from Abu Ja‘far.' He said: 'I was sitting with Abu Ja‘far when the son of ‘Abdullah ibn Salam passed by. I said: "May Allah make me your ransom, is this the son of the one who possesses the knowledge of the Book (علم الكتاب)?" He replied: "No, rather it is your companion 'Ali ibn Abi Talib, concerning whom verses from the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, were revealed: 'And he who possesses the knowledge of the Book,' 'Is he then who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and whom a witness from him follows,' and 'Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful...'"'"

And from al-Khatib al-Khwarizmi, in the reply to Mu‘awiyah’s correspondence to ‘Amr ibn al-‘As, ‘Amr ibn al-‘As said: "You well know, O Mu‘awiyah, what has been revealed in His Book of the recited verses (الآيات المتلوات) regarding his ['Ali's] virtues, in which no one shares with him, such as His saying, the Exalted: 'They fulfill their vows,' 'Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while they are bowing,' 'Is he then who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and whom a witness from him follows, and before him...' And Allah, the Exalted, has said: 'Among the faithful are men who fulfill what they have pledged to Allah,' and Allah, the Exalted, said to His Messenger: 'Say, "I do not ask you any reward for it except love of [my] relatives."'"

And from him, with his chain of narration to Abu Salih, from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "‘Abdullah ibn Salam approached with a group of his people who had believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). They said: 'O Messenger of Allah! Our houses are far away, and we have no place for assembly or conversation other than this assembly. When our people saw that we believed in Allah and His Messenger and followed him, they rejected us and swore among themselves not to sit with us, marry from us, or speak to us. This has become difficult for us.' So the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to them: 'Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while they are bowing.'"

"Then the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out to the mosque while the people were standing and bowing. He spotted a beggar and asked him: 'Has anyone given you anything?' He replied: 'Yes, a gold ring.' The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) asked: 'Who gave it to you?' He said: 'That man standing there'—and he gestured with his hand toward 'Ali ibn Abi Talib. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) asked: 'In what state did he give it to you?' He replied: 'He gave it to me while he was bowing (راكع).' The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) then proclaimed the Takbir (كبر) and recited: 'Whoever takes for his guardians Allah and His Apostle... the confederates of Allah are indeed the victorious.' Thereupon, Hassan ibn Thabit composed these verses:

O Abu al-Hasan, may my soul and my life-blood be your ransom, And every person, whether slow or swift in guidance.

Shall my praise and that of your lovers go to waste? Praise regarding the Essence of the God (ذات الإله) is never wasted.

For you are the one who gave while you were bowing, May the souls of the people be your ransom, O best of those who bow.

With your blessed ring, O best of masters, And best of buyers, and then best of sellers.

So Allah revealed concerning you the best of guardianship (ولاية), And clarified it within the firm laws (محكمات الشرائع).

It is narrated from al-Hamawayni, with his chain of transmission reaching Abu Hadbah Ibrahim ibn Hadbah, who said: Anas ibn Malik informed us that a beggar entered the mosque saying: "Who will provide a loan to the One who is Wealthy (الملي) and Faithful (الوفي)?" while ‘Ali was in the state of bowing (الركوع). ‘Ali signaled with his hand behind him to the beggar, indicating: "Take the ring from my hand." He said: Then the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "O ‘Umar, it has become mandatory (وجبت)." He replied: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah, what has become mandatory?" He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Paradise has become mandatory for him. By Allah, he did not remove it from his hand until he had stripped himself of every sin and every transgression."

And from him, with his chain of transmission from Zayd ibn ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn, from his father, from his grandfather, who said: I heard ‘Ammar ibn Yasir رضي الله عنه saying: A beggar stood before ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib while he was bowing (راكع) during a supererogatory prayer (صلاة التطوع). He took off his ring and gave it to the beggar. He then went to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and informed him of that. Consequently, this verse was revealed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ "Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while bowing down." (5:55). The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited it and then said: "Whosoever I am his master (مولاه), ‘Ali is his master."

And from al-Hafiz Abu Nu‘aym, from Abu al-Zubayr, from Jabir رضي الله عنه, who said: ‘Abdullah ibn Salam came—accompanied by a group of people complaining of the people's avoidance of them since they embraced Islam. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Seek a beggar for me." We entered the mosque and a beggar approached him. He asked him: "Did anyone give you anything?" He replied: "Yes, I passed by a man who was bowing (راكع) and he gave me his ring." He said: "Go and show him to me." He said: We went, and there was ‘Ali standing. He said: "This is the one." Then there was revealed: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ "Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle..." (5:55).

And from him, from Musa ibn Qays al-Hadrami, from Salamah ibn Kuhayl, who said: ‘Ali gave his ring in charity (تصدق) while he was bowing (راكع), and then إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ (the verse) was revealed.

And from him, from ‘Awf ibn ‘Ubayd ibn Abi Rafi‘, from his father, from his grandfather, who said: I entered upon the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while he was sleeping as revelation (الوحي) was being sent down to him. I noticed a snake in the corner of the house and disliked entering it or waking him. So, I lay down between him and the snake, so that if anything happened, it would be to me instead of him. He woke up while reciting this verse: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ "Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle..." (5:55). He said: "Praise be to Allah," then he came to my side and asked: "Why did you lie down here?" I said: "Because of this snake." He said: "Rise and kill it," so I killed it.

Then he took my hand and said: "O Abu Rafi‘, after me there will be a people who fight ‘Ali; it is a duty upon Allah to wage jihad against them. Whoever cannot wage jihad against them with his hand, then let him do so with his tongue, and whoever cannot with his tongue, then with his heart; there is nothing beyond that."

I say: The narrations regarding the revelation of the two verses in the context of the story of giving the ring in charity are numerous. We have extracted several of them from the book Ghayat al-Maram by al-Bahrani, and they are present in the books from which they were transmitted. We have limited ourselves to what has been cited regarding the variation in the wording (اختلاف اللحن) of the narration of the story.

A number of the companions (الصحابة) participated in transmitting it, such as Abu Dharr, Ibn ‘Abbas, Anas ibn Malik, ‘Ammar, Jabir, Salamah ibn Kuhayl, Abu Rafi‘, ‘Amr ibn al-‘As, ‘Ali, and al-Husayn, as well as al-Sajjad, al-Baqir, al-Sadiq, al-Hadi, and others from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام.

The masters of transmitted exegesis (أئمة التفسير المأثور) have agreed on its transmission without rejection, such as Ahmad, al-Nasa’i, al-Tabari, al-Tabarani, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, and other masters of Hadith (أئمة الحديث) and memorizers (الحفاظ). The theologians (المتكلمون) have accepted the occurrence of the narration, and the jurists (الفقهاء) have cited it in the issue of "excessive action" (الفعل الكثير) within the discussion of prayer, and in the issue of "whether supererogatory charity (صدقة التطوع) can be called zakat (زكاة)." Neither the giants of literature among the exegetes, such as al-Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf and Abu Hayyan in his exegesis, nor the transmitters who are masters of the language (أهل اللسان), have disputed the validity of the verse's application to the narration.

Therefore, no weight is given to what some have mentioned: that the hadith of the verse's revelation regarding the story of the ring is a fabricated (موضوع) invention. Some have even gone to extremes, such as Shaykh al-Islam Ibn Taymiyyah, who claimed a consensus (إجماع) among scholars that the narration is fabricated. This is among the most astonishing of claims, and you have already recognized the truth of the matter in the preceding commentary (البيان).

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَكُمْ هُزُوًا وَلَعِبًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَٱلْكُفَّارَ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٥٧)
O you who have faith! Do not take those who take your religion in derision and play, from among those who were given the Book before you, and the infidels, as friends, and be wary of Allah, should you be faithful. (57)
وَإِذَا نَادَيْتُمْ إِلَى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ٱتَّخَذُوهَا هُزُوًا وَلَعِبًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْقِلُونَ (٥٨)
When you call to prayer, they take it in derision and play. That is because they are a people who do not exercise their reason. (58)
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ هَلْ تَنقِمُونَ مِنَّآ إِلَّآ أَنْ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلُ وَأَنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٥٩)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! Are you vindictive toward us for any reason except that we have faith in Allah and in what has been sent down to us, and in what was sent down before, and that most of you are transgressors?’ (59)
قُلْ هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُم بِشَرٍّ مِّن ذَٰلِكَ مَثُوبَةً عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ مَن لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ وَغَضِبَ عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْقِرَدَةَ وَٱلْخَنَازِيرَ وَعَبَدَ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا وَأَضَلُّ عَن سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (٦٠)
Say, ‘Shall I inform you concerning something worse than that as a requital from Allah? Those whom Allah has cursed and with whom He is wrathful, and turned some of whom into apes and swine, and worshippers of fake deities! Such are in a worse situation and more astray from the right way.’ (60)
وَإِذَا جَآءُوكُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَقَد دَّخَلُوا۟ بِٱلْكُفْرِ وَهُمْ قَدْ خَرَجُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْتُمُونَ (٦١)
When they come to you, they say, ‘We believe.’ Certainly, they enter with disbelief and leave with it, and Allah knows best as to what they have been concealing. (61)
وَتَرَىٰ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَأَكْلِهِمُ ٱلسُّحْتَ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٦٢)
You see many of them actively engaged in sin and aggression, and consuming illicit gains. Surely, evil is what they have been doing. (62)
لَوْلَا يَنْهَىٰهُمُ ٱلرَّبَّـٰنِيُّونَ وَٱلْأَحْبَارُ عَن قَوْلِهِمُ ٱلْإِثْمَ وَأَكْلِهِمُ ٱلسُّحْتَ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ (٦٣)
Why do not the rabbis and the scribes forbid them from sinful speech and consuming illicit gains? Surely, evil is what they have been working. (63)
وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ يَدُ ٱللَّهِ مَغْلُولَةٌ ۚ غُلَّتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَلُعِنُوا۟ بِمَا قَالُوا۟ ۘ بَلْ يَدَاهُ مَبْسُوطَتَانِ يُنفِقُ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ ۚ وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم مَّآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا ۚ وَأَلْقَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ كُلَّمَآ أَوْقَدُوا۟ نَارًا لِّلْحَرْبِ أَطْفَأَهَا ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَسَادًا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٦٤)
The Jews say, ‘Allah’s hand is tied up.’ Tied up be their hands, and cursed be they for what they say! No, His hands are wide open: He bestows as He wishes. Surely many of them will be increased in rebellion and unfaith by what has been sent to you from your Lord, and We have cast enmity and hatred amongst them until the Day of Resurrection. Every time they ignite the flames of war, Allah puts them out. They seek to cause corruption on the earth, and Allah does not like the agents of corruption. (64)
وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ لَكَفَّرْنَا عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَلَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُمْ جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٦٥)
Had the People of the Book believed and been Godwary, We would surely have absolved them of their misdeeds and admitted them into gardens of bliss. (65)
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ أَقَامُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِم مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ لَأَكَلُوا۟ مِن فَوْقِهِمْ وَمِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِم ۚ مِّنْهُمْ أُمَّةٌ مُّقْتَصِدَةٌ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ سَآءَ مَا يَعْمَلُونَ (٦٦)
Had they observed the Torah and the Evangel, and what was sent down to them from their Lord, they would surely have drawn nourishment from above them and from beneath their feet. There is an upright group among them, but evil is what many of them do. (66)

(Commentary)

The verses forbid taking those who mock Allah and His signs—from among the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) and the disbelievers (الكفار)—as guardians (أولياء).

They enumerate several of their evil traits, their breaking of Allah’s covenants and pledges, and related matters in a manner that suits the purpose of the Surah—which is the exhortation to keep covenants and pledges and the condemnation of breaking them.

It appears as though the verses possess a single, continuous context, even if it is possible that some of its parts had independent occasions of revelation (شأن النزول).

His saying, the Exalted: «O you who have faith! Do not take those who take your religion» etc. Al-Raghib said: "Mockery (الهزء) is a joke made in secret, and it is sometimes said of that which is like a joke." He also said: "A person plays (لعب) when his action is not intended for a correct purpose." Indeed, a thing is taken in mockery (هزؤا) and is mocked when it is treated as having a quality that does not deserve serious attention, in order to show that it is something that should not be noticed. Similarly, a thing is played with (يلعب به) when it is not taken for any correct, rational purpose, unless it is taken for some non-real affairs. Thus, mocking the religion (الدين) and playing with it are only done to show that it is not worth anything except some false, incorrect, and non-serious purposes. Had they esteemed it as a true religion, or estimated that its Legislator, the one calling to it, and the believers in it were people of serious and sincere standing, and had they respected its and their position, they would not have placed it in such a position. Their taking the religion in mockery and play is a judgment from them that it has no reality or true standing, except to be taken as a joke or to be played with.

From here, several points become clear: First, the mention of their taking the religion in mockery and play as a description of those whose guardianship (الولاية) is forbidden is an indication of the reason for the prohibition. For guardianship—which entails spiritual intermingling and influence over psychological and social affairs—is not compatible with the guardian mocking and playing with what his protégé sanctifies, respects, and considers dearer than everything, even his own self. Thus, it is obligatory not to take one who is like this as a guardian, nor to hand over the reins of control over the soul and body to him.

Second: The suitability of using the description of faith (الإيمان) in the address in His saying: «O you who have faith» as a contrast to His saying: «those who take your religion in mockery and play», as well as the suitability of attributing the religion to them in His saying: «your religion».

Third: His saying: «and be wary of Allah, should you be faithful» is in the position of emphasizing His saying: «Do not take those who take your religion in mockery and play» etc., by repeating it with a more general and comprehensive phrasing. For the believer (المؤمن)—who has grasped the handle of faith—cannot possibly be pleased with mockery and play regarding what he believes in. If these people were truly characterized by faith—that is, if the religion was truly their religion—they would have no choice but to maintain godwariness (تقوى) toward Allah in their affairs, which means not taking them as guardians.

It is possible that His saying: «and be wary of Allah, should you be faithful» is an allusion to what He, the Exalted, mentioned in a similar verse shortly before: «And whoever among you takes them as allies is indeed one of them». The meaning would then be: Be wary of Allah regarding taking them as guardians if you are not of them. However, the first meaning is perhaps more apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: «When you call to prayer, they take it in mockery and play» etc., is a verification of what was mentioned—that they take the religion of those who believe in mockery and play. What is meant by the "call to prayer" (النداء إلى الصلاة) is the Adhan (الأذان) legislated in Islam before the daily obligatory prayers. The Adhan is not mentioned in the Noble Quran except in this place—as has been said.

The pronoun in His saying «they take it» (اتَّخَذُوها) refers back to the prayer (الصلاة) or to the verbal noun understood from His saying: «When you call», meaning the calling (المناداة). It is permissible for a pronoun referring to a verbal noun to be masculine or feminine. His saying: «That is because they are a people who do not exercise their reason» is a concluding remark (تذييل) that serves as an explanation for their action, clarifying that the occurrence of this act—namely, taking the prayer or the Adhan in mockery and play—is only because they are a people who do not exercise their reason (لا يعقلون). Thus, they cannot realize the reality of servitude (العبودية) and the benefits of proximity to Allah, and the sum of the happiness of life in this world and the Hereafter, that are contained within these pillars and religious acts of worship.

His saying, the Exalted: «Say, ‘O People of the Book! Do you take vengeance on us only because we have faith in Allah» (to the end of the verse). Al-Raghib said in Mufradat al-Quran: "You resented (نَقَمْتَ) a thing (with a kasrah) or (نَقَمْتَهُ) (with a fathah) when you disliked it, either with the tongue or by punishment." Allah the Exalted said: «They were vindictive only because Allah had enriched them», and «They were vindictive toward them only because they believed in Allah», and «Do you take vengeance on us» (the verse). And vengeance (النقمة) is punishment; Allah the Exalted said: «So We took vengeance on them and drowned them in the sea». (End of quote).

So the meaning of His saying: «Do you take vengeance on us only because we have faith» etc., is: Do you dislike or hate in us anything other than what you witness—that we have believed in Allah and what He has sent down, and that you are transgressors? This is similar to someone saying: "Do you hate anything in me except that I am chaste and you are profligate?" or "Do you resent anything in me except that I am wealthy and you are poor?" and other such instances of contrast and pairing. Thus, the meaning is: Do you resent anything in us except that we are believers and most of you are transgressors?

It has been said that His saying: «and that most of you are transgressors» assumes a hidden "lam" of causality, meaning: Do you take vengeance on us only because most of you are transgressors?

His saying: «that we have faith in Allah and what has been sent down to us and what was sent down before» carries the meaning of what was sent down to us and to you. He did not attribute it to them as a subtle reproach (تعريض), as if to say that since they did not fulfill the covenant they made with Allah and did not act according to what their books commanded them, then their books were not truly sent down to them and they are not worthy of them.

The gist of the meaning is: We do not differentiate between one book and another among what Allah sent down to His messengers, so we do not differentiate between His messengers. In this is a reproach to them, for they differentiate between the messengers of Allah and say: «We believe in some and disbelieve in others», just as they used to say: «Believe in what has been sent down to those who have faith at the beginning of the day, and disbelieve at its end». Allah the Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا بَيْنَ ذَلِكَ سَبِيلًا أُولَئِكَ هُمُ الْكَافِرُونَ حَقًّا وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَافِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُهِينًا — «Those who disbelieve in Allah and His apostles and seek to separate Allah from His apostles, and say, ‘We believe in some and disbelieve in some’ and seek to take a way in between—it is they who are truly faithless, and We have prepared a humiliating punishment for the faithless.» (An-Nisa: 150-151).

His saying, the Exalted: «Say, ‘Shall I inform you of something with a reward worse than that with Allah? Those whom Allah has cursed» (to the end of the verse). They mentioned that this is a command from Him, the Exalted, to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to address those mockers who play with the religion by way of concession (التسليم), adopting fairness in speech, to compel them to realize that if they resent the believers for their faith in Allah and what He sent down to His messengers, then they should resent themselves. For they are in a worse place and further astray from the right way (سواء السبيل) due to their being afflicted with the divine curse, transformation (المسخ) into apes and swine, and the worship of the Taghut (الطاغوت) — any entity worshipped or followed other than Allah. If they do not resent themselves for the causes of vengeance within them, they have no right to resent those who are not afflicted except with what is less than that in "evil"—namely, the believers in their faith—assuming for the sake of argument that their faith in Allah and His books is "evil," though it is never evil.

The intended meaning of "reward" (المثوبة) is absolute recompense. Perhaps it is used metaphorically for the ultimate consequence and the necessary attribute, as inferred from the qualification of His saying: «with a reward worse than that» with His saying: «with Allah». For that which is with Allah is a fixed, unchanging matter that Allah has decreed and commanded. Allah the Exalted said: «and what is with Allah is enduring» (An-Nahl: 96), and He said: «There is no reverser of His judgment» (Ar-Ra'd: 41). Thus, this reward is a necessary reward because it is with Allah, the Glorified.

In the speech, there is a kind of inversion (قلب). For the natural flow of the speech would require saying: "Cursing, transformation, and the worship of the Taghut are worse than faith in Allah and His books and more astray," rather than saying: "Those whom Allah has cursed and made into apes and swine and who worshipped the Taghut are in a worse place and more astray," except by placing the described (الموصوف) in place of the description (الوصف). This is common in the Noble Quran, such as His saying: «but righteousness is [the quality of] him who has faith in Allah» (the verse).

In short, the gist of the meaning is: If our faith in Allah and what He sent down to His messengers is evil in your view, then I shall inform you of something worse than that which you must resent, which is the attribute found in you.

It has been said that the reference in His saying: «that» (ذلِكَ) is to the collective of believers indicated by His saying: «Do you take vengeance on us». Based on this, the speech follows its natural order without inversion, and the meaning is: "Shall I inform you of those who are worse than the believers so that you may resent them? They are you yourselves, for you have been afflicted with the curse, transformation, and the worship of the Taghut."

It has also been said that His saying: «than that» refers to the verbal noun indicated by His saying: «Do you take vengeance on us», meaning: "Shall I inform you of something with a reward and recompense worse than this vengeance of yours? It is what you have been afflicted with of the curse, transformation, and so on."

His saying, the Exalted: «When they come to you, they say, ‘We have faith,’ though they certainly entered with faithlessness and they certainly left with it» (to the end of the verse). He, the Exalted, points to the hypocrisy (النفاق) of their hearts and their harboring of what Allah, the Glorified, does not approve of when they meet the believers. He said: «When they come to you, they say, ‘We have faith’», meaning they manifest faith while the reality is that they entered your presence with disbelief (الكفر) and left from your presence with disbelief. That is, they remain in the same state upon entry and exit, which is disbelief, without changing from it; they only manifest faith outwardly. Meanwhile, Allah knows what they were previously concealing of treachery and plotting.

So His saying: «though they certainly entered with faithlessness and they certainly left with it» carries the meaning of our saying: "Their state in disbelief did not change." The pronoun in His saying: «and they certainly left» (وَهُمْ قَدْ خَرَجُوا) is brought for emphasis, to distinguish them in this matter and to affirm the disbelief within them.

It has also been said that the meaning is that they are shifting through different states of disbelief.

His saying, the Exalted: «And you see many of them hastening in sin and transgression and their consuming of the unlawful» (to the end of the verse). It appears that what is meant by "sin" (الإثم) is delving into the verses of the religion sent down to the believers and speaking about the religious sciences (معارف الدين) in a way that necessitates disbelief and profligacy, as evidenced by what is in the following verse: «from their sinful speech and their consuming of the unlawful».

Based on this, the three matters—namely, sin (الإثم), transgression (العدوان), and consuming the unlawful (أكل السحت)—encompass examples of their profligacy in speech and action. They commit sin in speech, which is verbal sin; and sin in action, which is either between them and the believers, which is transgression against them, or within themselves, such as their consuming the unlawful (السحت), which is usury (الربا), bribery (الرشوة), and the like. Then He condemned this from them by saying: «Evil indeed is what they used to do». He followed this by rebuking the divines (الربانيون) and the scribes (الأحبار) for their silence regarding them and their failure to forbid them from committing these destructive sins and acts of disobedience, while they knew they were acts of disobedience and sins. He said: «Why do the divines and the scribes not forbid them from their sinful speech and their consuming of the unlawful? Evil indeed is what they used to produce».

It might be possible to infer from His saying: «from their sinful speech and their consuming of the unlawful» when applied to what is in the previous verse: «hastening in sin and transgression and their consuming of the unlawful»—where "transgression" was omitted in the second verse—that sin and transgression are one thing, which is the violation of the limits of Allah, the Glorified, in speech, as opposed to the practical disobedience exemplified by their consuming the unlawful.

Thus, the meaning of His saying: «hastening in sin and transgression and their consuming of the unlawful» would be to show a verbal evil from them, which is sin and transgression, and another practical evil from them, which is their consuming the unlawful.

"Hastening" (المسارعة) is an exaggeration of the meaning of speed (السرعة), which is the opposite of slowness. The difference between speed (السرعة) and haste (العجلة), as inferred from the instances of the use of the two words, is that speed is more related to the action of the limbs, while haste is related to the action of the heart—similar to the difference between submissiveness (الخضوع) and humility (الخشوع), or fear (الخوف) and awe (الخشية). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Speed (السرعة) is the opposite of slowness; it is used for bodies and actions... Saru'a (with a damma on the ra) means he is fast (sari'), and asra'a means he was quick (musri')... and they hastened (sara'u and tasara'u)." (End of quote).

It has been said that "hastening" (المسارعة) and "haste" (العجلة) carry the same meaning, except that "hastening" is mostly used for good. The use of "hastening" in this context—even though it is a context of condemnation and "haste" would be more indicative of condemnation—is only to indicate that they use it as if they were in the right. (End of quote). This is not without some far-fetchedness.

His saying, the Exalted: «The Jews say, ‘Allah’s hand is tied up.’ Tied up be their hands, and they are cursed for what they say! Rather, His hands are outspread: He spends as He wishes». The Jews did not believe in the permissibility of abrogation (النسخ) in religious laws; therefore, they did not accept the abrogation of the Torah and would reproach the Muslims for the abrogation of laws. Similarly, they did not believe in the permissibility of Bada' (البداء) — the manifestation of a divine decree after it was previously hidden — in ontological matters, as appears through the Quranic verses, as discussed previously in the commentary on His saying: «For any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or its like» (Al-Baqarah: 106) in the first volume of this book and in other places.

The verse—namely His saying: «The Jews say, ‘Allah’s hand is tied up’»—is capable of being applied to this saying of theirs. However, the apparent meaning of His saying in response to them: «Rather, His hands are outspread: He spends as He wishes» resists this and indicates that they only spoke this sinful word regarding something of the matter of provision (الرزق). This was either specifically regarding the believers, due to the widespread poverty, hardship, and narrowness of living among the generality of them, and they said this in mockery of Allah, the Glorified, implying that He is unable to enrich His believers and save them from poverty and humiliation. But this view does not suit the occurrence of the verse in Surah al-Ma'idah if it was revealed among its other verses, for the Muslims at the time of its revelation were in a state of prosperous living, ample provision, and well-being.

Or they said it because of a drought or high prices that afflicted them, causing their living to become narrow, their state to become miserable, and the order of their lives to be disrupted, as might appear from some reports regarding the occasions of revelation. This view is also resisted by the context of the verses, for it appears that the verses only address their various attributes regarding their transgression and plotting against the Muslims out of resentment, not what they uttered of sinful speech regarding themselves.

Or they uttered that when they heard the likes of His saying: «Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan» (Al-Baqarah: 245) and His saying: «and lend Allah a good loan» (Al-Muzzammil: 20). So they said: "Allah's hand is tied; He is unable to obtain what He spends for His needs to promote His religion and revive His call." They said this in sarcasm and mockery, as appears from other reports regarding the occasions of revelation. This view is closer to consideration.

In any case, this attribution—namely, the attribution of the hand being tied and being overcome during certain events—is something that their religious teaching and the views present in the Torah do not resist. For the Torah allows for matters to be frustrating to Allah, the Glorified, and to be a barrier preventing Him from executing some of what He intends of His purposes, like the powerful among humans. This is evidenced by the stories it narrates of the prophets, such as Adam and others.

Thus, they have aspects of belief that permit them to attribute to Him, the Exalted, what does not suit the courtyard of His holiness and the greatness of His essence, exalted is His majesty. Even if the word was only uttered by them in mockery, every action has foundations in belief from which a person is motivated and by which he is emboldened.

As for His saying: «Tied up be their hands, and they are cursed for what they say!», it is a supplication against them for a punishment similar to the deficiency they attributed to Him, the Exalted, which is unsuitable for the courtyard of His holiness—namely, the tying of the hand and the stripping of power over what He loves and wills. Based on this, His saying: «and they are cursed for what they say» is an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير) to His saying: «Tied up be their hands». For the tying of their hands is an instance of Allah’s curse upon them, since speech from Allah, the Glorified, is an act. His curse upon someone is only His punishing them with a punishment, either worldly or in the Hereafter. Thus, the curse is the punishment equal to the tying of their hands, or more general than it.

It is also possible that His saying: «Tied up be their hands» etc., is an informative statement about the occurrence of the word of punishment—which is the recompense for their audacity against Allah, the Glorified, by their saying: «Allah’s hand is tied up»—upon them. The first view is closer to understanding.

As for His saying: «Rather, His hands are outspread: He spends as He wishes», it is a response to their saying: «Allah’s hand is tied up», cast in the mold of a corrective transition (إضراب).

The sentence—namely His saying: «His hands are outspread»—is a metaphor (كناية) for the affirmation of power, and it is common in usage.

He said: «His hands» (يَداهُ) in the dual form, even though the Jews used the singular form in their saying: «Allah’s hand is tied up», to indicate the perfection of power, as might be inferred from the likes of His saying: «He said, ‘O Iblis! What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My two hands? Were you arrogant, or were you among the exalted?’» (Sad: 75), because of the intimation or indication of the exercise of the perfection of power. It is like the saying: "I have no two hands for this against you," which is an exaggeration in the negation of all power and blessing.

They have mentioned various meanings for "hand" (اليد) in the language other than the physical limb, such as power, strength, blessing, ownership, and so on. However, the truth is that the word was originally coined for the limb, and it was only used for other meanings by way of metaphor (استعارة) because they are among the affairs associated with the limb in some way—such as the association of spending and generosity with the hand in terms of its being outspread, and the association of ownership with it in terms of disposal, placing, lifting, and so on.

Thus, what the Book and the Sunnah affirm for Allah, the Glorified, of the "hand" differs in meaning according to the context. For instance, His saying: «Rather, His hands are outspread» (the verse) and His saying: «that which I have created with My two hands» (Sad: 75) refer to power and its perfection. His saying: «All good is in Your hand» (Al-Imran: 26), His saying: «So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things» (Ya-Sin: 83), and His saying: «Blessed is He in whose hand is the kingdom» (Al-Mulk: 1), and others, refer to ownership and authority. His saying: «do not venture ahead of Allah and His Apostle» (Al-Hujurat: 1) — literally "between the two hands of Allah" — refers to presence and the like.

As for His saying: «He spends as He wishes», it is an explanation of His saying: «His hands are outspread».

His saying, the Exalted: «What has been sent down to you from your Lord will surely increase many of them in rebellion and faithlessness». This sentence and what follows it to the end of the verse is a discourse arranged to clarify His saying: «The Jews say, ‘Allah’s hand is tied up.’ Tied up be their hands, and they are cursed for what they say», as provided by the context.

As for His saying: «will surely increase many of them» etc., it points out that their audacity against Allah the Great and their uttering the likes of their saying: «Allah’s hand is tied up» is not unlikely from them. For the people have been characterized by transgression and disbelief since their ancient days, and that has bequeathed them rebellion and envy. One whose disposition is such cannot be trusted, when he sees that Allah has favored someone else over him with a blessing whose value he cannot estimate, not to increase in rebellion and disbelief.

The Jews used to see themselves as having sovereignty and precedence over the world. They called themselves the People of the Book, boasted of the divines and the scribes, took pride in knowledge and wisdom, and called other people "unlettered" (أميين). When they saw a Quran revealed to a people who used to be humble before their knowledge and book—as was the respected status between them and the Arabs in the Jahiliyyah—and then they scrutinized it and found it to be a divine book dominant (مهيمنا) over the heavenly books that preceded it, and containing explicit truth, high teaching, and complete guidance, they then felt the humiliation and submissiveness that would follow in the very thing they used to take pride in—namely, knowledge and the book.

Inevitably, they would wake from their slumber, their transgressive nature would erupt, and their rebellion and disbelief would increase.

The attribution of the increase in their rebellion and disbelief to the Quran is only by virtue of the fact that their rebellious and envious souls were stirred with rebellion and disbelief upon witnessing the revelation of the Quran and perceiving the true knowledge and manifest call it contains.

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, frequently attributes guidance and misguidance in His Book to Himself, such as His saying: «To these and those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined» (Al-Isra: 20). He said specifically regarding the Quran: «We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the faithful, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss» (Al-Isra: 82). Misguidance, or what resembles it, is only considered blameworthy if it is an initial misguidance. As for that which is by way of recompense following profligacy and disobedience from the one who is astray—necessitating the descent of divine wrath upon him and requiring the arrival of what is more severe than the misguidance he is already in—there is no harm in misguidance in this sense, nor does any blame attach to it, as indicated by His saying: «and He misleads none by it except the transgressors» (Al-Baqarah: 26), and His saying: «So when they deviated, Allah made their hearts deviate» (As-Saff: 5).

Ultimately, the meaning of the Quran increasing them in rebellion and disbelief returns to the withdrawal of success (سلب التوفيق) and the lack of divine care in turning them back from the rebellion and disbelief in Allah’s signs they are in, toward submission and faith by answering the true call. The discussion of this meaning has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: «and He misleads none by it except the transgressors» (Al-Baqarah: 26) in the first volume of this book.

Let us return to the beginning of the discourse. His saying: «will surely increase many of them» etc., is as if it were intended to remove the improbability and wonder arising from the audacity of these people—who are called the People of the Book and who claim to be the children of Allah and His beloved ones—against their Lord with such a humiliating and disparaging word: "Allah's hand is tied."

Indeed, this increase in rebellion and disbelief—of which this word is one of the effects and which will be followed by one distorted effect after another—is a certain and necessary outcome for them. This is what is inferred from the emphasis indicated by the "lam" of the oath and the "nun" of emphasis in His saying: «will surely increase» (لَيَزِيدَنَّ).

In following "rebellion" (الطغيان) with "disbelief" (الكفر) rather than the reverse, it follows the natural order, for disbelief is among the effects and consequences of rebellion.

His saying, the Exalted: «And We have cast enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection». The pronoun "among them" refers to the Jews, as is apparent from the sentence occurring in the context of the discourse on the Jews specifically, even if the verses began by speaking about the People of the Book in general. Based on this, what is meant by "enmity and hatred among them" is what relates to the differences in sects and views. Allah, the Glorified, has pointed to this in several places in His speech, such as His saying: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ — (until He said) — فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ — «Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood... They did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ.» (Al-Jathiyah: 16-17), and other verses.

"Enmity" (العداوة) is intended to mean hatred that is accompanied by transgression in action, while "hatred" (البغضاء) is the absolute state of aversion in the heart, even if it does not result in transgression in action. Thus, their combination conveys the meaning of hatred that necessitates injustice toward others and hatred that falls short of that.

In His saying, the Exalted: «until the Day of Resurrection», there is an undeniable indication of the survival of their nation until the end of the world.

His saying, the Exalted: «Whenever they ignite a fire for war, Allah puts it out». Igniting a fire is lighting it, and putting it out is extinguishing it. The meaning is clear. It is possible that His saying: «Whenever they ignite» etc., is an explanation of His saying: «And We have cast enmity» etc., so the meaning would return to the fact that whenever they stir up a war against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the believers, Allah puts it out by casting discord among them.

The verse, as indicated by the context, records against them the failure of their efforts in igniting the fires they light against the religion of Allah, the Glorified, and against the Muslims because they are believers in Allah and His signs. As for the wars whose fire they might be able to ignite not for the sake of the true religion but for politics or racial or national dominance, they are outside the scope of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: «And they strive for corruption in the earth, and Allah does not love the agents of corruption». Striving (السعي) is walking quickly. His saying: «for corruption» (فَساداً) is an object of purpose, meaning they exert themselves to corrupt the earth. And Allah does not love the agents of corruption (المفسدين), so He does not leave them to attain what they intended of corrupting the earth, and thus their effort fails. Allah knows best.

All of this is an explanation of their hands being tied and their being cursed for what they said, in that they do not attain what they intended of stirring up wars against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Muslims, nor what they exerted themselves for of corrupting the earth.

His saying, the Exalted: «Had the People of the Book believed and been God-conscious, We would have surely absolved them of their misdeeds» etc. This is a return to the state of the People of the Book in general, just as the discourse began with them in general. The discourse concludes by summarizing what they missed of the blessing of happiness in the Hereafter and this world—namely, the Garden of Bliss and the blessing of a happy life.

What is meant by godwariness (التقوى) after faith is refraining from the prohibitions of Allah and avoiding the sins that necessitate divine wrath and the punishment of the Fire. These are shirk (الشرك) — associating partners with Allah — and other destructive major sins (الكبائر) for which Allah has promised the Fire. Thus, the "misdeeds" (السيئات) that Allah, the Glorified, promised to absolve would be the minor sins. This corresponds to His saying, the Glorified: «If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you to a noble abode» (An-Nisa: 31).

His saying, the Exalted: «Had they observed the Torah and the Evangel and what was sent down to them from their Lord, they would surely have been provided from above them and from beneath their feet». What is meant by the Torah and the Evangel (الإنجيل) are the two heavenly books that the Quran mentions Allah sent down to Moses and Jesus (peace be upon them), not the books in the hands of the people which it mentions have been played with by the hand of distortion (التحريف).

It appears that what is meant by "what was sent down to them from their Lord" are the other books attributed to the prophets that are present with them, such as the Psalms of David—which the Quran calls the Zabur (الزبور)—and other books.

As for the possibility that what is meant by it is the Quran, this is made unlikely by the fact that the Quran abrogated with its laws the legislations of the Torah and the Evangel. Thus, there is no reason to count them along with it and wish that they had observed them along with the Quran that abrogates them. The argument that acting upon the Quran is also acting upon them—just as acting upon the abrogating laws in Islam is acting upon the sum of the legislations of Islam which include both the abrogating and the abrogated, because the religion of Allah is one and its parts do not conflict, rather some laws are deferred and timed without contradiction—is refuted by the fact that Allah, the Glorified, expressed this action as "observing" (الإقامة), which is keeping a thing standing on its own. This does not suit the abrogated laws insofar as they are abrogated. Observing the Torah and the Evangel was only correct when the two legislations had not been abrogated by another legislation; and the Evangel did not abrogate the legislation of the Torah except in a few matters.

Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted: «and what was sent down to them from their Lord» considers them as those to whom it was sent down, and it is not customary in His speech to mention that the Quran was sent down to them.

So it appears that what is meant by "what was sent down to them from their Lord" after the Torah and the Evangel are the other books and types of revelation sent down to the prophets of the Children of Israel, such as the Zabur of David and others. What is meant by "observing" these books is maintaining the general practice of what is in them of the laws of Allah, the Exalted, and believing in what Allah, the Exalted, explained in them of the knowledge of the Beginning and the Return, without them being struck by the veils of distortion, concealment, and explicit abandonment. Had they observed them with this observation, they would have been provided from above them and from beneath their feet.

As for His saying, the Exalted: «they would surely have been provided from above them and from beneath their feet» — literally "they would have eaten" — the intended meaning of "eating" is enjoying blessings in general, whether by eating as in the case of food or otherwise. The use of "eating" for absolute disposal and enjoyment without hindrance is common in the language.

What is meant by "from above them" is the heaven, and "from beneath their feet" is the earth. Thus, the sentence is a metaphor for their enjoyment of the blessings of the heaven and the earth and the encompassing of their blessings upon them, similar to what occurred in His saying, the Exalted: «Had the people of the towns believed and been God-conscious, We would have surely opened for them blessings from the heaven and the earth; but they denied, so We seized them for what they used to earn» (Al-A'raf: 96).

The verse is part of the evidence that the faith of this species—namely, the human species—and its righteous deeds have an effect on the well-being of the cosmic order in terms of its connection to the human species. If this species becomes righteous, the order of the world becomes righteous in terms of its fulfilling what is necessary for a happy human life, by the repelling of afflictions and the abundance of blessings.

Many other verses in the Quran indicate this by the generality of their phrasing, such as His saying, the Exalted: ظَهَرَ الْفَسَادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلُ كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُشْرِكِينَ — «Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned, that He may let them taste some of what they have done, so that they may come back. Say, ‘Travel in the land and see how was the end of those who were before [you]: most of them were polytheists.’» (Ar-Rum: 41-42), and His saying, the Exalted: «Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned» (Ash-Shura: 30), and others. Some of what relates to this has already preceded in the discussion on the rulings of actions in the second volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: «Among them is a modest community, but many of them—evil is what they do». Modesty (الاقتصاد) is taking the middle path (القصد), which is moderation in affairs. Thus, the "modest community" (أُمَّةٌ مُقْتَصِدَةٌ) is the one that is moderate in the matter of religion and submission to the command of Allah.

The speech is a new sentence (مستأنف) intended to clarify the state of all that was attributed to them of transgressing the limits of Allah, disbelieving in Allah’s signs, and the descent of wrath and the curse upon their group—that all of this was only practiced by most of them, which is what justifies the attribution of these atrocities to them. And that among them is a moderate community that does not have this attribute. This is part of the fairness of the divine speech, as it does not waste any right and is careful to revive the matter of truth even if it is little.

This was also addressed in the course of the previous verses, but not with this level of explicitness, such as His saying: «and that most of you are transgressors», His saying: «And you see many of them hastening» etc., and His saying: «What has been sent down to you from your Lord will surely increase many of them in rebellion and faithlessness».

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: «When they come to you, they say, ‘We have faith’» (the verse)—he said: "It was revealed regarding Abdullah ibn Ubayy when he manifested Islam while they had entered with disbelief."

I say: The apparent context is that it was revealed regarding the People of the Book, not the hypocrites (المنافقين), unless it was revealed on its own.

And in it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «and they certainly left with it» (the verse)—he said: "He said: They left with it from faith."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, from Umar ibn Riyyah, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: It has reached me that you say—whoever divorces not according to the Sunnah, you do not consider his divorce to be anything." Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "It is not I who say it, but Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says it. By Allah, if we were to give you legal rulings (نفتيكم) with injustice, we would be worse than you! Indeed, Allah says: «Why do the divines and the scribes not forbid them from their sinful speech and their consuming of the unlawful?»"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Basir, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): Umar ibn Riyyah claimed that you said: 'There is no divorce except with evidence'?" He said: "He said: It was not I who said it, but Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, says it. By Allah, if we were to give you legal rulings with injustice, we would be worse than you! Indeed, Allah says: «Why do the divines and the scribes not forbid them...»"

In Majalis al-Shaykh, with its chain of narration from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Hisham ibn Salim, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding the saying of Allah, the Exalted: «The Jews say, ‘Allah’s hand is tied up’»—he said: "They used to say: The matter has been concluded."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated this meaning in his Tafsir from Ya'qub ibn Shu'ayb and from Hammad, from him (peace be upon him).

In Tafsir al-Qummi: "He said: They said: Allah has concluded the matter—He does not bring about anything other than what He decreed in the first decree. So Allah refuted them and said: «Rather, His hands are outspread: He spends as He wishes», meaning He brings forward and defers, and He increases and decreases, and to Him belongs Bada' and the Will (المشية)."

I say: Al-Saduq narrated this meaning in al-Ma'ani, with its chain of narration from Ishaq ibn Ammar, from one who heard it from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Hisham al-Mashriqi, from Abu al-Hasan al-Khurasani (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah is as He described Himself: One, Besought, Light." Then he said: «Rather, His hands are outspread». I said to him: "Does He have two hands like this?"—and I pointed with my hand to his hand. He said: "If it were like this, He would be a created being."
