I say: Al-Saduq narrated it in al-Uyun, with its chain of narration from al-Mashriqi, from him (peace be upon him).

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "I asked Ja'far (peace be upon him) and said: His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: «O Iblis! What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My two hands?»" He said: "The hand (اليد) in the speech of the Arabs is power (القوة) and blessing (النعمة)." He said: «And remember Our servant David, the man of strength (الأيد)», and «We have built the heaven with might (بأيد)—meaning with power—and We are indeed expanding it». He said: «and He strengthened them with a spirit from Him»—he said: "Meaning He empowered them. And it is said: 'So-and-so has a white hand (يد بيضاء) with me,' meaning a blessing."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: «Had they observed the Torah and the Evangel» (the verse): "Meaning the Jews and the Christians—«they would surely have been provided from above them and from beneath their feet»—he said: He said: From above them is the rain, and from beneath their feet is the vegetation."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: «Among them is a modest community» (the verse), from Abu al-Sahba al-Kubra, who said: "I heard Ali ibn Abi Talib call the Exilarch (رأس الجالوت) and the Bishop of the Christians. He said: 'I am asking you both about a matter that I know better than you, so do not conceal it.' Then he called the Bishop of the Christians and said: 'I adjure you by Allah who sent down the Evangel to Jesus, and placed blessing upon his foot, and he used to heal the blind and the leper, and removed the pain of the eye, and revived the dead, and made birds for you out of clay, and informed you of what you eat and what you store up.' He said: 'Less than this would be enough for me to be truthful.'"

Ali (peace be upon him) said: "Into how many sects did the Children of Israel split after Jesus?" He said: "No, by Allah, not even one sect." Ali (peace be upon him) said: "You have lied, by Allah besides whom there is no god! They certainly split into seventy-two sects—all of them are in the Fire except for one sect. Indeed, Allah says: «Among them is a modest community, but many of them—evil is what they do»; so this is the one that is saved."

In it, from Zayd ibn Aslam, from Anas ibn Malik, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to say: 'The community of Moses split into seventy-one sects, seventy of them are in the Fire and one is in Paradise. The community of Jesus split into seventy-two sects, seventy-one are in the Fire and one is in Paradise. And my community will surpass both communities together with one creed—one in Paradise and seventy-two in the Fire.' They said: 'Who are they, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'The groups, the groups (الجماعات).'"

In it: Ya'qub ibn Yazid said: "Whenever Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) narrated this tradition from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), he would recite Quranic verses regarding it: «Had the People of the Book believed and been God-conscious, We would have surely absolved them of their misdeeds»—to His saying—«evil is what they do». And he also recited: «Among those We have created are a nation who guide by the truth and do justice thereby», meaning the community of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

Ayah

۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۖ وَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٦٧)
O Apostle! Communicate that which has been sent down to you from your Lord, and if you do not, you will not have communicated His message, and Allah shall protect you from the people. Indeed Allah does not guide the faithless lot. (67)

(Commentary)

The meaning of the verse in itself is clear, as it contains a command to the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to convey the message in the form of a threat, and a promise to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of protection from the people. However, contemplating the verse in terms of its placement where it occurs reveals more; it is surrounded by verses addressing the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), censuring and rebuking them for the various types of transgression against the sanctities of Allah and disbelief in His signs that they used to alternate between. The two verses on either side are connected to it, namely His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ أَقَامُوا التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ وَمَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ لَأَكَلُوا مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ وَمِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِمْ — "Had they observed the Torah and the Evangel, and what was sent down to them from their Lord, they would surely have drawn nourishment from above them and from beneath their feet" (5:66), and His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَى شَيْءٍ حَتَّى تُقِيمُوا التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ وَمَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! You do not stand on anything until you observe the Torah and the Evangel and what was sent down to you from your Lord’" (5:68).

Furthermore, deep reflection on the verse itself and the relationship between the sentences arranged within it increases one's wonder upon wonder.

If the verse were connected to what precedes and follows it in a single context (سياق) regarding the People of the Book, its sum total would be a most emphatic command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to convey what Allah, the Glorified, had sent down concerning the People of the Book. According to the context, it would be determined that what was "sent down to him from his Lord" refers to what He commands him to convey in His saying: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَى شَيْءٍ حَتَّى تُقِيمُوا التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ وَمَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ (5:68).

However, the context of the verse rejects this, for His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ — "and Allah will guard you from mankind" (5:67) indicates that this revealed ruling commanded to be conveyed is a momentous matter involving fear of danger to the person of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or to the religion of Allah (دين الله) in terms of the success of its conveyance. It was not the habit of the Jews or the Christians during the era of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that a danger would be directed toward him from their side that would justify him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) withholding the conveyance or delaying it for a time, such that the matter would reach a point where Allah needed to promise him protection (العصمة) from them if he conveyed what he was commanded regarding them. This was true even in the early days of his migration (الهجرة) to Medina, when the Jews were at their peak of intensity and severity, until it ended with the events of Khaybar and others.

Moreover, the verse does not contain a severe command or harsh speech, and there had already preceded it the conveyance of what was more severe, harsher, and more bitter for the Jews than that. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had been commanded to convey what was even more intense, such as the conveyance of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the negation of idolatry to the disbelievers of Quraysh and the polytheists of the Arabs, who were more coarse, more violent, more prone to bloodshed, and more lethal than the Jews and the rest of the People of the Book. Yet, Allah did not threaten him regarding the conveyance to them, nor did He grant him security through protection from them.

Furthermore, the verses addressing the state of the People of the Book constitute the majority of Surah al-Ma'idah, so they were certainly revealed during that period. By the time this Surah was revealed, the power of the Jews had been broken, their fires extinguished, and they were covered by wrath and curse; whenever they kindled a fire for war, Allah extinguished it. Thus, there is no meaning for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to fear them regarding the religion of Allah, as they had by then entered into peace within the fold of Islam, and they and the Christians had accepted the tribute (الجزية). There is no meaning in the Exalted affirming his fear of them and his agitation in conveying Allah's command to them, when he had already conveyed to them what was greater than it, and had stood before this in situations far more terrifying and desolate.

Therefore, there should be no doubt that the verse does not share the context of the verses preceding and following it, nor is it connected to them in their sequence; rather, it is a single verse that was revealed alone.

The verse reveals a matter that had been sent down to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) (either the religion in its entirety or some of its parts), and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) feared the people regarding its conveyance and was delaying it until a suitable time. Were it not for his fear and withholding, there would have been no need for the threat in His saying: وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ — "and if you do not, you will not have communicated His message" (5:67), as occurred in the verses of the beginning of the Mission (البعثة) which were devoid of threats, such as His saying, the Exalted: اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ — "Read in the name of your Lord who created" (96:1) to the end of Surah al-Alaq, and His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ قُمْ فَأَنْذِرْ — "O you enwrapped in your mantle! Stand up and warn!" (74:1-2), and His saying: فَاسْتَقِيمُوا إِلَيْهِ وَاسْتَغْفِرُوهُ وَوَيْلٌ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ — "so take a straight path to Him and plead to Him for forgiveness. And woe to the polytheists" (41:6), among others.

Thus, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) feared them, but his fear was not for his own self in the presence of Allah, the Glorified. He is far too exalted to recoil from sacrificing himself or to be stingy with his lifeblood in any matter of Allah. This is something belied by his noble biography and the manifestations of his life. Moreover, Allah bore witness regarding His messengers to the contrary, as He, the Exalted, said: مَا كَانَ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ مِنْ حَرَجٍ فِيمَا فَرَضَ اللَّهُ لَهُ سُنَّةَ اللَّهِ فِي الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ قَدَرًا مَقْدُورًا الَّذِينَ يُبَلِّغُونَ رِسَالَاتِ اللَّهِ وَيَخْشَوْنَهُ وَلَا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَدًا إِلَّا اللَّهَ وَكَفَى بِاللَّهِ حَسِيبًا — "There is no blame on the Prophet in respect of what Allah has made obligatory for him: Allah’s precedent with those who passed away before, and Allah’s command is a determinate decree. Such as deliver the messages of Allah and fear Him, and fear no one except Allah, and Allah suffices as a reckoner" (33:38-39). Regarding such assumptions, He, the Exalted, said: فَلَا تَخَافُوهُمْ وَخَافُونِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "so do not fear them, but fear Me, should you be faithful" (3:175). Allah, the Glorified, praised a group of His servants for not fearing the people even when the people tried to frighten them, saying: الَّذِينَ قَالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ إِنَّ النَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ فَاخْشَوْهُمْ فَزَادَهُمْ إِيمَانًا وَقَالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللَّهُ وَنِعْمَ الْوَكِيلُ — "Those to whom the people said, ‘All the people have gathered against you, so fear them.’ That only increased them in faith, and they said, ‘Allah is sufficient for us, and He is an excellent trustee’" (3:173).

It is not permissible to say that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) feared for his life that they might kill him, thereby nullifying the effect of the call (الدعوة) and cutting off its roots, so he was delaying it until a time devoid of this corruption. For Allah, the Glorified, says to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ — "You have no hand in the matter" (3:128). Allah, the Glorified, would not have been rendered incapable, had they killed the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), of reviving His call by whatever means He willed and by whatever cause He desired.

Yes, it is possible to interpret the meaning of His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ as the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) fearing the people regarding the conveyance of this matter—that they might accuse him of something that would corrupt the call with a corruption from which it would never succeed. Such an opinion and exercise of judgment (الاجتهاد) was permissible for him and authorized, without the meaning of fear returning to his own self in any way.

From here it becomes clear that the verse was not revealed at the beginning of the Mission, as some commentators (المفسرين) hold, for there would be no meaning then for His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ unless the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were procrastinating in fulfilling the conveyance out of fear of the people for his life—that they might kill him and he would be deprived of life, or that they might kill him and the conveyance would go in vain without effect. All of this is impossible to entertain.

Furthermore, if the intended meaning of what was "sent down to him from his Lord" were the foundation of the religion or its entirety, the meaning of His saying: وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ would return to something like our saying: "O Messenger, convey the religion, and if you do not convey the religion, you have not conveyed the religion."

As for making it similar to the saying of Abu al-Najm: I am Abu al-Najm and my poetry is my poetry.

As mentioned by some, the meaning of the verse would be: "And if you do not convey the message, then the ignominy of shortcoming in conveyance and negligence in hastening to obey what Allah, the Glorified, commanded you and emphasized upon you has become necessary for you," just as the meaning of Abu al-Najm's saying is: "I am Abu al-Najm and my poetry is my poetry, known for eloquence and famous for ingenuity."

This is invalid because this rhetorical device (الصناعة الكلامية) is only correct in cases of the general (العام) and the specific (الخاص), the absolute (المطلق) and the restricted (المقيد), and their likes, where this context conveys their unity. For example, Abu al-Najm's saying "my poetry is my poetry" means: "No one should imagine that my talent has dulled or that events have exhausted me from composing the poetry I used to compose; the poetry I recite today is the same poetry I recited yesterday."

However, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ, such a nuance does not apply. The message (الرسالة), which is the entirety of the religion or its foundation—on the assumption that the verse was revealed at the start of the Mission—is a single matter that does not differ or change, such that it would be correct to say: "If you do not convey this message, you have not conveyed that message," or "you have not conveyed the foundation of the message," for the assumption is that it is the foundation of the message which is the sum of religious knowledge.

Thus, it has become clear that the verse, by its context, is not suitable to have been revealed at the start of the Mission with the intended meaning of what was sent down to the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being the entirety of the religion or its foundation. It also becomes clear that it is not suitable to have been revealed specifically regarding the conveyance of the entirety of the religion or its foundation at any other time besides the start of the Mission, for the problem arises from the necessity of redundancy (اللغو) in His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ, as mentioned.

Moreover, His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ does not suit revelation at any time other than the start of the Mission on the assumption that the "message" means the entirety of the religion or its foundation, which is obvious.

Furthermore, the problem of the indication of His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ—that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) feared the people in his conveyance—remains as it is.

It has thus appeared that this matter which was sent down to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and whose conveyance the verse emphasized, is not the entirety of the religion or its foundation under any of the assumed scenarios. Let us then posit that it is a part of the religion. The meaning would be: "Convey the ruling that has been sent down to you from your Lord, and if you do not, you have not conveyed His message," etc. This assumption necessitates that the "message" (الرسالة) refers to the sum of what the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) carried of the religion and his mission; otherwise, the previous problem—the necessity of redundancy in the speech—remains. For if the intended meaning of His saying: رِسَالَتَهُ were the specific message of this ruling, the meaning would be: "Convey this ruling, and if you do not convey it, you have not conveyed it," which is obvious redundancy.

So the meaning is: "Convey this ruling, and if you do not convey it, you have not conveyed the foundation of His message or its entirety." This is a correct and rational meaning. In this case, the speech occurs similarly to the instance of Abu al-Najm's saying: "I am Abu al-Najm and my poetry is my poetry."

As for this ruling being such that if it were not conveyed, it would be as if the message had not been conveyed, that is because religious knowledge and rulings are interconnected such that if one matter among them is neglected, all of them are neglected, especially in conveyance due to the perfection of the connection. While this assumption is in itself unobjectionable, the end of the verse, which is His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكَافِرِينَ, does not suit it. This conclusion reveals that a group of disbelievers (كافرين) among the people intended to oppose this revealed ruling, or it was expected from their state that they would oppose it severely and take every measure they could to nullify this call and leave it inoperative, neither producing an effect nor benefiting anything. Allah promised His Messenger that He would protect him from them, nullify their plot, and not guide them in their scheme.

This meaning does not hold with just any assumed revealed ruling. Religious knowledge and rulings in Islam are not all at the same level; among them is that which is the pillar of the religion, and among them is the supplication upon seeing the crescent moon, and among them is the adultery of a married person, and among them is looking at a stranger woman. It is not correct to assume this fear from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the promise of protection from Allah with every single ruling regardless of what it is, but rather in some rulings.

Thus, the failure to convey this ruling necessitating the failure to convey other rulings is only due to its importance and its position among the rulings—a position such that if its matter were neglected, it would in reality be a neglect of the matter of all other rulings, and they would become like a body devoid of the spirit by which there is lasting life, sensation, and movement. The verse would then reveal that Allah, the Glorified, had commanded His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with a ruling by which the matter of the religion is completed and by which it settles upon the throne of stability. It was expected that the people would oppose it and overturn the matter against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) such that the pillars of the structure of the religion he built would collapse and its parts would dissipate. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) perceived this and feared for his call from them, so he delayed its conveyance from time to time to find a suitable vessel and a safe atmosphere in which his call might succeed and his effort not be frustrated. So Allah commanded him to convey it immediately, explained to him the importance of the ruling, promised him that He would protect him from the people, would not guide them in their scheme, and would not let them overturn the matter of the call against him.

The overturning of the matter of the call against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the nullification of his work can only be imagined after the spread of the Islamic call, not from the side of the polytheists and the idolaters of the Arabs or others—as if the verse were revealed in Mecca before the Migration, and the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) fear of the people was due to their fabrication against him and their accusation of him in his matter, as Allah, the Glorified, recounted of their saying: مُعَلَّمٌ مَجْنُونٌ — "A tutored madman" (44:14).

And their saying: شَاعِرٌ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِهِ رَيْبَ الْمَنُونِ — "A poet for whom we await the vicissitudes of time" (52:30); and their saying: سَاحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ — "A magician or a madman" (51:52); and their saying: إِنْ تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَسْحُورًا — "You follow only a bewitched man" (17:47); and their saying: إِنْ هَذَا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ يُؤْثَرُ — "This is nothing but magic handed down" (74:24); and their saying: أَسَاطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ اكْتَتَبَهَا فَهِيَ تُمْلَى عَلَيْهِ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا — "Myths of the ancients which he has had written down, and they are dictated to him morning and evening" (25:5); and their saying: إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ — "It is only a human who teaches him" (16:103); and their saying: أَنِ امْشُوا وَاصْبِرُوا عَلَى آلِهَتِكُمْ إِنَّ هَذَا لَشَيْءٌ يُرَادُ — "Go and stand by your gods! This is indeed a thing intended [against you]" (38:6), among other things they said about him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

All of these are not among the things that cause the foundation of the religion to weaken; rather, they indicate—if they indicate anything—the agitation of the people in their affair and their lack of steadfastness in it. Moreover, these fabrications and aspersions were not exclusive to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) such that he would be agitated upon perceiving them and fear their occurrence; for the rest of the prophets and messengers shared with him the affliction of these trials and tribulations, and the facing of these hardships from their respective nations, as Allah, the Exalted, recounted regarding Noah and the prophets mentioned after him in the Quran.

Rather, if there were anything—and there must be—it can only be imagined after the Migration and the establishment of the matter of the religion in the Islamic society, while the Muslims were like a mixed paste of righteous believers and a group of hypocrites (منافقين) possessing a power not to be underestimated, and others in whose hearts was a sickness and who were "listeners" [to falsehood]—as the Mighty Book has stipulated. These people used to treat the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—while they had believed in him truly or outwardly—with the treatment of kings, and the religion of Allah with the treatment of man-made national laws, as suggested by groups of verses in the Book, some of which have been interpreted in previous volumes of this book (1).

Thus, it was possible that the conveyance of some rulings might be among those that would cause the illusion that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was benefiting from its legislation and implementation, necessitating that it occur in their hearts that he was a king in the form of prophecy and a royal law in the guise of religion, as some evidence of this may be found in the folds of the words of some of them (2).

This is a doubt which, had it or its like occurred in their hearts, would have cast into the religion such corruption and loss that no repelling force could ward it off, nor any reforming measure fix it. Therefore, this revealed ruling commanded to be conveyed is none other than a ruling in which there is a perceived benefit for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and an exclusivity for him of a vital privilege among the privileges of life in which no one else among the Muslims shares, similar to what is in the story of Zayd, the plurality of wives, the exclusivity of the fifth (خمس) of the spoils, and the like.

However, if the specific characteristics were among those that did not touch the general body of Muslims, it was not in their nature to stir doubt in the hearts. For example, marrying the wife of an adopted son was not exclusive to him. And marrying more than four women—if its permissibility for himself were out of desire without the permission of Allah, the Glorified—nothing would have prevented him from making the same permissible for the rest of the Muslims. His biography in preferring the Muslims over himself in the wealth he took for Allah and himself, and similar matters, leaves no doubt for a doubter nor does its matter become ambiguous for one who might be confused, without the doubt being removed.

It has appeared from all that has preceded that the verse reveals a revealed ruling in which there is a tinge of benefit for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and his exclusivity with a vital privilege desired by others as well, the conveyance and implementation of which would necessitate the deprivation of the people from it. Thus, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) feared disclosing it, so Allah commanded him to convey it, emphasized it, and promised him protection from the people and that He would not guide them in their plot if they plotted regarding it.

This supports what has been narrated in the reports (النصوص) from the paths of both parties [Sunni and Shi'a] that the verse was revealed regarding the matter of the guardianship (ولاية) of Ali (عليه‌السلام), and that Allah commanded its conveyance, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) feared that they would accuse him regarding his cousin, and he was delaying its conveyance from time to time until the verse was revealed. So he conveyed it at Ghadir Khumm and said regarding him: "Whomsoever I am his master (مولاه), this Ali is his master."

The fact that the guardianship of the affair of the nation (الأمة) is something the religion cannot do without is obvious and not hidden. How can it be permissible for a surmiser to surmise that the religion—which establishes in its breadth for all of humanity in all ages and regions everything related to the fundamental knowledge, the ethical principles, and the branch rulings general to all human movements and stillness, individually and collectively, contrary to all general laws—does not need a guardian to guard it with true guardianship? Or that the Islamic nation and the religious society are an exception among all human societies, being independent of a ruler to take charge of its affair, a manager to manage it, and an executor to execute it? With what excuse can one apologize to a researcher of the Prophet's social biography? For he sees that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to, whenever he went out for a battle, leave in his place a man to manage the millstone of society. He left Ali in his place over Medina—during his march to Tabuk—and he said: "O Messenger of Allah, do you leave me behind with the women and children?" He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) replied: "Are you not pleased to be to me as Aaron was to Moses—except that there is no prophet after me?"

He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to appoint governors and rulers in the lands held by the Muslims, such as Mecca, Ta'if, Yemen, and others, and he would appoint men over the detachments and armies he sent to the frontiers. What difference is there between the time of his life and after his death, except that the need for that after his absence through death is more intense, and the necessity for it is more and more urgent?

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ — He addressed him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by the title of "Messenger" (الرسول) because it is the most suitable attribute to what the verse contains of the command to convey the ruling of Allah that was sent down. It is like a proof for the obligation of conveyance which the verse manifests and strikes upon the ear of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); for the Messenger has no function except to convey the message he has been charged with, so carrying the message imposes upon him the duty of conveyance.

He did not explicitly name this which was sent down to him from his Lord, but rather expressed it by an attribute—that it is something sent down to him—to signal its magnification and to indicate that it is a matter in which the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has no hand, nor does he have any part in its command. This serves as another proof for his lack of choice in concealing it or delaying its conveyance, and it serves as an excuse for him in disclosing it to the people, and an allusion that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was correct in what he perceived from them and feared for it, and an intimation that it is something that must manifest from his side (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and by his tongue and explanation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ — The intended meaning of His saying: رِسَالَتَهُ (and it is read as "His messages" (رسالاته) as mentioned before) is the sum of the messages of Allah, the Glorified, which His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) carried. It has already been mentioned that the speech conveys the importance of this alluded-to ruling, and that it has such a status that if he did not convey it, it would be as if he had not conveyed any of the messages he carried.

The speech is placed in the form of a threat, but its reality is an explanation of the importance of the ruling—that it is such that if it did not reach the people and its right were not observed, it would be as if the right of none of the parts of the religion had been observed. So His saying: وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ is a conditional sentence (جملة شرطية) brought to explain the importance of the condition in its existence and non-existence, due to the most important consequence (الجزاء) being dependent upon it in its existence and non-existence.

It is not a conditional sentence brought according to the nature of the conditional sentences common among us; for we naturally use the conditional "if" (إن) in what we are ignorant of the realization of the consequence due to ignorance of the realization of the condition. Far be it from the station of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that the Quran would posit regarding him the possibility that he might convey the ruling sent down to him from his Lord and might not convey it, while He, the Exalted, has said: اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسَالَتَهُ — "Allah knows best where to place His message" (6:124).

So the sentence, namely His saying: وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ (etc.), only conveys a threat by its outward form, while it conveys the informing of him (عليه‌السلام) and the informing of others of the importance of this ruling, and that the Messenger is excused in its conveyance.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكَافِرِينَ — Al-Raghib said: al-Ism (العصم) means to hold back, and al-I'tisam (الاعتصام) means to hold fast... until he said: and al-Isam (العصام) is that which one holds fast with, i.e., binds. The protection (عصمة) of the prophets is His guarding them, first by what He distinguished them with of purity of essence, then by what He granted them of physical and spiritual virtues, then by victory and by making their feet firm, then by sending down tranquility (السكينة) upon them, by guarding their hearts, and by success (التوفيق). He, the Exalted, said: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ.

Al-Ismah (العصمة) is like a bracelet, and al-Ma'sim (المعصم) is its place on the hand [the wrist]. It is said of the whiteness on the wrist Ismah, likening it to a bracelet, just as the whiteness on the leg is called Tahjil. Based on this, it is said: Ghurab A'sam [a white-winged crow]. End quote.

What he mentioned regarding the meaning of the protection of the prophets is good and unobjectionable, except that it does not apply to the verse وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ. Rather, if it were to apply, it would apply to something like His saying: وَمَا يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَأَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ مَا لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ وَكَانَ فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيمًا — "they cannot harm you in any way. Allah has sent down to you the Book and wisdom, and He has taught you what you did not know, and Allah’s grace upon you has been great" (4:113).

As for His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ, its outward meaning is that it is a protection in the sense of guarding and shielding from the evil of the people directed toward the noble person of the Prophet, or his religious objectives, or the success of his conveyance and the prosperity of his effort—in short, the meaning suitable for his sacred station.

In any case, what is gathered from the instances of the word's usage is that it is in the sense of holding back and seizing. Its use in the sense of guarding is of the type of metaphorically using the necessary consequence for the thing necessitated, for guarding necessitates seizing.

The linking of protection to "the people" (الناس) without explaining from which aspect of the people's affairs the protection is—whether from their transgressions by physical harm such as killing, poison, or any assassination; or by speech such as insult and fabrication; or by other means such as overturning matters through some kind of plot, deception, and scheme—in short, the silence regarding the specification of what he is protected from is to convey a type of generalization. However, what the context does not deviate from is their evil which would necessitate the overturning of the matter against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) such that what he raised of the banners of the religion would fall thereby.

"The people" (الناس) is an absolute term for whoever possesses the meaning of humanity, without considering any of their natural, creational specificities such as masculinity or femininity, or non-natural ones such as knowledge, virtue, wealth, and others. Therefore, it rarely applies to other than a group. For that reason also, it sometimes indicates the virtuous among humans if virtue is considered in the existence of the meaning of humanity, such as His saying, the Exalted: إِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ آمِنُوا كَمَا آمَنَ النَّاسُ — "When they are told, ‘Believe as the people have believed’" (2:13), meaning those in whom the meaning of humanity is found, which is the criterion for perceiving truth and distinguishing it from falsehood.

Sometimes it indicates a type of meanness and lowliness of state, and that is when the matter being spoken of requires the consideration of some human virtues that are considered additional to the original meaning of the species, such as His saying: وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ — "but most people do not know" (30:30), and your saying: "Do not trust the promises of the people," and "Do not rely on their masses," looking at the fact that trust and reliance must be linked to the virtuous among humans who possess the trait of fulfilling covenants and steadfastness in resolve, not to those who have nothing but the mere application of the name of humanity. Sometimes it does not convey anything of praise or blame if the purpose relates to nothing more than the original meaning of humanity, such as His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثَى وَجَعَلْنَاكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَائِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ أَتْقَاكُمْ — "O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify yourselves with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you" (49:13).

Perhaps in His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ, the word "the people" was used in consideration of the mass of individuals in which there is the believer, the hypocrite, and the one in whose heart is a sickness, and they have mixed without distinction. So if fear is felt, it is felt from their generality. This is perhaps suggested by His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكَافِرِينَ, for the sentence is in the position of providing the reason for His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ. It has also been mentioned that the verse was revealed after the Migration and the appearance of the power of Islam, and the vast majority of the people were Muslims according to the outward appearance, even if there were hypocrites and others among them.

So the intended meaning of "the disbelieving people" (القوم الكافرين) is a group who are among the people, mentioned by their attribute but erased by their name, whom Allah, the Glorified, promised to nullify their plot and protect His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from their evil.

It is also apparent that the intended meaning of disbelief (الكفر) is disbelief in one of the signs of Allah, which is the ruling intended by His saying: مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ, as in His saying in the verse of Hajj: وَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعَالَمِينَ — "and should anyone disbelieve, Allah is indeed independent of all the worlds" (3:97). As for disbelief in the sense of arrogance against the foundation of the two testimonies (الشهادتين), it is something that does not suit the context of the verse at all, except according to the view that the intended meaning of His saying: مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ is the sum of the messages of the religion, and you have already seen the lack of its soundness.

The intended meaning of His, the Exalted, not guiding these disbelieving people is His not guiding them in their plot and scheme, and His preventing the current causes from being subservient to them in their pursuit of the evil and corruption they desire, similar to His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفَاسِقِينَ — "Allah does not guide the transgressing people" (63:6), and His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (2:258). This has already been discussed in the second volume of this book.

As for the intended meaning of the lack of guidance being the lack of guidance to faith (الإيمان), it is not correct at all because it contradicts the foundation of conveyance and the call. It is not sound to say: "Call them to Allah or to the ruling of Allah, and I will not guide them to it," except in the case of purely establishing the proof (إتمام الحجة).

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, has guided and continues to guide many of the disbelievers, as evidenced by observation, and He also said: وَاللَّهُ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشَاءُ إِلَى صِرَاطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — "and Allah guides whomever He wishes to a straight path" (2:213).

Thus, it has become clear that the intended meaning of not guiding the disbelievers is not leaving them alone to attain what they intend of nullifying the word of truth and extinguishing the light of the revealed ruling. For the disbelievers, as well as the wrongdoers and the transgressors, desire through the ill-omen of their souls and the misguidance of their opinion to change the sunnah of Allah current in creation and the sequence of causes leading to their effects, and to change the courses of the true causes apparent from the mark of disobedience to the Lord of the worlds toward their corrupt goal and their false objectives. But Allah, the Lord of the worlds, will never be rendered incapable by their formal powers, which none but He placed in them and none but He decreed in their structures.

So they may sometimes advance in their efforts and attain what they desired for moments, and they may rise and their matter may remain stable for a while, but it will not last before it is finally nullified and their plot is overturned against them, and the evil plot does not beset except its authors. Thus does Allah strike the truth and the falsehood; as for the falsehood, it goes away as scum, and as for what benefits the people, it remains in the earth.

Accordingly, His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكَافِرِينَ is an explanation of His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ by acting upon the breadth of its absolute sense. The intended meaning of protection (العصمة) would be His protecting him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from the people reaching him with evil without him attaining his desire in conveying this ruling and establishing it among the nation—as if they were to kill him before he conveyed it, or revolt against him and overturn matters against him, or accuse him of something by which the believers would apostatize from his religion, or plot a scheme that would kill this ruling and bury it. Rather, Allah manifests the word of truth and establishes the religion upon what He wills, wherever He wills, whenever He wills, and in whomever He wills. He, the Exalted, said: إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ وَيَأْتِ بِآخَرِينَ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَلَى ذَلِكَ قَدِيرًا — "If He wishes, He can take you away, O mankind, and bring others [in your place]; and Allah is powerful to do that" (4:133).

As for taking the verse, namely His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ, in its absolute sense with the breadth and inclusiveness it contains, it is something contradicted by the Quran and the narrated hadith and definitive history. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) suffered from his nation—including their disbelievers, believers, and hypocrites—such calamities, trials, and types of restraint and harm that no one could bear except his noble self. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said—as in the famous hadith: ما أوذي نبي مثل ما أوذيت قط — "No prophet was ever harmed as I have been harmed."

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Abu Salih, from Ibn Abbas and Jabir ibn Abdillah, they said: "Allah, the Exalted, commanded His Prophet Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to appoint Ali as a banner for the people to inform them of his guardianship (ولاية). The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) feared that they would say: 'He has favored his cousin,' and that they would criticize him for that. He said: So Allah revealed to him this verse: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ. So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) stood up for his guardianship on the day of Ghadir Khumm."

In the same source, from Hannan ibn Sadir, from his father, from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام), he said: "When Gabriel descended during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the Farewell Pilgrimage (حجة الوداع) with the announcement of the matter of Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه‌السلام): يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ — to the end of the verse — he said: The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) waited for three days until he reached al-Juhfah, and he did not take his hand out of fear of the people.

When he descended at al-Juhfah on the day of Ghadir in a place called 'Mahya'ah', he called out: 'The prayer is congregational (الصلاة جامعة).' The people gathered, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Who is more worthy of you than your own selves?' They spoke loudly and said: 'Allah and His Messenger.' Then he said it to them a second time, and they said: 'Allah and His Messenger.' Then he said it to them a third time, and they said: 'Allah and His Messenger.'

Then he took the hand of Ali (عليه‌السلام) and said: 'Whomsoever I am his master, Ali is his master. O Allah, befriend him who befriends him and be the enemy of him who is his enemy, and help him who helps him, and forsake him who forsakes him—for he is from me and I am from him, and he is to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me.'"

In the same source, from Abu al-Jarud, from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام), he said: "When Allah revealed to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكَافِرِينَ, he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took the hand of Ali (عليه‌السلام) and said: 'O people, there was no prophet among the prophets before me except that he lived for a time, then he was called and he answered. I am about to be called and I shall answer. I am responsible and you are responsible, so what will you say?' They said: 'We bear witness that you have conveyed, advised, and fulfilled what was upon you, so may Allah reward you with the best reward He has given to the messengers.' He said: 'O Allah, bear witness.'

Then he said: 'O assembly of Muslims, let the witness inform the absent—I enjoin whoever believes in me and confirms me with the guardianship of Ali. Indeed, the guardianship of Ali is my guardianship, a covenant my Lord made with me and commanded me to convey to you.' Then he said: 'Have you heard?'—saying it three times. Someone said: 'We have heard, O Messenger of Allah.'"

In al-Basa'ir, with its chain of narration from al-Fudayl ibn Yasar from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ, he said: "It is the guardianship (الولاية)."

I say: The revelation of the verse regarding the matter of guardianship and the story of Ghadir with it was narrated by al-Kulayni in al-Kafi, with his chain from Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) in a long hadith. Al-Saduq narrated this meaning in al-Ma'ani, with his chain from Muhammad ibn al-Fayd ibn al-Mukhtar from his father from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) in a long hadith. Al-Ayyashi also narrated it from Abu al-Jarud in a long hadith, and with his chain from Amr ibn Yazid from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) in brief.

From Tafsir al-Tha'labi, he said: Ja'far ibn Muhammad said: "The meaning of His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ is regarding the merit of Ali. When this was revealed, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took the hand of Ali and said: 'Whomsoever I am his master, Ali is his master.'"

From him, with his chain from al-Kalbi from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas: regarding this verse, he said: "It was revealed regarding Ali ibn Abi Talib. Allah commanded the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to convey regarding him, so he took the hand of Ali and said: 'Whomsoever I am his master, Ali is his master—O Allah, befriend him who befriends him, and be the enemy of him who is his enemy.'"

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibrahim al-Thaqafi with his chain from al-Khudri, Buraydah al-Aslami, and Muhammad ibn Ali: "It was revealed on the day of Ghadir regarding Ali."

From Tafsir al-Tha'labi, regarding the meaning of the verse, he said: Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Ali said: "Its meaning is: Convey what was sent down to you from your Lord regarding Ali."

In Tafsir al-Manar, from Tafsir al-Tha'labi: "This saying from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the guardianship of Ali spread and flew through the lands. It reached al-Harith ibn al-Nu'man al-Fihri, so he came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on his she-camel. He was at al-Abtah, so he dismounted and hobbled his camel, and said to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—who was in a gathering of his companions: 'O Muhammad, you commanded us from Allah to bear witness that there is no god but Allah and that you are the Messenger of Allah; we accepted that from you.' Then he mentioned the rest of the pillars of Islam. 'Then you were not satisfied with this until you stretched the arms of your cousin, favored him over us, and said: "Whomsoever I am his master, Ali is his master." Is this from you or from Allah?' He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'By Allah, besides whom there is no god, it is the command of Allah.' Al-Harith turned away heading for his mount, saying: 'O Allah, if this is the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky or bring us a painful punishment.'

He had not reached his mount before Allah struck him with a stone, which fell on his head and came out from his rear. And Allah, the Exalted, revealed: سَأَلَ سَائِلٌ بِعَذَابٍ وَاقِعٍ لِلْكَافِرِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُ دَافِعٌ — 'An asker asked for a punishment bound to happen, for the faithless, which none can avert' (70:1-2)." The hadith.

I say: The author of al-Manar said after quoting this hadith, verbatim: "This narration is fabricated. This Surah al-Ma'arij is Meccan. What Allah recounted of the saying of some of the disbelievers of Quraysh: اللَّهُمَّ إِنْ كَانَ هَذَا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ was a reminder of a saying they said before the Migration. This reminder is in Surah al-Anfal, which was revealed after the Battle of Badr, several years before the revelation of al-Ma'idah. The outward appearance of the narration is that this al-Harith ibn al-Nu'man was a Muslim who then apostatized, yet he is not known among the companions. Al-Abtah is in Mecca, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not return from Ghadir Khumm to Mecca; rather, he stopped there while returning from the Farewell Pilgrimage to Medina." End quote.

You see the arbitrariness in his words: As for his saying: "The narration is fabricated, and this Surah al-Ma'arij is Meccan," he relies in that on what is in some narrations from Ibn Abbas and Ibn al-Zubayr that Surah al-Ma'arij was revealed in Mecca. I wonder, what makes this narration preferable over that narration, when all are solitary reports (آحاد)? Even if we grant that Surah al-Ma'arij is Meccan, as the contents of most of its verses might support, what is the proof that all of its verses are Meccan? Let the Surah be Meccan, and these two verses specifically be non-Meccan, just as this Surah of ours, namely Surah al-Ma'idah, is Medinan, revealed at the end of the era of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), yet the verse under discussion, namely His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ, has been placed in it, while he and a number of commentators insist that it was revealed in Mecca at the start of the Mission. If it is permissible to place a Meccan verse (the verse: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ) in a Medinan Surah (al-Ma'idah), then let it be permissible to place a Medinan verse (the verse: سَأَلَ سَائِلٌ) in a Meccan Surah (Surah al-Ma'arij).

As for his saying: "What Allah recounted of the saying of some of the disbelievers of Quraysh," etc., it is as arbitrary as the previous one. Suppose Surah al-Anfal was revealed several years before al-Ma'idah; does that prevent some verses revealed after it from being placed in it during compilation, just as the verses of usury (الربا) and the verse: وَاتَّقُوا يَوْمًا تُرْجَعُونَ فِيهِ إِلَى اللَّهِ — "And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah" (2:281)—which is the last of what was revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) according to them—were placed in Surah al-Baqarah, which was revealed in the early days of the Migration, several years before it?

Then his saying: "The verse: وَإِذْ قَالُوا اللَّهُمَّ إِنْ كَانَ هَذَا هُوَ الْحَقَّ is a reminder of what they said before the Migration" is another arbitrary claim without proof, if the context of the verse were not proof to the contrary. For one who knows the styles of speech would hardly doubt that this, namely his saying: اللَّهُمَّ إِنْ كَانَ هَذَا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ فَأَمْطِرْ عَلَيْنَا حِجَارَةً مِنَ السَّمَاءِ أَوِ ائْتِنَا بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ, because it contains his saying: إِنْ كَانَ هَذَا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ with the demonstrative pronoun, the pronoun of separation, the "truth" (الحق) defined by the article, and his saying: مِنْ عِنْدِكَ, is not the speech of an idolater polytheist who mocks the truth and ridicules it. Rather, it is the speech of one who has submitted to the station of Lordship and sees that true matters are determined from His presence, and that laws, for example, descend from Him. Then he hesitates regarding a matter attributed to Allah, the Exalted, which a claimant claims is the truth and nothing else, and he cannot bear that and is distressed by it, so he prays against himself with the prayer of one who is repelled, weary, and tired of life.

As for his saying: "The outward appearance of the narration is that this al-Harith ibn al-Nu'man was a Muslim who then apostatized, yet he is not known among the companions," it is another arbitrary claim. Can anyone claim that they recorded the names of everyone who saw the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and believed in him, or believed in him and then apostatized? If such a thing exists, let this report be of that kind.

As for his saying: "Al-Abtah is in Mecca, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not return from Ghadir Khumm to Mecca," it bears witness that he took the word al-Abtah as a name for the specific place in Mecca and did not take it in its general meaning, which is every place with sand. There is no proof for what he took it as; rather, the proof is to the contrary, which is the story recounted in the narration and others. It might be inferred from something like the saying:

I was saved when the Muradi had wetted his sword From the son of the father of the Sheikh of the Abatih, Talib

that Mecca and its surroundings were called al-Abatih.

It is said in Marasid al-Ittila': Abtah (with fathah then sukun and fathah of the ta and ha): every watercourse in which there are fine pebbles is an abtah. Ibn Durayd said: al-Abtah and al-Batha are the level, spread-out ground on the face of the earth. Abu Zayd said: al-Abtah is the trace of the watercourse, whether narrow or wide. Al-Abtah is attributed to Mecca and to Mina because its distance from both is the same; perhaps it is closer to Mina, and it is al-Muhassab, which is the Khayf of Bani Kinanah. It has been said that it is Dhu Tuwa, but it is not. End quote.

Furthermore, the narration itself was narrated by other than al-Tha'labi, and there is no mention of al-Abtah in it; it is what follows from the narration of al-Majma' from the path of the general public (الجمهور) and others.

After all this, the narration is among the solitary reports (الآحاد); it is not among the mutawatir (المتواترات) — narrations transmitted by so many chains that their truth is certain — nor among those for whose correctness a definitive indicator has been established. You have already known from our previous discussions that we do not rely on solitary reports in matters other than the branch rulings (الأحكام الفرعية), according to the general rational criterion upon which human life is built. The purpose of the current discussion is merely to explain the corruption of the aspects he used as evidence to conclude that it is fabricated.

In al-Majma': "Al-Sayyid Abu al-Hamd informed us, saying: Al-Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani narrated to us, saying: Abu Abdillah al-Shirazi informed us, saying: Abu Bakr al-Jurjani informed us, saying: Abu Ahmad al-Basri informed us, saying: Muhammad ibn Sahl narrated to us, saying: Zayd ibn Isma'il, the client of the Ansar, narrated to us, saying: Muhammad ibn Ayyub al-Wasiti narrated to us, saying: Sufyan ibn Uyaynah narrated to us from Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq from his fathers, who said: When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) appointed Ali on the day of Ghadir Khumm, he said: 'Whomsoever I am his master, this Ali is his master.' That spread through the lands, so al-Nu'man ibn al-Harith al-Fihri came to the Prophet and said: 'You commanded us from Allah to bear witness that there is no god but Allah and that you are the Messenger of Allah, and you commanded us with Jihad, Hajj, fasting, prayer, and Zakat, and we accepted them. Then you were not satisfied until you appointed this youth and said: "Whomsoever I am his master, Ali is his master." Is this something from you or a command from Allah, the Exalted?' He said: 'Yes, by Allah besides whom there is no god, this is from Allah.'

Al-Nu'man ibn al-Harith turned away saying: 'O Allah, if this is the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky.' So Allah struck him with a stone on his head and killed him, and Allah revealed: سَأَلَ سَائِلٌ بِعَذَابٍ وَاقِعٍ."

I say: This meaning is also narrated in al-Kafi.

It is narrated in the book Nuzul al-Quran by the memorizer (الحافظ) Abu Nu’aym, tracing it back to ‘Ali ibn ‘Amir, from Abu al-Hijaf, from al-A’mash, from ‘Atiyyah, who said: "This verse was revealed to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ 'O Messenger! Proclaim what has been sent down to you from your Lord' (5:67). And Allah the Exalted had said: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلَامَ دِينًا 'Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you, and I have approved Islam as your religion' (5:3)."

And in al-Fusul al-Muhimmah by al-Maliki, he said: "The Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Wahidi narrated in his book titled Asbab al-Nuzul, tracing it with his chain of transmission to Abu Sa’id al-Khudri رضي الله عنه, who said: 'This verse: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ (5:67) was revealed on the day of Ghadir Khumm regarding ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib.'"

I say: It was also narrated in Fath al-Qadir from Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn ‘Asakir from Abu Sa’id al-Khudri, and likewise in al-Durr al-Manthur.

Regarding his saying: "at Ghadir Khumm" (بغدير خم), the word Khumm is with a damma on the letter kha and a shadda on the mim with nunation (التنوين). It is the name of a thicket (غيطة) located three miles from al-Juhfah, near which is a famous pond (غدير) attributed to the thicket. This was mentioned by Shaykh Muhyi al-Din al-Nawawi.

In Fath al-Qadir, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Mas’ud, who said: "During the time of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, we used to recite: (يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ — that ‘Ali is the master of the believers — وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ)."

I say: This is a small portion of the reports indicating the revelation of His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ (etc.) concerning the right of ‘Ali عليه السلام on the day of Ghadir Khumm. As for the Hadith of Ghadir itself—meaning his saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Whomsoever I am his master, ‘Ali is his master"—it is a mass-transmitted (متواتر) narration reported through the chains of the Shi’a and the Ahl al-Sunnah in more than a hundred ways.

It has been narrated from a large group of the Companions (الصحابة), including al-Bara’ ibn ‘Azib, Zayd ibn Arqam, Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab, ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib, Salman al-Farisi, Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, ‘Ammar ibn Yasir, Buraydah, Sa’d ibn Abi Waqqas, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abbas, Abu Hurayrah, Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah, Abu Sa’id al-Khudri, Anas ibn Malik, ‘Imran ibn al-Husayn, Ibn Abi Awfa, Sa’danah, and the wife of Zayd ibn Arqam.

The Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام have reached a consensus (أجمع عليه) upon it. ‘Ali عليه السلام publicly appealed to the people (ناشد الناس) at al-Rahbah regarding this narration, and a group of the Companions who were present in the gathering stood up and testified that they heard the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله say it on the day of Ghadir.

In many of these narrations, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "O people, do you not know that I have more authority (أولى) over the believers than their own selves?" They said: "Yes." He said: "Whomsoever I am his master, ‘Ali is his master," as found in several reports narrated by Ahmad ibn Hanbal in his Musnad and others. Works have been dedicated solely to counting its chains of transmission and investigating its text (المتن) by scholars from both the Ahl al-Sunnah and the Shi’a, who researched it to the utmost degree.

In the book al-Simtayn by al-Hamawini, with his chain to Abu Hurayrah, he said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'On the night I was taken up to the seventh heaven, I heard a call from beneath the Throne: Verily, ‘Ali is the sign of guidance (آية الهدى) and the beloved of those who believe in Me; convey this to ‘Ali عليه السلام.' When the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله descended from heaven, he forgot that, so Allah عز وجل revealed: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكَافِرِينَ 'O Messenger! Proclaim what has been sent down to you from your Lord; and if you do not, you will not have proclaimed His message; and Allah will guard you from mankind. Indeed Allah does not guide the faithless lot' (5:67)."

In Fath al-Qadir, Ibn Abi Hatim extracted from Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah, who said: "When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله campaigned against Banu Anmar, he halted at Dhat al-Riqa’ in the upper part of Nakhl. While he was sitting at the head of a well with his legs dangling—

A man named al-Warith from Banu al-Najjar said: 'I shall surely kill Muhammad.' His companions asked: 'How will you kill him?' He said: 'I will say to him: Give me your sword; then when he gives it to me, I will kill him with it.' So he came to him and said: 'O Muhammad, give me your sword that I may smell it.' He gave it to him, but the man's hand trembled until the sword fell from his hand. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Allah has intervened between you and what you intended.' Then Allah the Glorified revealed: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ 'O Messenger! Proclaim what has been sent down to you' (5:67), the verse."

I say: Then it is mentioned in Fath al-Qadir that Ibn Hibban extracted it in his Sahih, and Ibn Mardawayh also extracted it from Abu Hurayrah with a similar story but did not name the man. Ibn Jarir extracted a similar report from the narration of Muhammad ibn Ka’b al-Qurazi. The story of Ghawrath ibn al-Harith is established in the Sahih collections and is well-known and famous (end of quote). However, the issue lies in applying the story to the established meaning of the verse, and it will never fit.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Fath al-Qadir, and others, from Ibn Mardawayh and al-Diya’ in al-Mukhtarah, from Ibn ‘Abbas: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was asked: 'Which verse revealed from heaven was the hardest upon you?' He said: 'I was at Mina during the days of the pilgrimage season (الموسم) when the polytheists of the Arabs and the masses of people gathered for the season. Gabriel descended to me and said: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ (the verse).'"

He said: "So I stood at al-’Aqabah and called out: 'O people! Who will support me in proclaiming the message of my Lord, and for him is Paradise? O people! Say: There is no god but Allah, and I am the Messenger of Allah to you; you will prosper, succeed, and for you is Paradise.'"

He said: "Not a man, woman, or child remained except that they threw dust and stones at me, spat in my face, and said: 'A liar, a Sabian!' Then an apparition appeared to me and said: 'O Muhammad, if you are the Messenger of Allah, it is time for you to pray against them as Noah prayed for the destruction of his people.' The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'O Allah, guide my people, for they do not know.'"

Then his uncle al-’Abbas came, rescued him from them, and drove them away from him.

I say: The verse in its entirety does not apply to this story, based on the detailed discussion we have already provided.

Unless the narration is interpreted as the revelation of only a portion of the verse—which is His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ—on that day. However, the apparent sense (ظاهر) of the narration rejects this, and similar to it is what follows.

In al-Durr al-Manthur and Fath al-Qadir: ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu al-Shaykh extracted from Mujahid, who said: "When بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ 'Proclaim what has been sent down to you from your Lord' was revealed, he said: 'O Lord, I am but one; what shall I do? The people will gather against me.' Then وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ 'and if you do not, you will not have proclaimed His message' was revealed."

In the same sources, from al-Hasan: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Indeed, Allah sent me with His message and I felt distressed (ضقت بها ذرعا) by it, knowing that the people would call me a liar. He promised me that I must proclaim it or He would punish me; so He revealed: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ.'"

I say: These two narrations, despite being disconnected (القطع) and narrated without a full chain (الإرسال), suffer from the same issues as the previous ones. Similar to them in this confusion is some of what has been reported: that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to be guarded by men, but when the verse was revealed, he dismissed them and said عليه السلام: "My Lord has promised me that He will protect me."

In Tafsir al-Manar: "The scholars of traditional exegesis (أهل التفسير المأثور), al-Tirmidhi, Abu al-Shaykh, al-Hakim, Abu Nu’aym, al-Bayhaqi, and al-Tabarani narrated from over a dozen men among the Companions: that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to be guarded in Mecca before the revelation of this verse. When it was revealed, he left the guard. Abu Talib was the first person concerned with his protection, and al-’Abbas also guarded him."

In the same source: "Among what was narrated regarding that from Jabir and Ibn ‘Abbas: that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to be guarded, and his uncle Abu Talib would send men from Banu Hashim with him every day until the verse was revealed. He then said: 'O uncle, indeed Allah has protected me; I have no need for those you send.'"

I say: These two narrations—as you can see—indicate that the verse was revealed in the middle of the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله stay in Mecca, and that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had proclaimed his message for a time, but the people's harm and denial intensified against him until he feared for himself and ceased the proclamation and invitation (الدعوة). Then he was commanded a second time to proclaim, was threatened by Allah the Glorified, and promised protection (العصمة). So he resumed what he was doing initially. This is something from which the station of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is far removed.

In al-Durr al-Manthur and Fath al-Qadir: ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, al-Hakim, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu’aym, and al-Bayhaqi (both in al-Dala’il) extracted from ‘A’ishah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah used to be guarded until وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ 'and Allah will guard you from mankind' was revealed. He then poked his head out of the leather tent (القبة) and said: 'O people, depart, for Allah has protected me.'"

I say: This narration—as you can see—is apparent (ظاهرة) in its revelation being in Medina.

In Tafsir al-Tabari, from Ibn ‘Abbas: regarding His saying: وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ "and if you do not, you will not have proclaimed His message"—it means: if you conceal a verse revealed to you, you have not proclaimed His message.

I say: If the meaning intended is a specific verse—that is, a specific ruling (حكم) among those revealed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—then it has a basis of correctness. But if the meaning intended is a threat regarding any verse or ruling whatsoever, then you have already seen in what preceded that the content of the verse does not suit such an interpretation.

Ayahs

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰ تُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۗ وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم مَّآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا ۖ فَلَا تَأْسَ عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٦٨)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! You do not stand on anything until you observe the Torah and the Evangel and what was sent down to you from your Lord.’ Surely many of them will be increased in rebellion and unfaith by what has been sent down to you from your Lord. So do not grieve for the faithless lot. (68)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـُٔونَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦٩)
Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Sabaeans, and the Christians—those who have faith in Allah and the Last Day and act righteously—they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (69)
لَقَدْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَأَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمْ رُسُلًا ۖ كُلَّمَا جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُهُمْ فَرِيقًا كَذَّبُوا۟ وَفَرِيقًا يَقْتُلُونَ (٧٠)
Certainly We took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We sent apostles to them. Whenever an apostle brought them that which was not to their liking, they would impugn a part of them, and a part they would slay. (70)
وَحَسِبُوٓا۟ أَلَّا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ فَعَمُوا۟ وَصَمُّوا۟ ثُمَّ تَابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ عَمُوا۟ وَصَمُّوا۟ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (٧١)
They supposed there would be no testing, so they became blind and deaf. Thereafter Allah accepted their repentance, yet [again] many of them became blind and deaf, and Allah watches what they do. (71)
لَقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ (٧٢)
They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ But the Messiah had said, ‘O Children of Israel! Worship Allah, my Lord, and your Lord. Indeed whoever ascribes partners to Allah, Allah shall forbid him [entry into] paradise, and his refuge shall be the Fire, and the wrongdoers will not have any helpers.’ (72)
لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَـٰثَةٍ ۘ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّآ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۚ وَإِن لَّمْ يَنتَهُوا۟ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ لَيَمَسَّنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٧٣)
They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity,’ while there is no god except the One God. If they do not relinquish what they say, there shall befall the faithless among them a painful punishment. (73)
أَفَلَا يَتُوبُونَ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٧٤)
Will they not repent to Allah and plead to Him for forgiveness? Yet Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (74)
مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٧٥)
The Messiah, son of Mary, is but an apostle. Certainly, [other] apostles have passed before him, and his mother was a truthful one. Both of them would eat food. Look how We clarify the signs for them, and yet, look, how they go astray! (75)
قُلْ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٧٦)
Say, ‘Do you worship, besides Allah, what has no power to bring you any benefit or harm, while Allah—He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing?!’ (76)
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَ قَوْمٍ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ وَأَضَلُّوا۟ كَثِيرًا وَضَلُّوا۟ عَن سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (٧٧)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! Do not unduly exceed the bounds in your religion and do not follow the fancies of a people who went astray in the past, and led many astray, and [themselves] strayed from the right path.’ (77)
لُعِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَلَىٰ لِسَانِ دَاوُۥدَ وَعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ (٧٨)
The faithless among the Children of Israel were cursed on the tongue of David and Jesus son of Mary. That, because they would disobey and they used to commit transgression. (78)
كَانُوا۟ لَا يَتَنَاهَوْنَ عَن مُّنكَرٍ فَعَلُوهُ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (٧٩)
They would not forbid one another from the wrongs that they committed. Surely, evil is what they had been doing. (79)
تَرَىٰ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا قَدَّمَتْ لَهُمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ أَن سَخِطَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَفِى ٱلْعَذَابِ هُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ (٨٠)
You see many of them fraternizing with the faithless. Surely evil is what they have sent ahead for their souls, as Allah is displeased with them and they shall remain in punishment [forever]. (80)
وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلنَّبِىِّ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَا ٱتَّخَذُوهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٨١)
Had they believed in Allah and the Prophet and what has been sent down to him, they would not have taken them for allies. But most of them are transgressors. (81)
۞ لَتَجِدَنَّ أَشَدَّ ٱلنَّاسِ عَدَٰوَةً لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلْيَهُودَ وَٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۖ وَلَتَجِدَنَّ أَقْرَبَهُم مَّوَدَّةً لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا نَصَـٰرَىٰ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيسِينَ وَرُهْبَانًا وَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٨٢)
Surely You will find the most hostile of all people towards the faithful to be the Jews and the polytheists, and surely you will find the nearest of them in affection to the faithful to be those who say ‘We are Christians.’ That is because there are priests and monks among them, and because they are not arrogant. (82)
وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا۟ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ تَرَىٰٓ أَعْيُنَهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ ٱلدَّمْعِ مِمَّا عَرَفُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ ۖ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱكْتُبْنَا مَعَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٨٣)
When they hear what has been revealed to the Apostle, you see their eyes fill with tears because of the truth that they recognize. They say, ‘Our Lord, we believe; so write us down among the witnesses. (83)
وَمَا لَنَا لَا نُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَا جَآءَنَا مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَنَطْمَعُ أَن يُدْخِلَنَا رَبُّنَا مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٨٤)
Why should we not believe in Allah and the truth that has come to us, eager as we are that our Lord should admit us among the righteous people?’ (84)
فَأَثَـٰبَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا قَالُوا۟ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٨٥)
So, for what they said, Allah requited them with gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], and that is the reward of the virtuous. (85)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٨٦)
But those who are faithless and deny Our signs—they shall be the inmates of hell. (86)

(Commentary)

In themselves, the verses are capable of connection and consistency according to their arrangement, yet they do not accept connection with His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ أَقَامُوا التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ — "Had they observed the Torah and the Evangel" (5:66), if one disregards His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "O Apostle! Communicate that which has been sent down to you from your Lord" (5:67). As for the link regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ — "O Apostle! Communicate," you have already encountered the discussion concerning it.

It is most likely that these verses follow the context of the preceding verses from the beginning of the Surah until here—meaning the interconnection of the themes of the verses starting from His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ مِيثَاقَ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَبَعَثْنَا مِنْهُمْ اثْنَيْ عَشَرَ نَقِيبًا — "Certainly Allah took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We raised among them twelve chiefs" (5:12) until the end of these verses under discussion, with the exception of a few interspersed verses such as the Verse of Mastership (آية الولاية) and the Verse of Communication (آية التبليغ) and others previously discussed. The same applies to the connection of the verses at the end of the Surah with these verses, for they are all united by being discourse related to the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ حَتَّىٰ تُقِيمُوا التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! You do not stand on anything until you observe the Torah and the Evangel’" (5:68) until the end of the verse. A human being finds within himself during his actions that if he wishes to exert power and intensity in a matter that requires it, he must rely on a level surface upon which he stands firm or to which he is connected. For instance, one who wishes to pull, push, carry, or lift a heavy object first firms his feet upon the ground and then does as he pleases, knowing that were it not for this, he would not achieve his aim. This has been discussed in the sciences related to it.

If we apply this meaning to spiritual matters, such as the soul's internal actions or the physical actions of the limbs related to psychological states, it results in the conclusion that the performance of significant deeds and great works depends upon a spiritual foundation (أس معنوي) and a strong psychological base (مبنى قوى نفسي). This is similar to how momentous affairs depend on patience (الصبر), steadfastness (الثبات), high ambition (علو الهمة), and strength of resolve (قوة العزيمة), and how success in servitude (العبودية) depends on the reality of godwariness (حق التقوى) and piety (الورع) regarding the prohibitions of Allah.

From here, it becomes clear that His saying, the Exalted: لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ — "You do not stand on anything" is a metaphor (كناية) for their lack of reliance on anything upon which they could firm their feet so as to be able to observe the Torah, the Evangel, and what was sent down to them from their Lord. It is an allusion (تلويح) to the fact that the religion (الدين) of Allah and His judgment possess a weight (ثقل) that is not easily carried by a human until he relies on a firm foundation; he cannot establish it merely through his own whims (هوى). Allah points to this regarding the Noble Quran with His saying: إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا — "Soon We shall cast on you a weighty word" (73:5), and His saying: لَوْ أَنْزَلْنَا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ لَرَأَيْتَهُ خَاشِعًا مُتَصَدِّعًا مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ ۚ وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثَالُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ — "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled [and] rent asunder out of the fear of Allah. We coin such similitudes for mankind so that they may reflect" (59:21), and His saying: إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمَانَةَ عَلَى السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَالْجِبَالِ فَأَبَيْنَ أَنْ يَحْمِلْنَهَا وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْهَا — "Indeed We presented the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, but they refused to bear it, and were apprehensive of it" (33:72).
