You do not worship besides Him but names that you and your fathers have coined" (12:39-40); and He said: وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "And there is no god except Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (38:65); and He said: لَوْ أَرادَ اللهُ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ وَلَداً لَاصْطَفى مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ سُبْحانَهُ هُوَ اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "Had Allah intended to take a son, He would have chosen from what He creates whatever He wished. Immaculate is He! He is Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (39:4).

The verses, in their context—as you see—negate every oneness that is relative to a multiplicity corresponding to it, whether it be numerical oneness, like a single individual of a species (النوع) which, if another individual were assumed alongside it, they would be two—for this individual is subdued by the limit that defines it against the other individual negated from it and assumed opposite it—or whether it be specific (نوعية) or generic (جنسية) oneness, or any universal oneness (وحدة كلية) relative to a multiplicity of its own kind, like "human" which is one species relative to the many species resulting from it and from the horse, the cow, the sheep, and others—for it is subdued by the limit that defines it against the other species that parallel it. Since He, the Exalted, is not subdued by anything in anything whatsoever—neither in His essence (ذاته), nor His attribute (صفته), nor His act (فعله)—and He is the Subduer over everything, He is not limited (بمحدود) in anything that pertains to Him. He is an Existent (موجود) unmixed with non-existence, a Truth (حق) not subject to falsehood, the Living (الحي) unmingled with death, the All-Knowing (العليم) into whom no ignorance creeps, the All-Powerful (القادر) whom no incapacity overcomes, the Owner (المالك) and the King (الملك) without anything owning Him, and the Mighty (العزيز) who has no lowliness, and so on.

Thus, for Him, the Exalted, is the pure essence of every perfection (كمال). If you wish for further understanding and grasp of this Quranic reality, then assume something finite (متناهياً) and another infinite (غير متناهٍ); you will find the infinite encompassing the finite such that the finite does not repel the infinite from its assumed perfection in any way you might assume. Rather, the infinite dominates it such that the finite does not lack it in any of the pillars of its perfection. The infinite is self-subsistent (القائم على نفسه), a witness over it, and encompassing it. Then look at what is provided by the saying of the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ ، أَلا إِنَّهُمْ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْ لِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ أَلا إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ مُحِيطٌ — "Is it not sufficient that your Lord is witness to all things? Look! They are indeed in doubt about the encounter with their Lord! Look! He indeed encompasses all things!" (41:53-54).

This is what is indicated by the generality of the verses describing His attributes, the Exalted, which occur in the context of restriction (الحصر) or are apparent in it, such as the saying of the Exalted: اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى — "Allah—there is no god except Him. To Him belong the Best Names" (20:8); and His saying: وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ — "And they will know that Allah is the Manifest Truth" (24:25); and His saying: هُوَ الْحَيُّ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "He is the Living One, there is no god except Him" (40:65); and His saying: وَهُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْقَدِيرُ — "And He is the All-knowing, the All-powerful" (30:54); and His saying: أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً — "That power belongs entirely to Allah" (2:165); and His saying: لَهُ الْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ — "To Him belongs all sovereignty and to Him belongs all praise" (64:1); and His saying: إِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً — "Indeed all might belongs to Allah" (10:65); and His saying: الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "The truth is from your Lord" (2:147); and His saying: أَنْتُمُ الْفُقَراءُ إِلَى اللهِ وَاللهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ — "It is you who are the needy in relation to Allah, and Allah—He is the All-sufficient" (45:15); among other verses.

The verses—as you see—cry out with their loudest voice that every assumed perfection belongs to Allah, the Glorified, by original right (بالأصالة), and nothing belongs to any other except by His granting them ownership of it, without Him becoming detached from what He owns and grants ownership of, as we, the assembly of creation, become detached from what we have granted ownership of to others.

Thus, whenever we assume any thing among things possessing some perfection in opposition to Him, the Exalted, so as to be a second to Him or a partner, the meaning of perfection in its hand returns to being purely for Allah, the Glorified. He is the Truth (الحق) who owns everything, and everything else is Falsehood (الباطل) which owns nothing for itself. The Exalted said: لا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتاً وَلا حَياةً وَلا نُشُوراً — "They do not control any benefit or harm for themselves, nor do they control death, life, or resurrection" (25:3).

This meaning is what negates numerical oneness from Him, the Exalted. For if He were numerically one—that is, a limited existent whose essence is detached from encompassing other existents—it would be correct for the intellect to assume a second like Him, whether its realization in the external world were possible or impossible. It would also be correct for the intellect to describe Him with multiplicity (الكثرة) in its consideration, even if it assumed its impossibility in reality. But it is not so.

He, the Exalted, is One in the sense that He is of such an existence (الوجود) that He is not limited by any limit such that a second to Him could be assumed beyond that limit. This is the meaning of the saying of the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ ، اللهُ الصَّمَدُ ، لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ ، وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ كُفُواً أَحَدٌ — "Say, ‘He is Allah, the One. Allah is the All-embracing. He neither begat, nor was begotten, nor has He any equal’" (112:1-4). For the word "One" (أحد) is only used in a way that repels the possibility of assuming a number in opposition to it. It is said: "No one (أحد) came to me," which negates the coming of one, as well as two or more. The Exalted said: وَإِنْ أَحَدٌ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ اسْتَجارَكَ — "If any (أحد) of the polytheists seeks asylum with you" (9:6), which includes one, two, and a group, and no number is excluded from its ruling. And the Exalted said: أَوْ جاءَ أَحَدٌ مِنْكُمْ مِنَ الْغائِطِ — "Or if one (أحد) of you comes from the privy" (5:6), which includes one and what is beyond it, and nothing is an exception to it.

Thus, the use of the word "One" (أحد) in His saying: "He is Allah, the One" in the affirmative, without negation and without restriction by addition or description, provides that His identity (هويته), the Exalted, is such that it repels the assumption of anything that resembles Him in His identity in any way, whether it be one or many. Such an assumption is impossible according to correct conceptualization, regardless of its state in the external world.

Therefore, the Exalted described Him first as "All-embracing" (صمد), which is the solid that has no hollow and no empty space within it; second, that He did not beget; third, that He was not begotten; and fourth, that there is no equal to Him. All of these descriptions are among those that would necessitate a type of limitation and detachment.

This is the secret behind why the descriptions of others do not truly apply to Him, the Exalted, nor does He truly possess them. The Exalted said: سُبْحانَ اللهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "Immaculate is Allah from what they attribute [to Him]—[all] except Allah’s chosen servants" (37:159-160). And the Exalted said: وَلا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْماً — "And they do not encompass Him in knowledge" (20:110). For the meanings of perfection with which we describe Him, the Exalted, are limited attributes, while His courtyard, the Glorified, is exalted above limit and restriction. This is what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) intended in his famous word: "I cannot enumerate Your praise; You are as You have praised Yourself."

This meaning of oneness is what repels the Trinity (تثليث) of the Christians. For they are monotheists in the very midst of Trinity, but the oneness they submit to is a numerical oneness that does not negate multiplicity from another aspect. They say: the Persons (الأقانيم)—the Father, the Son, and the Spirit (Essence, Knowledge, and Life)—are three and they are one, like a living, knowing human being; he is one thing because he is a living, knowing human, and he is three because he is a human, life, and knowledge.

But the Quranic teaching negates that because it establishes a oneness with which the assumption of any multiplicity or distinction—neither in essence nor in attributes—is not tenable. Everything assumed in this regard is identical to the other due to the absence of limit. Thus, His essence, the Exalted, is identical to His attributes, and every assumed attribute is identical to the other. Exalted is Allah above what they associate, and immaculate is He from what they describe.

For this reason, you see that the verses that characterize Him, the Exalted, with Paramountcy (القهارية) begin first with the characterization of Oneness (الوحدة) and then describe Him with Paramountcy, to indicate that His oneness leaves no room for an assumer to assume a second resembling Him in any way, let alone for such to appear in existence and attain reality and fixity. The Exalted said: أَأَرْبابٌ مُتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ ما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلَّا أَسْماءً سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ — "Are discrete lords better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount? You do not worship besides Him but names that you and your fathers have coined" (12:39-40). He described Him with a oneness that subdues every assumed partner, leaving for any other assumed deity nothing but the name only. And the Exalted said: أَمْ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ خَلَقُوا كَخَلْقِهِ فَتَشابَهَ الْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ قُلِ اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "Have they set up partners for Allah who have created like His creation, so that the creations seemed much alike to them? Say, ‘Allah is the creator of all things, and He is the One, the All-paramount’" (13:16). And the Exalted said: لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارِ — "To whom does the sovereignty belong today? To Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (40:16), since His absolute sovereignty, the Exalted, does not leave any assumed owner besides Him without making him and what he owns the property of Allah, the Glorified. And the Exalted said: وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "And there is no god except Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (38:65). And the Exalted said: لَوْ أَرادَ اللهُ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ وَلَداً لَاصْطَفى مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ سُبْحانَهُ هُوَ اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "Had Allah intended to take a son, He would have chosen from what He creates whatever He wished. Immaculate is He! He is Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (39:4). Thus, He arranged Paramountcy in all the verses upon the attribute of Oneness.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Tawhid and al-Khisal, with its chain of transmission from al-Miqdam ibn Shurayh ibn Hani, from his father, who said: A Bedouin stood up on the Day of the Camel to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) and said: "O Commander of the Faithful, do you say that Allah is one?" He said: The people attacked him and said: "O Bedouin, do you not see the state of the Commander of the Faithful's heart—how it is divided [by the burdens of war]?" The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: "Leave him, for what the Bedouin desires is exactly what we desire from these people!"

Then he said: "O Bedouin, the statement that Allah is one is of four types: two of them are not permissible for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and two are established for Him. As for the two that are not permissible for Him, it is the saying of one who says 'one' intending the category of numbers; this is not permissible, because that which has no second does not enter into the category of numbers—do you not see that he who said 'He is the third of three' disbelieved? And the saying of one who says 'He is one of the people,' intending a species (النوع) of a genus (الجنس); this is not permissible because it is anthropomorphism (تشبيه), and our Lord is far exalted above that.

As for the two types that are established for Him, it is the saying of one who says: 'He is one, having no likeness among things'; such is our Lord. And the saying of one who says: 'He, the Mighty and Majestic, is unitary in meaning (أحدي المعنى)'—meaning by it that He is not divisible in existence (وجود), nor intellect (عقل), nor imagination (وهم); such is our Lord, the Mighty and Majestic."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Ma'ani with another chain from Abu al-Miqdam ibn Shurayh ibn Hani, from his father, from him (peace be upon him). And in Nahj al-Balaghah: "The beginning of religion is His gnosis (معرفته), and the perfection of His gnosis is the testimony (التصديق) to Him, and the perfection of the testimony to Him is His monotheism (توحيده), and the perfection of His monotheism is sincerity (الإخلاص) toward Him, and the perfection of sincerity toward Him is the negation of attributes (نفي الصفات) from Him—due to the witness of every attribute that it is other than the described, and the witness of every described that it is other than the attribute. So whoever describes Allah has associated Him, and whoever associates Him has made Him two, and whoever makes Him two has divided Him, and whoever divides Him is ignorant of Him, and whoever is ignorant of Him has pointed to Him, and whoever points to Him has limited Him, and whoever limits Him has numbered Him." (Sermon 1).

I say: This is among the most exquisite of explanations. The summary of the first part of the speech is that His gnosis ends in its completion with the negation of attributes from Him. The summary of the second part branching from the first—namely his saying (peace be upon him): "So whoever describes Allah has associated Him" etc.—is that the affirmation of attributes necessitates the affirmation of numerical oneness, which depends on limitation (التحديد) that is not permissible for Him, the Exalted. The two premises result in the conclusion that the perfection of His gnosis, the Exalted, requires the negation of numerical oneness from Him and the affirmation of oneness in another sense; this is what he (peace be upon him) intended by the sequence of the speech.

As for the issue of negating attributes from Him, he (peace be upon him) explained it by his saying: "The beginning of religion is His gnosis," for it is clear that whoever does not know Allah, the Glorified, even in some way, has not yet entered the courtyard of religion. Gnosis may be accompanied by action regarding the deeds related to it and the manifestation of the effects of the known, or it may be without action. It is well known that knowledge regarding what relates in some way to actions only becomes fixed and established in the soul if its practical effects follow; otherwise, knowledge continues to weaken by the performance of contrary acts until it is nullified or becomes a hollow thing with no effect. Among his words (peace be upon him) in this regard—as narrated in Nahj al-Balaghah—is: "Knowledge is coupled with action; so whoever knows, acts. Knowledge calls out to action; if it answers him, [it stays], otherwise it departs from him."

Thus, knowledge and gnosis of a thing only become complete when the knower takes his known truthfully and manifests that in his inner and outer being, his heart and his limbs, by submitting to it in spirit and body. This is the faith (الإيمان) spread over his secret and his public state, which is his saying: "and the perfection of His gnosis is the testimony to Him."

Furthermore, this submission called "testimony to Him," although its realization is possible alongside the affirmation of a partner to the Lord to whom one submits—as the idol-worshippers submit to Allah and to all their other deities together—yet submission to a thing is not complete without turning away from others by self-evidence. Thus, submission to one of the deities is, in meaning, a turning away from others and an arrogance, in a sense, toward them. Therefore, testimony to Allah and submission to His station is not complete except by turning away from the worship of partners and departing from the call of numerous deities; this is his saying: "and the perfection of the testimony to Him is His monotheism."

Then, monotheism has different levels, some above others. It is not complete until the One Deity is given His right of restricted Divinity (الألوهية المنحصرة), and one does not limit oneself to merely naming Him a "one God," but rather attributes to Him everything that has a share of existence and perfection, such as creation, provision, giving life, causing death, giving, and withholding. And that submission and worship be specialized for Him, such that one does not humble oneself to any other in any way; rather, one hopes for nothing but His mercy, fears nothing but His wrath, desires nothing but what is with Him, and stays at no door but His.

In other words, that one be sincere (يخلص) to Him in knowledge and action; this is his saying (peace be upon him): "and the perfection of His monotheism is sincerity toward Him."

When a person sits upon the throne of sincerity and the Divine care joins him to the near friends (أولياء) of Allah, there appear to his insight the flashes of incapacity to fulfill the right of gnosis and to describe Him with what befits the courtyard of His pride and greatness. For he may witness that what he describes Him with are perceived meanings from the things fashioned before him and matters he is familiar with from his possible observations. These are limited, restricted forms that repel one another and do not accept harmony or blending—look at the concepts of existence, knowledge, power, life, provision, might, wealth, and others.

Limited meanings repel one another because it is clear that every concept is devoid of the other concept, such as the meaning of knowledge being devoid of the meaning of power. When we conceptualize knowledge, we turn away from power, and we do not find its meaning within the meaning of knowledge. When we conceptualize the meaning of knowledge, which is one of the attributes, we are detached from the meaning of the Essence, which is the described.

Thus, these concepts, sciences, and perceptions fall short of truly corresponding to Him, may His station be glorified, and from truly recounting what He is. Therefore, the sincere one, in describing his Lord, finds a need to confess to an incurable deficiency and an irreparable incapacity; he returns to negating what he affirmed and wanders in a bewilderment from which there is no escape. This is his saying (peace be upon him): "and the perfection of sincerity toward Him is the negation of attributes from Him—due to the witness of every attribute that it is other than the described, and the witness of every described that it is other than the attribute."

What we have used to interpret this knot of his speech (peace be upon him) is supported by the beginning of the sermon where he says: "He whom the heights of ambition cannot reach, and the depths of cleverness cannot attain; He for whose attribute there is no defined limit, nor an existing description, nor a numbered time, nor an extended term," as appears to the clever contemplator.

As for his saying (peace be upon him): "So whoever describes Allah has associated Him" etc., it is his reaching the desired conclusion—which is that Allah, the Glorified, has no limit and no number—by way of analyzing the affirmation of the attribute, just as the first explanation was his reaching it by way of analyzing gnosis to the negation of the attribute.

So whoever describes Allah has associated Him because of what you have known of the difference between the described and the attribute, and the joining of two different things is association (قرن). Whoever associates Him has made Him two, because of taking Him as a described and an attribute, and they are two. Whoever makes Him two has divided Him into two parts. Whoever divides Him is ignorant of Him by pointing to Him with an intellectual pointing. Whoever points to Him has limited Him, because pointing necessitates the separation of the pointed-to from the pointer until the pointing mediates between them—which is the creation of a distance between the pointer and the pointed-to, beginning from the first and ending at the second. "And whoever limits Him has numbered Him" and made Him a numerical one, because number is a necessary consequence of division and existential detachment; exalted is Allah above that.

And in Nahj al-Balaghah: from a sermon of his (peace be upon him): "Praise be to Allah, for whom no state preceded another state—such that He would be first before being last, or be manifest before being hidden. Every one named with 'oneness' (الوحدة) other than Him is few; every mighty one other than Him is lowly; every strong one other than Him is weak; every owner other than Him is owned; every scholar other than Him is a learner; every powerful one other than Him is sometimes powerful and sometimes incapable; every hearer other than Him is deaf to subtle sounds, while loud ones make him deaf and what is far from him escapes him; every seer other than Him is blind to hidden colors and subtle bodies; every manifest one other than Him is hidden, and every hidden one other than Him is manifest."

I say: The basis of the explanation is that He, the Exalted, is unlimited while everything else is limited. For these meanings and descriptions, and all that is of their kind, when limit (الحد) occurs to them, they have some relation to something else. Limitation then necessitates that they be cut off and cease from what they were related to, and change into the opposite meaning.

Manifestation (الظهور), if assumed to be limited, would be in relation to one direction or one thing rather than another direction and another thing; thus the manifest thing becomes hidden and concealed in relation to that other direction and other thing. Might (العزة), if taken with a limit, is nullified beyond its limit, thus becoming lowliness in relation to it. Strength (القوة), if restricted, changes in relation to what is beyond its restriction into weakness. Manifestation is hiddenness in other than its place, and hiddenness is manifestation outside its level.

Ownership (الملك), if limited, means that whoever limits it is dominant over this owner; thus he and his ownership are under the ownership of another. Knowledge (العلم), if limited, would not be from its possessor [originally], because a thing does not limit itself; thus it would be by the bestowal and teaching of another, and so on.

The proof that he (peace be upon him) based his explanation on the meaning of limit is his saying: "and every hearer other than Him is deaf to subtle sounds" etc., for it and what follows are clear in indicating the limitation of creatures, and the context is one.

As for his saying (peace be upon him): "Every one named with 'oneness' other than Him is few"—and this sentence is the intended part of quoting the sermon—its basis on the meaning of limit is clear. For numerical oneness, which branches from the limitation of the one named, necessitates the division and multiplicity of the meaning. Whenever division and multiplicity increase, the "one" becomes more deeply "few" and weak in relation to the resulting multiplicity. Thus, every numerical "one" is few in relation to the "many" that stands opposite it, even if only by assumption.

As for the One whose meaning has no limit and no end, He does not admit the assumption of multiplicity, because He does not admit the occurrence of limit or the arising of distinction. Nothing of His meaning escapes His existence such that it could multiply Him and He could become strong by its joining, or become few and weak by its detachment. Rather, whenever a second is assumed for Him in His meaning, it is He Himself.

And in Nahj al-Balaghah: from a sermon of his (peace be upon him): "Praise be to Allah, who points to His existence through His creation, and through the originatedness (محدث) of His creation to His eternity (أزليته), and through their similarity to the fact that He has no likeness. Feelings do not touch Him, and veils do not hide Him—due to the separation between the Maker and the made, the Limiter (الحاد) and the limited (المحدود), and the Lord and the lorded. The One (الأحد) not by the interpretation of number; the Creator not in the sense of movement and fatigue; the Hearer not with an instrument; the Seer not by the dispersal of an organ; the Witness not by contact; the Distinct (البائن) not by the distance of space; the Manifest not by being seen; the Hidden not by subtlety. He is distinct from things by subduing them and having power over them, and things are distinct from Him by submitting to Him and returning to Him. Whoever describes Him has limited Him, and whoever limits Him has numbered Him, and whoever numbers Him has nullified His eternity."

I say: The beginning of his speech (peace be upon him) is based on the fact that all meanings and attributes witnessed in possible beings (الممكنات) are limited matters that are not realized except by a limiter who limits them, a maker who makes them, and a lord who lords them—and that is Allah, the Glorified. Since the limit is of His making, it is posterior to Him and not necessary for Him; thus the courtyard of His greatness is sanctified from these limits.

When it is so, that which He is described with of attributes is not limited by a limit—even if our word is falling short of Him and the meaning is not sufficient for Him. He, the Exalted, is One (أحد) not by the interpretation of number which dictates limitation. Upon this course is His creation, His hearing, His seeing, His witnessing, and so on.

Among the branches of that is that His distinction from His creation is not in the sense of separation and detachment—exalted is He above connection and separation, and indwelling and detachment—but rather in the sense of His subduing them and His power over them, and their submission and return to Him.

His saying (peace be upon him): "Whoever describes Him has limited Him, and whoever limits Him has numbered Him—and whoever numbers Him has nullified His eternity" is a branch of the affirmation of numerical oneness being the nullification of eternity (الأزل). For the reality of eternity is His being, the Exalted, infinite in His essence and attributes and unlimited. When He is considered from the aspect that He is not preceded by anything that goes before Him, that is His eternity (أزله). When He is considered from the aspect that He is not followed by anything that comes after Him, that is His perpetuity (أبده). Sometimes it is considered from both sides, and it is duration (دواماً).

As for what appears from a number of researchers that the meaning of His being, the Exalted, eternal (أزلياً) is that He is prior and advanced over His originated creation by infinite times in which there was no news of creation nor any trace of them, it is among the most hideous of errors. Where is the time (الزمان)—which is the measure of the movement of moving things—to participate with Him, the Exalted, in His eternity?!

And in Nahj al-Balaghah: from a sermon of his (peace be upon him): "Praise be to Allah, the Creator of the servants, the Spreader of the cradle, the Sender of the valleys, and the Fertilizers of the highlands. His priority has no beginning, and His eternity has no end. He is the First who never ceased, and the Lasting without a term. Foreheads have fallen prostrate to Him, and lips have unified Him. He limited things when He created them to distinguish them from His likeness. Imaginations do not estimate Him with limits and movements, nor with limbs and instruments. It is not said: 'When?' nor is a term set for Him with 'Until.' The Manifest—it is not said: 'From what?' and the Hidden—it is not said: 'In what?' Not a phantom that passes away, nor hidden such that He is contained. He did not draw near to things by contact, nor did He move far from them by separation. Not hidden from Him of His servants is the movement of a glance, nor the repetition of a word, nor the approach of a hill, nor the extension of a step in a dark night, nor in a still dusk, over which the luminous moon casts its shadow, followed by the sun with its light in setting and returning—and the turning of times and ages—from the coming of an approaching night and the departing of a retreating day. Before every goal and duration, and every enumeration and number. Exalted is He above what the limiters attribute to Him of the qualities of measures, the ends of regions, the establishing of dwellings, and the occupying of places. For the limit is set for His creation, and to other than Him it is attributed. He did not create things from eternal origins, nor from everlasting beginnings; rather, He created what He created and established its limit, and He fashioned what He fashioned and perfected its form."

And in Nahj al-Balaghah: from a sermon of his (peace be upon him): "He has not unified Him who has asked 'How?' about Him; nor has he attained His reality who has made a likeness for Him; nor has he intended Him who has compared Him; nor has he sought Him who has pointed to Him and imagined Him. Everything known by itself is made (مصنوع), and everything subsisting in another is an effect (معلول). An agent not by the agitation of an instrument; a determiner not by the turning of thought; wealthy not by acquisition. Times do not accompany Him, and instruments do not assist Him. His being preceded times, His existence preceded non-existence, and His eternity preceded beginning. By His making the senses perceive, it is known that He has no sense; and by His making opposites between matters, it is known that He has no opposite; and by His making comparisons between things, it is known that He has no peer. He opposed light with darkness, clarity with obscurity, solidity with moisture, and heat with cold. A joiner between their enmities, a coupler between their disparities, a bringer-near of their distances, and a separator of their proximities. He is not encompassed by a limit, nor calculated by a number. Indeed, instruments only limit themselves, and tools only point to their likes. They are prevented from eternity by 'since' (منذ), and protected from pre-eternity by 'already' (قد), and kept away from perfection by 'if only' (لو لا). Through them their Maker manifested Himself to the intellects, and through them He was prevented from the sight of the eyes. Stillness and movement do not run upon Him—and how could that which He set in motion run upon Him? Or that which He manifested return to Him? Or that which He originated occur within Him? If so, His essence would vary, His core would be divided, and His meaning would be prevented from eternity. He would have a 'behind' if He were found to have a 'front,' and He would seek completion if deficiency were necessary for Him. Then the sign of the made would arise within Him, and He would turn into a proof (دليلاً) after having been the proven-to (مدلولاً عليه)."

I say: The beginning of his speech (peace be upon him) is directed toward explaining the impossibility of His Sacred Essence being subject to limitation (الحد), and the necessity of limitation for everything other than Him; an initial clarification of this has already been provided.

His saying: "He is not encompassed by a limit (حد) nor accounted by number (بعد)" is like a conclusion to the preceding explanation. His saying: "Instruments (الأدوات) only limit themselves, and tools (الإله) only point to their likes" serves as another explanation for his saying: "He is not encompassed by a limit, etc." For the previous explanation was pursued from the perspective that these limits established in created things are made for the Transcendent Essence and are posterior to It, just as an act is posterior to its agent; thus, it is impossible for the Essence to be restricted by them, as there was an Essence and no act.

As for the explanation in his saying: "Instruments only limit," etc., it is pursued through another path. This is that the estimation (التقدير) and limitation (التحديد) which are the function of these instruments and limits only occur through a generic homogeneity (المسانخة النوعية). For instance, a weight (المثقال), which is a unit of measure, is used to weigh heavy objects, not colors or sounds; and time (الزمان), which is the measure of motion, only limits motions. Similarly, a human being is estimated by his average social weight, for example, by one who resembles him in humanity. In short, every one of these limits grants its limited object a meaning similar to itself. Every contingent attribute (صفة إمكانية), whatever it may be, is built upon a measure (قدر) and a limit (حد) and is necessitated by a term (أمد) and an end (نهاية). How then can its limited meaning be applied to an Eternal (أزلية), Everlasting (أبدية), and Infinite Essence?

This is his intended meaning (peace be upon him), and for this reason, he followed it with his saying: "The word 'since' (منذ) has prevented them from being eternal (القدمة)," etc. That is, the applicability of the word "since" (منذ) and the word "already" (قد)—which indicate temporal origination (الحدوث الزماني)—to things has prevented and protected them from being described as eternal. Likewise, the applicability of the word "if not" (لو لا) to things—which indicates deficiency (النقص) and the presence of an impediment—has kept them aside and distanced them from being perfect in every respect.

His saying: "Through them their Maker manifested (تجلى) to the intellects, and through them He was withheld from the sight of the eyes": the two pronouns refer to "things." That is, things, insofar as they are signs (آيات) for Him, the Exalted—and a sign only shows the possessor of the sign—are like mirrors that manifest nothing but Him, the Exalted. Thus, through them He manifested to the intellects, and through them He was also withheld from the sight of the eyes, since there is no path to looking at Him, the Exalted, except through these signs. Yet they are limited (محدودة) and can only perceive their likes, not their Lord Who encompasses all things.

This very meaning is the cause for His being withheld from the sight of the eyes, for they are composite instruments built upon limits; they only function within the limited, while the courtyard of the Lord of Might is far exalted above any limit.

His saying (peace be upon him): "Rest (السكون) and motion (الحركة) do not run upon Him," etc., is like a return to the beginning of the speech with another explanation. He explains that these acts and events, which terminate in motion and rest, do not run upon Him, nor do they recur in Him or originate [in Him]. For they are His effects (آثاره) that result from His influence (تأثيره) upon other than Himself. The meaning of an agent's influence is the directing of its effect, which branches from itself, toward another. There is no meaning for a thing influencing itself except through a type of division (التجزي) and composition (التركيب) occurring to its essence—like a human being, for instance, who manages his body with his soul, or strikes his head with his hand, or a physician who treats his own illness with his medical knowledge. All of this is only possible due to a difference in parts or aspects (الحيثيات); were it not for that, the occurrence of influence would be impossible.

The visual faculty (القوة الباصرة), for example, does not see itself, and fire does not burn its own essence. Thus, all agents (الفواعل) only act upon other than themselves, except in the case of composition and division, as you have seen. This is the meaning of his saying: "Otherwise, His essence would differ, His core (كنهه) would be divided, and His meaning would be prevented from eternity," etc.

His saying (peace be upon him): "And then the sign of the created (المصنوع) would arise in Him, and He would turn into a proof (دليلا) after having been the one proven (مدلولا عليه)." That is, if deficiency were to necessitate Him by these limits and measures reaching Him—and deficiency is among the signs of being created and the marks of contingency—He (the Exalted and Sanctified) would be associated with that which indicates His being created. He Himself, like all other created things, would be a proof for another existent who is eternal, perfect in existence, and unlimited in essence—the God Who is transcendent above every supposed deficiency and exalted above being reached by the hands of limits and measures.

Know that what his saying indicates—that being a proof (الدلالة) is among the affairs of the contingent created being—does not contradict what is gathered from the rest of his speech and the speech of the other Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them): that He, the Exalted, is known by His very Essence, and other than Him is known through Him; and that He is the indicator of His Essence and the proof for His creatures. For knowledge (العلم) is not [always] the same as knowledge, and proof (الدلالة) is not [always] the same as proof. I hope that Allah, the Exalted, will grant me the success to clarify this and expand upon it in some of the related upcoming discussions, if Allah the Mighty wills.

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of narration from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: While the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) was delivering a sermon on the pulpit of Kufa, a man called "Dhi'lib"—sharp-tongued, eloquent in speech, and brave of heart—stood up to him and said: "O Commander of the Faithful, have you seen your Lord?" He replied: "Woe to you, O Dhi'lib! I would not worship a Lord I have not seen!"

He asked: "O Commander of the Faithful, how did you see Him?" He replied: "O Dhi'lib, eyes do not see Him through the witnessing of sight, but hearts have seen Him through the realities of faith (حقائق الإيمان). Woe to you, O Dhi'lib! Indeed, my Lord is subtle in subtlety (لطيف اللطافة) but is not described by 'subtlety' (اللطف); great in greatness (عظيم العظمة) but is not described by 'greatness' (العظم); grand in grandeur (كبير الكبرياء) but is not described by 'grandeur' (الكبر); majestic in majesty (جليل الجلالة) but is not described by 'coarseness' (الغلظ). He is before everything, yet it is not said that anything was before Him; and He is after everything, yet it is not said of Him 'after.' He willed things without a resolution (همة); He is perceptive (دراك) without deception. He is in things without being intermingled with them, and not distinct (بائن) from them. He is manifest (ظاهر) without the interpretation of direct contact; He is self-manifesting (متجل) without the beginning of a vision. He is distinct (بائن) without distance; near (قريب) without proximity. He is subtle (لطيف) without corporeality (تجسم); existent (موجود) not after non-existence. He is an agent (فاعل) not by necessity; an estimator (مقدر) without motion; a willer (مريد) without hesitation. He is All-Hearing (سميع) without an instrument; All-Seeing (بصير) without a tool. Places do not contain Him, times do not accompany Him, attributes (الصفات) do not limit Him, and slumbers (السنات) do not overtake Him. His being preceded times, His existence preceded non-existence, and His eternity preceded beginning. By His endowing the senses (المشاعر) with sensation, it is known that He has no sense; by His making the substances (الجواهر) substantial, it is known that He has no substance; by His creating opposition between things, it is known that He has no opposite; and by His creating correlation between things, it is known that He has no peer. He opposed light with darkness, hardness with moisture, and cold with heat. He is the uniter of their adversaries and the separator of their affinities; they point by their separation to their Separator, and by their union to their Uniter. That is His saying, Mighty and Majestic: وَمِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (And of all things We created pairs so that you may take admonition. 51:49). Thus, He separated through them between 'before' and 'after' so that it may be known that He has no before or after. They testify by their innate dispositions (غرائزها) that there is no innate disposition for their Disposer; they inform by their timing (توقيتها) that there is no time for their Timer. He veiled some of them from others so that it may be known that there is no veil between Him and His creation other than His creation. He was a Lord when there was no one to be lorded over (مربوب), a God when there was no one to be deified (مألوه), All-Knowing when there was nothing to be known (معلوم), and All-Hearing when there was nothing to be heard (مسموع)." Then he began to recite:

My Master has never ceased to be known for praise, And my Master has never ceased to be described by generosity. He was when there was no light to be illuminated by, Nor darkness brooding over the horizons. So our Lord is unlike all of creation, And unlike everything described in the imaginations. —the verses.

I say: His speech (peace be upon him)—as you see—is directed toward explaining the meaning of the Unicity of the Essence (أحدية الذات) in everything that applies to It and refers to It. It explains that He, the Exalted, is infinite in Essence and unlimited; thus, no essence stands in opposition to His Essence, for otherwise, it would threaten It with limitation and overcome It with estimation. He is the one who encompasses all things, the Overseer (المهيمن) over every matter. No attribute (صفة) attaches to Him that is distinct from His Essence, for in that would be the nullification of His eternity and His lack of limitation.

Indeed, His attribute of perfection (صفته الكمالية) is not limited by a boundary that repels another or is repelled by another—just as knowledge in us is different from power due to the mutual exclusion (المدافعة) between them in concept (مفهوما) and instance (مصداقا). There is no such exclusion between them in Him, the Exalted; rather, the attribute is identical to the attribute (عين الصفة), and identical to every one of His supreme attributes, and the Name is identical to every one of His Beautiful Names.

In fact, there is a meaning even subtler and deeper than that: these meanings and concepts (المفاهيم) are for the intellect like scales and measures with which external existence and actual being are weighed and measured. They are limited boundaries (حدود محدودة) that do not relinquish this function even if we join some to others or seek aid from one for another. Nothing is scooped up by their vessels except what approximates them in limit. If we suppose a matter that is unlimited and then aim for it with these limited measures, we will perceive nothing of it except the limited, which is other than it. The more we persist in trying to perceive it, the more it increases in transcendence and distance.

The concept of knowledge (العلم), for example, is a meaning we have taken from a limited description in the external world which we count as a perfection for that in which it exists. In this concept, there is a limitation that prevents it from encompassing power (القدرة) and life (الحياة), for instance. If we apply it to Him, the Exalted, and then adjust its limitation by qualification, such as our saying: "Knowledge not like [other] knowledges," then even if it is freed from some limitation, it remains a concept that does not relinquish its nature—which is its failure to encompass what lies beyond it (and every concept has a "beyond" that it falls short of encompassing). Adding one concept to another concept does not lead to the nullification of the property of being a concept (المفهومية), as is evident.

This is what bewilders the intelligent person in describing Him, the Exalted, with what his heart and intellect affirm for Him. This is what is gathered from his saying (peace be upon him): "Attributes do not limit Him," and from his saying in the previously quoted sermon: "The perfection of sincerity to Him is the negation of attributes from Him," and also his saying in that sermon: "He whose attribute has no defined limit (حد محدود), nor an existing description (نعت موجود)." You see that he (peace be upon him) affirms the attribute while simultaneously negating it or negating its limit. It is well known that affirming the attribute itself is inseparable from the limit; thus, negating the limit from it is to drop it after establishing it. It results in the fact that affirming any attribute of perfection in Him does not negate what lies beyond it; thus, the attributes unite with one another and then unite with the Essence, and there is no limit. Furthermore, it does not negate what lies beyond them of that for which we have no concept to represent or perception to grasp—so understand that.

Were it not for the fact that concepts fall away when overlooking the courtyard of His Greatness and Grandeur in the sense mentioned, it would be possible for the intellect to encompass Him with the vague general concepts it possesses—such as describing Him as an Essence not like [other] essences, having knowledge not like [other] knowledges, power not like the power of others, and life not like other types of life. For this mode of description leaves nothing without counting it and encompassing it in a general way (إجمالا). Is it possible for anything to encompass Him, the Glorified? Or is it that what is prohibited is encompassing Him in detail (تفصيلا), while general encompassment is acceptable? Yet He, the Exalted, has said: وَلا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْماً (and they do not comprehend Him in [their] knowledge. 20:110), and He said: أَلا إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ مُحِيطٌ (Look! He indeed encompasses all things. 41:54). Allah, the Glorified, is not encompassed by anything from any direction by any mode of encompassment. His Sacred Essence does not accept [division into] general and detailed such that His generality would have one ruling and His detail another—so understand that.

In al-Ihtijaj, from him (peace be upon him) in a sermon: "His proof (دليله) is His signs, His existence (وجوده) is His affirmation (إثباته), His recognition (معرفته) is His monotheism (توحيده), and His monotheism is His distinction (تمييزه) from His creation. The ruling of distinction is a distinction of attribute (بينونة صفة), not a distinction of isolation (بينونة عزلة). He is a Creator Lord, not a created one lorded over. Whatever is conceptualized (تصور), He is contrary to it." Then he (peace be upon him) said: "He is not a God who is known through himself (عرف بنفسه); He is the one who points through the proof to Himself, and leads through recognition to Himself."

I say: Reflection upon what has preceded clarifies that the sermon is directed toward explaining that His unity (وحدته), the Exalted, is a non-numerical unity (وحدة غير عددية), due to its explicitness that recognition of Him is identical to His monotheism—that is, affirming His existence is identical to affirming His unity. If this unity were numerical, it would be other than the Essence, and the Essence in itself would not suffice for unity except by a cause external to the aspect of the Essence's establishment.

This is of the wondrous logic and most eloquent explanation in the chapter of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — the explanation of which requires a vast space that the style of discussion in this book cannot accommodate. Among the subtlest objectives placed within it is his saying (peace be upon him): "His existence is His affirmation," by which he means that the proof for Him is His very external existence; that is, He does not enter the mind, nor can the intellect contain Him.

His saying: "Whatever is conceptualized, He is contrary to it" does not mean that He is other than the mental image—for all external things are of this character. Rather, it means that He, the Exalted, is contrary to what the mental conceptualization (التصور الذهني) discloses, whatever it may be. No mental image encompasses Him. You should not be heedless of the fact that His courtyard is transcendent even from this conceptualization—I mean the conceptualization that He is contrary to every conceptualization.

And his saying: "He is not a God who is known through himself" is directed toward explaining His Majesty, the Exalted, above being the object of a [limited] recognition, and His overcoming every understanding and perception. For everyone to whom our recognition attaches is in himself other than ourselves and our recognition, and then our recognition attaches to him. But He, the Exalted, encompasses us and our recognition, being the Sustainer over that. Thus, there is no stronghold for our souls or our recognition to grasp from the encompassment of His Essence and the reach of His authority such that an attachment of one isolated thing to another isolated thing could occur.

He (peace be upon him) explained that by his saying: "He is the one who points through the proof to Himself, and leads through recognition to Himself." That is, He, the Exalted, is the Proof who points the proof toward pointing to Him, and leads recognition toward attaching to Him, the Exalted, with a type of attachment, due to His encompassment and authority over all things. How then can anything find its way to His Essence to encompass Him while He encompasses it and its finding the way?

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of narration from Umar ibn Ali from Ali (peace be upon him), he said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "Monotheism (التوحيد) — its manifest (ظاهره) is in its hidden (باطنه), and its hidden is in its manifest. Its manifest is described but not seen, and its hidden is existent and not concealed. It is sought in every place, and no place is devoid of it for the blink of an eye. It is present (حاضر) yet not limited, and absent (غائب) yet not missing."

I say: The speech of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) is directed toward explaining His unity, the Exalted, which is non-numerical, based on His being unlimited by any limit. For the lack of limitation is what necessitates the non-isolation of the manifest of His monotheism and description from its hidden, and its hidden from its manifest. For the manifest and the hidden only differ and become isolated from one another by a limit; when the limit is removed, they intermingle and unite.

Likewise, the "described manifest" is only encompassed, and the "existent hidden" is only concealed and veiled, if they are limited such that neither exceeds its designated limit. Similarly, the "present" is only limited and its existence gathered at the one before whom it is present, and the "absent" is missing, due to limitation. Were it not for that, the present would not be gathered in its entire existence before the one it is present with, nor would the absent be veiled by the hijab of absence, nor would there be any cover between it and the one it is absent from—and this is evident.

(Historical Discussion)

The statement that the world has a Maker, and then the statement that He is One, is among the oldest issues circulating among the thinkers of this species, to which their embedded primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — guides them. Even idolatry (الوثنية) based on polytheism, if we reflect on the reality of its meaning, we find it built upon the foundation of the monotheism of the Maker and the establishment of intercessors (شفعاء) with Him: ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللهِ زُلْفى (We do not worship them except that they may bring us near to Allah. 39:3), even if it later deviated from its course and its affair led to granting independence and primacy to gods other than Allah.

The primordial nature (الفطرة) that calls to the monotheism of the God, although it calls to a single God who is unlimited in greatness and grandeur in Essence and attribute—as previously explained by drawing from the Wise Book—nevertheless, the human being's familiarity and intimacy in the context of his life with numerical units on one hand, and the struggle of the monotheists against idolaters, dualists, and others to negate the plurality of gods on the other hand, recorded unity as numerical and caused the ruling of the aforementioned primordial nature to be as if forgotten.

For this reason, you see that what is transmitted from the words of the philosophers who researched in ancient Egypt, Greece, Alexandria, and others after them, grants numerical unity (الوحدة العددية)—until it was explicitly stated by the likes of the Master Abu Ali ibn Sina in the book al-Shifa'. The words of others after him followed this course until around the year one thousand of the Prophetic Hijra.

As for the scholars of theology (أهل الكلام) among the researchers, their arguments for monotheism do not yield more than numerical unity either, even though these arguments are generally taken from the Wise Book. This is what is obtained from the words of the people of research on this issue.

Thus, the meaning of monotheism (التوحيد) explained by the Quran was the first step taken in teaching this reality of knowledge. However, the people of exegesis and those who engaged with the sciences of the Quran among the Companions and the Successors, and then those who followed them, neglected this noble research. Thus, in the collections of hadith and the books of exegesis transmitted from them, you do not see a trace of this reality, neither by an explanatory statement nor by a demonstrative path.

We have found nothing that uncovers its veil except what has been reported specifically in the speech of Imam Ali ibn Abi Talib (upon whom be the best of peace). For his speech is the opener of its door and the lifter of its cover and veil, upon the best guidance and the clearest path of proof. Then [it appeared] in the words of the Islamic philosophers after the first millennium of the Hijra, and they explicitly stated that they only benefited from his speech (peace be upon him).

This is the secret behind our restricting the previous traditional research to quoting samples of the gems of his exquisite speech (peace be upon him), because the pursuit of this issue and its explanation through the path of demonstrative argument (الإحتجاج البرهاني) is not found in the speech of anyone other than him (peace be upon him).

For this very reason, we have refrained from holding an independent philosophical discussion for this issue, for the proofs provided for this purpose are composed of these premises explained in his speech; they do not add anything to what is in his speech. All are built upon the pureness of existence (صرافة الوجود) and the unicity of the Essence (أحدية الذات), exalted is His greatness (1).

(1) The insightful critic and the reflective person may find it wondrous the slip that occurred from a number of researching scholars when they mentioned that these Alawite sermons placed in Nahj al-Balagha are fabricated and intrusive; some of them mentioned that they were fabricated by al-Sharif al-Radi (may Allah have mercy on him). The discussion regarding the aspects of this blunder has preceded.

I wonder how fabrication and interpolation could find their way into a precise scholarly position that the understandings of scholars were not strong enough to grasp, even after he (peace be upon him) opened its door and lifted its veil for long centuries, until it was successfully understood after traveling the path of advanced thought for a thousand years. No one else among the Companions or the Successors could bear it. Rather, the words of those who charge [the sermons] with fabrication cry out at the top of their lungs that they thought Quranic realities and high scholarly principles were nothing but common, vulgar concepts, only excelling through eloquent wording and powerful expression.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُحَرِّمُوا۟ طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَآ أَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (٨٧)
O you who have faith! Do not prohibit the good things that Allah has made lawful to you, and do not transgress. Indeed Allah does not like the transgressors. (87)
وَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنتُم بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ (٨٨)
Eat the lawful and good things Allah has provided you, and be wary of Allah in whom you have faith. (88)
لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا عَقَّدتُّمُ ٱلْأَيْمَـٰنَ ۖ فَكَفَّـٰرَتُهُۥٓ إِطْعَامُ عَشَرَةِ مَسَـٰكِينَ مِنْ أَوْسَطِ مَا تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ أَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ ثَلَـٰثَةِ أَيَّامٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ كَفَّـٰرَةُ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ إِذَا حَلَفْتُمْ ۚ وَٱحْفَظُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٨٩)
Allah shall not take you to task for what is frivolous in your oaths; but He shall take you to task for what you pledge in earnest. The atonement for it is to feed ten needy persons with the average food you give to your families, or their clothing, or the freeing of a slave. He who cannot afford [any of these] shall fast for three days. That is the atonement for your oaths when you vow. But keep your oaths. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may give thanks. (89)

(Commentary)

These three verses, along with several subsequent verses up to verse one hundred and some of the chapter, are verses clarifying several subsidiary rulings (فروع الأحكام). They are all interspersed, as it were, between the verses addressing the stories of the Messiah (عليه‌السلام) and the Christians. Because they consist of scattered groups revealed concerning diverse rulings, each being independent and complete in the meaning it intends, it is difficult to judge whether they were revealed all at once or accompanied the rest of the chapter's verses in their revelation, as there is no evidence for this in their contents. As for what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول), some of the most significant reports will be mentioned in the "Discussion of Traditions."

The same applies to these three verses under discussion; the third verse is independent in its meaning, and the first verse is independent of it, even if it is not devoid of a certain type of suitability (مناسبة). There is some connection between them in that one of the instances of "vain oaths" (لغو اليمين) is when an oath pertains to the prohibition of certain wholesome things (طيبات) that Allah the Exalted has made lawful. Perhaps this was the motivation for those from whom it is narrated in the occasions of revelation that they mentioned the revelation of all these verses together regarding vain oaths.

This is the state of the first verse in relation to the third. As for the second verse, it is as if it is a completion of the first, as evidenced to some extent by its conclusion—namely, His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي أَنْتُمْ بِهِ مُؤْمِنُونَ (and be wary of Allah in whom you have faith). Indeed, its beginning also suggests this, as it contains a conjunction and a command to eat of the lawful and wholesome things which the first verse forbids prohibiting and avoiding. Thus, the two verses harmonize in meaning and unite in ruling, possessing a single context (سياق).

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ (O you who have faith! Do not prohibit the good things that Allah has made lawful to you). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The prohibited (الحرام) is that which is forbidden, either by divine decree (تسخير إلهي), by coercive prevention (منع قهري), or by prevention from the perspective of reason (العقل), the law (الشرع), or from one whose command is followed." This concludes the relevant portion.

He also said: "The root of lawfulness (الحل) is the untying of a knot (حل العقدة); from this is His saying, Mighty and Majestic: وَاحْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِنْ لِسانِي (and untie the knot from my tongue). And 'I settled' (حللت) means 'I descended'; its origin is from 'untying the loads' (حل الأحمال) upon descending, then its usage was stripped to mean simply descending. Thus it is said: 'he settled' (حل حلولا) and 'another settled him' (أحله غيره). Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: أَوْ تَحُلُّ قَرِيباً مِنْ دارِهِمْ (or it alights near their homes) and وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ (and settled their people in the abode of ruin). It is also said: 'the debt became due' (حل الدين), meaning its payment became obligatory. A hullah (الحلة) refers to a group that has settled, and a hayy halal (حي حلال) is similar. A mahalla (المحلة) is the place of settling. From 'untying the knot' (حل العقدة), their saying 'the thing became lawful' (حل الشيء حلا) was metaphorically derived."

Allah the Exalted said: وَكُلُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ حَلالاً طَيِّباً (Eat of what Allah has provided you as lawful and good) and He the Exalted said: هذا حَلالٌ وَهذا حَرامٌ (This is lawful and this is forbidden). End quote.

It appears that the opposition of lawfulness (الحل) is prohibition (الحرمة), just as the opposition between being out of ihram (الحل) and being in ihram (الحرم أو الإحرام) arises from the conceptualization of a "knot" (العقد) in the restriction—which is the meaning of prohibition and its like—and then its opposition with "untying" (الحل), which is metaphorically used for the meaning of permissibility (الجواز) and license (الإباحة). These two terms—lawfulness and prohibition—were linguistic realities (حقائق عرفية) before Islam, rather than being purely legal (شرعية) or technical religious terms (متشرعة).

The verse—namely His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا (O you who have faith! Do not prohibit...) etc.—forbids the believers from prohibiting what Allah has made lawful for them. Prohibiting what Allah has made lawful means making it forbidden (حرام) just as Allah the Exalted made it lawful (حلال). This occurs either by legislating in opposition to His legislation, or by prevention (المنع) or abstention (الامتناع) such that one leaves something lawful by refusing to perform it, or by preventing oneself or others from it. All of this constitutes prohibition, restriction, and a contention with Allah, the Glorified, in His sovereignty (سلطانه) and an encroachment (اعتداء) upon Him that contradicts faith in Allah and His signs. For this reason, the prohibition began with the address: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (O you who have faith!), for the meaning is: Do not prohibit what Allah has made lawful for you while you have believed in Him and submitted to His command.

This is also supported by His saying at the end of the following verse: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي أَنْتُمْ بِهِ مُؤْمِنُونَ (and be wary of Allah in whom you have faith).

The addition of His saying: طَيِّباتِ (good things) to His saying: ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ (what Allah has made lawful to you)—even though the sentence would be complete without it—is to point toward the completion of the reason for the prohibition. For the believers' prohibition of what Allah has made lawful for them, while being an encroachment upon Allah's sovereignty and a violation of their faith in Him and submission to His command, is also a departure from the judgment of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. This is because the primordial nature finds these lawful things wholesome (تستطيب) without any loathsomeness (استخباث). Allah, the Glorified, has informed of this in His description of His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the law (الشريعة) he brought, where He said: الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَالْإِنْجِيلِ يَأْمُرُهمْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهاهُمْ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ الطَّيِّباتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْخَبائِثَ وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَالْأَغْلالَ الَّتِي كانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِهِ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ (—those who follow the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet, whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel, who bids them to do what is right and forbids them from what is wrong, makes lawful to them all the good things and forbids them from all vicious things, and relieves them of their burdens and the shackles that were upon them —those who believe in him, honour him, and help him and follow the light that has been sent down with him, they are the felicitous) (7:157).

By what we have explained, it is supported, firstly: that the meaning of prohibiting the good things Allah has made lawful is the obligation (الإلزام) and commitment (الالتزام) to abandon lawful things.

Secondly: that the meaning of lawfulness (الحل) is the opposite of prohibition (الحرمة), and it encompasses the permissible (المباحات), the recommended (المستحبات), and even the obligatory (الواجبات), in fulfillment of the requirement of opposition.

Thirdly: that the addition of "good things" (الطيبات) to "what Allah has made lawful" in His saying: طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ is an explanatory addition (إضافة بيانية).

Fourthly: that the meaning of encroachment (الاعتداء) in His saying: وَلا تَعْتَدُوا (and do not transgress) is encroachment upon Allah, the Glorified, in His legislative sovereignty (سلطانه التشريعي), or transgressing the limits (حدود) of Allah by divesting oneself of obedience to Him and submission to Him, and prohibiting what He has made lawful. As He the Exalted said at the end of the verse of divorce: تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ فَلا تَعْتَدُوها وَمَنْ يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ اللهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ (These are the bounds of Allah, so do not transgress them, and whoever transgresses the bounds of Allah —it is they who are the wrongdoers) (2:229). And His saying at the end of the verses of inheritance: تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها وَذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ * وَمَنْ يَعْصِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَيَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَهُ يُدْخِلْهُ ناراً خالِداً فِيها وَلَهُ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ (These are Allah’s bounds, and whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, He shall admit him into gardens with conduits running thru them, to remain in them [forever]. That is the great triumph. But whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle and transgresses His bounds, He shall make him enter a Fire, to remain in it [forever], and there is a humiliating punishment for him) (4:13-14). These verses—as you see—consider steadfastness and commitment to what Allah has legislated as a praised obedience to Him and His Messenger, while departing from submission, commitment, and compliance is considered a censured encroachment and transgression of Allah's limits, for which one is punished.

The sum of the verse's meaning is the prohibition of making what Allah has made lawful forbidden by avoiding it and refraining from approaching it. For this contradicts faith in Allah and His signs and opposes the fact that these lawful things are wholesome (طيبات) with no viciousness (خباثة) in them that would necessitate avoidance. It is an encroachment, and Allah does not love the encroachers.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَعْتَدُوا إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِينَ (and do not transgress. Indeed Allah does not like the transgressors). You have already seen that the apparent context (ظاهر السياق) indicates that the intended encroachment is the aforementioned prohibition in the preceding sentence. Thus, His saying: وَلا تَعْتَدُوا serves as an emphasis (تأكيد) for His saying: لا تُحَرِّمُوا (Do not prohibit...) etc.

As for what some have mentioned: that the intended encroachment is exceeding the limit of moderation in lawful things by throwing oneself into their enjoyment and seeking pleasure from them, as opposed to abandoning and avoiding their consumption out of asceticism (تقشف) and monkery (ترهب). Thus, the meaning of the verse would be: Do not prohibit for yourselves what Allah has made lawful for you of the pleasurable good things by intentionally abandoning their enjoyment out of devotion (تنسك) and seeking proximity to Him, the Exalted; and do not encroach by exceeding the limit of moderation into extravagance (إسراف) and excess (إفراط) that is harmful to your bodies or souls.

Or that the intended encroachment is bypassing the lawful good things for the prohibited vicious things (الخبائث). The meaning would then return to: do not avoid the lawful things and do not commit the prohibited things. In other words: do not prohibit what Allah has made lawful for you, and do not make lawful what Allah has prohibited for you.

Each of these two meanings, while correct in itself and supported by the Book without any doubt, does not align with the verse according to its apparent context and the context of the following verse. Not every correct meaning can be imposed upon every word, regardless of how it is phrased or where it occurs.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكُلُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ حَلالاً طَيِّباً (Eat of what Allah has provided you as lawful and good) until the end of the verse. The apparent conjunction—namely the connection of His saying: وَكُلُوا (And eat) to His saying: لا تُحَرِّمُوا (Do not prohibit)—is that the meaning of this verse is in the position of repetition and emphasis for the content of the previous verse. This is supported by the context of the beginning of the verse, as it contains His saying: حَلالاً طَيِّباً (lawful and good), which parallels His saying in the previous verse: طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ (the good things that Allah has made lawful). Likewise, its conclusion parallels the conclusion of the previous verse, in the correspondence between His saying here: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي أَنْتُمْ بِهِ مُؤْمِنُونَ (and be wary of Allah in whom you have faith) and His saying in the previous verse: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (O you who have faith!). This has already been explained.

Accordingly, His saying: كُلُوا (Eat...) etc., is of the category of a command following a prohibition (الأمر عقيب الحظر). The specification of His saying: كُلُوا (Eat) after the generalization of His saying: لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ (Do not prohibit the good things...) etc., is either a specification in terms of wording only—where "eating" refers to any form of disposal (التصرف) of the wholesome blessings Allah has provided, whether by eating in the sense of nourishment or by other modes of disposal. It has been mentioned repeatedly that the use of "eating" (الأكل) to mean general disposal is a widespread and common usage.

Or it is possible that "eating" is intended in its literal sense, and the occasion of revelation for the two verses was that some believers during the time of revelation prohibited certain wholesome foods for themselves. Thus, the two verses were revealed to forbid that. The prohibition in the first verse was made general to include eating and other things to provide a universal rule (القاعدة الكلية), since the criterion (ملاك) for the prohibition applies equally to lawful foods and other things.

His saying: مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ (of what Allah has provided you), according to what we have inferred from the meaning of the two verses, is the object (مفعول) of His saying: كُلُوا (Eat), and His saying: حَلالاً طَيِّباً (lawful and good) are circumstantial qualifiers (حالين) for the relative pronoun (الموصول). In this way, the two verses harmonize. It has also been said that His saying: حَلالاً طَيِّباً is the object of His saying: كُلُوا, and His saying: مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ is connected to His saying: كُلُوا or is a circumstantial qualifier for "lawful" (الحلال) that was placed before it because it is indefinite (نكرة), or that His saying: حَلالاً is an adjective for an omitted verbal noun (مصدر محذوف), the estimation being: "a lawful and good provision" (رزقا حلالا طيبا), among other views.

Some have used His saying: حَلالاً (lawful) as evidence that "provision" (الرزق) includes both the lawful and the prohibited; otherwise, the qualifier would be redundant.

The response is that it is not a restrictive qualifier (قيد احترازي) to exclude provision that is not lawful or good, but rather an explanatory qualifier (قيد توضيحي) equal to what it qualifies. The point in mentioning it is to clarify that its being lawful and good leaves no excuse for anyone to avoid or refrain from it, as previously mentioned. The discussion on the meaning of "provision" (الرزق) has already passed under the commentary of verse 27 of Surah Al-Imran in the third volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ (Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths, but He will take you to task for the oaths you have pledged). "Vain" (اللغو) refers to that which results in no effect among actions. "Oaths" (الأيمان) is the plural of yamin (يمين), which is a vow or swearing. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The yamin in swearing is metaphorically derived from the 'hand' (اليد), considering what is done by those making a covenant or alliance and others. Allah the Exalted said: أَمْ لَكُمْ أَيْمانٌ عَلَيْنا بالِغَةٌ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ (Or do you have any oaths [binding] on Us, reaching until the Day of Resurrection?), وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ (And they swear by Allah with their most solemn oaths), and لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ (Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths)." End quote. "Pledging" (التعقيد) is an intensive form of "tying" (العقد); it has also been recited as ‘aqadtum (عقدتم) without intensification. His saying: فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ (in your oaths) is connected to His saying: لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ (He will not take you to task) or to His saying: بِاللَّغْوِ (for what is vain), which is closer.

The opposition between His saying: بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ (for what is vain in your oaths) and His saying: بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ (for the oaths you have pledged) indicates that "vain" in oaths refers to that which the swearer does not intend to bind (لا يعقد عليه), but rather it flows from his tongue out of habit or otherwise—such as their saying, especially in buying and selling: "No, by Allah" and "Yes, by Allah"—as opposed to what is bound by a pledge (عقد) of commitment to perform or abandon an act, such as one saying: "By Allah, I shall certainly do such-and-such," or "By Allah, I shall certainly leave such-and-such."

This is the apparent meaning of the verse. This does not contradict the fact that the law (شرعا) may consider the saying of one: "By Allah, I shall certainly do such-and-such a prohibited act," or "By Allah, I shall certainly leave such-and-such an obligation," as being among vain oaths, because the Legislator has nullified the oath in that which has no merit (رجحان). This is an inclusion by way of the Sunnah, and it is not necessary for the Quran to point to every specific detail established by the Sunnah.

As for His saying: وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ (but He will take you to task for the oaths you have pledged), it only includes oaths that are legally valid (الممضاة شرعا) because of His saying at the end of the verse: وَاحْفَظُوا أَيْمانَكُمْ (and keep your oaths). There is no escape from its inclusion of these oaths according to the generality of its wording, and there is no meaning in commanding the keeping of oaths that Allah, the Glorified, has nullified. Thus, it is determined that "vain" among oaths in the verse is that which contains no pledge (عقد), and that which is pledged is the valid oath.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَفَّارَتُهُ إِطْعامُ عَشَرَةِ مَساكِينَ (its atonement is the feeding of ten needy persons) until His saying: أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ (or the freeing of a slave). Atonement (الكفارة) is the act by which the evil of a sin is covered in some way, from kufr (الكفر) meaning "covering." Allah the Exalted said: نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ (We will absolve you of your misdeeds) (4:31). Al-Raghib said: "Atonement (الكفارة) is that which covers the sin, and from it is the atonement for an oath." End quote.

His saying: فَكَفَّارَتُهُ (its atonement) is a derivation (تفريع) based on the oath, considering an implied premise such as: "If you break the oath (حنثتم), then its atonement is such-and-such." This is because the word "atonement" indicates a sin to which the atonement relates, and this sin is not the oath itself. If it were so, He would not have mentioned at the end of the verse: وَاحْفَظُوا أَيْمانَكُمْ (and keep your oaths), as there is no meaning in keeping that which contains a sin. Thus, the atonement relates to the breaking of the oath (حنث اليمين), not to the oath itself.

From this, it becomes clear that the "taking to task" (المؤاخذة) mentioned in His saying: وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ is the taking to task for breaking the oath, not for the act of making it. It was only attributed to the oath because its object—namely the breaking—relates to it. Thus, His saying: فَكَفَّارَتُهُ is derived from the implied breaking because His saying: يُؤاخِذُكُمُ (He will take you to task...) etc., indicates it. A similar explanation applies to His saying: ذلِكَ كَفَّارَةُ أَيْمانِكُمْ إِذا حَلَفْتُمْ (That is the atonement for your oaths when you swear), the estimation being: "when you swear and then break it."

His saying: إِطْعامُ عَشَرَةِ مَساكِينَ مِنْ أَوْسَطِ ما تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ أَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ (the feeding of ten needy persons with the average of what you feed your families, or their clothing, or the freeing of a slave) are three options. The use of "or" (الترديد) indicates the determination of one of them upon breaking the oath without combining them. His saying afterward: فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ ثَلاثَةِ أَيَّامٍ (but whoever cannot afford [that], then a three-day fast) indicates that the aforementioned options are elective (تخييرية) without the necessity of observing the order in which they were mentioned. Otherwise, the derivation in His saying: فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ (but whoever cannot afford...) etc., would be redundant, and the context would have required saying: "or a three-day fast."

In the verse, there are many subsidiary discussions whose reference is the science of jurisprudence (علم الفقه).

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ كَفَّارَةُ أَيْمانِكُمْ إِذا حَلَفْتُمْ (That is the atonement for your oaths when you swear). It was previously mentioned that the speech is in the estimation of: "when you swear and break it."
