In His saying: ذلِكَ كَفَّارَةُ أَيْمانِكُمْ and likewise in His saying: كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللهُ لَكُمْ (Thus does Allah clarify for you), there is a type of transition (التفات) and return from addressing the believers to addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Perhaps the point in this is that both sentences are part of the divine clarification for the people, which is only through the mediation of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Thus, it preserves his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) station in clarifying what was revealed to him for the people, as He the Exalted said: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ (We have sent down the Reminder to you so that you may clarify for the people what has been sent down to them) (16:44).

His saying, the Exalted: كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللهُ لَكُمْ آياتِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may give thanks), meaning He clarifies His rulings for you through His Prophet so that you may thank Him by learning them and acting upon them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ (the verse): He said: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from some of his men, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "This verse was revealed concerning the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), Bilal, and ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un. As for the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), he swore that he would never sleep at night. As for Bilal, he swore that he would never break his fast during the day. As for ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un, he swore that he would never engage in sexual intercourse."

The wife of ‘Uthman entered upon ‘Aishah, and she was a beautiful woman. ‘Aishah said: "Why do I see you neglected?" She replied: "For whom should I adorn myself? By Allah, my husband has not approached me for such-and-such a time, for he has become a monk, wears haircloth (المسوح), and has renounced the world."

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered, ‘Aishah informed him of that. He went out and called for a congregational prayer. The people gathered, and he ascended the pulpit, praised Allah, and extolled Him, then said: "What is the matter with certain people who prohibit for themselves the good things? Behold, I sleep at night, I marry, and I break my fast during the day. Whoever turns away from my Sunnah is not of me."

These men stood up and said: "O Messenger of Allah, we have sworn to that." So Allah revealed to him: لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ فَكَفَّارَتُهُ إِطْعامُ عَشَرَةِ مَساكِينَ مِنْ أَوْسَطِ ما تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ أَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ ثَلاثَةِ أَيَّامٍ ذلِكَ كَفَّارَةُ أَيْمانِكُمْ إِذا حَلَفْتُمْ (Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths, but He will take you to task for the oaths you have pledged. Its atonement is the feeding of ten needy persons with the average of what you feed your families, or their clothing, or the freeing of a slave. But whoever cannot afford [that], then a three-day fast. That is the atonement for your oaths when you swear).

I say: There is some obscurity in the application of His saying, the Exalted: لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ (the verse) to their swearing, as some discussion has already passed. Al-Tabrisi narrated the story in Majma‘ al-Bayan from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) without mentioning the conclusion, so let it be reflected upon.

In al-Ihtijaj, from al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (peace be upon him) in a tradition: that he said to Mu‘awiyah and his companions: "I adjure you by Allah—do you not know that ‘Ali was the first of the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to prohibit desires for himself? So Allah revealed: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ."

In al-Majma‘, regarding the verse: The commentators said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sat one day and reminded the people, describing the Resurrection. The people became soft-hearted and wept. Ten of the companions gathered in the house of ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un al-Jumahi; they were ‘Ali, Abu Bakr, ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud, Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, Salim the client of Abu Hudhayfah, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar, al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad al-Kindi, Salman al-Farisi, and Ma‘qil ibn Muqarrin. They agreed to fast during the day, stand in prayer at night, not sleep on beds, not eat meat or fat, not approach women or perfume, wear haircloth, reject the world, and travel through the land. Some of them even intended to castrate themselves.

News of this reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so he went to the house of ‘Uthman but did not find him. He said to his wife, Umm Hakim bint Abi Umayyah—her name was Hawla’ and she was a perfumer: "Is what has reached me about your husband and his companions true?" She disliked lying to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and disliked exposing her husband, so she said: "O Messenger of Allah, if ‘Uthman informed you, then he has told you the truth." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) departed, and when ‘Uthman entered, she informed him of that.

He and his companions came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to them: "Have I not been informed that you agreed on such-and-such?" They said: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah, and we intended nothing but good." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I was not commanded with that." Then he said: "Indeed, your souls have a right over you; so fast and break your fast, stand in prayer and sleep. For I stand and sleep, I fast and break my fast, I eat meat and fat, and I approach women. Whoever turns away from my Sunnah is not of me."

Then he gathered the people and addressed them, saying: "What is the matter with certain people who have prohibited women, food, perfume, sleep, and the desires of the world? Behold, I do not command you to be priests or monks, for there is no abandoning of meat in my religion, nor of women, nor the taking of hermitages. Indeed, the travel (سياحة) of my community is fasting, and their monkery (رهبانية) is Jihad. Worship Allah and do not associate anything with Him, perform the Hajj and the ‘Umrah, establish the prayer, pay the Zakat, fast Ramadan, and be upright, and you will be made upright. For those before you only perished through severity (التشديد); they were severe upon themselves, so Allah was severe upon them. Those are their remnants in the monasteries and hermitages." Then Allah revealed the verse.

I say: It appears by referring to the narrations of the [Sunni] group that this narration is merely a summary of the narrations reported on this subject, which are very numerous. He has presented it by combining their scattered contents, inserting some into others, and casting them as a single narration.

As for these narrations themselves, despite their abundance, the names of these companions do not all appear together in any one of them. Rather, the most comprehensive of them in wording mentions these companions with the phrase "‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un and his companions"; in some, "people from the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)"; and in others, "men from the companions of the Prophet (ص)."

Likewise, what occurred in this narration regarding the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and its details are found as separate sentences in the narrations. Similarly, regarding what they pledged and intended to abandon, the narrations do not explicitly state that all of them agreed on all of those things. Rather, some narrations state their differences in what they intended or pledged, as in the Sahih of al-Bukhari and Muslim from ‘Aishah: "People from the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) asked the wives of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about his actions in private. Some of them said: 'I will not eat meat'; some said: 'I will not marry women'; and some said: 'I will not sleep on a bed.' This reached the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so he said: 'What is the matter with certain people, one of whom says such-and-such? But I fast and break my fast, I sleep and I stand, I eat meat, and I marry women. Whoever turns away from my Sunnah is not of me.'"

Perhaps the meaning of the statement in the narration "and they agreed to fast during the day..." etc., is that the group as a whole agreed on the set of actions, not that every single one of them resolved upon all of them. Although the narrations differ in their contents—and among them are the weak, the disconnected (المرسل), and the reliable—reflection upon all of them leads to the confidence that a group of the companions resolved upon this type of asceticism and devotion, and that ‘Ali (peace be upon him) and ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un were among them, and that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to them: "Whoever turns away from my Sunnah is not of me." Allah knows best. You should refer to the traditional commentaries such as the Tafsir of al-Tabari, al-Durr al-Manthur, Fath al-Qadir, and their like.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Al-Tirmidhi (who graded it fair), Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn ‘Adi in al-Kamil, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas: that a man came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, when I eat meat, I become aroused for women and my desire takes hold of me, so I have prohibited meat for myself." Then was revealed: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ.

In the same source: Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Zayd ibn Aslam: that a guest from his family visited ‘Abdullah ibn Rawahah while he was with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When he returned to his family, he found they had not fed their guest, waiting for him. He said to his wife: "You have kept my guest for my sake; it is prohibited (حرام) for me." His wife said: "It is prohibited for me." The guest said: "It is prohibited for me." When he saw that, he placed his hand and said: "Eat in the name of Allah." Then he went to the Prophet (ص) and informed him. The Prophet (ص) said: "You have done right." Then Allah revealed: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ.

I say: It is possible that the two reasons mentioned in the last two narrations are from the application (تطبيق) of the narrators, which is common in narrations of the occasions of revelation. It is also possible for a verse to have numerous occasions for its revelation.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: I asked him about a man who said to his wife: "[You are] divorced," or "my slaves are free if I drink anything prohibited or lawful." He said: "As for the prohibited, he should not approach it whether he swore or not. As for the lawful, he should not leave it, for it is not for him to prohibit what Allah has made lawful, because Allah says: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ. Thus, there is nothing upon him regarding his oath concerning the lawful."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Mas‘adah ibn Sadaqah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I heard him saying regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ — He said: "The 'vain' (اللغو) is the saying of a man: 'No, by Allah' and 'Yes, by Allah,' without pledging (يعقد) anything."

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated the same in his Tafsir from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, and from Muhammad ibn Muslim similarly, which adds: "and he does not pledge it."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: When يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ was revealed concerning the people who prohibited women and meat for themselves, they said: "O Messenger of Allah, what shall we do with our oaths that we swore?" So Allah revealed: لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ.

I say: This narration resembles the conclusion of the first narration we cited at the beginning of the discussion, except that it does not align with the apparent meaning of the verse. For swearing to abandon an obligation or a permissible act is not devoid of a pledge (عقد) upon it, and the verse has opposed His saying: بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ with His saying: بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ. This indicates that the vain oath is that which has no pledge. This apparent meaning only agrees with the narration interpreting the vain oath as the saying of one: "No, by Allah" and "Yes, by Allah," without pledging anything. As for the oath nullified by the law, it contains a pledge upon what was sworn; thus, its nullification must be based on the Sunnah rather than the Book.

Furthermore, the context of the verse is the strongest evidence that it was set forth to clarify the atonement for an oath and the command to keep it independently, not in a secondary manner (تطفل) as this interpretation would necessitate.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا ٱلْخَمْرُ وَٱلْمَيْسِرُ وَٱلْأَنصَابُ وَٱلْأَزْلَـٰمُ رِجْسٌ مِّنْ عَمَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ فَٱجْتَنِبُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٩٠)
O you who have faith! Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and the divining arrows are abominations of Satan’s doing, so avoid them, so that you may be felicitous. (90)
إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَن يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ فِى ٱلْخَمْرِ وَٱلْمَيْسِرِ وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَعَنِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّنتَهُونَ (٩١)
Indeed Satan seeks to cast enmity and hatred among you through wine and gambling, and to hinder you from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer. Will you, then, relinquish? (91)
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَٱحْذَرُوا۟ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا عَلَىٰ رَسُولِنَا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٩٢)
And obey Allah and obey the Apostle, and beware; but if you turn your backs, then know that Our Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (92)
لَيْسَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَا طَعِمُوٓا۟ إِذَا مَا ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّأَحْسَنُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٩٣)
There will be no sin upon those who have faith and do righteous deeds in regard to what they have eaten [in the past] so long as they are Godwary and faithful and do righteous deeds, and are further Godwary and faithful, and are further Godwary and virtuous. And Allah loves the virtuous. (93)

(Commentary)

The verses are harmonious in their context (سياق), as if they were revealed all at once, or at least very close to one another in time of revelation. The final verse serves to dispel a potential objection (دفع الدخل), as we shall explain in detail. Collectively, they address the state of wine (الخمر), and some of them add to it games of chance (الميسر), sacrificial altars (الأنصاب), and divining arrows (الأزلام).

It has been previously mentioned in the discussion of His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُما أَكْبَرُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِما — "They ask you about wine and gambling. Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them, and some profits for mankind, but their sin is greater than their profit’" (2:219) in the first volume, and in His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلاةَ وَأَنْتُمْ سُكارى حَتَّى تَعْلَمُوا ما تَقُولُونَ — "O you who have faith! Do not approach prayer when you are intoxicated, until you know what you are saying" (4:43) in the fourth volume of this book, that these two verses—along with His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ — "Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies (الفواحش) — shameful acts that exceed the bounds of morality — whether open or hidden, and sin (الإثم)...’" (7:33), and this verse under discussion: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ — "O you who have faith! Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and the divining arrows are abomination (رجس) — an inherently loathsome and impure thing — of Satan’s doing, so avoid it... [until His saying] ...Will you then desist?"—when joined together, their various contexts indicate the Lawgiver's gradual approach (تدرج) in prohibiting wine.

However, this does not mean a gradual progression in its prohibition from mere refinement and distaste to dislike and then to explicit prohibition such that it results in abrogation (النسخ). Nor does it mean moving from ambiguity in expression to clarity, or from subtle metaphor to explicit statement for the sake of religious policy in implementing legal rulings. For His saying, the Exalted: وَالْإِثْمَ (and sin) is a Meccan verse in Surah al-A'raf; when joined with His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ (Say, in both of them is a great sin), which is a Medinan verse located in Surah al-Baqarah—the first detailed Surah revealed after the Migration (الهجرة)—it yields the prohibition of wine in an explicit manner that leaves no excuse for the excuse-seeker, nor any room for the interpreter (المتأول).

Rather, it means that the verses progressed in forbidding it through prohibition in a general sense, which is His saying: وَالْإِثْمَ (and sin); then through a specific prohibition in the form of advice, which is His saying: قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُما أَكْبَرُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِما (Say, in both of them is a great sin and benefits for people, but their sin is greater than their benefit); and His saying: لا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلاةَ وَأَنْتُمْ سُكارى حَتَّى تَعْلَمُوا ما تَقُولُونَ (Do not approach prayer while you are intoxicated until you know what you are saying), if the verse refers to intoxication from wine and not the "intoxication" of sleep. Finally, it concluded with a specific prohibition accompanied by extreme emphasis, as indicated by His saying: إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنْصابُ وَالْأَزْلامُ رِجْسٌ — "Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and the divining arrows are abomination..." until the end of the two verses.

These verses are the last to be revealed regarding the prohibition of wine. This is evidenced by the various types of emphasis embedded within them: the use of the restrictive particle "Indeed" (إِنَّما), labeling it as an abomination (رجس), attributing it to the work of Satan, the explicit command to avoid it, the expectation of success (الفلاح) therein, the explanation of the corruptions (المفاسد) resulting from its consumption, the rhetorical question regarding desisting, then the command to obey Allah and the Messenger, the warning against opposition, and the declaration of His independence from them should they disobey.

This is also partially indicated by His saying at the end of the verses: لَيْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ — "There is no sin upon those who have faith and do righteous deeds..." as will be clarified later.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ (O you who have faith! Indeed wine and gambling...) to the end of the verse. Discussion has already preceded at the beginning of the Surah regarding the meaning of wine (الخمر), gambling (الميسر), sacrificial altars (الأنصاب), and divining arrows (الأزلام). Wine (الخمر) is that which "veils" (يخمر) the intellect, referring to every intoxicating liquid produced through fermentation (التخمير). Gambling (الميسر) refers to games of chance in general. Sacrificial altars (الأنصاب) are the idols or stones that were set up to slaughter sacrifices upon, which were honored and sought for blessings. Divining arrows (الأزلام) were the vessels used for seeking omens; sometimes the term was applied to the arrows used for seeking good fortune when starting matters or making resolutions, such as departing for a journey and the like. However, the word occurred at the beginning of the Surah in the first sense because it appeared among the prohibitions of eating, which supports the view that what is intended here is that same meaning.

If you say: Gambling (الميسر) in its generality includes divining arrows (الأزلام) in the other sense—which is seeking portions through arrows—and there is no reason to mention the specific after the general without a clear rhetorical point (نكتة). Thus, it is necessary to interpret the word as the arrows of seeking omens and choice (الخيرة) whose practice was well-known among them in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). A poet said:

If Jadhimah has killed its masters, Then its women are striking with the divining arrows (الأزلام).

This is as it is narrated: they used to take three thin pieces of wood like arrows; on one was written "Do it," on the second "Do not do it," and the third was blank (غفل) with no writing. The striker would place them in a pouch he had, and they were identical. When he wanted to embark on a matter of concern, like a journey or otherwise, he would pull one out. If the one with "Do it" came out, he resolved upon it; if "Do not do it" came out, he abandoned it; and if the third came out, he repeated the strike until one of the first two emerged. It was called "seeking a portion" (استقسام) because it involved seeking what was apportioned (قسم) for him of sustenance or other types of good.

Thus, the verse indicates its prohibition because it involves an attempt to claim knowledge of the Unseen (الغيب), and likewise everything that resembles it of actions, such as taking an omen (الخيرة) by prayer beads and the like.

I say: You have already known that the verse at the beginning of the Surah: وَأَنْ تَسْتَقْسِمُوا بِالْأَزْلامِ — "and to seek omens by divining arrows" (5:3) is apparent in referring to seeking portions by arrows, which is a type of gambling, because it occurs within the context of prohibited foods. This supports the view that this is what is intended by divining arrows (الأزلام) in this verse.

Even if we concede that this verse is not supported by the other, the word would revert to being a homonym (مشترك) with no contextual clue (قرينة) in the speech to clarify the intent; thus, it would depend on what the Sunnah (السنة) — the prophetic tradition and practice — explains. Numerous reports have been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) regarding the permissibility of taking an omen (الخيرة) through prayer beads and other means when in a state of hesitation (الحيرة).

Its reality is that when a person wishes to embark on a matter, he should first identify the point of benefit (المصلحة) therein through the gift of thought (الفكر) that Allah has instilled in him, or by consulting (الاستشارة) those qualified to know right from wrong. If that does not lead him to know the correct path and he remains wavering in confusion, he may determine what he ought to choose through a type of turning toward his Lord.

There is nothing in choosing what a person chooses through this type of seeking guidance (الاستخارة) that constitutes a claim to knowledge of the Unseen, nor an encroachment upon what is exclusive to Allah, the Glorified, of the affairs of divinity (الألوهية). Nor is it polytheism (شرك) by way of associating others with Allah in the management of affairs, nor any other religious prohibition. For this act has no function other than determining action or omission without necessitating obligation (إيجاب), prohibition (تحريم), or any other legal ruling (حكم تكليفي). Nor does it uncover what lies behind the veils of the Unseen of good or evil, except that the "good" for the one seeking guidance is that he acts or refrains so as to exit from confusion and vacillation.

As for the events that follow the action or omission, they may contain good or evil just as if he had acted or refrained based on thought or consultation. Thus, it is like thinking and consulting—a path to end confusion and hesitation in the station of action. The consequences that follow the action in accordance with it are the same as those that would follow if he had done it based on thought or advice.

Yes, it might be possible for a person to imagine an encroachment upon the claim of knowing the Unseen in what has been narrated regarding seeking omens (التفؤل) by the Quran and the like, as the soul might speak to him of good or bad fortune, expecting benefit or harm. However, it has been narrated in authentic reports (الصحيح) from the paths of both sects (Sunni and Shia) that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to take good omens (يتفاءل بالخير) and commanded it, while he forbade taking evil omens (التطير) and commanded proceeding despite them while relying upon Allah, the Exalted.

Thus, there is no barrier to taking omens by the Book and the like; if it brings a good omen, then well and good, otherwise one proceeds in the matter relying upon Allah. There is nothing in this more than what a person uses to comfort his soul in matters and actions wherein he senses success and benefit. We shall complete the discussion related to this station in a discourse dedicated specifically to this purpose.

It has thus become clear that what has occurred in some commentaries—interpreting divining arrows (الأزلام) as the arrows of seeking omens and concluding the prohibition of seeking guidance (الاستخارة) thereby—is something that should not be adopted.

As for His saying: رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ (an abomination of Satan's doing), abomination (الرجس) is a filthy thing (القذر) as mentioned by al-Raghib in his Mufradat. Abomination (الرجاسة) is like impurity (النجاسة) and filth (القذارة); it is the quality from which one distances and purifies oneself because the natural disposition (الطبع) recoils from it.

The fact that these listed items—wine, gambling, idols, and divining arrows—are an abomination (رجس) means they possess a quality because of which the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — does not permit approaching them. This is only because they do not contain anything of human felicity (سعادة) at all—a felicity that could ever be pure and unadulterated. This is perhaps alluded to by His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُما أَكْبَرُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِما (2:219), where sin (الإثم) outweighed benefit and He made no exception.

Perhaps for this reason, He attributed these abominations to the work of Satan and did not associate anyone else with him. Then He said in the following verse: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَداوَةَ وَالْبَغْضاءَ فِي الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَعَنِ الصَّلاةِ — "Satan only desires to cause enmity and hatred to spring up among you through wine and gambling, and to bar you from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer."

This is because Allah, the Glorified, has identified Satan in His speech as an enemy to man who desires no good for him whatsoever. He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ لِلْإِنْسانِ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ — "Satan is indeed man’s manifest enemy" (12:5). And He said: كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ مَنْ تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ — "about whom it has been decreed that whoever takes him for a friend, he will lead him astray" (22:4). And He said: وَإِنْ يَدْعُونَ إِلَّا شَيْطاناً مَرِيداً ، لَعَنَهُ اللهُ — "and they invoke none but a froward Satan, whom Allah has cursed" (4:118), thus establishing His curse upon him and his expulsion from every good.

He mentioned that his contact with man and his work upon him is only through enticement (التسويل), whispering (الوسوسة), and seduction (الإغواء) by way of casting into the heart, as He, the Exalted, said, narrating from him: قالَ رَبِّ بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ قالَ هذا صِراطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ ، إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ — "He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them all, except Your exclusive servants among them.’ He said, ‘This is a straight path [leading] to Me. As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you’" (15:39-42). Thus, Iblis threatened them with seduction only, and Allah, the Glorified, denied his authority (سلطان) except over his perverse followers. He also narrated from him in his address to the Children of Adam on the Day of Resurrection: وَما كانَ لِي عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا أَنْ دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي — "I had no authority over you, except that I called you and you responded to me" (14:22). Regarding the nature of his call, He said: يا بَنِي آدَمَ لا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ — "O Children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you..." until He said: إِنَّهُ يَراكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا تَرَوْنَهُمْ — "Indeed he and his hosts see you from whence you do not see them" (7:27). Thus, He explained that his call is not like the call of one human to another through face-to-face speech, but rather in a way where the caller observes the called without the reverse.

All of this is detailed in His saying, the Exalted: مِنْ شَرِّ الْوَسْواسِ الْخَنَّاسِ ، الَّذِي يُوَسْوِسُ فِي صُدُورِ النَّاسِ — "from the evil of the sneaking whisperer, who whispers in the breasts of mankind" (114:4-5). He explained that what Satan does by influencing man is to cast whispering (الوسوسة) into his heart, thereby calling him to misguidance (الضلال).

It becomes clear from all this that wine and what is mentioned after it being an abomination (رجس) of Satan's doing means they lead to the specific work of Satan. There is no motive for them except the satanic casting and whispering that calls to misguidance. For this reason, He called them an abomination (رجس), and Allah, the Glorified, has called misguidance (الضلال) an abomination in His saying: وَمَنْ يُرِدْ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً كَأَنَّما يَصَّعَّدُ فِي السَّماءِ كَذلِكَ يَجْعَلُ اللهُ الرِّجْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ ، وَهذا صِراطُ رَبِّكَ مُسْتَقِيماً — "and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow and straitened as if he were climbing into the sky. Thus does Allah lay abomination (الرجس) on those who do not have faith. This is the straight path of your Lord" (6:125-126).

Then He explained the meaning of them being an abomination arising from Satan's work by His saying in the following verse: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَداوَةَ وَالْبَغْضاءَ فِي الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَعَنِ الصَّلاةِ — meaning that He desires nothing for you in calling toward them except evil; therefore, they were an abomination of his doing.

If you say: The summary of this explanation is that the meaning of wine and its likes being an abomination is that their practice or drinking, for example, leads only to Satan's whispering and misguidance. However, several narrations (روايات) indicate that Satan is the one who appeared to man, produced it for the first time, and taught it to him.

I say: Yes, and while these reports do not exceed the level of solitary narrations (الآحاد) such that they must be accepted, there are many diverse reports in various chapters indicating Satan's manifestation (تمثل) to prophets, saints, and some other individuals. This is similar to other reports narrating the manifestation of angels, and others indicating the manifestation of the world (الدنيا), deeds, and so on. The Divine Book supports this to some extent, such as His saying, the Exalted: فَأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَها بَشَراً سَوِيًّا — "Then We sent to her Our Spirit, and he embodied (فتمثل) himself for her as a flawless human" (19:17). We shall complete this discussion, Allah willing, in the commentary on Surah al-Isra regarding His saying: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى بِعَبْدِهِ (17:1), or in another suitable place.

What must be known is that the occurrence of a story in a report or reports does not necessitate changing the apparent meaning (الظهور) of a verse that is supported by other verses. Satan has no influence over man except through intellectual manipulation (التصرف الفكري) where he is able to do so according to the noble verses. Even if he manifested to a human and did something or taught him something, that would not exceed manifestation and manipulation of his thought or contact through knowledge—so wait for the discussion that will reach you.

As for His saying, the Exalted: فَاجْتَنِبُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ — "so avoid it, so that you may be successful"—it is an explicit prohibition after explaining the corruption (المفسدة) so that it may be more impactful on the souls, followed by the hope for success (الفلاح) on the condition of avoidance. In this is the strongest emphasis of the prohibition, establishing that there is no hope for the success of one who does not avoid these abominations.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَداوَةَ وَالْبَغْضاءَ فِي الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ (Satan only desires to cause enmity and hatred to spring up among you through wine and gambling...) to the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: Enmity (العدو) is transgression and the negation of harmony. Sometimes it is considered in the heart, and it is called enmity (العداوة) and hostility (المعاداة). Sometimes it is in walking, and it is called running (العدو). Sometimes it is in the violation of justice in dealings, and it is called aggression (العدوان) and enmity (العدو); He said: فَيَسُبُّوا اللهَ عَدْواً بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ — "lest they should abuse Allah out of hostility (عدواً) without any knowledge" (6:108). Sometimes it is in the parts of a place, and it is called al-adwa' (العدواء); it is said: "a place of adwa'" meaning its parts are not harmonious. From hostility (المعاداة) it is said: "a man who is an enemy (عدو)" and "a people who are enemies (عدو)"; He said: بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ — "some of you being enemies of others" (2:36). It may be pluralized as ida (عدى) and a'da' (أعداء); He said: وَيَوْمَ يُحْشَرُ أَعْداءُ اللهِ — "On the day when the enemies of Allah are mustered" (41:19). [End of quote].

Hatred (البغض) and loathing (البغضاء) are the opposite of love. Barring (الصد) is turning away. Desisting (الانتهاء) is accepting the prohibition and is the opposite of beginning.

Furthermore, the verse—as previously mentioned—is intended as an explanation for His saying: مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ (of Satan's doing) or for His saying: رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ (an abomination of Satan's doing). That is, the reality of these matters being from Satan's work or an abomination of his work is that Satan has no aim or goal in wine and gambling—which were said to be abominations of his work only—except to cause enmity and hatred to spring up among you by transgressing your bounds and making you hate one another, and to turn you away from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer in all these matters, namely wine, gambling, idols, and divining arrows.

The restriction of causing enmity and hatred to wine and gambling is because these are among their manifest effects. As for wine, its consumption agitates the nervous system in a way that "veils" (يخمر) the intellect and brings out nervous emotions. If it falls in the path of anger, it permits the intoxicated person to commit any crime imaginable, no matter how great or how hideous, even those that predatory beasts would not permit themselves. If it falls in the path of lust and animality, it glamorizes for man any obscenity or profligacy regarding his soul, wealth, or honor, and everything he respects and sanctifies of religious laws and social boundaries, such as theft, betrayal, violating the sacred, revealing secrets, or entering into what leads to human destruction. Statistics have shown that wine has the largest share in the types of crimes occurring and in the categories of hideous profligacy in societies where its consumption is prevalent.

As for gambling (الميسر), it nullifies in the shortest time the human efforts spent in acquiring wealth, riches, and prestige over long periods. Thus, wealth vanishes, and often honor, life, and status follow. If he gambles and wins, acquiring wealth, it leads him to abandon a moderate course in life and to expand into idle play and profligacy, and to laziness and sluggishness in engaging in earning and acquiring the necessities of life through legitimate means. If he is the loser, the loss of wealth and the failure of effort lead him to enmity and hatred toward the winning gambler, and to regret and resentment.

These corruptions, though they may not appear to simple and naive minds with such clarity in rare and few instances, or once or twice, yet the rare calls to the common, the few leads to the many, and the single instance drags to multiple instances. It does not take long, if not prevented from the root, to spread among the public and permeate society, turning into a barbaric affliction where no government exists except for tyrannical emotions and destructive whims.

It has thus become clear from all the preceding that the restriction in His saying: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَداوَةَ وَالْبَغْضاءَ فِي الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَعَنِ الصَّلاةِ refers to the sum of the listed items as a whole, except that barring from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer is the nature of all of them, while enmity and hatred are specific to wine and gambling by nature.

The separation of prayer from remembrance in His saying, the Exalted: وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَعَنِ الصَّلاةِ (and to bar you from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer), despite prayer being a type of remembrance, indicates an increased importance (اهتمام) given to it, as it is a perfect instance of remembrance. It has been authentically narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: "Prayer is the pillar of religion." The indication of the Noble Quran in very many verses regarding the importance of prayer is beyond doubt, including His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ ، الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي صَلاتِهِمْ خاشِعُونَ — "Certainly, the faithful have attained salvation—those who are humble in their prayers" (23:1-2); and His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يُمَسِّكُونَ بِالْكِتابِ وَأقامُوا الصَّلاةَ إِنَّا لا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُصْلِحِينَ — "As for those who hold fast to the Book and maintain the prayer—indeed, We do not waste the reward of those who bring about reform" (7:170); and His saying: إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعاً ، إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ جَزُوعاً ، وَإِذا مَسَّهُ الْخَيْرُ مَنُوعاً ، إِلَّا الْمُصَلِّينَ — "Indeed man was created greedy: impatient when ill befalls him, and grudging when good befalls him, except the prayerful" (70:19-22); and His saying: اتْلُ ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ تَنْهى عَنِ الْفَحْشاءِ وَالْمُنْكَرِ وَلَذِكْرُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ — "Recite what has been revealed to you of the Book, and maintain the prayer. Indeed the prayer prevents indecencies and wrongs, and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater" (24:45). And He, the Exalted, said: فَاسْعَوْا إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ — "hasten to the remembrance of Allah" (62:9), meaning the prayer. And He said: وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي — "and maintain the prayer for My remembrance" (20:14), among other verses.

The Glorified mentioned His remembrance first and placed it before prayer because it is the sole aim of the divine call, the living spirit in the body of servitude (العبودية), and the leaven for the felicity of this world and the Hereafter. This is indicated by His saying, the Exalted, to Adam on the first day religion was legislated: قالَ اهْبِطا مِنْها جَمِيعاً بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ مِنِّي هُدىً فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدايَ فَلا يَضِلُّ وَلا يَشْقى ، وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً ، وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَعْمى — "He said, ‘Get down both of you from it, all together, being enemies of one another! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance will not go astray, nor will they be miserable. But whoever turns away from My remembrance, his indeed shall be a wretched life, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection’" (20:123-124). And His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ وَما يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ فَيَقُولُ أَأَنْتُمْ أَضْلَلْتُمْ عِبادِي هؤُلاءِ أَمْ هُمْ ضَلُّوا السَّبِيلَ ، قالُوا سُبْحانَكَ ما كانَ يَنْبَغِي لَنا أَنْ نَتَّخِذَ مِنْ دُونِكَ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ وَلكِنْ مَتَّعْتَهُمْ وَآباءَهُمْ حَتَّى نَسُوا الذِّكْرَ وَكانُوا قَوْماً بُوراً — "On the day He musters them and what they worship besides Allah, He will say, ‘Was it you who led these servants of Mine astray, or did they themselves stray from the way?’ They will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It did not behoove us to take any guardians besides You. But You let them and their fathers enjoy until they forgot the Remembrance and were a ruined people’" (21:17-18). And His saying, the Exalted: فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ — "So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge" (53:29-30).

Remembrance (الذكر) in the verses is that which stands in contrast to forgetting the aspect of Lordship (الربوبية), which entails forgetting servitude (العبودية). It is the religious conduct (السلوك الديني) without which there is no way to the soul's felicity. He, the Exalted, said: وَلا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللهَ فَأَنْساهُمْ أَنْفُسَهُمْ — "And do not be like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget their own souls" (59:19).

As for His saying, the Exalted: فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُنْتَهُونَ — "Will you then desist?"—it is a reproachful question (استفهام توبيخي) containing some indication that the Muslims were not desisting from the prohibitions prior to this forbidding. The verse—meaning His saying: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُوقِعَ (Satan only desires to cause...)—is like a commentary (تفسير) explaining His saying: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُما أَكْبَرُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِما (2:219). That is, the benefit assumed in them along with the sin is not such that it can sometimes be separated from the sin or from the sin that outweighs it, like lying, which contains sin and benefit, and sometimes its benefit is separated from its sin, such as lying for the sake of reconciling between people.

This is due to the restriction in His saying: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَداوَةَ وَالْبَغْضاءَ (Satan only desires to cause enmity and hatred...) after His saying: رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ (an abomination of Satan's doing). The meaning is that they only occur as an abomination of Satan's work, and that Satan desires nothing through them except to cause enmity and hatred among you in wine and gambling and to bar you from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer. Thus, no instance is found where the benefit is pure from the sin such that it would be permitted therein—so understand that.

His saying, the Exalted: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَاحْذَرُوا (Obey Allah and obey the Messenger, and beware...) to the end of the verse. This is an emphasis of the previous command to avoid these abominations: first, by the command to obey Allah, the Glorified, in Whose hand is the matter of legislation (التشريع); second, by the command to obey the Messenger, to whom belongs the implementation (الإجراء); and third, by explicit warning (التحذير).

Then, in His saying: فَإِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما عَلى رَسُولِنَا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ — "But if you turn your backs, then know that Our Messenger’s duty is only to deliver the manifest message"—is an emphasis containing the meaning of a threat. This is especially so because it includes His saying: فَاعْلَمُوا (then know), for it contains an allusion that if you turn away and commit these sins, it is as if you thought you had contended with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his prohibition and overcome him. You have been ignorant or have forgotten that he is a Messenger from Us, having no part in the matter except the manifest delivery (البلاغ المبين) of what is revealed to him and what he is commanded to deliver. You have only contended with your Lord in His Lordship.

It has been mentioned at the beginning of the discourse that the verses contain various arts of emphasis in prohibiting these matters: starting with the address يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (O you who have faith!), then using the restrictive particle (إِنَّما), then describing them as abomination (الرجس), then attributing them to Satan's work, then the explicit command to avoid them, then the hope for success in avoidance, then mentioning their general corruptions of enmity, hatred, and turning away from the remembrance of Allah and prayer, then the reproach for their lack of desisting, then the command to obey Allah and the Messenger and the warning against opposition, then the threat in the event of turning away after the manifest delivery.

His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جُناحٌ فِيما طَعِمُوا — "There is no sin upon those who have faith and do righteous deeds in regard to what they have tasted..." to the end of the verse. Al-ta'm (الطعم) and al-ta'am (الطعام) refer to nourishment. It is used for what is eaten rather than what is drunk, and in the dialect of the people of Medina, it refers specifically to wheat. Sometimes it comes with the meaning of tasting (الذوق), and in that case, it is used for drinking just as it is used for eating. He, the Exalted, said: فَمَنْ شَرِبَ مِنْهُ فَلَيْسَ مِنِّي وَمَنْ لَمْ يَطْعَمْهُ فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي — "Whoever drinks from it does not belong to me, and whoever does not taste (يطعمه) it does indeed belong to me" (2:249). In some narrations from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), he said regarding the water of Zamzam: "It is a food that nourishes (طعام طعم) and a cure for illness."

The verse, by its context, is only suitable to be connected to the preceding verses, serving as a dispelling of a potential objection (دفع دخل) addressing the state of those believers who were afflicted with drinking wine before the revelation of the prohibition or before the revelation of these verses. This is because His saying therein: فِيما طَعِمُوا (in what they have tasted) is absolute, not restricted by anything that could qualify it. The verse is intended to lift the restriction from this absolute "tasting." The lifting of the restriction was qualified by His saying: إِذا مَا اتَّقَوْا وَآمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَآمَنُوا ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَأَحْسَنُوا — "provided they are God-conscious and have faith and do righteous deeds, then are God-conscious and have faith, then are God-conscious and do good." What is certain from the meaning of this condition—wherein God-consciousness (التقوى) is mentioned three times—is the intense God-consciousness which is the "truth of God-consciousness" (حق التقوى).

The negation of sin for the God-conscious believers regarding whatever they tasted (lawful food)—if it were for the purpose of establishing the opposite implication (المفهوم) for others, i.e., establishing an absolute prohibition for those other than the people of God-consciousness among the rest of the believers and disbelievers—would be contradicted by verses such as His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ قُلْ هِيَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا خالِصَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ — "Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’ Say, ‘These are for the faithful in the life of this world, and exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection’" (7:32). Furthermore, it is known from the spirit of this religion that it does not bar anyone from the lawful good things (الطيبات المحللة) which the primordial nature (الفطرة) is compelled to permit in life.

If the verse were not intended to prohibit it for those other than the ones mentioned, the meaning would revert to something like our saying: Food is permissible for those who have faith and do righteous deeds on the condition that they are God-conscious, then God-conscious, then God-conscious. It is well known that permissibility is not exclusive to those who have faith and do righteous deeds, but rather includes them and others. Even assuming its exclusivity to them, this severe condition would not be required.

None of what they have mentioned in justifying the verse is free from one of these two problems, based on interpreting His saying: فِيما طَعِمُوا as absolute lawful food. For the meaning they mentioned does not exceed the bounds of our saying: There is no sin upon those who have faith and do righteous deeds, if they avoid the prohibited, to eat the lawful. This meaning is not safe from one of the two problems, as is clear.

Some mentioned that there is an omission (حذف) in the verse, and the estimation is: "There is no sin upon those who have faith and do righteous deeds in what they have tasted and other things, provided they avoid the prohibited." This is an estimation without evidence, while the problem remains as it is.

Some mentioned that both faith and righteous deeds are not a real condition; rather, the intent is to explain the obligation of avoiding the prohibited, so faith and righteous deeds were associated with it to indicate its obligation. The problem is that the apparent meaning of the verse is that it is intended to negate sin in what they have tasted, and it has no condition of faith, righteous deeds, or avoiding the prohibited as previously mentioned. How far the meaning he mentioned is from the apparent sense of the verse!

Some mentioned that it is correct to say of a believer that there is no sin upon him, while the disbeliever deserves punishment, so it is not correct to apply this expression to him. The problem is that it is not correct to specify the believers for mention; let it be like His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ (7:32), and His saying: قُلْ لا أَجِدُ فِي ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ مُحَرَّماً عَلى طاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُ إِلَّا أَنْ يَكُونَ مَيْتَةً أَوْ دَماً مَسْفُوحاً — "Say, ‘I do not find in what has been revealed to me anything forbidden to an eater who eats it, unless it be carrion, or spilt blood...’" (6:145), where neither believer nor disbeliever is mentioned in the address. Or like His saying: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى — "O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female..." until His saying: إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ — "Indeed the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you" (49:13), where the address is directed to mankind, including both believer and disbeliever.

Some mentioned that the disbeliever has blocked for himself the path of knowing the prohibited and the lawful, so the believer was specified for mention. This contains the same problem as the previous one, and it does not remove the problem arising from His saying: إِذا مَا اتَّقَوْا (provided they are God-conscious), etc.

What ought to be said is: The verse is in the same sense as the verses preceding it, as is apparent from its connection to them. it addresses the state of those Muslims who were afflicted with drinking wine and tasting it, or with tasting something of it, or what they acquired through gambling, or from the sacrifice of the idols. It is as if they asked, after the revelation of the explicit prohibition, about the state of those who were afflicted with drinking wine, or with it and other things Allah mentioned in the verse before the revelation of the prohibition—whether of their brothers who had passed away or those remaining who were submissive to Allah, the Glorified, in His ruling.

So their question was answered: there is no sin upon them if they were among those who had faith and did righteous deeds, provided they were proceeding on the path of God-consciousness (التقوى) through faith in Allah and righteous deeds, then faith in every ruling revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), then excellence (الإحسان) by acting according to the revealed ruling.

By this, it becomes clear that what is intended by the relative pronoun in His saying: فِيما طَعِمُوا (in what they have tasted) is wine in terms of its drinking, or all that was mentioned of wine, gambling, idols, and divining arrows in terms of what can correctly be related to the meaning of "tasting." The meaning is: There is no sin upon those who have faith and do righteous deeds in what they tasted before the revelation of the prohibition—whether of wine or of it and other mentioned prohibitions.

As for His saying: إِذا مَا اتَّقَوْا وَآمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَآمَنُوا ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَأَحْسَنُوا, the apparent sense of His saying: إِذا مَا اتَّقَوْا وَآمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ is that it is a repetition of the same subject mentioned in His saying: لَيْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جُناحٌ to indicate the involvement of the description in the ruling, which is the negation of sin. This is like His saying, the Exalted, in addressing the believers: ذلِكَ يُوعَظُ بِهِ مَنْ كانَ مِنْكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ — "This is an advice for those of you who believe in Allah and the Last Day" (2:232), and this is common in speech.

The apparent sense of His saying: ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَآمَنُوا is the consideration of faith after faith. This is nothing other than detailed faith (الإيمان التفصيلي) in every single ruling brought by the Messenger from his Lord without rejection or refusal. Its necessity is submission (التسليم) to the Messenger in what he commands and forbids. He, the Exalted, said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَآمَنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ — "O you who have faith! Be God-conscious of Allah and believe in His Messenger" (57:28). And He, the Exalted, said: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ — "We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave..." until He said: فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً مِمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً — "But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their hearts any dissent to your verdict and submit in full submission" (4:64-65). The verses in this sense are many.

The apparent sense of His saying: ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَأَحْسَنُوا is the addition of excellence (الإحسان) to faith after faith in consideration. Excellence (الإحسان) is performing the deed in a beautiful manner without any corrupt intention, as He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ إِنَّا لا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ مَنْ أَحْسَنَ عَمَلاً — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds—indeed We do not waste the reward of those who are excellent in conduct" (18:30). And He said: الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَصابَهُمُ الْقَرْحُ لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا مِنْهُمْ وَاتَّقَوْا أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ — "Those who responded to Allah and the Messenger after they had been wounded—for those of them who were excellent and God-conscious there shall be a great reward" (3:172). That is, their response was seeking the face of Allah and submission to His command, not for any other purpose. Among the types of excellence is that which extends to others, which is to bring to another what he deems good; He, the Exalted, said: وَبِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً — "and [show] kindness to parents" (2:83), and He said: وَأَحْسِنْ كَما أَحْسَنَ اللهُ إِلَيْكَ — "and do good as Allah has done good to you" (28:77).

What is suitable for the context of the verse is the first of the two meanings of excellence, which is performing the act in a beautiful manner. For religious God-consciousness (التقوى الديني) is not fulfilled in its right by mere faith in Allah and attesting to the truth of His religion as long as one does not believe in detail in every single one of the legislated rulings in the religion. For the rejection of one of them is a rejection of the foundation of the religion. Nor is detailed faith in every single one sufficient to fulfill the right of God-consciousness as long as one does not excel in acting upon them and within acting upon them—by proceeding according to what the ruling requires of action or omission, and that this proceeding arises from obedience and following, not from a hypocritical intention. Thus, it is mandatory for the one provisioned with the provision of God-consciousness to believe in Allah and do righteous deeds, to believe in His Messenger in all that he brought, and to proceed in all of that upon the path of following and excellence.

As for the repetition of God-consciousness (التقوى) three times, and qualifying all three levels with it, it is to emphasize the indication of the necessity of accompanying all levels with real God-consciousness without any other non-religious purpose. It has been mentioned in some previous discussions that God-consciousness is not a specific religious station, but rather a spiritual state that accompanies all spiritual stations; that is, for every spiritual station, there is a specific God-consciousness that pertains to it.

The summary of all that has passed is that the intent of the verse—meaning His saying: لَيْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جُناحٌ فِيما طَعِمُوا to the end of the verse—is that there is no sin upon those who have faith and do righteous deeds in what they tasted of wine or other listed prohibitions, on the condition that they are constant in God-consciousness in all their states, clothed in faith in Allah and His Messenger, and excellent in their deeds, acting upon the obligations and abandoning every prohibition they were forbidden from. If it happened that they were afflicted with something of the abomination (الرجس) which is of Satan's doing before the revelation of the prohibition, or before it reached them, or before they gained understanding of it, that would not harm them at all.

This is similar to His saying, the Exalted, in the verses regarding the change of the direction of prayer (القبلة) in response to their question about the state of the prayers they had performed toward other than the Kaaba: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمانَكُمْ — "And Allah would not let your faith go to waste" (2:143).

The context of this speech is another witness that this verse: لَيْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جُناحٌ etc., is connected to the verses before it and that it was revealed along with those verses whose tone bears witness that they are the last of the verses prohibiting wine to be revealed. It also indicates that some Muslims—as sensed from the tone of the verses, as we inferred just now—were not desisting from drinking it between the previous prohibiting verses and these verses.

Then the question arose after the revelation of these verses about the state of those who were afflicted with that, among whom were those afflicted before the revelation of the prohibition, those afflicted before gaining understanding, and those afflicted without excuse. They were answered with that which determines for each group the ruling of its case according to its specific state. So, whoever tasted it while in a state of faith and excellence—and this can only be one of the believers who tasted it before the revelation of the prohibition or out of ignorance of it—then there is no sin upon him. And whoever tasted it without this description, his ruling is other than this ruling.

Commentators have long discussions regarding the verse, some of which relate to His saying: فِيما طَعِمُوا (in what they have tasted), and the summary of the discourse on that has preceded.

Some relate to the end of the verse in terms of the repetition of God-consciousness (التقوى) three times, the repetition of faith, the repetition of righteous deeds, and its conclusion with excellence (الإحسان).

It was said: The intent of His saying: إِذا مَا اتَّقَوْا وَآمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ is that they avoided the prohibited and remained firm in faith and righteous deeds. By His saying: ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَآمَنُوا, then they avoided what was prohibited to them afterward, like wine, and believed in its prohibition. By His saying: ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَأَحْسَنُوا, then they continued and remained firm in avoiding sins and occupied themselves with beautiful deeds.

It was said: This repetition is in consideration of the three states: a person's use of God-consciousness and faith between himself and his soul, between himself and the people, and between himself and Allah, the Exalted. Excellence (الإحسان) according to this is excellence toward people outwardly.

It was said: The repetition is in consideration of the three levels: the beginning, the middle, and the end, which is the "truth of God-consciousness" (حق التقوى).

It was said: The repetition is in consideration of what is avoided; for it is necessary to abandon prohibited things to guard against punishment, doubtful things (الشبهات) to beware of falling into the prohibited, and some permissible things (المباحات) to preserve the soul from meanness and to purify it from the dross of nature.

It was said: The first God-consciousness is avoiding the drinking of wine and the first faith is faith in Allah. The second God-consciousness is the continuation of the first and the second faith is the continuation of the first. The third God-consciousness is performing the obligatory duties (الفرائض), and excellence (الإحسان) is performing the recommended acts (النوافل).

It was said: The first God-consciousness is avoiding intellectual sins, and the first faith is faith in Allah and the ugliness of these sins. The second God-consciousness is avoiding sins known through revelation (السمعية) and the second faith is faith in the obligation of avoiding these sins. The third God-consciousness is specific to the injustices against servants (مظالم العباد) and what relates to others of injustice and corruption, and the intent of excellence is excellence toward people.

It was said: The first condition is specific to the past, the second condition to the permanence and continuation of that, and the third condition is specific to the injustices against servants—among other sayings of theirs.

All that they have mentioned is without evidence from the wording of the verse or otherwise that would necessitate interpreting the verse accordingly. This is clear upon reflecting on the context of the discourse therein and referring to what we have presented.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Hisham ibn Salim from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: I heard him saying: "While Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib and companions of his were over a drink of theirs—called al-sukarkah—he said: They mentioned the noble one (the camel). Hamzah said to them: 'How can we get it?' They said: 'This is the she-camel of your nephew Ali.' So he went out to it and slaughtered it, then took its liver and its hump and brought them in to them." He said: "Ali (peace be upon him) approached and saw his she-camel, and he was distressed by that. They said to him: 'Your uncle Hamzah did this.' He said: So he went to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and complained of that to him."

He said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) approached with him. It was said to Hamzah: 'This is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) at the door.' He said: Hamzah came out while he was angry. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw the anger in his face, he turned away." He said: "Hamzah said to him: 'If the son of Abu Talib wanted to lead you by the rein, he would have done so.' So Hamzah entered his house and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) departed."

He said: "And it was before Uhud." He said: "Then Allah revealed the prohibition of wine. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) ordered their vessels to be overturned." He said: "Then the people were called to go out to Uhud. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out, and the people went out, and Hamzah went out. He stood to one side of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)." He said: "When they faced each other, he charged into the people until he was hidden among them, then he returned to his position. The people said to him: 'Allah! Allah! O uncle of the Messenger of Allah, do not go while there is something in the soul of the Messenger of Allah against you.' He said: Then he charged a second time until he was hidden among the people, then he returned to his position. They said to him: 'Allah! Allah! O uncle of the Messenger of Allah, do not go while there is something in the soul of the Messenger of Allah against you.'"

"So he approached the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). When he saw him approaching, he came toward him and embraced him, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) kissed him between his eyes. Then he said: 'Charge against the people.' So Hamzah was martyred, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) shrouded him in a namirah (a small striped garment)."

Then Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Something like this siryani (garment) of mine; when his face was covered, his feet were exposed, and when his feet were covered, his face was exposed." He said: "So he covered his face with it and placed idhkhar (lemon grass) over his feet."

He said: "The people were defeated and Ali (peace be upon him) remained. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to him: 'What did you do?' He said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I stuck to the ground.' He said: 'That was the expectation of you.'" He said: "And the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'O Lord, fulfill for me what You promised me, for if You will, You shall not be worshipped.'"

From al-Zamakhshari in Rabi' al-Abrar, he said: "Three verses were revealed regarding wine: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ —so the Muslims were between drinking and abstaining—until a man drank it and entered his prayer and spoke nonsense. Then was revealed: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلاةَ وَأَنْتُمْ سُكارى. So those of the Muslims who drank it continued to do so until Umar drank it. He took the jawbone of a camel and struck the head of Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, wounding him. Then he sat wailing over the slain of Badr with the poetry of al-Aswad ibn Yaghfur:

How many in the well, the well of Badr, Of the noble ones and the honorable drinkers. How many in the well, the well of Badr, Of the perfect masters with the hump. Does the son of Kabshah promise us that we shall live? And how is the life of echoes and skulls? Is he unable to ward off death from me, And will he resurrect me when my bones have decayed? Lo, who will convey to the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) from me, That I am abandoning the month of fasting? So say to Allah: prevent me from my drink, And say to Allah: prevent me from my food.

That reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), so he came out angry, dragging his cloak. He raised something that was in his hand to strike him. He said: 'I seek refuge in Allah from the anger of Allah and the anger of His Messenger.' Then Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, revealed: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ until His saying: فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُنْتَهُونَ. So Umar said: 'We have desisted.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Nahhas in his Nasikh narrated from Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, who said: "Regarding the revelation of the prohibition of wine: a man from the Ansar prepared food and invited us. Some people came to him, ate, and drank until they became intoxicated from the wine—and that was before wine was prohibited. They began to boast. The Ansar said: 'The Ansar are better,' and Quraysh said: 'Quraysh are better.' A man reached for the jawbone of a camel and struck my nose, splitting it—so Sa'd had a split nose. He said: I went to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and mentioned that to him. Then this verse was revealed: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ to the end of the verse."

I say: The narrations regarding the stories that followed the prohibition of wine in Islam are many from the paths of the Sunnis (الجمهور), despite the severe contradictions within them.

As for those companions who were mentioned as drinking, we have no concern in investigating them in what we are currently engaged in of research related to the commentary (التفسير). However, these narrations support what we mentioned in the previous commentary: that in the verses there is an allusion or indication that a group of Muslims did not abandon the drinking of wine after the revelation of the verse of al-Baqarah until the verse of al-Ma'idah was revealed.

Indeed, it has been mentioned in some narrations that ‘Ali (عليه السلام) and ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un had forbidden wine (الخمر) for themselves even before the revelation of its prohibition. The author of al-Milal wa al-Nihal mentions several men among the Arabs who forbade wine for themselves during the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) — the period of spiritual darkness and paganism in Arabia before the advent of Islam. Allah, the Glorified, granted some of them the success of reaching the era of Islam and entering into it. Among them were ‘Amir ibn al-Zarib al-‘Adwani and Qays ibn ‘Amir al-Tamimi, both of whom lived to see Islam. Others included Safwan ibn Umayyah ibn Muharrith al-Kinani, ‘Afif ibn Ma‘dikarib al-Kindi, and al-Aslum al-Yami, the latter of whom forbade both adultery (الزنا) and wine together. These were individual men upon whose tongues the word of truth flowed. As for the general masses during the Age of Ignorance—much like the rest of the world at that time, with the exception of the Jews—they were accustomed to drinking it without seeing any harm in it, until Allah, the Glorified, prohibited it in His Book.

What the verses of the Mighty Book indicate is that it was prohibited in Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة), as evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ وَالْبَغْيَ "Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies (الفواحش) — shameful and immoral acts — whether outward or inward, and sin (الإثم), and undue aggression’" (al-A‘raf: 33). This verse is Meccan (مكية). When this is joined with His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُما أَكْبَرُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِما "They ask you about wine and gambling. Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them, and some profits for mankind, but their sin is greater than their profit’" (al-Baqarah: 219)—which is a Medinan (مدنية) verse revealed in the early period of the Migration—there remains no doubt that its prohibition (حرمتها) was manifest to the Muslims at that time.

If we contemplate the context of the verses in Surah al-Ma'idah, especially what is conveyed by His saying: "Will you then desist?" and His saying: لَيْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جُناحٌ فِيما طَعِمُوا إِذا مَا اتَّقَوْا "There is no sin upon those who have faith and do righteous deeds (الصالحات) — virtuous actions performed with sincere intention — in regard to what they have eaten [in the past], so long as they are God-conscious (اتقوا)..." (al-Ma'idah: 93), it becomes clear that the drinking of wine by a group of them between the revelation of the verse in al-Baqarah and the verse in al-Ma'idah was merely a residual effect (ذنابة) of a long-standing bad habit. This is similar to what occurred regarding sexual intercourse during the nights of fasting as an act of disobedience, until His saying, the Exalted, was revealed: أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ لَيْلَةَ الصِّيامِ الرَّفَثُ إِلى نِسائِكُمْ هُنَّ لِباسٌ لَكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ لِباسٌ لَهُنَّ عَلِمَ اللهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَخْتانُونَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ فَتابَ عَلَيْكُمْ "It is made lawful for you to go in unto your wives on the night of the fast. They are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them. Allah knew that you used to betray yourselves, so He has turned to you [mercifully]" (al-Baqarah: 187).

Thus, it is evident that these narrations are problematic from two perspectives:

First: Regarding the discrepancy in the date of the prohibition of wine. It was mentioned in the first narration that it occurred shortly before the Battle of Uhud, while some narrations state it was after the Battle of the Trench (الأحزاب). However, this matter is generally manageable, as it is possible to interpret the "prohibition of wine" mentioned in them as referring to the revelation of the verse in al-Ma'idah, even if the wording of some narrations does not perfectly align with this.

Second: Regarding their indication that wine was not prohibited before the revelation of the verse in al-Ma'idah, or that its prohibition had not become manifest to the people—especially the Companions—before then. This contradicts the explicit nature of the verse in al-A‘raf which prohibits "sin" (الإثم), and the verse in al-Baqarah which explicitly identifies wine as a "sin." This is an explicitness (صراحة) that does not accept reinterpretation (تأويل).

Furthermore, it is highly improbable that the prohibition of "sin" would be revealed in Mecca before the Migration in a verse containing a list of prohibitions—namely His saying: قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ وَالْبَغْيَ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ وَأَنْ تُشْرِكُوا بِاللهِ ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ سُلْطاناً وَأَنْ تَقُولُوا عَلَى اللهِ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ "Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies, whether outward or inward, and sin and undue aggression, and that you should ascribe to Allah partners for which He has not sent down any authority, and that you should attribute to Allah what you do not know’" (al-A‘raf: 33)—and then a considerable amount of time passes without the believers seeking clarification of its meaning from their Prophet, nor the polytheists seeking to clarify it, whose greatest concern was to find contradictions or objections against the Book of Allah whenever they imagined a way to do so.

Rather, what is gathered from history is that the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prohibition of wine, like his prohibition of polytheism (الشرك) and adultery (الزنا), was well-known among the polytheists. This is indicated by what Ibn Hisham narrated in the Sirah from Khallad ibn Qurrah and others among the scholars of Bakr ibn Wa'il: that al-A‘sha of the Banu Qays set out toward the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) intending to embrace Islam, and he composed a poem praising the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله):

Did your eyes not close on a night of ophthalmia, While you spent the night like one bitten by a snake, sleepless? (The poem continues).

When he reached Mecca or near it, some polytheists of the Quraysh intercepted him and asked about his business. He informed them that he had come seeking the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to embrace Islam. They said to him, "O Abu Basir, he forbids adultery." Al-A‘sha replied, "By Allah, that is a matter for which I have no need." They then said, "O Abu Basir, he forbids wine." Al-A‘sha said, "As for this, there is still a lingering desire (علالات) for it in my soul. I shall return and quench my thirst with it for this year, then I will come to him and embrace Islam." So he departed, but he died that same year and never returned to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

Therefore, these narrations can only be understood as the narrators deriving this through their own personal judgment (اجتهاد) regarding the verses while being heedless of the verse in al-A‘raf. Commentators have offered strange justifications (توجيهات غريبة) to reconcile the meaning of these narrations.

Ultimately, the Book is a definitive text (نص) regarding the prohibition of wine in Islam before the Migration. The verse in al-Ma'idah was only revealed as a severe warning to the people due to their laxity in desisting from this divine prohibition and to firmly establish the ruling of its forbiddenness.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Hisham, from a reliable source in a raised (رفعه) report from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام): It was said to him, "It is narrated from you that 'wine, gambling, idols, and divining arrows' refer to certain men?" He replied: "Allah would never address His creation with that which they cannot comprehend."

In the same source, from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: Qudamah ibn Maz‘un was brought before ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab—having drunk wine and the evidence being established against him. ‘Umar consulted ‘Ali, who ordered him to lash him eighty times. Qudamah said, "O Commander of the Faithful, there is no legal punishment (حد) upon me—I am among the people of this verse: لَيْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جُناحٌ فِيما طَعِمُوا 'There is no sin upon those who have faith and do righteous deeds in regard to what they have eaten...'"—and he read the verse to its end. ‘Ali (عليه السلام) said to him: "You have lied; you are not among the people of this verse. Whatever the people of this verse ate was lawful (حلال) for them, and they do not eat or drink except what is lawful for them."

I say: This meaning is also narrated from Abu al-Rabi‘ from him (عليه السلام), and al-Shaykh narrated it in al-Tahdhib with his chain from Ibn Sinan from him (عليه السلام). This meaning is also narrated through Sunni channels.

His (عليه السلام) saying: ["Whatever the people of this verse ate was lawful for them, etc."] accords with what we established in the previous commentary regarding the meaning of the verse; so refer back to it.

In Tafsir al-Tabari, from al-Sha‘bi, who said: "Four verses were revealed concerning wine: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ 'They ask you about wine and gambling...' so they left it. Then was revealed: تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَراً وَرِزْقاً حَسَناً '...from which you obtain an intoxicant and a good provision' (al-Nahl: 67), so they drank it. Then the two verses in al-Ma'idah were revealed: إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ 'Indeed wine and gambling...' until His saying 'Will you then desist?'"

I say: The apparent meaning of this is that the verse in al-Nahl abrogated the verse in al-Baqarah, and then the two verses in al-Ma'idah abrogated the verse in al-Nahl. You do not need any further explanation to judge the falsity of this claim.
