In al-Kafi and al-Tahdhib, with their chains to Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "Allah never sent a prophet but that it was in the knowledge of Allah that when He completed His religion, it would include the prohibition of wine. Wine has always been prohibited; they were merely moved from one stage (خصلة) to another. Had it been imposed upon them all at once, they would have been cut off from the religion." He said: And Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) said: "No one is more gentle (أرفق) than Allah the Exalted. It is out of His gentleness, blessed and exalted is He, that He moves them from one stage to another; for had He imposed it upon them all at once, they would have perished."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to ‘Amr ibn Shimr from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "When Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed to His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنْصابُ وَالْأَزْلامُ رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ فَاجْتَنِبُوهُ 'Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and divining arrows are an abomination (رجس) — something inherently foul and spiritually polluting — of Satan’s doing, so avoid it' (al-Ma'idah: 90), it was asked: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is gambling (الميسر)?' He said: 'Everything that is used for betting, even gambling-bones and walnuts.' It was asked: 'What are the idols (الأنصاب)?' He said: 'That which they sacrificed to their gods.' It was asked: 'What are the divining arrows (الأزلام)?' He said: 'Their arrows by which they sought omens.'"

In the same source, with its chain to ‘Ata ibn Yasar from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Every intoxicant (مسكر) is prohibited (حرام), and every intoxicant is wine (خمر).'"

I say: This narration is also reported through Sunni channels from ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and its wording is: "Every intoxicant is wine, and every wine is prohibited." It was narrated by al-Bayhaqi and others. Narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) are widely transmitted (استفاضت) stating that every intoxicant is prohibited, and that everything gambled upon is maysir (gambling).

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Abu al-Sabbah from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام): He said: "I asked him whether date-wine (النبيذ) and grape-wine (الخمر) are of the same status?" He replied: "No, date-wine is not of the same status as grape-wine. Allah prohibited grape-wine, both a small amount and a large amount, just as He prohibited carrion, blood, and the flesh of swine. And the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prohibited other intoxicating drinks, and what the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has prohibited, Allah has prohibited."

In al-Kafi and al-Tahdhib, with their chains to Musa ibn Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "Allah did not prohibit wine for its name (إثمها), but He prohibited it for its consequence (عاقبتها). Thus, whatever has a consequence like that of wine is wine." In another narration: "Whatever acts as wine acts is wine."

I say: The reports censuring wine and gambling from the paths of both groups (the Shi‘a and Sunnis) are beyond counting. Whoever wishes to examine them should consult the compendiums of hadith.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَيَبْلُوَنَّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنَ ٱلصَّيْدِ تَنَالُهُۥٓ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَرِمَاحُكُمْ لِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَخَافُهُۥ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۚ فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٩٤)
O you who have faith! Allah will surely test you with some of the game within the reach of your hands and spears, so that Allah may know those who fear Him in secret. So whoever transgresses after that, there is a painful punishment for him. (94)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْدَ وَأَنتُمْ حُرُمٌ ۚ وَمَن قَتَلَهُۥ مِنكُم مُّتَعَمِّدًا فَجَزَآءٌ مِّثْلُ مَا قَتَلَ مِنَ ٱلنَّعَمِ يَحْكُمُ بِهِۦ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ هَدْيًۢا بَـٰلِغَ ٱلْكَعْبَةِ أَوْ كَفَّـٰرَةٌ طَعَامُ مَسَـٰكِينَ أَوْ عَدْلُ ذَٰلِكَ صِيَامًا لِّيَذُوقَ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِۦ ۗ عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا سَلَفَ ۚ وَمَنْ عَادَ فَيَنتَقِمُ ٱللَّهُ مِنْهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو ٱنتِقَامٍ (٩٥)
O you who have faith! Do not kill any game when you are in pilgrim sanctity. Should any of you kill it intentionally, its atonement, the counterpart from cattle of what he has killed, as judged by two fair men among you, will be an offering brought to the Ka‘bah, or an atonement by feeding needy persons, or its equivalent in fasting, that he may taste the untoward consequences of his conduct. Allah has excused what is already past; but should anyone resume, Allah shall take vengeance on him, for Allah is all-mighty, avenger. (95)
أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ ٱلْبَحْرِ وَطَعَامُهُۥ مَتَـٰعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِلسَّيَّارَةِ ۖ وَحُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ صَيْدُ ٱلْبَرِّ مَا دُمْتُمْ حُرُمًا ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٩٦)
You are permitted the game of the sea and its food, a provision for you and for the caravans, but you are forbidden the game of the land so long as you remain in pilgrim sanctity, and be wary of Allah toward whom you will be gathered. (96)
۞ جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكَعْبَةَ ٱلْبَيْتَ ٱلْحَرَامَ قِيَـٰمًا لِّلنَّاسِ وَٱلشَّهْرَ ٱلْحَرَامَ وَٱلْهَدْىَ وَٱلْقَلَـٰٓئِدَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِتَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٩٧)
Allah has made the Ka‘bah, the Sacred House, a [means of] sustentation for mankind, and [also] the sacred month, the offering and the garlands, so that you may know that Allah knows whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth, and that Allah has knowledge of all things. (97)
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٩٨)
Know that Allah is severe in retribution, and that Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (98)
مَّا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُونَ (٩٩)
The Apostle’s duty is only to communicate and Allah knows whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal. (99)

(Commentary)

The verses explain the ruling (الحكم) regarding land and sea hunting while in the state of pilgrim sanctity (الإحرام).

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَيَبْلُوَنَّكُمُ اللهُ بِشَيْءٍ مِنَ الصَّيْدِ تَنالُهُ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَرِماحُكُمْ — "O you who have faith! Allah will surely test you with some of the game that your hands and spears can reach" (5:94). Trial (البلاء) is examination and testing (الامتحان والاختبار). The lām of the oath and the emphasized nūn are for corroboration (التأكيد). His saying, "with some of the game" (بِشَيْءٍ مِنَ الصَّيْدِ), implies insignificance (التحقير), so that its instruction to the addressees may assist them in refraining from the prohibition they will face in the subsequent verse. His saying, "that your hands and spears can reach" (تَنالُهُ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَرِماحُكُمْ), is a generalization of game in terms of ease of capture—such as bird chicks, small wild animals, and eggs, which hands can reach and catch easily—and in terms of difficulty of capture, such as large wild animals that are usually only caught with weapons.

The apparent sense of the verse is that it is presented as a preamble (التوطئة) for the strict ruling to be revealed in the following verse. Therefore, He followed the speech by saying: لِيَعْلَمَ اللهُ مَنْ يَخافُهُ بِالْغَيْبِ — "so that Allah may know those who fear Him in the Unseen" (5:94). This contains an intimation that there is a ruling of the nature of prevention and prohibition. Then He followed it by saying: فَمَنِ اعْتَدى بَعْدَ ذلِكَ فَلَهُ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ — "So whoever transgresses after that, there is a painful punishment for him" (5:94).

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَعْلَمَ اللهُ مَنْ يَخافُهُ بِالْغَيْبِ — "so that Allah may know those who fear Him in the Unseen" (5:94). It is not improbable that His saying, "Allah will surely test you... so that He may know," is a metaphor (كناية) for the fact that He will ordain such a situation so that those among you who fear Allah in the Unseen may be distinguished from those who do not. This is because ignorance (الجهل) is impossible for Allah, the Glorified, such that He would remove it through knowledge. An exhaustive discussion on the meaning of testing (الامتحان) has already been presented in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ — "Do you suppose that you will enter paradise..." (3:142) in the fourth volume of this book, where another meaning for this "knowledge" was also mentioned.

As for His saying, "those who fear Him in the Unseen" (مَنْ يَخافُهُ بِالْغَيْبِ), the adverbial phrase relates to the fear. The meaning of fearing in the Unseen (الخوف بالغيب) is that a person fears his Lord and guards against the punishment of the Hereafter and His painful retribution with which He warns him—all of which is in the Unseen (غيب) for the human being, as he does not witness any of it with his outward senses. The Exalted said: إِنَّما تُنْذِرُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ الذِّكْرَ وَخَشِيَ الرَّحْمنَ بِالْغَيْبِ — "You can only warn him who follows the Reminder and fears the All-beneficent in the Unseen" (36:11). And He said: وَأُزْلِفَتِ الْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ ، هذا ما تُوعَدُونَ لِكُلِّ أَوَّابٍ حَفِيظٍ ، مَنْ خَشِيَ الرَّحْمنَ بِالْغَيْبِ وَجاءَ بِقَلْبٍ مُنِيبٍ — "And paradise will be brought near for the God-conscious, not far off. ‘This is what you were promised—for every penitent and mindful [servant], who fears the All-beneficent in the Unseen and comes with a penitent heart’" (50:31-33). And He said: الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَيْبِ وَهُمْ مِنَ السَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ — "those who fear their Lord in the Unseen and are apprehensive of the Hour" (21:49).

His saying: فَمَنِ اعْتَدى بَعْدَ ذلِكَ — "So whoever transgresses after that" (5:94), meaning whoever exceeds the limit set by Allah after the aforementioned trial, for him is a painful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَقْتُلُوا الصَّيْدَ وَأَنْتُمْ حُرُمٌ — "O you who have faith! Do not kill any game while you are in pilgrim sanctity" (5:95) etc. Ḥurum (حُرُمٌ) is the plural of ḥarām (حرام), which is an assimilative adjective (صفة مشبهة). It is stated in al-Majma‘: "A man is ḥarām and muḥrim with the same meaning, just as ḥalāl and muḥill are the same. A man aḥrama when he enters the sacred month, or when he enters the Sanctuary (الحرم), or when he begins the rites of Hajj. Al-ḥurm is the state of pilgrim sanctity (الإحرام); hence the hadith: 'I used to perfume the Prophet for his ḥurm.' The root of the word is 'prevention' (المنع). Women are called ḥuram because they are protected/prevented, and the maḥrūm is the one prevented from sustenance."

He said: "Al-mithl (المثل), al-mathal (المثل), al-shibh (الشبه), and al-shabah (الشبه) are all one [meaning 'like' or 'equivalent']." He said: "Al-na‘am (النعم) in the language refers to camels, cattle, and sheep. If camels are alone, they are called na‘am; if cattle and sheep are alone, they are not called na‘am—this was mentioned by al-Zajjāj."

He said: "Al-Farrā’ said: al-‘adl (العدل) with a fatḥah on the ‘ayn is that which equals something of a different kind. Al-‘idl (العدل) with a kasrah is the equivalent [of the same kind]; you say: 'I have the ‘idl of your boy or your sheep' if it is a sheep equaling a sheep or a boy equaling a boy. But if you mean its value in something of a different kind, you use the fatḥah and say ‘adl. The Basrans said: al-‘adl and al-‘idl both mean 'equivalent' (المثل), whether of the same kind or different."

He said: "Al-wabāl (الوبال) is the weightiness of something in a disliked matter. From this is their saying: 'heavy food' (طعام وبيل) and 'heavy water' (ماء وبيل) when they are heavy and do not promote well-being. From this is: فَأَخَذْناهُ أَخْذاً وَبِيلاً — 'so We seized him with a terrible seizing' (73:16), meaning heavy and severe. The fuller's staff is called wabīl for this reason." End quote.

His saying: لا تَقْتُلُوا الصَّيْدَ وَأَنْتُمْ حُرُمٌ — "Do not kill any game while you are in pilgrim sanctity" (5:95) is a prohibition against killing game, but it is partially explained by the subsequent verse: أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ الْبَحْرِ — "Lawful to you is the game of the sea" (5:96); this is from the perspective of the game. It is explained from the perspective of the meaning of killing by His saying: وَمَنْ قَتَلَهُ مِنْكُمْ مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاءٌ — "and whoever of you kills it intentionally, then the penalty..." (5:95). Thus, "intentionally" (مُتَعَمِّداً) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for "whoever kills it." The apparent meaning of intentionality is that which contrasts with error (الخطأ), which is killing without intending the act itself—such as one who shoots at a target but hits game. Its implication is the necessity of atonement (الكفارة) if he intended to kill the game, whether he was mindful of his state of pilgrim sanctity, or had forgotten it, or was inattentive.

His saying: فَجَزاءٌ مِثْلُ ما قَتَلَ مِنَ النَّعَمِ يَحْكُمُ بِهِ ذَوا عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ هَدْياً بالِغَ الْكَعْبَةِ — "then the penalty is an equivalent from the cattle of what he has killed, as settled by two just men from among you, as an offering reaching the Ka‘bah" (5:95). According to its apparent meaning: upon him is a penalty, and that penalty is the equivalent of what he killed of the game; that penalty shall be from the cattle (النعم) similar to what he killed. Two men from among you, possessing justice (ذوا عدل) in religion, shall judge regarding that equivalent penalty, while the said penalty is an offering (هدياً) brought as a gift, reaching the Ka‘bah (بالِغَ الْكَعْبَةِ), to be slaughtered or sacrificed in the Sanctuary (الحرم) in Mecca or in Mina, according to what the Prophetic Sunnah explains.

Thus, His saying "then the penalty" (فَجَزاءٌ) is in the nominative case as a subject (مبتدأ) for a deleted predicate (خبر) indicated by the context. His saying "equivalent of what he killed" (مِثْلُ ما قَتَلَ), His saying "from the cattle" (مِنَ النَّعَمِ), and His saying "as settled by..." (يَحْكُمُ بِهِ) are all descriptions (أوصاف) of the penalty. His saying "an offering reaching the Ka‘bah" (هَدْياً بالِغَ الْكَعْبَةِ) is a noun and its adjective, and "offering" is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the penalty mentioned earlier. Other interpretations have also been suggested.

His saying: أَوْ كَفَّارَةٌ طَعامُ مَساكِينَ أَوْ عَدْلُ ذلِكَ صِياماً — "or an atonement by feeding the needy, or its equivalent in fasting" (5:95) are two other options for the atonement of killing game. The word "or" (أَوْ) indicates nothing more than absolute choice (الترديد), and the Sunnah is the explainer. However, His saying "or an atonement" (أَوْ كَفَّارَةٌ), where He names the feeding of the needy an atonement and then considers the fasting as an equivalent to the food, is not devoid of an intimation of a sequence (الترتيب) between the options.

His saying: لِيَذُوقَ وَبالَ أَمْرِهِ — "so that he may taste the evil consequence of his conduct" (5:95). The lām is for the purpose (للغاية), and it and its object relate to His saying "then the penalty" (فَجَزاءٌ). The speech indicates that this is a type of retribution (مجازاة).

His saying, the Exalted: عَفَا اللهُ عَمَّا سَلَفَ وَمَنْ عادَ فَيَنْتَقِمُ اللهُ مِنْهُ — "Allah has excused what is past; but whoever resumes, Allah will take vengeance on him" (5:95) until the end of the verse. The attachment of pardon (العفو) to "what is past" (عَمَّا سَلَفَ) is a contextual clue that what is meant by "what is past" is the killing of game that occurred before the revelation of the ruling through this verse. For the attachment of pardon to what occurs at the time of revelation or after it would contradict the establishment of the ruling, which is obvious. Thus, the sentence is to dispel the illusion that the ruling of atonement includes incidents prior to the time of revelation.

The verse is evidence for the possibility of pardon attaching to acts that are not [yet] sins (معصية) if their nature warrants a masterly prohibition (النهي المولوي) due to their containing corruption (المفسدة). As for His saying: وَمَنْ عادَ فَيَنْتَقِمُ اللهُ مِنْهُ وَاللهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انْتِقامٍ — "but whoever resumes, Allah will take vengeance on him, and Allah is all-mighty, wielder of retribution" (5:95), the apparent meaning of "resuming" (العود) is the repetition of the act. This repetition is not a repetition of what passed of the act—such that the meaning would be: "whoever returns to the like of what he did in the past, Allah will take vengeance on him"—because it would then apply to the act to which the ruling attaches in His saying: "and whoever of you kills it intentionally, then the penalty..." and the "vengeance" would mean the ruling of atonement, which is an already established ruling. However, the apparent sense of His saying "Allah will take vengeance on him" (فَيَنْتَقِمُ اللهُ مِنْهُ) is a report of a future matter, not a current, actual ruling.

This is evidence that what is meant by "resuming" is returning a second time to an act for which atonement was already required, and "vengeance" refers to divine punishment (العذاب الإلهي) other than the prescribed atonement.

Accordingly, the verse in its beginning and end addresses the aspects of the issue of killing game: as for what occurred before the revelation of the ruling, Allah has pardoned it; as for after the establishment of the ruling, whoever kills it must pay a penalty equivalent to what he killed the first time; but if he resumes, Allah will take vengeance on him, and there is no [further] atonement upon him. This is what most of the narrations reported from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) indicate in the commentary on the verse.

Were it not for this meaning, it would be almost necessary to interpret "vengeance" in His saying "Allah will take vengeance on him" as encompassing the ruling of the obligation of atonement, and to interpret "resuming" as performing an act similar to what they did in the past of killing game—meaning: whoever returns to the like of what they were upon of killing game before this ruling, i.e., whoever kills game, Allah will take vengeance on him, meaning He will hold him accountable by obliging atonement. This—as you can see—is a meaning far from the literal wording.

His saying, the Exalted: أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ الْبَحْرِ وَطَعامُهُ مَتاعاً لَكُمْ وَلِلسَّيَّارَةِ — "Lawful to you is the game of the sea and its food, a provision for you and for the travellers" (5:96) until the end of the verse. The verses are in the position of explaining the ruling of hunting from the sea or land. This is the evidence that the object of "lawfulness" (الحل) is the act of hunting in His saying "Lawful to you is the game of the sea," rather than the eating of it. By this contextual clue, His saying "and its food" (وَطَعامُهُ) is determined to mean that which is eaten, rather than the verbal noun meaning "the act of eating." The meaning of the lawfulness of the food of the sea is the lawfulness of eating it. Thus, the sum of what is meant by the lawfulness of the game of the sea and its food is the permissibility of hunting sea animals and the lawfulness of eating what is taken from it.

What is taken as food from the sea, although it is more general than what is taken as game—such as preserved game meat or what the sea casts up of dead animals and the like—the reports from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) interpret it as salted fish and the like of preserved game. His saying: "a provision for you and for the travellers" (مَتاعاً لَكُمْ وَلِلسَّيَّارَةِ) is as if it were a circumstantial qualifier for the game of the sea and its food, and it contains an element of the meaning of favor (الامتنان).

Since the address is to the believers in their capacity as being in the state of pilgrim sanctity, the contrast between them and the "travellers" (السيارة) is equivalent to our saying: "a provision for those in pilgrim sanctity and others."

Know that in these verses there are many subsidiary (فرعية) discussions addressed in the books of jurisprudence (الفقه); whoever desires them should refer there.

His saying, the Exalted: جَعَلَ اللهُ الْكَعْبَةَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرامَ قِياماً لِلنَّاسِ وَالشَّهْرَ الْحَرامَ وَالْهَدْيَ وَالْقَلائِدَ — "Allah has made the Ka‘bah, the Sacred House, a [means of] support for mankind, and [also] the sacred month, the offering and the garlands" (5:97). The apparent attachment of the speech to the Ka‘bah, then its explanation as the House by its being a "Sacred House" (البيت الحرام), as well as the description of the month as "Sacred" (الحرام), and then the mention of the offering (الهدي) and the garlands (القلائد) whose status is linked to the sanctity of the House—all of this indicates that the criterion (الملاك) in what Allah, the Glorified, explains in this verse of the matter is indeed "sanctity" (الحرمة).

Qiyām (القيام) is that by which a thing stands. Al-Rāghib said: "Al-qiyām and al-qiwām are names for that by which a thing stands, i.e., is established, like the pillar (العماد) and the support (السناد) for that which is pillared and supported. Such as His saying: وَلا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ الَّتِي جَعَلَ اللهُ لَكُمْ قِياماً — 'Do not give the feeble-minded your property, which Allah has assigned you as a support' (4:5), meaning He made it that which sustains you. And His saying: 'Allah has made the Ka‘bah, the Sacred House, a support for mankind' (5:97), meaning a mainstay (قواما) for them by which their worldly livelihood and their Hereafter are established. Al-Aṣamm said: 'Established, not to be abrogated.' It is also recited as qiyaman (قيما) with the meaning of qiyāman." End quote.

Thus, the meaning of His saying "Allah has made the Ka‘bah, the Sacred House, a support for mankind" returns to the fact that He, the Exalted, made the Ka‘bah a Sacred House which He honored, and made some of the months sacred, and linked them through a ruling such as the Hajj in the sacred month of Dhu al-Hijjah, and established there matters suitable to the sanctity, such as the offering and the garlands—all of that so that the happy social life of the people may rely upon it.

For He made the Sacred House a direction (قبلة) toward which people turn their faces in their prayers, and toward which they turn their sacrifices and their dead, and which they honor in their worst states. Through this, their collective is unified, their disarray is gathered, and their religion is revived and sustained. They perform Hajj to it from various regions and the farthest horizons, witnessing benefits for themselves and traversing the paths of servitude (العبودية) through it.

Through it, the worlds (العالمون) are guided by His name, His remembrance, looking at it, seeking proximity through it, and turning toward it. Allah, the Exalted, has explained this in another way close to this one by His saying: إِنَّ أَوَّلَ بَيْتٍ وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ لَلَّذِي بِبَكَّةَ مُبارَكاً وَهُدىً لِلْعالَمِينَ — "Indeed the first house to be set up for mankind is the one at Bakkah, blessed and a guidance for all nations" (3:96). There has already reached you in the commentary on that verse in the third volume of this book speech by which this position is enlightened.

Similar is the speech regarding the sacred month being a support for mankind, as Allah has prohibited fighting therein, and made people secure therein regarding their blood, honor, and property, and they rectify therein what was corrupted or disturbed of their life affairs. The sacred month among the months is like the station and the stop where the tired traveler rests. In short, the Sacred House, the sacred month, and what relates to that of offerings and garlands are a support for people in all aspects of their worldly livelihood and their Hereafter. If the reflecting thinker were to survey the particulars of what people benefit from—continual or stable benefits—from the blessings of the Ancient House and the sacred month, such as the joining of kin, the connecting of friends, the spending on the poor, the profiting of markets, the affection of relatives and neighbors, the acquaintance of strangers and those far off, the drawing together of hearts, the purification of souls, the strengthening of powers, the support of the community, the life of religion, and the raising of the banners of truth and the flags of monotheism (التوحيد), he would find immense blessings and see wonders.

The purpose of mentioning this reality following the verses prohibiting hunting was to dispel the illusion that these are rulings of no or little utility. What benefit is there in prohibiting hunting in a certain place or time? What utility is there in driving the offering and the like? Are these rulings anything but similar to the superstitious laws (النواميس الخرافية) found among ignorant, barbaric nations?

The answer to that was that the consideration of the Sacred House, the sacred month, and the rulings that follow them is built upon a scientific reality and a serious foundation: that they are a support (قيام) by which the backbone of their life stands.

From here, the connection of His saying: ذلِكَ لِتَعْلَمُوا — "That is so that you may know..." (5:97) until the end of the verse, to what precedes it becomes clear. That which is pointed to by "That" (ذلِكَ) is either the ruling itself explained in the previous verses, whose wisdom of legislation is clarified by His saying: "Allah has made the Ka‘bah, the Sacred House, a support for mankind..." etc., or it is the explanation of the ruling clarified by His saying: "Allah has made the Ka‘bah..." etc., as indicated by the context.

The meaning according to the first possibility is that Allah made the Sacred House and the sacred month a support for mankind and established the rulings suitable for them so that they may transition from observing their sanctity and acting upon the rulings legislated therein to the fact that Allah is knowledgeable of what is in the heavens and the earth and what rectifies their affairs. Thus, He legislated what He legislated for you out of knowledge, without any of that being a superstitious ruling (حكما خرافيا) originating from the ignorance of imagination.

The meaning according to the second possibility is: We have explained to you this reality—which is the making of the Sacred House, the sacred month, and the rulings that follow them as a support for mankind—so that you may know that Allah is knowledgeable of what is in the heavens and the earth and the rulings that follow them which rectify their affairs. So do not imagine that these legislated rulings are futile without utility or that they are fabricated superstitions.

His saying, the Exalted: اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ وَأَنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "Know that Allah is severe in retribution, and that Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (5:98) until the end of the two verses. This is a corroboration of the explanation and a consolidation of the status of the mentioned rulings, and a threat and a promise for the obedient and the disobedient. It contains a trace of warning; therefore, He prioritized His description of being severe in retribution over His description of forgiveness and mercy. For this reason also, He followed the speech by saying: ما عَلَى الرَّسُولِ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تُبْدُونَ وَما تَكْتُمُونَ — "The Messenger’s duty is only to deliver [the message], and Allah knows whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal" (5:99).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kāfī, with its chain of narration from Ḥammād ibn ‘Īsā and Ibn Abī ‘Umayr, from Mu‘āwiyah ibn ‘Ammār, from Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: لَيَبْلُوَنَّكُمُ اللهُ بِشَيْءٍ مِنَ الصَّيْدِ ـ تَنالُهُ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَرِماحُكُمْ — "Allah will surely test you with some of the game that your hands and spears can reach" (5:94). He said: "The wild animals were gathered for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) during the ‘Umrah of al-Hudaybiyyah until their hands and spears reached them."

I say: It was also narrated by al-‘Ayyāshī from Mu‘āwiyah ibn ‘Ammār in a disconnected (مرسلا) form. This meaning was also narrated by al-Kulaynī in al-Kāfī and al-Shaykh in al-Tahdhīb with their chains to al-Ḥalabī from al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him), and al-‘Ayyāshī from Samā‘ah from him (peace be upon him) in a disconnected form, as well as al-Qummī in his Tafsīr in a disconnected form. It was also narrated from Muqātil ibn Ḥayyān as will follow.

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, Ibn Abī Ḥātim extracted from Muqātil ibn Ḥayyān who said: "This verse was revealed during the ‘Umrah of al-Hudaybiyyah. The wild animals, birds, and game would overwhelm them in their camps—they had never seen the like of it before. So Allah prohibited them from killing it while they were in pilgrim sanctity, 'so that Allah may know those who fear Him in the Unseen.'"

I say: The two narrations do not contradict what we presented in the previous commentary regarding the generality of the verse's meaning.

In al-Kāfī, narrated from Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad in an elevated (رفعه) form: regarding the saying of Him, the Blessed and Exalted: تَنالُهُ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَرِماحُكُمْ — "that your hands and spears can reach" (5:94). He said: "What the hands reach are the eggs and the chicks, and what the spears reach is that which the hands cannot reach."

In the Tafsīr of al-‘Ayyāshī, with its chain from Ḥarīz, from Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him), who said: "If a man in pilgrim sanctity kills a pigeon, then upon him is a sheep. If he kills a chick, then upon him is a lamb. If he treads on an egg and breaks it, then upon him is a dirham. All of this is to be given as charity in Mecca and Mina. This is the saying of Allah in His Book: 'Allah will surely test you with some of the game that your hands reach'—the eggs and chicks—'and your spears'—the large mothers."

I say: It was narrated by al-Shaykh in al-Tahdhīb from Ḥarīz from him (peace be upon him), restricted to the latter part of the hadith.

In al-Tahdhīb, with its chain from Ibn Abī ‘Umayr, from Ḥammād, from al-Ḥalabī, from Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him), who said: "When the person in pilgrim sanctity kills game, he must pay its penalty—and he gives the game as charity to a needy person. If he resumes and kills another game, there is no penalty upon him—and Allah will take vengeance on him, and the vengeance is in the Hereafter."

In the same source: from al-Kulaynī, from Ibn Abī ‘Umayr, from some of his companions, from Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him), who said: "If the person in pilgrim sanctity strikes game by error, he must pay an atonement. If he strikes it a second time intentionally, then he is among those from whom Allah takes vengeance, and there is no atonement upon him."

In the same source: from Ibn Abī ‘Umayr, from Mu‘āwiyah ibn ‘Ammār, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillāh: 'A person in pilgrim sanctity struck game?' He said: 'He must pay an atonement.' I said: 'What if he resumes?' He said: 'Upon him is an atonement every time he resumes.'"

I say: The narrations—as you see—differ. Al-Shaykh reconciled them by stating that the meaning is: if the person in pilgrim sanctity kills intentionally, he must pay an atonement; if he resumes intentionally, there is no atonement upon him, and he is among those from whom Allah takes vengeance. As for the one who forgets (الناسي), every time he resumes, he must pay an atonement.

In the same source: with its chain from Zurārah from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يَحْكُمُ بِهِ ذَوا عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ — "as settled by two just men from among you" (5:95). "The 'just one' (العدل) is the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imam after him—he judges by it and he is a possessor of justice. So when you know what Allah has judged through the Messenger of Allah and the Imam, that is sufficient for you and do not ask about it."

I say: There are several narrations with this meaning. In some of them: "I recited before Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him): ذَوا عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ (two just men from among you), and he said: ذو عدل منكم (a just one from among you); this is among the things where the Book was mistaken." This refers to the variant reading (القراءة) as is apparent.

In al-Kāfī, from al-Zuhrī from ‘Alī ibn al-Ḥusayn (peace be upon him), who said: "The fast for the penalty of game is obligatory. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'and whoever of you kills it intentionally, then the penalty is an equivalent from the cattle of what he has killed, as settled by two just men from among you, as an offering reaching the Ka‘bah, or an atonement by feeding the needy, or its equivalent in fasting' (5:95). Do you know how its equivalent in fasting is determined, O Zuhrī?" He said: "I said: 'I do not know.' He said: 'The game is valued, then that value is divided into wheat. Then that wheat is measured in ṣā‘s, and he fasts one day for every half ṣā‘.'"

In the same source, with its chain from Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad, from some of his men, from Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him), who said: "Whoever is obliged to give an offering (هدي) in his pilgrim sanctity, he may sacrifice it wherever he wishes, except for the ransom of game (فداء الصيد)—for Allah says: هَدْياً بالِغَ الْكَعْبَةِ — 'as an offering reaching the Ka‘bah' (5:95)."

In the Tafsīr of al-‘Ayyāshī, from Ḥarīz, from Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him), who said: أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ الْبَحْرِ وَطَعامُهُ مَتاعاً لَكُمْ — "Lawful to you is the game of the sea and its food, a provision for you" (5:96). He said: "Its salted [fish] which they eat." And he said: "The distinction between them is: every bird that is in the marshes but lays eggs on land and hatches on land is from the game of the land. And whatever bird is on land but lays eggs in the sea and hatches there is from the game of the sea."

In the same source: from Zayd al-Shaḥḥām, from Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: 'Lawful to you is the game of the sea and its food, a provision for you and for the travellers' (5:96). He said: 'It is salted fish, and also what you take as provision from it even if it is not salted; it is a provision.'"

I say: The narrations with these meanings are numerous from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) through Shi‘i paths.

In al-Durr al-Manthūr: Ibn Abī Shaybah extracted from Mu‘āwiyah ibn Qurrah, and Aḥmad from a man of the Anṣār: "That a man's camel trod on an ostrich's nest and broke its eggs. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Upon you for every egg is the fasting of one day or the feeding of a needy person.'"

I say: This meaning was also narrated from Ibn Abī Shaybah from ‘Abdullāh ibn Dhakwān from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and also narrated from him from Abu al-Zinād from ‘Ā’ishah from him (ص).

In the same source: Abu al-Shaykh and Ibn Mardawayh extracted through the path of Abu al-Mahzam from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: "Regarding ostrich eggs, their price [is the penalty]."

In the same source: Ibn Abī Ḥātim extracted from Abu Ja‘far Muḥammad ibn ‘Alī: "That a man asked ‘Alī about the offering (الهدي), what is it from? He said: 'From the eight pairs.' It was as if the man doubted, so ‘Alī said: 'Do you read the Qur’an?' It was as if the man said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Did you hear Allah saying: "O you who have faith! Keep your agreements. Cattle (بهيمة الأنعام) are permitted to you" (5:1)?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Did you hear Him saying: "that they may mention Allah’s name over the cattle He has provided them" (22:34), "and of the cattle, some are for burden and some for slaughter" (6:142), "so eat of the cattle"?' He said: 'Yes.'"

"He said: 'Did you hear Him saying: "eight pairs: two of sheep, two of goats... and two of camels and two of oxen" (6:143-144)?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Did you hear Him saying: "O you who have faith! Do not kill any game while you are in pilgrim sanctity... as an offering reaching the Ka‘bah" (5:95)?' The man said: 'Yes.'"

"He said: 'If you kill a gazelle, what is upon me?' He said: 'A sheep.' ‘Alī said: 'As an offering reaching the Ka‘bah?' The man said: 'Yes.' ‘Alī said: 'Allah has named it "reaching the Ka‘bah" as you hear.'"

In the same source: Ibn Abī Ḥātim extracted from ‘Aṭā’ al-Khurāsānī: "That ‘Umar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb, ‘Uthmān ibn ‘Affān, ‘Alī ibn Abī Ṭālib, Ibn ‘Abbās, Zayd ibn Thābit, and Mu‘āwiyah judged regarding what was an offering—from what the person in pilgrim sanctity kills of game for which there is a penalty—that its value be looked at and the needy be fed with it."

In the same source: Ibn Jarīr extracted from Abu Hurayrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Lawful to you is the game of the sea and its food, a provision for you.' He said: 'What it casts up dead is its food.'"

I say: What is in its meaning was also narrated from some of the companions, but what is narrated through the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt from them (peace be upon them) is contrary to it, as preceded.

In the Tafsīr of al-‘Ayyāshī, from Abān ibn Taghlib, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him): 'Allah has made the Ka‘bah, the Sacred House, a support for mankind' (5:97). He said: 'Allah made it for their religion and their livelihoods.'"

I say: The clarification of the narration's meaning has already preceded.

Ayah

قُل لَّا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْخَبِيثُ وَٱلطَّيِّبُ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ كَثْرَةُ ٱلْخَبِيثِ ۚ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (١٠٠)
Say, ‘The good and the bad are not equal, though the abundance of the bad should amaze you.’ So be wary of Allah, O you who possess intellect, so that you may be felicitous! (100)

(Commentary)

The verse appears to be independent and singular, as there is no apparent connection to what precedes it, nor is there a clear link between it and what follows. Thus, there is no need for strained efforts to explain its connection to the preceding text. Rather, it comprises a universal parable (مثل كلي) struck by Allah, the Glorified, to clarify a specific characteristic that distinguishes the true religion (الدين الحق) from all other religions and prevalent general ways of life. This characteristic is that:

Consideration is given to the truth (الحق), even if its adherents are few and its group is scattered; and reliance is placed upon goodness and felicity (السعادة), even if the majority turn away from it and the powerful forget it. For the truth, in its governing laws (نواميس), relies on nothing but the sound intellect (العقل السليم). Far be it from the sound intellect to guide toward anything except the welfare of human society in that which strengthens its foundations through the ordinances of life and the paths of a wholesome livelihood, whether this accords with the whims (أهواء) of the majority or opposes them—and often it does oppose them. This cosmic system (النظام الكوني), which is the source of true opinions, does not follow any of their whims; for had the truth followed their whims, the heavens and the earth would have been corrupted.

His saying, the Exalted: «قُلْ لا يَسْتَوِي الْخَبِيثُ وَالطَّيِّبُ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ كَثْرَةُ الْخَبِيثِ» — "Say, ‘The bad and the good are not equal, though the abundance of the bad may amaze you.’" (5:100). The intended meaning of the lack of equality between the bad (الخبيث) and the good (الطيب) is that the good is better than the bad. This is a self-evident matter; therefore, the speech is directed toward metonymy (الكناية). This is because the good, by its very nature and by the decree of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — is of a higher degree and a loftier station than the bad. If one were to suppose the reversal of this matter—such that the bad became better than the good due to some accidental factor—it would be necessary for the bad to gradually rise and ascend until it reached a level corresponding to the station of the good, equaling it, and then surpassing it. Thus, when the equality of the bad and the good is negated, it is more eloquent in negating the superiority of the bad over the good.

From this, the reason for mentioning the bad (الخبيث) before the good (الطيب) becomes clear. The speech is intended to demonstrate that the abundance of the bad does not make it better than the good. Such a change would only occur if the bad rose from the depths of vileness and baseness to the heights of nobility and honor until it equaled the good in its status and then surpassed it. Had it been said, "The good and the bad are not equal," the rhetorical focus would have been on clarifying that the good cannot be more wretched or base than the bad; in that case, it would have been necessary to mention the scarcity of the good instead of the abundance of the bad. Understand this well.

Goodness (الطيب) and badness (الخباثة), according to their literal meanings, are real attributes (وصفان حقيقيان) of real external things, such as good or bad food, and good or bad land. The Exalted says: «وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِداً» — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord; and as for that which is bad, it does not yield [anything] but what is useless." (7:58). And the Exalted says: «وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ» — "and the good things of [His] provision" (7:32). Although goodness and badness are sometimes applied to conventional, relative matters (الصفات الوضعية الاعتبارية), such as a good or bad ruling, or a good or bad character, this is only done through a type of rhetorical consideration (عناية).

However, the derivation of His saying: «فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ يا أُولِي الْأَلْبابِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ» — "So be wary of Allah, O you who possess intellects, so that you may be felicitous" (5:100) from His saying: «لا يَسْتَوِي الْخَبِيثُ وَالطَّيِّبُ» — "The bad and the good are not equal," etc., combined with the fact that godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and avoiding His disobedience — belongs to the category of actions or omissions, where goodness and badness are metaphorical, and the presentation of the statement "The bad and the good are not equal" as an accepted axiom, is the strongest evidence that what is intended by "good" and "bad" here is their real, external sense. Thus, the argument becomes successful. If the intended meaning were the "good" and "bad" of actions and ways of life, the matter would not be so clear, for every group believes its own way to be the good way, and whatever opposes its whims and contradicts its will to be the bad.

Therefore, the statement is based on another meaning that Allah, the Glorified, has clarified in various places in His speech: that the religion (الدين) is built upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) and creation (الخلقة), and that what the religion calls toward is the "good" of life, while what it forbids is the "bad." Furthermore, Allah has not permitted anything except the good things (الطيبات) and has not forbidden anything except the bad things (الخبائث). The Exalted says: «فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ» — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah on which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion." (30:30). And He says: «وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ الطَّيِّباتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْخَبائِثَ» — "and makes lawful to them all the good things and forbids them all the vicious things" (7:157).

And He says: «قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ» — "Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’" (7:32).

In summary, the statement—namely His saying: «لا يَسْتَوِي الْخَبِيثُ وَالطَّيِّبُ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ كَثْرَةُ الْخَبِيثِ» — is a parable struck to clarify that the principles of religion are constructed upon ontological attributes (صفات تكوينية) in things, whether of goodness or badness, which influence the paths of human felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة). These attributes are not affected by scarcity or abundance; the good is good even if it is little, and the bad is bad even if it is much.

Thus, it is incumbent upon every person of intellect (ذي لب) who distinguishes the bad from the good, and who judges that the good is better than the bad, and that it is necessary for man to strive for the felicity of his life and choose good over evil, to be conscious of Allah, his Lord, by following His path. He must not be deceived by the preoccupation of the many with bad deeds and destructive traits and states, nor should whims divert him from following the truth through intimidation or enticement, so that he may perhaps attain felicity by embarking upon the path of human success.

His saying, the Exalted: «فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ يا أُولِي الْأَلْبابِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ» — "So be wary of Allah, O you who possess intellects, so that you may be felicitous" (5:100). This is a derivation from the parable struck at the beginning of the verse. The sum of the meaning is that since godwariness (التقوى) pertains to the divine laws (الشرائع الإلهية), which are themselves built upon ontological goodness and badness—the observance of which constitutes man's felicity and success, as no person of intellect or reason (ذو لب وعقل) can doubt—it is obligatory upon you, O possessors of intellects, to be conscious of Allah by acting according to His laws, so that you may be felicitous.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ عَنْ أَشْيَآءَ إِن تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ وَإِن تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ عَنْهَا حِينَ يُنَزَّلُ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَنْهَا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (١٠١)
O you who have faith! Do not ask about things, which, if they are disclosed to you, will upset you. Yet if you ask about them while the Quran is being sent down, they shall be disclosed to you. Allah has excused it, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing. (101)
قَدْ سَأَلَهَا قَوْمٌ مِّن قَبْلِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَصْبَحُوا۟ بِهَا كَـٰفِرِينَ (١٠٢)
Certainly some people asked about them before you and then came to disbelieve in them. (102)

(Commentary)

The two verses do not appear to have a manifest connection to what precedes them. Their content is self-sufficient, requiring no external discourse to clarify what they independently convey. Thus, there is no need for the labored efforts undertaken by a group of commentators to justify their connection—sometimes to the preceding verses, sometimes to the beginning of the Surah, and at other times to the overall purpose of the Surah. It is better to overlook all such attempts.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ — "O you who have faith! Do not ask about things which, if they are disclosed to you, will upset you" (5:101). Disclosure (الإبداء) means manifestation (الإظهار), and to "upset" (ساءه) someone is the opposite of pleasing them.

The verse forbids the believers from asking about things that would distress them if revealed. Initially, it remains silent regarding the identity of the one being questioned. However, His subsequent saying: وَإِنْ تَسْئَلُوا عَنْها حِينَ يُنَزَّلُ الْقُرْآنُ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ — "and if you ask about them while the Quran is being sent down, they shall be disclosed to you", as well as His saying in the following verse: قَدْ سَأَلَها قَوْمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَصْبَحُوا بِها كافِرِينَ — "A people before you asked about them, then they came to disbelieve in them" (5:102), indicates that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is the intended object of the questioning. This means the verse was revealed to forbid questioning the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about matters of such-and-such a nature.

Nevertheless, the underlying reason (العللة) derived from the verse—which necessitates the prohibition—implies that the prohibition extends beyond the specific context. It applies to any instance where a person inquires and probes into matters that Divine pardon (العفو الإلهي) has left untouched, and over which He has drawn a veil, preventing access through ordinary means and familiar paths. Indeed, discovering the reality of such matters often leads to destruction and misery—such as a person investigating the day of his death, the cause of his demise, the lifespan of his loved ones, or the end of his sovereignty and prestige. Often, the very thing one discovers becomes the cause that cuts him off in annihilation or threatens him with wretchedness.

The system of life (نظام الحياة) that Allah, the Glorified, has organized and established within the cosmos—whereby He has manifested certain things and veiled others—did not manifest what it manifested except for a wisdom, nor did it hide what it hid except for a wisdom. That is to say, seeking the concealment of what is manifest or striving for the manifestation of what is hidden results in the disruption of the order spread across the universe. This is like human life, which is built upon a physical system composed of faculties, organs, and pillars; if one were missing or something were added, it would necessitate the loss of vital parts of life, which would then affect the functioning of the remaining faculties and organs, potentially leading to the nullification of life in its reality or meaning.

Furthermore, the verse initially leaves the nature of these "things" (الأشياء) ambiguous, clarifying only that they are such that "if they are disclosed to you, they will upset you." There is no doubt that His saying: إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ — "if they are disclosed to you, they will upset you" is an attribute (نعت) for the "things." It is a conditional sentence (جملة شرطية) indicating the certain occurrence of the consequence (الجزاء) upon the occurrence of the condition (الشرط). This necessitates that these are things that will certainly distress them if disclosed; thus, seeking their disclosure through questioning is a request for one's own distress.

An objection might be raised: a rational person does not seek what distresses him. Had the verse said, "Do not ask about things among which are those that will distress you if disclosed," or "Do not ask about things you are not safe from being distressed by if disclosed," the problem would not arise.

Among the strange answers given to this objection is that it is established in the rules of Arabic that the condition introduced by "if" (إن) is not certain to occur, and the consequence follows the condition in its occurrence or non-occurrence. Thus, the expression إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ (if they are disclosed...) rather than إذا أبديت لكم تسؤكم (when they are disclosed...) indicates that the mere possibility of their disclosure and the possibility of them being distressing is sufficient to necessitate refraining from asking about them. (End of the relevant quote).

This is an error. I wonder which rule of Arabic grammar stipulates that the condition (الشرط) must be uncertain of occurrence? Furthermore, does the consequence (الجزاء), qua consequence, have its existence linked to anything other than the condition? Does our saying, "If you come to me, I will honor you," imply anything other than the certainty of the honoring occurring on the condition that the coming occurs? Thus, his claim that the use of the conditional indicates that the possibility of disclosure and distress is sufficient to mandate refraining is only correct if the meaning of the condition in the verse were a prohibition against asking about things that might distress if disclosed. But that is not the case, as you have seen; rather, the meaning is a prohibition against asking about things that are certain to distress if disclosed. Thus, the objection remains.

Following this answer in weakness is the view of some—based on certain narrations—that the intended meaning of أَشْياءَ إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ refers to what some souls might desire in terms of knowing the Unseen (المغيبات) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — such as lifespans, the outcomes of affairs, the flow of good and evil, and the uncovering of every hidden matter. Knowledge of such things naturally involves that which distresses and saddens a person, such as a man asking about the remainder of his life, the cause of his death, his ultimate end, or who his true father is—the latter being a common inquiry among them during the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية).

According to this view, the meaning of لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ is a prohibition against asking about these matters, the disclosure of which usually entails what distresses and saddens a person—such as discovering that one's end is near, that the outcome is grim, or that one's father is actually someone other than the person to whom he is attributed.

These are matters that generally involve distress and sorrow. Furthermore, there is no guarantee that if the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) were asked, he would not provide an answer that the questioner dislikes, potentially leading the questioner's psychological arrogance (الاستكبار النفساني) and tribal prejudice (أنفة العصبية) to deny the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his answer, thereby falling into disbelief (الكفر). This is alluded to in His saying, the Exalted, in the following verse: قَدْ سَأَلَها قَوْمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَصْبَحُوا بِها كافِرِينَ — "A people before you asked about them, then they came to disbelieve in them."

While this explanation may seem sound at first glance, it does not harmonize with His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ تَسْئَلُوا عَنْها حِينَ يُنَزَّلُ الْقُرْآنُ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ — "and if you ask about them while the Quran is being sent down, they shall be disclosed to you." This is true whether we say its meaning is the permissibility of asking about these things during the revelation of the Quran, or that it intensifies the prohibition during the revelation by indicating that the respondent—the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—is at liberty not to answer such questions outside the time of revelation out of concern for the questioners' interests; however, these things (the objects of the questions) are essentially uncovered and the veil is inevitably lifted from them, so do not ask about them at all while the Quran is being revealed.

As for its lack of harmony with the first meaning: since asking about these things inherently involves corruption (المفسدة), there is no sense in permitting it during the revelation of the Quran, for the corruption remains the same.

As for the second meaning: although the time of the Quran's revelation is a time of clarification (البيان) and disclosure of what needs to be revealed, this characteristic is linked to the realities of divine knowledge (حقائق المعارف), the legislated laws (شرائع الأحكام), and similar matters. As for determining the lifespan of Zayd, the manner of 'Amr's death, or identifying who so-and-so's father is, these are not matters with which the Quranic clarification is concerned. Therefore, there would be no reason to conclude the prohibition of asking about such things with a phrase like: وَإِنْ تَسْئَلُوا عَنْها حِينَ يُنَزَّلُ الْقُرْآنُ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ, as is evident.

The most appropriate answer is what is derived from the words of others: that the verse قَدْ سَأَلَها قَوْمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ (A people before you asked...), as well as His saying: وَإِنْ تَسْئَلُوا عَنْها حِينَ يُنَزَّلُ الْقُرْآنُ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ, indicates that the things being asked about are related to the legislated laws (الأحكام المشرعة). These include specific details regarding the subjects of the laws (متعلقات الأحكام) which one might investigate excessively or insist upon with meticulousness. The result of such behavior is the appearance of severity and the descent of hardship (التحريج) whenever one delves deeper into questioning and persists in inquiry. This is similar to what Allah, the Glorified, recounted in the story of the Cow regarding the Children of Israel, where Allah intensified the restriction upon them every time they exaggerated in asking about the attributes of the cow they were commanded to slaughter.

Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted: عَفَا اللهُ عَنْها — "Allah has dispensed with them" is apparently an independent sentence intended to provide the reason (تعليل) for the prohibition in His saying: لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ. It is not, as some have mentioned, an attribute for "things" (أشياء) involving a transposition (تقديم وتأخير) in the speech, as if the meaning were: "Do not ask about things that Allah has dispensed with, which, if disclosed to you, will upset you."

This expression—namely, the use of the preposition "from" (عن) with "pardon" (العفو)—is the best evidence that the "things" mentioned refer to matters pertaining to the divine laws (الشرائع) and rulings (الأحكام). Had they been of the nature of cosmic affairs (الأمور الكونية), it would have been more appropriate to say: عفاها الله (Allah pardoned them).

In any case, providing the reason through "pardon" (العفو) implies that the "things" are the specific details related to the rulings and laws, and the constraints and conditions pertaining to their subjects. The silence regarding them was not due to oversight or neglect, but was rather a concession (تخفيف) from Allah, the Glorified, for His servants and an ease (تسهيل), as He said: وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ — "and Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing." Thus, their proposal to ask about these specifics is an exposure of themselves to restriction and hardship, which would certainly distress and sadden them. Indeed, such questioning constitutes a rejection of the Divine pardon (العفو الإلهي) which was intended solely for ease and concession, and for the implementation of the two Divine attributes of forgiveness (المغفرة) and forbearance (الحلم).

Thus, the meaning of His saying: لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ (Do not ask about things...) returns to something like: "O you who have faith! Do not ask the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about things left silent in the Sharia (الشريعة) — the divine system of law and way of life — which Allah has dispensed with and did not address for the sake of concession and ease; for they are such that they will be clarified to you if you ask about them during the revelation of the Quran, and they will distress you if they are disclosed and clarified to you."

From what has passed, several points have become clear:

First: His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ تَسْئَلُوا عَنْها حِينَ يُنَزَّلُ الْقُرْآنُ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ is a completion of the prohibition, as you have seen, and not for the purpose of lifting the prohibition of questioning during the revelation of the Quran, as has sometimes been claimed.

Second: His saying, the Exalted: عَفَا اللهُ عَنْها is an independent sentence intended to provide the reason for the prohibition of questioning; thus, it conveys the benefit of an attribute without being an attribute in terms of grammatical structure.

Third: The reason for concluding the discourse with His saying: وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ, despite the discourse containing a prohibition—which might seem inconsistent with the attributes of forgiveness and forbearance—is that the two Names refer back to the meaning of the pardon mentioned in His saying: عَفَا اللهُ عَنْها, rather than the prohibition established in the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ سَأَلَها قَوْمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَصْبَحُوا بِها كافِرِينَ — "A people before you asked about them, then they came to disbelieve in them" (5:102). It is said: "he asked it" (سأله) and "he asked about it" (سأل عنه) with the same meaning. The word "then" (ثُمَّ) indicates a delay in terms of the rank of the discourse (الرتبة الكلامية) rather than in terms of time.

The preposition "in" (بـ) in His saying بِها (in them) is connected to كافِرِينَ (disbelievers), according to the apparent meaning of the verse as being intended to prohibit asking about the constraints of rulings and laws left silent at the time of legislation. Thus, the disbelief (الكفر) is a disbelief in the rulings due to the souls' aversion to them and the hearts' constriction in accepting them. It is also possible that the preposition denotes causality (السببية), though this is not without some far-fetchedness.

Although the verse leaves the identity of the "people" (القوم) mentioned ambiguous and does not identify them, there are stories in the Noble Quran to which the verse could apply, such as the story of the Table (المائدة) from the stories of the Christians, and other stories from the people of Moses and others.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Jarir, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) addressed us and said: 'O people! Allah has prescribed the Hajj (الحج) — the pilgrimage to the House of Allah in Mecca — upon you.' 'Ukashah ibn Mihsan al-Asadi stood up and asked: 'Is it every year, O Messenger of Allah?' He replied: 'If I were to say yes, it would have become obligatory, and had it become obligatory and you then abandoned it, you would have gone astray. Leave me as long as I have left you, for those before you were only destroyed because of their questioning and their disagreement with their prophets.' Then Allah revealed: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ to the end of the verse."

I say: This story has been narrated from Abu Hurayrah, Abu Umamah, and others by several narrators. It is also narrated in al-Majma' and other books of the elite (الخاصة). It aligns with what we presented in the preceding commentary.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Suddi regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) became angry one day, so he stood to deliver a sermon and said: 'Ask me! For you shall not ask me about anything except that I will inform you of it.' A man from Quraysh, from the tribe of Banu Sahm, called 'Abdullah ibn Hudhafah—who used to be slandered—stood up and asked: 'O Messenger of Allah, who is my father?' He replied: 'Your father is so-and-so.' Thus, he attributed him to his father. Then 'Umar stood up to him, kissed his foot, and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, we are well-pleased with Allah as our Lord, with you as our Prophet, and with the Quran as our Imam (الإمام) — the guide and leader — so pardon us, may Allah pardon you.' He did not cease until he was pleased. On that day, he said: 'The child belongs to the bed, and for the fornicator is the stone.' And there was revealed to him: قَدْ سَأَلَها قَوْمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ."

I say: This narration is reported through several chains with variations in its text. You have already seen that it is not compatible with the verse.

In the same source as well: Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Tha'labah al-Khushani, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Indeed, Allah has set limits (حدودا), so do not transgress them; He has prescribed for you obligations (فرائض), so do not neglect them; He has forbidden things, so do not violate them; and He has left things [silent] without forgetfulness, but as a mercy from Him to you—so accept them and do not search into them.'"

In al-Majma' and al-Safi, narrated from Imam 'Ali (peace be upon him), who said: إن الله افترض عليكم فرائض فلا تضيعوها ـ وحد لكم حدودا فلا تعتدوها ، ونهاكم عن أشياء فلا تنتهكوها ، وسكت لكم عن أشياء ـ ولم يدعها نسيانا فلا تتكلفوها — "Indeed, Allah has made certain obligations (فرائض) mandatory for you, so do not neglect them; He has set limits (حدودا) for you, so do not transgress them; He has forbidden you from certain things, so do not violate them; and He has remained silent for you regarding certain things—not out of forgetfulness—so do not burden yourselves with them."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu al-Jarud, who said: "Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: 'Whenever I narrate something to you, ask me for its basis in the Book of Allah.' Then he said in part of his discourse: 'Indeed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade idle talk (القيل والقال), the wasting of wealth, and excessive questioning.' He was asked: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, where is this in the Book of Allah?' He replied: 'Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: لا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْواهُمْ إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلاحٍ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ — "There is no good in much of their confidential conferences, except him who enjoins charity or what is right or reconciliation between people" (4:114). And He said: وَلا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ الَّتِي جَعَلَ اللهُ لَكُمْ قِياماً — "Do not give the feeble-minded your property, which Allah has assigned to you for support" (4:5). And He said: لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ — "Do not ask about things which, if they are disclosed to you, will upset you" (5:101).'"

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, narrated from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, who said: "I wrote to Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), and he wrote at the end: 'Have you not been forbidden from excessive questioning? Yet you refuse to desist. Beware of that! For those before you were only destroyed because of their excessive questioning. For Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, says: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ until His saying, the Exalted: كافِرِينَ.'"

Ayahs

مَا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنۢ بَحِيرَةٍ وَلَا سَآئِبَةٍ وَلَا وَصِيلَةٍ وَلَا حَامٍ ۙ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ ۖ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (١٠٣)
Allah has not prescribed any such thing as Bahirah, Sa’ibah, Waseelah, or Haam; but those who are faithless fabricate lies against Allah, and most of them do not exercise their reason. (103)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ وَإِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ قَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا مَا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ ۚ أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ (١٠٤)
When they are told, ‘Come to what Allah has sent down and [come] to the Apostle,’ they say, ‘Sufficient for us is what we have found our fathers following.’ What, even if their fathers did not know anything and were not guided?! (104)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: ما جَعَلَ اللهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ وَلا سائِبَةٍ وَلا وَصِيلَةٍ وَلا حامٍ (Allah has not prescribed any bahirah, or sa’ibah, or wasilah, or ham) [5:103]. These are categories of livestock (الأنعام) for which the people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) observed certain rulings based on veneration and a form of liberation (التحرير) — the act of setting an animal free from labor or use for the sake of idols. Allah, the Glorified, has denied having prescribed any of this. The denied "prescribing" (الجعل) pertains to their descriptions (أوصاف) rather than their essences (ذوات), for their essences are undoubtedly created by Allah, the Glorified. Likewise, their descriptions, insofar as they are merely descriptions, are His creation. Rather, what accepts attribution to Him, the Exalted, or denial thereof, are these descriptions insofar as they were the sources of rulings they claimed for them. Thus, the denial of prescribing the bahirah and its counterparts in the verse is a denial of the lawfulness (مشروعية) of the rulings attributed to them that were well-known among them.

Although there is disagreement regarding the meaning of the names of these four categories of livestock—which leads to disagreement in identifying their specific rulings, as you will see—it is certain that their rulings were based on a type of liberation and veneration by respecting their condition. Three of them—the bahirah (البحيرة), the sa’ibah (السائبة), and the ham (الحامي)—pertain to camels, while one—the wasilah (الوصيلة)—pertains to sheep.

As for the bahirah (البحيرة), it is mentioned in al-Majma‘ that it was a she-camel that, if she gave birth to five litters and the last was a male, they would slit her ear (بحروا أذنها) — that is, they made a wide incision in it — and they would refrain from riding or slaughtering her. She would not be driven away from water nor barred from pasture, and if an exhausted traveler encountered her, he would not ride her; this is narrated from al-Zajjaj.

It is also said that if a she-camel gave birth to five litters, they would look at the fifth litter: if it was a male, they slaughtered it and both men and women ate it; if it was a female, they slit her ear, and she was the bahirah. Thereafter, her wool would not be shorn, the Name of Allah would not be mentioned over her if she were slaughtered, she would not be used for carrying loads, and women were forbidden from tasting any of her milk or benefiting from her. Her milk and benefits were exclusively for men until she died; upon her death, both men and women shared in eating her. This is narrated from Ibn Abbas. It is also said that the bahirah is the daughter of the sa’ibah, as narrated from Muhammad ibn Ishaq.

As for the sa’ibah (السائبة), it is mentioned in al-Majma‘ that it refers to what they used to set free (يسيبونه). If a man vowed to return from a journey or recover from an illness or the like, he would say: "My she-camel is a sa’ibah." She would then be like the bahirah in that she was not utilized, nor kept from water, nor barred from pasture. This is from al-Zajjaj and is the view of ‘Alqamah.

It is also said that she is the one set free for the idols—meaning emancipated for them. A man would set free whatever he wished of his wealth and bring it to the custodians (السدنة) — the servants of their gods — who would then feed her milk to wayfarers and the like. This is from Ibn Abbas and Ibn Mas‘ud.

It is also said that the sa’ibah is a she-camel that, if she gave birth to ten consecutive females without a male among them, she was set free. They would not ride her, shear her wool, or drink her milk except for a guest. Whatever female she gave birth to after that would have its ear slit and then be left free with its mother; that offspring was the bahirah. This is from Muhammad ibn Ishaq.

As for the wasilah (الوصيلة), it is mentioned in al-Majma‘ that it pertains to sheep. If a ewe gave birth to a female, it was for them, and if she gave birth to a male, they designated it for their gods. But if she gave birth to a male and a female together, they would say: "She has reached (وصلت) her brother," and they would not slaughter the male for their gods. This is from al-Zajjaj.

It is also said that if a ewe gave birth to seven litters, and the seventh was a male, they slaughtered it for their gods and its meat was for men only. If it was a female, they let it live and it remained among the sheep. If she gave birth in the seventh litter to both a male and a female, they said: "The sister has reached her brother due to his sanctity (حرمته) over us," so both became forbidden. Thus, the benefit and milk were for men and not women. This is from Ibn Mas‘ud and Muqatil.

It is also said that the wasilah is a ewe that, if she gave birth to ten females in five litters without a male among them, she was made a wasilah. They would say: "She has reached (وصلت)," and whatever she gave birth to after that was for males and not females. This is from Muhammad ibn Ishaq.

As for the ham (الحامي), it is mentioned in al-Majma‘ that it is the male camel. When ten litters had been sired from the stallion's loins, the Arabs would say: "He has protected (حمى) his back," so he would not be used for carrying loads, nor barred from water or pasture. This is from Ibn Abbas and Ibn Mas‘ud, and it is the view of Abu ‘Ubaydah and al-Zajjaj.

It is also said that it is the stallion that, when its grandson is sired from it, they say: "He has protected his back," so he is not ridden. This is from al-Farra’.

Although they differ in the interpretation of these names, it is highly probable that this difference arises from the varying tastes (سلائق) of different peoples in their customs (سنن); such variations are common in the customs prevalent among primitive (همجية) peoples.

In any case, the verse is directed at denying the rulings they had fabricated for these four categories of livestock, attributing them to Allah, the Glorified. This is evidenced first by His saying: ما جَعَلَ اللهُ (Allah has not prescribed...), and second by His saying: وَلكِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللهِ الْكَذِبَ (but the faithless fabricate lies against Allah) [5:103].

Therefore, His saying: وَلكِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (but the faithless...) serves as an answer to a presumed question. It is as if when it was said: ما جَعَلَ اللهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ (Allah has not prescribed any bahirah...), it was asked: "Then what is this that these disbelievers claim?" The answer was that it is a fabrication of lies against Allah on their part. Then, the explanation was furthered by the saying: وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ (and most of them do not apply reason) [5:103]. Its meaning is that they differ in this fabrication: most of them fabricate what they fabricate while they do not apply reason (لا يَعْقِلُونَ), while a few of these fabricators do understand the truth and know that what they attribute to Him, the Exalted, is a fabrication. These are the followed leaders who are obeyed by others and who manage their affairs; they are the people of obstinacy (عناد) and persistence in falsehood (لجاج).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا إِلى ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ (And when it is said to them, ‘Come to what Allah has sent down...) [5:104] to the end of the verse. This recounts the invitation to what Allah sent down to the Messenger, whose sole duty is delivery (البلاغ). Thus, the invitation is an invitation to the Truth (الحق), which is veracity (الصدق) free from fabrication, and knowledge (العلم) purified from ignorance. For the previous verse combined fabrication and lack of reasoning on their side, leaving nothing for that which they are invited to—namely, the side of Allah, the Glorified—except veracity and knowledge.

However, they did not repel it except through blind imitation (التقليد), as they said: حَسْبُنا ما وَجَدْنا عَلَيْهِ آباءَنا (Sufficient for us is what we found our fathers following) [5:104]. Although imitation (التقليد) — the following of another's authority without independent proof — is correct in certain instances and under certain conditions—namely, the recourse of the ignorant to the learned, which is a principle established in the conduct of human society in all life's affairs where it is not feasible for an individual to obtain direct knowledge of the vital path he must tread—the imitation of an ignorant person in his ignorance, meaning the recourse of an ignorant person to another ignorant person like himself, is blameworthy in the way of the rational (سنة العقلاء). Just as it is blameworthy for a learned person to turn to another learned person by abandoning what he can independently act upon and taking what another knows.

Therefore, Allah, the Exalted, rejected it with His saying: أَوَلَوْ كانَ آباؤُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ (What, even if their fathers knew nothing and were not guided?) [5:104]. Its meaning is that reason—if reason were present—does not permit a person to turn to one who possesses neither knowledge (علم) nor guidance (اهتداء). This is the law of life: it does not permit treading a path whose dangers are not secured, and whose description is unknown, neither through independence nor by following one who has expertise in it.

Perhaps the addition of His saying: وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ (and were not guided) to His saying: لا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً (knew nothing) is to complete the necessary conditions of the discourse according to the reality. For the recourse of the ignorant to his like, though blameworthy, is only so if the one followed is exactly like the follower in his ignorance, not distinguished from him by anything. However, if the followed one himself treads the path by the guidance of a learned expert, then he is guided (مهتدٍ) in his conduct, and there is no blame on the one who follows him in his journey and imitates him in treading the path. For the matter ultimately traces back to knowledge (العلم) in the end, like one who follows a person knowledgeable about the path, and then another ignorant person follows him.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: أَوَلَوْ كانَ آباؤُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً (What, even if their fathers knew nothing) would not have been sufficient to complete the proof (الحجة) against them, due to the possibility that their fathers whom they followed in imitation were guided through the imitation of guiding scholars. Thus, the ruling of blame would not apply to them, nor would the proof be completed against them. This was refuted by stating that their fathers knew nothing and were not guided, and there is no justification for following one in such a state.

Since it was established from the first verse—namely His saying: ما جَعَلَ اللهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ (Allah has not prescribed any bahirah...)—that they are divided between those who do not reason at all (and they are the majority) and those who are obstinate and arrogant, it follows that they are far removed from being worthy of direct address or the presentation of proof. Therefore, the proof in the second verse was not presented to them in the form of a direct address; rather, the speech was directed toward others, turning away from facing them directly, as it was said: أَوَلَوْ كانَ آباؤُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ (What, even if their fathers knew nothing and were not guided?).

A scholarly and ethical discussion on the meaning of imitation (التقليد) has already been presented in the first volume of this work, which you may refer to.

It is clear from the verse that turning to the Book of Allah and to His Messenger—which is turning to the Sunnah (السنة) — the established practice and teachings of the Prophet — is not in any way part of blameworthy imitation.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from al-Saduq, with his chain of narration to Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: ما جَعَلَ اللهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ وَلا سائِبَةٍ وَلا وَصِيلَةٍ وَلا حامٍ (Allah has not prescribed any bahirah, or sa’ibah, or wasilah, or ham). He said: "The people of the Age of Ignorance, if a she-camel gave birth to two offspring in one litter, they would say: 'She has reached (وصلت),' so they would not deem her slaughter or eating lawful. If she gave birth to ten, they made her a sa’ibah, and they would not deem her back [for riding] or her eating lawful. The ham was the stallion of the camels; they would not deem it lawful. So Allah sent down that He had not forbidden any of that."

He said: Then Ibn Babawayh said: "It has been narrated that the bahirah is the she-camel that, if she produced five litters—and if the fifth was a male—they slaughtered it and both men and women ate it.
