"Furthermore, among these people are those who see the soul as nothing but a material, natural thing like blood, or the vaporous spirit (الروح البخاري), or the original parts; and those who see the soul as a subtle body (جسم لطيف) resembling the elemental body and dwelling within it, being the carrier of life. How then is it permissible to say that everyone aims thereby at the matter of self-knowledge?"

However, you should remember what was previously mentioned: that the human being, in all these positions where he performs actions that divert the soul from preoccupation with external matters and diverse material enjoyments toward itself to obtain properties and effects that material causes and ordinary natural factors cannot reach, intends nothing but separation from external causes and independence with his self to obtain specific results that ordinary material factors have no way to.

The religious person who is ascetic in his religion sees that it is a human duty to choose for himself his true happiness, which is the good life of the Hereafter for those who believe in the Resurrection (المعاد), and the happy worldly life that gathers good for him and repels evil for those who deny it, such as the idolaters and the adherents of transmigration. Then he sees that indulgence in animalistic enjoyments does not secure his happiness nor lead him to his goal. Thus, he has no choice but to reject whims and cease the pursuit of everything his soul craves through its ordinary causes in general, and to be drawn toward a cause or causes above the ordinary material causes by drawing near to it and connecting with it. He sees that this proximity and connection are only attained by submission to it and surrender to its command, and that is a spiritual, psychological matter (أمر روحي نفساني) that is only preserved through bodily actions and abstentions. This is religious worship (العبادة الدينية), consisting of prayer, ritual, or what pertains to that.

Thus, religious actions, struggles (المجاهدات), and disciplines all return to a kind of preoccupation with the matter of the soul. The human being sees by primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — that he does not take anything nor leave anything except for the benefit of his self. It has already been stated that the human being is never devoid, even for a moment of his existence, of observing his self and the presence of his essence (حضور ذاته), and that he never errs in this consciousness at all. If he errs, he only errs in its interpretation according to theoretical opinion and intellectual research. Thus, it has become clear through this explanation that religions and doctrines, despite the diversity of their practices and paths, aim at nothing but preoccupation with the matter of the soul in general, whether their adherents know it or not.

Likewise, the practitioner of disciplines and struggles, even if he is not an adherent of a religion nor a believer in the reality of the soul, intends by the type of discipline he undergoes nothing but the acquisition of its promised result. The promised result is not linked to the actions and abstentions he performs by a natural link similar to the link existing between natural causes and their effects; rather, it is a volitional, non-material link related to the consciousness of the practitioner and his will, which are preserved by the type of action he performs, circulating between the soul of the practitioner and the promised result. Thus, the reality of the mentioned discipline is the strengthening of the soul and its perfection in its consciousness and will for the required result. If you wish, you could say: the effect of the discipline is that the soul obtains a state of knowledge (العلم) that the objective is within its power. When the discipline is sound and complete, it becomes such that if the soul wills the objective absolutely, or wills it under specific conditions — such as summoning the spirit for a pre-pubescent boy in a mirror — the objective is achieved.

To this category belongs the meaning of what is narrated: أنه ذكر عند النبي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله : أن بعض أصحاب عيسى عليه‌السلام كان يمشي على الماء ـ فقال صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله : لو كان يقينه أشد من ذلك لمشى على الهواء — "It was mentioned in the presence of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that some of the companions of Jesus (عليه السلام) used to walk on water. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Had his certainty (يقينه) been stronger than that, he would have walked on air.'"

The hadith — as you see — points out that the matter revolves around certainty (اليقين) in Allah, the Glorified, and the erasure of cosmic causes from independent influence. To whatever extent a person's reliance on the absolute divine power reaches, things will submit to him according to that measure; so understand that.

Among the most comprehensive sayings in this regard is the saying of Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): ما ضعف بدن عما قويت عليه النية — "A body does not weaken from what the intention (النية) is strong upon." And the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said in the mutawatir hadith: إنما الأعمال بالنيات — "Actions are only by intentions."

It has thus become clear that the religious effects of actions and worships, as well as the effects of disciplines and struggles, only establish their link between them and the human soul through its inner affairs. Therefore, engaging in any of them is an engagement with the matter of the soul.

Whoever imagines that the link of causality (السببية والمسببية) is only between the physical forms of these actions and the otherworldly goals, for example — such as ease, fragrance, and the Garden of Bliss — or between them and the strange worldly goals in which natural causes do not operate — such as influencing the perceptions of souls and types of their wills, movements without a mover, knowledge of inner thoughts and future events, and connection with spiritual beings and spirits, and the like — or imagines that the action entails the effect without a real link or by mere divine will without a specificator, has indeed deceived himself.

6 — Beware lest the matter become confused for you, leading you to conclude from the previous discussions that religion is gnosis (العرفان) and Sufism (التصوف) — meaning self-knowledge — as some materialist researchers have imagined, dividing the life path prevalent among people into two parts: materialism and gnosis, which is religion.

This is because what religion is based upon is that the human being has a true happiness (سعادة حقيقية) that he cannot attain except by submission to what is beyond nature and rejecting the limitation to material enjoyments. The previous discussions have produced the result that religions — whatever they may be, true or false — employ in the cultivation of people and leading them to the happiness they promise and invite them to, the reform and refinement of the soul in a manner suitable for the objective. Where is this from the claim that self-knowledge is religion?

Religion calls to the worship of the Deity, the Glorified, without a mediator or through intercessors and partners, because in it lies human happiness and the good life (الحياة الطيبة) beyond which there is no goal for man. Man does not and will not attain it except with a soul that is pure and purified from the stains of attachment to material things and unbridled animalistic enjoyments. Thus, the need arose to include within the parts of its call the reform and purification of the soul, so that the adherent raised in its lap may be prepared to be clothed in goodness and happiness, and not be like one who takes a thing with one hand and pushes it away with the other. Thus, religion is one thing, and self-knowledge is another thing beyond it, even if religion necessitates gnosis in a certain way.

By a similar explanation, it becomes clear that the paths of discipline and struggle followed for various purposes strange to habit are also other than self-knowledge, even if some are linked to others in a certain way.

Yes, we can conclude one thing: that self-knowledge (عرفان النفس), by whatever path its pursuit is assumed, is a matter taken from religion. Just as exhaustive and free research shows that religions, despite their diversity and dispersion, have branched out from a single, ancient religion to which the human primordial nature (الفطرة) invites, which is the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد).

For if we return to our simple primordial nature, disregarding the prejudices that have occurred to us through inheritance from our ancestors or contagion from our peers, we would not doubt that the world — despite its unity in its multiplicity and the connection of its parts in the very essence of their dispersion — ends at a single cause above all causes. This is the Truth (الحق) to whose side submission is obligatory, and according to whose management and cultivation the life path must be arranged. This is the religion based on monotheism.

Deep contemplation of all religions and sects shows that they contain this living spirit in some way, even idolatry and dualism. The difference only occurred in the application of the religious practice to this principle, and in being correct or erroneous regarding it. Some say, for example: He is closer to us than the jugular vein, and He is with us wherever we are, and we have no guardian or intercessor besides Him; thus, it is obligatory to worship Him alone without association. Others say: The terrestrial debasement of man and the vileness of his essence do not leave him a way to connect with that Presence — and where is the dust compared to the Lord of lords? Thus, it is obligatory that we draw near to some of His noble servants who are stripped of the cloak of matter and are pure and purified from the stains of nature; these are the spiritualities of the planets, or the lords of species (أرباب الأنواع), or those close to man, and ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللهِ زُلْفى — "We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah" (al-Zumar: 3).

Since they are absent from our senses and transcendent beyond our directions, it was necessary that we embody them with monuments and idols so that the matter of worshipful proximity may be completed. By this analogy, in other religions and nations, we find in their cores nothing but what is, in reality, a way of directing toward the monotheism of the Deity, exalted is His Name.

It is well known that the practices circulating among people, however they may branch out and however intensely they may differ, tend toward unity if we return to their previous eras, and end finally at the religion of the simple human primordial nature, which is monotheism. Thus, the religion of monotheism is the father of religions, and they are its children, whether righteous or corrupt.

Furthermore, the primordial religion only considers the matter of self-knowledge to reach through it the human happiness to which it invites, which is the knowledge of the Deity (معرفة الإله), which is the ultimate objective for it. In other words, religion only invites to self-knowledge as a path-oriented invitation (دعوة طريقية), not as an end-oriented one (غائية). For the religious taste does not accept preoccupation with a matter except in the way of servitude (العبودية). Indeed, the religion with Allah is Islam, and He does not approve of disbelief for His servants; how then would He approve of self-knowledge if it were independent in its desirability?

From here it appears that gnosis (العرفان) ends at the origin of the primordial religion, as it is not in itself an independent matter to which the human primordial nature invites, such that its branches and boughs would end at a single origin which is primordial gnosis.

It is possible to find support for this in another matter: that although humanity was driven by primordial nature toward society and civilization to make life happy, and transmission and research have established that men or social groups called to national ways or established social practices and implemented them among their nations — such as tribal practices, monarchical practices, democracy, and the like — it has not been established by transmission or research that anyone other than the people of religion has called to self-knowledge and the refinement of character throughout human history.

Yes, it is possible that some practitioners of these non-religious paths — such as the masters of sorcery and spirits and the like — only became aware of this type of self-knowledge through a path other than religion, but not from the direction of primordial nature, for primordial nature has no judgment in that as you have known. Rather, it was from the direction of observing some strange psychological effects by chance, so the soul yearns to attain a psychological station by which it possesses wondrous actions and rare influences in the cosmos that souls find strange. This yearning then drives it to search for it and journey toward it, and then journey after journey paves the way to the objective and eases the ruggedness of it.

7 — It is narrated from many of our righteous people among the people of religion that they attained, during their religious struggles, supernatural miracles (كرامات خارقة للعادة) and strange events that were specific to them among their peers — such as the representation of matters to their sight that were hidden from the sight of others, the observation of persons or events that the senses of people below them do not observe, the answering of supplication, the healing of the sick for whom there is no hope of successful treatment, and salvation from dangers and perils through non-ordinary ways. Similar things may happen to those other than the righteous if they possess a sincere intention and a detached soul (نفس منقطعة). These people see what they see while being heedless of its proximate cause; they only attribute that to Allah, the Glorified, without the mediation of a medium. Attributing matters to Him, the Exalted, although it is a truth that must be acknowledged, the denial of intermediate causes (الأسباب المتوسطة) is something for which there is no hope.

Sometimes a spiritualist summons the spirit of one of the people in a mirror or water and the like by influencing the soul of a boy — as is customary. He, like others, sees that the boy only perceives it with physical sight, and that between the sight of other observers and the summoned spirit there is a drawn veil which, if removed, would make them like the boy in attaining its observation.

Sometimes they find that the summoned spirits lie in their reports, which is strange because the world of spirits is a world of purity and clarity where there is no way for lying, fabrication, or falsehood.

Sometimes they summon the spirit of a living person and interrogate it regarding its secrets and inner thoughts, while the owner of the spirit is in a state of wakefulness, occupied with his daily tasks and needs, having no news that his spirit is summoned and interrogated, uttering speech that he would not approve of uttering.

Sometimes a person is put into a hypnotic sleep and then indoctrinated with an act until he graciously accepts it. When he is awakened and goes about his business, he performs the act he was indoctrinated with according to the condition intended for it, while being heedless of what they indoctrinated him with and of his gracious acceptance of it.

Some spiritualists, when they observed spiritual images resembling human images or images of some animals, thought that these images are in the world of matter and the vessel of changing nature — especially some of those who do not see existence for anything other than material matters. Some of these even tried to invent industrial tools to catch spirits, all based on a hypothesis they assumed regarding the soul: that it is a material principle or a property of a material principle that acts with consciousness and will, even though they have not solved the problem of life and consciousness to this day.

Similar to this hypothesis is the hypothesis of those who see that the spirit is a subtle body (جسم لطيف) resembling the elemental body in its forms and shapes, because they found that a person sees himself in a dream while in his form of wakefulness. Sometimes the images of their own selves are represented to the masters of struggles in front of them outside their bodies, perfectly resembling the bodily image. They thus judged that the spirit is a subtle body dwelling in the elemental body as long as the human being is alive, and when it departs the body, that is death.

They missed the fact that this is a perceptual image (صورة إدراكية) subsisting in the consciousness of the human being, similar to the image he perceives of his body, and similar to the images of all other external things separate from his body. Sometimes this separate image appears to some masters of struggle as more than one, or in a form other than the form of his own self. Sometimes he sees himself as the very self of another individual. If they do not judge these mentioned images to be the image of the spirit, then it is appropriate for them not to judge the single resembling image that appears to the masters of struggles to be the image of the spirit.

The reality of the matter is that these people attained something of the knowledges of the soul but missed the knowledge of its reality as it is; thus, they erred in interpreting what they attained and went astray in directing its matter. The truth to which proof and experience lead is that the reality of the soul — which is this rational consciousness (الشعور المتعقل) expressed by our saying "I" — is a matter distinct in its essence (جوهره) from these material matters as previously stated. Furthermore, the categories of its consciousness and types of its perceptions — whether sense, imagination, or intellection — from the aspect of being perceived objects, are established in its world and vessel, other than the natural properties occurring in the organs of sense and perception of the body, for those are material acts and passions devoid in themselves of life and consciousness. Thus, these observed matters specific to the righteous and the masters of struggles and disciplines are not outside the domain of their souls. The question is only how these pieces of information and knowledges were established in the soul and where their place is within it? And that the soul has a causal mark (سمة علية) for all events and matters linked to it in some way. All these strange matters that submit to the people of discipline and struggle only draw nourishment from their will and wish, and the will arises from consciousness. Thus, human consciousness has an entry into all events linked to it and matters touching it.

8 — It is appropriate to divide those engaged in self-knowledge in general into two groups: one group consists of those engaged in attaining some of the strange effects of the soul that are outside the normal range of material causes and effects, such as the masters of sorcery, talismans, and those engaged in the subjugation of the spiritualities of the planets and those entrusted with affairs, jinn, and human spirits, and the masters of supplications and incantations, and the like.

The second group consists of those engaged in self-knowledge by turning away from matters external to it and being drawn toward it to delve into it and observe its essence and its affairs, such as the Sufis (المتصوفة) in their various ranks and paths.

Sufism is not something that Muslims invented from themselves, as it is found among nations that preceded them in emergence, such as the Christians and others, even the idolaters among the Brahmins and Buddhists. Among them are those who follow the path to this day; rather, it is an inherited path they inherited from their ancestors.

However, this is not in the sense of ordinary taking and imitation, like people inheriting the colors of civilization from one another — a later nation from an earlier one — as several researchers in religions and doctrines have claimed. This is because you have known in the previous chapters that the primordial religion leads to asceticism, and asceticism guides to self-knowledge. Thus, the establishment of religion among a nation and its taking root in their hearts prepares and readies them for a path of self-knowledge to inevitably emerge among them. Some of those for whom the necessitating factors are complete will take it up. Thus, the remaining of religious life in a nation for a significant period inevitably creates among them this path, whether sound or corrupt, even if they are entirely cut off from other religious nations. That which is of this nature should not be counted among the inherited practices that one generation takes from another.

9 — Then we should divide the adherents of the second group of the two previous groups — who are the people of gnosis in reality — into two groups:

One group follows the path for its own sake, so they are granted something of its knowledges without the complete knowledge of it being finalized for them. This is because, since they do not intend other than the soul, they are in heedlessness of the matter of its Maker, who is Allah (عز اسمه), the True Cause who takes hold of the forelock of the soul in its existence and the effects of its existence. How can a person attain complete knowledge of a thing while being oblivious to the knowledge of the causes of its existence, especially the Cause who is the Cause of every cause? Is he not like one who claims knowledge of the bed while being ignorant of the carpenter, his adze, his saw, and his purpose in making it, among other causes of the bed's existence?

It is appropriate for this type of self-knowledge to be called divination (كهانة) due to what it entails of obtaining some of the sciences of the soul and its effects.

The other group intends the path of self-knowledge to be a means for them to the knowledge of the Lord, the Exalted. Their path is the one that religion approves of in general, which is that a person engages in the knowledge of his self insofar as it is a sign (آية) among the signs of his Lord, and the closest sign. Thus, the soul is a traversed path, and Allah, the Glorified, is the Goal toward which one journeys: وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى — "and that the terminus is toward your Lord" (al-Najm: 42).

These are various groups with dispersed doctrines among nations and sects. We do not have much expertise in the doctrines of non-Muslims among them and the paths they follow. As for the Muslims, their paths in this are many, perhaps reaching twenty-five chains (سلسلة) according to the origins, from each of which other particular chains branch out. They have traced them back — except for one — to Ali (عليه أفضل السلام). There are men among them who do not belong to any of these chains and are called the Uwaysis (أويسية) — in reference to Uways al-Qarani — and there are others among them who do not take a name nor manifest a slogan.

They have written books and treatises in which they have translated their chains and paths, the laws and etiquettes they have, and their men. They have recorded therein what is narrated of their unveilings (مكاشفاتهم) and expressed their proofs and objectives upon which they built them. Whoever wishes to stand upon them should refer to them. As for the research into the details of the paths and ways, and the correction of the sound and the critique of the corrupt, it has another place. A discussion that is not devoid of benefit in this regard has already preceded in the fifth volume of this book. This is the summary of what we intended to present of the research related to the meaning of self-knowledge.

Know that self-knowledge is a practical quest (بغية عملية) for which complete knowledge is not obtained except through the path of practical journeying (السلوك العملي) rather than the theoretical. As for the psychology (علم النفس) that the masters of theory among the ancients compiled, it does not avail anything in this regard. Likewise, the practical art of the soul that the moderns have recently compiled is only a branch of the art of ethics (فن الأخلاق) as the ancients compiled it. And Allah is the Guide.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ شَهَـٰدَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ حِينَ ٱلْوَصِيَّةِ ٱثْنَانِ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ أَوْ ءَاخَرَانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ إِنْ أَنتُمْ ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۚ تَحْبِسُونَهُمَا مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِٱللَّهِ إِنِ ٱرْتَبْتُمْ لَا نَشْتَرِى بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ ۙ وَلَا نَكْتُمُ شَهَـٰدَةَ ٱللَّهِ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْـَٔاثِمِينَ (١٠٦)
O you who have faith! The witness between you, when death approaches any of you, while making a bequest, shall be two fair men from among yourselves—or two from among others, if you are journeying in the land and the affliction of death visits you. You shall detain the two of them after the prayer, and, if you have any doubt, they shall vow by Allah, ‘We will not sell it for any gain, even if it were a relative, nor will we conceal the testimony of Allah, for then we would indeed be among the sinners.’ (106)
فَإِنْ عُثِرَ عَلَىٰٓ أَنَّهُمَا ٱسْتَحَقَّآ إِثْمًا فَـَٔاخَرَانِ يَقُومَانِ مَقَامَهُمَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَوْلَيَـٰنِ فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِٱللَّهِ لَشَهَـٰدَتُنَآ أَحَقُّ مِن شَهَـٰدَتِهِمَا وَمَا ٱعْتَدَيْنَآ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٠٧)
But if it is found that both of them were guilty of a sin, then two others shall stand up in their place from among those nearest in kinship to the claimants and swear by Allah: ‘Our testimony is surely truer than their testimony, and we have not transgressed, for then we would indeed be among the wrongdoers.’ (107)
ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهَآ أَوْ يَخَافُوٓا۟ أَن تُرَدَّ أَيْمَـٰنٌۢ بَعْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (١٠٨)
That makes it likelier that they give the testimony in its genuine form, or fear that other oaths will be taken after their oaths. Be wary of Allah and listen, and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (108)
۞ يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ مَاذَآ أُجِبْتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ (١٠٩)
The day Allah will gather the apostles and say, ‘What was the response to you?’ They will say, ‘We have no knowledge. Indeed You are knower of all that is Unseen.’ (109)

(Commentary)

The first three verses concern testimony (الشهادة), and the last one is not without some connection to them in terms of meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: «يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا شَهادَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ» — O you who have faith! The witness between you, when death approaches any of you at the time of making a will, shall be two honest men from among you... (5:106) until the end of the two verses. The summary of the content of these two verses is that if one of them is on a journey and wishes to make a will, he must have two just witnesses from among the Muslims present at the time of the will. If he does not find any, then two others from among the non-Muslims of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). If the guardians of the deceased harbor doubts regarding the matter of the will, the two witnesses are detained after the prayer and they swear by Allah to their truthfulness in what they testify, and thereby the dispute is settled. If it is discovered that the two witnesses lied in their testimony or committed treachery in the matter, two other witnesses are appointed in place of the first two to testify against them and swear by Allah to that effect.

This is what the two verses indicate by their literal meaning. Thus, His saying: «يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا» (O you who have faith) is an address to the believers, and the ruling is specific to them. «شَهادَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ حِينَ الْوَصِيَّةِ اثْنانِ ذَوا عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ» (The witness between you, when death approaches any of you at the time of making a will, shall be two honest men from among you) means: the testimony between you [shall be by] two honest men from among you. Thus, in the predicate (الخبر) part, there is an elided genitive (مضاف مقدر), or [the meaning is] "witnesses between you [shall be] two honest men from among you." The intent is that the number of witnesses is two. The verbal noun (المصدر) — testimony (الشهادة) — is used in the sense of the active participle (اسم الفاعل), similar to their saying: "a man [who is] justice" (رجل عدل) and "two men [who are] justice" (رجلان عدل).

The "approach of death" (حضور الموت) is a metaphor for the presence of the motive for making a will, for people, by their nature, do not occupy themselves with such matters without the presence of something that necessitates the suspicion of death, which is usually a severe illness by which a person overlooks death.

His saying: «حِينَ الْوَصِيَّةِ» (at the time of making a will) is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) related to "testimony," meaning: the testimony at the time of the will. What is meant by justice (العدل) — which is a verbal noun — is uprightness (الاستقامة) in an affair. The context (قرينة المقام) indicates that what is meant is uprightness in the matter of religion (الدين). Consequently, it becomes certain that what is meant by His saying: «مِنْكُمْ» (from among you) and His saying: «مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ» (from others) is Muslims and non-Muslims, rather than kinship or clan. This is because Allah, the Glorified, contrasted His saying: «اثْنانِ» (two) with His saying: «آخَرانِ» (two others), then described the first by His saying: «ذَوا عَدْلٍ» (two honest men) and «مِنْكُمْ» (from among you), while He did not describe the second except by His saying: «مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ» (from others) without describing them with justice. Being characterized by uprightness in religion or its lack only differs between a Muslim and a non-Muslim; there is no reason to require justice in witnesses if they are kin or from the clan of the one testified for, while waiving it if the witness is a stranger.

Based on this, His saying: «أَوْ آخَرانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ» (or two others from others) is an alternative based on order; that is, if there are Muslims present, two of them are taken as witnesses, and if there are none but non-Muslims, two of them are taken as witnesses—all of this is derived from the context.

This same context is what necessitates that His saying: «إِنْ أَنْتُمْ ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَأَصابَتْكُمْ مُصِيبَةُ الْمَوْتِ» (if you are journeying in the land and the calamity of death befalls you) be a condition related to His saying: «أَوْ آخَرانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ» (or two others from others). This is because a Muslim, by nature, lives in a Muslim society, so there is usually no need when at home (الحضر) to seek testimony from two non-Muslim witnesses, unlike the state of travel and journeying in the land (الضرب في الأرض), for it is a situation where such events, necessity, and the urgent need to benefit from a non-Muslim through testimony or otherwise are expected to occur.

The context—meaning the suitability between the ruling and the subject (الموضوع) according to the taste derived from the speech of the Exalted—indicates that what is meant by non-Muslims is specifically the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), because the speech of the Exalted does not honor polytheists (المشركين) with such dignity.

His saying, the Exalted: «تَحْبِسُونَهُما مِنْ بَعْدِ الصَّلاةِ» (you shall detain them after the prayer) means you stop them; "detention" (الحبس) is stopping. «فَيُقْسِمانِ بِاللهِ» (they shall both swear by Allah) refers to the two witnesses. «إِنِ ارْتَبْتُمْ» (if you doubt) means if you doubt what the executor (الوصي) manifests regarding the matter of the will, or the property to which the will pertains, or the manner of the will. That which is sworn upon is His saying:

«لا نَشْتَرِي بِهِ ثَمَناً وَلَوْ كانَ ذا قُرْبى» (‘We will not sell it for any price, even if it were a relative...) meaning: we will not sell the testimony for the executor in what he claims for a small price, even if he were a relative. Selling for a small price through testimony is for the witness to deviate in his testimony from the truth for a worldly goal—be it wealth, status, or the emotion of kinship—thus offering his testimony in exchange for a worldly price, which is the "small price."

Some have mentioned that the pronoun in His saying: «بِهِ» (it) refers to the oath (اليمين), meaning: we will not sell our oath for a price. This would necessitate performing the oath twice, but the verse is far from indicating that.

His saying: «وَلا نَكْتُمُ شَهادَةَ اللهِ» (‘...nor will we conceal the testimony of Allah...) meaning: by testifying contrary to reality. «إِنَّا إِذاً لَمِنَ الْآثِمِينَ» (‘...for then we would indeed be among the sinners.’) those who bear sin (الإثم). The sentence is coordinated with His saying: «لا نَشْتَرِي بِهِ ثَمَناً» (‘We will not sell it for any price...) as an explanatory coordination (عطف التفسير).

The attribution of testimony to Allah in His saying: "the testimony of Allah" (شَهادَةَ اللهِ) is either because the reality is witnessed by Allah, the Glorified, just as it is witnessed by the two witnesses; thus it is His testimony, the Glorified, just as it is their testimony. Allah has the greater right to the possession [of the testimony], so it is His testimony, the Exalted, in truth and by essence (بالأصالة), and their testimony by subordination (تبعاً). The Exalted has said: ...and Allah suffices as a witness. (4:79) and the Exalted said: ...and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes. (2:255).

Or it is because testimony is a right established by Allah upon His servants, which they must uphold in its proper form without distortion or concealment. This is like saying: "the religion of Allah" (دين الله), where the religion is attributed to Him, the Exalted, even though it is the servants who are characterized by it. The Exalted said: ...and maintain the testimony for the sake of Allah. (65:2) and said: ...and do not conceal the testimony. (2:283).

His saying: «فَإِنْ عُثِرَ عَلى أَنَّهُمَا اسْتَحَقَّا إِثْماً» (But if it is discovered that the two have committed a sin...) "Discovering" (العثور) something means coming upon it and finding it. This verse is an explanation and detail of the ruling in the case where the treachery of the two witnesses and their lying in their testimony becomes apparent.

What is meant by "committing a sin" (استحقاق الإثم) is criminality and offense. It is said: "the man deserved" (استحق الرجل), meaning he sinned. "So-and-so deserved a sin against so-and-so" is a metaphor for his criminality and offense against him. Therefore, it is made transitive with "against" (على) in His saying at the end: «اسْتَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَوْلَيانِ» (...against whom the two nearest [relatives]...), meaning they committed a crime and offense against them through lying and treachery. The original meaning of our saying: "the man deserved" (استحق الرجل) is that he sought that the sin or punishment be realized and established in him; thus its metaphorical use is of the type of mentioning the seeking while intending the sought, and placing the path in the place of the goal. Sin (الإثم) was mentioned in His saying: «اسْتَحَقَّا إِثْماً» (...the two have committed a sin) based on what preceded in His saying: «إِنَّا إِذاً لَمِنَ الْآثِمِينَ» (‘...for then we would indeed be among the sinners.’).

His saying, the Exalted: «فَآخَرانِ يَقُومانِ مَقامَهُما» (...then two others shall stand in their place...) means: if it is discovered that the two witnesses deserved [punishment] due to lying and treachery, then two other witnesses shall stand in their place in the oath regarding their testimony against them for lying and treachery.

His saying: «مِنَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَوْلَيانِ» (...from among those against whom the two nearest [relatives]...) is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال), meaning: while these two new ones are from those against whom the first two witnesses—who were the two nearest (الأوليان) to the deceased in terms of the will—committed a crime and offense, as mentioned by al-Razi in his Tafsir. What is meant by "those against whom the two nearest committed a crime" are the guardians of the deceased. The summary of the meaning is: if it is discovered that the two witnesses committed a crime against the guardians of the deceased through treachery and lying, then two other witnesses shall stand from the guardians of the deceased against whom the first two witnesses—who were the nearest to the deceased—committed a crime before their deserving of sin became apparent.

This is according to the reading of «اسْتَحَقَّ» (deserved) in the active voice (البناء للفاعل), which is the reading of ‘Asim according to the narration of Hafs. As for the reading of the majority, «اسْتُحِقَّ» (was deserved) in the passive voice (البناء للمفعول), the apparent context is that "the two nearest" (الأوليان) is the subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is His saying: «فَآخَرانِ يَقُومانِ» (...then two others shall stand...) etc., which was placed before it due to the concern for it. The meaning is: if it is discovered that they committed a sin, then the two nearest to the deceased are two others who stand in their place from among his guardians against whom the crime was committed.

In the reading of ‘Asim through the path of Abu Bakr, Hamzah, Khalaf, and Ya‘qub, it is "the first ones" (الأولين), the plural of "the first" (الأول) as opposed to "the last" (الآخر). It apparently means the guardians and those foremost, serving as an adjective or a substitute (بدل) for His saying: «الَّذِينَ» (those).

The exegetes have mentioned very many ways regarding the grammatical structure of the parts of the verse; if some were multiplied by others to obtain the full meaning of the verse, they would reach hundreds of forms. Al-Zajjaj mentioned, as narrated from him: "It is the most problematic verse in the Book of Allah in terms of structure."

What we have presented of the meaning is what is apparent from the context of the wording without affectation in understanding. We have refrained from exhausting the possibilities they mentioned because their multiplication only increases the wording in ambiguity and the researcher in confusion. (1)

(1) Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the seventh volume of Ruh al-Ma‘ani by al-Alusi, Majma‘ al-Bayan, the Tafsir of al-Razi, and other lengthy works.

His saying: «فَيُقْسِمانِ بِاللهِ» (...they shall both swear by Allah...) — referring to the two other witnesses from the guardians of the deceased — is branched from His saying: «فَآخَرانِ يَقُومانِ» (...then two others shall stand...) etc., as the branching of the goal from that which possesses the goal. «لَشَهادَتُنا» (‘Our testimony...) regarding what includes their lying and treachery «أَحَقُّ مِنْ شَهادَتِهِما» (‘...is more truthful than their testimony...) meaning: than the testimony of the first two witnesses regarding what they claim of the matter of the will «وَمَا اعْتَدَيْنا» (‘...and we have not transgressed...) against them by testifying contrary to what they testified «إِنَّا إِذاً لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ» (‘...for then we would indeed be among the wrongdoers.’).

His saying, the Exalted: «ذلِكَ أَدْنى أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِالشَّهادَةِ عَلى وَجْهِها أَوْ يَخافُوا أَنْ تُرَدَّ أَيْمانٌ بَعْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ» — That makes it more likely that they will give the testimony in its proper form, or fear that [their] oaths will be rebutted by [the] oaths [of the heirs]... (5:108). The verse is in the position of explaining the wisdom of the legislation (حكمة التشريع), which is that this ruling, according to the order established by Allah, the Exalted, is the most cautious way to attain the reality in this situation, and closer to ensuring that the two witnesses do not deviate in their testimony and fear that the matter will change against them by the rejection of their testimony after its acceptance.

For man possesses a desire (هوى) that calls him to enjoy everything he can enjoy and to seize everything he craves if there is no barrier to turn him away from it, whether that be through a right he deserves or through injustice, justice or wrongdoing and transgression against others by nullifying their right and overcoming them. Man only turns away from this transgression and overstepping either due to an external barrier that prevents him through policy, punishment, or scandal, or due to a deterrent that restrains him from within himself. The strongest internal deterrent is the belief in Allah, to Whom is the return of the servants, the reckoning of deeds, the decisive judgment, and the fully paid recompense.

Since the reality of the matter of the will, according to the premise of the situation, is unknown and there is no way to uncover it except the testimony of the two witnesses whom the deceased had testify, the strongest thing that brings their testimony closer to truthfulness is to take their oaths by Allah, the Exalted—which is the oath (اليمين)—and to return the oath to the heirs, the guardians, along with their oath, in the event that their lying and treachery are uncovered by the heirs. These two—meaning their oath first, then the returning of the oath to the heirs—are the closest means to their truthfulness in their testimony and their fear of the scandal of the rebuttal of the oath. These two deterrents are the strongest in restraining them from deviation.

Then the Exalted followed the speech with an exhortation and a warning, saying: «وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَاسْمَعُوا وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ» (...and be wary of Allah and listen, and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot.). The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: «يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللهُ الرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ ما ذا أُجِبْتُمْ قالُوا لا عِلْمَ لَنا إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ» — The day Allah will gather the apostles and say, ‘What was the response you received?’ They will say, ‘We have no knowledge. Indeed You are the Knower of the Unseen.’ (5:109). The verse does not resist connection to what preceded it, for the literal sense of His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the previous verse: «وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَاسْمَعُوا» (...and be wary of Allah and listen...) etc., although absolute, is—according to its application to the case—a prohibition against deviation and injustice in testimony and the belittling of the matter of the oath by Allah. Thus, it was appropriate to mention in this context what occurs between Him, the Glorified, and His messengers on the Day of Resurrection, as they are witnesses over their nations and the best of witnesses, when Allah, the Glorified, asks them about the response they received from their nations, while they are the most knowledgeable of people regarding the deeds of their nations and the witnesses from Allah over them. They respond to Him by saying: «لا عِلْمَ لَنا إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ» (‘We have no knowledge. Indeed You are the Knower of the Unseen.’).

If the matter is of this nature, and Allah, the Glorified, is the Knower of everything with true knowledge, then it is fitting for the witnesses to fear the station of their Lord, not to deviate from the truth that Allah has granted them knowledge of, and not to conceal the testimony of Allah, lest they be among the sinners, the wrongdoers, and the transgressors.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: «يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ» (The day [He] will gather...) etc., is an adverbial phrase related to His saying in the previous verse: «وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ» (...and be wary of Allah...) etc. The gathering of the messengers was mentioned, rather than saying: "the day Allah says to the messengers," because of the suitability with the gathering of witnesses for testimony, as suggested by His saying: «تَحْبِسُونَهُما مِنْ بَعْدِ الصَّلاةِ فَيُقْسِمانِ بِاللهِ» (...you shall detain them after the prayer and they shall both swear by Allah...).

As for their denial of knowledge on that day by their saying: «لا عِلْمَ لَنا إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ» (‘We have no knowledge. Indeed You are the Knower of the Unseen.’), their affirmation of all knowledge of the Unseen (الغيوب) for Allah, the Glorified, in the form of exclusivity (حصر) indicates that what is denied is not the essence of knowledge. This is because the literal sense of their saying: «إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ» (‘Indeed You are the Knower of the Unseen.’) indicates that it is to justify the denial. It is well known that the exclusivity of all knowledge of the Unseen to Allah, the Glorified, does not necessitate the removal of all knowledge from others, especially if it is knowledge by witnessing (الشهادة), and that which is asked about—namely, the manner of the people's response to their messengers—is of the nature of witnessing rather than the Unseen.

Thus, their saying: «لا عِلْمَ لَنا» (‘We have no knowledge’) is not a denial of absolute knowledge, but rather of the true knowledge (حق العلم) that is not free from connection to the Unseen. For it is well known that knowledge only uncovers for its knower from reality to the extent that it relates to a matter in terms of its causes and attachments. Reality in essence is linked to all parts of the external world—that which precedes the occurring matter in the external world and that which surrounds it and accompanies it in time. Thus, knowledge of a matter among external matters, in the true sense of knowledge, is not attained except by encompassing all parts of existence, and then by its Transcendent Maker from Whom nothing can encompass. This is a matter beyond human capacity.

Man has not been granted of knowledge in this universe—whose vastness of arena baffles thought, whose greatness of celestial bodies and galaxies terrifies the gaze, whose depth into the cores of its atoms makes the mind fly, and which causes dizziness if one wishes to run between these two ends—except a little knowledge, to the extent of what he needs in the course of his life, like a small candle carried by one who knocks at the door of the dark night, benefiting from its light only to distinguish where he places his foot on the ground.

Thus, what human knowledge relates to is embedded in its existence, connected to its reality by edges, then by the edges of edges, and so on—all of that in a realm unseen (غيب) from human perception. Thus, knowledge in the true sense of the word does not relate to anything unless it relates to all the unseens in existence, and that is not possible for a limited, measured creature—human or otherwise—except for Allah, the One, the Subduer, with Whom are the keys of the Unseen; none knows them but He. Allah, the Exalted, said: ...and Allah knows and you do not know. (2:216). This indicates that the nature of man is ignorance, so he is not granted of knowledge except what is limited and measured, as the Exalted said: There is not a thing but that its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure. (15:21). This is His saying, peace be upon him, when he was asked about the reason for Allah's veiling from His creation, he said: "Because He built them with a structure based on ignorance." And the Exalted said: ...and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes. (2:255). This indicates that all knowledge belongs to Allah, and man only encompasses of it what Allah wishes. And the Exalted said: ...and you have not been given of knowledge except a little. (17:85). This indicates that there is much knowledge of which man has been given only a little.

Therefore, the reality of the matter is that true knowledge does not exist with anyone other than Allah, the Glorified. Since the Day of Resurrection is a day in which things appear in their realities—as indicated by the verses describing its affair—there is no room in it except for true speech, as the Exalted said: ...they will not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted and who says what is right. That is the True Day. (78:38-39). It was part of the right answer when the messengers are asked and it is said to them: «ما ذا أُجِبْتُمْ» (‘What was the response you received?’) that they respond by denying knowledge of themselves because it is from the Unseen, and affirm it for their Lord, the Glorified, by their saying: «لا عِلْمَ لَنا إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ» (‘We have no knowledge. Indeed You are the Knower of the Unseen.’).

This response from them, peace be upon them, is a form of submission before the Presence of Grandeur and Majesty, and an acknowledgment of their essential need and their true nullity before their True Master, observing the etiquette of the Presence (أدب الحضور) and manifesting the reality of the matter. It is not a final answer after which there is no answer at all:

Firstly, because Allah, the Glorified, made them witnesses over their nations, as He mentioned in His saying: So how shall it be when We bring a witness from every nation and We bring you as a witness against these? (4:41) and He said: ...and the Book will be set up, and the prophets and the witnesses will be brought... (39:69). There is no meaning to making them witnesses except that they testify against their nations on the Day of Resurrection with what is the right of testimony on that day. Inevitably, they will testify on that day as Allah has decreed. Thus, their saying on that day: «لا عِلْمَ لَنا» (‘We have no knowledge’) follows the etiquette of servitude (الأدب العبودي) before the True King to Whom belongs the command and the kingdom on that day, and is an explanation of the reality of the situation, which is that He alone possesses knowledge by His essence, and none other possesses it except what He has given them to possess. There is no harm if they respond after this answer with the bestowed knowledge they have concerning the states of their nations. This supports what we previously presented in the discussion of His saying, the Exalted: Thus We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses to the people... (2:143) in the first volume of this book: that this knowledge and witnessing are not of the type of knowledge and witnessing known to us, and that they are from the knowledge specific to Allah, bestowed upon a group of His honored servants.

Secondly, because Allah, the Glorified, affirmed knowledge for a group of His near servants on the Day of Resurrection according to its significance. The Exalted said: But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, ‘Certainly you have stayed in Allah’s Book until the Day of Resurrection...’ (30:56). And the Exalted said: ...and on the Elevations will be men who recognize each of them by their mark. (7:46). And the Exalted said: Those whom they invoke besides Him do not have any power of intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and they know. (43:87). Jesus son of Mary, peace be upon him, is among those covered by the verse, and he is a messenger; thus he is among those who bear witness to the truth and they know. And the Exalted said: And the Apostle will say, ‘O my Lord! Indeed my people consigned this Quran to oblivion.’ (25:30). What the verse narrates of his saying is precisely an answer to the question contained in this verse, namely His saying, the Exalted: «فَيَقُولُ ما ذا أُجِبْتُمْ» (...and say, ‘What was the response you received?’). Thus it appeared that the messengers' saying, peace be upon them: "We have no knowledge" is not a final answer, as previously mentioned.

Thirdly, because the Quran mentions the questioning of the messengers and those to whom they were sent together, as the Exalted said: We will surely question those to whom [the apostles] were sent, and We will surely question the apostles. (7:6). Then He mentioned many answers from the nations to whom they were sent regarding many questions, and the answer necessitates knowledge just as the question establishes it. He also said regarding them: ‘You were certainly oblivious of this. Now We have removed from you your veil, and your sight is acute today.’ (50:22). And He also said: If you could but see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [saying], ‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard; so send us back [to the world] that we may act righteously. Indeed we [now] have certainty.’ (32:12), among many other verses. If the nations—especially the guilty among them—possess knowledge on this day, how can it be imagined that the noble messengers, peace be upon them, would lack it? Thus, the conclusion is what we have presented.

(A Discourse on the Meaning of Testimony)

The civil society (الاجتماع المدني) that revolves among us and the interaction occurring in all aspects of earthly life between our active powers leads us—inevitably—to various types of differences and disputes. That which one of us is specialized in enjoying, another may wish to share with him or specialize in it himself in his place; thus his soul craves it and disputes with him over it, leading to man's realization of the necessity of considering judgment and ruling (القضاء والحكم) so that these disputes may be removed.

The first thing judgment needs is that cases and events be preserved in the manner they occurred and recorded in a way that change and alteration do not find a path to them, so that the judge's ruling may fall upon them. This is something beyond doubt.

This is achieved by having witnesses to the event, such that a person becomes aware of it, bears it (يتحملها), and then performs (يؤدي) what he bore when necessary and required, or it is recorded in another way such as writing or other tools made for that purpose which man has been guided to reach.

Testimony (الشهادة) differs from other means of preservation and recording, firstly, in that means other than testimony are not universal. The most general and well-known of them is writing, yet it has not encompassed humanity even today, let alone others. This is in contrast to testimony and bearing [witness].

Secondly, testimony—which is the linguistic explanation from the witness himself regarding what he bore and preserved—is further from the occurrence of defect and more protected from the onset of various types of afflictions compared to writing and other means of preservation and recording.

Therefore, we see that testimony is not disregarded by any nation in their societies, despite their vast differences in social norms, national and religious temperaments, and advancement or backwardness in civilization and primitivity. It is not devoid of some consideration among them.

The consideration in it is for the individual of the people who is counted as a member of the nation and a part of the group. Therefore, the testimony of an undiscerning child is not valued, nor is the testimony of a madman who does not know what he says, for example. For this reason also, some primitive nations did not value the testimony of women because they did not count the woman as a part of society. Upon this, most social norms in ancient nations like the Romans, Greeks, and others were conducted.

Islam, which is the religion of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — considers testimony and gives it alone, among all other means, authoritative status (الحجية). As for other means, they have no consideration except when they provide knowledge. The Exalted said: ...and maintain the testimony for the sake of Allah. (65:2). And the Exalted said: ...and do not conceal the testimony; and whoever conceals it, his heart is indeed sinful. (2:283). And the Exalted said: ...and those who are upright in their testimonies. (70:33).

Islam has considered the number of witnesses in all cases except for adultery (الزنا) to be two, so that one may support the other. The Exalted said: ...and take as witness two just men from among your men, and if there are not two men, then a man and two women from among those you approve as witnesses, so that if one of them errs, the other will remind her. And let not the witnesses refuse when they are called. And do not be weary of writing it down, whether it be small or big, with its term. That is more equitable with Allah and more upright for testimony, and more likely to prevent you from doubting... (2:282). Thus, He informed that what the verse explained and considered of the rulings of testimony—including joining one to another to be two—is more in accordance with equity, the establishment of testimony, and the removal of doubt.

Furthermore, since Islam, in its identification of the individual of society—in other words, in its consideration of the unit from which human society is formed—counts the woman as a part included in the ruling, it shared her with the man in granting the right to establish testimonies. However, since it considered the society it formed to be built upon intellect (التعقل) rather than emotions (العواطف), and the woman is an emotional being, it gave her half the right and weight of the man. Thus, the testimony of two women equals the testimony of one man, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, in the previous verse: ...so that if one of them errs, the other will remind her. We have already discussed the rights of women in Islam in the fourth volume of this book, which is useful in this context. Testimony has many subsidiary rulings (أحكام فرعية) expounded in jurisprudence (الفقه) that are outside our purpose in this discussion.

(A Discourse on Justice)

A researcher in Islamic rulings often encounters the term justice (العدالة) during his research, and may find various definitions and diverse interpretations for the term according to the differences of researchers and their paths.

However, what suits our station of research—which is a Quranic research—in analyzing its meaning and the manner of its consideration by applying it to the primordial nature (الفطرة) upon which Islam is built, is to follow another path of research, so we say:

Justice—which is moderation (الاعتدال) and the middle ground between the two patterns: the high and the low, and the two sides: excess (الإفراط) and deficiency (التفريط)—has a true value and great weight in human societies. The just middle (الوسط العدل) is the essential part upon which social composition and synthesis rely. For the high, noble individual who is characterized by high social virtues and represents the ultimate goal of society is only produced by time in small numbers, one after another. It is well known that society is not composed of the rare individual, nor is its existence completed by him, even if he is the head member in its body wherever he is found.

And the low, base individual who does not fulfill social rights, and in whom the average measure of society's aspirations is not realized—one who has no motive calling him to observe the general social principles by which the life of society is maintained, and no deterrent restraining him from plunging into social sins that destroy society and nullify the necessary attraction between its parts—in short, there is no reliance on his part in the structure of society and no trust in his good influence and sound advice.

Rather, the judgment belongs to the average members of society by whom the structure of society is established, in whom its goals and purposes are realized, and through whom its good effects appear—those effects for which its parts and members were only synthesized to obtain and enjoy.

All of this is something the social human does not doubt upon his first glance into this matter.

It is necessary for him, in his social life, to have individuals in society upon whose social conduct he can rely, who are characterized by moderation in affairs and caution against indulgence in violating laws and contravening current norms and etiquettes without concern or hesitation in many areas such as government, judgment, testimonies, and others in general.

This necessary judgment, or that which is close to necessary according to the primordial nature, is what Islam considers in the witness. The Exalted said: ...and take as witness two just men from among you, and maintain the testimony for the sake of Allah. That is what he who believes in Allah and the Last Day is exhorted with. (65:2). And the Exalted said: «شَهادَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ حِينَ الْوَصِيَّةِ اثْنانِ ذَوا عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ» (The witness between you, when death approaches any of you at the time of making a will, shall be two honest men from among you) (5:106). The address in both verses is to the believers, so the condition that the two witnesses be "possessors of justice" (ذوي عدل) from among them means they possess a moderate, average state relative to their religious society. As for comparison to national or local society, Islam does not value such non-religious ties. It is apparent that the summary of their being in a moderate state relative to the religious society is their being those who are trusted in their religion, not committing what are counted as the destructive major sins (المعاصي الكبيرة) in the religion. The Exalted said: If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you to a noble entrance. (4:31). We have discussed the meaning of major sins in the commentary on this verse in the fourth volume of this book.

Upon this meaning, the speech of the Exalted proceeded in His saying: As for those who accuse honorable women and do not bring four witnesses, flog them with eighty lashes, and never accept their testimony again. It is they who are the transgressors, except those who repent after that and reform, for Allah is indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful. (24:4-5).

Similar to the previous verse conditioning justice is His saying, the Exalted: ...from among those you approve as witnesses... (2:282). The approval (الرضا) taken in the verse is the approval of the religious society. It is well known that the religious society, as religious, does not approve of anyone unless he follows a type of conduct that is trusted in the matter of religion.

This is what we call in the discipline of jurisprudence (فن الفقه) the "faculty of justice" (ملكة العدالة), and it is different from the faculty of justice according to the terminology of the discipline of ethics (فن الأخلاق). For jurisprudential justice is the psychological state (الهيئة النفسانية) that restrains one from committing major sins according to the conventional view (النظر العرفي), while that in the discipline of ethics is the firmly rooted faculty (الملكة الراسخة) according to reality.

What we have derived from the meaning of justice is what is derived from the school of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) according to what has been narrated through their paths:

In al-Faqih, with his chain of narration from Ibn Abi Ya‘fur, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'By what is a man's justice known among the Muslims—so that his testimony for them and against them may be accepted?' He said: 'That you know him (1) by concealment [of faults] and chastity—and the restraint of the stomach, the private parts, the hand, and the tongue; and he is known by the avoidance of the major sins for which Allah, the Exalted, has promised the Fire—such as drinking wine, adultery, usury, disobedience to parents, fleeing from the battlefield, and other than that.

The evidence for all of that is that he be a concealer of all his faults—such that it becomes forbidden for the Muslims to investigate what lies behind that of his slips and faults, and it becomes obligatory for them to commend him and manifest his justice among the people. And there should be from him the commitment to the five prayers when he is constant in them—and preserves their times by attending the congregation of the Muslims—and that he does not stay away from their congregation in their place of prayer except due to an excuse.

If he is thus constant at his place of prayer when the five prayers are present—then if he is asked about in his tribe and his neighborhood, they say: "We have seen nothing from him but good, constant in the prayers, committed to their times in his place of prayer"; for that permits his testimony and his justice among the Muslims. That is because the prayer is a cover and an expiation for sins, and it is not possible to testify for a man that he prays if he does not attend his place of prayer and does not commit to the congregation of the Muslims.

Indeed, the congregation and the gathering for prayer were only established so that he who prays may be known from him who does not pray—and he who preserves the times of prayer from him who wastes them. Were it not for that, it would not be for anyone to testify to another's righteousness, because he who does not pray has no righteousness among the Muslims. For the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) intended to burn a people in their houses for their leaving the attendance of the congregation of the Muslims—and there were among them those who prayed in their houses, yet that was not accepted from them. So how can testimony or justice among the Muslims be accepted from one regarding whom the ruling from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and from His Messenger was burning in the midst of his house with fire? And he (ص) used to say: "There is no prayer for one who does not pray in the mosque with the Muslims except due to an excuse."

I say: It was narrated in al-Tahdhib with an addition we have omitted. "Concealment" (الستر) and "chastity" (العفاف) both mean refraining, according to what is in al-Sihah. The narration—as you see—makes the essence of justice a matter known among the Muslims and explains that the effect resulting from it, which indicates this psychological quality, is the leaving of the prohibitions of Allah and the restraint from forbidden desires, and the identifier of that is the avoidance of the major sins among the acts of disobedience. Then it makes the evidence for all of that a good outward appearance (حسن الظاهر) among the Muslims according to what he (peace be upon him) explained in detail.

In the same work, from ‘Abdullah ibn al-Mughirah from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), who said: "Whoever is born upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) and is known for righteousness in himself, his testimony is permissible."

In the same work: Sama‘ah narrated from Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "There is no harm in the testimony of the weak if he is chaste and self-guarding."

In al-Kafi, with his chain of narration from ‘Ali ibn Mahziyar from Abu ‘Ali ibn Rashid, who said: "I said to Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): 'Your followers have differed, so shall I pray behind all of them?' He said: 'Do not pray except behind one whose religion you trust.'"

I say: The indication of the narrations for what we have presented is apparent, and in them are other discussions outside our purpose in this context.

(1) "That they know him" — variant.

(A Discourse on the Oath)

The reality of the meaning of your saying: "By my life, such and such is the case," and "By my life, the matter is as I have informed," is that you link that which you have informed of the news and restrict it with a type of restriction in its truthfulness to your age and your life, which have a status and respect with you, such that they are inseparable in existence and non-existence. If you were lying in your news, you would have nullified the status of your life and its respect with you, thus falling from the level of humanity that calls for respect for the matter of life.

And the meaning of your saying: "I adjure you by Allah to do such and such or leave such and such" is that you have linked your command or prohibition to the status and might (العزة) that belongs to Allah—exalted is His Name—among the believers, such that the violation of the command or the disobedience of the prohibition would be a belittling of His station, the Exalted, and a removal of the sanctity of faith in Him.

Likewise, the meaning of your saying: "By Allah, I will surely do such and such" is a special connection between your resolve upon what you have resolved of the matter and that which belongs to Allah, the Glorified, with you of status and sanctity according to your faith in Him, such that your revocation of your resolve and your breaking of your intention would be a nullification of what belongs to Allah, the Glorified, of status with you. The purpose of that is for you to be under a deterrent from revoking the resolve and breaking the intention. Thus, the oath (القسم) is the creation of a special link between something of news or origination (الإنشاء) and another thing possessing status and honor, such that that which is linked to is nullified by the nullification of the link according to the claim. Since that which is linked to possesses status and honor with the maker, for example, he does not accept the removal of its status and the insult to its station; thus he is truthful in his news, or obedient in what he commands or prohibits, or proceeding in his resolve without revocation inevitably, and its result is extreme emphasis.

There exists in languages another type of creating a link that contrasts with the oath, which is linking the news, for example, to something that has no value or honor with the informer, to indicate thereby the belittling of what he informs of or the news that reached him, and it is counted as a type of insult; it is very rare in the Arabic language.

The swearing (الحلف) and the oath (اليمين)—as far as we know—are among the customs circulating on the tongues of people, inherited generation after generation, and are not specific to one language over another. This is evidence that it is not among the linguistic-verbal affairs, but rather man is guided to it by his social life in cases where he realizes the necessity of resorting to it and benefiting from it.

The oath has not ceased to circulate among nations; it may be built upon and relied upon in various unrecorded cases occurring in their societies for diverse purposes: to repel accusation, to remove slander, to put the soul at ease, and to support news, until civil laws took interest in its matter and gave it a legal aspect in some cases, such as the oath of leaders and those in charge of affairs when assuming important positions and occupying great, high stations, and other than that.

Islam has taken complete interest in the matter of the oath if it falls upon Allah, the Glorified, specifically. This is only in light of the concern for observing the sanctity of the Lordly station and protecting His arena, the Exalted, from being faced with what the law of Lordship and servitude rejects. Therefore, a specific expiation (كفارة) was placed for the breaking of an oath, and it was disliked to swear much by Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. The Exalted said: Allah will not take you to task for what is inane in your oaths, but He will take you to task for what you have pledged in solemn oaths. The expiation for it is to feed ten needy persons... (5:89). And the Exalted said: Do not make Allah an obstacle to your oaths... (2:224).

The oath was considered in cases of judgment that lacked clear evidence (البينة). The Exalted said: «فَيُقْسِمانِ بِاللهِ لَشَهادَتُنا أَحَقُّ مِنْ شَهادَتِهِما وَمَا اعْتَدَيْنا» (...they shall both swear by Allah, ‘Our testimony is more truthful than their testimony, and we have not transgressed...) (5:107). And from his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "The clear evidence is upon the claimant and the oath is upon him who denies."

The reality of considering the oath is the sufficiency with the indication of faith itself where there is no other evidence. This is because the religious society is built upon the faith of individuals in Allah, and the believing human is the part of this composed whole, and he is the source from which the followed norms and current rulings spring. In short, all the prominent effects in the people arising from their religious state [spring from him], just as the non-religious society is built upon the faith of individuals in their national goals, and from them spring the civil norms, laws, etiquettes, and customs circulating among them.

If this is so, and it is correct to rely in all social affairs and depend in all necessities of life upon the faith of individuals in different ways, then it was permissible to rely upon their faith in that for which there is no other evidence to rely upon, which is the oath in that for which there is no clear evidence. This is by the denier linking what he denies of the claimant's claim and restricting it with his faith, such that the consideration of his faith in Allah would vanish with the nullification of his denial and the appearance of his lie in what he manifested and informed of.

Thus, his faith, because of what he pledged by the oath, is in the position of a pledged property (مال الرهن) that is placed under the control of the creditor, and in its return to the control of the pledgor, the truthfulness of his promise and his payment of the debt to a term are observed; otherwise, the property is gone and he remains empty-handed.

Likewise, the swearer is considered to have pledged his faith to what he swore upon as long as the contrary does not appear. If the contrary appears, he returns empty-handed of faith, falling from the degree of consideration, deprived of enjoying the fruit of faith—which, in the religious society, is all social advantages—and he returns expelled from the harmoniously joined society; no sky shades him and no earth carries him.

This explanation is supported by what is narrated of the people's manifestation of loathing for those who lagged behind the religious norms, such as the prayer in congregation and going out for jihad and the like, during the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when the complete authority and government belonged to the religion over desires.

As for these times in which the influence of religion has weakened and desire has seeped into the hearts, and a society has been formed among us composed of religious goals—despite the weakness in its structure and the people's turning away from it—and of modern civil goals—and they are joined by the indulgence in material enjoyments despite the strength in its foundation and the general inclination of all people toward it—then the severe dispute and struggle between the religious motives and the knocking civil motives took hold, and this one continues to overcome and that one to be defeated, and the unity of the system that must be spread over the level of society was breached, and chaos and confusion appeared in the spiritualities. At such a time, the oath hardly benefits, nor does that which is stronger than it and more protective of people's rights. Reliance has vanished not only upon the religious means present in society, but upon them and upon all modern laws together.

However, Allah, the Glorified, does not abrogate His rulings nor does He overlook His laws because of the people's turning away from them and their weariness of them. Indeed, the religion with Allah is Islam, and He does not approve of disbelief for His servants. If the truth were to follow their desires, the heavens and the earth would have been corrupted. Islam is a religion that addresses all affairs of human life, explaining them and clarifying their rulings, possessing harmonious, suitable, and inseparable parts that live by the spirit of the single monotheism (التوحيد). If some of its parts fall ill, all fall ill, and if some of them are corrupted, that affects the work of all, exactly like the individual human being.

If some of its parts are corrupted or fall ill, it was obligatory to keep the sound ones in their soundness, to treat the ill, and to reform the corrupt. It was not permissible to leave the ill in its illness and the corrupt in its corruption, and to turn away from the sound.

Islam, although it is a primordial (حنيفية), easy, and tolerant creed possessing different and vast levels, measuring its obligations according to what can be performed and implemented—its connected rope extending from a secure social state that includes its laws and regulations in their entirety without exception, to an individual, forced state in which the prayer is sufficient by gesture—yet the descent from one of its levels to that which is below it is conditioned by the necessity (الاضطرار) that negates the obligation and permits expansion. The Exalted said: Whoever renounces faith in Allah after his belief—excepting him who is forced while his heart is at rest in faith—but those who open up their breasts to unfaith, upon them shall be Allah’s wrath, and there is a great punishment for them... — until He said — Then indeed your Lord, to those who migrated after they were persecuted, then waged jihad and were patient—indeed your Lord, after that, is All-forgiving, All-merciful. (16:106-110).

As for building life upon material enjoyment and then making excuses for rejecting the religious materials that contradict it by saying that it does not agree with the current norm in the present world, this is proceeding according to material logic rather than the logic of religion.

Among the research related to this chapter is the saying of some: "Indeed, swearing by other than Allah is a form of polytheism (الشرك) with Allah." It is fitting to ask this speaker: what does he mean by this polytheism he mentioned?

If he meant that in the oath by other than Allah there is a glorification of the one sworn by and an exaltation of his matter—since the meaning of the oath is built upon that—and in it is a type of submission and worship to him, which is polytheism, then not every glorification is polytheism except the granting of the grandeur of independent Lordship by which one is independent of others.

Allah, the Exalted, has sworn by many of His creatures, such as the heaven, the earth, the sun, the moon, the receding and hiding stars, and by the star when it falls. He swore by the mountain, the sea, the fig, the olive, and the horse. He swore by the night, the day, the morning, the twilight, the time (العصر), the forenoon (الضحى), and the Day of Resurrection. He swore by the soul (النفس), and He swore by the Book, the Great Quran, the life of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and by the angels, among many other verses. No oath is sound except through glorification.

So what is the barrier to our proceeding according to what the speech of the Exalted proceeded upon in glorifying them with the bestowed grandeur and restricting ourselves to that? If that were from polytheism, the speech of the Exalted would have been more worthy of caution against it and more deserving of its observance.

Furthermore, Allah, the Exalted, has magnified many things in His speech, such as the Quran, the Throne, and the character of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَاكَ سَبْعًا مِّنَ الْمَثَانِي وَالْقُرْآنَ الْعَظِيمَ — "Certainly We have given you the seven [oft-repeated verses] and the great Quran" (15:87).
