He also said: وَهُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ — "and He is the Lord of the Great Throne" (9:129). And He said: وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ — "and indeed you possess a great character" (68:4). Moreover, He has established rights for His prophets, messengers, and the believers upon Himself, magnifying and honoring them. The Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنَا لِعِبَادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ * إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنصُورُونَ — "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles: * that they will indeed be the ones to be helped" (37:171-172). He also said: وَكَانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنَا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and it was a must for Us to help the faithful" (30:47).

What, then, prevents us from magnifying them and following the course of His speech regarding oaths in general? Why should we not swear by Allah through something He Himself has sworn by, or by one of the rights He has granted His friends (الأولياء) upon Himself? Indeed, the legal oath (اليمين الشرعي) that carries legal consequences in the chapters of oaths or judiciary cannot be contracted by other than Allah, the Glorified, as clarified in jurisprudence (الفقه), but that is not our subject here.

If the objector intends that absolute magnification (الإعظام), in any form, is impermissible for other than Allah—even magnifying them in the manner Allah Himself has magnified them—then there is no evidence for such a claim; rather, definitive evidence exists to the contrary.

It is sometimes said that swearing by the right of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the rest of the friends of Allah, seeking nearness to them, and seeking their intercession (الاستشفاع) in any manner constitutes worship (عبادة) and ascribing a ghaybi authority (سلطة غيبية) — a power belonging to the realm beyond sensory perception — to them. The response to this is the same as the previous one: if by this "ghaybi authority" one means an independent authority (سلطة مستقلة) exclusive to Allah, then no Muslim who believes in the Book of Allah would affirm it for anyone other than the Exalted. However, if one means absolute authority of a non-material nature, even if by the permission of Allah, then what is the evidence for the impossibility of some of Allah's servants, such as His friends, being characterized by it by His permission? The Noble Quran has explicitly mentioned many ghaybi authorities regarding the angels, as He said: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنَا وَهُمْ لَا يُفَرِّطُونَ — "until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him away, and they do not neglect [their duty]" (6:61). He also said: قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُم مَّلَكُ الْمَوْتِ — "Say, ‘The angel of death... will take your souls’" (32:11). And He said: وَالنَّازِعَاتِ غَرْقًا * وَالنَّاشِطَاتِ نَشْطًا * وَالسَّابِحَاتِ سَبْحًا * فَالسَّابِقَاتِ سَبْقًا * فَالْمُدَبِّرَاتِ أَمْرًا — "By those who wrest [the soul] violently, * by those who draw [it] out gently, * by those who float serenely, * by those who take the lead outstripping, * by those who direct the affairs" (79:1-5). He also said: مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ — "Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down on your heart" (2:97). The verses in this regard are very numerous.

Regarding Iblis and his hosts, He said: إِنَّهُ يَرَاكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا تَرَوْنَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّيَاطِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Indeed he and his hosts see you from whence you do not see them. We have indeed made the devils friends of those who do not have faith" (7:27). Furthermore, verses have been revealed regarding the intercession (شفاعة) of the prophets and others in the Hereafter, and their miraculous signs (آياتهم المعجزة) in this world are numerous.

I wish I knew what the difference is between the material effects (الآثار المادية) that these people affirm for objects without hesitation, and the non-material effects they term "ghaybi authority." If affirming causality (التأثير) for other than Allah is forbidden, there would be no difference between a material effect and any other. But if it is permissible by the permission of Allah, the Glorified, then all are equal in this regard.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his men in a raised (رفعه) report, he said: Tamim al-Dari, Ibn Bandi, and Ibn Abi Mariyah set out on a journey. Tamim al-Dari was a Muslim, while Ibn Bandi and Ibn Abi Mariyah were Christians. Tamim al-Dari had with him goods, gold-engraved vessels, and a necklace that he had brought to some Arab markets for sale.

Tamim al-Dari fell severely ill. When death approached him, he handed over what he had to Ibn Bandi and Ibn Abi Mariyah, commanding them to deliver it to his heirs. They arrived in Madinah, having taken the vessels and the necklace from the goods, and delivered the rest to his heirs. The family missed the vessels and the necklace. Tamim’s family asked them: "Did our companion suffer a long illness in which he spent a great deal of money?" They replied: "No, he was only ill for a few days." They asked: "Was anything stolen from him during this journey?" They replied: "No." They asked: "Did he engage in a trade in which he lost money?" They replied: "No." They said: "We find missing the finest things he had: gold-engraved vessels encrusted with jewels, and a necklace." They replied: "Whatever he handed to us, we have delivered to you."

They brought them before the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) required them to take an oath. They swore and were released. Later, those vessels and the necklace appeared with them. The guardians of Tamim came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, what we claimed against Ibn Bandi and Ibn Abi Mariyah has appeared." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) waited for the judgment from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, regarding that.

Then Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, revealed: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا شَهَادَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ حِينَ الْوَصِيَّةِ اثْنَانِ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ أَوْ آخَرَانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ إِنْ أَنتُمْ ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "O you who have faith! The witness between you, when death approaches any of you, while making a bequest, shall be two fair men from among you, or two others from among those who are not of you, if you are journeying in the land" (5:106). Thus, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, permitted the testimony of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) regarding a bequest only when one is on a journey and cannot find Muslims.

Then He said: فَأَصَابَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةُ الْمَوْتِ ۚ تَحْبِسُونَهُمَا مِن بَعْدِ الصَّلَاةِ فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِاللَّهِ إِنِ ارْتَبْتُمْ لَا نَشْتَرِي بِهِ ثَمَنًا وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ ۙ وَلَا نَكْتُمُ شَهَادَةَ اللَّهِ إِنَّا إِذًا لَّمِنَ الْآثِمِينَ — "and the affliction of death visits you. You shall detain the two of them after the prayer, and if you have any doubt, they shall vow by Allah, ‘We will not sell it for any price, even if it were a relative, and we will not conceal the testimony of Allah, for then we would indeed be among the sinners’" (5:106). This was the first testimony that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had them swear. فَإِنْ عُثِرَ عَلَىٰ أَنَّهُمَا اسْتَحَقَّا إِثْمًا — "Then, if it is discovered that the two of them have been guilty of a sin" (5:107), meaning they swore to a lie, فَآخَرَانِ يَقُومَانِ مَقَامَهُمَا — "two others shall take their place" (5:107), meaning from the guardians of the claimant, مِنَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَوْلَيَانِ — "from among those against whom the first two [witnesses] had sinned" (5:107), فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِاللَّهِ — "and they shall vow by Allah" (5:107), meaning they shall swear by Allah that they are more entitled to this claim than the other two, and that the others lied in what they swore by Allah: لَشَهَادَتُنَا أَحَقُّ مِن شَهَادَتِهِمَا وَمَا اعْتَدَيْنَا إِنَّا إِذًا لَّمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "‘Our testimony is truer than their testimony, and we have not transgressed, for then we would indeed be among the wrongdoers’" (5:107).

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) then commanded the guardians of Tamim al-Dari to swear by Allah as He had commanded them. They swore, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took the necklace and the vessels from Ibn Bandi and Ibn Abi Mariyah and returned them to the guardians of Tamim al-Dari. ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰ أَن يَأْتُوا بِالشَّهَادَةِ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهَا أَوْ يَخَافُوا أَن تُرَدَّ أَيْمَانٌ بَعْدَ أَيْمَانِهِمْ — "That makes it more likely that they will give the testimony in its proper form, or fear that [other] vows will be taken after their vows" (5:108).

I say: Al-Qummi recorded a similar report in his Tafsir, and in it, after the phrase: تَحْبِسُونَهُمَا مِن بَعْدِ الصَّلَاةِ — "You shall detain the two of them after the prayer," it adds: "meaning the afternoon (العصر) prayer." His (عليه‌السلام) saying "the first two" (الأولين) is apparently in the dual form, referring to the first two witnesses as an explanation of the Exalted's saying: الْأَوْلَيَانِ. It is apparent from his (عليه‌السلام) recitation of اسْتَحَقَّ in the active voice (البناء للفاعل), as it is attributed to Ali (عليه‌السلام). We have already explained in the preceding commentary that this is the clearest of the possible meanings according to this recitation.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Al-Tirmidhi (who graded it weak), Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Nahhas in his al-Nasikh, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu’aym in al-Ma’rifah narrated via Abu al-Nadr—who is al-Kalbi—from Badhan, the client of Umm Hani, from Ibn Abbas, from Tamim al-Dari regarding this verse: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا شَهَادَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ — "O you who have faith! The witness between you, when death approaches any of you..." (5:106). He said: "The people are innocent of this except for me and Adi ibn Bada. We were Christians who used to travel to Syria before Islam. We went to Syria for trade, and a client of the Banu Sahm called Budayl ibn Abi Maryam came to us with merchandise. He had a silver goblet (جام) intended for the king, which was his most valuable item. He fell ill and made a bequest to us, commanding us to deliver what he left to his family."

Tamim said: "When he died, we took that goblet and sold it for a thousand dirhams, then Adi ibn Bada and I divided it between us. When we came to his family, we gave them what we had with us. They missed the goblet and asked us about it. We said: 'He left nothing other than this, and nothing else was handed to us.'"

Tamim said: "When I accepted Islam after the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrived in Madinah, I felt guilty about it. I went to his family, told them the news, and paid them five hundred dirhams. I informed them that my companion had the same amount. They brought him to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who asked them for evidence (البينة), but they found none. He then commanded them to have him swear by that which is magnified in his religion. He swore, and then Allah revealed: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا شَهَادَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ until His saying أَن تُرَدَّ أَيْمَانٌ بَعْدَ أَيْمَانِهِمْ. Then Amr ibn al-Aas and another man stood up and swore, and the five hundred dirhams were taken from Adi ibn Bada."

I say: This narration, despite its weakness, does not perfectly align with the verse, as is evident. However, something close to the previous narration of al-Qummi has been narrated from Ibn Abbas and Ikrimah.

In the same source: Al-Faryabi, Abd ibn Humayd, Abu Ubayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib that he used to recite: مِنَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَوْلَيَانِ with a fathah on the ta (اسْتَحَقَّ).

In the same source: Ibn Mardawayh and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited: الَّذِينَ اسْتَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَوْلَيَانِ.

In the same source: Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Abbas that he said: "This verse is abrogated (منسوخة)."

I say: There is no evidence for the claim of abrogation (النسخ) mentioned in the narration.

In al-Kafi, from Muhammad ibn Isma’il, from al-Fadl ibn Shadhan, and Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Hisham ibn al-Hakam, from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) regarding the saying of Allah, the Blessed and Exalted: أَوْ آخَرَانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ — "or two others from among those who are not of you." He said: "If a man is in a land where there is no Muslim, the testimony of a non-Muslim regarding a bequest is permissible."

I say: The meaning of the narration is derived from the verse itself.

In the same source, with his chain to Yahya ibn Muhammad, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا شَهَادَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ حِينَ الْوَصِيَّةِ اثْنَانِ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ أَوْ آخَرَانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ? He said: "The 'two from among you' are Muslims, and the 'two from others' are from the People of the Book. If they cannot find any from the People of the Book, then from the Magians (المجوس), because the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) established for the Magians the same practice as the People of the Book regarding the tribute (الجزية)."

"That is when a man dies in a foreign land and finds no Muslims. He takes as witnesses two men from the People of the Book, who are detained after the afternoon prayer. They swear by Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: لَا نَشْتَرِي بِهِ ثَمَنًا وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ وَلَا نَكْتُمُ شَهَادَةَ اللَّهِ إِنَّا إِذًا لَّمِنَ الْآثِمِينَ — 'We will not sell it for any price, even if it were a relative, and we will not conceal the testimony of Allah, for then we would indeed be among the sinners.'" He said: "That is if the guardian of the deceased doubts their testimony. If it is discovered that they testified falsely, he does not nullify their testimony until he brings two witnesses who take the place of the first two witnesses: فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِاللَّهِ لَشَهَادَتُنَا أَحَقُّ مِن شَهَادَتِهِمَا وَمَا اعْتَدَيْنَا إِنَّا إِذًا لَّمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — 'and they shall vow by Allah, "Our testimony is truer than their testimony, and we have not transgressed, for then we would indeed be among the wrongdoers."' If he does that, the testimony of the first two is nullified, and the testimony of the latter two is permitted. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰ أَن يَأْتُوا بِالشَّهَادَةِ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهَا أَوْ يَخَافُوا أَن تُرَدَّ أَيْمَانٌ بَعْدَ أَيْمَانِهِمْ — 'That makes it more likely that they will give the testimony in its proper form, or fear that [other] vows will be taken after their vows.'"

I say: The narration, as you see, agrees with the meaning of the verse mentioned earlier. Other narrations with the same meaning are found in al-Kafi and Tafsir al-Ayyashi from Abu Abdillah and Abu al-Hasan (عليه‌السلام).

In some narrations, the phrase أَوْ آخَرَانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ is interpreted as "disbelievers" (الكافرين), which is more general than the People of the Book, as narrated in al-Kafi from Abu al-Sabbah al-Kinani from Abu Abdillah.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Usamah, also from him (عليه‌السلام) regarding the verse: "What are the 'two others from among those who are not of you'?" He said: "They are two disbelievers." I asked: "ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ?" He said: "Two Muslims." Although the previous narration restricted to the People of the Book cannot restrict this absolute (إطلاق) according to the technical rules of absolute and restricted (الإطلاق والتقييد)—since they are both affirmative and compatible—the context of the first narration is suitable to interpret the absolute nature of the second in a way that aligns with the restriction.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from al-Saduq, with his chain to Abu Zayd Ayyash ibn Yazid ibn al-Hasan, from his father Yazid ibn al-Hasan, who said: Musa ibn Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) narrated to me, saying: Al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) said regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللَّهُ الرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ مَاذَا أُجِبْتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا — "The day Allah will gather the apostles and say, ‘What was the response you received?’ They will say, ‘We have no knowledge’" (5:109). He said: "They say: 'We have no knowledge other than You.'" He also said: "The Quran is entirely rebuke (تقريع), and its inner dimension (باطنه) is drawing near (تقريب)."

The author of al-Burhan said: Ibn Babawayh said: "He means by that, that behind the verses of reproach and threat are verses of mercy and forgiveness."

I say: What he quoted from al-Saduq (may Allah have mercy on him) regarding the meaning of his (عليه‌السلام) saying: "The Quran is entirely rebuke, and its inner dimension is drawing near," does not align with it, neither in view of the beginning of the narration—for the meaning of the messengers' saying لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا being "we have no knowledge other than You" is unrelated to the Quran containing two types of verses: verses of promise and verses of threat—nor in view of the context of the sentence itself. The speech is apparent in that the Quran is entirely rebuke and entirely drawing near, and the matter only differs according to the inner (الباطن) and the outer (الظاهر). Its inner is drawing near and its outer is rebuke; it is not that the Quran is divided into two parts, one part being verses of rebuke followed by another part being verses of drawing near.

Contemplating his (عليه‌السلام) speech while considering the beginning of the narration reveals that his intent by "rebuke" (التقريع)—in view of its contrast with "drawing near" (التقريب)—is its necessary meaning, which is "distancing" (التبعيد). The Quran is entirely gnosis (معارف) and realities (حقائق); its outer aspect is the distancing of realities from one another and the detailing of their parts, while its inner aspect is the drawing near of parts to one another, their consolidation (إحكام), and their unification (توحيد). The sum of the intended meaning is that the Quran, according to its outer aspect, provides various realities of gnosis that are distinct and separate from one another. However, despite their multiplicity, distinction, and mutual distance according to the outer, they draw near to one another in the inner, and their various meanings harmonize until they unite into a single reality, like the spirit permeating through all. This is nothing other than the reality of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. Allah, the Exalted, said: كِتَابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آيَاتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ — "[This is] a Book whose signs have been made firm and then elaborated from One who is all-wise, all-aware" (11:1).

It then becomes clear how this aligns with what he (عليه‌السلام) mentioned at the beginning of the narration: that the meaning of the messengers' saying لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا is "we have no knowledge other than You." For a human being, or any assumed knower, only knows what he knows through Allah, in the sense that Allah, the Glorified, is the one known by His essence (المعلوم بذاته), and others are known through Him. In other words, when knowledge attaches to a thing, it first attaches to Allah, the Glorified, in a manner befitting His holy presence and greatness, and then attaches to that thing from His direction, because Allah, the Glorified, possesses the knowledge of everything, which He grants to whomever He wills of His servants according to what He wills. As He said: وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِمَا شَاءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ — "and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes. His seat embraces the heavens and the earth" (2:255). The narration of Abd al-A'la, the client of the family of Sam, from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) and other narrations have already been mentioned regarding this meaning.

Accordingly, the meaning of their saying: "لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا other than You, إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ" according to his (عليه‌السلام) interpretation, is: "We have no knowledge of anything apart from You; we only know what we know from the direction of our knowledge of You, because all knowledge belongs to You. Since it is so, You are more knowledgeable of it than us, because what we know of a thing is Your knowledge, of which we have encompassed a portion by Your will and provision."

Based on this, another meaning of His saying إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ — "Indeed You are the Knower of the Unseen" — manifests, which is more sublime than the previous meaning. It is that every thing in creation, since its existence is separate from others, is in a state of "unseen" (غيب) from them, because its existence is limited and measured, encompassing only what Allah willed it to encompass. Allah, the Glorified, is the one who encompasses everything, the Knower of every unseen; nothing knows anything except from His direction, the Exalted and Sanctified above every deficiency.

Thus, the division of matters into "unseen" (غيب) and "witnessed" (شهادة) is, in reality, a division into an unseen that Allah willed us to encompass and an unseen that is hidden from us. This meaning may be supported by the literal sense of His saying: عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلَا يُظْهِرُ عَلَىٰ غَيْبِهِ أَحَدًا * إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ مِن رَّسُولٍ — "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, * except to an apostle He approves of" (72:26-27), considering what the annexation of "unseen" to the pronoun implies. You must contemplate this station deeply.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Yazid al-Kunasi, from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) regarding the saying of the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللَّهُ الرُّسُلَ, the verse. He said: "He says: 'What was the response you received regarding your successors (أوصيائكم) whom you left behind for your nations?' He said: 'They say: We have no knowledge of what they did after us.'"

I say: Al-Qummi narrated it in his Tafsir from Muhammad ibn Muslim from him (عليه‌السلام).

In al-Kafi, from Yazid from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) is a similar meaning, and it is of the nature of "application" (الجري) or from the category of the "inner" (الباطن).

Ayahs

إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَـٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱذْكُرْ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَتِكَ إِذْ أَيَّدتُّكَ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ تُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًا ۖ وَإِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ ۖ وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِى فَتَنفُخُ فِيهَا فَتَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَتُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ تُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ كَفَفْتُ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَنكَ إِذْ جِئْتَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (١١٠)
When Allah will say, O Jesus son of Mary, remember My blessing upon you and upon your mother, when I strengthened you with the Holy Spirit, so you would speak to the people in the cradle and in adulthood, and when I taught you the Book and wisdom, the Torah and the Evangel, and when you would create from clay the form of a bird, with My leave, and you would breathe into it and it would become a bird, with My leave; and you would heal the blind and the leper, with My leave, and you would raise the dead, with My leave; and when I held off [the evil of] the Children of Israel from you when you brought them manifest proofs, whereat the faithless among them said, ‘This is nothing but plain magic.’ (110)
وَإِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى ٱلْحَوَارِيِّـۧنَ أَنْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِى وَبِرَسُولِى قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّنَا مُسْلِمُونَ (١١١)
And when I inspired the Disciples, [saying], ‘Have faith in Me and My apostle,’ they said, ‘We have faith. Bear witness that we are Muslims.’ (111)

(Commentary)

These two verses, as well as the subsequent verses narrating the story of the descent of the Table (المائدة) and those reporting what Allah will ask Jesus son of Mary (عليه السلام) regarding people taking him and his mother as two gods besides Allah, the Glorified, and his response thereto—all of these are linked to the central purpose of the Surah established at its opening. This purpose is the call to fulfill covenants (الوفاء بالعهد), gratitude for blessings (الشكر للنعمة), and a warning against breaking covenants and ingratitude toward divine favors. Through this, the end of the Surah returns to its beginning, preserving the unity of the intended meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قالَ اللهُ يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ (When Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary...) until His saying: وَإِذْ تُخْرِجُ الْمَوْتى بِإِذْنِي (...and when you brought forth the dead by My leave). This verse enumerates several of the manifest and wondrous signs (الآيات) performed at his hands (عليه السلام). However, they are mentioned here as a favor bestowed upon both him and his mother together. This is mentioned in nearly the same wording in what the Exalted relates of the angels’ discourse with Mary when giving her the glad tidings of Jesus (عليه السلام) in Surah Al-Imran, where the Exalted says: إِذْ قالَتِ الْمَلائِكَةُ يا مَرْيَمُ إِنَّ اللهُ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍ مِنْهُ اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ (When the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah gives you the good news of a Word from Him whose name is Messiah, Jesus son of Mary...) until He says: وَيُكَلِّمُ النَّاسَ فِي الْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلاً (...and he will speak to people in the cradle and in adulthood...) until He says: وَيُعَلِّمُهُ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَالتَّوْراةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ وَرَسُولاً إِلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ ، أَنِّي قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِآيَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ أَنِّي أَخْلُقُ لَكُمْ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ فَأَنْفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْراً بِإِذْنِ اللهِ وَأُبْرِئُ الْأَكْمَهَ وَالْأَبْرَصَ وَأُحْيِ الْمَوْتى بِإِذْنِ اللهِ (...and He will teach him the Book and wisdom, the Torah and the Evangel, and [he will be] an apostle to the Children of Israel, [saying]: “I have certainly brought you a sign from your Lord: I will create for you out of clay the form of a bird, then I will breathe into it, and it will become a bird by Allah’s leave. And I heal the blind and the leper and I revive the dead by Allah’s leave.”) (Al-Imran: 45-50).

Contemplating the context of the verses clarifies the reason for counting these signs—which ostensibly pertain specifically to the Messiah—as a blessing (النعمة) upon both him and his mother, as suggested by the verses of Al-Imran; for glad tidings (البشارة) are only given regarding a blessing. The matter is indeed so, for the signs and gifts specific to the Messiah (عليه السلام)—such as being born without a father, being strengthened by the Holy Spirit (روح القدس), creating birds, healing the blind and the leper, and reviving the dead by the leave of Allah, the Glorified—are themselves a mark of honor (الكرامة) for Mary just as they are a mark of honor for Jesus (عليه السلام). Thus, they are both recipients of the divine blessing, as He says: نِعْمَتِي عَلَيْكَ وَعَلى والِدَتِكَ (My blessing upon you and upon your mother).

The Exalted alludes to this in His saying: وَجَعَلْناها وَابْنَها آيَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ (and We made her and her son a sign for all the worlds) (Al-Anbiya: 91), where He counts them both together as a single sign (آية), not two.

Regarding His saying: إِذْ أَيَّدْتُكَ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ تُكَلِّمُ النَّاسَ (when I strengthened you with the Holy Spirit, so that you would speak to the people), it appears that the strengthening by the Holy Spirit (روح القدس) — the divine agency of purity and support — is the enabling cause for his speaking to people in the cradle. For this reason, He joined His saying تُكَلِّمُ النَّاسَ (you would speak to the people) to the preceding clause without a coordinating conjunction, indicating that the strengthening and the speaking together constitute a single matter composed of a cause (السبب) and an effect (المسبب). In other places in His speech, He suffices by mentioning one of the two matters instead of the other, such as His saying in the verses of Al-Imran quoted earlier: وَيُكَلِّمُ النَّاسَ فِي الْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلاً (and he will speak to people in the cradle and in adulthood), and His saying: وَآتَيْنا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ الْبَيِّناتِ وَأَيَّدْناهُ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ (and We gave Jesus, son of Mary, manifest proofs and confirmed him with the Holy Spirit) (Al-Baqarah: 253).

Furthermore, if the strengthening by the Holy Spirit referred to the matter of revelation (الوحي) through the mediation of the Spirit, it would not be unique to Jesus son of Mary (عليه السلام), as all other messengers would share in it, whereas the context of the verse resists such an interpretation.

Regarding His saying: وَإِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَالتَّوْراةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ (and when I taught you the Book and wisdom, the Torah and the Evangel), it is possible to infer from this that he (عليه السلام) received the knowledge of all these through a single reception by a single divine command, without gradualness (التدريج) or multiplicity. This is also suggested by the grouping of all these terms and their introduction with the word "when" (إذ) without repeating it.

Similarly, His saying: إِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِي فَتَنْفُخُ فِيها فَتَكُونُ طَيْراً بِإِذْنِي وَتُبْرِئُ الْأَكْمَهَ وَالْأَبْرَصَ بِإِذْنِي (when you would create out of clay the form of a bird by My leave, and you would breathe into it and it would become a bird by My leave, and you would heal the blind and the leper by My leave). The context, by virtue of not repeating the word "when" (إذ), suggests that the creation of the bird and the healing of the blind and leper were concurrent in time. The fact that the creation of the bird is concluded by mentioning "leave" (الإذن) without sufficing with the "leave" mentioned at the end of the sentence is due to the magnitude of the act of creation by the bestowal of life. Thus, special care was given to it, and the mention of "leave" was specified immediately after it without waiting for the end of the statement, so as to protect the hearts of the listeners from the thought occurring to them that someone other than Allah, the Exalted, could independently bestow life, or to prevent this thought from lingering even for a few moments. And Allah knows best.

Regarding His saying: وَإِذْ تُخْرِجُ الْمَوْتى بِإِذْنِي (and when you brought forth the dead by My leave), "bringing forth the dead" is a metaphor for reviving them. There is a clear emphasis here that the revival that occurred at his hands (عليه السلام) was the revival of the entombed dead by bestowing life upon them and bringing them out of their graves into worldly life. The phrasing also indicates frequency or plurality (الكثرة). Further discussion regarding these verses has already been provided in the commentary on the verses of Al-Imran, so refer to that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ كَفَفْتُ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ عَنْكَ (and when I held back the Children of Israel from you...). This indicates that they intended harm toward him, but Allah restrained them from it. This corresponds to what Allah mentioned in Surah Al-Imran in the accounts of him (عليه السلام): وَمَكَرُوا وَمَكَرَ اللهُ وَاللهُ خَيْرُ الْماكِرِينَ (Then they plotted [against Jesus], and Allah also devised, and Allah is the best of devisers) (Al-Imran: 54).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى الْحَوارِيِّينَ (And when I inspired the Disciples...). This verse corresponds to the verses of Surah Al-Imran: فَلَمَّا أَحَسَّ عِيسى مِنْهُمُ الْكُفْرَ قالَ مَنْ أَنْصارِي إِلَى اللهِ قالَ الْحَوارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنْصارُ اللهِ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ وَاشْهَدْ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ (So when Jesus sensed faithlessness on their part, he said, ‘Who will be my helpers [on the way] toward Allah?’ The Disciples said, ‘We will be helpers of Allah. We have faith in Allah, and bear witness that we are muslims.’) (Al-Imran: 52).

From here it becomes clear that this faith (الإيمان) mentioned in the verse: وَإِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى الْحَوارِيِّينَ أَنْ آمِنُوا بِي وَبِرَسُولِي قالُوا آمَنَّا (And when I inspired the Disciples, ‘Have faith in Me and My Apostle,’ they said, ‘We have faith...’) is different from their initial faith in him (عليه السلام). For the apparent meaning of His saying in the verse of Al-Imran: فَلَمَّا أَحَسَّ عِيسى مِنْهُمُ الْكُفْرَ (So when Jesus sensed faithlessness on their part) is that it occurred toward the end of the days of his mission, while the Disciples (الحواريون) — the elite and sincere companions of Jesus — were the foremost in faith and had been constantly with him.

Furthermore, the apparent meaning of His saying in the verse of Al-Imran: قالَ مَنْ أَنْصارِي إِلَى اللهِ قالَ الْحَوارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنْصارُ اللهِ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ وَاشْهَدْ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ (he said, ‘Who will be my helpers [on the way] toward Allah?’ The Disciples said, ‘We will be helpers of Allah. We have faith in Allah, and bear witness that we are muslims’) is that the call was made to take a covenant (الميثاق) for the support of Allah's religion, not for the origin of faith in Allah. For this reason, they concluded the verse by saying: وَاشْهَدْ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ (and bear witness that we are muslims), which refers to submission (التسليم) to the command of Allah by establishing His mission and enduring harm for His sake. All of this naturally follows the origin of faith in Allah.

Thus, it is clear that what is meant by His saying: وَإِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى الْحَوارِيِّينَ (And when I inspired the Disciples...) is the story of taking the covenant from the Disciples. There are other discussions regarding this verse that have already passed in the commentary of Surah Al-Imran.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of transmission from Abu Ya'qub al-Baghdadi, who said: Ibn al-Sikkit said to Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه السلام): "Why did Allah send Moses son of Imran (عليه السلام) with the white hand, the staff, and the tool of sorcery; and He sent Jesus with the tool of medicine; and He sent Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with speech and oratory?"

Abu al-Hasan (عليه السلام) replied: إن الله تعالى لما بعث موسى عليه‌السلام ـ كان الأغلب على أهل عصره السحر ـ فأتاهم من عند الله تعالى ـ بما لم يكن عند القوم وفي وسعهم مثله ، وبما أبطل به سحرهم ، وأثبت به الحجة عليهم ؛ وإن الله تعالى بعث عيسى ـ في وقت ظهرت فيه الزمانات ـ واحتاج الناس إلى الطب ـ فأتاهم من عند الله تعالى بما لم يكن عندهم مثله ، وبما أحيا لهم الموتى ، وأبرأ الأكمه والأبرص بإذن الله ، وأثبت به الحجة عليهم ؛ وإن الله تعالى بعث محمدا صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله في وقت ـ كان الأغلب على أهل عصره الخطب والكلام والشعر ـ فأتاهم من كتاب الله والموعظة والحكمة ـ بما أبطل به قولهم ، وأثبت به الحجة عليهم. — "When Allah, the Exalted, sent Moses (عليه السلام), sorcery was the dominant craft among the people of his time. So he brought them from Allah, the Exalted, that which the people did not possess and the like of which was not within their power, and by which he nullified their sorcery and established the proof (الحجة) against them. And Allah, the Exalted, sent Jesus at a time when chronic illnesses had appeared and people were in need of medicine. So he brought them from Allah, the Exalted, that which they did not possess the like of, and by which he revived the dead for them and healed the blind and the leper by the leave of Allah, and established the proof against them. And Allah, the Exalted, sent Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) at a time when oratory, speech, and poetry were dominant among the people of his time. So he brought them from the Book of Allah, exhortation, and wisdom that which nullified their speech and established the proof against them."

Ibn al-Sikkit said: "I have never seen anyone like you this day. So what is the proof (الحجة) for creation today?" He replied: العقل يعرف به الصادق على الله ـ فيصدقه والكاذب على الله فيكذبه — "The intellect (العقل); through it, the one who speaks the truth about Allah is recognized and thus believed, and the one who lies about Allah is recognized and thus rejected." Ibn al-Sikkit said: "By Allah, this is the answer."

In al-Kafi, from Muhammad ibn Yahya, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from Abu Jamilah, from Aban ibn Taghlib and others, from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام): That he was asked whether Jesus son of Mary ever revived anyone after their death such that they ate, received provision, lived for a duration, and had children? He replied: نعم ـ إنه كان له صديق مواخ له في الله تبارك وتعالى ، وكان عيسى عليه‌السلام يمر به وينزل عليه ، وإن عيسى غاب عنه حينا ـ ثم مر به ليسلم عليه فخرجت عليه أمه فسألها عنه ـ فقالت له : مات يا رسول الله ، فقال : أتحبين أن تراه؟ قالت : نعم. فقال : إذا كان غدا أتيتك ـ حتى أحييه لك بإذن الله تعالى. — "Yes. He had a friend who was a brother to him in Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He. Jesus (عليه السلام) used to pass by him and stay with him. Jesus was absent from him for a time, then he passed by to greet him, and his mother came out to him. He asked her about him, and she said to him: 'He has died, O Messenger of Allah.' He said: 'Would you like to see him?' She said: 'Yes.' He said: 'When it is tomorrow, I shall come to you so that I may revive him for you by the leave of Allah, the Exalted.'"

فلما كان من الغد أتاها فقال لها : انطلقي معي إلى قبره فانطلقا ـ حتى أتيا قبره فوقف عليه عيسى عليه‌السلام ـ ثم دعا الله عز وجل فانفرج القبر فخرج ابنها حيا ـ فلما رأته أمه ورءاها بكيا فرحمهما عيسى عليه‌السلام ـ فقال له عيسى : أتحب أن تبقى مع أمك في الدنيا؟ فقال : يا رسول الله ـ بأكل ورزق ومدة أم بغير أكل ورزق ومدة؟ فقال له عيسى عليه‌السلام : بأكل ورزق ومدة ـ تعمر عشرين سنة وتزوج ويولد لك ، قال : نعم إذا. قال : فدفعه عيسى عليه‌السلام إلى أمه فعاش عشرين سنة وولد له. — "When the next day came, he went to her and said: 'Come with me to his grave.' So they went until they reached his grave. Jesus (عليه السلام) stood over it and then supplicated to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. The grave opened and her son came out alive. When his mother saw him and he saw her, they wept, and Jesus (عليه السلام) had mercy on them. Jesus said to him: 'Would you like to remain with your mother in the world?' He said: 'O Messenger of Allah, with eating, provision, and a lifespan, or without eating, provision, and a lifespan?' Jesus (عليه السلام) said to him: 'With eating, provision, and a lifespan; you shall live for twenty years, marry, and have children.' He said: 'Yes, then.' So Jesus (عليه السلام) handed him over to his mother, and he lived for twenty years and had children."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-San'ani, from his father, who said: I asked Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) about إِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى الْحَوارِيِّينَ (When I inspired the Disciples). He said: ألهموا — "They were inspired (ألهموا)."

I say: The use of "revelation" (الوحي) in the sense of inspiration (الإلهام) — the casting of an idea into the heart by divine grace — occurs in the Quran in several places, such as His saying: وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلى أُمِّ مُوسى أَنْ أَرْضِعِيهِ (We revealed to Moses’ mother, [saying], ‘Suckle him...’) (Al-Qasas: 7), and His saying: وَأَوْحى رَبُّكَ إِلَى النَّحْلِ أَنِ اتَّخِذِي مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً (And your Lord inspired the bee [saying]: ‘Make your homes in the mountains...’) (Al-Nahl: 68), and His saying regarding the earth: بِأَنَّ رَبَّكَ أَوْحى لَها (because your Lord will have inspired her) (Al-Zilzal: 5).

Ayahs

إِذْ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ يَـٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ أَن يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةً مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ قَالَ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١١٢)
When the Disciples said, ‘O Jesus son of Mary! Can your Lord send down to us a table from the sky?’ Said he, ‘Be wary of Allah, should you be faithful.’ (112)
قَالُوا۟ نُرِيدُ أَن نَّأْكُلَ مِنْهَا وَتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُنَا وَنَعْلَمَ أَن قَدْ صَدَقْتَنَا وَنَكُونَ عَلَيْهَا مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (١١٣)
They said, ‘We desire to eat from it, and our hearts will be at rest: we shall know that you have told us the truth, and we shall be among the witnesses to it.’ (113)
قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ٱللَّهُمَّ رَبَّنَآ أَنزِلْ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةً مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ تَكُونُ لَنَا عِيدًا لِّأَوَّلِنَا وَءَاخِرِنَا وَءَايَةً مِّنكَ ۖ وَٱرْزُقْنَا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ (١١٤)
Said Jesus son of Mary, ‘O Allah! Our Lord! Send down to us a table from the sky, to be a festival for us, for the first ones and the last ones among us and as a sign from You, and provide for us; for You are the best of providers.’ (114)
قَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مُنَزِّلُهَا عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بَعْدُ مِنكُمْ فَإِنِّىٓ أُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًا لَّآ أُعَذِّبُهُۥٓ أَحَدًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١١٥)
Allah said, ‘I will indeed send it down to you. But should any of you disbelieve after this, I will indeed punish him with a punishment such as I do not punish anyone in all creation.’ (115)

(Commentary)

The verses recount the story of the descent of the Table (المائدة) to the Messiah (عليه السلام) and his companions. Although the text does not explicitly state that Allah sent it down to them, the final verse contains a definitive promise from Him to send it down without any qualification, and the Exalted has described Himself as One who does not break His promise.

The claim made by some—that "they asked Jesus (عليه السلام) to be excused from the request after hearing the severe threat from Allah the Exalted against whoever among them disbelieves after the descent of the Table"—is a claim without evidence from the Book or any reliable narration (حديث).

This has been reported from a group of exegetes, including Mujahid and al-Hasan. However, there is no proof in their words nor in the words of others. Even if their statements were considered narrations, they would be among the suspended reports (الموقوفات) which carry no authority due to their weakness. Furthermore, they are contradicted by other narrations indicating its descent. Moreover, even if such reports were authentic, they would only be solitary reports (الآحاد) which are not relied upon in matters other than legal rulings (الأحكام).

Some might argue against its descent by claiming that Christians are unaware of it and their sacred books are devoid of its mention; they argue that had it descended, there would have been a strong motivation to mention it in their books and preserve it among them through continuous practice, just as they preserved the Lord's Supper. However, anyone expert in the history of the spread of Christianity and the emergence of the Gospels pays no heed to such talk. Their books were neither written nor preserved through mass-transmission (التواتر) up to the time of Jesus (عليه السلام), nor does the present Christianity connect back to his time in a way that would make it useful regarding what they pass down hand-to-hand, or what they are ignorant of regarding that which is attributed to the Messianic call or related to it.

Yes, it occurs in some Gospels that the Messiah fed his disciples and a multitude of people with a few loaves of bread and fish by way of a miracle. However, the story does not correspond to what the Quran narrates in any of its specific details. It is recorded in the Gospel of John, Chapter Six, as follows:

"(1) After these things Jesus went over the sea of Galilee, which is the sea of Tiberias. (2) And a great multitude followed him, because they saw his miracles which he did on them that were diseased. (3) And Jesus went up into a mountain, and there he sat with his disciples. (4) And the passover, a feast of the Jews, was nigh. (5) When Jesus then lifted up his eyes, and saw a great company come unto him, he saith unto Philip, Whence shall we buy bread, that these may eat? (6) And this he said to prove him: for he himself knew what he would do. (7) Philip answered him, Two hundred pennyworth of bread is not sufficient for them, that every one of them may take a little. (8) One of his disciples, Andrew, Simon Peter's brother, saith unto him, (9) There is a lad here, which hath five barley loaves, and two small fishes: but what are they among so many? (10) And Jesus said, Make the men sit down. Now there was much grass in the place. So the men sat down, in number about five thousand. (11) And Jesus took the loaves; and when he had given thanks, he distributed to the disciples, and the disciples to them that were set down; and likewise of the fishes as much as they would. (12) When they were filled, he said unto his disciples, Gather up the fragments that remain, that nothing be lost. (13) Therefore they gathered them together, and filled twelve baskets with the fragments of the five barley loaves, which remained over and above unto them that had eaten. (14) Then those men, when they had seen the miracle that Jesus did, said, This is of a truth that prophet that should come into the world. (15) When Jesus therefore perceived that they would come and take him by force, to make him a king, he departed again into a mountain himself alone."

Furthermore, reflecting on this story within the context (سياق) narrated in the speech of the Exalted leads to another avenue of inquiry. The question mentioned at its beginning appears, on the surface, to lack the proper etiquette (الأدب) that must be maintained toward Allah, the Glorified. The discourse concludes with a threat from the Exalted against whoever disbelieves in this sign—a threat the like of which is not found among any of the signs (الآيات) that Allah the Glorified reserved for His prophets or those suggested to them by their nations, such as the suggestions of the nations of Noah, Hud, Salih, Shu'ayb, Moses, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

Was this because the Disciples (الحواريون)—who were the questioners—behaved improperly in their request? For their phrasing was that of one who doubts the power of Allah, the Glorified. Yet, in the suggestions of previous nations, there was such insult to the station of their Lord and mockery of their prophets—as well as what is recounted in the Quran regarding the tyrants among the people of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Jews contemporary to him—that was far more insolent and heinous than this!

Or was it because they were believers before the request and the descent, such that if they were to disbelieve after the descent and the witnessing of the magnificent sign, they would deserve this threat in its severity? While disbelief after witnessing a magnificent sign is indeed a great rebellion and transgression (عتو وطغيان), it is not unique to them. Other nations have similar examples, yet they were never threatened with such a warning—not even those who apostatized after being established in a station of proximity and realizing the signs of Allah the Glorified, such as the one Allah the Glorified mentions in His saying: وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ الَّذِي آتَيْناهُ آياتِنا فَانْسَلَخَ مِنْها فَأَتْبَعَهُ الشَّيْطانُ فَكانَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ — "Relate to them the story of him to whom We gave Our signs, but he cast them off. Thereupon Satan pursued him, and he became one of the perverse." (7:175).

What can be said here is that this story, by virtue of the question that initiated it, is distinguished by a meaning unique to it among all the miracles of the prophets that were brought forth at the suggestion of their nations or due to other necessitating circumstances.

This is because the miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزة) recounted in the Divine Speech are of several types: either signs Allah gave to the prophets when He commissioned them to serve as a supporting proof for their prophethood or messengership—such as Moses (عليه السلام) being given the white hand and the staff, Jesus (عليه السلام) being given the raising of the dead, the creation of birds, and the healing of the blind and the leper, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being given the Quran. These are signs given for the need of the call to faith and to complete the proof (إتمام الحجة) against the disbelievers, so that "he who perished might perish by a clear proof, and he who lived might live by a clear proof."

Or they are miraculous signs brought by the prophets and messengers at the suggestion of the disbelievers, such as the She-camel of Salih. Included in this category are the terrifying and punishing signs used in the call, such as the signs of Moses (عليه السلام) against the people of Pharaoh—the locusts, lice, frogs, and others among the seven signs—the flood of Noah, the earthquake of Thamud, the screaming wind of 'Ad, and so forth. These too are signs related to the stubborn deniers.

Or they are signs Allah showed to the believers due to a pressing need or a necessitating circumstance, such as the gushing of springs from the rock and the descent of manna and quails upon the Children of Israel in the wilderness, the raising of the Mount over their heads, and the parting of the sea for their salvation from Pharaoh and his works. These signs occurred to strike fear into the arrogant sinners or as an honor (كرامة) for the believers to fulfill the word of mercy regarding them, without them having suggested them.

To this category belong the promises Allah made in His Book to the believers as an honor to His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), such as the promise of the conquest of Mecca, the loathing of the polytheists among the disbelievers of Quraysh, the victory of the Romans, and so on.

These are the types of signs recounted in the Quran and mentioned in the Divine Teaching. As for suggesting a sign after a sign has already descended, it is considered a form of caprice (التهوس) which the Divine Teaching regards as nonsense (الهجر) that is not to be heeded—such as the People of the Book suggesting that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) bring down a book to them from heaven despite the presence of the Quran before them. The Exalted said: يَسْئَلُكَ أَهْلُ الْكِتابِ أَنْ تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ كِتاباً مِنَ السَّماءِ فَقَدْ سَأَلُوا مُوسى أَكْبَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ فَقالُوا أَرِنَا اللهَ جَهْرَةً — "The People of the Book ask you to bring down for them a Book from the sky. Certainly they asked Moses for something greater than that, for they said, ‘Show us Allah visibly.’" — until He said — لكِنِ اللهُ يَشْهَدُ بِما أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكَ أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ يَشْهَدُونَ وَكَفى بِاللهِ شَهِيداً — "But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you—He sent it down with His knowledge—and the angels [also] bear witness, and Allah suffices as witness." (4:153-166).

Similarly, the polytheists asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) for the descent of angels or to see their Lord, the Exalted and Sanctified. The Exalted said: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرى رَبَّنا لَقَدِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْا عُتُوًّا كَبِيراً — "Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why are the angels not sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?’ Certainly they are too proud of themselves and have become terribly rebellious." (21:21). And the Exalted said: وَقالُوا ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعامَ وَيَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْواقِ لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيراً ، أَوْ يُلْقى إِلَيْهِ كَنْزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْها وَقالَ الظَّالِمُونَ إِنْ تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلاً مَسْحُوراً ، انْظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلاً — "And they say, ‘What sort of messenger is this who eats food and walks in the marketplaces? Why has not an angel been sent down to him to be a warner with him? Or [why is] not a treasure thrown to him, or [why does] he [not] have a garden from which he may eat?’ And the wrongdoers say, ‘You are just following a bewitched man.’ Look how they coin metaphors for you! So they have gone astray, and cannot find a way." (25:7-9), among many other verses.

All of this is because the purpose of descending a sign is the manifestation of truth and the completion of proof. Once it has descended, the truth has appeared and the proof is complete. If the request for a sign is repeated after it has already descended and the objective has been achieved, it has no meaning other than trifling with the signs of Allah, playing with the Divine Station, and vacillating in acceptance; in this lies the greatest rebellion and arrogance.

If this were to come from believers, the sin would be denser and the transgression greater. For what does a believer do with the descent of a heavenly sign when he is already a believer—especially if he is among those who witnessed the signs of Allah and believed because of that witnessing? Is it not most similar to what the people of desire and the affluent suggest in gatherings of intimacy and amusement to magicians or practitioners of strange exercises—that they should brighten their lives by presenting them with the most amazing sorcery and strange acts they can perform?

What the literal meaning of the Exalted's saying: إِذْ قالَ الْحَوارِيُّونَ يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ أَنْ يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا مائِدَةً — "When the Disciples said, ‘O Jesus son of Mary! Can your Lord send down to us a table from the sky?’" (5:112) provides is that they suggested to the Messiah (عليه السلام) that he show them a specific sign, even though they were his elite Disciples who had already seen those magnificent signs and manifest honors. For he (عليه السلام) was not sent to his people except with miraculous signs, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted: وَرَسُولاً إِلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ أَنِّي قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِآيَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ أَنِّي أَخْلُقُ لَكُمْ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ فَأَنْفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْراً بِإِذْنِ اللهِ — "and [he will be] a messenger to the Children of Israel, [saying,] ‘I have certainly brought you a sign from your Lord: I will create for you out of clay the form of a bird, then I will breathe into it, and it will become a bird by Allah’s leave...’" (3:49).

How can it be conceived that one who believed in the Messiah (عليه السلام) would not have encountered a sign from him, when he (عليه السلام) by his very existence was a sign—Allah having created him without a father, supporting him with the Holy Spirit, and having him speak to people in the cradle and in maturity? He remained honored by sign after sign until Allah raised him to Himself and concluded his affair with the most amazing sign.

Thus, their suggestion of a sign they chose for themselves after all those numerous signs was of the nature of suggesting sign after sign, and they had committed a grave matter. For this reason, Jesus (عليه السلام) rebuked them by saying: اتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "Be God-conscious, if you are believers."

For this very reason, they justified what they had suggested to him and explained their words a second time in a way that broke the force of what their initial absolute statement implied, removing its sharpness. They said: نُرِيدُ أَنْ نَأْكُلَ مِنْها وَتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُنا وَنَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ صَدَقْتَنا وَنَكُونَ عَلَيْها مِنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ — "They said, ‘We desire to eat from it, and that our hearts may be at rest: and that we may know that you have told us the truth, and that we may be among the witnesses to it.’" (5:113). They added to the purpose of eating other objectives to justify their suggestion, intending thereby that this suggestion was not of the nature of amusing themselves with strange things or trifling with Divine signs, but rather had intended benefits: the perfection of their knowledge, the removal of evil thoughts from their hearts, and their witnessing of it.

However, despite this, they did not omit the mention of the desire to eat, and therein lay the fault. Had they said: "We desire to eat from it so that our hearts may be at rest, etc.," what befell them would not have been necessary. But they said: "We desire to eat from it and that our hearts may be at rest, etc." The first phrasing would have cut off all roots of caprice and recklessness, unlike the second.

When they persisted, Jesus (عليه السلام) granted what they suggested and requested, and he asked his Lord to honor them with it. It is a miracle unique in its kind to his community, for it is the only sign that descended to them upon a suggestion regarding a matter that was not outwardly necessary—namely, the believers eating from it. Therefore, he (عليه السلام) framed it in a way that made it appropriate to direct the request toward the Presence of Grandeur and Majesty, saying: اللهُمَّ رَبَّنا أَنْزِلْ عَلَيْنا مائِدَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ تَكُونُ لَنا عِيداً لِأَوَّلِنا وَآخِرِنا — "O Allah, our Lord! Send down to us a table from the sky, to be a festival for us, for the first of us and the last of us..." (5:114). He framed it as a festival (عيد). A festival for a people is the day on which they attained a gift or a distinction unique to them among people, and the descent of the Table upon them was characterized by this description.

When Jesus asked his Lord what he asked—and far be it from him to ask except what he knows is hoped to be answered and that his Lord would not loathe him or disgrace him, and far be it from his Lord to turn him back disappointed in his prayer—his Lord answered his prayer. However, He stipulated for it a condition: that whoever among them disbelieves in it shall face a punishment unique to him among all people, just as the sign was a special sign for them in which no other nation shared. Allah the Glorified said: إِنِّي مُنَزِّلُها عَلَيْكُمْ فَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بَعْدُ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنِّي أُعَذِّبُهُ عَذاباً لا أُعَذِّبُهُ أَحَداً مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ — "Allah said, ‘I will indeed send it down to you. But should any of you disbelieve after this, I will indeed punish him with a punishment such as I do not punish anyone in all the worlds.’" (5:115).

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قالَ الْحَوارِيُّونَ يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ أَنْ يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا مائِدَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ — "إِذْ" (When) is an adverbial particle (ظرف) related to an omitted verb, the estimation being "Mention when they said..." or something similar. Some have held that it is related to His saying in the previous verse: قالُوا آمَنَّا (They said, ‘We believe’), meaning the Disciples said: "We believe in Allah and bear witness that we are Muslims" at the time they said to Jesus: "Can your Lord send down to us a table from the sky?" The implication being that they were not sincere in their claim, nor serious in making Jesus (عليه السلام) witness their submission to him.

However, this is contrary to the apparent context. How could their faith be insincere when Allah mentioned that He Himself inspired them to believe in Him and His Messenger, and He the Exalted mentions this as a favor upon Jesus (عليه السلام)? Furthermore, there would be no reason then for the explicit mention in His saying: إِذْ قالَ الْحَوارِيُّونَ (When the Disciples said...).

The "Table" (المائدة) is a tray (خوان) when it has food on it. Al-Raghib said: "The ma'idah is the platter upon which food is placed, and each of them is called a ma'idah. It is said: madani yamiduni, meaning 'he fed me'."

The core of the question recounted from them in the verse—their saying: هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ أَنْ يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا مائِدَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ —is, according to its immediate literal meaning, something that the intellect finds unlikely to have been uttered by the Disciples. They were the companions of the Messiah, his students, and his intimates who remained with him, drawing from the lights of his knowledge and wisdom, and following his manners and traces. Faith, even at its lowest level, alerts a person to the fact that Allah the Glorified is powerful over all things, that incapacity is impossible for Him, and that He cannot be overcome by inability. How then could it be permissible for them to ask their Messenger about his Lord's ability to send down a table from the sky?

For this reason, al-Kisa'i, one of the seven reciters, read it as: "Can you [O Jesus] (هل تستطيع) [ask] your Lord (ربَّك)," with the prefix of the second person and "your Lord" in the accusative case as an object (مفعولية). That is: "Can you ask your Lord?" The verb that would make the object accusative was omitted, and "can you" (تستطيع) was put in its place, or it is the object of an omitted verb only.

The exegetes have differed in interpreting this, with most of them proceeding on the basis that what is intended is something other than the immediate literal meaning of doubting Allah's power, in order to keep their station free from such absurd ignorance.

The most plausible interpretation is that "ability" (الاستطاعة) in the verse is a metaphor (كناية) for the requirement of wisdom (المصلحة) and the occurrence of permission, just as possibility, power, and strength are used metaphorically for that. For example, it is said: "The king cannot (لا يقدر) listen to every needy person," meaning that the king's interest prevents him from doing so, otherwise, the act of listening itself is within his power. It is said: "The wealthy man cannot (لا يستطيع) give to every beggar," meaning the interest of preserving wealth does not require it. It is said: "The scholar cannot (لا يمكن) spread all that he knows," meaning he is prevented from doing so by the interest of religion or the interest of the people and the prevailing order among them. One of us says to his companion: "Can you (هل تستطيع) go with me to so-and-so?" The question is about the ability according to wisdom and interest, not according to the fundamental power to walk.

There are other interpretations they have mentioned:

One is that this question was for the purpose of attaining the "tranquility of visual certainty" (الاطمئنان بإيمان العيان), not due to doubt in Allah's power. Thus, it is on the level of the saying of Abraham (عليه السلام) in what Allah recounted from him: رَبِّ أَرِنِي كَيْفَ تُحْيِ الْمَوْتى قالَ أَوَلَمْ تُؤْمِنْ قالَ بَلى وَلكِنْ لِيَطْمَئِنَّ قَلْبِي — "‘My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead.’ He said, ‘Do you not believe?’ He said, ‘Yes indeed, but in order that my heart may be at rest.’" (2:260).

The critique of this is that the mere correctness of asking for a sign to increase faith and tranquility of the heart does not justify interpreting their question in this way. Their infallibility (عصمة) has not been established like that of Abraham (عليه السلام) so as to serve as an external proof necessitating the interpretation of their words in a way that has no flaw. Rather, the evidence is to the contrary, as they did not say: "We desire to eat from it so that our hearts may be at rest," as Abraham (عليه السلام) said: "Yes indeed, but in order that my heart may be at rest." Instead, they said: "We desire to eat from it and that our hearts may be at rest," thus counting the eating as an objective in its own right.

Furthermore, this interpretation only seeks to clear their hearts from the taint of doubt in Allah's power, while the flaw in the literal phrasing remains as it is.

Moreover, it has already been mentioned in the commentary on the saying of the Exalted: وَإِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّ أَرِنِي كَيْفَ تُحْيِ الْمَوْتى that his (عليه السلام) intention was not to witness the dead becoming clothed with life after death, as this interpretation assumes. Had it been so, it would have been of the nature of seeking a sign after visual certainty while in the station of direct address with his Lord. Rather, his intention was to witness the modality (كيفية) of reviving in the sense previously explained.

Another interpretation is that it is a question about the act rather than the power over it, so it was expressed by its necessary concomitant.

The critique is that there is no evidence for this. Even if granted, it would only negate from them ignorance of absolute Divine power, while the conflict between the literal expression and the etiquette of servitude (الأدب العبودي) remains.

Another is that there is an omission in the speech, the estimation being: "Can you [ask] your Lord?" This is supported by the reading "Can you [ask] your Lord" (هل تستطيع ربك). The meaning would be: "Are you able to ask Him without any deterrent turning you away from that?"

The critique is that omission and estimation do not turn the phrase هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ into "Can you ask your Lord" by any assumed means, due to the difference in the verb between the two readings in terms of third-person (غيبة) and second-person (حضور). Estimation never transforms the third-person into the second-person. If it must be so, let it be said that it is of the nature of attributing the act ascribed to Jesus (عليه السلام) to his Lord because his act is Allah's act, or because everything belonging to him (عليه السلام) belongs to Allah the Glorified. This interpretation, besides being invalid because only those acts of prophets and messengers are attributed to Allah that do not imply deficiency or limitation in His Presence—such as guidance and knowledge—whereas the concomitants of their servitude and humanity—such as incapacity, poverty, eating, and drinking—cannot be correctly attributed to Him at all, leaves the problem of the literal phrasing as it is.

Another is that "ability" (الاستطاعة) here means "obedience" (الإطاعة). The meaning would be: "Will your Lord obey you and answer your prayer if you ask Him for that?" The critique is that this is like replacing a problem with one that is even more problematic. For inquiring about Allah the Glorified's obedience to His Messenger and His compliance (انقياد) to him is more heinous and atrocious than inquiring about His ability.

Some have defended this view, stating in its formulation: "Ability (استطاعة) and obedience (إطاعة) are from the root taw' (طوع), the opposite of compulsion (karh). Thus, obeying a command is doing it out of pleasure and choice. The istif'al form in this root is like the istif'al form in the root of 'answering' (إجابة). If the meaning of istijabah is 'He answered his prayer or request,' then the meaning of istita'ah is 'He obeyed him,' meaning He complied with him and became within his obedience or obedient to him. The sin and ta in both roots are for their most famous meaning, which is 'seeking' (الطلب). But it is a seeking that entered upon an omitted verb indicated by the mentioned one that follows the omitted. The meaning of 'he was able (استطاع) of the thing' is: 'he sought and attempted for that thing to be obedient to him, so it obeyed him and complied.' And the meaning of 'he answered (استجاب)' is: 'he was asked for something and it was sought from him that he answer to it, so he answered.'"

He said: "By this precise explanation, you understand the correctness of the saying of those exegetes who said: 'يَسْتَطِيعُ' here means 'obeys' (يطيع), and the meaning of 'obeys' is: 'he acts by choice and pleasure, not by compulsion.' Thus, the resulting meaning of the sentence is: 'Does your Lord please and choose to send down to us a table from the sky if we ask Him or you ask Him for that for us?'"

The critique is, firstly: He has brought nothing except by analogizing istata'a to istajaba and then giving the meaning of the latter to the former, which is a prohibited analogy in linguistics.

Secondly: The fact that ability and obedience both return, according to their root, to taw' (obedience/volition) as the opposite of karh (compulsion) does not necessitate that usage must follow the preservation of the original root meaning in all the developments that occur to it. Many roots have abandoned the specificity of their original meaning in the morphological forms that have occurred to them, similar to daraba and adraba, qabila and aqbala, qabbala, qabala, and istaqbala, according to the immediate understanding in usage.

Considering the original root in the study of linguistic derivations is only intended to ascertain the extent to which the original root lives among its derivatives according to the morphological developments that occur to it, or the expiration of its life span in terms of meaning and its change to another meaning—not that the rule of developments should be nullified and the original meaning preserved as long as the tongue moves. Understand this.

The consideration in a word is what it provides according to the living, circulating usage, not what the linguistic root provides. The word "ability" (الاستطاعة) has been used in the Speech of the Exalted in more than forty places, and in all of them, it means "power" (القدرة). The word "obedience" (الإطاعة) has been used in nearly seventy places, and in all of them, it means "compliance" (الانقياد). The word "volition" (الطوع) has been used where it has been used as the opposite of compulsion. How then is it permissible to take "can" (يستطيع) to mean "obeys" (يطيع), then "obeys" to mean "volition" (الطوع), and then judge that "can" in the verse means "pleases" (يرضى)?

As for the matter of ajaba and istajaba, they have both been used in the Speech of the Exalted with the same meaning, and the usage of istijabah has occurred in instances many times more than ijabah. You find istijabah in nearly thirty places, while you do not find ijabah in more than ten places. How then can ata'a and istata'a be analogized to them?

Their having the same meaning is only due to the application of two different nuances to a single instance. The meaning of ajaba is that the answer passed from the one asked to the asker. The meaning of istajaba is that the one asked sought the answer from himself and then delivered it to the asker.

From here it becomes clear that the way he explained istijabah—namely his saying: "and the meaning of istajaba is: he was asked for something and it was sought from him that he answer to it, so he answered"—is not as it should be. For the istif'al form is the seeking of an "act" (فعل), not the seeking of "do the act" (افعل), and this is manifest.

Thirdly: The context (السياق) does not suit this meaning. For if the meaning of their saying: "Can your Lord send down to us a table from the sky" was "Does your Lord please that we ask Him or you ask Him to send down to us a table from the sky," and their purpose in this question or descent was to increase in faith and have their hearts at rest, then what was the reason for Jesus' (عليه السلام) rebuke to them: "Be God-conscious, if you are believers"? And what was the reason for the Exalted's threat to those who disbelieve in it with a punishment such as He does not punish anyone in all the worlds? They would have said nothing but the truth and asked nothing but a legitimate question, and the Exalted has said: وَسْئَلُوا اللهَ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — "and ask Allah of His grace." (4:32).

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ اتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "He said, ‘Be God-conscious, if you are believers.’" This is a rebuke from him (عليه السلام) to them for what the literal sense of their words contained—the inquiry about his Lord's ability to send down the Table—for their words were suspicious in any case.

As for what we previously presented—that the fundamental reason for their being taken to task, which resulted in the severe threat at the end of the verses, is that they asked for a sign where there was no need for it and suggested something that was in the sense of trifling with the signs of Allah the Glorified, and then their expression in a way that immediately suggested they had not firmly resolved their hearts upon the Divine power—then the reason for his (عليه السلام) rebuke to them: "Be God-conscious, if you are believers" is even more manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا نُرِيدُ أَنْ نَأْكُلَ مِنْها وَتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُنا وَنَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ صَدَقْتَنا وَنَكُونَ عَلَيْها مِنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ — "They said, ‘We desire to eat from it, and that our hearts may be at rest: and that we may know that you have told us the truth, and that we may be among the witnesses to it.’" The context makes it clear that this saying of theirs was an excuse they offered to escape his (عليه السلام) rebuke. What they mentioned clearly relates to their suggestion of the sign of the descent of the Table, rather than what appeared from their saying: "Can your Lord send down...", which carried the meaning suggesting doubt in the absolute nature of power. This is also one of the evidences that the criterion for being taken to task in this instance is that they asked for sign upon sign without a need for it.

As for their saying: "We desire to eat from it, etc.", they enumerated four matters in explaining their purpose for suggesting the sign:

First: Eating. Their intention in mentioning it was that they did not intend thereby to play with the signs of Allah, but rather they wanted to eat from it, which is a rational purpose (غرض عقلائي). It has already been mentioned that this statement from them is like an admission of their deserving the rebuke from Jesus (عليه السلام) and the severe threat from Allah for whoever among them disbelieves in the sign of the Table.

Some have mentioned that the purpose of mentioning eating was to show that they were in dire need of food and could find nothing to satisfy their hunger. Others mentioned that the purpose was "We desire to seek blessing (نتبرك) by eating it." You know that the meaning established in each of these two views is something that the mere mention of eating does not indicate. Had that been their intention—and it is something by which the rebuke would be averted—the requirement of the station of excuse-making would have been to state it explicitly. Since nothing of that was mentioned despite the station's need for it if it were intended, then nothing is intended by eating except its absolute meaning insofar as it is a rational purpose that is one of the parts of their objective in suggesting the descent of the Table.

Second: The tranquility of the heart (اطمئنان القلب), which is its stillness through the repelling of thoughts that contradict sincerity and presence.

Third: The knowledge that he (عليه السلام) had told them the truth in what he conveyed to them from his Lord. The "knowledge" intended here is the certain knowledge (العلم اليقيني) that occurs in the heart after the departure of thoughts and psychic whispers from it. Or it is the knowledge that he had told them the truth in what he promised them of the fruits of faith, such as the answering of prayer, as some have mentioned. However, this is made unlikely by the fact that the Disciples were only asking for the descent of the Table from the sky through the prayer and request of Jesus (عليه السلام)—in short, through a miracle from him (عليه السلام). They had already seen many signs from him (عليه السلام), for he (عليه السلام) was never in his life anything but a companion to great Divine signs. He was not sent to his people nor did he call them to any call except with the signs of his Lord. Thus, they never ceased to see the fruits of his faith in the answering of prayer—if the fruit intended was the answering of his (عليه السلام) prayer. If the fruit intended was the answering of their own prayers, they did not ask for the descent of the sign through their own prayers, and it did not descend except through the prayer of Jesus (عليه السلام).

Fourth: That they be among the witnesses to it when witnessing is needed, such as witnessing before the deniers, and witnessing before Allah on the Day of Resurrection. Thus, what is intended is absolute witnessing. It is possible that what is intended is merely witnessing before Allah the Glorified, as occurred in some of their speech which Allah recounted when they said: رَبَّنا آمَنَّا بِما أَنْزَلْتَ وَاتَّبَعْنَا الرَّسُولَ فَاكْتُبْنا مَعَ الشَّاهِدِينَ — "Our Lord, we believe in what You have sent down, and we follow the apostle, so write us among the witnesses." (3:53).

The result is that—in the excuse they offered—they joined beautiful and pleasing matters to their other purpose, which was eating from the heavenly Table, in order to cut off the source of flaw from their suggestion of a sign after witnessing sufficient signs. Thus, Jesus (عليه السلام) granted their request after their persistence.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ اللهُمَّ رَبَّنا أَنْزِلْ عَلَيْنا مائِدَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ تَكُونُ لَنا عِيداً لِأَوَّلِنا وَآخِرِنا وَآيَةً مِنْكَ وَارْزُقْنا وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ — "Jesus son of Mary said, ‘O Allah, our Lord! Send down to us a table from the sky, to be a festival for us, for the first of us and the last of us, and a sign from You, and provide for us; for You are the best of providers.’" He (عليه السلام) included himself with them in the request for the Table, and he began by calling upon his Lord with a general term, saying: "O Allah, our Lord" (اللهُمَّ رَبَّنا), whereas they had said to him: "Can your Lord" (رَبُّكَ), so that the address would match the prayer.

This prayer is unique among all the prayers and requests recounted in the Quran from the prophets (عليه السلام) in that it begins with "O Allah, our Lord" (اللهُمَّ رَبَّنا), while others among their prayers begin with the word "Lord" (رب) or "our Lord" (ربنا). This is only due to the delicacy of the situation and the gravity of the occasion. Yes, in the types of praise recounted, there is something similar to this beginning, such as His saying: قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ — "Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah...’" (27:59), and His saying: قُلِ اللهُمَّ مالِكَ الْمُلْكِ — "Say, ‘O Allah, Master of all sovereignty...’" (3:26), and His saying: قُلِ اللهُمَّ فاطِرَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Say, ‘O Allah, Originator of the heavens and the earth...’" (39:46).

Then he (عليه السلام) mentioned a title for this descending Table, which was the purpose for him and his companions in asking for its descent: that it descend and be a festival (عيد) for him and for his entire community. The Disciples had not mentioned in their suggestion that they wanted a festival unique to them, but he (عليه السلام) framed what he asked with a general title and cast it in a beautiful mold to remove it from being a request for a sign despite the presence of great Divine signs before them and under their observation. Thus, it became a request pleasing to Allah, not conflicting with the station of Might and Grandeur. For a festival is of such a nature that it unites the word, renews the life of the nation, invigorates the souls of those who return to it, and announces the greatness of the religion whenever it returns.

For this reason, he said: عِيداً لِأَوَّلِنا وَآخِرِنا — "a festival for us, for the first of us and the last of us," meaning the first of our group from the nation and the last of those who join them—as indicated by the context—for "festival" ('id) is from "returning" ('awd), and it is not a festival unless it returns time after time, and among the successors after the predecessors without limit.

This festival is among what was unique to the people of Jesus (عليه السلام), just as they were unique in the type of this descending sign, as previously explained.

His saying: وَآيَةً مِنْكَ — "and a sign from You." Since he had already presented the request for the festival—which is a good and beautiful request upon which there is no blame—he followed it with its being a sign from Him, the Exalted, as if it were an additional benefit resulting from the original purpose, not intended alone such that blame or wrath would attach to it.
