The proof for this is His saying: وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ — "But were they to ascribe any partners [to Him], what they used to do would surely be null and void." He mentioned nothing that contradicts what He bestowed upon them of guidance except polytheism (الشِّرْكُ); thus, He guided them only to monotheism.

However, monotheism is such that its judgment permeates their actions and is established within them. The proof for this is His saying: لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — "what they used to do would surely be null and void." For if polytheism did not run through actions and seep into them, it would not necessitate their nullification; thus, the monotheism that opposes it is the same.

The meaning of monotheism permeating actions is that their forms represent monotheism and reflect it as a mirror reflects what is seen in it, such that if it were assumed that monotheism took a form, it would be those very actions; and if those actions were stripped into pure belief, they would be monotheism itself.

This meaning has many instances in spiritual traits; for you see the actions of the arrogant person representing the trait of arrogance and conceit in his soul. Similarly, the movements and stillness of the wretched, poor person reflect the humiliation and submissiveness in his secret heart, and so on.

Then the Exalted disciplined His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, commanding him to follow the guidance of the prophets who preceded him (peace be upon them), not to follow them themselves. Following (الِاقْتِدَاءُ) only occurs in action, not in belief, for belief is not voluntary in itself. That is, he should choose their righteous deeds based on monotheism, proceeding from them through a practical Divine discipline.

By this practical discipline (التَّأْدِيبُ الْعَمَلِيُّ), we mean what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ وَإِقَامَ الصَّلَاةِ وَإِيتَاءَ الزَّكَاةِ ۖ وَكَانُوا لَنَا عَابِدِينَ — "We made them imams, guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat, and they used to be Our worshippers" (21:73). The annexation of the verbal noun in His saying فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ (the performance of good deeds) etc., indicates that what is intended is the action proceeding from them—of good deeds they performed, prayer they maintained, and zakat they gave—not merely a hypothetical action. This revelation (الْوَحْيُ) pertaining to actions at the stage of their proceeding from them is a revelation of guidance and discipline (وَحْيُ تَسْدِيدٍ وَتَأْدِيبٍ), not the revelation of prophethood and legislation (وَحْيُ النُّبُوَّةِ وَالتَّشْرِيعِ). Had the intention been the revelation of prophethood, it would have been said: "And We revealed to them that they should perform good deeds, maintain prayer, and give zakat," as in His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ أَنِ اتَّبِعْ — "Then We revealed to you: ‘Follow...’" (16:123) and His saying: وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَأَخِيهِ أَن تَبَوَّآ لِقَوْمِكُمَا بِمِصْرَ بُيُوتًا وَاجْعَلُوا بُيُوتَكُمْ قِبْلَةً وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ — "We revealed to Moses and his brother [saying], ‘Settle your people in city houses, and make your houses places of worship, and maintain the prayer’" (10:87), among other verses. The meaning of the revelation of guidance (وَحْيُ التَّسْدِيدِ) is that Allah singles out one of His servants with a holy spirit (رُوحٌ قُدُسِيٌّ) that guides him in good deeds and in avoiding evil, just as the human spirit guides us in thinking about good and evil, and the animal spirit guides us in choosing what we desire of attraction and repulsion by will. The detailed discussion on this will come, if Allah wills.

In short, His saying: فَبِهُدَاهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ — "So follow their guidance" — is a general Divine discipline for him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with the adab of monotheism spread over the actions of the prophets (peace be upon them), which are purified from polytheism.

Then the Exalted said—after mentioning several of His prophets (peace be upon them)—in Surah Maryam: أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِن ذُرِّيَّةِ آدَمَ وَمِمَّنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّةِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْرَائِيلَ وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنَا وَاجْتَبَيْنَا ۚ إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتُ الرَّحْمَنِ خَرُّوا سُجَّدًا وَبُكِيًّا * فَخَلَفَ مِن بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَوَاتِ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا * إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَأُولَٰئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ شَيْئًا — "They are the ones whom Allah has blessed from among the prophets of Adam’s offspring, and of those We carried with Noah, and of Abraham’s offspring and Israel’s, and of those We guided and chose. When the signs of the All-beneficent were recited to them, they would fall down weeping in prostration. But they were succeeded by a generations who neglected the prayer and followed their appetites. So they will soon encounter leeway [in hell], barring those who repent, believe, and act righteously. Such will enter paradise, and they will not be wronged in the least" (19:58-60).

The Exalted mentioned their general adab in their lives: that they live upon submission (الْخُضُوعُ) in action and upon humility (الْخُشُوعُ) in heart for Allah, exalted is His Name. For their prostration upon the mention of the signs of Allah is an example of submission, and their weeping—which is due to the tenderness of the heart and the self-abasement of the soul—is a sign of humility. Both together are a metaphor for the dominance of the trait of servitude (الْعُبُودِيَّةُ) over their souls, such that whenever they are reminded by a sign from the signs of Allah, its effect appears in their outward being just as the trait has dominated their inward being. Thus, they are upon their Divine adab, which is the mark of servitude, when they are alone with their Lord and when they are alone with people; they live upon a Divine adab with their Lord and with all people.

Among the proofs that what is intended is the general adab is His saying, the Exalted, in the second verse: فَخَلَفَ مِن بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَوَاتِ — "But they were succeeded by a generations who neglected the prayer and followed their appetites." For prayer—which is the turning toward Allah—is their state with their Lord, and following appetites is their state with others among people. Since those were contrasted with these, the speech implies that the general adab of the prophets is to refer to their Lord with the mark of servitude and to walk among people with the mark of servitude. That is, the structure of their lives is built on the foundation that they have a Lord Who owns them and manages their affairs; from Him was their beginning and to Him is their return. This is the principle in all their states and actions.

What the Exalted mentioned regarding the exception of the repentant among them is another Divine adab that began with Adam (peace be upon him), the first of the prophets, where He said: وَعَصَىٰ آدَمُ رَبَّهُ فَغَوَىٰ * ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ — "Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went amiss. Then his Lord chose him, and turned to him clemently, and guided him" (20:121-122). Some discussion on this will follow, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

The Exalted said: مَّا كَانَ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ مِنْ حَرَجٍ فِيمَا فَرَضَ اللَّهُ لَهُ ۖ سُنَّةَ اللَّهِ فِي الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِن قَبْلُ ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ قَدَرًا مَّقْدُورًا * الَّذِينَ يُبَلِّغُونَ رِسَالَاتِ اللَّهِ وَيَخْشَوْنَهُ وَلَا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَدًا إِلَّا اللَّهَ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ حَسِيبًا — "There is no blame on the Prophet in respect of what Allah has ordained for him: Allah’s custom with those who passed away before, and Allah’s command is a determinate decree. Such as deliver the messages of Allah and fear Him, and fear no one except Allah, and Allah suffices as a reckoner" (33:38-39).

This is a general adab with which Allah, the Glorified, disciplined His prophets (peace be upon them) and a custom of His that runs through them: that they should not feel constrained in what He has apportioned for them of life, nor should they burden themselves (يَتَكَلَّفُوا) in any matter. This is because they were upon the primordial nature (الْفِطْرَةُ) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and the primordial nature only guides to that for which Allah has equipped it with what suits it in attaining it. It does not burden itself to rise to what Allah has not made easy for it to ascend to its level. The Exalted said, narrating from His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ — "and I am not one of the affected" (38:86). And the Exalted said: لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا — "Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity" (2:286). And the Exalted said: لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا مَا آتَاهَا — "Allah does not task any soul except [according to] what He has given it" (65:7). Since self-burdening (التَّكَلُّفُ) is a departure from the primordial nature, it is part of following appetite, and the prophets are secure from it.

The Exalted said—and this is also part of the discipline with a comprehensive adab: يَا أَيُّهَا الرُّسُلُ كُلُوا مِنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ وَاعْمَلُوا صَالِحًا ۖ إِنِّي بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ * وَإِنَّ هَٰذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاتَّقُونِ — "O apostles! Eat of the good things and act righteously. Indeed I know best what you do. Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord, so be wary of Me" (23:51-52). He disciplined them to eat of the good things (الطَّيِّبَاتِ)—that is, to utilize the wholesome materials of life and not cross over to the foul things (الْخَبَائِثِ) from which the sound primordial nature recoils—and to perform righteous deeds (الْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ), which are those actions suitable for a human to perform, toward which the primordial nature inclines according to how Allah has equipped it with means that preserve its survival through its action until its time. Or, it means to perform the action that is suitable to be presented to the Presence of Lordship; the two meanings are close. This is an adab pertaining to the individual human being.

Then the Exalted connected it with a social adab, mentioning to them that people are but one community: the messengers and those to whom they were sent. They have but one Lord, so let them gather upon His godwariness (تَقْوَاهُ), and thereby cut off the root of differences and factions. When the two matters meet—I mean the individual and social adab—a single human society is formed, protected from difference, worshipping one Lord, and its individuals proceed upon the Divine adab; so be wary of foul deeds and evil actions, for they have then settled upon the throne of felicity.

This is what another verse has gathered, which is His saying, the Exalted: شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ ۖ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it’" (42:13).

Allah has separated the two adabs in another place, saying: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدُونِ — "We did not send any apostle before you but that We revealed to him that ‘There is no god except Me; so worship Me’" (21:25). Thus, He disciplined them with His monotheism and building worship upon it; this is their adab in relation to their Lord. And He said: وَقَالُوا مَالِ هَٰذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْوَاقِ ۙ لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيرًا * أَوْ يُلْقَىٰ إِلَيْهِ كَنزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْهَا — "And they say, ‘What sort of an apostle is this who eats food and walks in the marketplaces? Why has not an angel been sent down to him to be a warner with him? Or [why is not] a treasure thrown to him, or [why does] he [not] have a garden from which he may eat?’" (25:7-8)—until He said—وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا إِنَّهُمْ لَيَأْكُلُونَ الطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشُونَ فِي الْأَسْوَاقِ — "We did not send any apostles before you but that they indeed ate food and walked in the marketplaces" (25:20). He mentioned that the conduct of all the prophets—which is their Divine adab—is to mix with people and reject veiling, exclusivity, and distinction from among the people; all of that is rejected by the primordial nature. This is their adab among people.

6 - [The Adab of the Prophets Narrated in the Quran in Detail] — Among the adab of the prophets (peace be upon them) in turning their faces toward their Lord and supplicating Him is what Allah, the Exalted, narrated of the words of Adam (peace be upon him) and his wife: رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ — "They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy on us, we will surely be among the losers’" (7:23). This was a word they spoke after they ate from the tree which Allah had forbidden them to approach. That prohibition was merely a guiding prohibition (نَهْيُ إِرْشَادٍ), not a masterly one (مَوْلَوِيٌّ); they did not disobey Him with the disobedience of a legal obligation (عِصْيَانُ تَكْلِيفٍ), but rather it was a violation of advice in whose observance lay the goodness of their state and the felicity of their life in the Garden, secure from all wretchedness and toil. Their Lord had said to them, warning them against following Iblis: فَلَا يُخْرِجَنَّكُمَا مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقَىٰ * إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ * وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَأُ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَىٰ — "so let him not expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable. Indeed you will neither go hungry in it nor go naked. And you will neither be thirsty in it nor suffer from the sun" (20:117-119).

When they fell into the trial and the calamity encompassed them, and the felicity of life began to bid them a departing farewell, they did not occupy themselves with their own selves like the despairing and wretched, nor did hopelessness cut the connecting link between them and their Lord. Rather, they hastened to take refuge in Allah, to Whom their affair belongs and in Whose hand is every good they hope for themselves. So they took hold of and clung to the attribute of His Lordship (رُبُوبِيَّتِهِ), which encompasses everything by which evil is repelled and good is attracted; for Lordship is the noble attribute through which the servant links himself to Allah, the Glorified.

Then they mentioned the evil that threatened them by the appearance of its signs, which is loss (الْخُسْرَانُ)—as if they had purchased the pleasure of eating at the cost of obeying the Divine guidance, so it became clear to them that their felicity was thereby on the verge of vanishing—in life. They mentioned their need for what would repel this evil from them, saying: وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ — "If You do not forgive us and have mercy on us, we will surely be among the losers." That is, the loss of life threatens us and has dawned upon us, and it has no repeller except Your forgiveness for the sin that proceeded from us and Your covering us thereafter with Your mercy, which is felicity. This is because the human being—indeed every created being—senses through his implanted primordial nature that it is the nature of things occurring in the abode of existence and the path of survival to seek completion for the deficiency and defect that befalls them, and that the Cause Who repairs this fracture is Allah, the Glorified, alone; for this is from the custom of Lordship.

Therefore, it was sufficient merely to manifest the state and highlight the misery of need that had descended upon the servant; there was no need for a request in words, for in the onset of need lies the most profound request and the most eloquent proposal.

For this reason, they did not explicitly state what they were asking for; they did not say: "So forgive us and have mercy on us." And because—and this is the core point—they placed themselves, by what had proceeded from them of opposition, in the position of humiliation and misery in which there is no face nor dignity. This resulted in them having pure submission (التَّسْلِيمُ الْمَحْضُ) to whatever might proceed in that regard from the Court of Might and from the Judgment. So they refrained from every request and proposal, except that they mentioned He is their Lord, thus pointing to what they craved from Him while confessing their wrongdoing.

Thus, the meaning of their saying: رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ was: "We have done evil in that we wronged ourselves, so we have thereby come to the brink of the loss that threatens the entirety of our felicity in life. Here is humiliation and misery surrounding us, and the need for the erasure of the mark of wrongdoing and the encompassing of mercy has enveloped us. This has left us with no face or dignity with which to ask You. So here we are, submitting to Your judgment, O Mighty King; for Yours is the command and Yours is the judgment, except that You are our Lord and we are Your subjects, hoping from You what a subject hopes from his Lord."

Among their adab is what Allah, the Exalted, narrated of the call of Nuh (peace be upon him) regarding his son: وَهِيَ تَجْرِي بِهِمْ فِي مَوْجٍ كَالْجِبَالِ وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ ابْنَهُ وَكَانَ فِي مَعْزِلٍ يَا بُنَيَّ ارْكَب مَّعَنَا وَلَا تَكُن مَّعَ الْكَافِرِينَ * قَالَ سَآوِي إِلَىٰ جَبَلٍ يَعْصِمُنِي مِنَ الْمَاءِ — "It moved with them amid waves like mountains. Noah called out to his son, who stood aloof, ‘O my son! Board with us, and do not be with the faithless!’ He said, ‘I shall take refuge on a mountain; it will protect me from the flood’" (11:42-43)—until He said—وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ رَّبَّهُ فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ ابْنِي مِنْ أَهْلِي وَإِنَّ وَعْدَكَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنْتَ أَحْكَمُ الْحَاكِمِينَ * قَالَ يَا نُوحُ إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ ۖ إِنَّهُ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صَالِحٍ ۖ فَلَا تَسْأَلْنِ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ ۖ إِنِّي أَعِظُكَ أَن تَكُونَ مِنَ الْجَاهِلِينَ * قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِكَ أَنْ أَسْأَلَكَ مَا لَيْسَ لِي بِهِ عِلْمٌ ۖ وَإِلَّا تَغْفِرْ لِي وَتَرْحَمْنِي أَكُن مِّنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ — "Noah called out to his Lord, and said, ‘My Lord! My son is indeed from my family. Your promise is indeed true, and You are the fairest of all judges.’ He said, ‘O Noah! Indeed, he is not of your family. Indeed, he is [personified] unrighteous conduct. So do not ask Me for what you have no knowledge of. I advise you lest you should be among the ignorant.’ He said, ‘My Lord! I seek Your protection lest I should ask You for something of which I have no knowledge. If You do not forgive me and have mercy on me, I shall be among the losers’" (11:45-47).

There is no doubt that the apparent meaning of Nuh's (peace be upon him) words is that he intended to pray for his son's salvation. However, reflection upon the verses of the story uncovers the reality of the matter in another way:

On one hand, Allah had commanded him to board the Ark with his family and the believers, saying: احْمِلْ فِيهَا مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ الْقَوْلُ وَمَنْ آمَنَ — "Carry in it a pair of every kind [of animal], and your family, except those against whom the edict has already been passed, and those who have faith" (11:40). So He promised him the salvation of his family and excepted from them those against whom the word had preceded. His wife was a disbeliever, as Allah mentioned in His saying: ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا امْرَأَتَ نُوحٍ وَامْرَأَتَ لُوطٍ — "Allah draws an example for the faithless: the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot" (66:10). As for his son, no disbelief in Nuh's call had appeared from him; what Allah mentioned of his affair with his father while he was aloof was merely a disobedience in opposing his command (peace be upon him), not explicit disbelief. Thus, it was possible to think of him as being among the saved due to the apparent fact of his being among his sons and not among the disbelievers, so the Divine promise of salvation would encompass him.

On the other hand, Allah, the Exalted, had revealed to Nuh (peace be upon him) His final judgment regarding the people, as He said: وَأُوحِيَ إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ أَنَّهُ لَن يُؤْمِنَ مِن قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَن قَدْ آمَنَ فَلَا تَبْتَئِسْ بِمَا كَانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ * وَاصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا وَلَا تُخَاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ۚ إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ — "It was revealed to Noah: ‘None of your people will believe except those who have already believed; so do not grieve at what they used to do. Build the ark before Our eyes and by Our revelation, and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoing: they shall indeed be drowned’" (11:36-37). Was the intention by "those who are wrongdoing" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا) the disbelievers in the call, or does it encompass every wrongdoing, or is it an ambiguous, general term that needs interpretation from its Speaker, the Exalted?

These matters caused him (peace be upon him) doubt regarding his son's affair. Nuh (peace be upon him) was not one to be heedless of the station of his Lord—he being one of the five Ulu al-Azm (أُولِي الْعَزْمِ), the masters of the prophets—nor was he one to forget the revelation of his Lord: وَلَا تُخَاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ۚ إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ — "and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoing: they shall indeed be drowned." Nor would he be pleased with the salvation of his son if he were a pure disbeliever in his disbelief; for he (peace be upon him) is the one who said in his supplication against his people: رَّبِّ لَا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ دَيَّارًا — "My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless" (71:26). Had he been pleased with that for his son, he would have been pleased with the like for his wife.

For this reason, he (peace be upon him) did not dare to make a definitive request; rather, he presented his matter like one presenting an inquiry due to his lack of encompassing the factors actually gathered regarding his son's affair. Rather, he began by calling upon the Name of the Lord (الرَّبُّ) because it is the key to the supplication of the needy, asking subject. Then he said: إِنَّ ابْنِي مِنْ أَهْلِي وَإِنَّ وَعْدَكَ الْحَقُّ — "My son is indeed from my family. Your promise is indeed true"—as if he were saying: "And this necessitates the salvation of my son"—وَأَنْتَ أَحْكَمُ الْحَاكِمِينَ — "and You are the fairest of all judges." There is no error in Your affair and no oversight in Your judgment; so I do not know where his affair has led.

This is the Divine adab: that the servant stands upon what he knows, and does not hasten to ask for what he does not know the aspect of wisdom in.

So Nuh (peace be upon him) presented the speech out of a feeling of distress, as indicated by the word "called" (نَادَى) in His saying: وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ رَّبَّهُ — "Noah called out to his Lord." He mentioned the Divine promise and did not add anything to it, nor did he ask for a matter.

Then the Divine protection (الْعِصْمَةُ الْإِلَهِيَّةُ) reached him and cut off the speech for him. Allah, the Glorified, explained to him the meaning of His word in the promise: وَأَهْلَكَ — "and your family"—that the intention was the righteous family, and the son was not righteous. And He had said before: وَلَا تُخَاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ۚ إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ — "and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoing: they shall indeed be drowned." Nuh (peace be upon him) had taken the apparent meaning of "family" and that the exception from them was only his disbelieving wife. Then He branched from this the prohibition of asking for what he had no knowledge of, which was the request for his son's salvation that his speech was hinting he would ask for.

So the request was cut off from him by this Divine discipline, and he (peace be upon him) resumed with another speech whose form was the form of repentance and whose reality was gratitude for the blessing of this discipline, which is a bounty. He said: رَبِّ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِكَ أَنْ أَسْأَلَكَ مَا لَيْسَ لِي بِهِ عِلْمٌ — "My Lord! I seek Your protection lest I should ask You for something of which I have no knowledge." So he took refuge in his Lord from that which the nature of his speech was leading him toward, which was the request for his son's salvation while he had no knowledge of the reality of his state.

Among the proofs that no request had actually occurred from him yet is his saying: أَعُوذُ بِكَ أَنْ أَسْأَلَكَ — "I seek Your protection lest I should ask You," etc., and he did not say: "I seek Your protection from the request for what I have no knowledge of," which would have indicated by the annexation of the verbal noun to its subject that the act had already occurred from him.

"Do not ask Me" (لا تَسْأَلْنِ) etc. Had he asked Him, the context of the speech would have required a direct rejection, or it would have been said, for example: "Do not return to the like of it," as occurred in similar instances of His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: قَالَ رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ ۚ قَالَ لَن تَرَانِي — "He said, ‘My Lord, show [Yourself] to me, that I may look at You.’ He said, ‘You shall not see Me’" (al-A‘raf: 143), and His saying: إِذْ تَلَقَّوْنَهُ بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَتَقُولُونَ بِأَفْوَاهِكُم مَّا لَيْسَ لَكُم بِهِ عِلْمٌ — until He said — يَعِظُكُمُ اللَّهُ أَن تَعُودُوا لِمِثْلِهِ أَبَدًا — "when you were receiving it on your tongues, and uttering with your mouths that of which you had no knowledge... Allah advises you lest you should ever repeat the like of it" (al-Nur: 15-17).

Among the supplications of Nuh (peace be upon him) is what Allah, the Exalted, recounted in His saying: رَّبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيَّ وَلِمَن دَخَلَ بَيْتِيَ مُؤْمِنًا وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلِوَالِدَيَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتِ وَلَا تَزِدِ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا تَبَارًا — "My Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and whoever enters my house in faith, and the faithful men and women, and do not increase the wrongdoers in anything but ruin" (Nuh: 28). Allah, the Exalted, recounted this from him at the end of Surah Nuh, following many verses detailing his complaint (peace be upon him) which he poured out to his Lord regarding his struggle in calling his people night and day for nearly a thousand years of his life. He recounted the harshness he endured from them and the tribulation (المحنة) he suffered for the sake of Allah, the Glorified, having exerted his utmost effort and spent the limit of his capacity in the way of their guidance; yet his call only increased them in flight, and his sincere advice only increased them in arrogance.

He continued—after the sincere advice and beautiful exhortation he spread among them, and after striking their ears with truth and reality—to complain to his Lord about the stubbornness and persistence in sin they met him with, and the plotting and deception they confronted him with, until grief and sorrow surged within him and divine zeal (الغيرة الإلهية) seized him. Thus, he prayed against them, saying: رَّبِّ لَا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ دَيَّارًا * إِنَّكَ إِن تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبَادَكَ وَلَا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فَاجِرًا كَفَّارًا — "My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless. If You leave them, they will lead Your servants astray, and they will beget none but vicious ingrates" (Nuh: 26-27).

What he mentioned regarding their leading the servants of Allah astray—should Allah leave them on the earth—is what he had mentioned about them within his previous speech recounted from him: وَقَدْ أَضَلُّوا كَثِيرًا — "They have already led many astray." They had indeed led many of the believers astray, so he feared they would lead the remaining ones astray. His saying: وَلَا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فَاجِرًا كَفَّارًا — "and they will beget none but vicious ingrates" is an announcement of the cessation of the potential (استعداد) of their loins and wombs to produce a believer. He mentioned this—which is from the reports of the Unseen (أخبار الغيب)—based on prophetic intuition (تفرس نبوي) and divine revelation (وحي إلهي).

When he prayed against the disbelievers (الكافرين) out of the divine zeal that seized him—and he was the noble Prophet, the first to bring a Book and a Law (شريعة), rising to rescue the world from the depths of idolatry (الوثنية), yet none from human society responded to him except a few (nearly eighty souls according to the reports)—it was part of the etiquette (أدب) of this station not to forget the believers in his Lord who had accepted his call, and to pray for their well-being until the Day of Resurrection.

Thus he said: رَّبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي — "My Lord! Forgive me." He began with himself because the speech is in the sense of seeking forgiveness for those who follow his path, and he is their leader (إمام) and is before them (أمامهم). وَلِوَالِدَيَّ — "and my parents," which contains evidence of their faith. وَلِمَن دَخَلَ بَيْتِيَ مُؤْمِنًا — "and whoever enters my house in faith," referring to the believers among the people of his time. وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتِ — "and the faithful men and women," referring to all believers who uphold monotheism (أهل التوحيد), for all of them are his community (أمة) and are indebted to his favor until the Day of Resurrection. He was the first to establish the religious call in the world with a Book and a Law, and he raised the banners of monotheism among the people. For this reason, Allah, the Glorified, greeted him with the finest of His greetings when He said: سَلَامٌ عَلَىٰ نُوحٍ فِي الْعَالَمِينَ — "Peace be to Noah among all the nations!" (al-Saffat: 79). So, peace be upon him from a noble Prophet as often as a believer believes in Allah, or performs a righteous deed (عمل صالح) for Him, and as often as a name of Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), is mentioned, and as often as a trace of goodness and felicity (السعادة) exists among people; for all of that is from the blessing of his call and the consequence of his uprising. May Allah bless him and all the other prophets and messengers.

Among these is what Allah, the Exalted, recounted from Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in his disputation with his people: قَالَ أَفَرَأَيْتُم مَّا كُنتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ * أَنتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُمُ الْأَقْدَمُونَ * فَإِنَّهُمْ عَدُوٌّ لِّي إِلَّا رَبَّ الْعَالَمِينَ * الَّذِي خَلَقَنِي فَهُوَ يَهْدِينِ * وَالَّذِي هُوَ يُطْعِمُنِي وَيَسْقِينِ * وَإِذَا مَرِضْتُ فَهُوَ يَشْفِينِ * وَالَّذِي يُمِيتُنِي ثُمَّ يُحْيِينِ * وَالَّذِي أَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لِي خَطِيئَتِي يَوْمَ الدِّينِ * رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ * وَاجْعَل لِّي لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ * وَاجْعَلْنِي مِن وَرَثَةِ جَنَّةِ النَّعِيمِ * وَاغْفِرْ لِأَبِي إِنَّهُ كَانَ مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ * وَلَا تُخْزِنِي يَوْمَ يُبْعَثُونَ — "He said, ‘Have you considered what you have been worshipping, you and your ancestors? They are indeed my enemies, [all] except the Lord of all the worlds, who created me, and it is He who guides me, and who feeds me and gives me drink, and when I get sick, it is He who cures me; who will cause me to die, then He will bring me to life, and who, I hope, will forgive me my sin on the Day of Retribution. My Lord! Grant me authority and join me with the righteous, and give me a good name among the later generations, and make me one of the heirs to the garden of bliss. And forgive my father, for he was one of those who are astray. Do not disgrace me on the day they are resurrected’" (al-Shu‘ara: 75-87).

This is a supplication he (peace be upon him) made for himself and for his father, based on a promise he had made to him. This was at the beginning of his affair when he had not yet despaired of his father's faith; but when it became clear to him that he was an enemy of Allah, he disassociated himself from him.

He began it by praising his Lord with a beautiful praise, in accordance with the etiquette of servitude (أدب العبودية). This is the first detailed praise that Allah, the Glorified, recounted from him (peace be upon him). What was recounted from him before this was not of this manner, such as his saying: يَا قَوْمِ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ * إِنِّي وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ — "O my people! I am indeed quit of what you polytheize. Indeed I have turned my face toward Him who originated the heavens and the earth" (al-An‘am: 78-79), and his saying to his father: سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي ۖ إِنَّهُ كَانَ بِي حَفِيًّا — "I will plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed He is gracious to me" (Maryam: 47).

In his praise, he (peace be upon him) employed the etiquette of offering a comprehensive praise in which he included his Lord's care for him from the beginning of his creation until his return to his Lord. He placed himself in a position of absolute poverty (الفقر) and need, while attributing to his Lord nothing but absolute richness (الغنى) and pure generosity (الجود). He represented himself as a lowly servant (عبداً داخراً) who has no power over anything, being turned by divine power (المقدرة الإلهية) from state to state—from creation, then feeding and watering, then healing from illness, then causing death, then bringing to life, then being brought forth for the recompense on the Day of Recompense. He has nothing but pure obedience and the hope for the forgiveness of sin.

Among the etiquette observed in his expression is the attribution of illness to himself in his saying: وَإِذَا مَرِضْتُ فَهُوَ يَشْفِينِ — "and when I get sick, it is He who cures me." This is because attributing it to Him, the Exalted, in such a context—which is a station of praise—is not without some impropriety. Although illness is among the events (الحوادث) that are not devoid of attribution to Him, the Exalted, the speech is not intended to explain its occurrence so that it would be attributed to Him; rather, it is to explain that healing from illness is from His mercy and care. Therefore, he attributed the illness to himself and the healing to his Lord, asserting that nothing proceeds from Him except what is beautiful.

Then he began the supplication, employing an exquisite etiquette by starting with the name of the Lord (الرب) and restricting his request to the true, everlasting blessings, without turning toward the fleeting ornaments of the world. From what he chose, he selected the greatest and most magnificent: he asked for authority (الحكم)—which is the Law (الشريعة)—and to be joined with the righteous. He asked for a "good name among the later generations" (لسان صدق في الآخرين), which means that Allah should raise after him, time after time and era after era, those who would uphold his call and promote his Law. This is, in reality, a request to be distinguished with a Law that remains until the Day of Resurrection. Then he asked for the inheritance of Paradise, the forgiveness of his father, and not to be disgraced on the Day of Resurrection.

Allah, the Exalted, answered him in all that he asked for, as indicated by His speech, the Exalted, except for his prayer for his father. Far be it from the Lord of all the worlds to mention a prayer of one of His honored servants that went in vain without being answered. The Exalted said: مِلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْرَاهِيمَ — "the faith of your father, Abraham" (al-Hajj: 78), and He said: وَجَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةً بَاقِيَةً فِي عَقِبِهِ — "He made it a lasting word among his posterity" (al-Zukhruf: 28), and He said: لَقَدِ اصْطَفَيْنَاهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا ۖ وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "We certainly chose him in the [present] world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the righteous" (al-Baqarah: 130), and He greeted him with a general peace when He said: سَلَامٌ عَلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ — "Peace be to Abraham!" (al-Saffat: 109).

The course of history after him (peace be upon him) confirms all the praises and commendations mentioned in the Noble Quran. He (peace be upon him) was the noble Prophet who stood alone with the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد) and the revival of the creed of primordial nature (ملة الفطرة). He rose to demolish the pillars of idolatry and break the idols at a time when the signs of monotheism had been effaced, the traces of prophethood had been worn away by time, and the world had forgotten the name of Nuh and the honored prophets of Allah. Thus, he established the religion of primordial nature firmly, and spread the call of monotheism among the people. The religion of monotheism remains alive to this day—nearly four thousand years after his time—by his name, lasting among his posterity. For what the world knows of the religion of monotheism is the religion of the Jews and their prophet Musa, and the religion of the Christians and their prophet ‘Isa—both of whom are from the progeny of Israel, Ya‘qub son of Ishaq son of Ibrahim (peace be upon him)—and the religion of Islam brought by Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who is from the progeny of Isma‘il son of Ibrahim (peace be upon him).

Among what Allah mentioned of his prayer is his saying: رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "My Lord! Give me [an heir] from among the righteous" (al-Saffat: 100). In this, he asks Allah for a righteous son, and it contains a seeking of refuge in his Lord and a refinement of his request—which is in one respect worldly—by the description of righteousness (الصلاح), so that it may return to the side of Allah and His pleasure.

Among what the Exalted mentioned of his prayer is what he supplicated when he arrived at the land of Makkah, having settled Isma‘il and his mother there. The Exalted said: وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَٰذَا بَلَدًا آمِنًا وَارْزُقْ أَهْلَهُ مِنَ الثَّمَرَاتِ مَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُم بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ ۖ قَالَ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَأُمَتِّعُهُ قَلِيلًا ثُمَّ أَضْطَرُّهُ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ النَّارِ ۖ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ — "And when Abraham said, ‘My Lord, make this a city of peace, and provide its people with fruits—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day.’ He said, ‘As for him who is faithless, I will let him enjoy for a little while, then I will compel him to the punishment of the Fire, and it is an evil destination’" (al-Baqarah: 126).

He asks his Lord to take the land of Makkah—which was then a barren land and an uncultivated valley—as a sanctuary (حرماً) for Himself, to gather thereby the adherents of the religion. It would serve as a physical, earthly link between the people and their Lord, where they would head for the worship of their Lord, turn toward Him in their rites (مناسكهم), and observe its sanctity among themselves. Thus, it would be an enduring, eternal sign for Allah on earth; whoever remembers Him would remember it, and whoever seeks Him would head for it, whereby the direction (الوجهة) is specified and the word is unified.

The evidence that he (peace be upon him) intended by "peace" (الأمن) the legislative peace (الأمن التشريعي)—which is the meaning of taking it as a sanctuary—rather than external security from the occurrence of battles, wars, and other events that disrupt peace and welfare, is the saying of the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ نُمَكِّن لَّهُمْ حَرَمًا آمِنًا يُجْبَىٰ إِلَيْهِ ثَمَرَاتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "Have We not established for them a secure sanctuary, to which are brought the fruits of every kind" (al-Qasas: 57). For in the verse is a favor bestowed upon them by the security of the Sanctuary (الحرم), which is the place Allah honored for Himself; thus it is characterized by security because of the people's respect for it, not because of a cosmic factor (عامل تكويني) protecting it from corruption and killing. The verse was revealed while Makkah had witnessed devastating wars between Quraysh and Jurhum, as well as countless instances of killing, injustice, and corruption. Similarly, the saying of the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا جَعَلْنَا حَرَمًا آمِنًا وَيُتَخَطَّفُ النَّاسُ مِنْ حَوْلِهِمْ — "Have they not seen that We have appointed a secure sanctuary, while the people are despoiled all around them?" (al-‘Ankabut: 67), meaning they are not despoiled from the Sanctuary because of the people's respect for it due to the sanctity We have established.

In short, his objective (peace be upon him) was for Allah to have a sanctuary on earth inhabited by his progeny. This could only be achieved by building a city that people would head toward from every side, becoming a religious gathering place they would frequent for residence, refuge, and visitation until the Day of Resurrection. Therefore, he asked to make it a "city of peace." Since it was uncultivated, he asked to provide them with fruits so that it would be populated by its inhabitants and they would not disperse from it.

Then, when he sensed that his prayer for this honor might include both the believer and the disbeliever, he restricted his request to the faith of those prayed for in Allah and the Last Day, saying: مَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُم بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ — "those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day." As for how that would be possible in a city where believers and disbelievers might happen to live together and differ, or if only disbelievers resided there, and how they would be provided with fruits while the land was a barren plain without crops—he did not address this in his request.

This is part of his etiquette (peace be upon him) in the station of supplication; for it is superfluous speech (فضول القول) for the supplicant to teach his Lord how to fulfill his need or what the path to answering his request is. He is an All-Knowing, All-Wise, All-Powerful Lord; His command, when He intends a thing, is only that He says to it, "Be," and it is.

However, since Allah, the Glorified, willed to fulfill his need according to the established custom of ordinary causes (الأسباب العادية), and not to distinguish therein between the believer and the disbeliever, He completed his prayer (peace be upon him) with what He appended to his speech: وَمَن كَفَرَ فَأُمَتِّعُهُ قَلِيلًا ثُمَّ أَضْطَرُّهُ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ النَّارِ — "As for him who is faithless, I will let him enjoy for a little while, then I will compel him to the punishment of the Fire."

This supplication, which led to the legislation of the Divine Sanctuary and the building of the Sacred Ka‘bah—the first house set up for mankind in blessed Bakkah and a guidance for all the worlds—is one of the fruits of his high, sacred aspiration, for which He bestowed favor upon the Muslims after him until the Day of Resurrection.

Among his prayers (peace be upon him) is his supplication at the end of his life, as recounted by Allah, the Exalted, in His saying: وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَٰذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِنًا وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَن نَّعْبُدَ الْأَصْنَامَ * رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ النَّاسِ ۖ فَمَن تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي ۖ وَمَنْ عَصَانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ * رَّبَّنَا إِنِّي أَسْكَنتُ مِن ذُرِّيَّتِي بِوَادٍ غَيْرِ ذِي زَرْعٍ عِندَ بَيْتِكَ الْمُحَرَّمِ رَّبَّنَا لِيُقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ فَاجْعَلْ أَفْئِدَةً مِّنَ النَّاسِ تَهْوِي إِلَيْهِمْ وَارْزُقْهُم مِّنَ الثَّمَرَاتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْكُرُونَ * رَّبَّنَا إِنَّكَ تَعْلَمُ مَا نُخْفِي وَمَا نُعْلِنُ ۗ وَمَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى اللَّهِ مِن شَيْءٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي السَّمَاءِ * الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْمَاعِيلَ وَإِسْحَاقَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّي لَسَمِيعُ الدُّعَاءِ * رَبِّ اجْعَلْنِي مُقِيمَ الصَّلَاةِ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِي ۚ رَبَّنَا وَتَقَبَّلْ دُعَاءِ * رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسَابُ — "When Abraham said, ‘My Lord! Make this city a sanctuary, and save me and my children from worshipping idols. My Lord! They have indeed led many of the people astray. So whoever follows me indeed belongs to me, and as for him who disobeys me, You are indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. Our Lord! I have settled some of my descendants in a barren valley near Your sacred House, our Lord, that they may maintain the prayer. So make the hearts of some people fond of them, and provide them with fruits, so that they may give thanks. Our Lord! Indeed You know what we hide and what we proclaim, and nothing is hidden from Allah on the earth or in the sky. All praise belongs to Allah, who gave me Ishmael and Isaac despite [my] old age. Indeed my Lord hears all supplications. My Lord! Make me a maintainer of the prayer, and my descendants [too]. Our Lord, accept my supplication. Our Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and the faithful, on the day when the reckoning is held’" (Ibrahim: 35-41).

This is among what he (peace be upon him) supplicated at the end of his noble life, after the city of Makkah had been built. The evidence for this is his saying: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْمَاعِيلَ وَإِسْحَاقَ — "All praise belongs to Allah, who gave me Ishmael and Isaac despite [my] old age," and his saying: اجْعَلْ هَٰذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِنًا — "Make this city a sanctuary," where he did not say as in his previous prayer: "Make this a city of peace."

Among the etiquette he employed was his clinging to Lordship (الربوبية) in his supplication; whenever he mentioned what pertained to himself alone, he said: "My Lord" (رَبِّ), and whenever he mentioned what others shared with him, he said: "Our Lord" (رَبَّنَا).

Another etiquette employed in his supplication is that whenever he mentioned a need that could be asked for a legitimate or illegitimate purpose, he mentioned his correct purpose for that need. In this is an evocation of divine mercy that is not hidden. When he said: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ — "and save me and my children," etc., he followed it with his saying: رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ — "My Lord! They have indeed led... astray," etc. And where he said: رَّبَّنَا إِنِّي أَسْكَنتُ — "Our Lord! I have settled," etc., he said after it: رَّبَّنَا لِيُقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ — "our Lord, that they may maintain the prayer." And when he supplicated: فَاجْعَلْ أَفْئِدَةً مِّنَ النَّاسِ تَهْوِي إِلَيْهِمْ وَارْزُقْهُم مِّنَ الثَّمَرَاتِ — "So make the hearts of some people fond of them, and provide them with fruits," he concluded it with: لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْكُرُونَ — "so that they may give thanks."

Part of his etiquette therein is that he followed every need he mentioned with what suits its content from the Beautiful Names of Allah, such as the All-Forgiving (الغفور), the Most Merciful (الرحيم), and the Hearer of Supplication (سميع الدعاء). He repeated the name of the Lord (الرب) whenever he mentioned one of his needs, for Lordship is the connecting cause between the servant and Allah, and it is the key to the door of every supplication.

Among his etiquette therein is his saying: وَمَنْ عَصَانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ — "and as for him who disobeys me, You are indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful," where he did not pray against them with anything harmful; rather, he mentioned alongside them two of the names of Allah that are the intermediaries in the encompassing of the blessing of felicity for every human being—namely, the All-Forgiving and the Most Merciful—out of his love for the salvation of his community and the expansion of his Lord's generosity.

Among these is what Allah recounted from him and his son Isma‘il, in which they both shared, namely the saying of the Exalted: وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَاهِيمُ الْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ الْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَاعِيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّا ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ * رَبَّنَا وَاجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَا أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا وَتُبْ عَلَيْنَا ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ * رَبَّنَا وَابْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتْلُو عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتِكَ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — "As Abraham raised the foundations of the House with Ishmael, [they prayed]: ‘Our Lord, accept it from us! Indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing. Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You, and show us our rites [of worship], and turn to us clemently. Indeed You are the All-clement, the All-merciful. Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among them, who should recite to them Your signs, and teach them the Book and wisdom, and purify them. Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise’" (al-Baqarah: 127-129).

This is a supplication they made while building the Ka‘bah, and it contains the same beautiful etiquette as the previous one.

Among these is what Allah recounted from Isma‘il (peace be upon him) in the story of the sacrifice. The Exalted said: فَبَشَّرْنَاهُ بِغُلَامٍ حَلِيمٍ * فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ السَّعْيَ قَالَ يَا بُنَيَّ إِنِّي أَرَىٰ فِي الْمَنَامِ أَنِّي أَذْبَحُكَ فَانظُرْ مَاذَا تَرَىٰ ۚ قَالَ يَا أَبَتِ افْعَلْ مَا تُؤْمَرُ ۖ سَتَجِدُنِي إِن شَاءَ اللَّهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ — "So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son. When he was old enough to assist in his endeavors, he said, ‘My son! I see in a dream that I am sacrificing you. See what you think.’ He said, ‘Father! Do whatever you have been commanded. If Allah wishes, you will find me to be patient’" (al-Saffat: 101-102).

The beginning of his speech, though it is part of his etiquette with his father, its conclusion is between him and his Lord; moreover, showing etiquette toward the likes of Ibrahim, the Friend of Allah (peace be upon him), is showing etiquette toward Allah, the Exalted.

In short, when his father mentioned to him what he had seen in the dream—and it was a divine command, as evidenced by Isma‘il's saying: "Do whatever you have been commanded"—he asked him for his opinion. This was part of his (peace be upon him) etiquette with his son. Isma‘il said to him: "Father! Do whatever you have been commanded," etc. He did not mention that it was the opinion he himself held, out of self-effacement and humility toward his father, as if he had no opinion contrary to his father's. Therefore, he began the speech by addressing him with fatherhood, and did not say: "If you wish, do that," so that his definitive request would be a comfort to his father's soul, and because he mentioned in his speech that it was a command Ibrahim had been ordered to perform. It is inconceivable for one like him to deliberate or hesitate in performing what he was commanded, rather than obeying his Lord's command.

Furthermore, in his saying: سَتَجِدُنِي إِن شَاءَ اللَّهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ — "If Allah wishes, you will find me to be patient," there is another comfort for his father's soul. All of this is part of his etiquette with his father (peace be upon him).

He also showed etiquette toward his Lord, as he did not present what he promised in a form of absolute certainty and decisiveness without making it conditional upon the will of Allah. For in decisiveness without making the matter conditional upon Allah's will, there is a taint of claiming independence in causality (الاستقلال في السببية), from which the realm of prophethood is free. Allah has censured a people for this when they decided on a matter without making it conditional, as He said in the story of the owners of the garden: إِنَّا بَلَوْنَاهُمْ كَمَا بَلَوْنَا أَصْحَابَ الْجَنَّةِ إِذْ أَقْسَمُوا لَيَصْرِمُنَّهَا مُصْبِحِينَ * وَلَا يَسْتَثْنُونَ — "Indeed We have tested them just as We tested the owners of the garden when they swore that they would surely harvest it next morning, and did not say, ‘If Allah wishes’" (al-Qalam: 17-18). Allah, the Glorified, disciplined His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in His Book to make things conditional in his speech, as a discipline through a wondrous allusion, when He said: وَلَا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَيْءٍ إِنِّي فَاعِلٌ ذَٰلِكَ غَدًا * إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ — "Do not say about anything, ‘I will indeed do it tomorrow,’ without [adding], ‘if Allah wishes’" (al-Kahf: 23-24).

Among these is what Allah recounted from Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) when his sons returned from Egypt, having left Benjamin and Yehuda there. The Exalted said: وَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَا أَسَفَىٰ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ وَابْيَضَّتْ عَيْنَاهُ مِنَ الْحُزْنِ فَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ * قَالُوا تَاللَّهِ تَفْتَأُ تَذْكُرُ يُوسُفَ حَتَّىٰ تَكُونَ حَرَضًا أَوْ تَكُونَ مِنَ الْهَالِكِينَ * قَالَ إِنَّمَا أَشْكُو بَثِّي وَحُزْنِي إِلَى اللَّهِ وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ اللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ — "And he turned away from them and said, ‘Alas for Joseph!’ And his eyes turned white with grief, and he choked with suppressed agony. They said, ‘By Allah! You will go on remembering Joseph until you are wrecked or you are among the perished.’ He said, ‘I only complain of my grief and sorrow to Allah, and I know from Allah what you do not know’" (Yusuf: 84-86).

He says to his sons that his constant remembrance of Yusuf is a complaint from him of his poor state to Allah, and he does not despair of his Lord's mercy that He will return him to him from where he does not expect. This is because it is part of the etiquette of the prophets with their Lord to turn in all their states toward their Lord and to direct all their movements and stillness in His way. For Allah, the Glorified, explicitly states that He guided them to Him on a straight path (صراطاً مستقيماً); He said: أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللَّهُ — "They are the ones whom Allah has guided" (al-An‘am: 90), and He said specifically regarding Ya‘qub: وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ كُلًّا هَدَيْنَا — "And We gave him Isaac and Jacob and guided each of them" (al-An‘am: 84). Then He mentioned that following desire (الهوى) is a straying from the way of Allah, saying: وَلَا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوَىٰ فَيُضِلَّكَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ — "and do not follow desire, or it will lead you astray from the way of Allah" (Sad: 26).

So the prophets, being guided by the guidance of Allah, do not follow desire at all. Their psychological emotions and inner inclinations—whether of lust, anger, love, hate, joy, or sorrow—pertaining to the manifestations of life such as wealth, children, marriage, food, clothing, housing, and so on, all occur in the way of Allah; they intend thereby nothing but Allah, may His majesty be magnified. For there are only two paths trodden: a path in which truth is followed and a path in which desire is followed; or if you wish, say: a path of remembering Allah and a path of forgetting Him.

The prophets (peace be upon them), since they were guided to Allah and did not follow desire, were in remembrance of their Lord, intending nothing by movement or stillness other than Him, the Exalted. They did not knock on the door of any cause (سبب) other than Him for any of their life's needs—meaning that if they attached themselves to a cause, that did not make them forget their Lord and that the matter belongs to Him, the Exalted. It is not that they deny causes absolutely such that their existence no longer remains in conception at all—for that is something not to be hoped for—nor that they see the essences of things and deny them the attribute of causality, for that would be a departure from the path of human primordial nature (صراط الفطرة الإنسانية). Rather, the attachment is that one does not see independence for anything other than Him, and places everything in the position Allah has placed it.

Since their state (peace be upon them) was as we mentioned—of true attachment to Allah—this divine etiquette took hold of them: to be mindful of their Lord's station and observe the side of His Lordship, so they intend nothing except for Allah, leave nothing except for Allah, and attach themselves to no cause except that they are attached to their Lord before it, with it, and after it. He is their goal in every state.

So his saying (peace be upon him): إِنَّمَا أَشْكُو بَثِّي وَحُزْنِي إِلَى اللَّهِ — "I only complain of my grief and sorrow to Allah" means that my continuous remembrance of Yusuf and my sorrow over him is not like the idle talk of one of you when a calamity strikes him and he loses one of Allah's blessings, mentioning it to one who possesses neither benefit nor harm out of ignorance. Rather, this is a complaint from me to Allah regarding what has entered upon me from the loss of Yusuf. This is not a request from me for a matter that cannot be, for I "know from Allah what you do not know."

Among these is what Allah recounted from Yusuf the Truthful (الصديق) when the wife of the Governor threatened him with imprisonment if he did not do what she commanded him: قَالَ رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ ۖ وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُن مِّنَ الْجَاهِلِينَ — "He said, ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than what they invite me to. If You do not turn away their stratagems from me, I might lean toward them and become one of the ignorant’" (Yusuf: 33).

He (peace be upon him) mentions to his Lord that his affair with them in that situation revolves between the prison and responding to what they ask of him. With the knowledge Allah honored him with—which is recounted in the saying of the Exalted: وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ آتَيْنَاهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا — "When he came of age, We gave him authority and knowledge" (Yusuf: 22)—he chooses the prison over responding to them. However, the causes are arranged according to what they hope from him, being strong and dominant. Thus, they threaten him with ignorance (الجهل) of his Lord's station and the nullification of the knowledge of Allah he possesses. There is no judgment in this except for Him, the Exalted, as he said to his companion in the prison: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ — "Judgment belongs only to Allah" (Yusuf: 40). Therefore, he showed etiquette (peace be upon him) and did not mention a specific need for himself because he had judged in a certain way; rather, he alluded to the threat of ignorance nullifying the blessing of knowledge with which his Lord had honored him, and mentioned that his salvation from the peril of ignorance and the repelling of their stratagem depended on His turning it away. So he surrendered the matter to Him and remained silent.

His Lord answered him and turned away their stratagem from him—which was the inclination (الصبوة)—otherwise it would be the prison. Thus he was saved from both the prison and the inclination. From this, it is known that his meaning by "their stratagem" was both the inclination and the prison. As for his saying (peace be upon him): "The prison is dearer to me," etc., it was only a heart's leaning toward the prison on the assumption of the matter's hesitation, and an allusion to the aversion and hatred for the indecency (الفحشاء). It was not a request from him for the prison, as he (peace be upon him) said:

Death is better than committing shame, And shame is better than entering the Fire.

It is not as some might think—that he asked for the prison thereby and it was decreed for him. The evidence for what we mentioned is the saying of the Exalted after it: ثُمَّ بَدَا لَهُم مِّن بَعْدِ مَا رَأَوُا الْآيَاتِ لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ — "Then, after they had seen the signs, it occurred to them to imprison him for a while" (Yusuf: 35), for the verse clearly shows that his imprisonment was due to an opinion that occurred to them after that, whereas Allah, the Glorified, had already turned it away from him before that through the call to themselves and the threat of imprisonment.

Among these is what Allah, the Glorified, recounted of his praise and supplication (peace be upon him) where He said: فَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ آوَىٰ إِلَيْهِ أَبَوَيْهِ وَقَالَ ادْخُلُوا مِصْرَ إِن شَاءَ اللَّهُ آمِنِينَ * وَرَفَعَ أَبَوَيْهِ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ وَخَرُّوا لَهُ سُجَّدًا ۖ وَقَالَ يَا أَبَتِ هَٰذَا تَأْوِيلُ رُؤْيَايَ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَعَلَهَا رَبِّي حَقًّا ۖ وَقَدْ أَحْسَنَ بِي إِذْ أَخْرَجَنِي مِنَ السِّجْنِ وَجَاءَ بِكُم مِّنَ الْبَدْوِ مِن بَعْدِ أَن نَّزَغَ الشَّيْطَانُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِي ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّي لَطِيفٌ لِّمَا يَشَاءُ ۚ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ * رَبِّ قَدْ آتَيْتَنِي مِنَ الْمُلْكِ وَعَلَّمْتَنِي مِن تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحَادِيثِ ۚ فَاطِرَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَنتَ وَلِيِّي فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ ۖ تَوَفَّنِي مُسْلِمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ — "When they entered into the presence of Joseph, he gave a place to his parents with himself and said, ‘Enter Egypt, if Allah wishes, in security.’ And he seated his parents high upon the throne, and they fell down in prostration before him. He said, ‘Father! This is the fulfillment of my dream of old. My Lord has certainly made it come true, and He was surely kind to me when He released me from the prison and brought you from the desert after Satan had sown dissension between me and my brothers. Indeed my Lord is kind in whatever He wishes. Indeed He is the All-knowing, the All-wise. My Lord! You have granted me a share in the kingdom, and taught me the interpretation of dreams. Originator of the heavens and the earth! You are my guardian in this world and the Hereafter! Let my death be in submission [to You], and join me with the righteous’" (Yusuf: 99-101).

Let the researcher reflect upon what the verses provide of the etiquette of prophethood (أدب النبوة). Let him represent before him the state Yusuf (peace be upon him) was in—of kingship and the execution of command—and the state his parents were in—of the soul's longing to meet him—and the state his brothers were in—of humility—while all of them were mindful of the history of his life from the time they lost him until the time they found him, as a mighty one established upon the throne of glory and dominance.

He (peace be upon him) did not utter a word except that his Lord had a share in it, or rather the entire share, except for the command he issued in his saying: ادْخُلُوا مِصْرَ إِن شَاءَ اللَّهُ آمِنِينَ — "Enter Egypt, if Allah wishes, in security." He commanded them to enter and judged for them security, yet he did not complete the speech until he made it conditional upon the will of Allah, lest it suggest independence in judgment apart from Allah—and he (peace be upon him) is the one who said: "Judgment belongs only to Allah."

Then he began praising his Lord regarding what had happened to him since he parted from them until he met them. He started with the story of his dream and the realization of its interpretation, confirming his father therein—not only in what he had interpreted, but even in what he had mentioned at the end of his speech regarding Allah's knowledge and wisdom, out of his deep immersion in praising his Lord. For his father had said to him: وَكَذَٰلِكَ يَجْتَبِيكَ رَبُّكَ وَيُعَلِّمُكَ — until he said — إِنَّ رَبَّكَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ — "That is how your Lord will choose you, and teach you... Indeed your Lord is all-knowing, all-wise" (Yusuf: 6). And Yusuf said here, after confirming him in his interpretation of the dream: إِنَّ رَبِّي لَطِيفٌ لِّمَا يَشَاءُ ۚ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ — "Indeed my Lord is kind in whatever He wishes. Indeed He is the All-knowing, the All-wise" (Yusuf: 100).

Then he alluded to the summary of what had happened to him between his dream and its interpretation, attributing it to his Lord and describing it as "kind" (أحسن), for from Allah it is kindness (إحسان). Among his most subtle etiquette is his description of what he encountered from his brothers—when they cast him into the depths of the pit until they sold him for a paltry price, a few dirhams, and accused him of theft—with his saying: نَزَغَ الشَّيْطَانُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِي — "Satan had sown dissension between me and my brothers."

He continued to mention his Lord's blessings and praise Him, saying "My Lord" and "My Lord," until he was overcome by ecstasy and a divine attraction (جذبة إلهية) seized him. He became occupied with his Lord and left them as if he did not know them, saying: رَبِّ قَدْ آتَيْتَنِي مِنَ الْمُلْكِ وَعَلَّمْتَنِي مِن تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحَادِيثِ — "My Lord! You have granted me a share in the kingdom, and taught me the interpretation of dreams." He praised his Lord for the blessings present with him, which were kingship and the knowledge of the interpretation of dreams. Then his noble soul moved from the mention of blessings to the fact that his Lord, who blessed him with what He blessed, is the "Originator of the heavens and the earth" (فاطر السماوات والأرض) and the bringer of everything from sheer non-existence into existence, without anything possessing anything of itself whereby it could own harm, benefit, blessing, affliction, or the capacity to manage its own affair in this world or the Hereafter.

Since He is the Originator of everything, He is the Guardian (الولي) of everything. Therefore, he mentioned after his saying: فَاطِرَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Originator of the heavens and the earth" that he is a lowly servant who does not possess the management of himself in this world or the Hereafter; rather, he is under the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, the Glorified, who chooses for him whatever good He wishes and establishes him in whatever station He desires. So he said: أَنتَ وَلِيِّي فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ — "You are my guardian in this world and the Hereafter!" At that point, he mentioned the request he had, in which he needed his Lord: to pass from this world to the Hereafter in a state of submission (الإسلام) to his Lord, just as Allah had granted his fathers Ibrahim, Isma‘il, Ishaq, and Ya‘qub. The Exalted said: وَلَقَدِ اصْطَفَيْنَاهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا ۖ وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ * إِذْ قَالَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ أَسْلِمْ — which is the choosing (الاصطفاء) — قَالَ أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ * وَوَصَّىٰ بِهَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ بَنِيهِ وَيَعْقُوبُ يَا بَنِيَّ إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفَىٰ لَكُمُ الدِّينَ فَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ — "We certainly chose him in the [present] world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the righteous. When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit to the Lord of all the worlds.’ Abraham enjoined this on his sons, and [so did] Jacob, [saying], ‘My sons! Allah has indeed chosen this religion for you; so never die except as Muslims’" (al-Baqarah: 130-132).

This is his saying: تَوَفَّنِي مُسْلِمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ — "Let my death be in submission [to You], and join me with the righteous." He asks for death upon Islam, then to be joined with the righteous. This is what his grandfather Ibrahim (peace be upon him) had asked for in his saying: رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ — "My Lord! Grant me authority and join me with the righteous" (al-Shu‘ara: 83). He was answered, as in the verses mentioned just now. This is the last of what Allah mentioned of his story and with which He concluded his narrative—and indeed, to your Lord is the final end. This is among the wondrous subtleties in the Quranic contexts.

Among these is what Allah, the Glorified, recounted from His Prophet Musa (peace be upon him) in the early stages of his upbringing in Egypt, when he struck the Copt and killed him: قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي فَاغْفِرْ لِي فَغَفَرَ لَهُ ۚ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ — "He said, ‘My Lord! I have wronged myself. Forgive me.’ So He forgave him. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful" (al-Qasas: 16). And his saying when he fled from Egypt and reached Madyan, watered for the two daughters of Shu‘ayb, then withdrew to the shade and said: رَبِّ إِنِّي لِمَا أَنزَلْتَ إِلَيَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ — "My Lord! I am indeed in need of any good You may send down to me" (al-Qasas: 24).

In his two requests, he (peace be upon him) employed the etiquette—after seeking refuge in Allah and clinging to His Lordship—of explicitly stating the request in the first supplication because it pertained to forgiveness, and Allah, the Glorified, loves to be asked for forgiveness, as He said: وَاسْتَغْفِرُوا اللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ — "and plead with Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (al-Baqarah: 199). This is what Nuh and the prophets after him (peace be upon them) called for. However, he did not explicitly state his specific need in the second supplication—the outward meaning of which, according to the context, was that he wanted the fulfillment of life's needs such as food and housing, for example—rather, he only mentioned the need and then remained silent. For the world has no value with Allah.

Know that his saying (peace be upon him): رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي فَاغْفِرْ لِي — "My Lord! I have wronged myself. Forgive me" follows the course of confessing to wrong and seeking forgiveness, just like the saying of Adam and his wife: رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ — "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy on us, we will surely be among the losers." This means that what is intended by "wrong" (الظلم) is his wronging of himself by committing an act that contradicts the interest of his life, just as the matter was in this manner for Adam and his wife.

For Musa (peace be upon him) only did what he did before Allah had sent him with His Law (شريعة) prohibiting killing; he only killed a disbelieving soul that was not inviolable, and there is no evidence for the existence of a prohibition against such killing before his Law. The matter regarding the disobedience of Adam and his wife was of this sort; they wronged themselves by eating from the tree before Allah had legislated a Law among the human species, for Allah only established the Laws—whatever they may be—after their descent from Paradise to the earth.

The mere prohibition against approaching the tree is no evidence for it being a legislative prohibition (نهي مولوي) necessitating the realization of "disobedience" (المعصية) in its technical sense by its violation. Moreover, the indications are established that the prohibition pertaining to them was advisory (إرشادي), as in the verses of Surah Taha, according to what we explained in the commentary on the story of Adam's Paradise in the first volume of the book.

Furthermore, the Divine Book explicitly states that Musa (peace be upon him) was "sincere" (مخلصاً), and that Iblis has no way to seduce the sincere ones among the servants of Allah. It is necessary that there be no disobedience without the seduction of Iblis. Allah, the Exalted, said: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ مُوسَىٰ ۚ إِنَّهُ كَانَ مُخْلَصًا وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا — "And mention in the Book Moses. Indeed he was sincere, and he was an apostle and a prophet" (Maryam: 51), and the Exalted said: قَالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ * إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your exclusive servants among them’" (Sad: 82-83).

From here it appears that the "forgiveness" (المغفرة) requested in his supplication—as in their supplication (peace be upon them)—is not the erasure of the punishment that Allah decrees for criminals as in legislative disobediences; rather, it is the erasure of the evil effects that the wronging of the self would entail in the course of life. Musa (peace be upon him) feared that his affair would spread and what was a "sin" (ذنب) to them would appear, so he asked Him, the Exalted, to cover it for him and forgive it. Forgiveness in the usage of the Quran is more general than the erasure of punishment; rather, it is the erasure of the evil effect, whatever it may be. There is no doubt that the matter of all things is in the hand of Allah, the Glorified.

Similar to this in one respect is the saying of Nuh (peace be upon him) in his previously mentioned prayer: وَإِلَّا تَغْفِرْ لِي وَتَرْحَمْنِي — "If You do not forgive me and have mercy on me," meaning: if You do not discipline me with Your discipline, and do not protect me with Your protection and safeguard, and have mercy on me thereby, I will be among the losers. So understand that.

Among these is his supplication (peace be upon him) when the revelation was first cast to him and he was sent with the message to his people, as recounted by Allah. The Exalted said: قَالَ رَبِّ اشْرَحْ لِي صَدْرِي * وَيَسِّرْ لِي أَمْرِي * وَاحْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِّن لِّسَانِي * يَفْقَهُوا قَوْلِي * وَاجْعَل لِّي وَزِيرًا مِّنْ أَهْلِي * هَارُونَ أَخِي * اشْدُدْ بِهِ أَزْرِي * وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي * كَيْ نُسَبِّحَكَ كَثِيرًا * وَنَذْكُرَكَ كَثِيرًا * إِنَّكَ كُنتَ بِنَا بَصِيرًا — "He said, ‘My Lord! Open my breast for me. Make my affair easy for me. Remove the hitch from my tongue, [so that] they may understand my speech. Appoint for me a minister from my family, Aaron, my brother. Strengthen my back by him, and make him a partner in my affair, so that we may glorify You much, and remember You much. Indeed You have been watchful of us’" (Taha: 25-35).

He (peace be upon him) offers counsel regarding the religious mission he was sent with, and reminds his Lord—as the speech implies with the aid of the context—saying: "You have been observant of my state, both mine and my brother's, since we grew up loving Your glorification. Tonight, You have placed upon me the weight of the message, while in my soul there is a sharpness and in my tongue an impediment of which You are most aware. I fear they will call me a liar if I invite them to You and convey Your message, causing my breast to be straitened and my tongue to fail. So, expand for me my breast and ease for me my affair." This is the removal of the constraint (التحرج) which Allah mentioned in His saying: ما كانَ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ مِنْ حَرَجٍ فِيما فَرَضَ اللهُ لَهُ سُنَّةَ اللهِ فِي الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلُ — "There is no blame on the Prophet in respect of what Allah has ordained for him: Allah’s way with those who passed away before" (33:38). وَاحْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِنْ لِسانِي يَفْقَهُوا قَوْلِي — "And remove the impediment from my tongue, that they may understand my speech" (20:27-28). "And my brother Aaron is more eloquent in speech than I, and he is of my family; so make him a partner in this affair and appoint him a vizier for me, so that we may glorify You—as we have always loved to do—much, and remember You much in the presence of the people through mutual support." This is the sum of what he (peace be upon him) asked of his Lord regarding the means of the mission and its conveyance. The etiquette (الأدب) he employed therein was mentioning his ultimate goal and purpose for his requests, lest his speech suggest he was asking for his own sake; thus he said: كَيْ نُسَبِّحَكَ كَثِيراً وَنَذْكُرَكَ كَثِيراً — "that we may glorify You much, and remember You much" (20:33-34). He attested to the truth of his claim by invoking Allah's own knowledge, placing themselves before Him and presenting their state to Him, saying: إِنَّكَ كُنْتَ بِنا بَصِيراً — "Indeed You have been watchful of us" (20:35). The presentation of the needy petitioner (السائل المحتاج) himself and his need before the Self-Sufficient, Generous One (المسئول الغني الجواد) is among the most powerful ways to stir the emotion of mercy, for it demonstrates the very essence of the need more effectively than a verbal mention of the need, which is not immune to falsehood.

Among these instances is what Allah narrated from him regarding his prayer against Pharaoh and his elite, when he said: وَقالَ مُوسى رَبَّنا إِنَّكَ آتَيْتَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَأَهُ زِينَةً وَأَمْوالاً فِي الْحياةِ الدُّنْيا رَبَّنا لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِكَ رَبَّنَا اطْمِسْ عَلى أَمْوالِهِمْ وَاشْدُدْ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَلا يُؤْمِنُوا حَتَّى يَرَوُا الْعَذابَ الْأَلِيمَ ، قالَ قَدْ أُجِيبَتْ دَعْوَتُكُما فَاسْتَقِيما وَلا تَتَّبِعانِّ سَبِيلَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "Moses said, ‘Our Lord! You have given Pharaoh and his elite trappings and possessions in the life of this world. Our Lord! Let them lead [people] astray from Your way. Our Lord! Blot out their possessions and harden their hearts, so that they do not believe until they sight the painful punishment.’ He said, ‘The prayer of the two of you has been granted. So be steadfast, and do not follow the way of those who do not know’" (10:88-89).

The supplication (الدعاء) was on behalf of Moses and Aaron, which is why it began with the word "Our Lord" (رَبَّنا), and this is indicated by the subsequent verse: قالَ قَدْ أُجِيبَتْ دَعْوَتُكُما — "He said, ‘The prayer of the two of you has been granted.’" They first prayed against their wealth, that Allah might blot it out, then against their souls, that Allah might harden their hearts so they would not believe until they saw the painful punishment—meaning their faith would not be accepted, as the Exalted said: يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ لا يَنْفَعُ نَفْساً إِيمانُها لَمْ تَكُنْ آمَنَتْ مِنْ قَبْلُ أو كَسَبَتْ فِي إِيمانِها خَيْراً — "the day when some of your Lord’s signs come, its faith shall not benefit any soul that had not believed before, or had not earned some good in its faith" (6:158). That is: take vengeance upon them by depriving them of faith through the sudden arrival of punishment, just as they deprived Your servants of it by leading them astray. This is the severest prayer one can make against another, for it is a prayer for eternal wretchedness (الشقوة الدائمة), and nothing is worse than that for a human being.

Praying for evil (الدعاء بالشر) differs in its ruling from praying for good (الدعاء بالخير), for Divine Mercy precedes His Wrath. He said to Moses in what He revealed to him: عَذابِي أُصِيبُ بِهِ مَنْ أَشاءُ وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ — "I visit My punishment on whomever I wish, but My mercy embraces all things" (7:156). The vastness of Divine Mercy necessitates a dislike for evil or harm to befall any of His servants, even if they are wrongdoers (ظالماً). This is evidenced by the blessings Allah pours upon them, His covering them with His generosity, and His commanding His servants to be clement and patient in the face of their ignorance and insolence—except in the establishment of a necessary right, or out of necessity in a grievance, provided they know that a compelling interest (مصلحة ملزمة), such as the interest of the religion or its people, necessitates it.

Furthermore, aspects of good and happiness (السعادة), the more subtle and refined they are in rank, the more they resonate with souls according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — upon which Allah created mankind. This is in contrast to aspects of evil and wretchedness, for man by his nature flees from confronting them and contrives not to notice their essence, let alone the details of their specifics. This meaning necessitates a difference in etiquette between the two types of supplication—praying for good and praying for evil.

Among the etiquettes of praying for evil is to mention the matters that prompted the prayer through metonymy (التكنية), especially in heinous and atrocious matters. This is unlike praying for good, where explicit mention (التصريح) of the factors of the prayer is sought.
