He (peace be upon him) observed this in his prayer when he said: لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِكَ — "Let them lead [people] astray from Your way," without detailing the atrocities committed by the people of Pharaoh.

Another etiquette is the frequent use of seeking aid (الاستغاثة) and entreaty (التضرع), which he observed in saying "Our Lord" (رَبَّنا), repeating it several times in his prayer despite its brevity.

Another etiquette is not to embark upon it except with the knowledge that it serves the interest of the Truth (مصلحة الحق) regarding the religion or its people, without acting on mere suspicion or accusation. He (peace be upon him) had knowledge of this, as Allah said regarding him: وَلَقَدْ أَرَيْناهُ آياتِنا كُلَّها فَكَذَّبَ وَأَبى — "Certainly We showed him all Our signs, but he denied and refused" (20:56). It is as if for this reason Allah commanded him and his brother, when informing them of the response, by saying: فَاسْتَقِيما وَلا تَتَّبِعانِّ سَبِيلَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "So be steadfast, and do not follow the way of those who do not know." And Allah knows best.

Among the prayers of Moses is what Allah narrated from him in His saying: وَاخْتارَ مُوسى قَوْمَهُ سَبْعِينَ رَجُلاً لِمِيقاتِنا فَلَمَّا أَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ قالَ رَبِّ لَوْ شِئْتَ أَهْلَكْتَهُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَإِيَّايَ أَتُهْلِكُنا بِما فَعَلَ السُّفَهاءُ مِنَّا إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا فِتْنَتُكَ تُضِلُّ بِها مَنْ تَشاءُ وَتَهْدِي مَنْ تَشاءُ أَنْتَ وَلِيُّنا فَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْغافِرِينَ ، وَاكْتُبْ لَنا فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ إِنَّا هُدْنا إِلَيْكَ — "Moses chose seventy men from his people for Our tryst, and when the earthquake seized them, he said, ‘My Lord! Had You wished, You would have destroyed them and me before. Will You destroy us because of what the fools amongst us have done? It is only Your test by which You lead astray whomever You wish and guide whomever You wish. You are our master, so forgive us and have mercy on us, for You are the best of those who forgive. And appoint for us good in this world and in the Hereafter, for we have indeed turned to You’" (7:155-156).

The supplication begins from His saying: "so forgive us..." etc. However, since the situation was a difficult one, where they had been seized by Divine Wrath and a might that nothing can withstand—and the request for forgiveness and mercy from an angry Master whose sanctity has been violated and whose sovereignty has been insulted is not like the request of one in a normal state—he (peace be upon him) first presented that which would calm the surge of Divine Wrath until he could transition to the request for forgiveness and mercy.

He said: رَبِّ لَوْ شِئْتَ أَهْلَكْتَهُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَإِيَّايَ — "My Lord! Had You wished, You would have destroyed them and me before." He (peace be upon him) intended—as indicated by the context—"My Lord, my soul and their souls are all in the grasp of Your power and subject to Your will. Had You wished, You could have destroyed them and me among them before today, just as You destroyed them today and kept me alive. What shall I say to my people when I return to them and they accuse me of killing them, while their condition is as You best know? This would invalidate my mission and frustrate my work."

Then he (peace be upon him) considered the destruction of the seventy as a destruction of himself and his people. He mentioned that they were the fools (سفهاء) of his people whose actions are not to be given weight, thus appealing to his Lord's mercy, as it was not His way to destroy a people for the actions of the fools among them. This was but one instance of the general trial (الامتحان العام) that continually applies to man, by which many are led astray and many are guided, and it is met only with pardon and concealment.

Since the matter of my soul and our souls is in Your hand, and You are able to destroy us whenever You wish, and this event is not unprecedented in the course of Your general trial which results in the straying of some and the guidance of others, and ends only with Your will—then You are our Master (ولينا) who, by Your command and will, manages our affairs, and we have no part in it. So decree for us forgiveness and mercy, for among Your attributes is that You are the best of those who forgive. And ordain for us in this world a life secure from punishment—which is what one who is overwhelmed by Divine Wrath finds desirable—and in the Hereafter, good through forgiveness and Paradise.

This is what he (peace be upon him) presented in his petition while the earthquake had seized them and calamity had enveloped them. Observe how he employed the beautiful etiquette of servitude (أدب العبودية) and sought mercy from his Lord, continuing to ask for mercy and calming the surge of Divine Wrath with his praise, until he was granted the need he did not explicitly mention among those he did: the restoration of their lives after their destruction. He was then revealed what Allah narrated: قالَ عَذابِي أُصِيبُ بِهِ مَنْ أَشاءُ وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَسَأَكْتُبُها لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِآياتِنا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "He said, ‘I visit My punishment on whomever I wish, but My mercy embraces all things. I will appoint it for those who are God-conscious and give the alms-tax, and those who believe in Our signs’" (7:156). What then do you think of Him, the Exalted, after He said to Moses (peace be upon him) in response to his petition: "but My mercy embraces all things"?

The Exalted explicitly mentioned His pardon of them and His response to Moses' (peace be upon him) petition by restoring their lives after they had been destroyed and returning them to the world, in His saying: وَإِذْ قُلْتُمْ يا مُوسى لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى نَرَى اللهَ جَهْرَةً فَأَخَذَتْكُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ وَأَنْتُمْ تَنْظُرُونَ ، ثُمَّ بَعَثْناكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ — "And when you said, ‘O Moses, we will not believe you until we see Allah visibly.’ Thereupon the thunderbolt seized you as you looked on. Then We raised you up after your death so that you might be grateful" (2:55-56). Similar to this is what is in Surah al-Nisa.

He (peace be upon him) employed etiquette in his speech when he said: تُضِلُّ بِها مَنْ تَشاءُ — "You lead astray whomever You wish," without mentioning that this was due to the poor choice of those who went astray, so as to exalt Him, the Exalted, in word as he exalted Him in heart. This is similar to the saying of the Exalted: يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ — "He misleads many thereby, and guides many thereby; and He misleads no one thereby except the transgressors" (2:26). This is because the situation diverted him from addressing anything other than the fact that He, the Exalted, is the absolute Master (الولي على الإطلاق) to whom all management returns.

He also did not explicitly mention the primary request in his soul—which was that Allah restore them to life after their destruction—because the situation, with its terror and danger, diverted him from speaking at length. He only alluded to it by his saying: رَبِّ لَوْ شِئْتَ أَهْلَكْتَهُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَإِيَّايَ — "My Lord! Had You wished, You would have destroyed them and me before," etc.

Among his (peace be upon him) prayers is what he supplicated when he returned to his people from the tryst and found they had worshipped the calf after him, and Allah had already informed him of that. The Exalted said: وَأَلْقَى الْأَلْواحَ وَأَخَذَ بِرَأْسِ أَخِيهِ يَجُرُّهُ إِلَيْهِ ، قالَ ابْنَ أُمَّ إِنَّ الْقَوْمَ اسْتَضْعَفُونِي وَكادُوا يَقْتُلُونَنِي فَلا تُشْمِتْ بِيَ الْأَعْداءَ وَلا تَجْعَلْنِي مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "and he threw down the tablets and seized his brother by the head, pulling him towards himself. He [Aaron] said, ‘Son of my mother! Indeed the people thought me weak and they were about to kill me. So do not let the enemies gloat over me, and do not count me among the wrongdoing people’" (7:150). At that, he felt compassion for him and prayed for him and for himself, so they might be distinguished thereby from the wrongdoing people: قالَ رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِأَخِي وَأَدْخِلْنا فِي رَحْمَتِكَ وَأَنْتَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ — "He said, ‘My Lord, forgive me and my brother, and admit us into Your mercy, for You are the most merciful of the merciful’" (7:151).

He only desired distinction from them and for Allah to admit them both into His mercy because he knew that Divine Wrath would reach the people for their wrongdoing, as Allah mentioned in His saying thereafter: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا الْعِجْلَ سَيَنالُهُمْ غَضَبٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ وَذِلَّةٌ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا — "Indeed those who took up the calf [for worship] shall be overtaken by their Lord’s wrath and abasement in the life of this world" (7:152). From what has preceded, various aspects of etiquette in his speech are recognized.

Among his (peace be upon him) prayers—which is in the sense of a prayer against his people when they said to him, upon his commanding them to enter the Holy Land: يا مُوسى إِنَّا لَنْ نَدْخُلَها أَبَداً ما دامُوا فِيها فَاذْهَبْ أَنْتَ وَرَبُّكَ فَقاتِلا إِنَّا هاهُنا قاعِدُونَ — "‘O Moses, we will never enter it so long as they are in it. Go ahead, you and your Lord, and fight! We will be sitting right here’" (5:24)—is what Allah narrated in His saying: قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي لا أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِي وَأَخِي فَافْرُقْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ — "He said, ‘My Lord! I have no control over [anyone] but myself and my brother. So part us from the transgressing people’" (5:25).

He (peace be upon him) adopted a beautiful etiquette by using metonymy to express his cessation from commanding them and conveying their Lord's command to them a second time, after they had met his first command with the ugliest rejection and most heinous speech, by saying: رَبِّ إِنِّي لا أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِي وَأَخِي — "My Lord! I have no control over [anyone] but myself and my brother." That is: none obey me in what I command except myself and my brother; meaning they have responded to me in a way that leaves no hope for them, so here I am, refraining from commanding them by Your command and guiding them to what is for the welfare of their community.

He only attributed the "possession" (ملك) of himself and his brother to himself because his meaning of possession, by the context of the situation, is the possession of obedience (ملك الطاعة). Had it been ontological possession (الملك التكويني), he would not have attributed it to himself except with the clarification that its reality belongs to Allah, and he only possesses what Allah has enabled him to possess. When he presented to his Lord his cessation and despair of their response, he referred the judgment in that matter [to Allah], saying: فَافْرُقْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ — "So part us from the transgressing people."

Among such instances is what Shu'ayb (peace be upon him) prayed against his people when he said: رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْفاتِحِينَ — "Our Lord! Judge between us and our people with justice, and You are the best of judges" (7:89).

This was a seeking of the fulfillment of the Divine promise after he despaired of the success of his mission among them, and a petition for judgment between him and them with truth, according to what Allah the Exalted said: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ — "There is a messenger for every nation. So when their messenger comes, judgment is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged" (10:47).

He only said "between us" (بَيْنَنا) because he joined the believers with him to himself. The disbelievers among his people had threatened both him and the believers with him, saying: لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يا شُعَيْبُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَكَ مِنْ قَرْيَتِنا أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنا — "‘We will surely expel you, O Shu‘ayb, and those who have believed with you, from our town, or you shall revert to our creed’" (7:88). So he joined them to himself, left his people to their deeds, and journeyed with them toward his Lord, saying: رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنا — "Our Lord! Judge between us..." etc.

He held fast in his prayer to His noble Name: "the Best of Judges" (خَيْرُ الْفاتِحِينَ), for as previously mentioned, holding fast to an attribute suitable to the core of the prayer is a profound support, equivalent to an oath. This is unlike the saying of Moses (peace be upon him): رَبِّ إِنِّي لا أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِي وَأَخِي فَافْرُقْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ — "My Lord! I have no control over [anyone] but myself and my brother. So part us from the transgressing people," mentioned just now; for as stated, his (peace be upon him) words were not a supplication in reality, but rather a metonymy for ceasing the mission and returning the matter to Allah, so there was no requirement for an oath, unlike the saying of Shu'ayb.

Among such instances is what Allah narrated of the praise of David and Solomon (peace be upon them). The Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا داوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ عِلْماً وَقالا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي فَضَّلَنا عَلى كَثِيرٍ مِنْ عِبادِهِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "Certainly We gave knowledge to David and Solomon, and they said, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who excelled us over many of His faithful servants’" (27:15).

The aspect of etiquette in their praise and gratitude, and the attribution of the virtue of knowledge they possessed to Allah, is apparent. They did not say what was narrated from others, such as the saying of Korah to his people when they exhorted him not to be arrogant on earth with his wealth: إِنَّما أُوتِيتُهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ عِنْدِي — "‘I have indeed been given it because of the knowledge I have’" (28:78); and as Allah narrated of another people: فَلَمَّا جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فرحوا بِما عِنْدَهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — "When their messengers brought them manifest proofs, they exulted in the knowledge they possessed, and they were besieged by what they used to deride" (40:83).

There is no harm in praising [Allah] for His excelling them over many of the believers, for it is a mention of a specific blessing and a statement of fact. This is not of the arrogance toward Allah's servants that would incur blame. Allah has mentioned a group of believers asking for excellence and praised them for their high nature and lofty ambition, saying: وَالَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا — until He said — وَاجْعَلْنا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ إِماماً — "And those who say, ‘Our Lord... make us leaders of the God-conscious’" (25:74).

Among such instances is what He narrated from Solomon (peace be upon him) in the story of the ant, in His saying: حَتَّى إِذا أَتَوْا عَلى وادِ النَّمْلِ قالَتْ نَمْلَةٌ يا أَيُّهَا النَّمْلُ ادْخُلُوا مَساكِنَكُمْ لا يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ سُلَيْمانُ وَجُنُودُهُ وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ ، فَتَبَسَّمَ ضاحِكاً مِنْ قَوْلِها وَقالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ وَعَلى والِدَيَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صالِحاً تَرْضاهُ وَأَدْخِلْنِي بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِي عِبادِكَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "When they came to the Valley of Ants, an ant said, ‘O ants, enter your dwellings, lest Solomon and his hosts crush you while they are unaware.’ Thereat he smiled, laughing at its words, and said, ‘My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing with which You have blessed me and my parents, and that I may do righteous deeds which may please You, and admit me, by Your mercy, among Your righteous servants’" (27:18-19).

The ant reminded him by what it said of the great kingdom he possessed, whose pillars were established by the subjugation of the wind to blow at his command, and the jinn working for him as he willed, and the knowledge of the speech of birds and other things. However, this kingdom did not appear in his (peace be upon him) mind as the most vivid aspiration a human can reach, as it does with us, nor did it make him forget his servitude and poverty. Rather, it occurred in his soul as a blessing bestowed upon him by his Lord. So he remembered his Lord and the blessing He bestowed upon him and his parents by what He specifically granted them. This, from one like him (peace be upon him) in such a state, is the highest etiquette with his Lord.

He mentioned the blessing of his Lord, and though it was vast in his case, his focus (peace be upon him)—in that specific situation—was on the great kingdom and the overwhelming authority. Therefore, he mentioned righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) and asked his Lord to inspire him to do righteous deeds, for righteous deeds and a good conduct are what is required of one who sits upon the throne of power.

For all this, he asked his Lord first to inspire him to thank Him for His blessing, and second to do righteous deeds. He was not satisfied with asking for righteous deeds without qualifying it with His saying: تَرْضاهُ — "which may please You," for he is a servant who has no concern other than his Lord; he does not desire the righteousness of the deed except because his Lord is pleased with it. Then he completed the request for success in the righteousness of action with the request for the righteousness of the self (صلاح الذات), saying: وَأَدْخِلْنِي بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِي عِبادِكَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "and admit me, by Your mercy, among Your righteous servants."

Among such instances is what Allah narrated from Jonah (peace be upon him) when he prayed while in the belly of the whale that had swallowed him. The Exalted said: وَذَا النُّونِ إِذْ ذَهَبَ مُغاضِباً فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ فَنادى فِي الظُّلُماتِ أَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "And [remember] the Man of the Fish, when he left in a rage, thinking that We would not put him to hardship. Then he cried out in the darkness, ‘There is no god except You! Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!’" (21:87).

He (peace be upon him)—according to what the Quran narrates—had asked his Lord to send punishment upon his people, and He responded to that, so he informed them of it. When the punishment was about to descend upon them, they repented to their Lord, and He removed the punishment from them. When Jonah witnessed this, he left his people and went his way until he boarded a ship. A whale obstructed it, so they cast lots to repel the whale by throwing one of them to it so it might swallow him and leave the rest. The lot fell upon his name, so he was thrown into the sea and the whale swallowed him. He continued to glorify Allah in its belly until Allah commanded it to cast him onto the shore. This was nothing but a Divine discipline (تأديب إلهي) with which He disciplines His prophets according to what their various states require. The Exalted said: فَلَوْ لا أَنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الْمُسَبِّحِينَ ، لَلَبِثَ فِي بَطْنِهِ إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ — "And had he not been one of those who glorify [Allah], he would surely have remained in its belly till the day they are resurrected" (37:143-144). His state in not returning to his people and going his own way represented the state of a servant who objected to some of his Master's actions, became angry, fled from Him, and abandoned his service and the duty of his servitude. Allah did not approve of this for him, so He disciplined him, afflicted him, and seized him in a prison where he could not expand even a finger's breadth, in layers of darkness one above another. Then he cried out in the darkness: "There is no god except You! Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!"

All of this was only so that it might be represented to him—contrary to what his state had represented—that Allah, the Glorified, is able to seize him and imprison him wherever He wills, and to do with him as He wills, for there is no escape from Allah, the Glorified, except to Him. Therefore, the state represented to him while in his prison in the belly of the whale prompted him to confess to Allah that He is the Worshipped One (المعبود) besides whom there is no other, and there is no escape from His servitude. So he said: لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ — "There is no god except You." He did not call upon Him by Lordship (الربوبية), and this is the only prayer among the prayers of the prophets (peace be upon them) that was not issued with the name "Lord" (الرب).

Then he mentioned what had occurred of his leaving his people following Allah's not destroying them with the punishment He had sent down upon them. He affirmed wrongdoing (الظلم) for himself and sanctified Allah, the Glorified, from anything that carries a taint of wrongdoing or deficiency, saying: سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!"

He did not mention his request—which was to return to his previous station of servitude—considering himself without the worthiness to ask or the entitlement to receive, out of total immersion in shame and embarrassment. The evidence for his request is the saying of the Exalted after the previous verse: فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ وَنَجَّيْناهُ مِنَ الْغَمِّ — "So We answered his prayer and delivered him from the agony" (21:88).

The evidence that his request was to return to his previous station is the saying of the Exalted: فَنَبَذْناهُ بِالْعَراءِ وَهُوَ سَقِيمٌ ، وَأَنْبَتْنا عَلَيْهِ شَجَرَةً مِنْ يَقْطِينٍ ، وَأَرْسَلْناهُ إِلى مِائَةِ أَلْفٍ أَوْ يَزِيدُونَ ، فَآمَنُوا فَمَتَّعْناهُمْ إِلى حِينٍ — "Then We cast him on a barren shore while he was sick, and We caused a gourd plant to grow over him. And We sent him to [a community of] a hundred thousand or more. They believed [in him], so We let them enjoy [their life] for a while" (37:145-148).

Among such instances is what Allah the Exalted mentioned regarding Job (peace be upon him) after illness had incapacitated him and his wealth and children had perished, when he said: وَأَيُّوبَ إِذْ نادى رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الضُّرُّ وَأَنْتَ أرحم الرَّاحِمِينَ — "And [remember] Job, when he called out to his Lord, ‘Indeed distress has befallen me, and You are the most merciful of the merciful’" (21:83).

The aspects of etiquette therein are apparent from what has been previously explained. He (peace be upon him) did not explicitly mention his need, similar to the aforementioned prayers of Adam, Noah, Moses, and Jonah (peace be upon them), out of self-abasement and belittling of his own affair. The prayers of the prophets, as mentioned and as will follow, are devoid of explicit mention of the need when it pertains to worldly matters, even though they do not desire any of it out of personal whim.

From another perspective, he mentioned the cause prompting the petition, such as the touch of distress, and the attribute present in the One petitioned that gives hope to the petitioner, such as His being the Most Merciful of the merciful. Remaining silent about the mention of the need itself is a more eloquent metonymy (كناية) indicating that the need does not require mentioning; for mentioning it might suggest that the stated causes are not sufficient to stir the mercy of the One who is the Most Merciful of the merciful, but rather require support by mention and clarification through words.

Among such instances is what He narrated from Zechariah (peace be upon him), where He said: ذِكْرُ رَحْمَتِ رَبِّكَ عَبْدَهُ زَكَرِيَّا ، إِذْ نادى رَبَّهُ نِداءً خَفِيًّا ، قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي وَهَنَ الْعَظْمُ مِنِّي وَاشْتَعَلَ الرَّأْسُ شَيْباً وَلَمْ أَكُنْ بِدُعائِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا ، وَإِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوالِيَ مِنْ وَرائِي وَكانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عاقِراً فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا ، يَرِثُنِي وَيَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا — "[This is] an account of your Lord’s mercy on His servant Zechariah, when he called out to his Lord with a secret cry. He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed my bones have become feeble, and my head has flared up with hoariness, and I have never been disappointed in supplicating You, my Lord. Indeed I fear my kinsmen after me, and my wife is barren. So grant me from Yourself an heir who may inherit from me and inherit from the House of Jacob, and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]’" (19:2-6).

What urged him to this prayer and made him desire to be granted a son by his Lord was what he witnessed of the affair of Mary, the daughter of Imran, in her asceticism and worship, and how Allah the Exalted honored her with the etiquette of servitude and specifically granted her the dignity of sustenance from Him, as Allah narrates in Surah Al Imran. The Exalted said: وَكَفَّلَها زَكَرِيَّا كُلَّما دَخَلَ عَلَيْها زَكَرِيَّا الْمِحْرابَ وَجَدَ عِنْدَها رِزْقاً قالَ يا مَرْيَمُ أَنَّى لَكِ هذا قالَتْ هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ ، هُنالِكَ دَعا زَكَرِيَّا رَبَّهُ قالَ رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً إِنَّكَ سَمِيعُ الدُّعاءِ — "and Zechariah took care of her. Whenever Zechariah visited her in the sanctuary, he would find provisions with her. He said, ‘O Mary, from where does this come for you?’ She said, ‘It is from Allah. Indeed Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning.’ Thereat Zechariah supplicated his Lord. He said, ‘My Lord! Grant me from Yourself a good offspring. Indeed You are the hearer of supplication’" (3:37-38).

So he was overwhelmed by a strong longing for a good, righteous son who would inherit from him and worship his Lord with a pleasing worship, just as Mary inherited from Imran and reached her utmost effort in worshipping her Lord and attained dignity from Him. However, he found himself reached by old age, his strength collapsed, and likewise his wife, who had been barren in her childbearing years. He was overtaken by the sorrow of deprivation from the blessing of a good, pleasing child, of which Allah is most aware. Yet he could not restrain himself from the surge of Divine zeal (الغيرة الإلهية) and pride in his Lord, so he returned to his Lord and mentioned to Him that which stirs mercy and compassion: his state of having never ceased clinging to the door of servitude and petition since his youth until his bones became feeble and his head flared with hoariness, and he had never been disappointed in his prayer. He found Him, the Glorified, to be the Hearer of prayer, so let Him hear his prayer and grant him a pleasing heir.

The evidence for what we have mentioned—that he only asked what he asked because of the surge of longing and grief that possessed him—is what Allah the Exalted narrated from him after he was revealed the response, in His saying: قالَ رَبِّ أَنَّى يَكُونُ لِي غُلامٌ وَكانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عاقِراً وَقَدْ بَلَغْتُ مِنَ الْكِبَرِ عِتِيًّا ، قالَ كَذلِكَ قالَ رَبُّكَ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْئاً — "He said, ‘My Lord! How shall I have a son, when my wife is barren, and I have already reached extreme old age?’ He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord says, ‘‘It is easy for Me; and I created you before, when you were nothing’’" (19:8-9). This clearly shows that he (peace be upon him), when he heard the response, recovered from his state and began to marvel at the strangeness of the request and the response, until he asked his Lord about it in the form of deeming it remote and asked for a sign for himself, which was also granted.

In any case, the etiquette he (peace be upon him) employed in his prayer is what his state of longing and grief, which possessed him, led him to. Therefore, he preceded his prayer with a clarification of what his state had reached in the way of his Lord; for he had spent his life following the path of turning [to Him] and petitioning until he stood in a position that would move the heart of every merciful observer. Then he asked for the son and justified it by his Lord being the Hearer of prayer.

This is the meaning of what he mentioned as a preamble to his petition; it was not that he was reminding his Lord of his long servitude—far be it from the station of prophethood. The meaning of his saying in Surah Al Imran: رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً إِنَّكَ سَمِيعُ الدُّعاءِ — "My Lord! Grant me from Yourself a good offspring. Indeed You are the hearer of supplication" is: "I ask You what I ask, not because my long servitude—which is my extended prayer—has value with You or constitutes a favor upon You, but because I ask You having found You to be the Hearer of the prayer of Your servants and the Responder to the call of the distressed petitioners. The fear of my kinsmen after me and the intense urge for a good offspring who will worship You have compelled me to ask You."

It was previously mentioned that among the etiquette he employed in his prayer was attaching to his fear of the kinsmen his saying: وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا — "and make him, my Lord, pleasing." Although "pleasing" (الرضي) by its form indicates the establishment of pleasure for the one described, and pleasure in its absolute sense includes Allah's pleasure, Zechariah's pleasure, and John's pleasure, his saying in the verse of Al Imran: ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً — "a good offspring" indicates that the meaning of his being pleasing is his being pleasing to Zechariah, for offspring are only "good" (طيبة) for their parent and none other.

Among such instances is what Allah the Exalted narrated from the Messiah when he asked for the table, in his saying: قالَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ اللهُمَّ رَبَّنا أَنْزِلْ عَلَيْنا مائِدَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ تَكُونُ لَنا عِيداً لِأَوَّلِنا وَآخِرِنا وَآيَةً مِنْكَ وَارْزُقْنا وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ — "Said Jesus son of Mary, ‘O Allah! Our Lord! Send down to us a table from the sky, to be a festival for us, for the first ones of us and the last ones of us, and a sign from You, and provide for us; for You are the best of providers’" (5:114).

The story mentioned in His speech regarding the disciples' request to Jesus (peace be upon him) for a table to descend upon them from the sky indicates by its context that this request was among the difficult requests for Jesus (peace be upon him). This is because what was narrated of their saying to him: يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ أَنْ يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا مائِدَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ — "‘O Jesus son of Mary! Can your Lord send down to us a table from the sky?’" (5:112) first contained, in its literal sense, an inquiry about the power of Allah the Exalted, which does not accord with the etiquette of servitude, even if their core intent was to ask about the wisdom [of it] rather than the power itself; for the harshness of the wording remains.

Secondly, it involved proposing a new sign (آية) despite his (peace be upon him) brilliant signs having surrounded them from every side. His noble self was a sign, his speaking in the cradle was a sign, his reviving the dead, creating the bird, healing the blind and the leper, his informing of the unseen, and his knowledge of the Torah, the Gospel, the Book, and wisdom were all Divine signs that left no doubt for a doubter nor suspicion for a skeptic. Their choosing a sign for themselves and their asking him was, in its literal sense, like trifling with the signs of Allah and playing with His side. Therefore, he rebuked them, saying: اتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "‘Be wary of Allah, should you be faithful’" (5:112).

But they insisted on that and justified their request by saying: نُرِيدُ أَنْ نَأْكُلَ مِنْها وَتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُنا وَنَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ صَدَقْتَنا وَنَكُونَ عَلَيْها مِنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ — "‘We desire to eat from it, and that our hearts may be at rest: and that we may know that you have told us the truth, and that we may be among the witnesses to it’" (5:113). They compelled him to ask, so he asked.

He (peace be upon him), through his God-given etiquette, rectified what they had proposed in the request into something suitable to be presented to the Presence of Might and Grandeur. He first framed it as being a festival (عيد) for them, specific to him and his community, for it was a proposed sign without parallel among the signs of the prophets (peace be upon them); for their signs were sent down either to complete the proof or due to the community's need for their descent, whereas this sign possessed neither of these two qualities.

Secondly, he summarized what the disciples had detailed of the benefits of its descent—the rest of their hearts, their knowledge of his (peace be upon him) truthfulness, and their witnessing it—in his saying: وَآيَةً مِنْكَ — "and a sign from You."

Thirdly, he mentioned what they had mentioned of the desire to eat, but he placed it last although they had placed it first in their saying: "We desire to eat from it..." etc., and he clothed it in another garment more suited to the etiquette of Presence, saying: وَارْزُقْنا — "and provide for us." Then he concluded it with His saying: وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ — "for You are the best of providers," to be a support for the request from one perspective, and a praise for Him, the Exalted, from another.

He began his petition by calling upon Him: اللهُمَّ رَبَّنا — "O Allah! Our Lord!" adding to what is found in other prayers of the prophets (peace be upon them) of their saying "Lord" or "Our Lord," because the situation was difficult, as previously explained.

Among these is his (peace be upon him) direct address to his Lord narrated in the saying of the Exalted: وَإِذْ قالَ اللهُ يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ أَأَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّي إِلهَيْنِ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ قالَ سُبْحانَكَ ما يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أَقُولَ ما لَيْسَ لِي بِحَقٍّ إِنْ كُنْتُ قُلْتُهُ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُ تَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِي وَلا أَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِكَ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ ، ما قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا ما أَمَرْتَنِي بِهِ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ رَبِّي وَرَبَّكُمْ وَكُنْتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً ما دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنْتَ أَنْتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَنْتَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ ، إِنْ تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبادُكَ وَإِنْ تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ فَإِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — "And when Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary, was it you who said to the people, ‘‘Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah’’?’ He will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It does not behoove me to say what I have no right to [say]. Had I said it, You would certainly have known it: You know whatever is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self. Indeed You are the Knower of all that is Unseen. I did not say to them [anything] except what You had commanded me [to say]: ‘‘Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord.’’ And I was a witness to them so long as I was among them. But when You had taken me, You were Yourself the watcher over them, and You are witness to all things. If You punish them, they are indeed Your servants; but if You forgive them, You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise’" (5:116-118).

He (peace be upon him) showed etiquette in his speech, first by beginning it with His sanctification, the Exalted, from what does not befit the holiness of His presence, just as His own speech, the Exalted, proceeds: وَقالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمنُ وَلَداً سُبْحانَهُ — "They say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He!" (21:26).

Secondly, by considering himself too lowly and humble for it to be imagined of him that he would say such a thing, such that he would need to deny it. Therefore, he did not say from the beginning of his speech to its end: "I did not say" or "I did not do." Rather, he denied it time and again through metonymy and under a veil, saying: ما يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أَقُولَ ما لَيْسَ لِي بِحَقٍّ — "It does not behoove me to say what I have no right to [say]," thus denying it by denying its cause; meaning, I had no right to that such that it would be possible for me to utter such a momentous statement. Then he said: إِنْ كُنْتُ قُلْتُهُ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُ — "Had I said it, You would certainly have known it," etc., denying it by denying its necessary consequence; meaning, had I said it, it would necessarily follow that You would know it, for Your knowledge encompasses me and all the Unseen.

Then he said: ما قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا ما أَمَرْتَنِي بِهِ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ رَبِّي وَرَبَّكُمْ — "I did not say to them [anything] except what You had commanded me [to say]: ‘‘Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord,’" denying it by presenting what contradicts it in its place through the method of restriction (الحصر) using "not" and "except" (ما وإلا); meaning, I did say a word to them, but it was that which You commanded me, which is "Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord." How then could I possibly say to them along with that, "Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah"?

Then he said: وَكُنْتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً ما دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنْتَ أَنْتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ — "And I was a witness to them so long as I was among them. But when You had taken me, You were Yourself the watcher over them." This is a denial from him (peace be upon him) that serves as a completion to his saying: "I did not say to them except what You had commanded me..." etc. This is because its meaning is: I did not say to them anything of what is attributed to me, and what I did say to them was only by a command from You, which is "Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord." I did not turn to any matter besides that, and I had no connection to them except witnessing and watching over their deeds as long as I remained. When You took me, I was severed from them, and You were the Watcher over them with Your eternal and universal witnessing, both before You took me and after, over them and over everything else.

Since the speech reached this point, it became appropriate for him (peace be upon him) to deny that statement from himself in another way that completes the ways he mentioned, and by which the denial becomes total. So he said: إِنْ تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبادُكَ — "If You punish them, they are indeed Your servants," etc. He says—as supported by the context—"If the matter is as I have mentioned, then I am apart from them and they are apart from me. So You and these servants of Yours: if You punish them, they are Your servants, and it is for the Master and Lord to punish His servants for their opposition and their associating partners with Him, and they are deserving of punishment. And if You forgive them, there is no reproach upon You, for You are the All-mighty (العزيز) who is never overcome, and the All-wise (الحكيم) who does not perform a foolish or vain act, but only does what is most beneficial."

From what we have explained, subtle aspects of the etiquette of servitude appear in his (peace be upon him) speech. He did not utter a sentence in his speech except that he mixed it with the most beautiful praise, with the most eloquent expression and the most truthful tongue.

Among such instances is what Allah the Exalted narrated from His Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny), and He joined the believers of his community with him in that. The Exalted said: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ كُلٌّ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَمَلائِكَتِهِ وَكُتُبِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ لا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِنْ رُسُلِهِ وَقالُوا سَمِعْنا وَأَطَعْنا غُفْرانَكَ رَبَّنا وَإِلَيْكَ الْمَصِيرُ ، لا يُكَلِّفُ اللهُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها لَها ما كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْها مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ رَبَّنا لا تُؤاخِذْنا إِنْ نَسِينا أَوْ أَخْطَأْنا رَبَّنا وَلا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنا إِصْراً كَما حَمَلْتَهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِنا رَبَّنا وَلا تُحَمِّلْنا ما لا طاقَةَ لَنا بِهِ وَاعْفُ عَنَّا وَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا أَنْتَ مَوْلانا فَانْصُرْنا عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكافِرِينَ — "The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful. Each [of them] has faith in Allah, His angels, His scriptures and His apostles. [They say,] ‘We make no distinction between any of His apostles.’ And they say, ‘We hear and obey. Our Lord, forgive us, and toward You is the return.’ Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity. To its benefit is what it earns, and to its detriment is what it acquires. ‘Our Lord! Do not take us to task if we forget or make a mistake! Our Lord! Do not place upon us a burden as You placed on those who were before us! Our Lord! Do not lay upon us what we have no strength to bear! Excuse us, forgive us, and be merciful to us! You are our Master, so help us against the faithless people’" (2:285-286).

The speech of the Exalted—as you see—narrates the faith of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) in the Noble Quran regarding the principles of knowledge (أصول المعارف) it contains, and regarding all the Divine rulings it contains. Then He joins with him (peace be upon him and his progeny) the believers of his community—not only his contemporaries present with him (peace be upon him and his progeny), but the believers of the entire community, as is apparent from the context.

The necessary implication of this is that the confession, praise, or supplication mentioned therein is, with respect to some of them, recounted as the "language of their state" (لسان حالهم), even if it is possible that it was uttered by others as the "language of their speech" (لسان قالهم). Alternatively, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) may have been the one addressing his Lord directly on behalf of his own noble self and on behalf of the believers, for by virtue of their faith, they are branches of the tree of his own pure and blessed soul.

The two verses encompass what serves as a comparison and a balance between the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) and the believers of this community (الأمة) in terms of how they received what was revealed to them in the Book of Allah. If you prefer, you could say: in terms of their refinement through the etiquette of servitude (أدب العبودية) toward the Book revealed to them. Indeed, it is evident that Allah, the Glorified, praised these [believers] and lightened their burden in these two verses by the very same measures for which He rebuked the former and shamed them in the preceding verses of Surah al-Baqarah. He condemned the People of the Book for differentiating between the angels of Allah—hating Gabriel and loving others—and between the revealed Books of Allah—disbelieving in the Quran while believing in others—and between the Messengers of Allah—believing in Moses, or in him and Jesus, while disbelieving in Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and between His rulings—believing in some of what is in the Book of Allah and disbelieving in other parts. In contrast, the believers of this community believed in Allah, His angels, His Books, and His Messengers: "We make no distinction between any of His apostles."

Thus, they showed refinement (تأدبوا) toward their Lord by submitting to the truths (المعارف) He established and conveyed to them. Then they showed refinement by responding to the rulings He called them to, saying: سَمِعْنا وَأَطَعْنا — "We hear and obey" (2:285), unlike the saying of the Jews: سَمِعْنا وَعَصَيْنا — "We hear and disobey" (2:93). Furthermore, they showed refinement by regarding themselves as owned servants (عبادا مملوكين) of their Lord, possessing nothing of themselves and not bestowing any favor upon Him for their faith or obedience. Thus they said: غُفْرانَكَ رَبَّنا — "Your forgiveness, our Lord!" (2:285), unlike the Jews who said: سَيُغْفَرُ لَنا — "We shall be forgiven" (7:169), and said: إِنَّ اللهَ فَقِيرٌ وَنَحْنُ أَغْنِياءُ — "Indeed Allah is poor and we are rich" (3:181), and said: لَنْ تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّاماً مَعْدُودَةً — "The Fire shall not touch us except for a few days" (2:80), among other such lapses.

Then Allah, the Glorified, said: لا يُكَلِّفُ اللهُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها لَها ما كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْها مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ — "Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity. To its merit is what it has earned, and within its debt is what it has incurred" (2:286). Divine tasking (التكليف), by its very nature, follows the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — upon which people were created. It is well known that the primordial nature, which is the mode of creation, only calls toward that for which it has been equipped, and in that lies the absolute happiness of life.

Indeed, if a matter were of such gravity as to demand extra attention, or if the commanded servant departed from the rule of the primordial nature and the garb of servitude, it would be permissible—by another rule of the primordial nature—for the Master, or whoever holds authority, to direct toward him a command that exceeds his usual capacity. For instance, he might be commanded to exercise precaution (الاحتياط) upon mere doubt, or to avoid forgetfulness and error when the importance of the matter is intensified—similar to the obligation of precaution regarding blood, private parts, and property in Islamic law. Alternatively, a burden (الكلفة) might be placed upon him, increasing his constraints whenever he increases in obstinacy and persistence in questioning, as Allah has informed us regarding similar instances with the Children of Israel.

In any case, His saying: لا يُكَلِّفُ اللهُ نَفْساً — "Allah does not task any soul" (2:286) is either the conclusion of the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers, which they uttered as a preamble to their saying: "Our Lord, do not take us to task...", so that it serves as a praise of the Exalted and a refutation of the delusion that Allah, the Glorified, punishes for what is beyond one's power or tasks with burdensome (الحرجي) rulings. This is refuted by the fact that Allah does not task a soul except within its capacity, and that what they asked for in their saying: "Our Lord, do not take us to task..." refers only to rulings under secondary titles (عناوين ثانوية) arising from the ruling itself or from the subjects due to their obstinacy, not from Him, the Exalted.

Alternatively, it is the speech of the Exalted placed between two segments of the supplication recounted in their speech—namely, their saying: "Your forgiveness, our Lord..." and their saying: "Our Lord, do not take us to task..."—to provide the aforementioned benefit and to serve as a refinement and instruction for them from Allah, the Exalted. In this case, it would follow the flow of their speech because they are believers in what Allah has revealed, and it is from Him. In any event, it is something upon which their speech relies and their supplication leans.

Then He mentioned the remainder of their supplication, or if you prefer, another group of their requests: رَبَّنا لا تُؤاخِذْنا — "Our Lord, do not take us to task if we forget or make a mistake" (2:286); رَبَّنا وَلا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنا إِصْراً — "Our Lord, do not place upon us a burden as You placed on those before us" (2:286); رَبَّنا وَلا تُحَمِّلْنا ما لا طاقَةَ لَنا بِهِ وَاعْفُ عَنَّا — "Our Lord, do not lay upon us what we have no strength to bear. Excuse us" (2:286). Their intention therein was to seek excuse for what issued from them of forgetfulness, error, and other causes of hardship. وَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا — "and forgive us, and have mercy on us" (2:286) regarding our other sins and mistakes. The mention of forgiveness (المغفرة) here does not necessitate repetition with respect to their previous saying: "Your forgiveness, our Lord," because that was a word recounted from them for the purpose of comparing their state and refinement toward their Lord with that of the People of the Book in their dealings with their Lord and their revealed Book. Furthermore, the station of supplication (الدعاء) does not preclude repetition, just like other stations.

The fact that this supplication encompasses the etiquette of servitude (أدب العبودية) in clinging to the hem of Lordship (الربوبية) time after time, acknowledging one's status as owned (المملوكية) and under guardianship (الولاية), and standing in the position of abasement and the poverty of servitude before the Lord of Might, is something that requires no further explanation.

In the Noble Quran, there are divine refinements (تأديبات إلهية) and high instructions for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding various types of praise with which he should praise his Lord, or requests he should ask of Him, as in His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ اللهُمَّ مالِكَ الْمُلْكِ تُؤْتِي الْمُلْكَ مَنْ تَشاءُ — "Say, ‘O Allah, Master of all sovereignty! You give sovereignty to whomever You wish’" (3:26) until the end of the two verses; and His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ اللهُمَّ فاطِرَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ عالِمَ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ أَنْتَ تَحْكُمُ بَيْنَ عِبادِكَ — "Say, ‘O Allah, Originator of the heavens and the earth, Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, You will judge between Your servants’" (39:46); and His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ وَسَلامٌ عَلى عِبادِهِ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفى — "Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, and Peace be to His servants whom He has chosen’" (27:59); and His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّ صَلاتِي وَنُسُكِي وَمَحْيايَ وَمَماتِي لِلَّهِ — "Say, ‘Indeed my prayer, my sacrifice, my life and my death are all for Allah’" (6:162); and His saying, the Exalted: وَقُلْ رَبِّ زِدْنِي عِلْماً — "and say, ‘My Lord! Increase me in knowledge’" (20:114); and His saying: وَقُلْ رَبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ هَمَزاتِ الشَّياطِينِ — "And say, ‘My Lord! I seek Your protection from the promptings of the devils’" (23:97), among many other verses.

What unites them all is that they encompass a magnificent etiquette (أدب) with which Allah refined His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and to which He invited his community.

7 - Their Etiquette with their Lord among the People: Their observance of etiquette toward their Lord in their dialogues with their people. This is also a vast subject, and it is appended to the etiquette of praising Allah, the Glorified. From another perspective, it is one of the chapters of practical propagation (التبليغ العملي), which is no less—and perhaps more—effective than verbal propagation.

There is much of this in the Quran. The Exalted said regarding a dialogue that took place between Noah and his people: قالُوا يا نُوحُ قَدْ جادَلْتَنا فَأَكْثَرْتَ جِدالَنا فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ ، قالَ إِنَّما يَأْتِيكُمْ بِهِ اللهُ إِنْ شاءَ وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ ، وَلا يَنْفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِي إِنْ أَرَدْتُ أَنْ أَنْصَحَ لَكُمْ إِنْ كانَ اللهُ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يُغْوِيَكُمْ هُوَ رَبُّكُمْ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ — "They said, ‘O Noah, you have disputed with us already, and you have disputed with us at great length. Now bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful.’ He said, ‘Allah will bring it to you only if He wishes, and you cannot frustrate [Him]. My advice will not benefit you, were I to wish to advise you, if Allah wishes to let you go astray. He is your Lord, and to Him you shall be brought back’" (11:32-34). He (peace be upon him) negated from himself what they attributed to him regarding the bringing of the sign to challenge them, and attributed it to his Lord, showing profound refinement by saying: "if He wishes," and then by saying: "and you cannot frustrate [Him]"—meaning Allah. For this reason, he attributed it to Him, the Exalted, using the name "Allah" rather than "my Lord," because Allah is the one to whom all beauty (جمال) and majesty (جلال) return. He did not stop at negating the power to bring the sign from himself and affirming it for Allah; he doubled it by negating the benefit of his advice to them if Allah did not intend for them to benefit. Thus, he completed the negation of power from himself and its affirmation for his Lord, justifying this by saying: "He is your Lord, and to Him you shall be brought back."

This is a dialogue filled with beautiful etiquette toward Allah, the Glorified, with which Noah (peace be upon him) addressed the tyrants of his people while arguing with them. He was the first of the prophets (peace be upon them) to open the door of argumentation in the call to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and to rise against idolatry, as mentioned in the Noble Quran.

This is the widest of these chapters for the researcher into the etiquette of the prophets (peace be upon them). One discovers subtleties of their biographies, which are filled with refinement and perfection. All their words, deeds, movements, and stillness are built upon the foundation of vigilance (المراقبة) and the presence of servitude (الحضور العبودي), even if their outward form appears as the action of one who is absent from his Lord or whose Lord is absent from him. The Exalted said: وَمَنْ عِنْدَهُ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَلا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ ، يُسَبِّحُونَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ لا يَفْتُرُونَ — "Those who are with Him are not too proud to worship Him, nor do they weary. They glorify [Him] night and day, and they do not flag" (21:19-20).

Allah, the Exalted, has recounted in His speech many dialogues from Hud, Salih, Abraham, Moses, Shu'ayb, Joseph, Solomon, Jesus, Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and other prophets (peace be upon them) in their various states, such as hardship and ease, war and peace, public and private, glad tidings and warnings, and so on.

Reflect upon His saying, the Exalted: فَرَجَعَ مُوسى إِلى قَوْمِهِ غَضْبانَ أَسِفاً قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَلَمْ يَعِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ وَعْداً حَسَناً أَفَطالَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْعَهْدُ أَمْ أَرَدْتُمْ أَنْ يَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُمْ مَوْعِدِي — "So Moses returned to his people, angry and sorrowful. He said, ‘O my people! Did not your Lord give you a good promise? Did the period seem too long to you? Or did you desire that your Lord’s wrath should descend upon you, so you failed your tryst with me?’" (20:86). It mentions Moses (peace be upon him) when he returned to his people filled with rage and indignation, yet that did not distract him from observing etiquette in the mention of his Lord.

And His saying, the Exalted: وَراوَدَتْهُ الَّتِي هُوَ فِي بَيْتِها عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَغَلَّقَتِ الْأَبْوابَ وَقالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ قالَ مَعاذَ اللهِ إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ — "And she in whose house he was solicited him. She shut the doors and said, ‘Come!’ He said, ‘Allah forbid! Indeed He is my Lord; He has given me a goodly home. Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous’" (12:23). And His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا تَاللهِ لَقَدْ آثَرَكَ اللهُ عَلَيْنا وَإِنْ كُنَّا لَخاطِئِينَ ، قالَ لا تَثْرِيبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْيَوْمَ يَغْفِرُ اللهُ لَكُمْ وَهو أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ — "They said, ‘By Allah, Allah has certainly preferred you over us, and we have indeed been erring.’ He said, ‘There shall be no reproach on you today. Allah will forgive you, and He is the most merciful of the merciful’" (12:91-92). It mentions Joseph in the midst of solicitation—which usually seizes a person's intellect and nullifies all resolve—yet it did not distract him from godwariness (التقوى), nor from observing etiquette in the mention of his Lord and with others.

And His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا رَآهُ مُسْتَقِرًّا عِنْدَهُ قالَ هذا مِنْ فَضْلِ رَبِّي لِيَبْلُوَنِي أَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ وَمَنْ شَكَرَ فَإِنَّما يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ رَبِّي غَنِيٌّ كَرِيمٌ — "When he saw it set before him, he said, ‘This is by the grace of my Lord, to test me whether I am grateful or ungrateful. Whoever is grateful is grateful only for his own sake, and whoever is ungrateful [should know that] my Lord is indeed all-sufficient, all-generous’" (27:40). This is Solomon (peace be upon him), who was given such great sovereignty, effective command, and wondrous power that he ordered the throne of the Queen of Sheba to be brought from Sheba to Palestine, and it was brought in less than the blink of an eye. Yet, pride of soul and vanity did not seize him, nor did he forget his Lord; he did not hesitate to praise his Lord in his assembly with the most beautiful praise.

Compare this to what Allah mentioned of the story of Nimrod with Abraham (peace be upon him) when he said: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِي حَاجَّ إِبْراهِيمَ فِي رَبِّهِ أَنْ آتاهُ اللهُ الْمُلْكَ إِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّيَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ قالَ أَنَا أُحْيِي وَأُمِيتُ — "Have you not regarded him who argued with Abraham about his Lord, because Allah had given him kingdom? When Abraham said, ‘My Lord is He who gives life and causes to die,’ he said, ‘I [too] give life and cause to die’" (2:258). He said that when he brought two men from prison, ordering the execution of one and the release of the other.

Or to what Pharaoh of Egypt said, as recounted by Allah: يا قَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَهذِهِ الْأَنْهارُ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي أَفَلا تُبْصِرُونَ ، أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْ هذَا الَّذِي هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلا يَكادُ يُبِينُ ، فَلَوْ لا أُلْقِيَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ — "‘O my people! Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me and these rivers that flow at my feet? Do you not perceive? Am I not better than this one who is a lowly wretch and can hardly speak clearly? Why have not golden bracelets been cast upon him?’" (43:51-53). He boasts of the kingdom of Egypt, its rivers, and a certain amount of gold that he and his assembly possessed, and he does not hesitate to say, as Allah recounted: أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلى — "I am your exalted lord" (79:24). Yet he was the one being humbled by the signs of Moses day after day—the flood, the locusts, the lice, the frogs, and so on.

And His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ هُما فِي الْغارِ إِذْ يَقُولُ لِصاحِبِهِ لا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَنا — "when the two of them were in the cave, he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us’" (9:40). And His saying: وَإِذْ أَسَرَّ النَّبِيُّ إِلى بَعْضِ أَزْواجِهِ حَدِيثاً — "When the Prophet confided a matter to one of his wives" until He said: فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَها بِهِ قالَتْ مَنْ أَنْبَأَكَ هذا قالَ نَبَّأَنِيَ الْعَلِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ — "When he informed her about it, she said, ‘Who informed you of this?’ He said, ‘The All-knowing and the All-aware informed me’" (66:3). The severity of the matter, the horror, and the panic on the day of fear did not shake him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from mentioning that his Lord was with him, nor did his noble soul incline toward the threat that loomed over him. Likewise, what he confided to some of his wives in private encompassed the observance of etiquette in the mention of his Lord.

Following the pattern of these cited examples are all the other instances in their stories (peace be upon them) in the Noble Quran regarding magnificent etiquette and noble traditions. Were it not that our discourse has become lengthy in these discussions, we would have exhausted their stories and provided a full investigation of them.

8 - The Etiquette of the Prophets with the People: The etiquette of the prophets (peace be upon them) with people in their social interactions and dialogues. The manifestations of this category are the arguments recounted from them in the Quran with the disbelievers, the dialogues they had with the believers among them, and a small portion of their recounted biographies.

As for etiquette in speech, you will not find in the fragments of their words with the tyrants and the ignorant that they addressed them with anything that would offend them, or with insult, humiliation, or scorn. Although their opponents reached every extreme in insulting, slandering, mocking, and ridiculing them, they did not respond except with the best of speech and the most sincere exhortation, turning away from them with peace: "and when the ignorant address them, they say, ‘Peace!’" (25:63).

The Exalted said: فَقالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ —meaning the people of Noah— ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً مِثْلَنا وَما نَراكَ اتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَراذِلُنا بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ وَما نَرى لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا مِنْ فَضْلٍ بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كاذِبِينَ ، قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَآتانِي رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِهِ فَعُمِّيَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنُلْزِمُكُمُوها وَأَنْتُمْ لَها كارِهُونَ — "The elite of the faithless of his people said, ‘We do not see you to be anything but a human being like ourselves, and we do not see anyone following you except the lowest of us, [who follow you] without any reflection, nor do we see in you any superiority over us; rather we think you are liars.’ He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I possess a manifest proof from my Lord and He has granted me a mercy from Himself, and it should be obscured for you, shall we force it upon you while you are averse to it?’" (11:27-28).

And the Exalted said, recounting the words of 'Ad, the people of Hud: إِنْ نَقُولُ إِلَّا اعْتَراكَ بَعْضُ آلِهَتِنا بِسُوءٍ قالَ إِنِّي أُشْهِدُ اللهَ وَاشْهَدُوا أَنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ ، مِنْ دُونِهِ — "All we say is that some of our gods have visited you with some evil.’ He said, ‘I call Allah to witness, and you [too] bear witness, that I am quit of what you polytheize besides Him’" (11:54-55). By "visiting him with some evil," they meant afflicting him (peace be upon him) with something like madness or folly and the like.

And the Exalted said, recounting the words of Azar: قالَ أَراغِبٌ أَنْتَ عَنْ آلِهَتِي يا إِبْراهِيمُ لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ لَأرْجُمَنَّكَ وَاهْجُرْنِي مَلِيًّا ، قالَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ كانَ بِي حَفِيًّا — "He said, ‘Are you averse to my gods, O Abraham? If you do not desist, I will surely stone you. Now leave me for a long while.’ He said, ‘Peace be to you! I will plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed He is gracious to me’" (19:46-47).

And the Exalted said, recounting the words of the people of Shu'ayb (peace be upon him): قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ إِنَّا لَنَراكَ فِي سَفاهَةٍ وَإِنَّا لَنَظُنُّكَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ ، قالَ يا قَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِي سَفاهَةٌ وَلكِنِّي رَسُولٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ ، أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسالاتِ رَبِّي وَأَنَا لَكُمْ ناصِحٌ أَمِينٌ — "The elite of the faithless of his people said, ‘Indeed we see you in folly, and we indeed consider you to be a liar.’ He said, ‘O my people! There is no folly in me; rather I am an apostle from the Lord of all the worlds. I deliver to you the messages of my Lord, and I am a trustworthy advisor for you’" (7:66-68).

And the Exalted said: قالَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَما رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ ، قالَ رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُمَا — "Pharaoh said, ‘And what is the Lord of all the worlds?’ He said, ‘The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them’" until He said: قالَ إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ الَّذِي أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمَجْنُونٌ ، قالَ رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقِ وَالْمَغْرِبِ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ — "He said, ‘Indeed your apostle who has been sent to you is surely crazy.’ He said, ‘The Lord of the east and the west and whatever is between them, if you use your reason’" (26:23-28).

And the Exalted said, recounting the words of the people of Mary: قالُوا يا مَرْيَمُ لَقَدْ جِئْتِ شَيْئاً فَرِيًّا ، يا أُخْتَ هارُونَ ما كانَ أَبُوكِ امْرَأَ سَوْءٍ وَما كانَتْ أُمُّكِ بَغِيًّا ، فَأَشارَتْ إِلَيْهِ قالُوا كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَنْ كانَ فِي الْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا ، قالَ إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللهِ آتانِيَ الْكِتابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا — "They said, ‘O Mary, you have certainly brought a monstrous thing! O sister of Aaron[’s lineage]! Your father was not an evil man, nor was your mother unchaste.’ Thereat she pointed to him. They said, ‘How can we speak to one who is yet a baby in the cradle?’ He said, ‘Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet’" (19:27-30).

And the Exalted said, consoling His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding what they accused him of—soothsaying, madness, and poetry: فذَكِّرْ فَما أَنْتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِكاهِنٍ وَلا مَجْنُونٍ ، أَمْ يَقُولُونَ شاعِرٌ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِهِ رَيْبَ الْمَنُونِ ، قُلْ تَرَبَّصُوا فَإِنِّي مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الْمُتَرَبِّصِينَ — "So remind! By the blessing of your Lord, you are neither a soothsayer nor crazy. Do they say, ‘[He is] a poet; we await for him some adverse turn of fortune’? Say, ‘Wait! I too am waiting with you’" (52:29-31).

And He said: وَقالَ الظَّالِمُونَ إِنْ تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلاً مَسْحُوراً ، انْظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلاً — "And the wrongdoers say, ‘You are just following a bewitched man.’ Look, how they coin allegories for you! So they have gone astray, and cannot find a way" (25:8-9).

Among other types of insults, accusations, and humiliations recounted from them in the Quran. It is not narrated from the prophets (peace be upon them) that they met them with harshness or obscenity, but rather with correct speech and good, gentle logic, following the divine instruction that taught them the best of speech and beautiful etiquette. The Exalted said, addressing Moses and Aaron (peace be upon them): اذْهَبا إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغى ، فَقُولا لَهُ قَوْلاً لَيِّناً لَعَلَّهُ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشى — "Let the two of you go to Pharaoh. Indeed he has rebelled. Speak to him in a soft manner; maybe he will take admonition or fear" (20:43-44). And He said to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): وَإِمَّا تُعْرِضَنَّ عَنْهُمُ ابْتِغاءَ رَحْمَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكَ تَرْجُوها فَقُلْ لَهُمْ قَوْلاً مَيْسُوراً — "And if you have to turn away from them, seeking a mercy from your Lord which you expect, then speak to them a gentle word" (17:28).

Among their etiquette in dialogue and address was that they would lower themselves to the level of the people, speaking to every class according to their level of understanding. This is evident upon reflection on the dialogues with various people recounted from Noah and those after him. Both schools [Sunni and Shi'i] have narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): إنا معاشر الأنبياء أمرنا أن نكلم الناس على قدر عقولهم — "We, the assembly of prophets, have been commanded to speak to people according to the capacity of their intellects."

It should be known that the mission of prophethood is built upon the foundation of guidance to the truth, its clarification, and victory for it. Therefore, they must equip themselves with the truth in their call, divest themselves of falsehood, and avoid the snares of misguidance, whatever they may be—whether this accords with people's pleasure or their wrath, and whether it results in their obedience or their aversion. Indeed, the most severe prohibition has come from Him, the Exalted, to His prophets, and the most eloquent warning even against following falsehood in word or deed for the purpose of aiding the truth. For falsehood is falsehood, whether it occurs on the path of truth or not. The call to truth is incompatible with the permissibility of falsehood, even if it were on the path of truth; and the truth to which falsehood leads and which it produces is not truth from all its aspects.

For this reason, the Exalted said: وَما كُنْتُ مُتَّخِذَ الْمُضِلِّينَ عَضُداً — "nor do I take those who mislead as assistants" (18:51). And He said: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً ، إِذاً لَأَذَقْناكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَياةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَماتِ ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنا نَصِيراً — "Had We not fortified you, you would have certainly inclined toward them a little. Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death, and then you would not have found for yourself any helper against Us" (17:74-75). Thus, there is no compromise, no involvement, and no flattery regarding the truth, nor is there any sanctity for falsehood.

Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, equipped the men of His call and the guardians of His religion—who are the prophets (peace be upon them)—with that which eases for them the path to following the truth and aiding it. The Exalted said: ما كانَ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ مِنْ حَرَجٍ فِيما فَرَضَ اللهُ لَهُ سُنَّةَ اللهِ فِي الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَكانَ أَمْرُ اللهِ قَدَراً مَقْدُوراً ، الَّذِينَ يُبَلِّغُونَ رِسالاتِ اللهِ وَيَخْشَوْنَهُ وَلا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللهَ وَكَفى بِاللهِ حَسِيباً — "There is no blame on the Prophet in respect of what Allah has made obligatory for him: Allah’s precedent with those who passed away before, and Allah’s command is a decree ordained. Such as deliver the messages of Allah and fear Him, and fear no one except Allah, and Allah suffices as a reckoner" (33:38-39). Thus, He informed that they do not feel constrained in what Allah has made obligatory for them; they fear Him and fear no one else. There is no obstacle to their manifesting the truth, no matter what level it reaches or where it leads them.

Then He promised them victory in what they rose for, saying: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا لِعِبادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ ، إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ ، وَإِنَّ جُنْدَنا لَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ — "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they will indeed be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will indeed be the victors" (37:171-173). And He said: إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا — "Indeed We shall help Our apostles" (40:51).

For this reason, we find them, in what is recounted from them, paying no heed to anything in manifesting the truth and speaking the truth, even if people do not approve of it and find it bitter to their taste. The Exalted said, recounting Noah addressing his people: وَلكِنِّي أَراكُمْ قَوْماً تَجْهَلُونَ — "but I see that you are an ignorant people" (11:29). And He said regarding the saying of Hud: إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا مُفْتَرُونَ — "you are nothing but fabricators" (11:50); and his saying to his people: قَدْ وَقَعَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ رِجْسٌ وَغَضَبٌ أَتُجادِلُونَنِي فِي أَسْماءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ ما نَزَّلَ اللهُ بِها مِنْ سُلْطانٍ — "Punishment and wrath from your Lord have already come down upon you. Do you dispute with me regarding names which you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority?" (7:71). And the Exalted said, recounting Lot: بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُسْرِفُونَ — "Rather you are a profligate people" (7:81). And He recounted from Abraham his saying to his people: أُفٍّ لَكُمْ وَلِما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ — "Fie on you and on what you worship besides Allah! Do you not exercise your reason?" (21:67). And He recounted from Moses, in response to Pharaoh's saying to him: إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يا مُوسى مَسْحُوراً ، قالَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ ما أَنْزَلَ هؤُلاءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ بَصائِرَ وَإِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يا فِرْعَوْنُ مَثْبُوراً — "‘O Moses, I indeed think you are bewitched.’ He said, ‘You certainly know that no one has sent these down except the Lord of the heavens and the earth as eye-openers, and I indeed think you are doomed, O Pharaoh!’" (17:101-102)—meaning one who is barred from believing in the truth, expelled, and perishing—among other instances.

All of these are from the observance of etiquette toward the truth and following it. No objective is dearer than it, and no goal is more noble or precious, even if some of it contradicts the etiquette prevalent among people, which is built upon following desire and pursuing the goods of life through flattering the practitioners of falsehood and submitting to and fawning over the corrupt and the affluent as a matter of practical policy.

The sum of the matter is that etiquette (الأدب), as previously mentioned at the beginning of these discussions, only manifests in permissible speech and righteous deeds. It varies according to the different paths of life in societies and the opinions and beliefs that take root therein and from which they are formed. The divine call, upon which the religious society is based, only follows the truth in belief and action. The truth does not mix with falsehood, nor does it blend with it, rely upon it, or seek assistance from it. Thus, there is no escape from manifesting it and following it. The etiquette that manifests therein is to follow the best of paths on the way of truth and to clothe oneself in the most elegant of garbs—such as choosing gentle speech when it is possible to speak both gently and harshly, and choosing haste in reporting when both speed and slowness are possible.

This is what He commands in His saying, the Exalted: وَكَتَبْنا لَهُ —meaning Moses— فِي الْأَلْواحِ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مَوْعِظَةً وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ فَخُذْها بِقُوَّةٍ وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا بِأَحْسَنِها — "We wrote for him in the Tablets advice concerning all things and an elaboration of all things: ‘Hold on to them with power, and bid your people to hold on to the best of them’" (7:145). And He gave glad tidings to His servants who take hold of it in His saying: فَبَشِّرْ عِبادِ ، الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَداهُمُ اللهُ وَأُولئِكَ هُمْ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ — "So give good news to My servants who listen to the word [of Allah] and follow the best [sense] of it. They are the ones whom Allah has guided, and it is they who possess intellect" (39:17-18). Thus, there is no etiquette in falsehood, nor is there etiquette in a mixture of truth and falsehood. For whatever departs from the pure truth is misguidance that the Guardian of Truth does not approve of, as He said: فَما ذا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ إِلَّا الضَّلالُ — "So what is there after the truth except error?" (10:32).

This is what called the prophets of truth to clarity of speech and truthfulness of tongue, even if in some instances it was something not approved by the tradition of flattery, compromise, and the false etiquette prevalent in non-religious societies.

Among their etiquette with people in their social interactions and their conduct among them was the respect for the weak and the strong alike, and the emphasis and exaggeration regarding the rights of the people of knowledge and godwariness among them. Since they built upon the foundation of servitude and the cultivation of the human soul, there followed from it the equality of judgment for the rich and the poor, the young and the old, the man and the woman, the master and the slave, the ruler and the ruled, the prince and the subject, and the sultan and the commoner. At that point, the distinction of attributes and the exclusivity of the strong to social advantages became void. The division of possession and lack, deprivation and enjoyment, and happiness and misery between the attributes of wealth and poverty, and strength and weakness, was abolished—the idea that the strong and the rich deserve the highest of every position, the most luxurious of every life, the most restful and easiest of every struggle, and the lightest of every duty. Rather, people were equal in that regard. He said: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ لِتَعارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ — "O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify yourselves with one another. Indeed the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you" (49:13). The arrogance of the strong in their strength and the boasting of the rich in their wealth were replaced by humility toward the truth, hastening toward forgiveness and mercy, racing in good deeds, and struggling in the way of Allah seeking His pleasure.

Respect was then accorded to the poor just as it was to the rich, and etiquette was observed with the weak just as with the rich; rather, the former were distinguished by additional compassion, kindness, and mercy. The Exalted said, refining His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): وَاصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ وَلا تَعْدُ عَيْناكَ عَنْهُمْ تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَلا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنا قَلْبَهُ عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ وَكانَ أَمْرُهُ فُرُطاً — "Content yourself with those who pray to their Lord morning and evening, desiring His face, and do not let your eyes pass beyond them, desiring the adornment of the life of this world, and do not obey him whose heart We have made oblivious to Our remembrance, and who follows his own desires, and whose conduct is [mere] profligacy" (18:28). And the Exalted said: وَلا تَطْرُدِ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ ما عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَما مِنْ حِسابِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَتَطْرُدَهُمْ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Do not drive away those who call their Lord morning and evening desiring His face. Their account is not your responsibility, and your account is not their responsibility, that you should drive them away and thus become one of the wrongdoers" (6:52). And He said: لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَاخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ، وَقُلْ إِنِّي أَنَا النَّذِيرُ الْمُبِينُ — "Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided to certain groups of them, and do not grieve for them, and lower your wing to the believers, and say, ‘I am indeed a manifest warner’" (17:88-89).

This beautiful etiquette is encompassed in what Allah recounted of the dialogue between Noah (peace be upon him) and his people when he said: فَقالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً مِثْلَنا وَما نَراكَ اتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَراذِلُنا بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ وَما نَرى لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا مِنْ فَضْلٍ بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كاذِبِينَ ، قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَآتانِي رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِهِ فَعُمِّيَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنُلْزِمُكُمُوها وَأَنْتُمْ لَها كارِهُونَ ، وَيا قَوْمِ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مالاً إِنْ أَجرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ وَما أَنَا بِطارِدِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا رَبِّهِمْ وَلكِنِّي أَراكُمْ قَوْماً تَجْهَلُونَ —meaning in your belittling of the poor and weak— وَيا قَوْمِ مَنْ يَنْصُرُنِي مِنَ اللهِ إِنْ طَرَدْتُهُمْ أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ ، وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلا أَقُولُ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ —meaning I do not claim anything that distinguishes me from you except that I am a messenger to you— وَلا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ خَيْراً اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ —meaning of the goodness and happiness expected from them— إِنِّي إِذاً لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "The elite of the faithless of his people said, ‘We do not see you to be anything but a human being like ourselves, and we do not see anyone following you except the lowest of us, [who follow you] without any reflection, nor do we see in you any superiority over us; rather we think you are liars.’ He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I possess a manifest proof from my Lord and He has granted me a mercy from Himself, and it should be obscured for you, shall we force it upon you while you are averse to it? O my people! I do not ask you any wealth for it. My reward lies only with Allah. I will not drive away those who have faith; they will indeed encounter their Lord. But I see that you are an ignorant people. O my people! Who would help me against Allah were I to drive them away? Will you not then take admonition? I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, neither do I know the Unseen, nor do I say that I am an angel. Neither do I say of those who are despicable in your eyes that Allah will never grant them any good—Allah knows best what is in their souls—for then I would indeed be one of the wrongdoers’" (11:27-31).
