Similar to this negation of distinction is the saying of Shu'ayb to his people as recounted by Allah: وَما أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخالِفَكُمْ إِلى ما أَنْهاكُمْ عَنْهُ إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا الْإِصْلاحَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُ وَما تَوْفِيقِي إِلَّا بِاللهِ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ — "I do not desire to oppose you by [doing] what I forbid you. I only desire reform as much as I can. My success lies only with Allah: in Him I have put my trust, and to Him I turn penitently" (11:88). And Allah, the Exalted, introduced His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to the people, saying: لَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ ما عَنِتُّمْ حَرِيصٌ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ — "There has certainly come to you an apostle from among yourselves. Grievous to him is your distress; he has a deep concern for you, and is most kind and merciful to the believers" (9:128). And He also said: وَمِنْهُمُ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ النَّبِيَّ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ قُلْ أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ لَكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَرحْمَةٌ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ — "Among them are those who molest the Prophet and say, ‘He is [all] ear.’ Say, ‘An ear that is good for you: he has faith in Allah and trusts the believers, and is a mercy for those of you who have faith’" (9:61). And He also said: وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ — "And indeed you possess a great character" (68:4). And He also said, which summarizes all the above: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ — "We did not send you but as a mercy to all the worlds" (21:107).

These verses, although according to their literal meaning they refer to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) good character (أخلاقه) rather than his etiquette (أدبه)—which is something beyond character—nevertheless, the type of etiquette—as previously explained—is derived from the type of character. Furthermore, etiquette itself is among the derivative traits of character.

(Another Narrative Discussion)

The Quranic verses from which his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) noble character and beautiful etiquette are derived are mostly revealed in the form of commands and prohibitions. Therefore, we have seen fit to cite in this place narrations regarding his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) traditions (سننه) that contain the summaries of his character, which point toward his beautiful divine etiquette. These are, moreover, supported by the noble Quranic verses.

1 - In Ma'ani al-Akhbar, through a chain from Abu Halah al-Tamimi from al-Hasan ibn 'Ali (عليه‌السلام); and through another chain from al-Rida from his fathers from 'Ali ibn al-Husayn from al-Hasan ibn 'Ali (عليه‌السلام); and through another chain from a man from the descendants of Abu Halah from al-Hasan ibn 'Ali (عليه‌السلام):

He said: I asked my maternal uncle, Hind ibn Abi Halah—who was a describer of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and I desired for him to describe something of him to me so that I might cling to it—and he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was magnificent and honored. His face would glow like the glowing of the moon on the night of the full moon. He was taller than one of medium height and shorter than one who is lanky. He had a large head and wavy hair; if his hair parted, he would leave it parted, otherwise his hair would not exceed the lobes of his ears when it was full. He was of a bright complexion, with a wide forehead and long, arched eyebrows that were thick without being joined; between them was a vein that would swell when he was angry. He had a light over him that surpassed him, such that one who did not observe him closely would think him to have a high-bridged nose. He had a thick beard, smooth cheeks, a wide mouth, and bright, spaced teeth. He had a fine line of hair from the chest to the navel. His neck was like the neck of a statue in the purity of silver. He was of balanced build, firm-bodied, with his stomach and chest being level. He was broad-shouldered, with large joints and a wide chest. His unclothed parts were radiant. There was a line of hair connecting his upper chest to his navel like a thread, while his breasts and stomach were otherwise bare of hair. He had hair on his forearms, shoulders, and upper chest. He had long forearms, wide palms, and thick hands and feet. He had long fingers and straight limbs. His arches were high, and his feet were smooth such that water would roll off them. When he moved, he moved with vigor, stepping firmly and walking with ease. He was swift of pace; when he walked, it was as if he were descending a slope. When he turned, he turned with his whole body. He kept his gaze lowered; his looking toward the ground was longer than his looking toward the sky. Most of his looking was observing. He would initiate the greeting of peace to whomever he met."

He said: I said to him: "Describe his speech to me." He said: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was constantly sorrowful, perpetually in thought, having no rest. He was long in silence, not speaking without need. He would begin and end his speech with the corners of his mouth. He spoke with concise speech (جوامع الكلم)—decisive, neither superfluous nor deficient. He was gentle, neither harsh nor humiliating. He would magnify a blessing even if it were small, never disparaging any part of it; however, he would neither disparage a taste nor praise it. The world and what it contains would not make him angry, but when the truth was transgressed, no one would recognize him, and nothing could stand before his anger until he had vindicated it. When he pointed, he pointed with his whole palm. When he was amazed, he would turn it over. When he spoke, he would join his hands, striking his right palm against the inside of his left thumb. When he was angry, he would turn away and avert his face. When he was joyful, he would lower his gaze. Most of his laughter was a smile, revealing teeth like hailstones."

Al-Saduq said: Up to here is the narration of al-Qasim ibn al-Mani' from Isma'il ibn Muhammad ibn Ishaq ibn Ja'far ibn Muhammad, and the rest is the narration of 'Abd al-Rahman to the end:

Al-Hasan (عليه‌السلام) said: "I kept this hidden from al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام) for a time, then I narrated it to him, only to find that he had preceded me to it. I asked him about it and found that he had already asked his father (عليه‌السلام) about the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entry, his exit, his assembly, and his appearance, and he had left nothing out."

Al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام) said: "I asked my father (عليه‌السلام) about the entry of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he said: 'His entry into his home was permitted for him. When he retired to his house, he divided his entry into three parts: a part for Allah, a part for his family, and a part for himself. Then he divided his own part between himself and the people, conveying it through the elite to the general public, withholding nothing from them.'"

It was of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) conduct in the portion for the community to prefer the people of merit with his refinement (أدبه), and he would divide it according to their merit in religion. Among them were those with one need, those with two needs, and those with many needs. He would occupy himself with them and occupy them with what would reform them and the community by asking them about their affairs and informing them of what is appropriate. He would say: "Let those present among you inform those who are absent, and convey to me the need of one who is unable to convey his own need. For whoever conveys to a ruler the need of one who cannot convey it, Allah will make his feet firm on the Day of Resurrection." Nothing would be mentioned in his presence except that, and he would not accept anything else from anyone. They would enter as seekers and would not depart except having tasted something, and they would leave as guides.

"I asked him about the exit of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), how he would act therein. He said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would guard his tongue except regarding what concerned him. He would unite them and not repel them. He would honor the noble person of every people and appoint him over them. He would warn the people and be on guard against them, without withholding his cheerfulness or his character from anyone. He would check on his companions and ask the people about the people. He would beautify what is good and strengthen it, and disparage what is ugly and weaken it. He was balanced in his affairs, not inconsistent. He was never heedless for fear that they might become heedless and incline away. He would not fall short of the truth nor exceed it. Those who were closest to him among the people were the best of them; the most virtuous of them in his sight were those most comprehensive in their sincere advice to the Muslims, and the greatest of them in status in his sight were those best in consolation and support.'"

He (peace be upon him) said: "I then asked him about his assembly (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he replied: 'He would neither sit nor stand except while remembering Allah. He did not reserve specific places for himself and forbade others from doing so. When he joined a group, he would sit wherever the assembly ended—and he commanded others to do the same. He gave every person sitting with him their due share of attention, such that none of his companions would think that anyone was more honored by him than themselves. Whoever sat with him, he would remain patient with them until they were the one to depart. If someone asked him for a need, he would not return except with it fulfilled or with a kind word (بميسور من القول). His noble character (خلقه) encompassed all people, such that he became like a father to them, and they were all equal before him in matters of truth (الحق). His assembly was one of forbearance (حلم), modesty (حياء), truthfulness (صدق), and trustworthiness (أمانة). Voices were not raised therein, nor were sanctities (الحرم) violated, nor were slips (فلتاته) repeated. They were balanced and connected to one another through godwariness (التقوى), being humble, honoring the elderly, showing mercy to the young, preferring the needy over themselves, and looking after the stranger.'"

I then asked: "How was his conduct (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) among his companions?" He (peace be upon him) replied: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was perpetually cheerful, easy-tempered, and gentle in disposition (لين الجانب). He was neither harsh nor coarse, nor clamorous, nor obscene, nor a fault-finder, nor a flatterer. He would overlook what he did not desire, yet he never made those who hoped in him despair or feel disappointed. He had freed himself from three things: hypocrisy (المراء), talkativeness (الإكثار), and that which did not concern him. He spared people from three things: he never disparaged anyone, nor shamed them, nor sought out their slips or private faults (عورته). He only spoke in matters where he hoped for a reward. When he spoke, those sitting with him would lower their heads as if birds were perched upon them; when he fell silent, they would speak. They did not dispute in his presence; if someone spoke, they would listen attentively until he finished. Their conversation in his presence was like that of their foremost ones (أوليتهم). He would laugh at what they laughed at and marvel at what they marveled at. He was patient with the stranger's coarseness in questioning and speech—to the point that his companions would try to draw them away, but he would say: 'If you see a person in need seeking it, then assist him.' He did not accept praise except from one who was reciprocating (مكافئ), and he never interrupted anyone's speech until they exceeded the limit, at which point he would end it by a prohibition or by standing up."

He said: "I then asked him about the silence of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)." He (peace be upon him) replied: "His silence (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was based on four things: forbearance (الحلم), caution (الحذر), deliberation (التقدير), and reflection (التفكير). As for deliberation, it was in treating all people equally in his gaze and listening. As for his reflection, it was upon that which remains and that which perishes. Forbearance and patience were combined for him, such that nothing would anger or provoke (يستفزه) him. Caution was gathered for him in four things: his adopting what is good so that he might be followed, his abandoning what is ugly so that it might be desisted from, his exertion of opinion in the rectification of his nation, and his undertaking that which combined for them the good of this world and the Hereafter."

I say: This was also narrated in Makarim al-Akhlaq, citing the book of Muhammad ibn Ishaq ibn Ibrahim al-Taliqani, on the authority of his trustworthy sources from al-Hasan and al-Husayn (peace be upon them). It is stated in al-Bihar: "The narration is among the well-known reports (الأخبار المشهورة) which the Sunnis (العامة) have narrated in most of their books." End quote.

Many narrations have been reported from the companions with the same meaning or the meaning of some of its parts.

His saying: "well-proportioned (المربوع)" refers to one who is between tall and short. "Slender (المشذب)" refers to a tall person who does not have much flesh on his body. "Wavy hair (رجل الشعر)" is from the category of knowledge (علم), meaning it was between lank and curly. "Lock (العقيقة)" refers to a straight strand of hair. "Luminous in complexion (أزهر اللون)" means his color was bright and clear. "Arched (الأزج)" refers to eyebrows that are thin and long. "Ample (السوابغ)" refers to wide eyebrows. "Joined (القرن)" with two fathahs refers to the meeting of the two eyebrows. "Aquiline (الشمم)" refers to the elevation of the bridge of the nose along with beauty and straightness. "Thick-bearded (كث اللحية)" refers to hair that is gathered and dense without being overly long. "Smooth-cheeked (سهل الخد)" means they were even without much flesh. "Wide-mouthed (ضليع الفم)" is considered a mark of beauty in men. "Gapped (المفلج)" is from al-faljah with two fathahs, referring to a space between the feet, hands, or teeth. "Bright-toothed (الأشنب)" refers to one with white teeth.

"The line of hair (المشربة)" is the hair from the middle of the chest to the navel. "Statue-like (الدمية)" refers to a gazelle. "Shoulder (المنكب)" is the meeting point of the head of the shoulder and the upper arm. "Joints (الكراديس)" is the plural of kurdus, which are the two bones where they meet at a joint. "Luminous in his bare parts (أنور المتجرد)": al-mutajarrid is the active participle of al-tajarrud, which is to be stripped of clothing and the like. The meaning is that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was beautiful in appearance and fine in physical build when his body was uncovered.

"The pit of the throat (اللبة)" is the place where a necklace sits on the chest. The "navel (السرة)" is well-known. "Wrist (الزند)" is the joint of the forearm and the palm. "Wide-palmed (رحب الراحة)" means having a broad palm. "Thick (الشثن)" with two fathahs refers to thickness in the feet and palms. "Straight-limbed (سبط القصب)" means having smooth, straight bones without protrusions. "Arch of the foot (أخمص القدم)" is the part that does not touch the ground. "Lean-waisted (الخمصان)" means having a slender belly; thus, "hollow-arched (خمصان الأخمصين)" refers to them having a significant elevation from the ground. "Spaciousness (الفسحة)" is width. "Vigor (القلع)" with two fathahs refers to strength in walking.

"Leaning (التكفؤ)" in walking refers to swaying or tilting. "Swift-paced (ذريع المشية)" means fast in walking. "Slope (الصبب)" is the downward incline of a road or land. "Lowering the gaze (خافض الطرف)" is explained by what follows it: "his gaze was toward the ground," etc.

"Corners of the mouth (الأشداق)" is the plural of shidq—with a kasrah then a sukun—which is the corner of the mouth from the inside of the cheeks. Opening and closing speech with the corners of the mouth is a metaphor for eloquence (الفصاحة). It is said: "he spoke with his corners" (تشدق) when one twists his mouth for the sake of eloquent speech. "Gentle (الدمث)" is from al-damathah (gentleness), and its explanation follows it: "neither harsh nor humiliating." "Taste (الذواق)" with a fathah is what is tasted of food. "Turned away (انشاح)" is from al-nashuh, meaning he averted himself. "He smiled revealing teeth like hailstones (يفتر عن مثل حب الغمام)": a man aftarra when he smiles a beautiful smile. "Hailstones (حب الغمام)" refers to hail. The meaning is that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would smile a beautiful smile that revealed his teeth.

His saying: "He would direct that [time] through the elite to the general public (فيرد ذلك بالخاصة على العامة)" etc., means that although he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in the portion of time reserved for himself, he did not completely cut himself off from the people. Rather, he remained connected to them through his close companions, answering their questions and fulfilling their needs, withholding nothing from them of his own time. "Seekers (الرواد)" is the plural of ra'id, who is the one who precedes a group or caravan to find pasture or a dwelling place.

His saying: "He did not reserve specific places (لا يوطن الأماكن) and forbade doing so" refers to assemblies; meaning he did not designate a specific seat for himself among those present to avoid taking a position of prominence or precedence. Thus, his saying: "And when he joined..." etc., serves as an explanation for it. "Sanctities were not violated (لا تؤبن فيه الحرم)" means the honors of people were not disparaged in his presence. Al-ubnah with a dammah means a defect, and al-huram is the plural of hurmah (sanctity/honor).

His saying: "Nor were his slips repeated (ولا تثنى فلتاته)" is from al-tathniyah meaning repetition. Al-falatat is the plural of faltah, which is a slip or error. Meaning, if a slip occurred from one of those sitting with him, he would clarify it to them so they would be cautious not to fall into it a second time. "Cheerfulness (البشر)" with a kasrah then a sukun is the radiance of the face. "Clamorous (الصخاب)" is one who shouts loudly.

His saying: "Their conversation in his presence was like that of their foremost ones (حديثهم عنده حديث أوليتهم)": al-awliyah is the plural of wali (successor/follower). It likely means the one who follows, and the meaning is that they would speak one after another in turns without one interrupting the speech of another, or interfering, or causing a disturbance. His saying: "to the point that his companions would try to draw them away (حتى إن كان أصحابه يستجلبونهم)" means they wanted to pull them away from him and relieve him of them.

His saying: "He did not accept praise except from one who was reciprocating (ولا يقبل الثناء إلا من مكافئ)" means in exchange for a favor he had bestowed upon one of them, which is the praiseworthy gratitude (الشكر). It is from kafa'ahu meaning he rewarded him. Or it is from al-mukafa'ah meaning equality, i.e., from one who praises with the deserved praise for the favor bestowed without flattery or exaggeration. His saying: "He never interrupted anyone's speech until they exceeded (حتى يجوز)" means they overstepped the truth, at which point he would interrupt with a prohibition or by standing up. "Provocation (الاستفزاز)" means belittling or unsettling.

2 — In al-Ihya': "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was the most eloquent of people in speech and the sweetest of them—until it says—and he spoke with concise speech (جوامع الكلم), neither superfluous nor deficient—as if one part followed the other. Between his words were pauses that the listener could memorize and comprehend. He was loud-voiced and had the most beautiful tone among people."

3 — In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Ishaq ibn Ja'far, from his brother Musa, from his forefathers, from Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "I heard the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) say: 'I was sent with the noble traits of character (مكارم الأخلاق) and their beauties.'"

4 — In Makarim al-Akhlaq, from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was more modest than a virgin in her private quarters. If he disliked something, we would recognize it in his face."

5 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "I heard Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) mention that an angel came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'Indeed, Allah gives you the choice—to be a humble servant-messenger or a king-messenger.'"

He said: "He looked toward Gabriel, who gestured with his hand to be humble. So he said: 'A humble servant-messenger.' The messenger said: 'Even though it would not decrease anything of what is with your Lord.' He said: 'And with him were the keys to the treasures of the earth.'"

6 — In Nahj al-Balaghah, he (peace be upon him) said: "Follow your Prophet, the purest and most wholesome—until he said—He took from the world only a morsel and did not give it even a glance. He was the leanest of the people of the world in his waist and the hungriest of them in his belly. The world was offered to him, but he refused to accept it. He knew that Allah, the Glorified, hated something, so he hated it; and He belittled something, so he belittled it. If there were nothing in us but our love for what Allah hates—and our aggrandizement of what Allah has belittled—it would suffice as a defiance of Allah and a deviation from His command. The Messenger of Allah used to eat on the ground, sit like a servant, mend his sandals with his own hand, ride an unsaddled donkey with someone behind him, and if there was a curtain at the door of his house with images on it, he would say to one of his wives: 'O so-and-so, hide it from me, for when I look at it, I remember the world and its ornaments.' Thus, he turned away from the world with his heart, killed its mention within himself, and loved that its adornment be hidden from his eyes—so that he would not take from it any finery, nor consider it a place of rest, nor hope for a stay within it. So he expelled it from his soul, removed it from his heart, and hid it from his sight. Likewise, whoever hates something hates to look at it or have it mentioned in his presence."

7 — In al-Ihtijaj, from Musa ibn Ja'far, from his father, from his forefathers, from al-Hasan ibn Ali, from his father Ali (peace be upon him) in a long report: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to weep until his place of prayer became wet out of fear of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, without having committed any sin." The narration continues.

8 — In al-Manaqib: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to weep until he would faint. It was said to him: 'Has Allah not forgiven you your past and future sins?' He replied: 'Should I not then be a grateful servant (عبدا شكورا)?' Likewise were the faintings of Ali ibn Abi Talib, his successor, in his stations of worship."

I say: The questioner's premise was based on the assumption that the purpose of worship (العبادة) is safety from punishment—which has been reported as being "the worship of slaves." The Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) answer was based on the motive being gratitude (الشكر) to Allah, the Glorified, which is "the worship of the noble." This is another category of worship. It has been reported in the traditions from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them): "Indeed, some worship is out of fear of punishment, and that is the worship of slaves; some is out of desire for reward, and that is the worship of merchants; and some is out of gratitude to Allah, the Glorified." In some narrations, it is "out of love for Allah," and in others, "because He is worthy of it."

We have investigated the meaning of these narrations in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: ...وَسَيَجْزِي اللَّهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ — ...and Allah will reward the grateful (Surah Al-Imran 3:144) in the fourth volume of this book. We explained there that gratitude to Allah in His worship is sincerity (الإخلاص) toward Him, and that the "grateful ones" are the purified (المخلصون) servants of Allah intended by such verses as His saying, the Exalted: سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ * إِلَّا عِبادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — Clear is Allah of what they allege, except for the chosen servants of Allah (Surah As-Saaffat 37:160).

9 — In Irshad al-Daylami: "From Abraham (peace be upon him), a humming sound could be heard during his prayer—like the humming of one terrified out of fear of Allah, the Exalted. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was likewise."

10 — In the Tafsir of Abu al-Futuh, from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "When His saying, the Exalted, was revealed: ...اذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ ذِكْراً كَثِيراً — ...remember Allah with much remembrance (Surah Al-Ahzab 33:41), the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) occupied himself with the remembrance of Allah until the disbelievers said: 'He has gone mad.'"

11 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Zayd al-Shahham, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to repent to Allah seventy times every day." I asked: "Did he say: 'I seek forgiveness from Allah and repent to Him'?" He replied: "No, but he would say: 'I repent to Allah (أتوب إلى الله).'" I said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would repent and not return [to the act], while we repent and return." He said: "Allah is the one whose help is sought."

12 — In Makarim al-Akhlaq, citing the Kitab al-Nubuwwah from Ali (peace be upon him): "When he described the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he would say: 'He was the most generous of people in giving, the boldest of people in heart, the most truthful of people in speech, the most faithful of them in fulfilling obligations, the gentlest of them in nature (عريكة), and the most honorable of them in companionship. Whoever saw him unexpectedly was awestruck by him, and whoever associated with him through knowing him loved him. I have never seen anyone like him before him or after him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).'"

13 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Umar ibn Ali, from his father (peace be upon him), who said: "Among the oaths of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was: 'No, and I seek forgiveness from Allah.'"

14 — In Ihya' al-Ulum: "When his grief intensified, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would frequently touch his noble beard."

15 — Also in it: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was the most liberal of people; neither a dinar nor a dirham would stay with him. If something remained and he found no one to give it to before night fell, he would not enter his home until he had divested himself of it to whoever needed it. He took nothing from what Allah gave him except his yearly sustenance only—from the simplest dates and barley he could find—and placed the rest in the way of Allah.

He was never asked for anything but that he gave it. Then he would return to his yearly sustenance and prefer others from it, such that he might become needy before the year ended if nothing else came to him. He would execute the truth even if it resulted in harm to himself or his companions. He would walk alone among his enemies without a guard. Nothing of the worldly affairs would daunt him.

He would sit with the poor, eat with the destitute, honor the people of merit for their character, and win over the people of nobility by being kind to them. He maintained ties with his relatives without preferring them over those who were more virtuous than them. He was not harsh toward anyone and accepted the excuse of those who apologized to him.

He had male and female slaves, yet he did not elevate himself over them in food or clothing. No time passed for him without an action for the sake of Allah, the Exalted, or for what was necessary for the rectification of himself. He would go out to the gardens of his companions. He did not despise a destitute person for his poverty or chronic illness, nor did he fear a king for his kingdom. He called both to Allah with an equal invitation."

16 — Also in it: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was the furthest of people from anger and the quickest of them to be pleased. He was the most compassionate of people toward people, the best of people for people, and the most beneficial of people to people."

17 — Also in it: "When he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was happy and pleased, he was the most beautiful of people in his pleasure. If he exhorted, he did so with seriousness. If he became angry—and he only became angry for the sake of Allah—nothing could stand against his anger. Such was he in all his affairs. When a matter befell him, he would entrust the matter to Allah, disavow any power or strength [of his own], and seek the descent of guidance."

I say: Reliance upon Allah (التوكل), entrusting affairs to Him (تفويض الأمور), disavowing power and strength, and seeking the descent of guidance from Allah all return to one another and arise from a single root: that all matters depend upon the divine will (الإرادة الإلهية) which is dominant and never defeated, and the subduing power (القدرة القاهرة) which is infinite. The Book and the Sunnah are unanimous in encouraging this, such as His saying, the Exalted: ...وَعَلَى اللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ — ...and in Allah let all the trusting put their trust (Surah Ibrahim 14:12), and His saying: ...وَأُفَوِّضُ أَمْرِي إِلَى اللَّهِ... — ...I entrust my affair to Allah... (Surah Ghafir 40:44), and His saying: ...وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ... — ...And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him... (Surah At-Talaq 65:3), and His saying: ...أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ... — ...Look! To Him belong the creation and the command... (Surah Al-A'raf 7:54), and His saying: وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى — and that the terminus is toward your Lord (Surah An-Najm 53:42), among other verses. The narrations regarding these meanings are beyond counting.

Adopting these traits and disciplining oneself with these manners—besides the fact that it aligns a person with the realities and makes his actions correspond to what they should in reality, establishing him upon the primordial nature (الدين الفطرة)—for the reality of the matter is that all affairs return in truth to Allah, the Glorified, as He said: ...أَلا إِلَى اللَّهِ تَصِيرُ الْأُمُورُ — ...Look! To Allah do all matters come (Surah Ash-Shura 42:53). This has a valuable benefit: a person's leaning and reliance upon his Lord—knowing Him to possess infinite power and a dominant will that is never defeated—strengthens his own will and fortifies the pillars of his determination. Thus, he is not broken by any obstacle that appears to him, nor does he falter before any fatigue or hardship he encounters, nor is his resolve removed by any psychological lure or satanic whispering that appears to his inner self in the form of illusory dangers.

(From His Sunnah and Manners in Social Interaction)

18 — In Irshad al-Daylami: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to patch his own clothes, mend his sandals, milk his sheep, eat with the servant, sit on the ground, ride a donkey with someone behind him, and modesty did not prevent him from carrying his own needs from the market to his family. He would shake hands with the rich and the poor alike, and he would not withdraw his hand from anyone's hand until they withdrew theirs. He greeted everyone he met, whether rich or poor, old or young. He did not belittle anything he was invited to, even if it were just dried dates.

He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was light in burden, noble in nature, beautiful in social interaction, cheerful in face, smiling without laughing [loudly], sorrowful without frowning, humble without humiliation, generous without extravagance—tender-hearted, and merciful to every Muslim. He never belched from being full, and he never extended his hand toward greed."

19 — In Makarim al-Akhlaq, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "He used to look in the mirror, arrange his hair (جمته), and comb it. Sometimes he would look into water and arrange his hair in it. He would beautify himself for his companions just as he beautified himself for his family. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, Allah loves that His servant, when he goes out to his brothers, should prepare himself for them and beautify himself.'"

20 — In al-Ilal, al-Uyun, and al-Majalis, with its chain to al-Rida, from his forefathers (peace be upon them), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Five things I shall not abandon until death: eating on the ground with servants, riding an unsaddled mount, milking a goat with my own hand, wearing wool, and greeting children so that it may become a Sunnah after me.'"

21 — In al-Faqih, from Ali (peace be upon him), who said to a man from the Banu Sa'd: "Shall I not tell you about myself and Fatimah—until he said—The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) came to us in the morning while we were under our blanket. He said: 'Peace be upon you (السلام عليكم).' We remained silent and felt shy because of our position. Then he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Peace be upon you.' We remained silent. Then he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Peace be upon you.' We feared that if we did not respond, he would depart, for he used to do that—he would greet three times; if he was given permission [he entered], otherwise he departed. So we said: 'And upon you be peace, O Messenger of Allah, enter.' So he entered." The report continues.

22 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Rib'i ibn Abdillah, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to greet women and they would return the greeting. Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) used to greet women, but he disliked greeting the young among them, saying: 'I fear that her voice might please me, and thus more [burden] would enter upon me than the reward I seek.'"

I say: Al-Saduq narrated it as a mursal report, as did the grandson of al-Tabarsi in al-Mishkat, citing the book al-Mahasin.

23 — Also in it, with its chain to Abd al-Azim ibn Abdillah al-Hasani in a marfu' report: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to sit in three ways: al-qurfasa', which is to keep the shins upright and embrace them with the hands, clasping the hands over the forearms; he would also kneel on his knees; and he would fold one leg and spread the other over it. He was never seen sitting cross-legged."

24 — In al-Makarim, citing the Kitab al-Nubuwwah from Ali (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) never shook hands with anyone and withdrew his hand from theirs until they were the one to withdraw their hand. No one ever spoke to him regarding a need or a conversation and he departed until the person was the one to depart. No one ever disputed with him in conversation but that he remained silent until they were the one to fall silent. He was never seen extending his leg in front of someone sitting with him.

Between two matters, he would only choose the more difficult of them. He never sought revenge for himself for a wrong done to him until the sanctities of Allah were violated—at which point his anger would be for the sake of Allah, the Blessed and Exalted. He never ate while reclining until he left this world. He was never asked for anything and said 'no'. He never turned away a seeker of a need except that he provided it or gave a kind word. He was the lightest of people in prayer while being complete, and the shortest of people in sermon and the least of them in idle talk. He was recognized by his pleasant scent when he approached. When he ate with a group, he was the first to start and the last to withdraw his hand. When he ate, he ate from what was in front of him, but when it was fresh dates or dry dates, his hand would move around. When he drank, he drank in three breaths. He would sip the water and not gulp it down. His right hand was for his food, drink, taking, and giving—he would only take with his right and give with his right. His left hand was for other parts of his body. He loved starting from the right (التيمن) in all his affairs—in his dressing, putting on sandals, and combing hair.

When he supplicated, he did so three times. When he spoke, he spoke in odd numbers [of repetitions]. When he sought permission, he did so three times. His speech was distinct (فصلا), understood by everyone who heard it. When he spoke, it was as if light was coming out from between his front teeth. If you saw him, you would say he had a gap between his teeth, yet he did not.

His gaze was a quick glance (اللحظ). He never spoke to anyone with something they disliked. When he walked, it was as if he were descending a slope. He used to say: 'The best of you are those with the best character.' He neither disparaged a food (ذواقا) nor praised it. Those conversing did not dispute in his presence. The one reporting about him would say: 'I have never seen with my eyes anyone like him before him or after him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).'"

25 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Jamil ibn Darraj, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to divide his glances among his companions—looking at this one and looking at that one equally." He said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) never stretched his legs among his companions. If a man shook hands with him, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would not leave his hand from the man's hand until the man was the one to leave it. When they realized this, a man, when shaking hands with him, would pull his hand away."

26 — In al-Makarim: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) spoke a narration, he would smile in his speech."

27 — Also in it, from Yunus al-Shaybani, who said: "Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said to me: 'How is your joking with one another?' I said: 'Little.' He said: 'Why do you not do so? For joking is part of good character, and through it, you bring joy to your brother. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to joke with a man wanting to make him happy.'"

28 — Also in it, from Abu al-Qasim al-Kufi in the Kitab al-Akhlaq, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "There is no believer but that there is some humor (دعابة) in him. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to joke, but he only spoke the truth."

29 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Mu'ammar ibn Khallad, who said: "I asked Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him), saying: 'May I be your ransom—a man is with a group, and speech passes between them where they joke and laugh?' He replied: 'There is no harm as long as it is not...'—I thought he meant obscenity.

Then he said: 'A Bedouin used to come to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) bringing him a gift, and then he would say on the spot: "Give us the price of our gift!" and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would laugh. When he was grieved, he would say: "What has the Bedouin done? I wish he would come to us."'"

30 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Talhah ibn Zayd, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to sit facing the Qiblah most of the time."

31 — In al-Makarim: "A small child would be brought to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to pray for blessings for him. He would place the child in his lap out of honor for the family. Sometimes the child would urinate on him, and some of those who saw it would shout, but he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would say: 'Do not interrupt (لا تزرموا) the child until he finishes his urination.' Then he would finish his prayer or naming for him, and the joy of the family would be complete. They would not see that he was bothered by the urination of their child. When they departed, he would wash his garment afterward."

32 — Also in it, it is reported: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would not let anyone walk with him if he was riding until he carried them with him. If the person refused, he would say: 'Go ahead of me and meet me at the place you intend.'"

33 — Also in it, from Abu al-Qasim al-Kufi in the Kitab al-Akhlaq: "It has come in the reports that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) never took revenge for himself against anyone; rather, he would forgive and overlook."

34 — Also in it: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) missed one of his brothers for three days, he would ask about him. If he was absent, he would pray for him; if he was present, he would visit him; and if he was ill, he would attend to him."

35 — Also in it, from Anas: "I served the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for nine years, and I do not know that he ever said to me: 'Why did you not do such and such?' nor did he ever find fault with me for anything."

36 — In al-Ihya', Anas said: "By the One who sent him with the truth, he never said to me regarding anything he disliked: 'Why did you do it?' and if his wives blamed me, he would say: 'Leave him, for this was by decree and destiny (بكتاب وقدر).'"

37 — Also in it, from Anas: "No one among his companions or others would call him but that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would say: 'At your service (لبيك).'"

38 — Also in it, from him: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to call his companions by their agnomens (بكناهم) to honor them and win their hearts. He would give an agnomen to those who did not have one, and they would be called by what he named them. He also gave agnomens to women who had children and those who did not, and he gave agnomens to children, thereby softening their hearts."

39 — Also in it: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would prefer the one who entered upon him by giving them the cushion that was under him. If the person refused to accept it, he would insist until they did so."

40 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Ajlan, who said: "I was with Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) when a beggar came. He (peace be upon him) went to a measure containing dates, filled his hand, and gave it to him. Then another came and asked, so he stood, took a handful, and gave it to him. Then another came and asked, so he stood, took a handful, and gave it to him. Then another came, and he (peace be upon him) said: 'Allah is the provider for us and for you.'

Then he said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was such that no one asked him for anything of the world but that he gave it. A woman sent her son to him and said: "Go to him and ask him; if he says: 'We have nothing,' then say: 'Give me your shirt.'" He said: 'So he took his shirt and threw it to him (and in another version: gave it to him).' So Allah disciplined him toward moderation, saying: وَلا تَجْعَلْ يَدَكَ مَغْلُولَةً إِلى عُنُقِكَ وَلا تَبْسُطْها كُلَّ الْبَسْطِ فَتَقْعُدَ مَلُوماً مَحْسُوراً — Do not keep your hand fastened to your neck, nor open it altogether, or you will sit back blamed and consumed (Surah Al-Isra 17:29).'"

41 — Also in it, with its chain to Jabir, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would eat from a gift (الهدية) but would not eat from charity (الصدقة)."

42 — Also in it, from Musa ibn Imran ibn Bazi', who said: "I said to al-Rida (peace be upon him): 'May I be your ransom, the people have reported that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), when he took one path, would return by another! Was it so?' He said: 'Yes, and I do it often, so do it.' Then he said to me: 'Know that it is more conducive to your sustenance (أرزق لك).'"

43 — In al-Iqbal, with its chain to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to go out after sunrise."

44 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abdullah ibn al-Mughirah, from one who mentioned it: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered a house, he would sit in the nearest available seat upon entering."

I say: It was also narrated by the grandson of al-Tabarsi in al-Mishkat, citing al-Mahasin, and others.

Among his Sunnah and manners (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding cleanliness and adornment is what is in al-Makarim: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) washed his head and beard, he would wash them with lote-tree leaves (السدر)."

46 — In al-Ja'fariyyat, with its chain to Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his forefathers, from Ali (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to comb his hair, and most of the time he combed it with water, saying: 'Water is sufficient as a perfume for the believer.'"

47 — In al-Faqih: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, the Magians cut their beards and let their mustaches grow, but we cut the mustaches and let the beards grow.'"

48 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Trimming the nails is from the Sunnah."

49 — In al-Faqih: "It is reported: It is from the Sunnah to bury hair, nails, and blood."

50 — Also in it, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Muslim: "He asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about dyeing [the hair]. He replied: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to dye his hair, and this hair of his is with us.'"

51 — In al-Makarim: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to apply depilatory cream (يطلي); someone would apply it for him until he reached what was under the waist-wrap, at which point he would take over himself."

52 — In al-Faqih: "Ali (peace be upon him) said: 'Plucking the armpit hair removes the offensive odor—it is a purification and a Sunnah from what the Fragrant One (peace be upon him) commanded.'"

53 — In al-Makarim: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had a kohl container with which he would apply kohl every night, and his kohl was antimony (الإثمد)."

54 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu Usamah, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Brushing the teeth (السواك) is among the Sunnahs of the Messengers."

55 — In al-Faqih, with its chain to Ali (peace be upon him) in the "Four Hundred" narration: "Brushing the teeth is a cause for Allah's pleasure, a Sunnah of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and a purification for the mouth."

I say: The reports regarding his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) practice of brushing the teeth are very numerous from the paths of both sects.

56 — In al-Faqih, al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "Four things are from the character of the Prophets: using perfume, cleaning with a razor, removing body hair with depilatory cream, and frequent intimacy (كثرة الطروقة)."

57 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Sinan, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had a perfume container (ممسكة); when he performed ablution, he would take it with his hand while it was wet. When he went out, people would know he was the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) [by the scent]."

58 — In al-Makarim: "No perfume was offered to him but that he applied it, saying: 'It is a perfume whose scent is pleasant and whose burden is light.' If he did not apply it, he would place his finger in that perfume and then lick it."

59 — Also in it: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to fumigate with Qumari aloeswood."

60 — In Dhakhirat al-Ma'ad: "Musk was the most beloved perfume to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)."

61 — In al-Kafi, with its chain to Ishaq al-Tawil al-Attar, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to spend on perfume more than he spent on food."

62 — Also in it, with its chain to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: 'Perfuming the mustache is from the character of the Prophets and an honor for the recording angels (الكاتبين).'"

63 — Also in it, with its chain to al-Sakan al-Khazzaz: "I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: 'It is a right upon every adult male to trim his mustache and nails every Friday and to touch some perfume. If it was Friday and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had no perfume, he would call for some of his wives' saffron-scented water, moisten it in water, and then place it on his face.'"

64 — In al-Faqih, with its chain to Ishaq ibn Ammar, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was brought perfume on the day of Eid al-Fitr, he would start with his wives."

65 — In al-Makarim: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to oil himself with various types of oil. He used to oil himself with violet oil, saying: 'It is the best of oils.'"

66 — Among his manners (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in travel is what is in al-Faqih, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Sinan, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to travel on Thursdays."

I say: There are many hadiths with this meaning.

67 — In Aman al-Akhtar and Misbah al-Za'ir: "The author of the book Awarif al-Ma'arif mentioned that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), when he traveled, carried five things with him: a mirror, a kohl container, a hair-parting tool (المذرى), and a toothbrush. In another narration: and scissors."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Makarim and al-Ja'fariyyat.

68 — In al-Makarim, from Ibn Abbas: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) walked, he walked with a pace that showed he was neither incapable nor lazy."

69 — In al-Faqih, with its chain to Mu'awiyah ibn Ammar, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in his travel, when he descended, would say tahlil (لا إله إلا الله), and when he ascended, he would say takbir (الله أكبر)."

70 — In Lubb al-Albab by al-Qutb: "From the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): He never departed from a lodging place without praying two units of prayer in it, saying: 'So that it may bear witness for me to my prayer.'"

71 — In al-Faqih: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) bid farewell to the believers, he would say: 'May Allah provide you with godwariness (التقوى), direct you toward every good, fulfill every need for you, safeguard your religion and your world for you, and return you safely and enriched (غانمين).'"

I say: The narrations regarding his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) supplication at farewell vary, but despite their differences, they agree on praying for safety and gain.

72 — In al-Ja'fariyyat, with its chain to Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his forefathers, from Ali (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to say to one arriving from Mecca: 'May Allah accept your ritual (نسكك), forgive your sin, and compensate your expenditure.'"

73 — Among his manners (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding clothing and related matters is what is in al-Ihya': "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to wear whatever clothes were available, whether a waist-wrap (إزار), a cloak (رداء), a shirt (قميص), a tunic (جبة), or otherwise. He liked green garments. Most of his clothes were white, and he would say: 'Dress your living in them and shroud your dead in them.'

He would wear a padded tunic (القباء المحشو) for war and other than war. He had a tunic of fine silk (سندس) which he would wear, and its greenness looked beautiful against his white complexion. All his clothes were tucked up above the ankles, and the waist-wrap would be above that to the middle of the shin. His shirt was fastened at the waist-wrap—sometimes he would unfasten it during prayer and at other times.

He had a blanket dyed with saffron, and sometimes he would lead the people in prayer wearing only that. Sometimes he would wear a single cloak with nothing else on him. He had a felted cloak (كساء ملبد) which he would wear, saying: 'I am only a servant; I dress as a servant dresses.' He had two garments specifically for his Friday prayer besides his clothes for other days. Sometimes he would wear a single waist-wrap with nothing else on him, tying its ends between his shoulders; sometimes he would lead the people in funeral prayers in it. Sometimes he would pray in his house in a single waist-wrap, wrapping himself in it and crossing its ends—and that would be the same waist-wrap in which he had been intimate that day. Sometimes he would pray at night in a waist-wrap, cloaking himself with part of the garment near its fringe and throwing the rest over one of his wives, praying in that manner."

He had a black cloak (كساء) which he gave away as a gift. Umm Salamah said to him: "May my father and mother be your ransom! What happened to that black cloak?" He replied: "I clothed someone with it." She said: "I have never seen anything more beautiful than your whiteness against its blackness." Anas said: "I would sometimes see him leading us in the Dhuhr prayer wearing a wrap (شملة) with its ends tied together." He used to wear a ring (يتختم), and sometimes he would go out with a string tied to his ring to remind him of something. He would use it to seal letters, saying: "The seal on a letter is better than suspicion (التهمة)."

He used to wear caps (القلانس) under turbans and without turbans. Sometimes he would take off his cap from his head and place it as a barrier (سترة) in front of him and pray toward it. Sometimes, if there was no turban, he would tie a headband (العصابة) over his head and forehead. He had a turban called al-Sahab (السحاب) — the Cloud — which he gifted to ‘Ali; thus, when ‘Ali would appear wearing it, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would say: "‘Ali has come to you in the Cloud."

Whenever he put on a garment, he would start from his right side, saying: "Praise be to Allah who has clothed me with that which covers my nakedness (عورتي) and by which I adorn myself among the people." When he took off his garment, he would start from his left side. Whenever he wore something new, he would give his old clothes (خلق ثيابه) to a needy person, then say: "There is no Muslim who clothes a fellow Muslim with his worn-out clothes, doing so only for the sake of Allah, but that he remains under the protection (ضمان), guardianship (حرز), and goodness of Allah as long as it covers him, whether alive or dead."

He had a bed made of leather (أدم) stuffed with palm fiber (ليف); its length was two cubits or thereabouts, and its width was a cubit and a span or thereabouts. He had a cloak (عباءة) that would be spread for him wherever he moved, folded into two layers beneath him. He would also sleep on a reed mat (الحصير) with nothing else beneath him.

It was part of his character to name his animals, weapons, and belongings. The name of his banner was al-‘Uqab (العقاب); his sword with which he witnessed wars was Dhu al-Faqar (ذو الفقار); he had another sword called al-Mikhdham (المخذم), another called al-Rasub (الرسوب), and another called al-Qadhib (القضيب). The hilt of his sword was adorned with silver. He used to wear a belt (المنطقة) made of leather with three silver rings. The name of his bow was al-Katum (الكتوم), and his quiver was al-Kafur (الكافور). The name of his she-camel was al-‘Adhba’ (العضباء), his mule was al-Duldul (الدلدل), his donkey was Ya‘fur (يعفور), and the name of his ewe whose milk he drank was ‘Aynah (عينة).

He had a ceramic vessel (مطهرة) for purification from which he would perform ablution and drink. People would send their young children who had reached the age of discernment (عقلوا), and they would enter upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and would not be turned away. If they found water in the vessel, they would drink from it and wipe it over their faces and bodies, seeking blessing (البركة) thereby.

74 — In al-Ja‘fariyyat, narrated from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad, from his forefathers, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to wear quilted caps (القلانس المضربة)..." until he said: "And he had a coat of mail (درع) called Dhat al-Fudul (ذات الفضول), which had three silver rings, one in front and two behind." (The narration continues).

75 — In al-‘Awali, it is reported: "He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had a black turban which he would wrap and pray in."

I say: It is also reported that his turban (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) consisted of three or five wraps (أكوار).

76 — In al-Khisal, with its chain of narration from ‘Ali in the "Four Hundred Hadith" discourse, he said: "Wear cotton garments, for they are the clothing of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and he did not wear hair or wool except due to an ailment."

I say: Al-Saduq also narrated this as a mursal report, and al-Safwani narrated it in the book al-Ta‘rif. This clarifies the meaning of what was previously mentioned regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wearing of wool, showing there is no contradiction.

77 — In al-Faqih, with its chain of narration from Isma‘il ibn Muslim, from al-Sadiq, from his father (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had a short staff (عنزة) with a metal ferrule (عكاز) at its base; he would lean on it and take it out on the two ‘Eids to pray toward it."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Ja‘fariyyat.

78 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Hisham ibn Salim, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "The ring of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was made of silver (ورق)."

79 — In the same source, with its chain of narration from Abu Khadijah, who said: "He said: 'The gemstone (الفص) is round,' and he said: 'This is how the ring of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was.'"

80 — In al-Khisal, with its chain of narration from ‘Abd al-Rahim ibn Abi al-Bilad, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had two rings: on one was inscribed 'There is no god but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah' (لا إله إلا الله محمد رسول الله), and on the other, 'Allah has spoken the truth' (صدق الله)."

81 — In the same source, with its chain of narration from al-Husayn ibn Khalid, from Abu al-Hasan the Second (عليه السلام) in a tradition: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), the Commander of the Faithful, al-Hasan, al-Husayn, and the Imams (عليهم السلام) used to wear their rings on the right hand."

82 — In al-Makarim, from al-Sadiq, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "The Prophets wore the shirt (القميص) before the trousers (السراويل)."

I say: This was also narrated in al-Ja‘fariyyat, and there are many other reports with similar meanings.

83 — Regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) etiquette in his dwelling and related matters, it is mentioned in the book al-Tahsin by Ibn Fahd: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) passed away without ever having placed one brick upon another."

84 — In Lubb al-Albab, he (عليه السلام) said: "The mosques are the sitting-places of the Prophets."

85 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from al-Sakuni, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would leave his house in the summer, he would leave on a Thursday, and when he wished to enter from the cold in the winter, he would enter on a Friday."

I say: Al-Saduq also narrated this in al-Khisal as a mursal report.

86 — From the book al-‘Adad al-Qawiyyah by Shaykh ‘Ali ibn al-Hasan ibn al-Mutahhar, the brother of al-‘Allamah (may Allah have mercy on them both), from Khadijah (رضي الله عنها), who said: "When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) entered the house, he would call for a vessel and purify himself for prayer, then he would stand and pray two brief units (ركعتين), then he would retire to his bed."

87 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from ‘Abbad ibn Suhayb, who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) say: 'The house of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was never raided by an enemy.'"

88 — In al-Makarim: "The bed of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was a cloak (عباءة), and his pillow was of leather stuffed with palm fiber. One night it was folded [to make it softer]; when morning came, he said: 'The bed prevented me from my prayer tonight,' so he ordered it to be made into a single layer. He had a bed of leather stuffed with palm fiber, and a cloak that was spread for him wherever he moved, folded in two."

89 — In the same source, from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) never woke up from sleep without immediately falling into prostration (ساجدا) for Allah."

90 — Regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) etiquette in marriage and children: In the treatise al-Muhkam wa al-Mutashabih by al-Murtada, with its chain to the Tafsir of al-Nu‘mani, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "A group of the Companions had forbidden themselves from women, from breaking their fast during the day, and from sleeping at night. Umm Salamah informed the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), so he went out to his Companions and said: 'Do you turn away from women? For I go unto women, I eat during the day, and I sleep at night. Whoever turns away from my Sunnah (سنتي) — my established way of life — is not of me.'" (The narration continues).

I say: This meaning is narrated in the books of both sects (Sunni and Shi'a) through numerous paths.

91 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Ishaq ibn ‘Ammar, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "Among the character traits of the Prophets is the love of women."

92 — In the same source, with its chain of narration from Bakkar ibn Kardam and more than one person, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The delight of my eye has been placed in prayer, and my pleasure in women.'"

I say: Similar reports have been narrated through other paths.

93 — In al-Faqih: "Whenever the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) intended to marry a woman, he would send someone to look at her." (The narration continues).

94 — In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from al-Husayn ibn Bint Ilyas, who said: "I heard Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه السلام) say: 'Allah has made the night a repose (سكنا), and He has made women a repose. It is part of the Sunnah to marry at night and to provide food.'"

95 — In al-Khisal, with its chain of narration from ‘Ali (عليه السلام) in the "Four Hundred Hadith" discourse, he said: "Perform the ‘aqiqah (عقوا) — the sacrificial offering for a newborn — for your children on the seventh day, and give the weight of their hair in silver as charity to a Muslim. This is what the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did for al-Hasan, al-Husayn, and the rest of his children."

96 — Regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) etiquette in food, drink, and matters related to the table: In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Hisham ibn Salim and others, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "Nothing was more beloved to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) than to remain hungry and fearful for the sake of Allah."

97 — In al-Ihtijaj, with its chain of narration from Musa ibn Ja‘far, from his forefathers, from al-Husayn ibn ‘Ali (عليه السلام) in a long tradition regarding the questions of a Syrian Jew to the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام)... until he said: "The Jew said to him: 'They claim that Jesus was an ascetic (زاهدا)!’ ‘Ali (عليه السلام) replied: 'He was indeed so, but Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was the most ascetic of the Prophets. He had thirteen wives besides the maidservants who attended to him, yet a table (مائدة) with food was never raised for him [after he finished], he never ate wheat bread (خبز بر) at all, and he never sated himself with barley bread (خبز شعير) for three consecutive nights.'"

98 — In the Amali of al-Saduq, from al-‘Ays ibn al-Qasim, who said: "I said to al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): 'A tradition is narrated from your father that he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) never sated himself with wheat bread at all." Is this correct?' He replied: 'No, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) never ate wheat bread at all, nor did he ever sate himself with barley bread.'"

99 — In al-Da‘awat by al-Qutb, it is reported: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) never ate while reclining (متكئا) except once; then he sat up and said: 'O Allah, I am Your servant and Your messenger.'"

I say: This meaning was narrated by al-Kulayni and al-Shaykh [al-Tusi] through many paths, as well as by al-Saduq, al-Barqi, and al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id in the book al-Zuhd.

100 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Zayd al-Shahham, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) never ate while reclining from the time Allah commissioned him until He took his soul. He used to eat like a servant and sit like a servant." I asked: "Why?" He replied: "Out of humility (تواضعا) toward Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

101 — In the same source, with its chain of narration from Abu Khadijah, who said: "Bashir al-Dahhan asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) while I was present: 'Did the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) eat while reclining on his right or his left?' He replied: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did not eat reclining on his right or his left, but he would sit like a servant.' I asked: 'Why was that?' He said: 'Out of humility toward Allah, the Mighty and Majestic.'"

102 — In the same source, with its chain of narration from Jabir, from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to eat like a servant and sit like a servant. He would eat on the ground (الحضيض) and sleep on the ground."

103 — In al-Ihya’: "When he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sat to eat, he would bring his knees and feet together, just as a person praying sits, except that one knee was above the other and one foot was above the other. He would say: 'I am but a servant; I eat as a servant eats, and I sit as a servant sits.'"

104 — In the book al-Ta‘rif by al-Safwani, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام): "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sat at the table, he sat as a servant sits, and he would lean on his left thigh."

105 — In al-Makarim, from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to sit on the ground, hobble the sheep (يعتقل الشاة), and accept the invitation of a slave."

106 — In al-Mahasin, with its chain of narration from Hammad ibn ‘Uthman, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to lick his fingers when he ate."

107 — In al-Ihtijaj, quoting from the book Mawalid al-Sadiqin: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to eat all types of food. He would eat what Allah made lawful for him with his family and servants when they ate, and with whoever invited him from among the Muslims, eating from what they ate and on what they ate, unless a guest arrived, in which case he would eat with his guest..." until he said: "And the most beloved food to him was that which had many hands (ضفف) over it."

I say: His saying "and on what they ate" refers to things like the table (المائدة) or the large bowl (الصحفة). His saying "and what they ate" (وما أكلوا) is either a relative pronoun or a temporal particle. His saying "unless a guest arrived..." is an exception to his eating "with his family and servants." Al-Dafaf (الضفف) refers to a large number of dependents and the like, and al-daffah (الضفة) with a fathah means a group.

108 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Ibn al-Qaddah, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) ate food with a group of people, he was the first to place his hand and the last to withdraw it, so that the people could eat."

109 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "The Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) said: 'The dinner of the Prophets is after the late evening prayer (العتمة); do not abandon dinner, for abandoning dinner is the ruin of the body.'"

110 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from ‘Anbasah ibn Najad, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "No food containing dates was ever presented to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) but that he would begin with the dates."

111 — In al-Kafi and Sahifat al-Rida, with its chain of narration from his forefathers (عليهم السلام), it is said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) ate dates, he would place the pits on the back of his hand and then cast them away."

112 — In al-Iqbal, quoting from the second volume of the History of Nishapur, in the biography of al-Hasan ibn Bishr, with its chain of narration, it is said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to praise Allah between every two morsels."

113 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Wahb ibn ‘Abd Rabbih, who said: "I saw Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) using a toothpick (يتخلل), so I looked at him. He said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to use a toothpick, and it sweetens the breath.'"

114 — In al-Makarim, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "When he drank, he would begin by mentioning the Name of Allah (سمى)..." until he said: "And he would sip the water (يمص الماء مصا) and not gulp it down (يعبه عبا), saying: 'Liver ailments (الكباد) come from gulping.'"

115 — In al-Ja‘fariyyat, from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad, from his forefathers, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "I observed the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) more than once; when he drank, he would breathe three times, mentioning the Name of Allah (تسمية) with each drink and praising Him (تحميد) when he finished. I asked him about that, and he said: 'O ‘Ali, the praise is gratitude to Allah the Exalted, and the naming is a protection from disease.'"

116 — In al-Makarim: "He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would not breathe into the vessel when he drank; if he wished to breathe, he would move the vessel away from his mouth until he had breathed."

117 — In al-Ihya’: "When he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) ate meat, he would not lower his head toward it, but would raise it to his mouth, then bite it off (ينهشه انتهاشا)." He then said: "When he ate meat specifically, he would wash his hands thoroughly, then wipe the remaining water over his face."

118 — In al-Makarim, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "He used to eat various types of food."

I say: Al-Tabarsi then mentioned types of food he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to eat, such as bread, various kinds of meat, watermelon, muskmelon, sugar, grapes, pomegranate, dates, milk, harisah (a meat and wheat dish), clarified butter, vinegar, chicory, basil, and cabbage. It is reported that he loved dates, that he liked honey, and that his favorite fruit was the pomegranate.

119 — In the Amali of al-Tusi, with its chain of narration from Abu Usamah, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "The food of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was barley when he found it, his sweet was dates, and his fuel was palm branches."

120 — In al-Makarim, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "He would not eat hot food until it cooled, saying: 'Allah has not fed us fire; hot food is devoid of blessing (بركة).'"

When he ate, he would mention the Name of Allah, eat with three fingers, and eat from what was in front of him, never reaching in front of others. When food was brought, he would start before the people, and then they would start. He ate with his three fingers: the thumb, the one next to it, and the middle finger; sometimes he used the fourth. He would eat with his whole palm but never with two fingers, saying: "Eating with two fingers is the eating of Satan." One day his companions brought him faludhaj (a sweet dish), so he ate with them and asked: "What is this made of?" They said: "We mix clarified butter and honey, and it becomes as you see." He said: "This is good food."

He used to eat unsifted barley bread. He never ate wheat bread at all, nor did he ever sate himself with barley bread. He never ate at a table (خوان) — a raised dining tray — until he died. He ate watermelon and grapes. He ate fresh dates and would feed the pits to the sheep. He did not eat garlic, onions, or leeks, nor honey containing maghafir (المغافير) — a resinous substance bees collect from certain trees — which leaves an odor in the mouth.

He never criticized food at all. If he liked it, he ate it; if he disliked it, he left it without forbidding it to others. He would lick the bowl and say: "The end of the dish has the greatest blessing." When he finished, he would lick the three fingers he ate with, one by one. He would wash his hands after eating until they were clean. He never ate alone.

I say: His saying "the thumb, the one next to it, and the middle finger" is a beautiful etiquette of the narrator, as he did not say "the thumb and the index finger (السبابة)" etc., to protect the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from applying the term "the pointer/insulter" (السبابة) to his noble finger due to the negative connotation of the word.

What he narrated regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) eating faludhaj contradicts what is in al-Mahasin, narrated with a chain from Ya‘qub ibn Shu‘ayb, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "While the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) was in the courtyard (الرحبة) with a group of his companions, a tray of faludhaj was gifted to him. He said to his companions: 'Stretch out your hands.' They did so, and he stretched out his hand, then withdrew it and said: 'I remembered that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did not eat it, so I disliked eating it.'"

121 — In al-Makarim, he said: "He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to drink from glass cups brought from Syria, and he would drink from cups made of wood, leather, and clay."

I say: A similar report is found in the beginning of al-Kafi and al-Mahasin, which adds: "He liked drinking from the Syrian cup and would say: 'It is the cleanest of your vessels.'"

122 — In al-Makarim, from the Prophet: "He used to drink from his palm, pouring water into it, saying: 'There is no vessel better than the hand.'"

123 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to sacrifice two rams on the Day of Adha: one for himself and the other on behalf of those of his nation who could not afford it."

124 — Regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) etiquette in private: In the commentary on al-Nafliyyah by al-Shahid al-Thani, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "He was never seen [by anyone] while urinating or defecating."

125 — In al-Ja‘fariyyat, with its chain of narration from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad, from his forefathers, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wanted to clear his throat (يتخنع), he would cover his head and then bury it [the phlegm]; when he wanted to spit, he would do the same. When he wanted to go to the privy (الكنيف), he would cover his head."

I say: The use of a privy (الكنيف) among the Arabs was something that emerged after Islam; before that, they used to go out into the open desert, as understood from some narrations.

126 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from al-Husayn ibn Khalid, from Abu al-Hasan the Second (عليه السلام), who said: "I said to him: 'We have narrated a tradition that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to perform istinja’ (استنجي) — ritual cleaning after using the toilet — while his ring was on his finger, and the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) did the same, and the inscription on the ring of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was "Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah"?' He said: 'They spoke the truth.' I said: 'Should we then do the same?' He said: 'Those ones used to wear the ring on the right hand, whereas you wear it on the left.'" (The narration continues).

I say: A similar report was narrated in al-Ja‘fariyyat and in al-Makarim, quoting from the book al-Libas by al-‘Ayyashi, from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام).

127 — Regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) etiquette during calamities and trials, and concerning the deceased: In al-Makarim: "Whenever the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw a pimple (بثرة) on his body, he would seek refuge in Allah, humble himself before Him, and beseech Him. It would be said to him: 'O Messenger of Allah, it is nothing serious.' He would reply: 'When Allah wishes to make a small thing great, He makes it great, and when He wishes to make a great thing small, He makes it small.'"

128 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Jabir, from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "The Sunnah is to carry the bier (السرير) from its four sides; any carrying beyond that is voluntary."

129 — In Qurb al-Isnad, from al-Husayn ibn Tarif, from al-Husayn ibn ‘Alwan, from Ja‘far, from his father: "Al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (عليه السلام) was sitting with some of his companions when a funeral procession passed by. Some of the people stood up, but al-Hasan (عليه السلام) did not. When they had passed, someone said: 'Why did you not stand, may Allah grant you health, for the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to stand for a funeral when it passed by?' Al-Hasan (عليه السلام) replied: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) only stood once, and that was when the funeral of a Jew passed by and the place was narrow; the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood because he disliked that it [the bier] should be higher than his head.'"

130 — In the Da‘awat of al-Qutb, he said: "Whenever the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) followed a funeral, he would be overcome by sadness, engage much in inner reflection, and speak very little."

131 — In al-Ja‘fariyyat, with its chain of narration from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad, from his forefathers, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to throw three handfuls of earth onto the grave."

132 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Zurarah, from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to do something for those who died from the Banu Hashim specifically that he did not do for any other Muslim: when he had prayed over a Hashimite and sprinkled his grave with water, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would place his palm on the grave until his finger marks could be seen in the mud. Thus, a stranger or a traveler arriving in Medina would see a new grave with the mark of the palm of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and ask: 'Who from the family of Muhammad has died?'"

133 — In Musakkin al-Fu’ad by al-Shahid al-Thani, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام): "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) offered condolences (عزى), he would say: 'May Allah reward you and have mercy on you,' and when he offered congratulations (هنأ), he would say: 'May Allah bless you and shower His blessings upon you.'"

134 — Regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) etiquette in ablution (الوضوء) and ritual bathing (الغسل): In Ayat al-Ahkam by al-Qutb, from Sulayman ibn Buraydah, from his father: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to perform ablution for every prayer. In the year of the Conquest [of Mecca], he performed several prayers with a single ablution. ‘Umar said: 'O Messenger of Allah, you have done something you never used to do.' He replied: 'I did it intentionally.'"

135 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Zurarah, who said: "Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) said: 'Shall I not describe for you the ablution of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)?' We said: 'Yes.' He called for a bowl (قعب) with some water and placed it before him.
