Then he bared his forearms and dipped his right palm into it, saying: 'This is how it is done if the palm is clean.' Then he scooped a handful of water and placed it on his forehead, saying: 'In the Name of Allah' (بسم الله), and let it flow down to the ends of his beard. Then he passed his hand over his face and the front of his forehead once. Then he dipped his left hand, scooped a handful, and placed it on his right elbow, passing his palm over his forearm until the water reached his fingertips. Then he scooped a handful with his right hand, placed it on his left elbow, and passed his palm over his forearm until the water reached his fingertips. Then he wiped the front of his head and the top of his feet with the moisture remaining on his left hand and the rest of the moisture on his right hand."

He said: "Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) said: 'Allah is One (وتر) and loves the odd number; three handfuls may suffice you for ablution: one for the face and two for the forearms. You wipe your forelock with the moisture of your right hand, and with what remains of it, the top of your right foot; and you wipe the top of your left foot with the moisture of your left hand.'"

Zurarah said: "Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) said: 'A man asked the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) about the ablution of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and he described it to him in the same way.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated from Zurarah, Bukayr, and others through multiple paths recorded by al-Kulayni, al-Saduq, al-Shaykh [al-Tusi], al-‘Ayyashi, al-Mufid, al-Karajiki, and others. The reports from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) regarding this are extensive (مستفيضة), approaching the level of mass-transmission (التواتر).

136 — In the Amali of Mufid al-Din al-Tusi, with its chain of narration from Abu Hurayrah: "When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) performed ablution, he would start with his right side."

137 — In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) about ablution, and he said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to perform ablution with one mudd (مد) — approximately 750ml — of water and perform ghusl with one sa‘ (صاع) — approximately 3 liters.'"

I say: The same was narrated from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) through another path.

138 — In al-‘Uyun, with its chain of narration from al-Rida, from his forefathers (عليهم السلام) in a long tradition: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'We are a household for whom charity (الصدقة) is not permissible; we have been commanded to perform the purification thoroughly (إسباغ الطهور), and we do not mate a donkey with a noble mare.'"

139 — In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of narration from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "Rinsing the mouth (المضمضة) and sniffing water into the nose (الاستنشاق) are among the practices established by the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

140 — In the same source, with its chain of narration from Mu‘awiyah ibn ‘Ammar, who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) say: 'The Messenger of Allah used to perform ghusl with one sa‘, and if one of his wives was with him, he would perform ghusl with one sa‘ and one mudd.'"

I say: Al-Kulayni narrated this meaning in al-Kafi with his chain from Muhammad ibn Muslim from him, adding: "They would both perform ghusl from a single vessel." Al-Shaykh [al-Tusi] also narrated it through another path.

141 — In al-Ja‘fariyyat, with its chain of narration from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad, from his father (عليه السلام), who said: "Al-Hasan ibn Muhammad asked Jabir ibn ‘Abdillah about the ghusl of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Jabir said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to pour water over his head three times.' Al-Hasan ibn Muhammad said: 'My hair is thick, as you see.' Jabir replied: 'O free man, do not say that, for the hair of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was thicker and more fragrant.'"

142 — In al-Hidayah by al-Saduq: "Al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) said: 'The Friday bath (غسل الجمعة) is an obligatory Sunnah (سنة واجبة) for men and women, whether traveling or at home...'" until he said: "Al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) said: 'The Friday bath is a purification and an expiation (كفارة) for the sins committed between the two Fridays.' He said: 'The reason for the Friday bath is that the Ansar used to work with their camels and properties, and when Friday came, they would attend the mosque, and people would be bothered by the odor of their armpits. So Allah commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to perform ghusl, and it became an established Sunnah.'"

I say: Among his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) Sunnahs regarding ghusl are the bath for the Day of Fitr, the bath for all ‘Eids, and many other baths, some of which may be mentioned later, Allah willing.

143 — Regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) etiquette and Sunnahs in prayer (الصلاة) and related matters: In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, ‘Abd al-Malik, and Bukayr, who said: "We heard Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) say: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to perform twice the amount of the obligatory prayer in recommended prayers (التطوع), and he would fast twice the amount of the obligatory fast in recommended fasts.'"

I say: Al-Shaykh [al-Tusi] also narrated it.

144 — In the same source, with its chain of narration from Hannan, who said: "‘Amr ibn Hurayth asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) while I was sitting: 'May I be your ransom, inform me about the prayer of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).' He replied: 'The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to pray eight units at noon (الزوال), four for the first [Dhuhr], eight after it, four for ‘Asr, three for Maghrib, four after Maghrib, four for the late evening prayer (العشاء الآخرة), eight for the Night Prayer (صلاة الليل), three for the Witr, two for the dawn (الفجر), and two units for the morning prayer (صلاة الغداة).'"

I said: "May I be your ransom, if I am strong enough for more than this, will Allah punish me for praying much?" He replied: "No, but He will punish you for abandoning the Sunnah."

I say: It appears from the narration that the two units performed while sitting after the late evening prayer—known as al-‘Atamah—are not part of the fifty [units], but rather they complete the number to fifty-one when counted as one unit performed while standing. Rather, al-‘Atamah was legislated as a substitute for the Witr in case death occurred before standing for the Witr at the end of the night. Al-Kulayni (may Allah have mercy on him) narrated in al-Kafi with his chain from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "Whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day should not sleep without having performed the Witr." I asked: "Do you mean the two units after the late evening prayer?" He said: "Yes, they are equivalent to one unit. Whoever prays them and then passes away, dies having performed the Witr; if he does not die, he prays the Witr at the end of the night."

I asked: "Did the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) pray these two units?" He said: "No." I asked: "Why?" He said: "Because revelation would come to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and he knew whether he would die that night or not, whereas others do not know. For that reason, he did not pray them but commanded them." (The narration continues).

It is possible that the meaning of "he did not pray them" in the hadith is that he did not do so consistently, but rather prayed them sometimes and left them at others, as understood from some other traditions; thus, it does not contradict the reports stating that he did pray them.

145 — In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of narration from Zurarah, who said: "I heard Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) say: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would not pray anything during the day until the sun passed its zenith (تزول الشمس). When it had passed by the measure of half a finger, he would pray eight units. When the shadow reached one cubit, he prayed Dhuhr, then he prayed two units after Dhuhr and two units before the time of ‘Asr. When the shadow reached two cubits, he prayed ‘Asr. He prayed Maghrib when the sun disappeared. When the twilight (الشفق) vanished, the time for ‘Isha’ began; the end of the time for Maghrib is the disappearance of the twilight, and when it disappears, the time for ‘Isha’ begins, and the end of the time for ‘Isha’ is one-third of the night.'"

"He would not pray after ‘Isha’ until midnight. Then he would pray thirteen units, including the Witr and the two units of dawn before the morning prayer. When the dawn broke and became bright, he prayed the morning prayer."

I say: The narration did not encompass the entirety of the recommended prayers for ‘Asr, which are known from other narrations.

146 — In the same source, with its chain of narration from Mu‘awiyah ibn Wahb, who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) say—mentioning the prayer of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—: 'He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would have his purification water brought and covered near his head, and his miswak (سواكه) — tooth-cleaning twig — would be placed under his bed. Then he would sleep as long as Allah willed. When he woke, he would sit up, turn his gaze toward the sky, and recite the verses from Al ‘Imran: Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth... (3:190). Then he would clean his teeth and purify himself, then go to the mosque and pray four units; his bowing (ركوعه) was as long as his recitation, and his prostration (سجوده) was as long as his bowing. He would bow until it was said: "When will he raise his head?" and prostrate until it was said: "When will he raise his head?"'"

"Then he would return to his bed and sleep as long as Allah willed. Then he would wake, sit, recite the verses, turn his gaze toward the sky, clean his teeth, purify himself, and go to the mosque to pray four units as he had prayed before."

"Then he would return to his bed and sleep as long as Allah willed. Then he would wake, sit, recite the verses from Al ‘Imran, turn his gaze toward the sky, clean his teeth, purify himself, and go to the mosque to perform the Witr and the two units [of dawn], then go out for the [morning] prayer."

I say: This meaning was also narrated in al-Kafi through two paths.

147 — It is reported: "He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to be brief in the two recommended units of the morning, praying them at the first break of dawn, then going out for the prayer."

148 — In al-Mahasin, with its chain of narration from ‘Umar ibn Yazid, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "Whoever says in his Witr when he performs it: 'I seek forgiveness from Allah, my Lord, and I turn to Him in repentance' (أستغفر الله ربي وأتوب إليه) seventy times, and maintains this for a year, Allah records him among those who seek forgiveness at dawn (المستغفرين بالأسحار)."

"The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to seek forgiveness from Allah in the Witr seventy times, and he would say: 'This is the station of one who seeks refuge in You from the Fire' (هذا مقام العائذ بك من النار) seven times." (The narration continues).

149 — In al-Faqih: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to say in the supplication (قنوت) of the Witr: 'O Allah, guide me among those You have guided, grant me health among those You have granted health, take me as a charge among those You have taken as a charge, bless me in what You have given, and protect me from the evil of what You have decreed. Indeed, You decree and none decrees against You. Glory be to You, Lord of the House. I seek Your forgiveness and turn to You in repentance; I believe in You and rely upon You. There is no power nor strength except by You, O Most Merciful.'"

150 — In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of narration from Abu Khadijah, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "When the month of Ramadan arrived, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would increase his prayer, and I increase mine, so you should increase yours."

I say: He (عليه السلام) means by the "increase" the thousand units—the Tarawih—the recommended prayers of the month of Ramadan which the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to pray in addition to the fifty daily recommended units. Many reports have been narrated regarding their method and distribution over the nights of Ramadan. It has been narrated through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to pray them individually and forbade performing them in congregation (جماعة), saying: "There is no congregation in recommended prayer."

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had other specific prayers narrated from him in books of supplication, which we have omitted as they fall outside our purpose here. Likewise, he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had Sunnahs regarding prayers, supplications, and litanies (الأوراد); whoever wishes to study them should refer to the appropriate sources.

151 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Yazid ibn Khalifah, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام): '‘Umar ibn Hanzalah brought us a report from you regarding the time [of prayer].' He said: 'Then he does not lie against us...'" until he said: "I said: 'And he said: "The time for Maghrib is when the sun's disk disappears, except that when the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was in a hurry during travel, he would delay Maghrib and combine it with ‘Isha’."' He replied: 'He spoke the truth.'"

152 — In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of narration from Talhah ibn Zayd, from Ja‘far, from his father (عليه السلام): "On a rainy night, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would be brief in Maghrib and hasten in ‘Isha’, praying them both together, saying: 'Whoever does not show mercy will not be shown mercy.'"

153 — In the same source, with its chain of narration from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from Hammad, from al-Halabi, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was on a journey or was pressed by a need, he would combine Dhuhr and ‘Asr, and Maghrib and the late evening prayer." (The narration continues).

I say: There are many narrations with this meaning recorded by al-Kulayni, al-Shaykh [al-Tusi], his son, and al-Shahid al-Awwal (may Allah have mercy on them).

154 — In al-Faqih, with its chain of narration from Mu‘awiyah ibn Wahb, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "The caller to prayer (المؤذن) would come to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the heat for the Dhuhr prayer, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would say to him: 'Cool it down, cool it down' (أبرد أبرد)."

I say: Al-Saduq said: "He meant 'hasten, hasten,' taking it from the word barid (postal courier)." However, it is apparent that the meaning is to delay it so that the intensity of the heat subsides, as indicated by what is in the book of al-‘Ala’, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) passed by me in the Mosque of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while I was praying. He met me afterward and said: 'Beware of praying the obligatory prayer at that hour; do you perform it in the intensity of the heat?' I said: 'I was performing recommended prayers.'"

155 — In al-Ihya’, it is mentioned: "Whenever someone would sit with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while he was praying, he would shorten his prayer and turn toward the person, saying: 'Do you have a need?' Once he had fulfilled their needs, he would return to his prayer."

156 — In the book Zuhd al-Nabi by Ja‘far ibn Ahmad al-Qummi, it is stated: "When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood for prayer, his face would turn pale out of fear of Allah, and a humming sound would emanate from his chest or his inner being, like the humming of one struck by awe."

I say: This meaning has also been narrated by Ibn al-Fahd and others.

157 — In the same source, it is mentioned: "In another narration: When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood for prayer, he was as if he were a discarded garment."

158 — In al-Bihar, it is mentioned: "Aishah said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to speak to us and we to him, but when the time for prayer arrived, it was as if he did not know us and we did not know him.'"

159 — In al-Majalis by Mufid al-Din al-Tusi, with his chain of transmission to Ali (عليه السلام), in his letter to Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr when he appointed him over Egypt—until he said: "Then observe your bowing (الركوع) and your prostration (السجود); for the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was the most complete of people in prayer, yet the lightest of them in his actions therein."

160 — In al-Ja‘fariyyat, with its chain of transmission from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad, from his forefathers, from Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "Whenever the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) yawned during prayer, he would repel it with his right hand."

I say: A similar report is narrated in al-Da‘a’im.

161 — In al-Ilal, with its chain of transmission from Hisham ibn al-Hakam, from Abu al-Hasan Musa (عليه السلام), in a tradition where Hisham said: "I asked him: 'For what reason is it said during bowing: Glory be to my Lord, the Great, and praise be to Him (سبحان ربي العظيم وبحمده)? And said during prostration: Glory be to my Lord, the Most High, and praise be to Him (سبحان ربي الأعلى وبحمده)?' He replied: 'O Hisham, when the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was taken on the Night Journey (أسري به) and prayed, and recalled what he had seen of the greatness of Allah, his limbs trembled and he knelt down upon his knees and began saying: Glory be to my Lord, the Great, and praise be to Him. When he stood upright from his bowing, he looked toward Him at a station higher than that, so he fell upon his face saying: Glory be to my Lord, the Most High, and praise be to Him. When he had said it seven times, that awe subsided; thus did it become the established practice (السنة).'"

162 — In Tanbih al-Khawatir by Shaykh Warram ibn Abi Firas, from al-Nu‘man, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to straighten our rows as if he were straightening arrows (القداح), until he saw that we had become heedless of him. Then he came out one day and stood until he was about to say the takbir (تكبير) — the proclamation of Allah's greatness — when he saw a man whose chest was protruding. He said: 'O servants of Allah, you shall surely straighten your rows, or Allah will cause discord between your faces.'"

163 — In the same source, from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to touch our shoulders during prayer and say: 'Be straight and do not differ, lest your hearts differ.'"

164 — In al-Faqih, with its chain of transmission from Dawud ibn al-Husayn, from Abu al-Abbas, from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) performed spiritual retreat (الاعتكاف) — the practice of secluded worship in a mosque — in the month of Ramadan during the first ten days, then he performed it in the second ten days, and thereafter he always performed it during the last ten days."

165 — In the same source, it is mentioned: "Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) said: 'The Battle of Badr took place in the month of Ramadan, so the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did not perform spiritual retreat. In the following year, he performed retreat for twenty days: ten for that year and ten as a makeup (قضاء) for what he had missed.'"

I say: This and the preceding report were narrated by al-Kulayni in al-Kafi.

166 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from al-Halabi, from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "When the last ten days (meaning of the month of Ramadan) began, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would perform spiritual retreat in the mosque. A tent made of hair would be pitched for him, he would tighten his waist-wrap (الميزر), and fold away his bedding." Someone asked: "And did he withdraw from women?" He replied: "As for withdrawing from women, no."

I say: This meaning is narrated in many reports. The intended meaning of the denial of "withdrawal"—as scholars have mentioned and as the reports indicate—is the permissibility of socializing and living with them, excluding sexual intercourse (المجامعة).

167 — Among his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) etiquettes and practices regarding fasting is what is in al-Faqih, with its chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Marwan, who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) say: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to fast until it would be said: He does not break his fast; and he would break his fast until it would be said: He does not fast. Then he fasted one day and broke his fast the next. Then he fasted Mondays and Thursdays. Finally, he moved from that to fasting three days every month: the first Thursday of the month, the Wednesday in the middle of the month, and the last Thursday of the month. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to say: That is the fasting of a lifetime (صوم الدهر).'"

"My father (عليه السلام) used to say: 'There is no one more hated by Allah than a man to whom it is said: The Messenger of Allah used to do such and such, and he replies: Allah will not punish me for striving in prayer and fasting—as if he believes that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) left out something of merit due to inability.'"

168 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام): "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was first commissioned, he would fast until it was said: He does not break his fast; and break his fast until it was said: He does not fast. Then he left that and fasted one day and broke his fast the next, which is the fast of David. Then he left that and fasted the three 'bright days' (الثلاثة الأيام الغر). Then he left that and distributed them, one day in every ten: two Thursdays with a Wednesday between them. He passed away (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while practicing this."

I say: There are many widely transmitted (مستفيضة) reports regarding this meaning.

169 — In the same source, with its chain of transmission from Anbasah the Worshipper, who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) passed away while observing the fasts of Sha‘ban, Ramadan, and three days of every month."

170 — In al-Nawadir by Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Isa, from Ali ibn Nu‘man, from Zur‘ah, from Sama‘ah, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) about the fast of Sha‘ban: 'Did the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) fast it?' He said: 'Yes, but he did not fast all of it.' I asked: 'How much of it did he break?' He said: 'He broke some.' I repeated the question and he repeated the answer three times, adding nothing more than 'He broke some.' Then I asked him the following year about it, and he gave me the same answer."

171 — In al-Makarim, from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had a drink with which he would break his fast and a drink for the pre-dawn meal (السحر). Sometimes it was one drink, sometimes it was milk, and sometimes the drink was bread soaked in water."

172 — In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Ibn al-Qaddah, from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "The first thing the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would break his fast with in the season of fresh dates (الرطب) was fresh dates, and in the season of dried dates (التمر) was dried dates."

173 — In the same source, with its chain of transmission from al-Sakuni, from Ja‘far, from his father (عليه السلام), who said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) fasted and found no sweets, he would break his fast with water." In some narrations: "He would sometimes break his fast with raisins."

174 — In al-Muqni‘ah, it is narrated from the Family of Muhammad (عليه السلام) that they said: "The pre-dawn meal (السحور) is recommended, even if it is just a sip of water." It is narrated that the best of it is dates and parched grain flour (السويق), because the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used them.

I say: This pertains to his ongoing practices (سننه الجارية). Among his exclusive characteristics (مختصاته) was the "continuous fast" (صوم الوصال) — fasting for more than one day without breaking the fast in between. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade the community from doing so, saying: "You cannot endure that; for I have with my Lord that which feeds me and gives me drink."

175 — In al-Makarim, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "He used to eat meat and grain porridge (الهريسة) more than anything else, and he would take it for his pre-dawn meal."

176 — In al-Faqih, it is mentioned: "Whenever the month of Ramadan began, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would release every captive and give to every petitioner."

177 — In al-Da‘a’im, from Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would fold his bedding and tighten his waist-wrap during the last ten days of the month of Ramadan. He would wake his family on the twenty-third night and sprinkle water on the faces of those sleeping on that night. Fatimah (عليه السلام) would not let anyone from her family sleep on that night; she would treat them with a small amount of food and prepare for it during the day, saying: 'Deprived is the one who is deprived of its goodness.'"

178 — In al-Muqni‘: "The practice (السنة) is for a man to break his fast on the Day of Sacrifice (الأضحى) after the prayer, and on the Day of Fast-breaking (الفطر) before the prayer."

179 — Among his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) etiquettes in reciting the Quran and supplication is what is in the Majalis of the Shaykh, with its chain of transmission from Abu al-Dunya, from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), who said: "Nothing prevented the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from reciting the Quran except major ritual impurity (الجنابة)."

180 — In Majma‘ al-Bayan, from Umm Salamah: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to pause his recitation verse by verse."

181 — In the Tafsir of Abu al-Futuh: "He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would not sleep until he had recited the Musabbihat (المسبحات) — the chapters of the Quran beginning with the glorification of Allah — and he would say: 'In these Surahs is a verse that is better than a thousand verses.' They asked: 'What are the Musabbihat?' He said: 'Surah al-Hadid, al-Hashr, al-Saff, al-Jumu‘ah, and al-Taghabun.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated in Majma‘ al-Bayan from al-Irbad ibn Sariyah.

182 — In Durar al-La’ali by Ibn Abi Jumhur, from Jabir, who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would not sleep until he had recited Tabarak (Surah al-Mulk) and Alif Lam Mim al-Tanzil (Surah al-Sajdah)."

183 — In Majma‘ al-Bayan: Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) narrated: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) loved this Surah: Celebrate the Name of your Lord, the Most Exalted (Surah al-A‘la). The first to say 'Glory be to my Lord, the Most High' was Michael (ميكائيل)."

I say: The beginning of the tradition is narrated in al-Bihar from al-Durr al-Manthur. There are other reports regarding what he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would say upon reciting the Quran or specific Surahs or verses; whoever seeks them should look in the appropriate sources.

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had sermons and statements in which he encouraged and urged clinging to the Quran, contemplating it, being guided by its guidance, and seeking illumination from its light. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was the most worthy of people regarding the perfection he called for, and the foremost and quickest of people toward every good. He is the one who said—in the famous narration—: "Surah Hud has made me gray-haired." It has been narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) commanded me to recite some of the Quran to him. I recited from Surah Yunus until I reached the saying of the Exalted: وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ (Then they are returned to Allah, their real master) [10:30]—I saw that tears were welling in his noble eyes."

These are fragments (شذرات) of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) etiquettes and practices. The narrations are widely transmitted and the reports are repeated in many of them in the books of both sects, and the Divine Speech supports them and does not reject any of them. And Allah is the Guide.

(A Discourse on Slavery and Servitude)

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبادُكَ (If You punish them, they are indeed Your creatures) [5:118]. This is a statement that informs of the meaning of slavery (الرق) and servitude (العبودية). Although the verses containing this meaning are numerous in the Noble Quran, this verse includes a rational justification (التعليل العقلي) revealing that if there were a slave, it would be accepted by reason that his master has the right to dispose of him through punishment, because he is his master (المولى) who owns him.

Reason does not judge the permissibility of punishment and the authorization of burdensome disposal except after its judgment on the permissibility of all other non-burdensome disposals. Thus, the master may dispose of his slave however he wishes and with whatever he wishes. Reason only excludes those disposals it deems repugnant (يستهجنها) insofar as they are heinous and repugnant acts, not because the slave is a slave.

The necessary consequence of this is also that the slave must obey his master in what he charges him with and follow him in what he desires; he has no independence in any action if his master does not approve of it, as indicated to some extent by His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ (Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command) [21:26-27], and His saying, the Exalted: ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً عَبْداً مَمْلُوكاً لا يَقْدِرُ عَلى شَيْءٍ وَمَنْ رَزَقْناهُ مِنَّا رِزْقاً حَسَناً فَهو يُنْفِقُ مِنْهُ سِرًّا وَجَهْراً هَلْ يَسْتَوُونَ (Allah draws a parable: a slave owned [by another] who has no power over anything, and another whom We have provided a goodly provision from Us, and who spends out of it secretly and openly. Are they equal?) [16:75].

An exhaustive investigation into the aspects of what the Noble Quran views regarding the issue of servitude and slavery depends on several chapters:

1 — The Consideration of Servitude to Allah, the Glorified: In the Noble Quran, there are very many verses that count people as servants (عباد) of Allah, the Glorified, and build upon that the foundation of the religious call: people are servants and Allah is their true Master (مولاهم الحق). Indeed, this sometimes extends to include everyone in the heavens and the earth as being marked with the mark of servitude, such as the reality called "the angels" (الملك) despite their multitude, and the other reality which the Noble Quran calls "the jinn" (الجن). The Exalted said: إِنْ كُلُّ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِلَّا آتِي الرَّحْمنِ عَبْداً (There is none in the heavens and the earth but he comes to the All-beneficent as a servant) [19:93].

There is no doubt that the consideration of servitude to Allah, the Glorified, is a matter derived through analysis (التحليل) — the process of breaking down a concept into its essential components. This involves analyzing the meaning of servitude into its original parts and then judging the establishment of its reality after discarding the extraneous, accidental characteristics that occur to the original meaning in the rational beings of creation. For there are individuals among people, each of whom is called a servant (عبدا), and he is only called so because his self (نفسه) is owned by another—an ownership that permits that other, who is his owner and master, to dispose of him however he wishes and with whatever he intends, and strips from the servant absolute independence of will.

Contemplation of this meaning necessitates the judgment that man—and if you wish, you may expand and say: every being possessed of consciousness and will—is a servant of Allah, the Glorified, in the true sense of servitude. For Allah, the Glorified, is the owner of everything that is called a "thing" in the true sense of ownership (الملك). Nothing owns for itself or for another any harm or benefit, nor death, nor life, nor resurrection. No matter in existence is independent in essence (ذات), attribute (وصف), or act (فعل), except what Allah has granted it—a granting that does not nullify His ownership, the Exalted, nor does the ownership transfer from Him to another. Rather, He is the Owner of what He has granted them, and the One Powerful over what He has empowered them with, and He is Powerful over all things and encompasses all things.

This true authority (السلطة الحقيقية) and actual ownership (الملك الواقعي) is the source of the obligation of their submission to what He desires from them through His legislative will (إرادته التشريعية), and what He institutes for them of the laws of religion and the statutes of the Sharia (قوانين الشريعة) by which their affairs are rectified and their happiness in both abodes is attained.

In short, He, the Exalted, is their owner with a creative ownership (ملكا تكوينيا) by which they are His servants, humbled before His decree, whether they know Him or are ignorant of Him, whether they obey His charges or disobey Him. And He is their owner with a legislative ownership (ملكا تشريعيا) that necessitates for Him upon them listening and obedience, and judges upon them with godwariness (التقوى) and worship (العبادة).

This ownership and mastership (المولوية) is distinguished in terms of judgment from the ownership and mastership prevalent among people—as is the servitude corresponding to it—by the fact that since Allah, the Glorified, is the absolute creative owner and there is no owner besides Him, it is not permissible in the stage of legislative servitude to take a master besides Him or to worship anyone other than Him. The Exalted said: وَقَضى رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ (Your Lord has decided that you shall not worship anyone but Him) [18:23]. This is unlike the masters among men, for ownership there belongs to whoever prevails through one of the means of dominance.

Also, since there is nothing in the servants of Allah, the Glorified, that is not owned by Him, and they are not divided in their existence into owned and not owned, but rather were—in terms of their essences, attributes, states, and actions—owned by Him creatively, the legislation followed that. Thus, He judged among them with the permanence of servitude and its encompassing of everything that relates to them in any way. They cannot worship Allah from the aspect of some things relating to them to the exclusion of others—such as worshipping Him with the tongue but not the hand—just as they cannot make some of their worship for Allah and some for another. This is unlike the mastership prevalent among people, where it is not rationally permissible for the master to do whatever he wishes; contemplate this.

This is what indicates the absolute nature of verses such as His saying, the Exalted: ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا شَفِيعٍ (You have no guardian or intercessor besides Him) [32:4], and His saying: وَهُوَ اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْأُولى وَالْآخِرَةِ وَلَهُ الْحُكْمُ (He is Allah, there is no god except Him. To Him belongs all praise in this world and the Hereafter, and His is the judgement) [28:70], and His saying: يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ لَهُ الْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth glorifies Allah. To Him belongs all sovereignty and to Him belongs all praise, and He has power over all things) [64:1].

In any case, the servitude considered in relation to Him, the Exalted, is an analytical meaning derived from the servitude that rational beings consider for man in their society. It has an origin in human society, so let us see what its origin is.

2 — The Enslavement of Man and Its Causes: Slavery (الاستعباد) and servitude (الاسترقاق) were prevalent in human society, widespread and well-known until approximately seventy years before this date. Perhaps it is still practiced among some remote and distant tribes in Africa and Asia even today. The taking of male and female slaves was among the established practices (السنن) circulating among ancient peoples, the historical beginning of which can hardly be pinpointed. It had a specific system and general rules and laws among nations, and others specific to each nation.

The origin of its meaning is that the human self (النفس الإنسانية), upon the existence of specific conditions, becomes a owned commodity (سلعة مملوكة) like all other owned commodities of animals, plants, and minerals. When the self is owned, it is stripped of choice, its actions and effects are owned, and it is disposed of as desired.

This was their circulating practice regarding slavery, except that it was not based on a haphazard will or absolutely without being built on any condition. It was not permissible for one of them to possess everyone he liked, nor to own everyone he wished and intended through sale, gift, or otherwise. Thus, the origin of the meaning was not based on haphazardness, even if many haphazard matters might be found within the laws followed in it according to the differing opinions and practices of peoples.

Enslavement was based on a type of dominance and control (الغلبة والسيطرة), such as the dominance of war which results in the victorious conqueror doing to his defeated opponent whatever he wishes of killing, captivity, or otherwise; and the dominance of leadership (الرئاسة) which makes the tyrannical leader a doer of what he wishes within the sphere of his leadership; and the exclusivity of procreation and production which places the guardianship of the weak newborn in the hand of his strong father, who does with him as he sees fit, even sale, gift, exchange, lending, and the like.

It has been repeated in our previous discussions that the origin of ownership (الملك) in human society is built upon the power inherent in man to benefit from everything he can benefit from in some way—and man is a seeker of utility by nature (مستخدم بالطبع). Thus, man utilizes for the sake of preserving his life everything he is capable of, utilizing it and benefiting from the utilities of its existence, starting from the original matter, then the elements, then the various mineral compounds, then animals, and even man who is like him in humanity.

However, his dire need for society and cooperation forced him to accept partnership with other individuals of his species in benefiting from the utilities obtained from things through their joint actions. He and the other individuals of his species thus form a society (مجتمع) in which every part and every side is specialized in an action or actions, then the whole benefits from the whole. Or if you wish, say: then the results of the actions are divided among them, so each one of them enjoys that according to his social weight (زنته الاجتماعية). Therefore, we see that the individual human, being social, whenever he becomes strong and powerful, he nullifies natural civility and begins to utilize people through dominance, possessing their necks, and ruling over their selves, honors, and wealth as he proposes.

For this reason, if you contemplate freely their practice in the enslavement of man, you will find that they do not consider the ownership of man as long as he is part of the society and one of its components. Rather, either the owned person is judged to be outside the society, like the warring enemy (العدو المحارب) whose only concern is to destroy crops and progeny and erase man by name and mark—so he is outside the society of his enemy, and the enemy has the right to destroy him by annihilation and possess from him what he wishes because sanctity is removed; and similarly the father in relation to his small children and those following him, for he sees that they are among his followers in society without them being equal, similar, or balanced with him, so he may dispose of them even by killing, sale, and other means.

Or the owning person possesses a characteristic that calls him to believe he is above the society, without equaling them in weight or sharing with them in benefit; rather, he has the influence of judgment, the enjoyment of the best of what he chooses, and disposal over their selves even by ownership and enslavement.

Thus, it has become clear that the fundamental origin upon which man built the practice of enslavement and servitude is the right of exclusivity and absolute ownership (حق الاختصاص والتملك المطلق) that man believes for himself, and that man excludes no one from it except his partners in his human society who equal him in social weight and are fortified from him in the fortress of cooperation and mutual support. As for the rest, he sees no barrier to owning and enslaving them.

Their main reliance in that was on three groups: the warring enemy, weak children in relation to their fathers as well as women in relation to their guardians, and the defeated and debased in relation to the victorious and exalted.

3 — The Course of Slavery in History: Although the practice of slavery is unknown in terms of the history of its prevalence in human society, it is most likely that the slaves were taken in the first instance through fighting and dominance, then children and women were added. For this reason, we find in the history of powerful warring nations stories and tales, as well as laws and judgments related to slavery through captivity, that are not found in others.

It was prevalent among ancient civilized nations such as India, Greece, Rome, and Persia, and among the religious communities such as the Jews and Christians, as is understood from the Torah and the Gospel, until Islam appeared. It implemented its origin while narrowing its circle and reforming its established laws. Then the matter led to the Brussels Conference deciding to abolish slavery approximately seventy years ago.

Ferdinand Totel said in his dictionary of the luminaries of the East and West: "Slavery was widespread among the ancients, and slaves were taken from prisoners and captives of war and from defeated peoples. Slavery had a known system among the Jews, Greeks, Romans, and Arabs in the pre-Islamic period (الجاهلية) and Islam.

The system of slavery was abolished gradually: in India in the year 1843, in the French colonies in 1848, in the United States after the Civil War in 1865, and in Brazil in 1888, until the Brussels Conference took a decision to abolish slavery in 1890. However, it still exists actually among some tribes in Africa and Asia.

The principle of abolishing slavery is the equality of humans in rights and duties." End of quote.

4 — What was the View of Islam in this Regard? Islam categorized slavery according to its causes. It has been mentioned that the main causes were three: war, dominance, and guardianship such as fatherhood and the like. It abolished two of the three causes from their root, namely dominance and guardianship.

It considered the respect of people as a matter of Sharia, whether king or subject, ruler or ruled, commander or soldier, master or servant, by abolishing vital privileges and exclusivities, and establishing equality between individuals in the sanctity of their lives, honors, and wealth, and paying attention to their consciousness and will—which is complete choice within the limits of respected rights—and their actions and what they have earned—which is their authority over their wealth and the utilities of their existence from actions. The guardian of affairs (والي الأمر) in Islam has no authority over people except in the execution of legal punishments (الحدود) and judgments, and in aspects of public interests returning to the religious society. As for what his self desires and what he likes for his individual life, he is like any other person, not distinguished among them by any characteristic, nor is his command effective in much of what he desires for himself nor in little. Thereby, dominance-based slavery is removed by the removal of its subject matter.

It modified the guardianship of fathers over their children; they have the right of custody and protection, and they have the duty of upbringing, education, and preserving their wealth as long as they are restricted by childhood. When they reach maturity (الرشد), they and their fathers are equal in social religious rights, and they are free in their lives, having the choice in what they approve for themselves.

Yes, the recommendation to their fathers was emphasized upon them with kindness and observing the sanctity of upbringing. The Exalted said: وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُ وَهْناً عَلى وَهْنٍ وَفِصالُهُ فِي عامَيْنِ أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِي وَلِوالِدَيْكَ إِلَيَّ الْمَصِيرُ ، وَإِنْ جاهَداكَ عَلى أَنْ تُشْرِكَ بِي ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلا تُطِعْهما وَصاحِبْهُما فِي الدُّنْيا مَعْرُوفاً وَاتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ مَنْ أَنابَ إِلَيَّ (We have enjoined man concerning his parents: his mother carried him through weakness upon weakness, and his weaning takes two years: ‘Give thanks to Me and to your parents. To Me is the return.’ But if they urge you to ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them. Keep their company honourably in this world and follow the way of him who turns to Me) [31:14-15]. And the Exalted said: وَقَضى رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ وَبِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِنْدَكَ الْكِبَرَ أَحَدُهُما أَوْ كِلاهُما فَلا تَقُلْ لَهُما أُفٍّ وَلا تَنْهَرْهُما وَقُلْ لَهُما قَوْلاً كَرِيماً ، وَاخْفِضْ لَهُما جَناحَ الذُّلِّ مِنَ الرَّحْمَةِ وَقُلْ رَبِّ ارْحَمْهُما كَما رَبَّيانِي صَغِيراً (Your Lord has decided that you shall not worship anyone but Him and [He has enjoined] kindness to parents. Should any of them or both reach old age at your side, do not say to them, ‘Fie!’ And do not chide them, but speak to them with noble words. Lower the wing of humility to them out of mercy, and say, ‘My Lord! Have mercy on them, just as they reared me when I was [a] little [child]’) [17:23-24]. Disobedience to them (عقوقهما) has been counted in the Islamic Sharia as among the major, destructive sins.

As for women, a status was established for them in society and a social weight was considered for them that sound reason does not permit overstepping even by a step. Thus, they became one of the two halves of human society, whereas they had been deprived of that in the world. They were given the reins of marriage and wealth, whereas they had been deprived or were not independent in that.

They shared with men in matters and were specialized from them in matters, and men were specialized in matters—all of that out of complete consideration for the constitution of their existence and the composition of their structure. Then matters were made easy for them in which it was difficult for men, such as the matter of maintenance (النفقة), attending battlefields, and the like.

The discussion on all of that has preceded in detail at the end of Surah al-Baqarah in the second volume of the book, and at the beginning of Surah al-Nisa’ in the fourth volume. It was clarified there that women are specialized in Islam with more kindness (الإرفاق) in relation to men than what is found in other social practices, ancient or modern.

The Exalted said: لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا اكْتَسَبُوا وَلِلنِّساءِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا اكْتَسَبْنَ (Men shall have a share of what they earn, and women a share of what they earn) [4:32]. And the Exalted said: فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيما فَعَلْنَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ (There is no sin upon you in what they may do with themselves honourably) [2:234]. And the Exalted said: وَلَهُنَّ مِثْلُ الَّذِي عَلَيْهِنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ (And they have rights similar to those [of men] over them, honourably) [2:228]. And He said: أَنِّي لا أُضِيعُ عَمَلَ عامِلٍ مِنْكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ (I do not waste the work of any worker among you, whether male or female; you are all on the same footing) [3:195]. Then He gathered everyone in one statement, saying: لَها ما كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْها مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ (It shall have what it has earned, and it shall bear [the responsibility of] what it has done) [2:286]. And He said: وَلا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ إِلَّا عَلَيْها وَلا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى (No soul earns [anything] except against itself, and no bearer shall bear another’s burden) [6:164], among other absolute verses that take the individual human as a complete, perfect part of society, and give him individual independence by which he is separated from any other individual in the results of his actions, whether good or evil, benefit or harm, without excluding small or large, male or female.

Then He made them all equal in might (العزة) and dignity (الكرامة), then He nullified every might and dignity except religious dignity acquired through godwariness and action, saying: لِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (All might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful) [63:8]. And He said: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ لِتَعارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ (O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you) [43:13].

Islam has retained the third cause of the three causes of slavery, namely war. This is when the disbeliever who wars against Allah, His Messenger, and the believers is taken captive. As for the fighting of believers with one another, there is no captivity in it nor enslavement; rather, the rebellious group (الباغي) of the two parties is fought until it submits to the command of Allah. The Exalted said: وَإِنْ طائِفَتانِ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ اقْتَتَلُوا فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَهُما فَإِنْ بَغَتْ إِحْداهُما عَلَى الْأُخْرى فَقاتِلُوا الَّتِي تَبْغِي حَتَّى تَفِيءَ إِلى أَمْرِ اللهِ فَإِنْ فاءَتْ فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَهُما بِالْعَدْلِ وَأَقْسِطُوا إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ ، إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ (If two groups of the faithful fight one another, make peace between them. But if one of them aggresses against the other, then fight the one which aggresses until it returns to Allah’s ordinance. Then, if it returns, make peace between them with justice, and be equitable. Indeed Allah loves the equitable. The faithful are indeed brothers. Therefore, make peace between your brothers) [49:9-10].

That is because the warring enemy whose only concern is to annihilate humanity and destroy crops and progeny—the human primordial nature (الفطرة) does not doubt in the least that he must not be counted as part of the human society that has the right to enjoy the advantages of life and the rights of society, and that he must be repelled by annihilation or less. Upon this has the practice of the children of Adam proceeded since they inhabited the earth until our day, and upon this it will proceed.

When Islam established the structure of society—the religious society—on the basis of monotheism (التوحيد) and the governance of the Islamic religion, it nullified the membership of everyone who disdains monotheism and the governance of religion from human society, except with a covenant of protection (ذمة) or a treaty (عهد). Thus, the one outside the religion, its governance, and its treaty was outside human society, not to be dealt with except as a non-human whom man has the right to deprive of any blessing man enjoys in his life, and to repel him by purifying the earth from the filth of his arrogance and corruption. He is stripped of sanctity regarding his self, his action, and the results of any of his efforts. Thus, the Islamic army has the right to take prisoners and enslave upon victory.

5 — What is the Path to Enslavement in Islam? The Muslims prepare themselves against the disbelievers adjacent to them, so they complete the proof (الحجة) upon them and call them to the word of truth with wisdom, goodly exhortation, and arguing in the best manner. If they respond, they are brothers in religion, having what the Muslims have and owing what they owe. If they refuse except rejection, then if they are People of the Book (أهل كتاب) and accept the tribute (الجزية), they are left under their protection. If they take a treaty, whether they are People of the Book or not, their treaty is fulfilled. If none of that occurs, they are given notice of war and fought.

Among them, whoever draws a sword and enters the battle is killed, but whoever offers peace is not killed. The oppressed (المستضعفون) among men, women, and children are not killed. They are not attacked by night, nor assassinated, nor is water cut off from them, nor are they tortured or mutilated. They are fought until there is no more discord (فتنة) and religion is for Allah. If they desist, then there is no aggression except against the wrongdoers.

When they overcome them and the war lays down its burdens, whatever the Muslims have gained control over of their persons and wealth belongs to them. The history of the battles and expeditions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has included brilliant, shining pages full of just and beautiful conduct, containing subtleties of chivalry and manliness, and wonders of righteousness and kindness.

6 — What is the Conduct of Islam Regarding Male and Female Slaves? When slavery is established upon those it is established upon, they become "what the right hand possesses" (ملك يمين); the benefits of their labor belong to another and their maintenance is upon their master.

Islam has recommended that the master treat his slave as one of his family, and he is of them; he should make them equal in the necessities and needs of life. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to eat with his slaves and servants and sit with them, and he did not prefer himself over them in food, clothing, or the like.

They should not be burdened beyond their capacity, nor tortured, nor insulted, nor wronged. It was permitted for them to marry among themselves with the permission of their masters, and for free people to marry them, and for them to participate in testimonies and share in actions during slavery and after manumission (الانعتاق).

The kindness of Islam toward them reached such a point that they shared with free people in general matters. A group of them were appointed to governorship, leadership, and army command, as recorded in the history of early Islam. Among the great companions were several freedmen (الموالي) such as Salman, Bilal, and others.

Here is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) manumitting his slave-girl Safiyyah bint Huyayy ibn Akhtab and marrying her, and marrying Juwayriyah bint al-Harith after the encounter with Banu al-Mustaliq when she was among their captives—and they were two hundred households of women and children—and that became the cause for the manumission of all of them. The summary of the story has passed in the fourth volume of the book.

It is a necessary part of the conduct of Islam that it prefers the God-conscious servant over the profligate free master, and that it permits the servant to own wealth and enjoy general advantages of life with permission from his master. This is a summary of Islam's treatment of them.

Then it emphasized the recommendation and urged in the most beautiful manner the freeing of slaves (تحرير رقبتهم) and bringing them out from the condition of slavery into the atmosphere of freedom. Their number continued to decrease thereby, and their collective changed into freedmen and free people for the sake of Allah. It did not stop at that but made the freeing of a slave one of the types of expiations (الكفارات), such as the expiation for killing and the expiation for breaking the fast. It permitted for them the contract of manumission (المكاتبة), the conditional manumission (الاشتراط), and the manumission effective upon the master's death (التدبير)—all of that out of concern for them and intending their liberation and joining them to the righteous human society in a complete joining that cuts off the root of debasement.

7 — The Result of the Investigation in the preceding chapters: From what has passed, three matters are obtained:

First: That Islam spared no effort in nullifying the causes of slavery, reducing them, and weakening them, until it stood upon one of them that cannot be avoided by the decisive judgment of the primordial nature, which is the permissibility of enslaving every person who wars against the religion, opposes human society, and does not submit to the truth in any way.

Second: That it employed all possible means in honoring them—male and female slaves—and bringing their vital affairs close to the life of the free members of society until they became like one of them, even if they did not become one of them. Nothing remained between them except one thin veil, which is that the surplus of their labor beyond the requirement of their life—an average life—belongs to their masters, not to them. Or if you wish, say: there is no real distinction between the free person and the servant in Islam except the permission of the master for the servant.

Third: That it used every effective means to join the class of the owned to the society of the free through encouragement and urging in some instances, and through obligation and necessity in others such as expiations, and through authorization and implementation in matters like conditional manumission, tadbir, and mukatabah.

8 — The Course of Enslavement in History They have mentioned (1) that enslavement first appeared through raiding and captivity. Before that, when tribes were victorious in their wars and battles and took captives, they would kill them to the last man. Later, they saw fit to leave them alive and possess them like other spoils of war—not initially to benefit from their labor, but as an act of kindness toward them, to preserve the species, and out of respect for the moral laws that began to emerge within them through gradual progress on the path of civilization.

This custom only appeared among the tribes after the method of subsistence through hunting had departed from them, as they did not possess the abundance in that lifestyle to justify spending on male and female slaves, until they transitioned to a nomadic and migratory life and became capable of doing so.

With the spread of enslavement among tribes and nations, regardless of its form, human social life transformed through the emergence of aspects of order and discipline in societies first, and the division of labor second.

Enslavement, while prevalent in the world, did not follow a single pattern across the regions of the inhabited earth. It was not established as a custom in some regions at all, such as Australia, Central Asia, Siberia, North America, the Eskimo regions, and some parts of Africa north of the Nile and south of the Zambezi.

Conversely, it was widespread in the Arabian Peninsula, wild Africa, Europe, and South America. It was prevalent among the Jews; in the Torah, there is a call for slaves to obey their masters. Likewise, among the Christians; in Paul’s letter to Philemon, Onesimus was a runaway slave whom Paul returned to his master.

The Jews were the most lenient of people toward their slaves. One piece of evidence for this is that we have not found among them towering structures similar to the pyramids built in Egypt or the historical Assyrian buildings, for those were the products of the arduous labor of slaves. The Romans and Greeks were the most severe of nations toward slaves.

In the Eastern Roman Empire after Constantine, the idea of liberation spread until slavery was abolished there in the 13th century AD. It remained in the Western Roman Empire in another form: they would buy and sell farms along with their farmers—farming being among the occupations of slaves—but forced labor was abolished among them.

Enslavement was prevalent in most European kingdoms until the year 1772 AD. Sometime before that, a treaty was concluded between the two states of England and Spain, stipulating that the English would collect four thousand eight hundred souls annually from the slaves of Africa for thirty years to sell to them in exchange for significant sums of money.

Public opinion revolted in 1761 against slavery and enslavement among them. The earliest group to stand against it was the religious sect of the "Quakers" (1). They persisted in this until a legal article was established in 1772 stating that anyone who enters British soil is free.

In 1788, after careful investigation, it appeared that England dealt with two hundred thousand slave souls every year, and those brought from Africa to America alone numbered one hundred thousand.

This continued until enslavement was abolished in Britain in 1833, and the state paid twenty million pounds to the slave-trading companies as the price for the male and female slaves it liberated. In this event, (770,380) souls were manumitted.

(1) Taken from: 1 — Encyclopedia of Religion and Ethics by James Hastings, British edition; 2 — The Outline of History by H.G. Wells, British edition; 3 — The Spirit of the Laws by Montesquieu, Tehran edition. (1) Quaker.

Enslavement was abolished in America in 1862 after intense struggles endured by the American people. The north and south of this kingdom differed in their taking of slaves: North America took male and female slaves merely for luxury, whereas in the South, most work was agricultural and tilling, and they were in dire need of many working hands, so they took slaves to profit from their labor. Therefore, they were reluctant to accept general liberation.

Enslavement continued to be abolished kingdom after kingdom until the Brussels Declaration was concluded in 1890 AD to abolish the custom of enslavement. The nations signed it, it was implemented in the kingdoms, and slavery was abolished in the world, thereby manumitting millions of souls. This concludes the summary of what they mentioned.

You will find, with your piercing insight, that this long struggle and dispute, followed by the enactment of abolition laws and the execution of judgments, all revolved around enslavement through subjugation or conquest, as evidenced by the fact that most or all slaves were brought from the regions of Africa where this was practiced. As for enslavement through wartime captivity (السبي الحربي), which Islam implemented, it was never a subject of this discussion.

9 — A Look at Their Foundation: This primordial freedom (الحرية الفطرية) which we call the freedom gifted to man (and we do not know what reason strips it from other animal species, given they resemble man in psychological perception and the will that motivates action, except to say that it is man who strips them of it to benefit from them) does not branch from any principle except that man is equipped with an internal perception (شعور باطني) that distinguishes for him what he finds pleasurable and what he finds painful, and then with a will (إرادة) that motivates him to attract what pleases him and repel what pains him; thus, he has the right to choose for himself what he wishes.

Human perception is not restricted to attaching to one thing and not another, as if the weak, humiliated human does not perceive what the strong, empowered human perceives. Nor is the human will limited by a boundary that prevents it from attaching to some of what it desires, or forces it to attach to what the will of another has attached to, so that it might act for the benefit of another and forget itself. The weak, conquered human desires for himself the likeness of everything that the human who conquered and subdued him desires for himself. There is no natural link between the will of the weak and the will of the strong that forces the will of the weak not to attach to what the will of the strong has attached to, or to be annihilated within the will of the strong so that the two wills become one will acting for the benefit of the strong, or that the will follows in a manner that strips it of independence (الاستقلال).

Since this is the case, and since it is the right of the laws of life to be built upon the foundation of the natural constitution (البنية الطبيعية), it was mandatory that man live free in himself and free in his actions. From this breast, the abolition of enslavement is suckled.

However, we ought to contemplate this freedom gifted to man: is it absolute within human society since it was born and lived within human structures?

The human species—as far as we know—has always lived in a state of society (حال الاجتماع), and according to its existential apparatus (جهازه الوجودي), it cannot do otherwise. It is impossible for a society to persist in a state of society, even for a moment, except with a shared custom (سنة مشتركة) among the members of the society, whether it be a just, rational custom or an unjust or arbitrary one, or however it may be described. This custom, whatever it may be, limits individual freedom.

Furthermore, man cannot live except with some degree of disposal (تصرف) over matter that ensures his survival, and he cannot achieve this except by specializing in what he disposes of with a type of exclusivity that we call ownership (الملك)—broader than both legal right and technical ownership. That which this person wears while wearing it, the other cannot wear; and that which an individual eats, drinks, or occupies by settling in it, another cannot independently possess. This is nothing but a limitation for the one not disposing in the exercise of his absolute will, and a restriction of his freedom.

Dispute (الاختلاف) has never ceased to accompany this species since it inhabited the earth. Not a day has passed over these individuals scattered across the vast earth but that the sun rises upon disputes and sets upon disputes that lead them to the destruction of souls, the loss of honors, and the looting of wealth. If man saw for himself—that is, for humanity—absolute freedom, these disputes would have no effect among them.

The custom of retribution (المجازاة) and accountability (المؤاخذة) has never ceased to be operational among diverse societies, whether civilized or primitive. There is no meaning to retribution except that society possesses from the criminal human some of the blessings that creation gifted him, and that it strips from him some freedom. If society, or the one in whose hand authority lies in society, did not possess the life of the murdering criminal sentenced to retaliation (القصاص), it would not be able to strip it from him. If the sinner, taken for his sin and held accountable with types of punishment and injury such as amputation, striking, imprisonment, and the like, did not have the judgment and its execution possess from him what it strips of the affairs of life, comfort, or financial authority, this would not be valid. How could it be valid to prevent the unjust transgressor from being unjust and transgressing, or to defend the sanctuary of life, honor, or wealth, except by stripping some of the freedom of the preventing conqueror?

In short, what no person of reason can doubt is that the persistence of absolute human freedom in human society, even for a moment, would cause the collapse of the social order (النظام الاجتماعي) instantly. Thus, this society—which is also primordial (فطري) to man and without which he cannot live—is what restricts the absolute primordial freedom that his instinctive will and perception gifted him. A human society cannot live except with some restriction of absolute freedom, just as it cannot live with the total nullification of freedom from its root. Human society has always preserved this freedom between these two boundaries—the freedom which we are led to imagine, due to the abundance of Western propaganda, that they are the ones who created its name after inventing its meaning and preserved it in its absolute form.

So, this primordial society is what restricts that primordial freedom and limits it, just as the physical and non-physical natural powers restrict one another. Some cease action out of regard for the state of another that accompanies it, like the power of sight (قوة الإبصار), which is the principle of sight in an absolute sense; it performs its function until the sense of touch in the eye tires or the thinking power becomes exhausted, so the sight ceases its action, restricted by the action of its companion. The sense of taste (الذائقة) finds pleasure in taking delicious food, masticating, and swallowing it until the jaw muscles tire, so the taste is restricted and ceases its desire.

Thus, primordial society is not realized for man except by him yielding some of his freedom in action and his unrestrained pursuit of enjoyment.

10 — What is the Extent of the Limitation? As for the extent to which the gifted freedom is limited by primordial society and its primordial absoluteness is restricted, it varies according to the variation of human societies based on the abundance or scarcity of the operational laws considered in the society. For that which restricts freedom after the principle of society is the law (القانون) implemented among the people. Whenever laws increase and become more precise in monitoring their actions, the deprivation of freedom and unrestrained behavior increases, and whenever they decrease, it decreases.

However, that which is unavoidable in any society for any assumed community, and the duty which the social human cannot belittle or be negligent about, is the preservation of the existence of the society and its being, since there is no life for man without it, and the preservation of the operational customs and the laws current within it from deficiency and violation. Therefore, you will not find any human society except that it has a defensive aspect that protects lives and offspring and guards them from annihilation and destruction, and a guardian (ولي) who manages their affairs and preserves the current custom and the respected habits among them from violation by spreading social security and disciplining the transgressing oppressor. Existing history also confirms this.

If this is the case, then the first legitimate right for society in the law of primordial nature (شريعة الفطرة) is to strip freedom from the enemy of society in the very principle of its gathering. Or if you wish, say: to possess from its enemy—who destroys its life and ruins its tilth and progeny—his self and his labor, and to take away the freedom of his will through whatever it wishes of killing or less, and to strip from the enemy of the custom and law the freedom of action and unrestrained violation, and to possess from him what he loses through retribution of life, wealth, or otherwise.

How can man—even the individual man—concede to the freedom of an enemy who neither respects the life of his society so as to befriend him, partner with him, and blend with him, nor turns a blind eye to the destruction and annihilation of his society so as to leave them to their own affairs? Is the combination of the primordial concern for society with the leaving of this enemy and his freedom of action anything but a combination of explicit contradictions, foolishness, or madness?

Thus, it has become clear from what has passed: First: That building upon the absolute freedom of man is a matter contrary to the explicit primordial right (الحق الفطري) legislated for man, which is among the first of the legislated primordial rights. Second: That the right of enslavement (حق الاستعباد) which Islam considered is that which accords with the law of primordial nature. It is to enslave the enemies of the true religion who fight the Islamic society, stripping them of freedom of action, bringing them into the religious society, and requiring them to live in the garb of servitude until they are raised with righteous religious upbringing, and are gradually manumitted to join the free society safe and sound. The Guardian (ولي الأمر) has the right to purchase them and manumit them all if he sees the benefit of the religious society in that, or to follow another path in that which does not abrogate the divine rulings.

10 — What was the Outcome of the Abolition? The great powers implemented the Brussels Conference resolution and strictly prohibited the sale of slaves. Male and female slaves were manumitted, so they are no longer lined up in the shops of slave-traders nor driven like sheep. Following that, the practice of taking eunuchs was abolished, and today one can hardly find any of these or those, even as few examples, except what is perhaps mentioned regarding primitive peoples.

But does this amount—meaning the disappearance of the name of enslavement and slavery from tongues and the absence of those called by this name from sight—satisfy the critical researcher in this matter? Or does he not ask whether this issue is a verbal one where it suffices to prohibit the mention of the name, and it is enough in its implementation to call the slave free even if the benefits of his labor are stripped and he follows another in his will, or is the issue a conceptual one (معنوية) where the state of the meaning is observed according to its reality and its external effects?

Behold the Second World War; only a few decades have passed since it forced the victorious nations upon their defeated enemy to surrender unconditionally. Then they occupied their lands, took millions from their wealth, dominated their souls and offspring, and moved millions of their captives into their own kingdoms, employing them in whatever they wished and however they wished, and the matter continues as such until today.

I wonder, does enslavement have a manifestation (مصداق) that is not it, even if the use of its word is prohibited? Does it have any meaning other than the stripping of absolute freedom, the possession of will and labor, and the strong, empowered one enforcing his judgment upon the weak, humiliated one however he wishes and desires, whether in justice or injustice?

By Allah, how strange! The ruling of Islam is called enslavement when it is of a similar nature in the best possible way, yet their ruling is not called such. Islam takes the easiest and lightest of ways in it, while they take the most arduous and violent. We have seen their "love" and "friendship" when they occupied our lands under the titles of love, protection, and guardianship; so what is the state of the one over whom they triumphed with enmity and injury?

From here it appears that the abolition resolution was nothing but a political game—in reality, a taking in the form of a returning. As for enslavement through war and fighting, Islam implemented it and they implemented it in practice, even if they prohibited the utterance of its name with the tongue. As for enslavement through fathers selling their children, which they prohibited, Islam had prohibited it before. As for enslavement through conquest and governmental authority, Islam had prohibited it before. As for these people, they agreed on its prohibition, but did the prohibition stop at the stage of the word like its counterpart, or did it cross over to the stage of the meaning and was it matched by action?!

You can extract the answer to this question by casting a glance over the history of European colonialisms in Asia, Africa, and America, and the atrocities they committed, and the blood, honors, and wealth they shed, violated, and looted, and the dominations they brought about—not by the one, the hundred, or the thousand.

You do not need to travel this path in its distance—if it is distant—for it may suffice you to contemplate the news of what the people of Algeria have been suffering from France for years of the annihilation of souls, the destruction of the country, and the severity toward its people; and what the Arab kingdoms receive from the English; and what the Blacks and the Reds endure in America; and Eastern Europe from the socialist republics; and what we suffer at the hands of these and those. All of that, in its wording, is advice and compassion, but in its meaning, it is enslavement and slavery!

It has appeared from all that has passed that they have adopted in the stage of action what Islam legislated of the permissibility and stripping of absolute freedom when its primordial cause exists—which is the war of those who wish to demolish society and destroy humanity. This is a legislated ruling in the law of primordial nature that has a factual basis (أصل واقعي) that does not change, which is the need of humanity for its survival to repel what opposes its existence and contradicts its persistence; then a social, rational principle (أصل اجتماعي عقلائي) that does not alter, branching from its factual basis, which is the obligation to preserve human society from non-existence and demolition.

This is what they aimed for in their action and adopted in meaning while denying it in name, except that they transgressed this legislated category to another non-legislated one, which is enslavement due to conquest and authority. They continue to enslave thousands and millions before the talk of abolition and after it, and they buy, sell, gift, and lend, except that they do not call that enslavement. Rather, it is called colonialism (استعمار), or possession (استملاك), or guardianship (قيمومة), or protection (حماية), or care and assistance (عناية وإعانة), or other such words, none of which are intended except to be a cover for the meaning of enslavement. Whenever one of them wears out or is torn, it is cast away and another is brought.

Nothing remains of what the Brussels resolution abolished—and which still strikes the ears of the world and its people, and which the civilized nations, who are the pioneers of advanced civilization and in whose hands is the banner of human freedom, boast about—except enslavement through the sale of sons and daughters and castration. There is no significant benefit in it returning to them, along with it being more like an individual issue than a social one. Its abolition, nonetheless, is a verbal, promotional argument in their hands like their other arguments that do not exceed the station of the word yet produce the effect of the meaning.

Yes, there remains another point of discussion: that Islam begins in its spoils of war—of slaves or wealth other than land conquered by force—with the individuals of its society, dividing them among them, then it ends with the state, as was the practice in the early days of Islam. These people keep the benefit from them as a right restricted to the state. This is another issue different from the issue of the principle of enslavement; perhaps we will be successful in exhaustively researching it in what will come, if Allah wills, of the discussion on the verses of Zakat and Khums, and Allah is the One whose help is sought.
